Scrobipalpuloides. Tuta. The taxonomic revision published in 2021 returned the species to the genus Phthorimaea after morphological analysis showed that it belongs to an evolutionary group that includes Phthorimaea operculella.

A genus is a group of closely related species that share more specific anatomical and evolutionary characteristics than those shared at the family or tribe levels. The insect is currently placed in the genus: Phthorimaea This genus includes small moths, several of which are associated with plants of the family Solanaceae. Among the best-known agricultural species within this genus are: Phthorimaea absoluta The tomato leaf and fruit borer, known as Tuta absoluta. Phthorimaea operculella The potato tuber moth, an important pest that attacks potato leaves and tubers in the field and in storage. Relationship Between the Two Species Characteristic Phthorimaea absoluta Phthorimaea operculella Common name Tuta absoluta Potato tuber moth Principal economic host Tomato Potato Most important damage Leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits Leaves, stems, and tubers Pheromone trap Tuta-specific pheromone Potato tuber moth-specific pheromone Species absoluta operculella Both pests may occur in the same agricultural area, particularly where tomato and potato are grown. Therefore, observing a small moth alone is not sufficient to identify the species.

The species is the most precise principal level in scientific classification used to identify an organism. The complete species name is: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The first part refers to the genus: Phthorimaea While the second part refers to the specific epithet: absoluta The word absoluta alone is not written as a complete scientific name because the correct name consists of both the genus name and the specific epithet. This species is distinguished by an integrated set of characteristics, including: External morphology of the egg, larva, pupa, and moth. Wing structure and venation. Shape of body and wing scales. Characteristics of male and female genitalia. Molecular traits and genetic material. Composition of the sex pheromone. Biological behavior. Strong association with tomato and solanaceous hosts. Nature of mines and damage. Scientific identification of the species is not based on a single symptom, such as the presence of a leaf mine, because several insects can form similar mines.

sectionTitle: Why Was the Genus Changed from Tuta to Phthorimaea?
text: For many years, the species was known as: Tuta absoluta However, its position within the tribe Gnorimoschemini remained taxonomically debated because of the similarity among several genera within this group. The modern taxonomic revision relied on analysis of a set of morphological characters that could be compared among closely related genera. The analysis showed that the species known as Tuta absoluta falls within a monophyletic group that includes: Phthorimaea operculella Which is the type species of the genus Phthorimaea. Based on this relationship, the species was returned to its original genus, and the name became: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The genus Tuta remained taxonomically valid, but according to the modern revision, it no longer includes this species. The difference between the two names does not indicate any change in the insect or the nature of its damage, but rather a revision of its position within the taxonomic system.

Scientific classification can be compared to a hierarchical address that precisely identifies the location of the insect. If the species represents a specifically identified individual, the higher levels resemble: Country. Governorate. District. Village. Street. House. The lower the level, the more precise the identification becomes. For Tuta absoluta: Animalia: indicates that it is an animal. Arthropoda: indicates that it is an arthropod with an exoskeleton. Hexapoda: indicates that it belongs to the six-legged animals. Insecta: indicates that it is an insect. Lepidoptera: indicates that it is a moth or butterfly with scale-covered wings. Gelechiidae: places it among the small moths belonging to this family. Gnorimoschemini: associates it with a more specific group of closely related moths. Phthorimaea: identifies its genus. Phthorimaea absoluta: identifies the species itself individually. Classification therefore does not merely involve memorizing Latin names; it progressively clarifies the identity of the insect and its relationship to other organisms.

sectionTitle: Taxonomic Difference Between Tuta and Leaf Miner
text: Taxonomy helps clarify that Tuta absoluta differs fundamentally from leaf-mining flies commonly known in the field as leaf miners. Rank Tuta absoluta Leaf-Mining Flies Kingdom Animalia Animalia Phylum Arthropoda Arthropoda Class Insecta Insecta Order Lepidoptera Diptera Family Gelechiidae Agromyzidae Genus Phthorimaea Usually Liriomyza Adult insect Moth Fly Damaging stage Lepidopteran larva Dipteran larva Both groups are capable of forming mines inside leaves, but similarity in damage does not indicate close taxonomic relationship. Tuta absoluta is taxonomically closer to the potato tuber moth than to Liriomyza flies, even though both may infest tomato leaves.

sectionTitle: Taxonomic Difference Between Tuta and True Weevils
text: Some growers refer to Tuta as “the weevil,” but taxonomy demonstrates that this name is scientifically incorrect. Rank Tuta absoluta True Weevils Class Insecta Insecta Order Lepidoptera Coleoptera Adult insect Moth Beetle Wings Covered with scales Forewings usually hardened Larva Lepidopteran larva Beetle larva Metamorphosis Complete Complete Tuta is a lepidopteran insect, whereas true weevils belong to the order Coleoptera.

sectionTitle: Importance of Taxonomy in Integrated Pest Management
text: Identification of the Pheromone Each species has a specific pheromone composition, and the pheromone of Tuta absoluta is not suitable for monitoring all moths or leaf-mining insects. Larval Diagnosis A lepidopteran larva differs from a dipteran larva in: Head shape. Presence of legs. Larval movement. Structure of mouthparts. Feeding behavior. Selection of Natural Enemies Some parasitoids and predators are associated with specific groups or life stages. Therefore, correct species identification affects the selection of biological control agents. Insecticide Resistance Management Sites of action and effective insecticide groups differ according to pest species. Resistance records and IRAC rotation programs are also based on correct identification of the pest species. Preventing Confusion Among Traps Tuta absoluta, potato tuber moth, and other small moth species may occur in the same agricultural area. Taxonomic diagnosis prevents all moths found in a trap from being interpreted as a single species. Plant Quarantine Competent authorities use the scientific name and international code to identify the pest in: Phytosanitary certificates. Survey reports. Distribution records. Quarantine procedures. International notifications. Inspection of transplants and fruits. Tuta absoluta carries the EPPO code: GNORAB This is a stable code that permits tracking of the pest even when its scientific name changes.

Routine field inspection does not require memorizing all taxonomic ranks, but it does depend on understanding the group to which the pest belongs. When diagnosing Tuta absoluta in the field, several indicators are examined: Shape of leaf mines. Presence of frass inside mines. Form of the larva and its head. Presence of thoracic legs and abdominal prolegs. Infestation of growing points and stems. Presence of holes in fruits. Appearance of the adult moth. Results of the pheromone trap. Nature of the host plant. Laboratory diagnosis when uncertainty exists. Specialized taxonomic diagnosis may rely on: Characteristics of the genitalia. Microscopic features of the adult insect. Fine larval characteristics. Molecular fingerprinting. Sequencing of DNA regions. Structure of the mitochondrial genome. Laboratory examination becomes necessary when the pest is detected in a new region or when morphologically similar species are present.

Tuta absoluta belongs to the kingdom Animalia, phylum Arthropoda, and class Insecta. It is a moth in the order Lepidoptera and is not a fly or a weevil. Its complete classification is as follows: Kingdom: Animalia. Phylum: Arthropoda. Subphylum: Hexapoda. Class: Insecta. Order: Lepidoptera. Superfamily: Gelechioidea. Family: Gelechiidae. Subfamily: Gelechiinae. Tribe: Gnorimoschemini. Genus: Phthorimaea. Species: Phthorimaea absoluta. The complete modern scientific name is: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The name widely recognized in agriculture is: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) Taxonomy confirms that: Tuta is a moth belonging to Lepidoptera. Liriomyza leaf miners are flies belonging to Diptera. True weevils are beetles belonging to Coleoptera. Potato tuber moth is a species closely related to Tuta within the genus Phthorimaea, but it is not the same species. This classification enables precise identification of the pest, prevents confusion with other insects, and links field diagnosis to the appropriate monitoring, control, and plant-quarantine measures. Key References EPPO Global Database. Phthorimaea absoluta (GNORAB): Taxonomy and Distribution. Chang, P. E. C. & Metz, M. A. (2021). Classification Review of Phthorimaea absoluta and Related Gnorimoschemini. GBIF Backbone Taxonomy. Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917. References

The spread of Tuta absoluta represents one of the fastest cases of biological invasion recorded among modern agricultural pests. For many years, the insect remained associated with South America, before appearing in Spain in 2006 and subsequently spreading within a short period across most of the Mediterranean Basin, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and Asia. In less than two decades, the pest shifted from being a regional problem known in South American countries to a global threat to tomato production in both open fields and protected cultivation. Its spread was not the result of the insect’s flight capacity alone, but rather the interaction of several factors, most importantly: International trade in tomato fruits and plant material. Movement of transplants, packaging materials, and means of transport. Natural movement between neighboring areas. Continuous cultivation of tomato and solanaceous hosts throughout the year. The insect’s ability to produce successive generations. Concealment of eggs and larvae within or on plant tissues. Climatic suitability of Mediterranean, African, and Asian regions for establishment. Delayed detection of infestation in newly invaded regions. Weak agricultural sanitation and inadequate disposal of crop residues. A distinction must be made between the date of the insect’s actual introduction and the date of its first official record. The pest may arrive in a given area and remain at low population levels for several weeks or months before being detected and diagnosed. Therefore, the years of record presented in this chapter represent the first documented detection and do not always establish the exact date on which the first individual entered a country.

Geographical Origin Tuta absoluta is a pest of Neotropical origin, and its native range lies in South America, particularly in the western part of the Neotropical region, known as: Western Neotropics Historically, its presence has been associated with areas extending along both sides of the Andes and with tomato-producing regions in South American countries. Within its native range, the pest has been recorded in countries including: Peru. Chile. Argentina. Bolivia. Brazil. Colombia. Ecuador. Paraguay. Uruguay. Venezuela. Accordingly, the insect is not considered an introduced or invasive species throughout most of South America, but rather an indigenous component of the pest community associated with tomato and the family Solanaceae in the region. First Scientific Description in Peru The British entomologist Edward Meyrick scientifically described the insect in 1917 based on specimens collected from the Huancayo region of Peru. At the time of its original description, it was named: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 Its taxonomic position then changed several times until it became internationally known as: Tuta absoluta Before being recently reassigned to the genus Phthorimaea. The fact that the insect was described in 1917 does not mean that it first appeared in Peru during that year; rather, it means that this was the date of its formal scientific recognition and taxonomic naming. Spread Within South America During the twentieth century, the insect became one of the principal pests of tomato in South America. By the final decades of that century, it had been recorded in most of the continent’s major tomato-producing countries. Its spread within South America was facilitated by: Large-scale tomato cultivation. Overlapping planting dates. Movement of fruits and transplants between regions. Presence of potato, eggplant, and other solanaceous hosts. Suitable temperatures. Ability of moths to move between fields. Persistence of volunteer plants and solanaceous weeds. The pest became a major problem in both fresh-market and processing tomatoes, especially in Argentina, Brazil, Chile, and Peru. Ecological Balance in the Native Range In South America, the insect evolved over long periods within an environment containing a diverse group of natural enemies, including: Egg parasitoids. Larval parasitoids. Predators. Entomopathogenic fungi. Naturally occurring pathogens. For this reason, Tuta absoluta in its native range has historically been associated with a more complex ecological network than that present in areas it invaded more recently. However, the presence of natural enemies did not prevent it from becoming an economic pest, particularly where intensive spray programs killed predators and parasitoids and promoted the development of insecticide resistance. The accumulated experience in South America provides an important foundation for understanding: Insecticide resistance in the pest. Effectiveness of larval parasitoids. Role of predators. Influence of resistant cultivars. Importance of integrated pest management.

sectionTitle: Movement from South America to Europe
text: How Did the Insect Leave Its Native Range? The exact route through which Tuta absoluta moved from South America to Europe has not been conclusively determined. Pest risk analyses indicate that introduction may have occurred through agricultural trade, particularly through: Infested tomato fruits. Fruits carrying eggs or larvae near the calyx. Packaging materials and crates. Sorting residues associated with shipments. Infested plants or transplants. Plant material belonging to the family Solanaceae. Tuta absoluta moths have been found in European wholesale markets and packing facilities receiving imported fruits, demonstrating the ability of fruit-handling chains to transport the pest. However, the presence of the insect in a packing facility does not by itself prove that the facility was the source of the initial spread. Therefore, the precise route of introduction into Spain remains uncertain. First Record in Europe: Spain in 2006 Tuta absoluta was first recorded in Europe at the end of 2006 in tomato fields in the province of Castellón, within the Valencia region of eastern Spain. In 2007, infestation appeared at other locations along the Spanish Mediterranean coast, particularly in Valencia Province, and the pest was also detected on the island of Ibiza in the Balearic Islands. Some winter tomato crops suffered severe infestation, demonstrating at an early stage that the pest could: Establish under Mediterranean climatic conditions. Reproduce in both greenhouses and open fields. Move between production areas. Cause major losses within a short period. The record in Spain represented the principal turning point in the pest’s global history, as the Iberian Peninsula became the gateway for its spread into the Mediterranean Basin and Afro-Eurasia. Rapid Spread Within Spain After its first detection, the pest did not remain confined to Castellón but moved into tomato-producing areas along the Spanish Mediterranean coast. This spread was facilitated by: High density of tomato cultivation. Connectivity among production areas. Movement of fruits and packaging materials. Presence of greenhouse production. Mild winters in coastal areas. Movement of moths between fields. Continuous cultivation of suitable hosts. Within a short period, the pest became established in most Spanish tomato-producing regions, including the Canary Islands.

sectionTitle: Spread of Tuta absoluta in Europe
text: Spread in Western and Southern Europe Following its record in Spain, the insect moved rapidly eastward and northward through tomato production and marketing networks. Major milestones in its European spread included: Spain: first record at the end of 2006. France: detections during 2008. Italy: first record in spring 2008 in Calabria, followed by spread into several Italian regions. Portugal: officially recorded in 2009. Malta: recorded in 2009. Switzerland: recorded in 2009. Greece: detected in 2009. Cyprus: detected in protected tomato production areas in 2009. United Kingdom: outbreaks appeared in tomato production and handling facilities during 2009. Germany, the Netherlands, and other northern countries: individual moths or outbreaks were recorded, often in association with packing facilities or protected cultivation. Within a few years, the insect reached more than thirty countries across Europe and the western Palaearctic region. More Than One Pathway of Spread The spread of Tuta absoluta in Europe did not result entirely from continuous natural movement of moths from Spain into neighboring countries. In some countries, geographically separated outbreaks appeared at approximately the same time, as occurred in Greece, where the distribution pattern indicated the possibility of several independent introductions rather than gradual natural spread from a single source. This demonstrates the presence of two overlapping pathways: Natural Spread This occurs through the flight of moths between neighboring areas and fields. Human-Assisted Spread This occurs through the transport of: Fruits. Transplants. Packaging materials. Vehicles. Sorting residues. Plant products between countries and regions. This pattern explains the sudden appearance of the insect at locations far from the nearest known outbreak. Southern Europe and Northern Europe The probability of pest establishment was greater in southern European countries with Mediterranean climates, where there are: Mild winter temperatures. Open-field and protected tomato production. Long production seasons. High densities of fields and greenhouses. In some northern European countries, lower temperatures limited the insect’s ability to persist year-round in open fields. However, heated greenhouses, packing stations, and wholesale markets provided environments in which the pest could survive or temporary outbreaks could occur. Consequently, its status differed among European countries, where the pest was: Established and widely distributed in some countries. Limited to certain areas in others. Transient or subject to eradication in some colder regions.

North Africa in 2008 Tuta absoluta crossed the Mediterranean Sea shortly after its appearance in Spain. During 2008, it was recorded in three major North African countries: Morocco. Algeria. Tunisia. These records marked the beginning of the pest’s invasion of Africa. Geographical and commercial proximity between southern Europe and North Africa facilitated its movement, particularly given the intensive tomato trade and the continuity of production areas along the Mediterranean coasts. After reaching the Maghreb, the pest moved eastward toward Libya and Egypt, and then southward into sub-Saharan Africa.

sectionTitle: Introduction of Tuta absoluta into Egypt in 2009
text: First Record in Egypt The first documented observations of Tuta absoluta in Egypt were recorded in 2009. Egyptian studies indicate that the first record occurred in Marsa Matruh Governorate, located in northwestern Egypt near the Libyan border and the Mediterranean coast. During 2010, the pest appeared in other areas and subsequently spread rapidly among tomato-producing governorates. The European and Mediterranean Plant Protection Organization database records the status of the pest in Egypt as follows: First record: 2009. Subsequent spread: rapid at the national level. Status: present and widely distributed. Did the Insect Enter Egypt from Libya? The geographical location of the first record in Marsa Matruh is consistent with the possibility that the pest moved from the west after spreading through the Maghreb and Libya. Movement may have occurred through: Transport of fruits and packaging materials along the coast and across borders. Regional trade. Means of transport. Gradual movement of moths between production areas. More than one pathway at the same time. However, this geographical sequence does not provide conclusive evidence that Libya was the only direct source of the insect’s introduction into Egypt. There is insufficient evidence to determine: The specific shipment that transported the pest. The exact point of entry. The date on which the first individual arrived. Whether introduction occurred once or on multiple occasions. Therefore, the scientifically confirmed fact is that the pest was detected in Egypt in 2009 and subsequently spread rapidly, while the direct pathway of introduction remains unresolved. Reasons for Its Rapid Spread Within Egypt Tuta absoluta encountered highly favorable conditions for establishment and reproduction in the Egyptian agricultural environment. Multiple Tomato Planting Seasons Tomato is cultivated in Egypt across several planting seasons and dates, providing suitable host plants during most months of the year. Movement of Produce Between Governorates Fruits, transplants, crates, and means of transport move between production areas, markets, packing stations, and processing plants, increasing the probability of transporting live stages. Overlapping Field Ages The same production area may simultaneously contain: Nurseries. Recently transplanted fields. Fields in the vegetative-growth stage. Fruiting fields. Fields in which harvest has ended but crop residues have not been removed. This overlap allows moths to move continuously from old fields into younger ones. Climatic Conditions Temperatures across large areas of Egypt permit the development of multiple generations, while protected cultivation and warmer regions allow continued activity during winter. Alternative Hosts Potato, eggplant, and some solanaceous weeds provide plants that may support pest survival between tomato seasons. Weak Agricultural Sanitation Leaving plants in the field after harvest allows larvae and pupae to survive and a new generation of moths to emerge. Early Dependence on Insecticides The severity of the pest led many producers to intensify spraying, but repeated use of the same insecticide groups resulted in reduced efficacy in some cases and harmed natural enemies. Impact of the Pest’s Introduction on Egyptian Tomato Production The emergence of Tuta absoluta caused major changes in tomato protection programs in Egypt. Management increasingly came to depend on a set of practices that had not previously been implemented with the same level of regularity, including: Pheromone traps. Weekly moth counting. Inspection of mines and larvae. Greater attention to pest-free nurseries. Rotation of insecticide groups. Rapid disposal of crop residues. Monitoring of fruit infestation. Implementation of biological control programs. Consideration of pesticide residues and preharvest intervals. Control costs also increased, and crop success became strongly linked to the timing of infestation detection and the effectiveness of the integrated management program.

sectionTitle: Spread from North Africa to Sub-Saharan Africa
text: Eastward and Southward Movement After the pest became established in North African countries, it moved along several pathways: From Egypt and Libya toward Sudan and East Africa. From the Maghreb toward West Africa. Through trade and transport networks connecting capitals and markets. From tomato production centers into neighboring areas. Through coastal and inland countries. The pest was recorded in Sudan in 2010 and subsequently continued its spread into the Horn of Africa and eastern parts of the continent. East Africa The pest reached tomato-producing areas in East African countries during the first half of the second decade of the twenty-first century. It was recorded in countries including: Sudan. Ethiopia. Kenya. Tanzania. Uganda. Rwanda. Burundi. In Kenya, moths were collected in pheromone traps from the beginning of 2014, and high numbers were recorded in some tomato-producing areas. Movement of tomatoes between production areas and markets, together with natural flight, facilitated rapid spread among East African countries. West Africa Tuta absoluta also spread into West Africa, where it appeared in tomato-growing regions in countries including: Senegal. Niger. Nigeria. Benin. Togo. Ghana. Burkina Faso. Other countries in the region. Its spread in West Africa was associated with cross-border movement of fruits, interconnected regional markets, weak internal quarantine systems, and successive production seasons. Central and Southern Africa The pest continued to spread into: Democratic Republic of the Congo. Angola. Zambia. Zimbabwe. Malawi. Mozambique. Botswana. Namibia. South Africa. It was recorded in Mozambique in 2016 and subsequently appeared in several provinces from north to south. It was also recorded in South Africa during 2016–2017, completing a movement extending from the northern coast of the continent to its southernmost areas. By the end of the second decade of the twenty-first century, the pest had reached all major African regions within approximately ten years of its first detection in North Africa.

Turkey as the Gateway to Asian Spread Tuta absoluta was first recorded in Turkey in August 2009 in the Urla district of İzmir Province on the Aegean coast. The larvae caused: Blotch mines in leaves. Infestation of aboveground plant parts. Superficial tunnels on fruits. The insect then spread rapidly through Turkish tomato-producing regions, and Turkey became a major point of departure for its eastward movement into Asia. The Levant During the subsequent period, the pest appeared in several eastern Mediterranean countries, including: Israel. Palestinian territories. Syria. Lebanon. Jordan. Geographical and commercial connectivity among these countries, together with movement of fruits and transplants, facilitated the pest’s movement between tomato-producing areas. In Syria, moths and damage were recorded in several coastal regions, after which the pest continued its movement toward Iraq and countries of the Arabian Peninsula. Iraq Tuta absoluta was first recorded in Iraq during autumn 2010 near the Rabia area in Nineveh Governorate, adjacent to the Syrian border. The location of the record suggests possible movement along the northwestern route from the Levant and Turkey. The pest was subsequently recorded in other areas, particularly with the movement of tomatoes between governorates and the availability of suitable climatic conditions. Iran The pest reached Iran at the beginning of the second decade of the twenty-first century and subsequently appeared in several tomato-producing areas. Iran represented an important corridor for pest movement toward: Central Asia. Pakistan. Eastern and southern Asia. Climatic diversity in Iran, together with the presence of protected and open-field production, also supported establishment in several regions. The Arabian Peninsula Tuta absoluta continued spreading through the Arabian Peninsula and the Gulf and was recorded in: Saudi Arabia. Sultanate of Oman. Qatar. United Arab Emirates. Yemen. It was recorded in Qatar in 2012 and in the United Arab Emirates in 2012 in tomato and eggplant greenhouses. In Yemen, it was detected in January 2013, after which official surveys showed that it had spread through a large number of farms and governorates within a short period. Rapid spread in arid environments demonstrates that limited rainfall does not prevent establishment when the following are available: Irrigated cultivation. Suitable temperatures. Continuous plant hosts. Protected agricultural structures. Markets that move fruits between regions.

From Turkey to Central and South Asia After the insect was recorded in Turkey in 2009, it moved eastward through parallel routes: 1. Turkey, the Levant, Iraq, and Iran. 2. Iran and Central Asia. 3. The Arabian Peninsula. 4. Pakistan and India. 5. Nepal and Bangladesh. 6. Western regions of China. Spread within Asia was rapid, particularly in countries cultivating tomato year-round or exchanging fruits and transplants across borders. India Tuta absoluta was first recorded in India in 2014 in areas of southern and western India. It subsequently appeared in: Central India. Northwestern India. Eastern regions. Northeastern India. The large tomato-growing area, multiple production seasons, and movement of fruits between states facilitated expansion of the pest. Its emergence in India represented an important milestone in the invasion pathway because India is a major center of tomato production and trade in South Asia. Nepal The insect was officially recorded in Nepal in May 2016 in a commercial tomato farm in the Kathmandu Valley. Identification was confirmed through: External morphological characters. Examination of male genitalia. Comparison with reference specimens. Initial surveys showed that the pest was not confined to a single farm but had already spread across the Kathmandu Valley and surrounding areas. Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Central Asia During approximately the same period, the pest appeared in other countries of South and Central Asia, including: Pakistan. Bangladesh. Kyrgyzstan. Tajikistan. Uzbekistan. Kazakhstan. The location of these countries along overland trade routes, together with connectivity among tomato-producing areas, facilitated its spread. It was recorded in Kyrgyzstan during the second decade of the current century, followed shortly afterward by the first documented record in Tajikistan. China Tuta absoluta reached northwestern China during the second half of the second decade of the twenty-first century. The western border regions represented the first interface for pest entry, consistent with its movement through Central Asian countries. Its arrival in China raised major concerns because of: The vast area devoted to tomato production. Extensive processing tomato cultivation. Diversity of climatic regions. High intensity of internal trade. Potential movement from western regions to eastern production areas. Climatic suitability models indicated that large areas of China and Asia may allow pest survival or seasonal development when suitable hosts and temperatures are available.

Year or Period Main Event 1917 Scientific description of the insect from specimens collected in Peru Twentieth century Spread and establishment as a major tomato pest in South America Early 1980s Widespread recording in many South American countries End of 2006 First European record in Castellón, Spain 2007 Spread along the Spanish coast and on the island of Ibiza 2008 Recorded in Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, and appeared in Italy and France 2009 Recorded in Egypt, Turkey, and many Mediterranean and European countries 2010 Appeared in Sudan, Syria, Iraq, and several areas of the Middle East 2011–2013 Expanded through East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Iran 2014 Recorded in India, Kenya, and other African and Asian areas 2015–2016 Spread in Nepal, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Mozambique, and parts of sub-Saharan Africa 2016–2017 Reached South Africa and parts of Central Asia and China After 2017 Continued local expansion and new records in areas previously pest-free or only lightly infested This table presents selected milestones and does not constitute a complete list of every country or first recording site.

International Trade in Tomato Fruits Infested fruits may carry: Eggs near the calyx. Larvae inside hidden tunnels. Pupae or larvae concealed in associated residues. Moths inside packages or means of transport. A fruit may appear externally sound despite containing a small larva, particularly during the early stage of infestation. Trade can transport fruits over distances within hours or days that greatly exceed the insect’s natural flight capacity. Movement of Transplants and Plants Infested transplants may represent a greater risk than fruits in some cases because they may carry: Eggs on leaves. Larvae inside small mines. Pupae among leaves or in the growing medium. A living plant that allows completion of the pest’s life cycle. A limited number of infested transplants can establish a new outbreak in an area previously free of the pest. Packaging Materials and Transport Crates Live stages may hide in: Crates. Plastic or wooden packaging. Remnants of leaves and calyces. Floors of transport vehicles. Packing-station residues. Sorting and temporary storage units. Risk increases when packaging materials are reused between farms and markets without cleaning. Natural Dispersal of Moths Adult moths can fly and move between fields, particularly when: Population density is high in an old field. Spraying stops before plants are removed. The crop is drying down. Harvest has ended. A recently planted field is nearby. Winds are directed toward production areas. This capacity is sufficient to expand infestation locally between villages and adjacent production areas, but by itself it does not explain the sudden appearance of the pest in distant countries or islands. Overlapping Tomato-Growing Seasons The pest requires a plant host to continue reproducing. When tomatoes are cultivated throughout the year, moths move from one season to the next without a clear interruption. The continuous presence of host plants between seasons is known as: Green Bridge This bridge may consist of: Tomato fields of overlapping ages. Nurseries. Volunteer plants. Potato. Eggplant. Wild solanaceous hosts. Crop residues that have not been removed. Reproductive Capacity and Multiple Generations Tuta absoluta produces successive generations when suitable temperature and host plants are available. The short generation time under warm conditions leads to: Rapid multiplication of population numbers. Movement from an undetectable level to a severe outbreak. Increased probability of individuals being transported with shipments. Rapid colonization of new sites. Overlap of eggs, larvae, pupae, and moths. The pest does not depend on a single annual generation but can complete multiple generations depending on climate. Absence of Obligatory Diapause Tuta absoluta does not depend on an obligatory winter diapause that prevents activity when suitable conditions are available. When tomato and appropriate temperatures are present inside greenhouses or warm regions, the life cycle can continue during long periods of the year. This characteristic supports: Survival in protected cultivation. Overwintering in temperate regions. Early infestation in the following season. Continuous generations in tropical and subtropical regions. Concealment of Live Stages The pest is difficult to detect during the early stages of invasion because: Eggs are small. Newly hatched larvae rapidly enter plant tissues. Early mines are limited in size. Larvae may be present inside fruits. Pupae may occur in soil or crop residues. Moths are small and hide within the canopy. The insect’s internal feeding habit sometimes allows it to pass through rapid inspection procedures. Similarity to Other Damage Early Tuta infestation may be confused with: Liriomyza leaf-mining flies. Tomato fruitworm. Potato tuber moth. Leaf spots. Mechanical injuries or fruit rots. Delayed diagnosis gives the pest sufficient time to produce one or more generations before containment measures begin. Climatic Suitability Conditions across extensive regions of the world match the pest’s thermal requirements, particularly in: The Mediterranean Basin. North Africa. Irrigated areas of the Middle East. Tropical Africa and temperate highlands. South and Central Asia. Protected cultivation in colder European regions. Extremely high temperatures do not always eliminate the pest because crop canopies, greenhouses, and irrigation provide local microclimates that are less severe than exposed air temperatures. Broad Host Range Tomato is the principal economic host, but the pest can survive on other plants, particularly those belonging to the family Solanaceae. Associated hosts or plants include: Potato. Eggplant. Some pepper species under certain conditions. Wild eggplant. Black nightshade. Datura. Other wild solanaceous species. The presence of these plants prevents complete interruption of the pest’s life cycle between tomato seasons. Delayed Quarantine and Monitoring Measures When the pest appeared in new regions, many producers and inspectors were insufficiently familiar with its symptoms. This led to: Delayed diagnosis. Continued movement of infested fruits. Transfer of transplants between regions. Failure to install traps early. Failure to remove crop residues. Expansion of the outbreak before official reporting. Once the pest has spread over a large area, complete eradication becomes extremely difficult. Insecticide Resistance Insecticide resistance is not a geographical pathway for pest movement, but it increases the insect’s capacity to establish after arrival. When an insect population carrying some degree of resistance enters an area that depends on the same insecticides, the first treatments may fail to eliminate it. Intensive spraying after pest detection may also: Select more resistant individuals. Kill local natural enemies. Increase control costs. Leave surviving outbreaks capable of further spread. For this reason, chemical spraying alone is insufficient to prevent expansion of the pest.

Movement of Tuta absoluta can be divided into two levels: Short- and Medium-Distance Spread This occurs mainly through moth flight and transfers infestation among: Plants. Adjacent fields. Villages. Nearby production areas. Long-Distance Spread This is mainly associated with human activity, especially: International trade. Transport of fruits. Transport of transplants. Movement of packaging materials. Crop trucks. Wholesale markets. Packing stations. The combination of both patterns explains the rapidity of the global invasion: trade introduces the pest into a distant location, and moth flight together with local movement of produce then spreads it within the newly invaded region.

sectionTitle: Why Is Eradication Difficult After Establishment?
text: A small outbreak can be eradicated if it is detected early and remains isolated, but eradication becomes difficult after the pest reaches several farms. This is due to: Presence of multiple life stages at the same time. Concealment of larvae within plants. Presence of pupae in soil and crop residues. Movement of moths between fields. Overlapping planting seasons. Daily movement of fruits. Presence of alternative hosts. Difficulty coordinating control among all growers. Ability of a small number of individuals to establish a new population. Resistance of some populations to insecticides. Once the pest becomes widely distributed, the objective shifts from eradication to: Preventing population increase. Reducing damage. Protecting fruits. Reducing infestation sources. Slowing the development of resistance. Preventing movement into new areas.

Monitoring Must Precede Visible Damage When fruit infestation becomes widespread, the pest is already established. Monitoring must therefore begin with traps and plant inspection before losses appear. The Nursery Is Part of the Protection System An infested transplant can move the pest into a new field even when that field is far from the nearest known outbreak. Crop-Residue Management Is a Regional Measure Leaving an infested field after harvest does not affect only its owner; it turns the field into a source of moths that attack surrounding crops. Administrative Borders Do Not Stop the Pest Fruits and moths move between districts, governorates, and countries. Management therefore requires coordination among producers and regulatory authorities. Correct Naming Is Essential Confusing Tuta with leaf miner delays diagnosis and leads to the use of unsuitable monitoring and control methods. Spraying Alone Does Not Prevent Spread The history of the invasion demonstrates that reliance on insecticides did not stop pest expansion and, in many regions, led to resistance development and damage to natural enemies.

Tuta absoluta originated in South America and was scientifically described from Peru in 1917. It subsequently became a major tomato pest in many countries across the continent. The global invasion phase began with its detection in Spain at the end of 2006. It then spread rapidly across southern Europe and the Mediterranean Basin. It reached North Africa in 2008, when it was recorded in Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, and subsequently appeared in Egypt in 2009. Egyptian studies indicate that the first record occurred in Marsa Matruh before the pest spread during subsequent years across most Egyptian tomato-producing regions. In the same year, the pest was recorded in Turkey, from which it extended into the Levant, Iraq, Iran, and the Arabian Peninsula, and then into South, Central, and East Asia. Within approximately ten years of entering Africa, it had reached all major regions of the continent, while continuing to expand through India, Nepal, Pakistan, Central Asia, and China. This spread did not result from a single factor but from the interaction of: International and regional trade. Transport of fruits, transplants, and packaging materials. Natural flight of moths. Overlapping tomato-growing seasons. Presence of alternative hosts. Concealment of larvae within plant tissues. Multiple generations. Climatic suitability. Delayed detection and diagnosis. Difficulty of eradication after multiple outbreaks become established. This invasion history demonstrates that management of Tuta absoluta does not begin with spraying an infested field. It begins with preventing its movement, inspecting transplants and fruits, installing traps early, removing crop residues, and coordinating management among fields and production regions. References

Morphological description refers to the study of the external and structural characteristics that distinguish a living organism, such as shape, size, color, body segmentation, appendages, wings, setae, and diagnostic markings. Morphological description provides an essential basis for identifying Tuta absoluta and distinguishing it from other pests that attack tomato crops, particularly species that may cause similar symptoms, such as leaf-mining flies, potato tuber moth, tomato pinworm, and tomato fruitworm. Tuta absoluta undergoes complete metamorphosis involving four clearly distinct stages that differ in both form and function: 1. Egg. 2. Larva. 3. Pupa. 4. Adult insect or moth. The transition between these stages is not merely a gradual increase in size. Each stage differs from the others in body structure, behavior, location on the plant, and role within the life cycle. The egg represents the beginning of a new individual, the larva is the feeding and damaging stage, the pupa is the stage of body reorganization and transformation, while the adult moth represents the reproductive, dispersal, and egg-laying stage.

sectionTitle: Importance of Morphological Description in Pest Diagnosis
text: Finding a mine inside a tomato leaf is not sufficient to confirm the presence of Tuta absoluta, because several insects are capable of forming mines within leaves. Likewise, the presence of a larva inside a tomato fruit is not sufficient to conclude that it is a Tuta larva, because fruits may be infested by other larval species. Correct diagnosis depends on combining several lines of evidence, including: Egg shape and oviposition site. Larval form and color. Presence of a distinct head and legs. Markings located behind the head. Number of larval instars. Shape of the mine and the frass present within it. Location of the pupa and nature of the surrounding covering. Appearance of the adult moth. Wing color and markings. Shape of the antennae. Insect size. Results of pheromone traps. Microscopic examination when required. The level of diagnostic confidence increases as several compatible characteristics are observed together.

Tuta absoluta belongs to the insects that undergo complete metamorphosis, a type of development known as: Complete Metamorphosis or Holometabolous Development In this type of development, the larva differs clearly from the adult insect. The larva: Has a worm-like body. Possesses chewing mouthparts. Feeds on plant tissues. Moves within mines and between plant parts. Represents the stage responsible for agricultural damage. The adult insect: Is a small winged moth. Does not produce mines or holes within plant tissues. Mates and lays eggs. Transfers infestation between plants and fields. The pupal stage separates these two stages. During pupation, profound internal and external changes occur, leading to the formation of the adult moth.

sectionTitle: Morphological Changes During the Life Cycle
text: The insect begins as a very small egg, usually laid singly on plant parts. After embryonic development is completed, a minute larva emerges and begins searching for a suitable location to penetrate plant tissue. The larva passes through four larval instars: First larval instar, L1. Second larval instar, L2. Third larval instar, L3. Fourth larval instar, L4. Each instar is separated from the next by a molt, during which the larva sheds its old external covering so that the new covering can accommodate an increase in body size. The larva does not change only in size; changes also occur in: Head-capsule width. Body length. Body color. Visibility of body segmentation. Quantity of tissue consumed. Size of mines. Ability of the larva to move between plant parts. Visibility of diagnostic markings. After completing the fourth larval instar, the larva stops feeding, enters the prepupal stage, and then transforms into a pupa. After metamorphosis is completed, a small moth emerges from the pupa. The moth differs completely from the larva in body form, wings, mouthparts, and biological function.

Morphological description must be distinguished from biological description. Morphological Description It addresses: Shape. Color. Measurements. Body parts. External markings. Wings. Legs. Antennae. Diagnostic characteristics. Biological Description It addresses: Duration of each stage. Growth rate. Behavior. Fecundity. Feeding sites. Response to temperature and humidity. Timing of activity. Number of generations. The two fields may be related, but egg length or larval color is a morphological characteristic, whereas egg-incubation period or larval growth rate is a biological characteristic.

The color of the different stages of Tuta absoluta is not completely constant, but changes according to several factors, most importantly: Age of the stage. Degree of development. Proximity to molting. Proximity to hatching or moth emergence. Type of food. Environmental conditions. Method of specimen preservation. Lighting used during examination. A newly laid egg differs in color from an egg approaching hatching, a newly hatched larva differs from a fully grown larva, and a newly formed pupa differs from one approaching adult emergence. For this reason, color alone should not be used for diagnosis. It should be interpreted together with size, shape, location of the specimen, and other anatomical characteristics. Published measurements also vary to a limited extent among studies as a result of differences in: Geographical strains. Temperature. Host plant. Measurement methods. Whether the specimen is alive or preserved. Exact age within the developmental stage. When measurements are presented, an approximate range should therefore be used to represent natural variation rather than treating a single number as a fixed limit for all individuals.

Many characteristics of Tuta absoluta can be provisionally identified in the field using: Hand lens. White sheet of paper. Fine brush. Fine forceps. Collection bags or containers. Pheromone traps. Mobile-phone camera equipped with a magnifying lens. However, some fine characteristics cannot be confirmed with the naked eye, particularly: Details of the egg surface. Head-capsule measurements. Arrangement of larval setae. Wing venation. Genitalic characteristics. Differences among closely related species. These characteristics require a stereomicroscope, and final confirmation in difficult cases may require molecular analysis.

Different life stages require appropriate collection methods. Eggs A small section of the leaf surrounding the egg should be cut rather than attempting to remove the egg directly, because slight pressure may crush it. Larvae The mine should be opened gradually using a needle or fine forceps while avoiding pressure on the larval body. Pupae Pupae should be collected together with part of the leaf, cocoon, or surrounding soil, because forceful removal of the covering may damage the pupa. Adults Adults should be collected from traps or plants while preserving the wings and scales, because excessive friction causes scales to detach and alters the external appearance. Specimen information should be recorded, including: Collection date. Location. Host plant. Infested plant part. Life stage present. Type of damage. Type of trap when the moth is collected from a trap.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Field Diagnosis and Taxonomic Confirmation
text: An agricultural engineer can diagnose infestation in the field with a good degree of confidence when several characteristic signs occur together, such as: Irregular blotch mines. Presence of frass inside the mine. A larva with a distinct head and legs. Presence of the dark band behind the head in advanced larvae. Infestation of growing points and fruits. Capture of Tuta moths in pheromone traps. However, taxonomic confirmation becomes necessary when: The pest appears in an area where it has not previously been recorded. Similar species are present. Results will be used in scientific publication. A plant-quarantine study is being conducted. The specimen is damaged or incomplete. Trap results and field symptoms are inconsistent. In such cases, diagnosis depends on microscopic or molecular characteristics rather than on the appearance of damage alone.

sectionTitle: Relationship Between Morphology and the Location of Each Stage
text: The location where a life stage is found provides supporting evidence, but is not sufficient by itself. Eggs may be found on leaves or young plant parts, whereas larvae occur inside leaves, stems, or fruits. Pupae may form inside a folded leaf, within a silken cocoon, or near the soil surface. Adult moths commonly hide among plants, and males can be monitored using pheromone traps. However, these locations may vary according to conditions. Diagnosis should therefore combine: Location of the stage. Its shape. Its size. Its color. Diagnostic markings. Nature of the surrounding damage.

sectionTitle: Diagnostic Sequence of the Life Stages
text: Identification of the insect proceeds from the smallest stage to the largest stage: Egg Its shape, color, size, oviposition site, and changes occurring before hatching are examined. Larva The larval instar, color, head capsule, legs, prothoracic shield, body form, and distinguishing characteristics from other leaf-mining insects are examined. Pupa Its shape, color, length, location, presence of a silken cocoon, and changes preceding moth emergence are examined. Adult Insect Body length, wingspan, body and wing color, dark markings, fringes, antennae, and sexual differences are examined. These stages are presented in detail in the subsequent parts of this chapter.

The egg represents the first stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta, from which the formation of a new generation begins within the field. Although the egg does not feed on the plant and causes no direct damage, it represents a critical stage in the development of infestation because every viable, unparasitized egg can develop into a mining larva that penetrates plant tissues within a short period after hatching. The egg stage is particularly important in integrated pest management because it is the final stage before the larva enters leaves, growing points, stems, or fruits. Once the larva penetrates plant tissue, it becomes more protected from contact insecticides and from many environmental factors and natural enemies. The Tuta egg is characterized by the following: Extremely small size. Usually laid singly. Attached directly to the plant surface. Its color is close to that of plant tissues. Located mainly on young leaves. Its incubation period is affected by temperature. It may be exposed to predation, parasitism, desiccation, and non-viability. Its rate of development increases as temperature rises within the suitable range. It hatches within a few days under warm conditions. Therefore, egg monitoring is not merely a description of the insect’s life cycle; it is also an early-warning tool for predicting the emergence of newly hatched larvae.

The egg of Tuta absoluta is small, oval or elliptical in shape, and may appear short-cylindrical when viewed from the side. It is characterized by the following: Its length is greater than its width. Both ends are rounded rather than pointed. Its surface is slightly convex. It adheres directly to plant tissue. It is not borne on a long stalk. It is not usually laid in large, organized masses. Its longitudinal axis is approximately parallel to the surface of the leaf or stem. Describing the egg as oval does not conflict with describing it as short-cylindrical, because its apparent shape varies according to the viewing angle. It appears oval when viewed from above, while the body appears elongated and slightly raised when viewed from the side.

The usual measurements of a Tuta absoluta egg are approximately: Length: 0.35–0.38 mm. Width: 0.20–0.22 mm. The average measurement reported in several studies is approximately: 0.38 mm long × 0.21 mm wide. These measurements mean that the egg is far too small to be examined efficiently with the naked eye. Detection requires: A hand lens. Good side lighting. Slow and systematic inspection. Concentration on young leaves and plant growing points. A stereomicroscope when confirmation of viability or parasitism is required. Measurements may vary slightly as a result of differences in: Geographical strain. Female age. Nutritional condition of the female. Host plant. Thermal conditions. Measurement method. Position of the egg during examination. Whether the specimen is alive or preserved.

sectionTitle: Egg Color and Its Change During Development
text: Egg color does not remain constant from oviposition until hatching; rather, it changes gradually as the embryo develops. Newly Laid Egg A newly laid egg is usually: Creamy white. Pale pearly. Pale yellow. Slightly translucent. This coloration makes the egg difficult to detect on tomato leaves, especially in the presence of plant trichomes, dust, and residues of spray solutions. Mid-Incubation Period As embryonic development continues, the color changes to: A more distinct yellow. Dark cream. Sometimes yellow with a light orange tinge. The embryo gradually becomes visible through the semi-transparent eggshell. Egg Approaching Hatching Before hatching, the egg becomes darker, and a dark area becomes visible inside it, representing the larval head and head capsule. This stage is known as: Blackhead Stage At this stage, hatching is imminent, especially at high temperatures. The appearance of a small dark area inside the egg does not mean that the egg has been parasitized. In a healthy egg approaching hatching, the larval head appears on one side, whereas parasitized eggs tend to darken more uniformly.

The egg is surrounded by an outer shell known as the: Chorion This shell performs several functions: Protecting the embryo from mechanical damage. Maintaining the external shape. Reducing water loss. Permitting gas exchange. Protecting the internal contents. Partially isolating the embryo from some external influences. The presence of the chorion does not mean that the egg is completely protected, because it can still be affected by heat, desiccation, insecticides with ovicidal activity, parasitoids, and predators. Penetration of control products through the shell varies according to: Active ingredient. Egg age. Shell thickness. Presence of a surfactant or oil. Quality of spray coverage. Temperature and humidity. Position of the egg on the plant. Presence of trichomes or wax layers on the leaf.

The female Tuta absoluta usually lays eggs singly, although two or several eggs may occasionally be found close together in a small, irregular group. The female does not usually form large egg masses as occurs in some other insects. Laying eggs singly provides the insect with several advantages: Distributing offspring over several parts of the plant. Reducing competition among newly hatched larvae. Reducing the probability of losing all eggs at once. Making eggs more difficult to detect and remove. Increasing the probability that larvae will reach suitable mining sites. Reducing exposure of all eggs to a single predator or parasitoid. The female may lay eggs on several plants within the field, helping to spread the infestation rather than concentrating it on a single plant.

sectionTitle: Distribution of Eggs on Tomato Plant Parts
text: The female does not distribute eggs equally among all plant parts, but prefers young leaves and tissues near the plant apex. The commonly reported approximate distribution of eggs is as follows: Plant Part Approximate Percentage of Eggs Leaves, especially young leaves 73% Leaf veins, petioles, and stems 21% Sepals and calyx 5% Green fruits 1% These percentages represent a common pattern reported in the literature and are not fixed values for every field, cultivar, or crop-growth stage. Egg distribution is affected by: Plant growth stage. Presence of flowers and fruits. Pest population density. Canopy density. Leaf age. Cultivar. Density of plant trichomes. Temperature. Wind direction. Location at which moths enter the field. Presence of neighboring infested fields. Distribution on Leaves Young leaves represent the principal oviposition sites, especially recently fully expanded leaves located in the upper part of the plant. Females prefer these leaves because they: Are close to the growing point. Provide tender tissues for newly hatched larvae. Permit easier penetration of leaf tissue. Provide relative protection among trichomes. Are close to growing points, flowers, and young fruits. Before flowering, oviposition occurs more frequently on the lower leaf surface, whereas females may use both upper and lower surfaces to a greater extent after the plant develops and enters flowering and fruiting. Vertical Distribution on the Plant Most eggs are concentrated in the upper or younger layer of the canopy at the time of oviposition. However, the position of the leaf changes as the plant grows. An egg may be laid near the apex, but after several days the leaf may become positioned lower because the stem continues elongating and new leaves are produced above it. Inspection should therefore not be limited to the uppermost two leaves, but should include: The growing point. Several leaves below the apex. Both upper and lower surfaces of leaflets. Leaf petioles. Tender stems. Young flower and fruit clusters. Distribution Within the Field Eggs are not distributed completely at random within the field, but tend to have an aggregated distribution. At the beginning of invasion, their density increases in: Field edges. The side facing an old infested field. Areas near roads. Areas adjacent to tomato residues. Areas near infested nurseries or greenhouses. The direction from which moth-carrying winds enter. The first plants in planting rows. Open-field studies have shown that egg aggregations are often greater at field edges and that spread along planting rows may occur faster than movement across rows.

The egg incubation period is the time from oviposition on the plant until larval emergence. The egg does not have a fixed incubation period because the rate of embryonic development depends primarily on temperature. Under moderate and warm conditions, eggs usually hatch within: 4–6 days. However, this period may be shortened to approximately two or three days under suitably high temperatures, or extended to more than ten days under low temperatures.

sectionTitle: Effect of Temperature on Incubation Period
text: Embryonic growth becomes faster as temperature increases within the suitable range. Therefore, temperature and incubation period are inversely related: > The higher the suitable temperature, the shorter the incubation period; the lower the temperature, the longer the period. One controlled-temperature study reported the following results: Constant Temperature Mean Egg Incubation Period 10°C 24.4 days 15°C 11.9 days 20°C 6.9 days 25°C 4.5 days 30°C 2.5 days 35°C No hatching occurred in this study The data show that increasing temperature from 15 to 25°C reduced the incubation period from approximately 12 days to approximately 4.5 days. Another study conducted at a relative humidity of 60±10% reported the following results: Temperature Incubation Period 15°C 12.08 days 20°C 8.42 days 25°C 4.42 days 30°C 4.08 days 35°C 1.83 days Differences between the two studies demonstrate that the response of eggs to extreme heat is not identical among all populations and experimental conditions. Results differ because of: Genetic variation among populations. Adaptation of insects to local climate. Differences in relative humidity. Differences in host plant or cultivar. Differences in egg age at the time of temperature exposure. Differences in rearing method. Constant laboratory temperature versus fluctuating field temperature. Differences in the definition of egg viability, survival, or hatching percentage.

In an experiment conducted at a relative humidity of 60±10%, the following hatching percentages were recorded: Temperature Egg-Hatching Percentage 15°C 73.33% 20°C 70.00% 25°C 93.33% 30°C 100% 35°C 70.00% However, egg-hatching percentage alone is not sufficient to determine the optimum temperature for the pest. Although high hatching was recorded at 30°C in this experiment, survival from egg to adult declined to approximately 33.33%, compared with approximately 76.67% at 25°C. At 35°C, 70% of eggs hatched, but the percentage of individuals completing development from egg to adult did not exceed 10%. Temperature Egg Hatching Survival from Egg to Adult 15°C 73.33% 16.67% 20°C 70.00% 46.67% 25°C 93.33% 76.67% 30°C 100% 33.33% 35°C 70.00% 10.00% This demonstrates that rapid hatching or a high hatching percentage in a particular experiment does not mean that the same temperature is optimal for completion of the entire generation. Differences Among Studies at High Temperatures Another thermal study recorded egg survival or hatching percentages as follows: Temperature Egg Survival or Hatching Percentage 10°C 4.0% 15°C 80.6% 20°C 67.2% 25°C 81.0% 30°C 1.3% No eggs hatched at 35°C in this study. This differs clearly from the study that recorded 100% hatching at 30°C and 70% at 35°C. This scientific difference is important because it shows that temperatures close to the upper threshold produce highly variable results, and a single percentage cannot be considered a fixed rule for all pest populations. The most consistent conclusion across studies is that: Severe cold slows development and reduces hatching. A temperature of 25°C repeatedly provides a short incubation period and high hatching. Temperatures of 30–35°C accelerate development, but survival may become unstable. Prolonged high temperature adversely affects the subsequent stages even when some eggs hatch successfully.

sectionTitle: Most Suitable Temperature for Egg Incubation and Hatching
text: The most suitable and stable thermal range for egg development is: 20–25°C. A temperature of approximately: 25°C Is considered one of the best practical temperatures for egg incubation because it combines: A short incubation period of approximately 4–5 days. A high hatching percentage. High survival of larvae and pupae after hatching. A high percentage of generation completion. Lower thermal disruption than at 30–35°C. A temperature of 30°C may result in faster hatching within approximately 2.5–4 days, but it does not always provide the highest survival, and results differ greatly among populations and studies. The range of 20–25°C is also among the most suitable ranges for the overall development, survival, and reproduction of the pest, while the optimum estimated by some models lies between 21 and 23°C.

One study estimated the lower developmental threshold for eggs at approximately: 9.6°C The thermal constant required to complete the egg stage was estimated at approximately: 58.5 accumulated heat units — Degree-Days The upper thermal threshold for egg development was estimated at approximately: 35.1°C. These values mean that embryonic development is extremely slow near 10°C and stops or becomes abnormal at low or high temperatures exceeding physiological limits. In another experiment: Eggs did not hatch at 7°C. Only approximately 17% of eggs hatched at 10°C. Individuals hatching at 10°C were unable to complete development to the adult stage. Approximate Calculation of Hatching Time Using Degree-Days Daily heat accumulation can be calculated in a simplified form using the following relationship: Daily degree-days = Mean daily temperature − 9.6 A negative value is not counted when the mean is below the lower threshold. Hatching is expected to approach when approximately: 58.5 degree-days Have accumulated. Example at a mean daily temperature of 25°C: Daily units = 25 − 9.6 Daily units = 15.4 degree-days. Theoretical duration = 58.5 ÷ 15.4 Theoretical duration ≈ 3.8 days. This estimate is close to the laboratory incubation period recorded at 25°C, which ranged around 4.4–4.5 days. This calculation remains approximate because leaf-surface temperature may differ from air temperature, and temperatures fluctuate between day and night.

sectionTitle: Effect of Relative Humidity on Eggs
text: Relative humidity affects water balance within the egg and the embryo’s ability to complete development. A severe decrease in humidity, particularly when combined with high temperature, increases: Water loss. Desiccation of the shell. Embryonic mortality. Hatching failure. Weakness of emerging larvae. In a study comparing two thermal and humidity regimes, egg survival reached: 95.8% at 20.5±2°C and 55±5% relative humidity. 83.8% at 32±2°C and 40±5% relative humidity. This comparison represents the combined effect of temperature and humidity and does not separate the effect of humidity alone because temperature increased at the same time that humidity declined. Moderate humidity, which in many rearing experiments lies around 55–70%, provides favorable conditions for egg development, provided that temperature remains within the suitable range. Very high humidity accompanied by continuous wetness or water condensation may lead to: Increased fungal growth on the leaf surface. Damage to some eggs. Reduced gas exchange when the egg is covered with water. Changes in the efficacy of some control products.

Eggs may tolerate short periods of heat better than continuous exposure in some cases, but severe heat stress reduces hatching. Heat-stress experiments showed that exposing eggs to: 44°C for six hours Significantly reduced hatching percentage. The degree of damage varies according to: Exposure duration. Egg age. Relative humidity. Leaf-surface temperature. Whether the egg is exposed to direct sunlight or shade. Nighttime thermal fluctuations. Adaptation of the insect population. Leaf or fruit surface temperature may exceed measured air temperature, especially under direct solar radiation, thereby increasing egg exposure to desiccation and heat stress.

Sensitivity to Cold Cold conditions lead to: Slower embryonic growth. A longer incubation period. Reduced hatching percentage. Increased probability of embryonic mortality. Delayed larval emergence. At 10°C, the incubation period may extend beyond 20 days, whereas hatching may stop at temperatures close to 7°C in some experiments. Delayed hatching under cold conditions does not necessarily result in the death of all eggs. Some may resume development when temperature rises, depending on the duration and intensity of exposure. Sensitivity to High Temperature High temperatures within the acceptable range shorten the incubation period, but prolonged exposure near the upper threshold causes: Disruption of embryonic development. Reduced egg viability. Emergence of weak larvae. Increased larval mortality after hatching. Reduced survival of the generation. Variable responses among populations. Conflicting results at 30–35°C demonstrate that the capacity of eggs to hatch does not guarantee normal completion of subsequent development. Sensitivity to Desiccation Eggs exposed on the plant surface are subject to water loss, particularly when the following occur together: High temperature. Low humidity. Dry winds. Direct sunlight. Low canopy humidity. The outer shell reduces water loss but does not prevent it completely. Newly laid eggs or eggs damaged mechanically are more susceptible to desiccation than intact eggs with a complete shell. Sensitivity to Rainfall and Overhead Irrigation Heavy rain or overhead-irrigation droplets may mechanically affect some exposed eggs through: Dislodging them from the plant surface. Temporarily submerging them in water. Damaging the shell. Increasing fungal activity. Altering the temperature and humidity surrounding them. However, adhesion of the egg to plant tissue makes water washing an unreliable control method, and rainfall or overhead irrigation does not remove all eggs. Sensitivity to Mechanical Damage The shell is thin, and eggs may therefore be damaged by: Finger pressure. Use of coarse forceps. Leaf friction during handling. Crushing during sampling. Leaf desiccation and shrinkage. Poor specimen preservation. For this reason, the egg should be collected together with a small section of the leaf rather than being removed directly. Sensitivity to Insecticides Eggs occur on the plant surface and can therefore be directly exposed to spray solution, but they do not respond equally to all insecticides. Sensitivity depends on: Presence of ovicidal activity in the active ingredient. Ability of the material to penetrate the chorion. Egg age. Applied concentration. Quality of spray coverage. Presence of a surfactant. Repetition of treatment. Temperature. Resistance within the insect population. Many insecticides used against Tuta are more effective against larvae than eggs. Therefore, spraying an insecticide that is effective against larvae does not necessarily eliminate all eggs present on the plant. One Egyptian experiment recorded an ovicidal effect of a product based on azadirachtin or neem oil, reaching approximately: 43.8% egg mortality Under the experimental conditions. This result demonstrates partial ovicidal activity but does not indicate complete egg control. Sensitivity to Predators Eggs are suitable prey for several predators present in tomato production systems, particularly certain predatory bugs, including: Nesidiocoris tenuis. Macrolophus pygmaeus. Other mirid predators. Some generalist predators. Predators pierce the eggshell with their mouthparts and suck out its contents. Predated eggs appear as: An empty shell. Marked collapse. A small puncture. Partial rupture. Absence of egg contents. Absence of a normal hatching opening. Sensitivity to Egg Parasitoids Eggs are direct targets of parasitoids belonging to the genus: Trichogramma Species used or studied against Tuta absoluta include: Trichogramma achaeae. Trichogramma pretiosum. Trichogramma evanescens. The female parasitoid lays its egg inside the Tuta egg, after which the parasitoid larva feeds on the embryo and egg contents, preventing the emergence of a Tuta larva. Parasitized eggs gradually become dark or black. Success of parasitism varies according to: Age of the Tuta egg. Parasitoid species and strain. Temperature. Humidity. Egg density. Presence of pesticide residues. Timing of release. Distribution of parasitoid cards or capsules. Density of plant trichomes. Newly laid eggs are usually more suitable for parasitism than eggs approaching hatching. Natural Non-Viability Not all eggs laid by females hatch. Some may die naturally as a result of: Lack of fertilization. Failure of embryonic development. Genetic factors. Female age. Poor nutrition. Thermal conditions. Desiccation. Shell damage. Physiological disorders. A field study of natural mortality factors showed that mortality during the egg stage reached approximately: 38.76% The principal causes were: Predation. Parasitism. Egg non-viability. This result demonstrates that the egg stage is important for natural population regulation before larval damage occurs.

sectionTitle: Importance of the Egg Stage in Integrated Pest Management
text: The egg stage represents one of the most important targets in the integrated management of Tuta absoluta for the following reasons: The Egg Precedes Damage The egg does not produce mines or holes. Therefore, killing or parasitizing it prevents damage before it begins. Every egg prevented from hatching means: One fewer larva inside leaves. A lower probability of fruit infestation. Less pressure on the insecticide program. A reduced population in the next generation. The Egg Is Located Outside Plant Tissue The egg is attached to the surface of the leaf, stem, or sepals, whereas the larva becomes concealed inside tissues after hatching. Eggs are therefore more exposed to: Egg parasitoids. Predators. Insecticides with ovicidal activity. Oils and certain contact products. Heat and desiccation. Direct inspection. The Egg Determines the Timing of Larval Emergence Examination of egg color and age allows estimation of hatching time. New creamy eggs require more time, whereas the appearance of the black head means that larval emergence is imminent. This information becomes important when timing: Release of egg parasitoids. Use of products with ovicidal activity. Use of biological insecticides targeting newly hatched larvae. Intensification of field inspection. Prediction of the appearance of new mines. The Control Window Becomes Shorter Under Warm Conditions At 20°C, the egg stage may persist for approximately 7–8 days, whereas eggs may hatch within 2.5–4 days at 30°C. Therefore, the period available for intervention becomes shorter as temperature rises. Under warm conditions, weekly inspection alone is not sufficient when trap catches are high and eggs are present, because a large proportion of eggs may hatch and larvae may enter leaves between two widely spaced inspections. Supporting Biological Control The egg stage is the principal target of Trichogramma parasitoids and egg predators. The effectiveness of a Trichogramma program depends on the continued presence of the parasitoid in the field at the same time as newly laid Tuta eggs are present. Releases made after most eggs have hatched do not control larvae that have already entered plant tissues.

sectionTitle: Relationship Between the Egg and Larval Emergence
text: After embryonic development is completed, the larva chews through part of the eggshell and emerges onto the plant surface. The newly hatched larva remains exposed for a short period before entering the tissue, and this period may be less than: 90 minutes under some conditions. During this period, the larva is more exposed to: Contact insecticides. Biological insecticides. Predation. Desiccation. Heat. Falling from the plant. Once it enters the leaf, stem, or fruit, the effectiveness of many external control methods declines. Successful control is therefore linked to two successive stages: 1. Targeting eggs before hatching. 2. Targeting the larva immediately after hatching and before it enters plant tissues.

Egg Condition Main Appearance Newly laid Creamy white or pale yellow Mid-development Yellow or dark cream Approaching hatching A dark head visible at one end Hatched Empty transparent or white shell with an emergence opening Non-viable Dry, malformed, or brown without normal development Parasitized Dark or black in a relatively uniform manner Predated Collapsed, empty, or bearing a fine puncture Insecticide-damaged Does not hatch and may change color or its contents may dry Empty eggshells are not counted as live eggs when estimating infestation density.

Accurate field inspection includes: 1. Dividing the field into sectors. 2. Inspecting both field edges and the interior. 3. Focusing on the side adjacent to old fields. 4. Selecting plants distributed among several rows. 5. Inspecting growing points and young leaves. 6. Turning leaflets over to inspect the lower surface. 7. Inspecting petioles and tender stems. 8. Inspecting sepals and calyces when fruits are present. 9. Using a hand lens. 10. Classifying eggs as live, approaching hatching, parasitized, or empty. 11. Recording the number of plants carrying eggs. 12. Linking the results to moth numbers in traps. 13. Repeating inspection at shorter intervals when temperature rises. The number of plants carrying eggs may sometimes describe the spread of infestation more clearly than the total number of eggs on a single heavily infested plant.

The egg should be collected together with a small piece of leaf by following these steps: 1. Locate the egg using a hand lens. 2. Cut a section of the leaflet around it. 3. Place the specimen in a well-ventilated container. 4. Prevent water condensation inside the container. 5. Record the location, date, and cultivar. 6. Record the egg’s position on the plant. 7. Examine it daily. 8. Record emergence of a Tuta larva or egg parasitoid. 9. Do not remove the egg directly with forceps. 10. Do not expose it to sunlight or heat inside a vehicle. Emergence of the parasitoid or larva is more accurate than color alone for determining parasitism and viability.

sectionTitle: Comprehensive Reference Table for the Egg Stage
