text: Factor Value or Description Shape Oval, elliptical, or short-cylindrical Length 0.35–0.38 mm Width 0.20–0.22 mm Color when newly laid Creamy white to pale yellow Color before hatching Darker, with appearance of the black head Oviposition pattern Usually singly or in small groups Principal location Young leaves Percentage of eggs on leaves Approximately 73% Veins, petioles, and stems Approximately 21% Sepals and calyx Approximately 5% Green fruits Approximately 1% Usual incubation period 4–6 days under suitable conditions Incubation period at 15°C Approximately 12 days Incubation period at 20°C Approximately 7–8 days Incubation period at 25°C Approximately 4.4–4.5 days Incubation period at 30°C Approximately 2.5–4 days Most stable thermal range 20–25°C Practical optimum temperature Approximately 25°C Estimated lower developmental threshold 9.6°C Estimated upper developmental threshold 35.1°C Thermal constant Approximately 58.5 degree-days Best stable hatching percentage Repeatedly observed near 25°C Principal mortality factors Predation, parasitism, and non-viability Importance in control Preventing larval emergence before tissue penetration

The egg of Tuta absoluta is extremely small and oval or elliptical in shape, measuring approximately 0.35–0.38 mm in length and 0.20–0.22 mm in width. When laid, it is creamy white or pale yellow, then becomes progressively darker as the embryo develops until the black head appears before hatching. The female lays approximately 73% of eggs on leaves, 21% on veins, petioles, and stems, 5% on sepals, and 1% on green fruits. Eggs are commonly concentrated on young leaves and in the upper part of the plant, with greater aggregations at field edges during the early stages of infestation. The incubation period usually ranges from 4 to 6 days under suitable conditions, but it is strongly affected by temperature. It may extend to approximately 12 days at 15°C, decline to approximately 4.5 days at 25°C, and to 2.5–4 days at 30°C. The most suitable and stable thermal range lies between 20 and 25°C, and 25°C is considered one of the best temperatures for egg development because it combines rapid hatching, high viability, and the ability of subsequent stages to complete development. Results at 30–35°C vary among studies. Rapid hatching may occur, but overall survival of the generation declines, especially when high temperature persists or is accompanied by low humidity. Eggs are also affected by desiccation, extreme heat, cold, insecticides with ovicidal activity, predators, Trichogramma parasitoids, and natural non-viability. The egg stage is a principal target in integrated pest management because it occurs outside plant tissues and precedes the onset of damage. Killing or parasitizing the egg prevents larval emergence and penetration into leaves or fruits, thereby reducing damage, larval density, and the next pest generation.

The larva represents the damaging stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It is the only stage that feeds directly on tomato plant tissues and forms mines within leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits. The egg, pupa, and adult moth do not cause direct mining damage. The egg represents the stage of embryonic development, the pupa is the transformation stage, while the adult moth is responsible for mating, dispersal, and egg laying. The larva passes through four successive instars: 1. First larval instar, L1. 2. Second larval instar, L2. 3. Third larval instar, L3. 4. Fourth larval instar, L4. These instars are separated by three molts. At the end of the fourth instar, the larva enters the prepupal stage, stops feeding, and searches for a suitable site in which to pupate. The four instars differ in: Body length and width. Head-capsule width. Color. Quantity of food consumed. Size of mines. Ability to move between plant parts. Depth of position within plant tissues. Level of protection from insecticides and natural enemies. Amount of damage caused. Newly hatched larvae consume less tissue, but they establish the infestation by entering the plant. Advanced larvae consume greater quantities, form wider mines, and are more capable of attacking growing points, stems, and fruits.

The larva of Tuta absoluta is a lepidopteran larva with an elongated cylindrical body divided into relatively distinct segments. The body consists of: Head. Thorax composed of three segments. Abdomen composed of approximately ten visible segments. Three pairs of true thoracic legs. Fleshy abdominal prolegs that assist movement. Fine setae distributed over the body. Small cuticular plates or areas bearing the setae. The head is characterized by being: Distinct and separated from the body. Relatively sclerotized. Dark in color. Equipped with chewing mouthparts. Increasing in size as the larva passes from one instar to the next. The hardened layer covering the head is known as the: Head Capsule Head-capsule width is one of the most accurate indicators used to distinguish larval instars because body length changes temporarily as the larva contracts or extends, whereas head measurement is more stable within a given instar.

Head The head bears: Short antennae. Simple eyes. Strong chewing mandibles relative to larval size. Mouthparts adapted for biting plant tissues. Sensory setae that assist in selecting feeding sites. The larva uses its mandibles to: Open the eggshell at hatching. Penetrate the leaf epidermis. Remove mesophyll cells. Enlarge mines. Penetrate growing points, stems, and fruits. Open an exit route from the mine or move to another site. The head is distinctly dark in the first instar, then ranges from brown to dark brown or partially black in advanced instars. Thorax The thorax consists of three segments, each bearing one pair of true legs, giving a total of: Three pairs — six true legs. On the first thoracic segment, immediately behind the head, there is a sclerotized plate known as the: Prothoracic Shield The color pattern of this shield is an important diagnostic feature in advanced larvae. In Tuta absoluta, the shield is relatively pale, with a dark shading or band along its posterior margin. This band becomes more distinct in advanced instars, whereas it may be absent or weak in very young larvae. Abdomen The abdomen consists of successive segments, some of which bear fleshy abdominal prolegs. The abdominal prolegs do not resemble the jointed thoracic legs, but they help the larva to: Anchor the body. Move within mines. Crawl over the leaf surface. Grip plant tissue. Move along silken threads. The body contains lateral respiratory openings known as: Spiracles These permit air to enter the tracheal system. Setae and Seta-Bearing Areas Fine sensory setae occur over the larval body and arise from small cuticular areas known as: Pinacula These setae help the larva detect: Contact with tissue. Vibration. Obstacles inside the mine. Predators or parasitoids. Changes in the surrounding environment. These characteristics are not easy to observe during field inspection, but they are used in microscopic diagnosis and in comparisons between Tuta absoluta and closely related species.

Tuta absoluta usually passes through four distinct larval instars. The larva moves from one instar to the next through molting, during which: 1. Feeding stops or temporarily decreases. 2. The old epidermis separates from the new epidermis. 3. The old cuticular layer splits. 4. The larva emerges with a new, soft covering. 5. The body expands before the new covering hardens. 6. The larva resumes feeding. Three molts occur during the larval stage: First molt between L1 and L2. Second molt between L2 and L3. Third molt between L3 and L4. After completing L4, the larva does not enter a fifth instar; instead, it transforms into the prepupal stage and then the pupa. Instars are accurately determined using: Head-capsule width. Head-capsule length. Mandible width. Cast larval skin. Body size and color as supporting indicators. Body length alone is not a decisive criterion because it is affected by movement, feeding, preservation method, environmental conditions, and host plant.

Appearance and Color The first-instar larva emerges from the egg as a small and delicate larva with a body that is: White or creamy. Pale yellowish. Slightly translucent. Extremely slender. Provided with a distinct dark-brown or black head. After feeding begins, the contents of the digestive tract may be visible through the translucent body. The dark band behind the head is not always visible in this instar. Therefore, its absence in a newly hatched larva does not exclude Tuta absoluta. Measurements Published measurements vary according to the study and the method used to collect larvae. The length of the first instar in different studies ranges approximately between: 0.6 and 1.5 mm. Head-capsule width usually ranges around: 0.13–0.18 mm One study recorded a range of 0.16–0.22 mm and a mean of 0.18 mm. The relatively wide variation in body length results from the fact that some measurements are taken immediately after hatching, whereas others are taken near the end of the first instar. Behavior After Hatching After emerging from the egg, the larva begins searching for a suitable point at which to penetrate the plant. It may move over the leaf surface for a short period before biting through the epidermis and entering the tissue. Preferred sites include: Near veins. At the bases of plant trichomes. Within folds of the leaf surface. At leaflet junctions. Young tissues. Areas near the calyx or growing points. The period before the larva enters the tissue is one of the most vulnerable periods in its life because it is exposed on the plant surface and is not yet protected within a mine. Once entry is completed, the larva becomes positioned between the two epidermal layers of the leaf and begins consuming internal tissue. Nature of Damage The newly hatched larva forms a small and delicate mine that may appear as: A small pale blotch. A short line. A limited translucent area. A mining point that is difficult to detect. An area containing a small amount of frass. Damage caused by each individual larva is initially limited, but the importance of L1 lies in establishing the infestation site, which expands as the larva grows. Duration of the First Instar In one laboratory study, the duration of the first instar ranged between: Two and three days With a mean of approximately: 2.08 days. However, this period varies according to temperature, host, cultivar, humidity, and the nutritional condition of the larva. Importance in Control The first instar is one of the most important control targets because: It remains exposed for a short period after hatching. It has not yet consumed a large quantity of tissue. It is generally located close to the leaf surface. It requires less food to ingest a lethal dose of some ingestion-active products. It has a lower capacity to move between plant parts. It generally has not yet entered fruits or deep stem tissues. However, the window for targeting this instar is short because the larva enters plant tissues rapidly after hatching.

Appearance and Color After the first molt, the larva increases in size and the body becomes: Creamy or yellowish green. More robust. Less translucent. Provided with a black or dark-brown head. More clearly visible within the mine. Green coloration begins to appear as the larva feeds on plant tissues and the digestive tract fills with green plant material. Measurements The length of the second instar in different studies ranges approximately between: 1.6 and 2.7 mm. Head-capsule width is approximately: 0.19–0.30 mm One study recorded a range of 0.24–0.35 mm and a mean of 0.30 mm. The clear increase in head width allows L2 to be distinguished from L1 more reliably than by body color alone. Behavior and Feeding Most second-instar larvae remain inside leaves, but they become more capable of: Enlarging the mine. Moving between parts of the leaflet. Leaving an old mine. Starting a new mine. Approaching the midrib. Attacking thicker tissues. Dark frass becomes more visible within the mine, and the larva is easier to observe when the leaf is held against the light. Duration of the Second Instar In one study, the duration of the second instar ranged between: Two and four days With a mean of approximately: 2.75 days. The period becomes longer under cool conditions and shorter under warm temperatures within the suitable range. Importance in Control The second instar remains a relatively suitable control target, particularly when larvae are located within superficial leaf mines. However, its survival capacity improves compared with the first instar, and the quantity of tissue consumed increases. Control is more successful when the active ingredient reaches the mine or the larva is forced to ingest it during feeding, rather than relying only on contact with the outer leaf surface.

Appearance and Color In the third instar, the body becomes: Yellowish green. Bluish green in some individuals. Light or dark green depending on the food. Provided with a dark-brown head. Thicker and more distinctly segmented. The dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield begins to become more visible. The body color may shift to pinkish or brownish shades when feeding inside fruits or when the larva approaches the end of its development. Measurements The length of the third instar is generally approximately: 3.8–4.3 mm. Head-capsule width ranges approximately between: 0.34 and 0.41 mm One study recorded a range of 0.35–0.45 mm and a mean of 0.38 mm. Activity and Damage The third instar becomes more active and consumes more food. It can: Form wide mines. Move from one leaflet to another. Bore into leaf petioles. Enter growing points. Infest buds. Penetrate tender stems. Attack fruits. The amount of frass inside mines increases and may appear as irregular dark aggregations. Economic damage begins to increase clearly during this instar, particularly when larvae reach reproductive plant parts. Duration of the Third Instar In one study, the duration of the third instar ranged between: Three and five days With a mean of approximately: 3.56 days. The duration varies according to temperature, cultivar, host, and the type of tissue on which the larva feeds. Importance of the Third Instar in Control Control of the third instar becomes more difficult than control of L1 and L2 because it: Occurs within wider and deeper mines. Can move into stems and fruits. Consumes a larger amount of tissue. May leave the leaf before treatment reaches it. Has a larger body size and greater ability to tolerate inadequate doses. This does not mean that control is impossible, but treatment success depends more heavily on: Active ingredient. Quality of coverage. Ability of the insecticide to penetrate plant tissues. Timing of application. Absence of resistance. Continued feeding on treated tissue.

Appearance and Color The fourth instar is the largest and most conspicuous larval instar. Body color ranges between: Yellowish green. Bluish green. Brownish green. Light pink. Purplish pink in some individuals. Pinkish brown near the prepupal stage. The head is dark brown, and the dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield becomes more distinct. Pink bands or areas may appear on the dorsal surface of the body, particularly in fully grown larvae. Measurements The length of the fourth instar in several studies is approximately: 7.2–7.6 mm The fully grown larva may reach approximately: 9–10 mm In some guides and geographical strains. Head-capsule width usually measures approximately: 0.49–0.58 mm One study recorded a range of 0.53–0.63 mm and a mean of 0.58 mm. Body width in some measurements reaches approximately 1.1 mm. Feeding and Damage The fourth instar consumes the largest amount of tissue compared with earlier instars and can cause: Collapse of large areas of the leaf. Coalescence of several mines. Drying of leaflets. Death of growing points. Boring into stems and petioles. Penetration of fruits. Enlargement of cavities within fruits. Increased probability of entry by rot pathogens. The larva may leave an old mine and move to a new location. Therefore, empty mines may be present while active infestation continues elsewhere. Large larvae may also be observed hanging from silken threads during movement or while searching for a pupation site. Duration of the Fourth Instar In one study, the duration of the fourth instar ranged between: Three and five days With a mean of approximately: 3.86 days. The final part of this instar includes the period in which the larva becomes fully grown and prepares to stop feeding. End of the Fourth Instar When development is completed: Larval appetite declines. The larva stops enlarging the mine. Part of the digestive tract is emptied. Body color changes. The body may become shorter and more robust. The larva leaves the feeding site. It searches for a protected pupation site. It produces silken threads. It enters the prepupal stage. Pupation may occur within: A folded leaf. A mine or protected plant part. A silken cocoon on the leaf. Crop residues. Near the soil surface. The upper soil layer.

Measurements vary according to strain, host, temperature, and exact stage at measurement. Therefore, the following values represent approximate practical ranges: Instar Approximate Body Length Approximate Head-Capsule Width Predominant Color L1 0.6–1.5 mm 0.13–0.18 mm White or creamy with a dark head L2 1.6–2.7 mm 0.19–0.30 mm Creamy to light green L3 3.8–4.3 mm 0.34–0.41 mm Yellowish or bluish green L4 7.2–7.6 mm, and may reach 9–10 mm 0.49–0.58 mm Green to pink or pinkish brown One morphometric study recorded the following means: Instar Mean Length Mean Width Mean Head Width L1 1.51 mm 0.19 mm 0.18 mm L2 2.66 mm 0.39 mm 0.30 mm L3 4.22 mm 0.64 mm 0.38 mm L4 7.59 mm 1.15 mm 0.58 mm A small difference in length alone should not be used to determine the instar; the head, cast skin, color, and behavior should be examined together.

Larval color is not a fixed characteristic and is not sufficient by itself for diagnosis. Color is affected by: Larval instar. Type of tissue consumed. Color of gut contents. Proximity to molting. Proximity to pupation. Temperature. Host plant. Health condition of the larva. Exposure to an insecticide or pathogen. Specimen-preservation method. The larva remains pale for several hours after molting because the new covering has not yet acquired its final color and hardness. Advanced larvae may become: Green while feeding inside leaves. Pink when the body is fully fed or approaching pupation. Brown or dark when diseased or dead. Pale white immediately after molting. Therefore, observing a pink larva does not mean that it is a different species, and observing a green larva does not confirm that it is Tuta.

One of the most important characters used to identify Tuta absoluta larvae is the presence of a dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield immediately behind the head. This band appears as: A dark-brown line. Black shading. A curved dark margin. A transverse band behind the head. It is more distinct in the third and fourth instars, whereas it may be absent or weak in young larvae. However, this character is not definitive by itself because tomato pinworm: Keiferia lycopersicella Also possesses a dark band behind the head, although it is generally less distinct. Reliable differentiation between the two species requires examination of additional characters and may involve: Fine cuticular structure. Setae and pinacula. Genitalia of the adult insect. Molecular examination.

During transition between instars, the larva leaves behind its old external covering. The mine may contain: Transparent cast skin. An empty head capsule. Remnants of the old covering. Frass accumulated near the molting site. The old head capsule is dark and relatively hard and may be used to determine the previous instar. Finding more than one head capsule within a mine may indicate that the larva spent several instars in the same area, but does not necessarily mean that several larvae were present. Larval movement and feeding temporarily decline before molting and resume after the new covering hardens.

The larva feeds between the two epidermal layers of the leaf, consuming mesophyll tissue while part of the outer epidermis remains intact. The mine begins small and then expands as the larva grows, becoming: An irregular blotch. A semi-transparent area. A broad mine. A gray or white area. A dry brown blotch in old infestation. Dark excreta known as: Frass Are commonly present within the mine. The frass may accumulate in one part of the mine rather than being distributed evenly. Tuta mines are generally broad and blotch-like, whereas mines formed by Liriomyza flies are usually narrower, winding, or serpentine. However, several overlapping Liriomyza mines may form a broad area. Therefore, the mine should be opened and the larva itself examined.

sectionTitle: Movement of Larvae Between Feeding Sites
text: The larva does not necessarily remain inside the first mine throughout the larval stage. It may leave and move to: A new site within the same leaf. Another leaflet. The growing point. The leaf petiole. The stem. A flower bud. Sepals. A fruit. The larva uses a silken thread to assist in: Descending from the leaf. Moving between plant parts. Anchoring the body. Escaping disturbance. Searching for a pupation site. Large larvae may hang from the plant by a silken thread, particularly when plants are shaken or the feeding site is disturbed.

sectionTitle: Infestation of Growing Points and Stems
text: When the larva enters the growing point or a tender stem, it may cause: Death of the terminal bud. Cessation of stem elongation. Emergence of lateral shoots. Irregular plant architecture. Damage to flower clusters. Wilting of the plant part beyond the infestation site. Reduced fruit set and early yield. Transplants and young plants are more sensitive to growing-point infestation because loss of the main apex represents a large proportion of the plant’s vegetative structure. Frass may be present at the entry hole or inside the infested bud.

Larvae enter fruits from different sites. Entry is commonly found: Near the calyx. Beneath the sepals. At the pedicel–fruit attachment zone. At points where a leaf contacts the fruit. On the sides. Near a crack or wound. The larva leaves: A small entry hole. Dark frass. Tunnels beneath the epidermis. Cavities within the flesh. An exit hole. Softened areas. Secondary rots. The size of the entry hole does not reflect the extent of internal damage. The hole may be minute while the tunnel extends through a large area of the fruit. Fruit infestation is particularly serious because it converts partially compensable leaf damage into direct loss of marketable or processable yield.

The duration of the larval stage changes greatly according to temperature. In one study conducted under constant temperatures, the total duration of the larval stage was as follows: Temperature Duration of the Larval Stage 10°C 56.4 days 15°C 34.1 days 20°C 17.8 days 25°C 11.0 days 30°C 10.4 days These values demonstrate that cold conditions do not necessarily prevent feeding, but they prolong the period during which larvae remain on the plant. In other studies conducted under warm conditions, the total duration generally ranged around: 10–14 days And may extend for longer periods depending on temperature, cultivar, and host. A shorter duration at high temperature does not always mean greater damage by an individual larva because daily food intake, survival, and physiological condition also change.

sectionTitle: Most Suitable Temperatures for Larval Development
text: Development becomes faster as temperature increases within the suitable range, but survival does not continue to increase at extreme temperatures. In one study: Larval survival was approximately 42.1% at 10°C. It increased to approximately 80.8% at 25°C. Thermal studies indicate that a range of approximately: 20–25°C Provides an appropriate balance among: Developmental rate. Survival. Feeding. Successful transformation to the pupal stage. Continuity of the generation. Temperatures around 30°C shorten the duration of the stage, but the overall effect depends on exposure duration, humidity, strain, and host plant. Extremely high or low temperatures cause: Slow or abnormal growth. Increased mortality. Reduced feeding. Molting failure. Development of abnormal larvae or pupae.

sectionTitle: Effect of Host Plant and Cultivar
text: Larvae do not develop at the same rate on all plants or tomato cultivars. Their performance is affected by: Leaf toughness. Epidermal thickness. Trichome density. Glandular trichomes. Secondary compounds. Nutritional content. Nitrogen concentration. Tissue moisture. Leaf age. Cultivar resistance. Studies on processing tomato cultivars recorded larval durations ranging from less than nine days on some cultivars to more than twelve days on others. Larval duration alone is not sufficient to evaluate cultivar resistance. The following should also be measured: Larval survival. Pupal weight. Fecundity of resulting adults. Percentage of fruit infestation. Population growth rate.

sectionTitle: Sensitivity of Larvae to Different Factors
text: Desiccation Larvae inside mines are relatively protected from dry air because the leaf provides a more humid microclimate. However, larvae become more exposed to desiccation when they: Leave the mine. Move between leaves. Hang from a silken thread. Search for a pupation site. Are newly hatched before penetrating the tissue. High temperature and dry air increase water loss, especially in small larvae. Rainfall and Overhead Irrigation Water droplets do not easily reach larvae established inside mines, but they may affect exposed larvae by: Knocking them from the plant. Disrupting their movement. Increasing humidity and encouraging some entomopathogens. Removing a proportion of newly hatched larvae. Damaging thin tissue around mines. Overhead irrigation or rainfall is not a sufficient control method because a large proportion of larvae remain protected within plant tissues. Predators Larvae, especially young larvae close to the mine surface, are preyed upon by organisms including: Nesidiocoris tenuis. Macrolophus pygmaeus. Other mirid bugs. Lacewings. Some spiders and generalist predators. Predators may pierce the leaf epidermis and suck the larva located inside the mine or capture it while it is leaving and moving. Larval Parasitoids Different parasitoids attack Tuta larvae, including species belonging to groups such as: Necremnus. Bracon. Dolichogenidea. Other local or introduced parasitoids. The suitable larval instar for parasitism varies according to parasitoid species. The parasitoid may lay its egg: Inside the larval body. On the larval body. Beside the larva within the mine after paralyzing it. Excessive use of broad-spectrum insecticides kills these parasitoids and reduces their natural contribution. Entomopathogenic Bacteria Products containing Bacillus thuringiensis act after the larva ingests the crystals and bacteria deposited on treated tissue. Young instars are generally more suitable targets because they: Consume newly treated superficial tissue. Require a lower dose. Have not yet entered fruits or deep stem tissues. Are less capable of tolerating intestinal damage. Efficacy declines when: The larva is large. The material does not reach the feeding site. The larva stops feeding. The product degrades under radiation and environmental conditions. Resistance or reduced susceptibility is present. Entomopathogenic Fungi Fungi such as Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae can infect larvae when conidia reach the body and suitable humidity permits germination. Exposed larvae or larvae moving outside mines are more likely to come into contact with the fungi, whereas direct contact is less likely for larvae deep inside fruits. Entomopathogenic Nematodes Some entomopathogenic nematodes can reach larvae inside mines when a film of moisture allows their movement. Their efficacy is associated with: Surface moisture. Temperature. Nematode species. Duration of wetness. Mine depth. Application quality. Chemical Insecticides Larval susceptibility to insecticides varies according to: Larval instar. Mode of action of the insecticide. Ability of the material to penetrate the leaf. Quantity ingested by the larva. Larval location. Quality of coverage. Timing of application. Presence of resistance. History of use of the insecticide group in the field. Young instars are generally easier to target than large larvae located inside fruits and stems. Changing the trade name does not mean changing the mode of action because several products may belong to the same IRAC group.

sectionTitle: Why Are Advanced Larvae More Difficult to Control?
text: Control becomes more difficult from L1 to L4 for the following reasons: Larger body size and greater ability to tolerate an incomplete dose. Movement into deeper tissues. Increased infestation of fruits and stems. Reduced direct exposure of the body to spray solution. Ability to leave the treated mine. Rapid production of damage before death. Possible resistance to the active ingredient. Reduced efficacy of some biological insecticides against large instars. Therefore, program efficacy is not measured only by its ability to kill L4, but by its ability to prevent a large proportion of the pest population from reaching that stage.

sectionTitle: Distinguishing Tuta Larvae from Liriomyza Larvae
text: Characteristic Tuta absoluta Larva Liriomyza Fly Larva Group Moth larva Fly larva Head Distinct and sclerotized No distinct external head capsule Legs Thoracic legs and abdominal prolegs Legless Body form Segmented lepidopteran larva Relatively smooth maggot-like larva Color Creamy, then green or pink Yellowish white or yellow Mine Usually broad and blotch-like Usually narrow and winding Fruit infestation Possible and economically important Not the usual form of damage Growing-point and stem infestation Common Very limited When there is uncertainty, the mine should be opened carefully. The presence of a distinct head and legs confirms that the larva is lepidopteran and not a leaf-mining fly larva.

The larva of Tuta absoluta differs from Helicoverpa armigera in several characteristics: Characteristic Tuta absoluta Tomato Fruitworm Full-grown size Usually less than 10 mm May exceed 30 mm Feeding pattern Within mines Exposed or partially inside fruits Leaf mines Common Not characteristic Fruit opening Usually small Larger and more obvious Amount of frass Inside the mine or near the opening Larger quantities near the fruit opening Head Relatively small Larger and more distinct Body Slender More robust

sectionTitle: Distinguishing Tuta absoluta from Tomato Pinworm
text: Tuta absoluta resembles tomato pinworm, Keiferia lycopersicella, in: Size. Infestation of leaves and fruits. Formation of blotch mines. Presence of a dark band behind the head. For this reason, reliable separation based on the larva or damage alone may be difficult. The dark marking behind the head is usually stronger in Tuta, but final diagnosis in regions where both species occur may require specialist examination.

Field examination begins with recent mines rather than only with dry leaves. The following steps are used: 1. Select plants from field edges and the interior. 2. Examine young and medium-aged leaves. 3. Search for recent translucent mines. 4. Hold the leaf against the light to locate the larva. 5. Open the mine using a fine needle. 6. Record the presence or absence of a live larva. 7. Estimate larval size approximately. 8. Examine the head and prothoracic band. 9. Record frass and cast skins. 10. Inspect growing points, stems, and fruits. 11. Do not count an old empty mine as active infestation. 12. Record the number of infested plants, not only the number of mines. Assessment should distinguish among: New mines containing L1 or L2. Wide active mines containing L3 or L4. Old empty mines. Dead larvae. Parasitized larvae. Mines caused by Liriomyza flies.

The larva should be collected together with the plant tissue in which it occurs because forceful removal may tear it. The collection method includes: Cutting the infested leaflet. Placing it in a well-ventilated container. Adding a fresh leaf when necessary. Preventing water condensation. Recording the location, date, and cultivar. Separating fruit-infesting larvae from leaf samples. Avoiding exposure of containers to sunlight. Transferring samples rapidly to the examination site. When preserving a specimen for morphological diagnosis, an appropriate preservation method should be used to avoid loss of body color or deformation of the head capsule.

The four instars are not equal in their importance for decision-making. Instar Position Within the Plant Damage Level Ease of Targeting L1 Beginning of penetration or a small mine Low per larva Relatively highest L2 Inside a leaf mine Increasing Good if intervention is early L3 Wide mines, growing points, stems, and fruits High More difficult L4 Deep mines, fruits, and movement toward pupation Highest Relatively lowest A successful program focuses on: Preventing hatching as much as possible. Targeting newly hatched larvae. Preventing their movement into fruits. Reducing the number of larvae reaching L3 and L4. Removing sources of larvae and pupae at the end of the season.

sectionTitle: Reference Table for the Larval Stage
text: Characteristic Description Number of instars Four Number of molts Three Stage responsible for damage All larval instars Color at hatching White or creamy Head Dark brown or black Advanced color Yellowish green, bluish green, or pink Length of L1 Approximately 0.6–1.5 mm Length of L2 Approximately 1.6–2.7 mm Length of L3 Approximately 3.8–4.3 mm Length of L4 Approximately 7.2–7.6 mm and may reach 9–10 mm Important marking Dark band behind the head in advanced instars Duration near 25°C Approximately 11 days Duration near 20°C Approximately 18 days Suitable range Approximately 20–25°C Feeding sites Leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits Mine shape Usually broad, blotch-like, and irregular Frass Dark and visible within mines End of the stage Exit and search for a pupation site

The larva is the stage responsible for all direct damage caused by Tuta absoluta. It passes through four instars separated by three molts. The first-instar larva emerges small, white or creamy, and with a dark head, then rapidly enters plant tissue. The body gradually increases in size and becomes green or pink during advanced instars. Approximate instar lengths range from less than one millimeter to approximately 7.5 mm, while the fully grown larva may reach approximately 9–10 mm. Head-capsule width is more accurate than body length for determining larval instar. Advanced larvae are characterized by a dark band behind the head along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield, but this character alone is not sufficient to distinguish them from some closely related species. Larvae begin by mining leaves and then become increasingly capable of attacking growing points, petioles, stems, and fruits. They form irregular blotch mines that commonly contain dark frass. The larval stage lasts approximately 10–14 days under suitable warm conditions but may extend beyond one month under cold conditions. The most suitable thermal range for larval development and survival is approximately 20–25°C. Control becomes more difficult as larval age advances because large larvae enter deeper tissues, cause greater damage, and become less exposed to insecticides and natural enemies. Effective management therefore focuses on detecting eggs and recent mines and targeting L1 and L2 before larvae reach advanced instars and fruits.

The pupa represents the transitional stage between the larva and the adult insect in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It is a non-feeding stage; the pupa does not mine leaves, stems, or fruits and causes no direct damage to the plant. However, the absence of feeding does not mean that this stage is economically unimportant. Every viable pupa is capable of producing an adult moth that emerges from the pupation site, mates, lays eggs, and initiates a new pest generation. The importance of the pupal stage lies in the fact that it: Represents the transition from the damaging stage to the reproductive and dispersal stage. Is often located in protected sites that are not easily reached by foliar spray treatments. Can survive in soil or crop residues after the crop has ended. May be transported with crates, packages, and sorting residues. Helps the pest persist between cropping seasons. May contribute to pest survival during cold periods. Represents a target for cultural practices directed at soil and crop residues. Therefore, management of Tuta absoluta is not completed by controlling eggs and larvae alone, but must also include reducing pupal numbers and preventing adult moth emergence.

A fully grown larva does not transform immediately into a pupa, but first passes through a short transitional stage known as the: Prepupal Stage This stage begins at the end of the fourth larval instar. External Signs of the Prepupal Stage As the larva approaches pupation, several clear changes appear, including: Feeding stops or declines sharply. The larva leaves the mine or fruit in most cases. The body becomes relatively shorter. The body becomes more robust. Color changes from green to light green or pink. Frass production decreases. Part of the digestive-tract contents is evacuated. Movement increases while searching for a suitable site. Silken threads are produced. The larva hides in a folded leaf, crevice, or surface soil. At this stage, the larva is less closely associated with the feeding site, and it may therefore be found far from the mine it created. Searching for a Pupation Site The fully grown larva leaves the feeding site and moves to a location that provides: Protection from direct sunlight. Moderate humidity. Relative thermal stability. Protection from predators. A surface on which the cocoon can be secured. Reduced risk of flooding or mechanical disturbance. The larva may move across the plant or descend to the soil on a silken thread, then enter between soil particles or beneath surface residues.

sectionTitle: Difference Between the Pupa and the Cocoon
text: The terms pupa and cocoon are commonly confused, although they do not have the same meaning. Pupa The pupa is the insect’s own body during transformation from larva to adult moth. Inside it, the following processes occur: Breakdown and reorganization of larval tissues. Formation of the wings. Formation of the antennae. Formation of the adult legs. Development of compound eyes. Formation of the adult reproductive system. Completion of the moth’s body form. Cocoon The cocoon is the external silken covering spun by the larva around itself before transforming into a pupa. The cocoon may consist of: White silken threads. Plant trichomes. Dry leaf fragments. Soil and sand particles. Fine plant debris. Therefore: > The pupa is the insect itself, whereas the cocoon is the covering surrounding it. The pupa is not always enclosed within a dense, clearly visible cocoon; the silken covering may be thin or nearly transparent, particularly when pupation occurs on leaves.

The pupa of Tuta absoluta is of the type known as: Obtect Pupa In this type, the developing adult appendages, such as the wings, legs, and antennae, are fixed close to the body surface and are not freely movable. The pupa is characterized by: An elongated body. An oval or spindle-shaped form. A relatively rounded anterior end. A narrower and more tapered posterior end. A slightly flattened ventral surface. Visible abdominal segments. Distinct wing cases on both sides of the thorax and abdomen. Leg and antennal sheaths attached closely to the body. A posterior structure that assists anchorage inside the cocoon. The pupa remains motionless most of the time, but it may move the abdominal segments when touched or disturbed, particularly during the early part of the pupal period.

Although the external boundaries are less distinct than in the larva or moth, the main body regions can still be recognized. Head Region The head is located at the anterior end, where the following can be observed: Positions of the developing compound eyes. Antennal sheaths. Developing mouthparts. General outline of the head compressed against the thorax. Thorax The thorax carries structures that will develop into: Forewings. Hindwings. Three pairs of legs. Muscles required for flight and movement. The wing cases appear as long, smooth areas extending from the thorax toward the abdomen. Abdomen The abdomen appears to consist of successive segments, with the posterior segments being more distinct and more mobile. Anchoring structures occur at the posterior end and help keep the pupa secured within the cocoon and prevent slipping as the moth moves during emergence.

The average length of a Tuta absoluta pupa is approximately: 4.3–4.7 mm In many field descriptions, the general length is close to: Approximately 5 mm. Female pupae are larger than male pupae on average. Measurements of Male Pupae Mean length: approximately 4.27 mm. Mean width: approximately 1.23 mm. Measurements of Female Pupae Mean length: approximately 4.67 mm. Mean width: approximately 1.37 mm. These measurements alone should not be used to determine sex because large males may overlap in size with small females. Pupal dimensions are affected by: Sex. Plant cultivar. Quality of larval nutrition. Temperature. Larval density. Plant stress. Larval age at pupation. Laboratory or field conditions. Larval exposure to insecticides or diseases.

Pupal weight reflects the quantity of nutritional resources stored by the larva before pupation. Pupae produced from well-fed larvae are usually: Larger. More capable of producing healthy moths. More likely to produce highly fecund females. Less susceptible to deformities. Small or low-weight pupae may result from: Low-quality food. A less suitable plant cultivar. High competition among larvae. Exposure to a sublethal insecticide dose. Infection with a pathogen. Unsuitable temperature. Premature cessation of feeding. Small pupal size does not necessarily mean mortality, but it may affect the characteristics, fecundity, and longevity of the resulting moth.

Pupal color changes clearly as internal development progresses. Newly Formed Pupa During the first hours, the pupa is: Light green. Yellowish green. Greenish yellow. Relatively soft. Partly translucent. At this stage, the outer covering has not yet hardened completely. Mid-Pupal Period The color gradually changes to: Light brown. Olive brown. Amber brown. Greenish brown. The covering becomes more sclerotized, and details of the wings and segments become clearer. Near Adult Emergence Before adult emergence, the pupa becomes: Dark brown. Blackish brown. Nearly black in some areas. The dark eyes and wings of the developing moth become visible through the pupal covering. Gradual darkening before emergence is a normal change, but it differs from irregular blackening caused by death or fungal infection.

Indicators of a Live Pupa The body remains full and is not shrunken. Abdominal segments respond to touch. Color changes gradually with age. No odor of decomposition is present. No fungal growth is present. A healthy moth emerges at the end of the developmental period. Indicators of a Dead Pupa Obvious shrinkage. Severe desiccation. Abnormal softness and decomposition. Fixed black coloration unrelated to moth development. Abnormal odor. White, green, or dark fungal growth. No response to movement. No moth emergence after the expected period has passed. Dark coloration alone is not sufficient to determine pupal death because a healthy pupa naturally becomes dark before adult emergence.

Tuta absoluta can pupate in several locations. Selection of the pupation site depends on: Plant age. Production system. Canopy density. Soil moisture. Availability of residues. Infestation intensity. Weather conditions. Proximity of the larva to the soil. Degree of disturbance to the plant. Pupation sites include: 1. Soil. 2. Leaf surfaces. 3. Folded leaves. 4. Between two contacting leaves. 5. Inside leaf mines. 6. On stems and petioles. 7. Within plant residues. 8. Near fruits or the calyx. 9. On greenhouse floors. 10. In packages, crates, and packing materials.

A proportion of fully grown larvae leave the plant and descend to the soil surface, then enter the upper soil layer. Pupae are usually found: At the soil surface. Beneath fallen leaf residues. Between cracks. Within approximately the upper 1–2 cm of soil. Among sand particles and loose soil. The larva spins a silken cocoon to which soil particles may adhere, making its color resemble the surrounding medium and rendering detection difficult. Pupation in soil increases when: Plants have sparse foliage. Infested leaves become dry. The crop approaches the end of the season. Soil is loose and moderately moist. Plants are disturbed by movement or harvesting. Larvae descend from the canopy on silken threads.

Larvae can pupate on the canopy inside a thin silken cocoon. Common sites include: A fold along the leaf margin. A rolled leaf. Between two touching leaflets. Inside a dry leaf mine. Near the midrib. At the leaf–stem junction. Between crowded leaves. Beneath sepals or in other protected sites. The larva spins silken threads that close the space or secure the leaf margins around its body. The cocoon may be: Thin and white. Nearly transparent. Covered with leaf trichomes. Mixed with frass or plant fragments. Difficult to see without lifting the leaf or opening the fold.

sectionTitle: Pupation in Processing and Fresh-Market Tomatoes
text: The distribution of pupation sites varies according to the production system. In some processing tomato fields, soil becomes more important as a pupation site because of: Branches being close to the soil surface. Abundant residues. Advanced plant age. Gradual canopy drying. Once-over or semi-concentrated harvesting. In some fresh-market and greenhouse crops, a greater proportion of pupae may be found: Inside folded leaves. On plants. On greenhouse structures. On fabrics and floors. Inside containers and sorting areas. There is no single fixed pupation site for all production systems. Some field studies recorded higher numbers on leaves than in soil, whereas other observations showed a predominance of ground pupation.

sectionTitle: Pupation in Containers and Handling Areas
text: Pupation is not restricted to the field. Larvae may leave fruits or accompanying leaves during: Transport. Sorting. Packing. Temporary storage. Arrival of fruits at the market or processing plant. They may then pupate in: Corners of crates. Crevices in wooden packages. Beneath cards and labels. Between fruits and residues. Vehicle floors. Sorting residues. Corners of packing stations. This characteristic allows the pest to move into new locations because the adult moth may emerge after the package has reached an area far from the original field.

The larva produces silken threads from the silk glands, then moves its head and body to construct a covering around itself. The process proceeds through the following stages: 1. Selection of a protected site. 2. Attachment of initial threads to the substrate. 3. Formation of a silken network. 4. Incorporation of leaf fragments or soil particles. 5. Partial closure of the space. 6. Cessation of external larval movement. 7. Shedding of the larval skin. 8. Appearance of the pupa inside the cocoon. The cocoon may be relatively strong when pupation occurs in soil, where silk becomes mixed with sand particles, whereas it is thinner when pupation occurs on leaves.

The duration of the pupal stage depends primarily on temperature. Under moderate and warm conditions, the pupal stage generally lasts approximately: 6–10 days. However, it may extend for several weeks under cold conditions. Studies conducted under constant temperatures recorded the following values: Temperature Mean Duration of the Pupal Stage 10°C 36.8 days 15°C 18.4 days 20°C 10.2 days 25°C 8.4 days 30°C 5.4 days These values demonstrate a clear inverse relationship between temperature and pupal duration: > The higher the temperature within the suitable range, the shorter the period required for transformation into the adult moth. However, a shorter duration does not always mean that the highest temperature is optimal, because extreme heat may reduce survival or the quality of emerging moths.

sectionTitle: Thermal Requirements of the Pupal Stage
text: The lower developmental threshold for pupae has been estimated at approximately: 7.3°C The thermal constant required to complete this stage has been estimated at approximately: 131.6 accumulated heat units — Degree-Days The upper thermal threshold for pupal development has been estimated at approximately: 36°C. These values mean that the pupa must accumulate a certain quantity of heat above the lower threshold in order to complete transformation into the adult insect. Practical Example At a mean daily temperature of 25°C: Daily heat units: 25 − 7.3 = 17.7 degree-days The theoretical duration required: 131.6 ÷ 17.7 = approximately 7.4 days This value is close to the actual duration recorded at 25°C, which was approximately 8.4 days. The calculation remains approximate because soil or cocoon temperature may differ from air temperature.

sectionTitle: Most Suitable Temperatures for Pupal Development
text: The thermal range suitable for pupal development and successful adult emergence is generally around: 20–25°C This range provides a balance among: Developmental rate. High survival. Reduced deformities. Production of normal moths. Continuity of the life cycle. One study recorded pupal survival of approximately: 83.5% at 25°C Compared with approximately: 52.5% at 10°C. The effect of temperature on pupal survival was less severe than its effect on eggs, indicating that pupae are relatively more tolerant of some thermal conditions.

The pupa is one of the important stages for pest survival during cold periods, particularly when protected within soil, crop residues, or greenhouses. A study of continuous cold exposure showed that pupae were able to survive at: 5°C for 30 or 60 days And subsequently produced moths after being returned to suitable conditions, although emergence declined as exposure duration increased. After continuous exposure for 90 days, some pupae appeared to remain alive, but no complete adult moths emerged. Under a gradual cooling program: Approximately 47% of moths emerged after a period at 11°C. Emergence declined to approximately 12% during the following period at 10°C. No emergence occurred when temperature was subsequently lowered to 8°C. These results show that pupae can tolerate extended cold exposure, but not indefinitely, and their ability to complete development declines as the duration and intensity of cold increase.

Pupae require moderate moisture to prevent desiccation, but they are damaged by excessive water saturation. Soil moisture affects: Larval entry into the soil. Cocoon formation. Cohesion of soil particles. Gas exchange. Water loss from the pupa. Fungal growth. Adult emergence to the soil surface. In a recent laboratory experiment using different soil-moisture levels, pupal survival was approximately: Experimental Soil Moisture Final Survival Percentage Dry soil 24% 20% moisture 80% 40% moisture 70% 60% moisture 53% 80% moisture 22% The results indicate that: Severe dryness reduces survival. Moderate moisture provides the highest survival. Saturation or high moisture reduces survival and emergence. Moderately moist soil provides the best balance for pupae. These percentages are not fixed for all soil types; results vary according to soil texture, temperature, aeration, salinity, and pupal depth.

The pupa requires oxygen through its spiracles. Therefore, continuous waterlogging or soil saturation leads to: Reduced aeration. Disrupted respiration. Increased microbial growth. Pupal decomposition. Delayed emergence. Increased adult deformity. Reduced survival. However, a single heavy irrigation does not guarantee elimination of all pupae, particularly in well-drained sandy soils where water passes rapidly and some air spaces remain.

Severe desiccation causes water loss from the pupa, particularly when it occurs: At the soil surface. Inside a thin cocoon. On dry leaves. In packages exposed to air and heat. A pupa damaged by desiccation may appear as: A shrunken body. Reduced weight. A hard, dry covering. Failure of adult emergence. Emergence of a deformed or weak moth. The cocoon and surface soil provide partial protection from desiccation, but they do not completely prevent water loss.

Pupal duration becomes shorter as temperature rises to 30°C within the tolerable range, but prolonged high temperature or temperatures above the upper threshold cause: Increased mortality. Disruption of wing formation. Failure of transformation into the adult. Emergence of deformed insects. Reduced subsequent fecundity. Desiccation of surface pupae. Pupae inside soil are less exposed to temperature fluctuations than pupae located on leaves or exposed containers.

When adult development inside the pupa is completed: 1. The pupal covering becomes dark. 2. Body parts separate from the inner surface of the covering. 3. The adult presses against the anterior end. 4. The pupal shell splits. 5. The moth emerges with soft, folded wings. 6. Fluids are pumped into the wing veins. 7. The wings expand. 8. The scales dry and the body hardens. 9. The moth begins moving and flying. A newly emerged moth is weak and is not immediately capable of full flight. It requires a period of time for: Wing expansion. Drying of the body. Hardening of the legs. Completion of scale coloration. Initiation of normal activity.

The empty pupal case remains at the pupation site after adult emergence. It is characterized by being: Light in weight. Brown or relatively transparent. Open at the anterior end. Empty of contents. Sometimes fixed within the cocoon. Easily collapsed under pressure. Finding an empty pupal case indicates that an adult moth has already emerged and does not indicate the presence of a live pupa that can still be controlled.

Pupal sex can be determined by examining the ventral surface under a stereomicroscope. Female Pupa It is characterized by: A longitudinal genital opening or slit. Located in the middle of the eighth abdominal segment. Positioned between two small projections. Larger size and weight on average. Male Pupa It is characterized by: The genital opening being located on the ninth abdominal segment. Absence of the female slit on the eighth segment. Relatively smaller size. A more slender body on average. Sex determination is used in: Laboratory rearing experiments. Calculation of sex ratio. Formation of selected mating pairs. Behavioral studies. Sterile-insect programs. Evaluation of insecticide effects on each sex. In routine field inspection, determining the sex of every pupa is not necessary.

The pupa is a non-feeding stage and therefore is not affected by insecticides that require ingestion in the same manner as larvae. The efficacy of many foliar sprays against pupae is also reduced because pupae: Occur inside cocoons. Hide in folded leaves. Occur inside soil. Do not feed on treated tissue. Possess a hardened external covering. May occur beneath residues. Some materials may act through contact, fumigation, or penetration, but success depends on direct delivery of the material to the pupa. A spray that is effective against larvae should not automatically be considered effective against pupae present in the soil.

sectionTitle: Natural Enemies of the Pupal Stage
text: Pupae are exposed to varying degrees to: Soil predators. Ants. Spiders and ground-dwelling arthropods. Entomopathogenic fungi. Entomopathogenic nematodes. Some specialized or general parasitoids. However, a pupa enclosed within a cocoon and located inside soil is relatively protected. The efficacy of biological agents is affected by: Soil moisture. Temperature. Pupal depth. Soil type. Pupal age. Type of biological organism. Presence of pesticide residues. Duration of contact. Some experiments have shown that infection of Tuta pupae by entomopathogenic nematodes is possible, but mortality rates may be lower than those against larvae. Therefore, a management program should not rely on this method alone.

sectionTitle: Importance of the Pupal Stage in Infestation Persistence
text: Pupae may remain present after: Harvest has ended. The spray program has stopped. Part of the crop has been removed. The canopy has dried. Produce has been transported from the field. Adult moths may then emerge and move to: Recently planted fields. Nurseries. Other solanaceous plants. Nearby greenhouses. Subsequent planting seasons. This explains the continued appearance of moths after fruits have been removed or after visible larval numbers on plants have declined.

sectionTitle: Importance of the Pupal Stage in Integrated Pest Management
text: Disposal of Crop Residues Crop residues may contain: Late-instar larvae. Pupae on leaves. Cocoons between stems. Pupae near the soil surface. Leaving these residues allows a new generation of moths to emerge. Agricultural sanitation therefore includes: Shredding plants immediately after harvest ends. Collecting dense residues. Avoiding the accumulation of plant piles near the field. Disposing of infested rejected fruits. Cleaning field margins. Tillage Tillage after crop removal contributes to: Exposing some pupae to sunlight and desiccation. Moving pupae to depths that impede moth emergence. Exposing them to birds and predators. Breaking cocoons. Interrupting continuity between cropping seasons. However, tillage efficacy varies with depth, soil type, and moisture, and shallow tillage alone does not guarantee complete pupal control. Soil Solarization Soil solarization during suitable seasons helps raise the temperature of the upper soil layer, affecting pupae and other stages beneath the cover. Its efficacy depends on: Intensity of solar radiation. Duration of covering. Soil moisture. Proper sealing of the plastic. Pupal depth. Season of the year. Cleaning Containers and Sorting Stations Cleaning containers and handling areas prevents movement of: Larvae emerging from fruits. Pupae inside corners. Moths emerging later. Leaf, calyx, and damaged-fruit residues should be removed daily, and contaminated crates should not be returned to farms without cleaning. Soil-Moisture Management Irrigation should not be increased indiscriminately in an attempt to kill pupae because this may damage roots, increase diseases, and waste water. However, understanding pupal sensitivity to severe desiccation and saturation helps explain changes in pupal numbers under different irrigation systems and soil textures.

Pupae are collected from: Folded leaves. Dry mines. Stems and crop residues. Soil surface. The upper soil layer. Crates and containers. Greenhouse floors. The following steps are used: 1. Lift dry leaves carefully. 2. Examine silken threads. 3. Sieve the upper soil layer. 4. Collect the complete cocoon. 5. Avoid pressing the pupa. 6. Record the collection site. 7. Separate live pupae from empty cases. 8. Preserve them in a ventilated container. 9. Monitor adult emergence. 10. Examine any fungal growth or parasitism.

sectionTitle: Reference Table for the Pupal Stage
text: Characteristic Description Pupal type Obtect pupa Shape Oval or spindle-shaped and elongated Newly formed color Green or greenish yellow Intermediate color Light brown or olive Before adult emergence Dark brown to blackish Male length Approximately 4.27 mm Male width Approximately 1.23 mm Female length Approximately 4.67 mm Female width Approximately 1.37 mm General field length Approximately 5 mm Pupation site Soil, leaves, mines, or crop residues Usual soil depth Surface layer, commonly 1–2 cm Pupal duration at 20°C Approximately 10.2 days Pupal duration at 25°C Approximately 8.4 days Pupal duration at 30°C Approximately 5.4 days Lower thermal threshold Approximately 7.3°C Thermal constant Approximately 131.6 degree-days Upper thermal threshold Approximately 36°C Female sex Opening on the eighth abdominal segment Male sex Opening on the ninth abdominal segment Feeding stage? No Direct damage None Importance Production of the moth and initiation of the next generation

The pupa is the non-feeding stage within which the larva of Tuta absoluta transforms into an adult moth. It is elongated or spindle-shaped and measures approximately 4.3–4.7 mm in length, with female pupae being larger than male pupae on average. A newly formed pupa is green or yellowish green, then turns brown and becomes dark before adult emergence. The insect pupates in soil, on leaves, inside folded leaves, within mines and crop residues, and in containers. Soil pupae are commonly located in the upper soil layer inside a silken cocoon to which sand particles adhere. Pupal duration is affected by temperature. It is approximately 36.8 days at 10°C, declines to 10.2 days at 20°C, 8.4 days at 25°C, and 5.4 days at 30°C. A range of 20–25°C provides suitable conditions for pupal development and adult emergence, while pupae can tolerate extended cold periods, especially in protected sites. Pupae require moderate moisture. Both severe desiccation and water saturation reduce survival and emergence. Most foliar spray treatments do not reach pupae located inside soil or cocoons. Their management therefore depends mainly on: Removal of crop residues. Shredding and tilling fields after harvest. Cleaning containers and sorting areas. Preventing neglected fields from persisting. Soil management and agricultural sanitation. Use of appropriate biological agents within an integrated program. The pupal stage causes no direct damage, but neglecting it allows new adult moths to emerge and initiate a new infestation cycle in the same field or in neighboring crops.

The adult insect represents the final stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It appears as a small grayish-brown moth with a slender body and narrow wings covered with scales. The adult moth does not produce the mines or holes found in leaves, stems, and fruits because direct damage is caused by the larvae. The principal functions of the adult stage are: Emerging from the pupa. Expanding the wings and acquiring the ability to fly. Searching for the opposite sex. Mating. Laying eggs. Moving between plants and fields. Establishing new infestation foci. Its economic importance results from its capacity for reproduction and dispersal. A single moth does not consume the fruit or leaf, but a fertilized female lays enough eggs to initiate a new generation of mining larvae. The adult insect is known in field usage by names such as: Tuta moth. Tuta butterfly. Adult moth. Adult insect. Adult Tuta. The term Tuta moth is more entomologically accurate because it belongs to the micro-moths of the order Lepidoptera and is not one of the large diurnal butterflies.

sectionTitle: Emergence of the Moth from the Pupa
text: When development of the insect inside the pupa is completed, the pupa becomes dark brown or blackish, and the developing moth structures become visible through the pupal covering. Emergence then occurs in the following sequence: 1. The adult insect separates from the inner pupal covering. 2. It moves the abdomen and legs inside the pupal shell. 3. It presses against the anterior end of the pupa. 4. The shell splits in the head and thoracic region. 5. The moth emerges with soft, folded wings. 6. It moves to a stable and suitable location. 7. Hemolymph is pumped into the wing veins. 8. The wings gradually expand. 9. The body dries and the legs and appendages harden. 10. The moth acquires its normal ability to move and fly. The moth cannot fly efficiently immediately after emergence because the wings are small, soft, and folded around the body. Wing expansion and hardening may require several hours depending on: Temperature. Humidity. Condition of the pupa. Space available around the emergence site. Nutritional condition of the larva before pupation. Exposure of the pupa to insecticides or heat stress. If the moth emerges in a confined space or is compressed before the wings have fully expanded, it may develop twisted or shortened wings and have limited ability to fly and mate.

sectionTitle: General Appearance of the Adult Insect
text: The Tuta absoluta moth is a small insect with a slender, elongated body. It has a mottled gray appearance that helps it remain concealed on dry leaves, stems, soil, and crop residues. Its body consists of three main regions: 1. Head. 2. Thorax. 3. Abdomen. The adult insect bears: One pair of filiform antennae. One pair of compound eyes. One pair of upturned labial palpi. Two pairs of wings. Three pairs of legs. An elongated segmented abdomen. Specialized genital structures at the end of the abdomen. The general coloration is a mixture of: Silvery gray. Grayish brown. Light brown. Pale yellowish orange. Dark spots or dots on the forewings. Color is not completely constant. The intensity of mottling and color shades may vary according to age, sex, geographical region, rearing conditions, and the condition of the scales.

Tuta absoluta is classified among the small moths. Body length in a normal resting position is usually approximately: 6–7 mm. Some guides may mention a measurement close to: 10 mm When the entire insect is measured with the wings in a particular position or when a total length different from body length alone is used. Forewing length is approximately: 4.2–5.0 mm. Wingspan with the wings fully extended generally ranges around: 8–12 mm. Measurement method explains much of the variation among published values. A researcher may measure: Body length only. Forewing length. Total length of the insect with wings folded. Distance between the tips of the fully extended wings. A live or preserved specimen. A male or female. Females are larger and heavier than males on average, but there is clear size overlap between the sexes. Therefore, body length alone is not sufficient for sex determination.

The moth’s head is small and covered with gray, brown, and pale scales. It bears: Compound eyes. Antennae. Labial palpi. Mouthparts. A number of sensory setae and scales. The head may sometimes appear lighter than the thorax because of creamy or pale-yellow scales among the gray scales. Compound Eyes The moth possesses one pair of dark compound eyes on both sides of the head. The eyes assist in: Perceiving light and darkness. Determining direction of movement. Moving between plants. Responding to movement of objects. Selecting hiding places. Moving under low-light conditions. The eyes are adapted to the moth’s crepuscular and nocturnal activity. However, vision is not the only mechanism used to locate the opposite sex. Chemical olfaction through the antennae plays the principal role in male response to the sex pheromone. Antennae The antennae are long, slender, and thread-like. This form is known as: Filiform Antennae Each antenna consists of a large number of small successive segments. The antenna is nearly as long as the body or a substantial proportion of it and may appear banded with pale and dark rings because of the arrangement of scales on the segments. The antennae perform important sensory functions, including: Detecting the sex pheromone. Recognizing plant odors. Responding to humidity and temperature. Assisting balance during flight. Detecting chemical substances in the environment. The male is highly sensitive to the components of the sex pheromone released by the female. This forms the basis of pheromone traps used for monitoring. Male antennae are not strongly feathered as in some moth species; they remain filiform in appearance. Therefore, sex cannot be determined reliably in the field from antennal form alone. Labial Palpi Relatively long labial palpi occur at the front of the head and are directed upward. They are known as: Upturned Labial Palpi They are usually composed of three segments. These palpi give the head a pointed or projecting appearance when the moth is viewed from the side. The palpi bear setae and sensory receptors that help the insect examine its surroundings and detect chemical substances. The upward direction of the palpi is an important general diagnostic feature in many moths belonging to the family Gelechiidae. Mouthparts and Feeding The moth does not possess chewing mouthparts like the larva and cannot mine leaves or penetrate fruits. The ability of adult insects to utilize water or sugar solutions varies according to conditions. Longevity in laboratory rearing may increase when the following are provided: Water. Sugar solution. Diluted honey. Suitable nectar sources. However, the moth does not feed on plant tissues in a manner that causes direct economic loss. Its principal function remains reproduction and dispersal.

The thorax consists of three segments: Prothorax. Mesothorax. Metathorax. It bears: Three pairs of legs. Forewings on the mesothorax. Hindwings on the metathorax. Muscles responsible for flight and movement. The thorax is covered with gray, brown, and silvery scales and may also carry pale or faint yellow-orange scales. The thorax is slightly wider than the abdomen but remains slender compared with many larger butterfly species.

The forewings are narrow and elongated and are among the most important body parts used for external identification of the moth. They are characterized by: Silvery gray or grayish-brown coloration. Intermingling of dark and pale scales. Presence of small black spots or dots. Presence of pale yellow or orange scales. A rounded or slightly pointed tip. Fringes along the posterior and terminal margins. Dark spots are not always distributed in the same pattern among all individuals; they may be distinct in some moths and weak in others. Wing appearance is affected by: Age of the insect. Friction against leaves. Contact with the trap. Adhesive material. Collection and preservation method. Loss of scales. Humidity. Geographical region. Old moths may become paler and less mottled because part of the scales has been lost.

The hindwings are: Lighter than the forewings. Pale gray or silvery. Relatively narrower. Partly translucent in some areas. Provided with a long margin of fine setae. The long setae along the wing margins are known as: Fringes These fringes increase the effective aerodynamic surface of the wing without a major increase in insect weight, a common feature among small moths. The hindwings are not clearly visible when the moth is at rest because they are covered by the forewings.

The moth’s wings and body are covered with minute scales, the feature from which the name of the order: Lepidoptera Is derived. Scales perform functions that include: Producing coloration and patterns. Protecting the body surface. Reducing friction. Improving aerodynamic properties. Helping the insect escape from some surfaces or predators. Scales detach easily when the wings are touched or when the moth contacts sticky traps. For this reason, a moth intended for diagnosis should be held by the legs or body with fine instruments, and the wings should not be pressed with the fingers.

The moth has three pairs of legs attached to the three thoracic segments. The legs are: Long and slender. Covered with scales and setae. Banded or marked with pale and dark colors. Equipped with fine claws at their ends. Capable of gripping leaf and stem surfaces. The legs are used for: Walking over the plant. Stabilizing the body at rest. Cleaning the antennae. Gripping the mate during copulation. Stabilizing the female during oviposition. Moving within dense foliage. Sensory structures occur on the legs and help the moth examine plant surfaces before resting or laying eggs.

The abdomen is long, slender, and composed of successive segments. It contains: Digestive system. Respiratory system. Reproductive organs. Stores of fat and energy. Muscles that move the segments. Glands associated with reproduction and pheromone release in the female. The female abdomen is usually: Wider. Fuller. Relatively rounded at the tip. Capable of expansion when containing eggs. The male abdomen is generally: More slender. More tapered. Less full. Equipped at its end with male copulatory structures. These differences are not always sufficient for sex determination in small or damaged specimens.

When the moth rests on a leaf or stem, it folds its wings over the body in a roof-like or narrow covering position. In this position: The forewings cover the hindwings. The body appears long and slender. The two wing margins meet above the back. The antennae extend forward or to the sides. The legs are positioned beneath the body. The gray-brown coloration blends with: Dry leaves. Stems. Soil. Bark. Crop residues. This camouflage makes the moth difficult to detect during the day, particularly when it remains motionless within the canopy.

Males and females are highly similar in color and wing shape. There is no strong and obvious sexual dimorphism that permits rapid sex determination by simple visual inspection. General differences include: Characteristic Male Female Size Smaller on average Larger on average Weight Lighter Heavier Abdomen More slender and tapered Wider and fuller Abdominal tip Bears male copulatory structures Bears the ovipositor Pheromone production Not the principal source of sexual attraction Releases the sex pheromone Response to traps Strongly attracted to the female pheromone Usually not captured by male-targeted sex traps Main function Searching for and fertilizing females Laying eggs and establishing the generation Size alone should not be relied upon because the sexes overlap. Accurate sex determination depends on: The abdominal tip. Microscopic examination. Dissection of genital structures. Internal characteristics of male and female genitalia.

Examination of adult genitalia is among the most accurate morphological methods for distinguishing Tuta absoluta from closely related moths. Male genitalia have specific structures, including: A broad gnathos with a form resembling a horseshoe. A finger-like or elongated valva. Other internal structures with species-specific forms. The female also possesses diagnostic characteristics in: The genital duct. Bursa copulatrix. Plates surrounding the copulatory opening. Internal structures that differ from those of related species. These characters cannot be seen with the naked eye and require: 1. Separation of the abdomen. 2. Treatment with suitable chemicals to remove soft tissues. 3. Washing the specimen. 4. Mounting and spreading the genitalia on a slide. 5. Microscopic examination. 6. Comparison with reliable taxonomic keys. This examination becomes important when closely related species are suspected, such as: Potato tuber moth, Phthorimaea operculella. Tomato pinworm, Keiferia lycopersicella. Symmetrischema species. Other moths in the tribe Gnorimoschemini. Molecular analysis may be used alongside morphological examination when definitive confirmation is required.

The moth displays clear crepuscular and nocturnal activity. During the day, it commonly hides: Among leaves. Beneath the canopy. Near the soil surface. Within plant residues. In shaded areas of the greenhouse. On stems and supports. Between crates or in corners of structures. Its activity generally increases: Near sunset. During the first hours of darkness. Before dawn. Under low-light conditions. When temperature is moderate. When plants are shaken during the day, moths may fly a short distance and then quickly return to hiding within the canopy. This behavior explains why few moths may be observed visually despite high numbers being captured in traps.

The adult insect uses its wings for: Moving among plants within the field. Searching for a mating partner. Reaching suitable plants for oviposition. Moving from old fields to newly planted fields. Leaving fields after drying or harvest completion. Colonizing neighboring areas. Dispersal occurs at two levels: Local Dispersal This takes place through flight among: Plants. Planting rows. Adjacent fields. Nearby nurseries and greenhouses. Old and newly planted fields. Long-Distance Dispersal This is more strongly associated with human activity, such as: Transport of fruits. Transport of transplants. Packages and crates. Vehicles. Sorting residues. Markets and packing stations. Wind may assist moth movement, but it does not by itself explain rapid spread among countries and continents.

The unmated female releases a volatile sex pheromone from glands near the tip of the abdomen. The pheromone disperses in the air and is detected by the male through sensory receptors on the antennae. The male then moves toward the odor source through: Zigzag flight. Continuous correction of direction. Approaching the plant. Local searching for the female. Mating usually occurs during darkness or under low-light conditions. The mating process includes: 1. Female pheromone release. 2. Arrival of the male. 3. Courtship and tactile behavior. 4. Connection of the abdominal tips. 5. Transfer of sperm and associated substances. 6. Separation of the pair. 7. Female oviposition after the pre-oviposition period. The female may mate more than once, and multiple mating can increase reproductive output under some conditions. [3]

The period between female emergence and the beginning of egg laying is known as the: Pre-oviposition Period During this period: The body hardens. Sexual maturation is completed. Mating occurs. Part of the egg complement matures. The female searches for a suitable host plant. This period generally lasts one or several days depending on: Temperature. Whether mating occurs. Age of male and female. Nutritional condition. Plant cultivar. Light. Humidity. Exposure to stress. The female then begins distributing eggs among plant parts, with a high proportion of oviposition occurring during the first days of adult life.

The female does not lay the same number of eggs each day. The usual pattern is: Low during the pre-oviposition period. High during the first days after mating. Reaching a peak during the first few days. Gradually declining with age. Continuing at low levels until near the end of female life. Field studies showed that females may lay approximately: 68–72% of their total eggs during the first five days. In another behavioral study, oviposition peaked after the second and third days, and approximately: 72% of eggs were laid during the first seven days. This rapid pattern means that the entry of a limited number of mated females into a recently planted field can result in the distribution of a large number of eggs before larval infestation is noticed.

The female of Tuta absoluta has a high reproductive capacity and can lay a large number of eggs on the aerial parts of tomato plants during its lifetime. The commonly reported maximum in many references is approximately: 260 eggs per female. Under favorable environmental and nutritional conditions, fecundity in some studies may rise to: 250–300 eggs per female. This does not mean that every female lays 260 or 300 eggs. These values represent maximum reproductive capacity or high values recorded under suitable conditions.
