The actual mean number laid by a female is usually lower and may range from tens of eggs to more than 200 depending on conditions. Fecundity is affected by: Temperature. Relative humidity. Host-plant species. Tomato cultivar. Nutritional value of the tissues consumed by the larva. Pupal size and weight. Female size and weight. Female age. Male age and reproductive condition. Occurrence and frequency of mating. Access of the adult to water or a sugar source. Exposure to insecticides or heat stress. Duration of the pre-oviposition and oviposition periods. The female lays most of its eggs during the first days after mating. It may lay approximately 68–76% of the total egg complement during the first five to seven days, after which the daily oviposition rate gradually declines with age. High fecundity, which may reach 260 eggs and may approach 300 eggs per female under optimum conditions, is one of the main reasons for the rapid multiplication of Tuta absoluta and the shift of infestation from a low level to a severe outbreak within a short period. The severity of the pest population does not depend on egg number alone, but also on: Percentage of fertilized eggs. Hatching percentage. Natural egg mortality. Rate of parasitism and predation. Larval survival. Number of successive generations. Overlap of pest stages within the field. Three terms should therefore be distinguished: Term Meaning Mean fecundity Mean number of eggs laid by a group of females Individual fecundity Total number of eggs laid by one female Maximum fecundity Highest number recorded for a female under the study conditions The reproductive capacity of Tuta absoluta can therefore be expressed scientifically as follows: > A female lays a variable number of eggs. Maximum fecundity may reach approximately 260 eggs, while some references report a high range of 250–300 eggs per female under favorable conditions.

There is no single fixed lifespan for the adult moth of Tuta absoluta. Recorded male and female longevity varies according to study conditions. Under natural conditions: Males lived approximately 3.4–6.1 days. Females lived approximately 7.7–9.8 days. In experiments on different tomato cultivars: Adult longevity generally ranged around 10–16 days. Some females lived for approximately 16 days or more. Under cool laboratory conditions, adult longevity increased. One study recorded approximately: 40 days at 10°C. 16 days at 19°C. Longer life at low temperature does not mean higher reproduction because activity, mating, and oviposition rates decline as temperature decreases. Females live longer than males in many studies, but the result varies with food, temperature, mating, and strain.

sectionTitle: Effect of Temperature on the Moth
text: Temperature affects: Activity. Flight. Mate searching. Pheromone release. Pheromone response. Mating. Egg laying. Longevity. Fecundity. Survival. Demographic models indicate that a range close to: 21–23°C Provides favorable conditions for survival of immature stages and female fecundity. The optimum temperature for oviposition has been estimated at approximately: 22.8°C. Females can lay eggs over a wider thermal range, but reproductive performance is not equal at all temperatures. Low Temperature It leads to: Reduced activity and flight. Delayed mating. Lower daily oviposition rate. Increased adult longevity. Slower population growth. Moderately High Temperature It leads to: Increased metabolic activity. Faster mating and oviposition. Shorter lifespan. Faster generation development. Extreme Heat Exposure to very high temperatures leads to: Reduced survival. Shortened adult lifespan. Disrupted fecundity. Reduced sperm efficiency. Reduced egg laying. Increased oxidative stress. A heat-stress study showed that exposure to: 40°C for more than three hours Significantly reduced adult survival.

Dense foliage creates a more humid microclimate with less exposure to sunlight and wind. Moths use this environment as a daytime refuge. Severe desiccation leads to: Increased water loss. Shortened adult lifespan. Reduced activity. Shorter oviposition period. Movement of moths into shaded sites. Increased mortality when combined with high temperature. High humidity does not always mean higher moth abundance because humidity interacts with temperature, rainfall, ventilation, and entomopathogenic diseases.

sectionTitle: Effect of Light and Ultraviolet Radiation
text: Moth behavior is affected by the alternation of light and darkness. Reproductive and locomotor activity increase during darkness or low-light periods. Ultraviolet radiation also affects insect physiology. Recent experiments showed that UV exposure reduced: Adult longevity. Female egg-laying capacity under severe exposure. Activity of some antioxidant enzymes. However, laboratory results based on concentrated radiation sources cannot be converted directly into field doses because natural exposure varies according to: Canopy cover. Time of day. Season. Glass or plastic used in greenhouses. Moth hiding site.

Wind affects moth movement in two ways: It may assist passive dispersal under some conditions. It may interfere with flight and female location when strong. Wind may push moths toward particular field edges or sectors, sometimes explaining uneven trap catches. Heavy rainfall may: Temporarily reduce activity. Knock some moths down. Interfere with pheromone release and dispersal. Drive insects into hiding. Reduce flight during wet periods. However, rainfall does not eliminate the pest population because eggs, larvae, and pupae occur in protected sites.

The adult insect is exposed to insecticides mainly through: Contact with spray droplets. Walking over treated surfaces. Inhalation of some volatile materials. Feeding on contaminated sources. Exposure to insecticide residues on leaves. Moths may die after treatment, but targeting adults alone is insufficient because the field generally contains: Eggs. Larvae inside mines. Pupae. New moths immigrating from outside. A female can lay a large proportion of its eggs during the first days of its life. Therefore, it may lay eggs before dying if insecticidal action is delayed. The efficacy of insecticides against moths is affected by: Active ingredient. Speed of action. Coverage. Timing relative to adult activity. Age of the insect. Resistance. Temperature. Exposure to sunlight. Repeated use of the same IRAC group.

sectionTitle: Natural Enemies of the Adult Insect
text: Adult moths are preyed upon by generalist predators such as: Spiders. Some predatory bugs. Nocturnal predatory insects. Small birds under some conditions. Organisms feeding on moths near the soil surface. Adult insects may also become infected with some entomopathogenic fungi when humidity and contact are sufficient. However, the adult stage is mobile and capable of flight, so its exposure to natural enemies differs from the relatively stationary egg and larval stages.

sectionTitle: Distinguishing the Adult from Similar Moths
text: Color and size alone cannot be used to identify Tuta absoluta because it is externally similar to other small moths. Species or groups with which it may be confused include: Phthorimaea operculella. Keiferia lycopersicella. Symmetrischema spp. Other species in the family Gelechiidae. Difference from Potato Tuber Moth Both species: Are small. Are gray or brown. Have narrow wings. Belong to the genus Phthorimaea under modern classification. Are associated with the family Solanaceae. Scale and spot patterns may differ, but reliable diagnosis depends on: Genitalia. Host and type of damage. Species-specific pheromone trap. Molecular analysis. Difference from Tomato Pinworm The two insects are similar in: Size. Color. Wing shape. Infestation of tomato. Membership in Gelechiidae. Therefore, separation requires diagnostic expertise and examination of the genitalia.

Pheromone traps simulate the sexual odor released by the female. The trap primarily attracts: Male Tuta absoluta. They are used for: Early detection. Determining the beginning of moth activity. Monitoring population trends. Identifying periods of increase and decline. Comparing different field areas. Supporting intervention decisions. Evaluating control programs. Mass trapping when used at suitable densities. However, the number of captured males does not equal: Number of females. Number of eggs. Number of larvae. Percentage of infested fruits. Trap catches may be low while larvae remain inside plants, or high because males are immigrating from a neighboring field. Therefore, traps must be interpreted together with: Egg inspection. Recent mines. Live larvae. Infested growing points. Fruit infestation. Condition of neighboring fields.

Mating disruption depends on releasing a quantity of synthetic pheromone throughout the production area so that the air becomes saturated with multiple chemical signals. This makes it difficult for: Males to locate females. Males to reach females. Mating to occur. Fertilized eggs to be produced. This method performs better when: Applied early. Initial pest density is low. A continuous area is treated. The greenhouse is well sealed or protected. Immigration of mated females from outside is prevented. Dispenser density is maintained. No severe neighboring outbreaks are present. Mating disruption does not kill eggs or larvae already present and must therefore be integrated with other components of integrated pest management.

Mass trapping increases trap density in order to remove a large proportion of males. Reducing male numbers decreases mating probability, particularly when the initial pest density is low. Its efficacy declines when: Infestation is severe. Mated females immigrate from outside. Old fields are nearby. Trap numbers are insufficient. The lure has expired. Traps are not maintained. Distribution is uneven. Traps are not a substitute for residue removal, plant inspection, biological control, and selective chemical control.

Adult insects are collected from: Pheromone traps. Plants. Greenhouse walls. Packages. Packing stations. Pupae reared until adult emergence. When collecting a specimen for diagnosis: 1. Do not hold the moth by the wings. 2. Use a fine brush or soft forceps. 3. Keep the specimen away from moisture. 4. Prevent several moths from rubbing against one another. 5. Record trap data, location, and date. 6. Record the host plant. 7. Preserve the specimen according to morphological or molecular examination requirements. Moths in sticky traps may be damaged by: Adhesive material. Loss of scales. Breakage of legs. Sticking of wings. Body decomposition when traps are left for long periods. An adult reared from a larva or pupa inside a clean container is preferable when genital examination is required.

External morphology is examined with a hand lens or stereomicroscope while recording: Body length. Forewing length. Wing color. Number and distribution of dark spots. Shape of the labial palpi. Shape of the antennae. Fringed wing margins. Abdominal form. Condition of scales. Host or collection site. A match with a single characteristic is not sufficient to confirm the species, especially in areas where related solanaceous moths occur.

sectionTitle: Applied Importance of the Adult Insect
text: The importance of the moth in integrated pest management is represented by the following: Early Warning Males appear in traps before severe damage is observed, allowing inspection intensity to increase. Identifying the Source of Immigration A sudden increase at field edges may reveal movement of moths from a nearby old field. Predicting Egg Laying Increased male activity in the presence of females indicates the possibility of a new wave of eggs and larvae. Evaluating Agricultural Sanitation Continued trap catches after harvest indicate continued moth emergence from pupae or immigration from residues and neighboring fields. Mating Disruption The moth is the direct target of pheromone dispensers. Mass Trapping Traps reduce male numbers when used at suitable densities. Preventing Spread Cleaning packages, vehicles, and packing stations helps prevent movement of moths and pupae into new areas.

sectionTitle: Common Errors in Interpreting the Adult Stage
text: Error One: The Moth Is the Stage That Bores into the Fruit The larva mines plant tissues, whereas the moth lays eggs. Error Two: Failure to See Moths Means There Is No Infestation Moths hide during the day, and larvae may remain inside the plant even when moth activity is low. Error Three: Every Small Gray Moth Is Tuta Similar species occur, and the moth must be linked to damage, pheromone response, and diagnostic examination. Error Four: Trap Catch Equals the Number of Insects per Feddan The trap measures male activity around it and is affected by location, wind, lure performance, and plant density. Error Five: Low Trap Catch Means Complete Control Success Male catches may decline while eggs, larvae, and pupae remain in the field. Error Six: Traps Attract Mated Females Conventional sex-pheromone traps primarily attract males and do not remove females present in the field. Error Seven: Replacing the Lure Alone Is Sufficient When the Trap Is Full The trap also requires cleaning or replacement of the adhesive surface and adjustment of height and location.

sectionTitle: Reference Table for the Adult Insect
text: Characteristic Description Name Tuta absoluta moth Stage causing direct damage? No Function Mating, egg laying, and dispersal Body length Usually approximately 6–7 mm Forewing length Approximately 4.2–5.0 mm Wingspan Approximately 8–12 mm Color Silvery gray to grayish brown Forewings Narrow and mottled with dark spots Hindwings Pale with long marginal fringes Antennae Long and filiform Labial palpi Long and upturned Legs Slender and banded with pale and dark scales Resting position Wings folded over the body Activity Mainly crepuscular and nocturnal Larger sex Female on average Pheromone source Female Sex captured by pheromone Mainly male Peak oviposition First days after mating Common field longevity Several days to approximately two weeks Principal monitoring method Pheromone traps Confirmatory diagnosis Genitalia or molecular analysis

The adult of Tuta absoluta is a small moth whose body length is usually approximately 6–7 mm and whose forewing length ranges from 4.2 to 5.0 mm. Its coloration is silvery gray or grayish brown, with dark and pale scales and small black spots on the forewings. The hindwings are lighter and bear long marginal fringes. The moth has long filiform antennae, upturned labial palpi, and three pairs of slender legs. It folds the wings over the body at rest, giving it a long, narrow form that assists camouflage. Females are larger and fuller than males on average, but accurate sex determination cannot be made from size alone. Confirmatory diagnosis of sex and species depends on the abdominal tip, genitalia, and microscopic or molecular examination. Moths are most active during evening and night and hide during the day within foliage, crop residues, and shaded sites. The female releases a sex pheromone that attracts males. This is the scientific basis of pheromone traps, mating disruption, and mass trapping. The female lays the largest proportion of its eggs during the first days after mating, and its lifetime egg production may range from tens of eggs to more than 200 depending on conditions. The moth causes no direct damage to the plant, but its economic importance is substantial because it moves infestation among plants and fields and produces the next generation of mining larvae. The integrated pest-management program therefore targets the adult stage through: Pheromone traps. Mating disruption. Mass trapping. Removal of crop residues. Prevention of moth migration from old fields. Cleaning packages and packing stations. Linking trap catches to inspection of eggs, larvae, and fruits. References

Tuta absoluta undergoes complete metamorphosis, and its life cycle therefore consists of four principal successive stages: 1. Egg. 2. Larva, comprising four larval instars. 3. Pupa. 4. Adult insect or moth. The cycle begins when the mated female lays eggs on the tomato plant. The eggs then hatch, and the larvae enter plant tissues and feed within leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits. After completing the fourth larval instar, the larva stops feeding and transforms into a pupa, from which an adult moth emerges that is capable of mating and producing a new generation. The life cycle of Tuta absoluta is characterized by three principal features: Short generation duration under suitable warm conditions. Ability to produce numerous overlapping generations during the year. Continued reproduction without obligatory diapause as long as food and suitable temperatures are available. For this reason, the same field may simultaneously contain: Newly laid eggs. Eggs approaching hatching. Larvae of all four instars. Pupae. Adult moths. This overlap makes a single control treatment insufficient because each stage differs in its location and susceptibility to control measures.

The life cycle begins after adult moths emerge from pupae. A newly emerged moth requires a short period to: Expand its wings. Harden its body. Complete maturation of its reproductive organs. Begin locomotor activity. Search for a mating partner. Moths are mainly active during the evening and night, while they hide during the day within the canopy, beneath leaves, and in shaded locations. The female releases a sex pheromone that attracts males. After mating, the female begins laying eggs following a pre-oviposition period that averages approximately two days under suitable warm conditions and may extend beyond one week under cold conditions. The female lays eggs singly or in small groups on the aerial parts of the plant, particularly on young leaves. The commonly reported maximum fecundity is approximately: 260 eggs per female And under some studies and favorable conditions may reach: 250–300 eggs per female. However, the actual mean is lower than the maximum and varies according to temperature, host plant, female size, nutrition, and mating history.

sectionTitle: Definition of Developmental Duration and Generation Time
text: The terms developmental cycle and generation time are sometimes confused, although they differ. Development from Egg to Adult This is the period beginning with oviposition and ending with adult moth emergence from the pupa. It includes: Egg incubation. Four larval instars. Pupal stage. It is referred to as the: Egg-to-adult developmental period Biological Generation Time This is the period from the laying of an egg in one generation until the resulting female begins laying eggs of the next generation. It includes: Egg duration. Larval duration. Pupal duration. Pre-oviposition period. Generation time is therefore slightly longer than the period from egg to adult emergence. Adult Longevity The entire adult lifespan is not included in calculating the beginning of the next generation because the female begins laying eggs long before it dies. The female may continue laying eggs for several days after the next generation has begun. Generations therefore overlap rather than one complete cohort ending before the next begins.

sectionTitle: General Sequence of the Life Cycle
text: Under suitable warm conditions, the cycle can be summarized as follows: Stage Usual Approximate Duration Egg 4–6 days Larva, including four instars 10–15 days Pupa 6–10 days Pre-oviposition period 1–3 days Complete generation from egg to egg Approximately 24–35 days These durations are not fixed. The generation may be shortened to approximately 20 days under particular thermal conditions, while it may extend beyond 60 days during winter and cold periods.

sectionTitle: Duration of Development at Different Temperatures
text: Temperature largely controls the rate of development of all stages of Tuta absoluta. A study conducted under constant temperatures recorded the following durations: Temperature Egg Larva Pupa Egg to Adult Emergence ----------: --: ----: ---: ---------------------: 10°C 24.4 days 56.4 days 36.8 days 115.4 days 15°C 11.9 days 34.1 days 18.4 days 63.9 days 20°C 6.9 days 17.8 days 10.2 days 34.8 days 25°C 4.5 days 11.0 days 8.4 days 23.5 days 30°C 2.5 days 10.4 days 5.4 days 18.3 days These results show that increasing temperature within the range tolerated by the insect greatly accelerates development. Development declined from: 115.4 days at 10°C To: 23.5 days at 25°C And then to: 18.3 days at 30°C. However, developmental speed alone does not determine temperature suitability. Development may be completed rapidly at high temperature while survival, fecundity, and hatching success decline.

sectionTitle: Actual Generation Time at Different Temperatures
text: When the pre-oviposition period is added to the developmental period from egg to adult, approximate generation time becomes: Temperature Egg to Adult Pre-Oviposition Period Approximate Generation Time 10°C 115.4 days 9.5 days 124.9 days 15°C 63.9 days 3.3 days 67.2 days 20°C 34.8 days 2.0 days 36.8 days 25°C 23.5 days 2.3 days 25.8 days 30°C 18.3 days 1.9 days 20.2 days These values show that the generation can be completed from egg to egg in approximately: 26 days at 25°C. At 10°C, it may require more than four months. Nevertheless, in the same study, 30°C was not the most favorable temperature for reproduction despite producing the shortest generation time because female fecundity and egg viability declined sharply.

sectionTitle: The Fastest Temperature Is Not Always the Optimum
text: A distinction must be made between: The temperature producing the fastest growth. The temperature producing the highest survival. The temperature producing the highest fecundity. The temperature producing the fastest population increase. In one study, the insect completed development from egg to adult in 18.3 days at 30°C compared with 23.5 days at 25°C. However, at 30°C the study recorded: A major reduction in female fecundity. A severe decline in egg-hatching percentage. Shorter adult longevity. A negative effect on overall survival. At 20–25°C, the following occurred together: Suitable developmental rate. High survival of eggs, larvae, and pupae. High fecundity. High proportion of fertilized eggs capable of hatching. Better continuity of the next generation. Therefore, 30°C is not an absolute optimum even if developmental duration is shorter at that temperature.

The most suitable and stable thermal range for development and reproduction of Tuta absoluta is approximately: 20–25°C. Recent demographic models estimated the optimum range for survival of immature stages and female fecundity at approximately: 21–23°C. A temperature of 25°C is highly suitable for generation development. One study recorded at this temperature: Egg incubation: 4.5 days. Larval duration: 11 days. Pupal duration: 8.4 days. Egg-to-adult development: 23.5 days. Pre-oviposition period: 2.3 days. Mean fecundity: approximately 149 eggs per female. Egg-hatching percentage: approximately 81%. Larval survival: approximately 81%. Pupal survival: approximately 84%. Values vary among populations, strains, and cultivars, but most studies indicate that moderately warm temperatures provide the best balance among development, survival, and reproduction.

Cold conditions slow all growth and reproductive processes. As temperature decreases: Egg incubation becomes longer. Larval instars become longer. Pupal duration increases. Adult mating is delayed. The pre-oviposition period becomes longer. Daily egg-laying rate decreases. Moth movement declines. The number of annual generations decreases. Reduced activity does not mean disappearance of the pest. Some stages can survive during winter, particularly: Pupae in soil and crop residues. Eggs on plants. Moths in protected cultivation. Larvae inside mines and fruits. Stages occurring on persistent solanaceous plants. The life cycle may extend in winter to: More than 60 days And may exceed 90 days during very cold periods.

High temperature accelerates development up to a certain point, after which heat-stress effects begin to appear. Continuous extreme heat may cause: Reduced egg hatching. Increased mortality of newly hatched larvae. Failure of some molts. Pupal mortality. Emergence of deformed moths. Shorter adult lifespan. Reduced fecundity. Reduced sperm efficiency. Lower survival of the next generation. Air temperature alone does not represent the temperature experienced by the pest. A larva inside a mine occupies a different microclimate, and dense foliage, irrigation, and greenhouses provide milder conditions than the surrounding atmosphere. Infestation may therefore continue during periods of high temperature, especially within: Dense canopy. Leaf mines. Fruits. Surface soil layer. Greenhouses and shaded locations.

Insect development depends not only on the number of days, but also on the amount of heat accumulated above the lower developmental threshold. The lower thermal threshold for the egg-to-adult cycle has been estimated at approximately: 8°C. The heat required to complete development from egg to adult has been estimated at approximately: 416.7 accumulated heat units — Degree-Days. The upper thermal threshold for development has been estimated at approximately: 37.3°C. Daily heat accumulation can be calculated in a simplified form as follows: Daily degree-days = Mean daily temperature − 8 When the mean temperature is above 8°C. Example at a Mean Temperature of 25°C Daily heat units: 25 − 8 = 17 degree-days. Theoretical duration required to complete development: 416.7 ÷ 17 = approximately 24.5 days. This result is close to the experimentally recorded egg-to-adult duration at 25°C. The complete pre-oviposition period is not included in the egg-to-adult thermal constant, so generation time from egg to egg is slightly longer.

Tuta absoluta larvae do not enter obligatory diapause as long as food is available and conditions permit development. This means that the insect does not require a complete seasonal cessation of growth at a fixed time. When the following are available: Living tomato plants. Suitable solanaceous hosts. Temperatures permitting development. Protected sites. The stages can continue developing and reproducing during long periods of the year. This is particularly important in: Heated greenhouses. Warm regions. Overlapping planting seasons. Nurseries. Volunteer plants. Fields in which residues have not been removed. Regions where tomato is cultivated year-round.

Not all Tuta absoluta individuals in a field develop at the same time. Some females may enter the field earlier than others, egg laying continues for several days, and developmental rate varies according to: Position on the plant. Local temperature. Time of oviposition. Food quality. Tomato cultivar. Exposure to insecticides. Parasitism and predation. Generations therefore overlap, making it difficult to distinguish first, second, and third generations through field inspection alone. A single plant may simultaneously carry: A newly laid egg. A first-instar larva. A third-instar larva. A pupa inside a folded leaf. An adult moth hidden among leaves. This overlap is one of the reasons a continuous management program is required rather than a treatment directed at a single day.

Tuta absoluta is a multivoltine insect: Multivoltine insect Meaning that it can produce several generations during the year. Under suitable conditions with continuous availability of the host, it may produce: 10–12 generations annually. Some references estimate: 9–10 generations in field tomatoes. Up to 12 generations in greenhouses and continuous production systems. Fewer generations in cold regions or areas with short growing seasons. Recording 12 generations does not mean that every generation lasts a fixed 30.4 days. Summer generations are short, winter generations are longer, and generations overlap.

The expected number of generations in Egypt varies according to region, temperature, planting season, and calculation method. One study based on temperatures recorded during 2012 estimated generation numbers as follows: Governorate Mean Annual Temperature Mean Generation Duration Annual Number of Generations Marsa Matruh 20.53°C 59.6 days Approximately 7 generations Giza 22.8°C 52.44 days Approximately 8.05 generations Qena 26.04°C 43.74 days Approximately 9.52 generations These values show that warmer regions can produce more generations during the year. Other models using different degree-day values predicted: 11–13 generations under some Egyptian field conditions. These estimates do not necessarily conflict directly because they differ in: Definition of generation beginning and end. Thermal threshold used. Thermal constant. Type of climatic data. Region and year. Use of monthly means or daily temperatures. Inclusion or exclusion of the adult stage. Year-round host availability. There is therefore no single fixed number applicable to every Egyptian governorate and every year.

sectionTitle: Number of Generations Within One Tomato Crop
text: The number of generations occurring within a single crop depends on: Duration of the crop. Time of pest entry. Temperature. Presence of nearby infested fields. Success of control measures. Persistence of the field after final harvest. In a tomato crop lasting 120 days, with a mean generation time of 25–30 days, it is theoretically possible for: Four to five generations To occur. However, infestation may not begin on transplanting day, and moths may enter midway through the season. The actual number may therefore be lower or more strongly overlapping. During winter, a generation may require 50–80 days, so only one or two complete generations may occur within the crop. Nevertheless, prolonged crop duration allows larvae and different stages to accumulate.

Tuta absoluta succeeds across a wide humidity range, but survival and reproduction are favorable under moderate relative humidity. The practical suitable range in many studies and laboratory-rearing systems is approximately: 50–70% relative humidity. Moderate humidity helps: Reduce egg desiccation. Improve survival of newly hatched larvae. Protect pupae from water loss. Increase adult longevity. Maintain tender plant tissues. Improve activity of some stages. A study conducted at 20.5°C and 55% relative humidity recorded: High egg hatching. Good larval survival. High adult emergence. Greater fecundity than under conditions of 32°C and 40% relative humidity. Humidity alone does not determine performance because it interacts with temperature. Reduced survival at low humidity may also result from high temperature rather than desiccation alone.

sectionTitle: Effect of High and Low Humidity
text: Low Humidity It may cause: Egg desiccation. Reduced hatching. Mortality of exposed newly hatched larvae. Desiccation of surface pupae. Shorter adult lifespan. Shorter oviposition period. Very High Humidity It may cause: Increased fungal growth. Damage to some eggs and pupae. Increased rots within infested tissues. Changes in activity of entomopathogens. Reduced soil aeration when combined with water saturation. Moderate humidity with good ventilation remains more suitable than severe dryness or continuous wetness.

sectionTitle: Effect of the Host Plant on Generation Duration
text: Tomato is the most suitable host for the development and reproduction of Tuta absoluta, but developmental rate varies among tomato cultivars. Generation performance is affected by plant characteristics including: Leaf thickness. Epidermal toughness. Trichome density. Glandular trichomes. Nitrogen content. Defensive compounds. Leaf age. Plant water status. Balance between vegetative and reproductive growth. Egg-to-adult developmental time may vary by several days among cultivars. Host quality also affects: Pupal weight. Female size. Fecundity. Survival percentage. Number of eggs. Population growth rate. A short developmental period alone is not sufficient to identify the most suitable host. Generation duration must be studied together with survival and fecundity.

Females prefer to lay eggs on young leaves and plant parts, and larvae begin feeding in tender tissues that are easier to penetrate. Plants with continuous vegetative growth therefore help: Increase oviposition sites. Improve survival of newly hatched larvae. Maintain movement of larvae among leaves and growing points. Provide renewable food. Support successive generations. Excessive nitrogen fertilization may produce tender and dense growth, increasing plant suitability for the pest and providing shaded sites where moths can hide. This does not mean that reducing nitrogen alone controls Tuta, but that balanced fertilization forms part of reducing environmental suitability for infestation.

Overlapping planting seasons are among the most important causes of life-cycle continuity. A single region may simultaneously contain: New nurseries. Recently transplanted fields. Vegetatively growing fields. Fruiting fields. Fields in which harvest has ended. Crop residues that have not been removed. When an old field completes harvest, moths emerge from it and move to younger fields. This situation forms a continuous green bridge that allows the pest to complete generations without interruption. Risk increases when: Spraying stops in the old field. Plants remain alive after the final harvest. Residues are not shredded. Young fields are located nearby. Wind movement is directed toward the new field.

The highest reproductive and population-growth capacity occurs when the following conditions are combined: Factor Suitable Condition Temperature Approximately 20–25°C Estimated optimum range Approximately 21–23°C Relative humidity Moderate, generally 50–70% Host Actively growing tomato plants Larval food Young leaves and tender tissues Crop continuity Overlapping planting seasons throughout the year Canopy Dense, providing shade and a favorable microclimate Pupation sites Loose soil, residues, and folded leaves Moth activity Evening and night periods Mating Sufficient numbers of males and females Natural mortality factors Low Insecticides Absence or weakness of an effective program Resistance Presence of resistance to the groups used Agricultural sanitation Poor, with infested residues present No single factor alone produces a population explosion. High density results from the combination of suitable temperature, continuous host availability, high fecundity, and weak mortality factors.

A single female can lay up to 260 eggs, and some females may reach 300 eggs. Not all eggs develop into adults because losses occur due to: Lack of fertilization. Embryonic mortality. Predation. Parasitism. Insecticides. Larval mortality. Pupal mortality. Temperature and desiccation. Plant resistance. Even when only a limited proportion of offspring survives, the pest can increase rapidly because of: High fecundity. Short generation time. Overlapping generations. Multiple annual generations. Continuous host availability. If a female lays 260 eggs and only 10% reach the adult stage, this can produce 26 adults from one female. If approximately half are females, a new group of females begins the cycle again.

sectionTitle: Life Cycle in Summer and Winter
text: Summer Cycle It is usually characterized by: Short egg incubation. Rapid larval instars. Short pupal duration. Rapid adult emergence. Short intervals between generations. Increased generation overlap. Rapidly increasing risk of fruit infestation. The generation may be completed in approximately: 20–30 days When temperatures are warm but not severely stressful. Winter Cycle It is characterized by: Slow embryonic growth. Longer larval stage. Longer pupal stage. Reduced moth movement. Delayed mating and oviposition. Fewer generations. The generation may extend to: 60–90 days or more Depending on severity of cold, greenhouse conditions, and the local microclimate within plants. Slow winter development does not mean absence of risk because larvae may remain inside plants for longer periods, and stages may persist until weather improves.

sectionTitle: Life Cycle in Open Fields and Greenhouses
text: Open Fields The cycle is directly affected by: Daily temperature fluctuations. Rainfall. Wind. Solar radiation. Nighttime cold. Soil desiccation. Presence of neighboring fields. The number of generations may decline in regions with cold winters or periods without a tomato crop. Greenhouses Greenhouses generally provide: More stable temperature. Continuous host plants. Protection from rain and wind. Moderate humidity. Numerous hiding and pupation sites. The pest can therefore complete more generations and may reach approximately 12 generations annually in structures where tomato production continues during most of the year.

sectionTitle: Applied Importance of Understanding the Life Cycle
text: Understanding the life cycle allows control measures to be timed rather than applied randomly. Targeting Eggs This prevents larval emergence and produces the best result when using: Egg parasitoids. Predators. Products with ovicidal activity. Early monitoring. Targeting Newly Hatched Larvae L1 and L2 are more susceptible than larger larvae, especially before entering fruits and stems. Targeting Pupae Most foliar sprays do not reach pupae, so they are managed through: Residue removal. Tillage. Greenhouse sanitation. Cleaning of packages. Prevention of neglected-field persistence. Targeting Moths Moths are monitored with pheromone traps and reduced through: Mass trapping. Mating disruption. Prevention of migration from old fields. Removal of moth-emergence sources.

sectionTitle: Use of Traps to Monitor Generations
text: Pheromone traps detect activity of male moths. An increase in trap catches may indicate: Emergence of a new cohort of adults from pupae. Immigration of moths from a neighboring field. Increased mating activity. Expected increase in oviposition. However, the trap alone cannot determine the generation number because generations overlap and moths continue emerging over extended periods. Trap interpretation should be linked with inspection of: Eggs. Recent mines. Larval instars. Pupae. Fruit infestation. Condition of neighboring fields. Accumulated degree-days.

Element Value or Description Type of metamorphosis Complete metamorphosis Number of principal stages Four Number of larval instars Four Suitable egg incubation 4–6 days Suitable larval period 10–15 days Suitable pupal period 6–10 days Pre-oviposition period Approximately 1–3 days under warm conditions Suitable generation duration Approximately 24–35 days Generation at 25°C Approximately 25.8 days from egg to egg Egg to adult at 25°C 23.5 days Egg to adult at 20°C 34.8 days Egg to adult at 15°C 63.9 days Egg to adult at 10°C 115.4 days General optimum temperature 20–25°C Estimated optimum range 21–23°C Suitable relative humidity Approximately 50–70% Lower thermal threshold for the cycle Approximately 8°C Thermal constant Approximately 416.7 degree-days Estimated upper thermal threshold Approximately 37.3°C Suitable annual generations 10–12 generations Generations in open fields Generally 7–10 depending on climate Generations in greenhouses May reach 12 Maximum fecundity Approximately 260 and may reach 300 eggs Obligatory diapause Absent while food and suitable temperature are available Nature of generations Overlapping

Tuta absoluta passes through four stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult. The larval stage comprises four successive instars and is responsible for damage. Development from egg to adult requires approximately 23.5 days at 25°C, while generation time from egg until the beginning of oviposition by the next generation is approximately 25.8 days. Generation duration increases sharply as temperature declines. Egg-to-adult development may require 63.9 days at 15°C and more than 115 days at 10°C. Increasing temperature to 30°C shortens developmental duration but does not guarantee optimal reproduction because extreme temperatures may reduce survival, fecundity, and egg-hatching percentage. The most suitable and stable range for pest development and reproduction is 20–25°C. Optimum conditions for immature-stage survival and female fecundity are estimated at approximately 21–23°C, with moderate relative humidity generally between 50 and 70%. Under suitable conditions, the pest produces approximately 10–12 generations annually. The number in Egypt may range from approximately seven generations in mild coastal regions to more than nine generations in warm regions, while some models predict higher values when host plants and suitable temperatures remain continuously available. Larvae do not enter obligatory diapause as long as food is available. The cycle therefore continues in greenhouses, overlapping crops, and field residues. The pest’s severity results from the combination of high fecundity, which may reach 260–300 eggs per female, short generation time, overlapping stages, and multiple annual generations. Successful integrated management therefore depends on breaking the life cycle at several points, beginning with eggs and young larvae, continuing through pupae in soil and residues, and ending with monitoring adult moths and preventing their movement among fields.

Tuta absoluta is closely associated with plants belonging to the family Solanaceae, particularly tomato. Nevertheless, the female can lay eggs, or the larvae can feed and develop, on a number of other cultivated and wild plants. Not all plants on which the insect has been recorded have the same degree of importance. An adult moth may be found on a plant used only as a resting site; a female may lay an egg on a plant that cannot support larval development; or a larva may begin feeding but die before reaching the pupal stage. Therefore, a plant should not be considered a true complete host merely because of finding: An adult moth on it. A single egg on its leaves. An unconfirmed leaf mine. A young larva that was not followed through pupation. Field damage for which the causative insect was not confirmed. A true host is confirmed when the insect can complete its life cycle on the plant from egg to adult, with the resulting individuals capable of survival and reproduction. Tuta absoluta is classified as a relatively oligophagous insect: Oligophagous insect Most of its true host range is concentrated within the family Solanaceae, especially the genus: Solanum Tomato remains the most preferred and suitable plant for reproduction and population increase.

sectionTitle: Criteria for Classifying a Plant as a Host
text: Classification of a plant as a host of Tuta absoluta depends on a sequence of evidence. Female Acceptance of the Plant This is measured through: Female landing on the plant. Examination of the leaf surface. Oviposition. Preference when more than one choice is available. Oviposition alone is insufficient to prove that the plant is a complete host. Egg Hatching A female may accept a plant for oviposition, but the eggs may fail to hatch because of: Unsuitability of the plant surface. Desiccation. Effects of plant chemicals. Poor adhesion of the egg. Larval Survival Larvae must be able to: Penetrate the tissue. Form mines. Complete all four larval instars. Feed without total mortality. Pupation If a larva begins feeding but dies before pupation, the plant is not considered a complete host. Adult Emergence Emergence of a healthy adult moth provides strong evidence that the plant can support the life cycle. Reproductive Capacity of the Resulting Insect A high-quality host produces: Healthy moths. Fertile females. Fertilized eggs. Offspring capable of initiating a new generation. Repeated Field Infestation Evidence becomes stronger when infestation recurs: On several plants. In more than one field. During more than one season. With several insect stages present.

Plants associated with Tuta absoluta in this chapter are divided into the following categories: 1. Principal host. 2. Confirmed hosts. 3. Alternative hosts. 4. Experimental or poorly suitable hosts. 5. Suspected hosts. 6. Plants with incidental association. 7. Weeds and wild hosts. A “confirmed host” describes the strength of scientific evidence, whereas an “alternative host” describes the ecological role played by the plant in sustaining the pest when tomato is absent. A plant may therefore be scientifically confirmed as a host and simultaneously function ecologically as an alternative host.

Solanum lycopersicum Tomato is the principal and most preferred host of Tuta absoluta. The insect generally achieves on tomato: The highest number of eggs laid. The highest larval survival. The fastest larval development. The highest pupation percentage. The highest adult-emergence percentage. The greatest pupal weight. The highest female fecundity. The fastest rate of population increase. Comparative studies have shown that tomato clearly exceeds potato, eggplant, and black nightshade in oviposition preference and biological performance of the insect. [1, 2] Reasons for Tomato Preference Tomato preference is associated with factors including: Volatile compounds released from the leaves. Chemical composition of the tissues. Suitability of mesophyll tissue for larval feeding. Ease of penetrating the leaf epidermis. Continuous formation of young growth. Water and nutrient content. Evolutionary adaptation of the insect to plants of the genus Solanum. The female uses its antennae and sensory receptors on the legs and palpi to recognize the plant before laying eggs. Infested Plant Parts Larvae can attack most aerial parts, including: Leaves. Growing points. Buds. Leaf petioles. Tender stems. Flowers. Sepals. Green fruits. Ripe fruits. Roots are not a feeding host for the larvae, although pupae may occur in soil surrounding the plant. Volunteer Tomato Plants Volunteer tomato plants arise from: Seeds of fallen fruits. Harvest residues. Sorting piles. Processing-plant wastes. Seeds remaining from the previous season. Under field conditions, these plants behave as highly dangerous weeds because they provide the principal host outside the normal production season. They may carry: Eggs. Larvae. Pupae. Adult moths. Volunteer tomatoes are among the most important components of the green bridge between cropping seasons.
A confirmed host is a plant on which Tuta absoluta has been shown to complete its life cycle, either in the field or under controlled experiments.

Solanum tuberosum Potato is a confirmed host, but it is less preferred and less suitable than tomato. Larvae mainly infest: Leaves. Tender stems. Young growth. Tuta absoluta is not known as a principal pest of potato tubers in the same manner as the potato tuber moth: Phthorimaea operculella The two species must therefore not be confused. In one comparative experiment, mean developmental duration was: 22.2 days on tomato. 24.4 days on potato. Survival was: 84% on tomato. 40% on potato. [3] These results show that potato can support the complete life cycle, but is a lower-quality host than tomato.

Solanum melongena Eggplant is a confirmed host on which the insect can complete development. However, performance is generally lower than on tomato and is characterized by: Lower oviposition. Longer developmental duration. Reduced larval survival. Production of smaller pupae. Lower population-growth rate. In one study, mean developmental duration on eggplant was approximately: 25.9 days And survival was approximately: 24% Compared with 84% on tomato. [3] Low survival does not mean that eggplant is not a host; it means that it is a less suitable host.

Solanum aethiopicum Tuta absoluta has been shown to complete its life cycle on African eggplant. In a comparative experiment: Mean developmental duration was 24.3 days. Survival was 52%. Performance was lower than on tomato but better than on some cultivars of common eggplant in the same experiment. [3]

Solanum muricatum Pepino is a confirmed experimental host on which the insect can: Lay eggs. Complete the larval instars. Pupate. Produce adult moths. Its field importance is greater in regions where it is cultivated near tomato or inside protected production systems. [4]

Solanum nigrum Black nightshade is one of the most important confirmed wild hosts. On this plant, Tuta absoluta can: Lay eggs. Complete the larval instars. Transform into pupae. Produce adults. Form a new generation. Several experiments have demonstrated that the plant can support the complete life cycle, although oviposition preference and overall performance are generally higher on tomato. [1, 5] Its importance is increased because it grows: Inside tomato fields. Along field margins. Around drainage channels and irrigation canals. Near nurseries. Between cropping seasons.

Solanum sarrachoides The insect has been shown to complete its life cycle on this plant in experiments conducted on wild solanaceous species. It is considered an experimentally confirmed host and may contribute to pest persistence in regions where it occurs naturally. [5]

Solanum coagulans This species has been recorded as a confirmed host in host-range studies, and the insect completed development on it. Its field importance depends on: Local distribution. Proximity to tomato. Persistence between cropping seasons. Presence of several living pest stages on the plant. [1]

Nicotiana glauca Tree tobacco is a wild plant in the family Solanaceae. Recent review of host records indicates that Tuta absoluta can complete its life cycle on it. [4] It becomes important when growing: Around greenhouses. Along roads and embankments. In neglected land. Beside tomato fields. During periods when the principal crop is absent.

Solanum indicum or closely related species A confirmed infestation of Tuta absoluta on Indian nightshade was reported in a study published in 2025. This record represents a recent addition to the list of solanaceous plants associated with the pest, but its economic and geographical importance requires broader investigation beyond the initial confirmation of infestation. [6]

An alternative host is a plant used by the insect when tomato is absent or less abundant and that helps the pest survive and move between cropping seasons. An alternative host is usually biologically confirmed but performs an ecological function in sustaining the pest population.

Solanum lycopersicum These are the most dangerous alternative hosts because they combine: Highest suitability. Persistence outside the production season. Absence of a control program. Proximity to the following crop.
Solanum nigrum This is the most important alternative weed because it is a confirmed host and persists inside and around fields.
Solanum tuberosum It acts as an alternative host when potato and tomato planting seasons overlap.
Solanum melongena It functions as an alternative host in long-duration crops, particularly when tomato is absent.
Solanum aethiopicum It may maintain part of the pest population in regions where it is cultivated.
Solanum muricatum It may act as an alternative host within greenhouses or mixed-production areas.
Nicotiana glauca It acts as a wild alternative host, particularly when it remains green during periods without tomato.
Some wild species may function as local pest reservoirs depending on their suitability and distribution.

sectionTitle: Fourth: Experimental or Poorly Suitable Hosts
text: An experimental host is a plant tested in the laboratory or in cages on which oviposition, feeding, or partial development occurred, but whose ability to support a complete life cycle or a persistent field population is limited or inconsistent.

Capsicum annuum and other Capsicum species Oviposition or infestation has been recorded on pepper in some regions, but experimental results have not been consistent. In a study involving 27 crops, the insect was unable to complete its life cycle on the tested Capsicum species. [3] In other experiments: A small number of eggs were laid. Pepper was less preferred than tomato. Larval survival declined. A large proportion of individuals failed to complete development. Pepper is therefore classified as a weak or experimental host rather than a principal host.

Nicotiana tabacum Females can lay some eggs on cultivated tobacco, but preference is low when tomato is available. In one host-choice study, tobacco was excluded from advanced comparison stages because few eggs were laid on it. [2] Its ability to support development varies according to: Cultivar. Pest population. Geographical region. Testing method.
Nicotiana rustica This species has been studied as a potential solanaceous host, but available evidence remains experimental and does not demonstrate broad field importance. [7]

Datura stramonium Jimsonweed appeared in older host lists as an alternative host. However, recent experiments showed that the insect could not complete its life cycle on it, and some studies recorded either no oviposition or high larval mortality. [4, 8] It is therefore classified as an incomplete experimental host rather than a confirmed host.
Datura ferox A recent review reported no oviposition on this plant in the evaluated experiments. There is therefore insufficient evidence to regard it as a true complete host. [4]

Solanum dulcamara This plant has been tested as an alternative solanaceous host, and varying degrees of development occurred on it, but its field importance remains lower than that of tomato, black nightshade, and potato. [7]

Lycium barbarum This plant has been tested in some studies as a potential solanaceous host. Evidence that it can support stable field generations remains limited, and it is therefore classified among experimental plants. [7]

Solanum americanum This species has been included among spontaneous plants tested for development of Tuta absoluta. Its field importance remains limited and variable according to region, and it should not automatically be treated as a confirmed host equivalent to Solanum nigrum. [4]

Solanum viarum Oviposition or partial development occurred on this plant in some studies, but results differ among pest populations. In one host-choice study, it did not produce the expected oviposition response despite appearing in other reports. [2]

Phaseolus vulgaris Bean has appeared in some host lists, and oviposition or development has been recorded in local studies. Other experiments showed that the insect could not complete a stable life cycle on it as it does on plants of the genus Solanum. It is therefore classified as a local or poorly suitable host rather than a general principal host. [1, 3]

Allium cepa In a West African experiment, some larvae began developing on onion leaves but did not reach the pupal stage. Onion therefore represents an example of a plant permitting partial feeding but not supporting the complete life cycle. [3]

Ipomoea purpurea This species has recently been tested among spontaneous plants, although it does not belong to the family Solanaceae. Its importance remains experimental until the following are demonstrated: Completion of the larval instars. Pupation. Adult emergence. Production of a fertile generation. [4]
A suspected host is a plant whose name has appeared in a report or host list but for which sufficient information on completion of the insect’s life cycle is unavailable.

These include: Watermelon Citrullus lanatus It has appeared in some lists without sufficient life-cycle data. Squash and Pumpkin Cucurbita pepo Oviposition was recorded in some experiments, but the insect did not complete its life cycle. [4] Beet Beta vulgaris No oviposition was recorded in some tests, and it is therefore not a confirmed host. [4] Alfalfa Medicago sativa It appeared in older lists, but sufficient life-cycle data are unavailable. Spinach Spinacia oleracea No information confirms completion of the life cycle on this plant. Jatropha Jatropha curcas Its name has appeared in host lists but without sufficient data on hatching, development, and pupation. Spiny Amaranth Amaranthus spinosus There is insufficient information demonstrating support of a complete life cycle. Cocklebur Xanthium strumarium It has been reported in field records, but sufficient information on completion of the life cycle is unavailable. Red Goosefoot Oxybasis rubra It has been recorded as an associated wild plant, but complete developmental information is unavailable. Some Lycium Species Lycium spp. Records exist for some species, but evidence of complete life-cycle development differs among species.
Tuta absoluta may occur on a plant without that plant being a true host.

Adult Resting An adult moth may rest on: Non-solanaceous plants. Trees. Weeds. Supports. Greenhouse fabrics. The presence of an adult does not mean that larvae can feed on the plant. Laying of a Single Egg A female may lay an egg on an unsuitable plant because of: Absence of tomato. High moth density. Confinement in a cage. Contact between plants. Larval Movement A larva may leave its mine and move to a neighboring plant, then begin limited feeding without completing development. Fallen Infested Leaf An infested tomato leaf may fall into another weed, making the larva appear to occur on that weed. Confusion with Other Leaf Miners Mines may be caused by: Liriomyza flies. Larvae of other moths. Other mining insects. A host should therefore not be confirmed from mine appearance alone.

Some weeds are important in sustaining the pest between tomato crops. Host weeds perform the following functions: Providing larval food. Providing oviposition sites. Protecting pupae. Maintaining adult emergence. Forming a green bridge between seasons. Transferring infestation to newly planted fields. However, not all weeds are Tuta hosts, and weeds should not be removed indiscriminately without identification because some non-host plants provide food and shelter for natural enemies.

Black Nightshade Solanum nigrum The most important confirmed weed host of Tuta absoluta. The insect can complete its entire life cycle on it. Hairy Nightshade Solanum sarrachoides An experimentally confirmed wild host. Wild Eggplant Solanum coagulans Recorded as a confirmed host in host-range studies. Tree Tobacco Nicotiana glauca A confirmed wild host in recent reviews and capable of supporting the complete life cycle. Volunteer Tomato Plants Solanum lycopersicum These are not a separate wild species but are treated under field conditions as highly dangerous weeds.

These include plants on which oviposition or partial development occurred, or that were tested without confirmation of broad field importance. Jimsonweed Datura stramonium The insect does not complete its life cycle on this plant according to recent experiments. Long-Spined Thorn Apple Datura ferox No oviposition was recorded in some tests. American Black Nightshade Solanum americanum Experimental results are variable. Tropical Soda Apple Solanum viarum Its acceptance for oviposition and biological performance are inconsistent among studies. Bittersweet Nightshade Solanum dulcamara It has been tested as a potential host but is less important than confirmed hosts. Aztec Tobacco Nicotiana rustica Its association is experimental and does not demonstrate general field importance. Goji Berry Lycium barbarum Further field confirmation is required. Common Morning Glory Ipomoea purpurea Evidence of association is recent and experimental.

Field Bindweed Convolvulus arvensis It has been recorded in Egypt and Turkey as a plant bearing mines or larvae attributed to Tuta absoluta. However, evidence is conflicting because other experiments indicated either absence of oviposition or unsuitability of plants in the family Convolvulaceae for pest development. [9, 10] Field bindweed is therefore classified as: A locally recorded host requiring confirmation of complete life-cycle development. It should not be treated as a confirmed host equivalent to black nightshade. Common Lambsquarters Chenopodium album It has been recorded during some field surveys, but suitability experiments showed that it is unsuitable for completion of pest development. Field records may result from: Forced oviposition. Initial feeding. Larval movement. Confusion with other leaf miners. Silverleaf Nightshade Solanum elaeagnifolium This species belongs to the family Solanaceae and has appeared in weed lists associated with the pest, but evidence that it supports the complete life cycle remains weaker than that available for black nightshade. Chilean Boxthorn Lycium chilense It has been recorded as a wild plant, but complete life-cycle information is unavailable. [4] Red Goosefoot Oxybasis rubra It has appeared in weed lists without sufficient information on completion of the life cycle. [4]

sectionTitle: Ranking of Weeds According to Risk
text: Category Plant Risk Level Confirmed and highly important Volunteer tomato plants Very high Confirmed and highly important Solanum nigrum Very high Confirmed Solanum sarrachoides Locally high Confirmed Solanum coagulans Locally high Confirmed Nicotiana glauca High where widespread Experimental Datura stramonium Low Experimental Datura ferox Very low Experimental Solanum americanum Variable Experimental Solanum viarum Variable Conflicting record Convolvulus arvensis Requires confirmation Unsuitable in experiments Chenopodium album Unconfirmed Record without complete cycle Solanum elaeagnifolium Requires study Suspected Xanthium strumarium Unproven Suspected Amaranthus spinosus Unproven

Priority field inspection in Egypt should focus on: 1. Volunteer tomato plants. 2. Black nightshade, Solanum nigrum. 3. Wild Solanum species. 4. Silverleaf nightshade, Solanum elaeagnifolium. 5. Tree tobacco, Nicotiana glauca, where present. 6. Field bindweed, Convolvulus arvensis. 7. Jimsonweed, Datura stramonium. 8. Long-spined thorn apple, Datura ferox. The highest priority is assigned to volunteer tomato and black nightshade because evidence that they support the complete life cycle is stronger than for most other weeds.

The green bridge refers to the continued presence of living plants that the pest can use between two cropping seasons. The persistence chain develops as follows: 1. Tomato harvest ends. 2. Larvae and pupae remain in crop residues. 3. Adult moths emerge. 4. They move to volunteer tomato, black nightshade, or alternative hosts. 5. Eggs and larvae persist. 6. A new tomato crop is planted. 7. Moths move into the newly planted crop. The risk of a green bridge increases when: Planting dates overlap. Old fields are left after the final harvest. Volunteer plants are not removed. Solanaceous weeds grow around nurseries. Potato and eggplant are grown beside tomato.

sectionTitle: Effect of the Host on Insect Performance
text: Plant species affects: Oviposition preference. Larval duration. Survival percentage. Pupal weight. Generation duration. Adult size. Female fecundity. Population-growth rate. In one comparative study involving several crops: Plant Developmental Duration Survival Percentage Tomato 22.2 days 84% Potato 24.4 days 40% African eggplant 24.3 days 52% Eggplant 25.9 days 24% The results show that the ability to complete the life cycle does not mean that all plants are equal in quality.

The female passes through several stages before oviposition: 1. Detecting plant odors. 2. Moving toward the odor source. 3. Landing on the plant. 4. Examining the leaf surface. 5. Contacting trichomes and chemical compounds. 6. Accepting or rejecting the plant. 7. Laying eggs. Choice studies showed the following general preference order: Tomato > potato > eggplant > black nightshade Biological performance of the immature stages was largely consistent with female preference. [2] However, preference order may vary among regions because of: Differences among pest populations. Host availability. Local cultivars. Climatic conditions. Previous insect experience.

Crop rotation does not successfully reduce Tuta absoluta if tomato is replaced directly by a suitable solanaceous crop such as: Potato. Eggplant. African eggplant. Rotation becomes more effective when it includes: Absence of plants belonging to the genus Solanum. Removal of volunteer tomato. Removal of infested solanaceous weeds. Disposal of crop residues. A host-free period. West African experiments showed that the insect could not complete its life cycle on most crops outside the genus Solanum, supporting the importance of a period during which this genus is absent from the production area. [3]

When a new plant is suspected, the following steps should be followed: 1. Determine the scientific name of the plant. 2. Photograph the plant and its location. 3. Examine for eggs. 4. Search for recent mines. 5. Open the mines. 6. Confirm that the larva is lepidopteran and possesses a head and legs. 7. Exclude Liriomyza larvae. 8. Collect the infested plant part. 9. Rear the larva until pupation. 10. Monitor adult emergence. 11. Confirm the identity of the moth. 12. Repeat the observation on several plants. 13. Evaluate the reproductive capacity of the resulting adult. Emergence of an adult moth from a larva that fed on the plant provides stronger evidence than the presence of eggs or mines alone.

Removal of Volunteer Tomato Plants They should be removed before they permit development of a new generation. Management of Black Nightshade Plants should be inspected inside fields, along margins, and around drainage channels, and infested plants should be removed in a manner that prevents emergence of larvae and adults. Removal of Crop Residues Residues should be shredded, buried, or disposed of away from the new crop. Avoid Leaving Piles of Infested Weeds Larvae may continue developing within removed plants, then pupate and produce adults. Nursery Sanitation Wild solanaceous hosts should be removed from the area surrounding the nursery. Avoid Indiscriminate Removal Not every weed should be removed merely because it is present. Some non-host plants may support: Predators. Parasitoids. Pollinators. Nectar sources. Beneficial non-host plants should be retained when they do not represent a pest source. Timing of Removal Adults emerging from an old host must be prevented from moving into the new crop. Removal should be integrated with: Traps. Monitoring. Shredding of residues. Inspection of neighboring fields. Safe disposal of infested tissues.

Error One: Every Plant Bearing a Mine Is a Host The mine may have been caused by another insect. Error Two: Oviposition Proves Host Status A female may lay eggs on a plant that does not permit completion of the life cycle. Error Three: All Solanaceous Plants Are Equal Tomato, potato, eggplant, pepper, and jimsonweed differ greatly in suitability. Error Four: Every Plant Listed in an Old Host List Is Confirmed Older lists included plants on which only an egg or mine had been recorded. Error Five: Field Bindweed Is Definitively Confirmed as a Host Local records exist, but study results are conflicting, and it should not be treated as a complete host without confirmation. Error Six: Removing Tomato Alone Breaks the Cycle The pest may persist on volunteer tomato, black nightshade, potato, and eggplant. Error Seven: Potato Is Unimportant Potato is a confirmed host, although tomato is more preferred. Error Eight: All Weeds Must Be Removed Removal should focus on confirmed and infested hosts while preserving non-host plants that support natural enemies.

Category Plant Scientific Name Assessment Principal host Tomato Solanum lycopersicum Most preferred and suitable Principal field host Volunteer tomato Solanum lycopersicum Highly dangerous reservoir Confirmed host Potato Solanum tuberosum Complete cycle, lower performance than tomato Confirmed host Eggplant Solanum melongena Complete cycle, lower survival Confirmed host African eggplant Solanum aethiopicum Complete cycle Confirmed host Pepino Solanum muricatum Complete cycle experimentally Confirmed wild host Black nightshade Solanum nigrum Most important host weed Confirmed wild host Hairy nightshade Solanum sarrachoides Experimentally confirmed Confirmed wild host Wild eggplant Solanum coagulans Locally confirmed Confirmed wild host Tree tobacco Nicotiana glauca Complete cycle Recent record Indian nightshade Solanum indicum Confirmed infestation; broader assessment needed Experimental host Pepper Capsicum spp. Weak and inconsistent Experimental host Cultivated tobacco Nicotiana tabacum Low preference Experimental host Jimsonweed Datura stramonium Usually does not complete the cycle Experimental host Long-spined thorn apple Datura ferox No oviposition recorded in tests Experimental host Bittersweet nightshade Solanum dulcamara Limited Experimental host Goji berry Lycium barbarum Not field-confirmed Poorly suitable host Common bean Phaseolus vulgaris Variable results Partial development Onion Allium cepa Feeding without pupation Conflicting record Field bindweed Convolvulus arvensis Requires confirmation Unsuitable Common lambsquarters Chenopodium album Does not complete development Suspected Watermelon Citrullus lanatus No documented complete cycle Suspected Squash and pumpkin Cucurbita pepo Oviposition without complete cycle Suspected Beet Beta vulgaris No confirmed oviposition Suspected Alfalfa Medicago sativa No complete life-cycle data Suspected Spinach Spinacia oleracea No complete life-cycle data Suspected Cocklebur Xanthium strumarium Record without confirmation

Tomato is the principal and most suitable host of Tuta absoluta, on which the insect achieves the highest oviposition preference, fastest development, highest survival, and greatest reproduction. Confirmed hosts include: Potato. Eggplant. African eggplant. Pepino. Black nightshade. Hairy nightshade. Some wild eggplant species. Tree tobacco. Volunteer tomato, black nightshade, potato, eggplant, and tree tobacco function as alternative hosts that help the pest persist between cropping seasons. Pepper, cultivated tobacco, jimsonweed, bean, and several wild plants vary in suitability and should not be treated as confirmed hosts in every region. Suspected hosts include several non-solanaceous plants that appeared in older lists without proof of complete life-cycle development. Solanaceous weeds, especially black nightshade and volunteer tomato, are the most important sources of the green bridge between seasons. Confirmation of a true host depends on completion of the insect’s life cycle, not merely on finding an egg, mine, or larva. Host management depends on: Removing volunteer tomato plants. Managing black nightshade and confirmed wild hosts. Disposing of crop residues. Preventing overlap among solanaceous crops. Inspecting weeds around nurseries and fields. Maintaining a host-free period without plants of the genus Solanum. Avoiding indiscriminate removal of non-host weeds when they support natural enemies. References

Tuta absoluta is one of the most serious insect pests of tomato because its larvae can attack most aerial parts of the plant from the seedling stage until fruit maturity. Damage is not caused by the adult insect or the pupa, but by larvae that mine within plant tissues, feeding between the two leaf epidermal layers or entering growing points, stems, flowers, and fruits. Infestation is particularly serious because larvae remain concealed within plant tissues, where the mines provide partial protection from: Sunlight. Desiccation. Rainfall. Some predators. Insecticides remaining on the external plant surface. Infestation causes losses through two principal pathways: 1. Direct losses resulting from tissue consumption and fruit damage. 2. Indirect losses resulting from pathogen entry, reduced plant efficiency, increased sorting and control costs, and crop rejection. Losses may range from low levels that the plant can compensate for to near-total loss of marketable yield under severe infestation without effective management.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Infestation, Damage, and Loss
text: Three basic terms must be distinguished. Infestation Infestation This means the presence of the pest or any of its stages on the plant, such as: Presence of eggs. Presence of a live larva. Presence of an active mine. Presence of a pupa. Presence of adult moths in traps. Infestation does not necessarily mean that an economic loss has occurred. Damage Damage Damage is the visible or functional change caused by the insect in the plant, such as: Leaf mines. Death of the growing point. Stem boring. Fruit holes. Leaf desiccation. Reduction in green leaf area. Economic Loss Economic Loss Economic loss is the reduction in crop value resulting from infestation and includes: Reduced yield weight. Increased rejected fruit. Lower proportion of marketable yield. Rejection of a shipment or factory delivery. Increased sorting costs. Increased control costs. Reduced harvesting and packing efficiency. Loss of part of the quality or shelf life. Low leaf infestation may occur without a clear economic loss, whereas a single hole in a fresh-market tomato fruit may cause it to be rejected.

The larva is responsible for all direct damage. Damage severity varies according to larval instar: Larval Instar Nature of Damage First instar, L1 Initiation of small, limited mines Second instar, L2 Enlargement of mines and increased tissue consumption Third instar, L3 Broad mines and movement into growing points, stems, and fruits Fourth instar, L4 Highest tissue consumption and most severe damage to fruits and stems Not all larvae are equally important. A young larva may be present without producing extensive damage, whereas an advanced larva may spoil more than one leaf or fruit by leaving one mine and moving to another site.

Infestation may begin in the nursery or immediately after transplanting. The first symptoms commonly appear as: Small eggs on young leaves. A minute penetration point. A small transparent mine. A pale area between the two leaf epidermal layers. Dark frass within the mine. A small larva visible when the leaf is held against the light. Early infestation may pass unnoticed, particularly when: Larval numbers are low. Mines are small. Inspection focuses on old leaves. Symptoms are confused with Liriomyza infestation. Mines are not opened to confirm the presence of larvae. Damage increases as larvae develop and generations accumulate.

Leaves are the plant parts most frequently used for oviposition and the initial feeding of larvae. Recent Leaf Mine A mine begins as: A small, nearly transparent point. A pale spot. A short track. A limited silvery area. The larva feeds on mesophyll tissue between the two leaf epidermal layers, while the outer epidermis remains relatively intact during the early stage of infestation. Advanced Mine As feeding continues, the mine expands and becomes: Irregular in shape. Blotch-like. White or silvery. Gray or brown as it ages. Containing black or brown frass. Frass may accumulate on one side of the mine or appear as dark lines and dots. Old Mine After the larva leaves or dies, the mine becomes: Dry. Brown. Brittle. Empty. Easily torn. Dead tissue may fall away, leaving irregular holes in the leaf. Coalescence of Mines At high pest density, mines formed by several larvae may overlap, converting a large part of the leaf into tissue that is: Transparent. Silvery. Brown. Dead and dry. Coalescence of mines causes loss of a large proportion of the effective green leaf area. Leaf Curling and Desiccation Severe damage may lead to: Curling of leaflets. Drying of leaf margins. Death of parts of the leaf. Leaf fall. Exposure of fruits to sunlight. Curling does not always occur in a regular pattern and may overlap with symptoms of water stress, viruses, or mites.

sectionTitle: Effect of Leaf Infestation on Photosynthesis
text: Larvae feed on mesophyll cells, which contain a large proportion of the chloroplasts responsible for photosynthesis. This results in: Reduced green leaf area. Reduced light interception. Reduced sugar production. Disturbance of gas exchange. Reduced chlorophyll content. Redirection of plant resources toward defensive responses. Reduced root and shoot growth under severe infestation. The effect is not limited to the visible mine area. Physiological and defensive responses may also occur in surrounding tissues. Physiological studies have shown that infestation can reduce: Photosynthetic rate. Stomatal conductance. Water-use efficiency. Vegetative growth. Biomass. The magnitude of the reduction varies according to: Infestation severity. Plant age. Cultivar. Timing of infestation. Nutrition and irrigation. Ability of the plant to produce new leaves.

sectionTitle: Ability of the Plant to Compensate for Leaf Damage
text: Not every level of leaf infestation causes a loss equal to the proportion of affected leaf area. The plant may compensate for part of the damage through: Production of new leaves. Increased activity of healthy leaves. Redistribution of carbohydrates. Continued vegetative growth. Compensatory capacity is greater when: Infestation is early and low. The plant is vigorous. Nutrition and irrigation are balanced. Fruits and growing points are not directly attacked. Pest pressure declines rapidly. Compensatory capacity decreases when: Infestation is severe and persistent. Damage occurs during fruit filling. Leaves close to fruit clusters are damaged. Generations recur without reducing the population. The plant is also affected by other pests or diseases. The plant is exposed to water or salinity stress.

Larvae attack tender growing points and terminal and lateral buds. Symptoms include: Wilting of the terminal bud. Death of the growing point. Presence of dark frass. Presence of silken threads. Young leaves stuck together. Deformation of new growth. Cessation of stem elongation. Emergence of irregular lateral shoots. Growing-point infestation is particularly serious in: Nurseries. Recently transplanted seedlings. Young plants. Determinate cultivars. Death of the growing point in a young plant may cause: Delayed growth. Irregular plant architecture. Reduced number of clusters. Delayed flowering and harvest. Reduced early yield.

Larvae may infest: Flower buds. Flowers. Flower pedicels. Young clusters. Sepals. Symptoms appear as: Minute holes. Dark frass. Silken threads. Bud desiccation. Flower drop. Poor fruit set. Deformation of young fruits. Flowers are not the most common infestation site compared with leaves, but their infestation directly affects the number of fruits formed.

sectionTitle: Symptoms on Leaf Petioles and Stems
text: Larvae enter petioles and tender stems, particularly near: Nodes. Leaf bases. Buds. Cluster–stem junctions. Tender tissues. Signs include: A small entry hole. Frass accumulating at the opening. An internal tunnel. Wilting of the leaf beyond the infestation point. Weakening of the stem. Drying of terminal growth. Breakage of tender tissues. The external tunnel may not be obvious, while wilting or weakening of the affected part becomes visible. In seedlings, infestation of the main stem may cause: Growth cessation. Death of the upper part. Weakening of the seedling. Plant death in severe cases.

Fruit infestation represents the most important form of economic damage because infested fruits lose a large proportion of their fresh-market or processing value. Larval Entry Sites The larva may enter through: The area beneath the calyx. Near the pedicel. Between the sepals. The point where a leaf touches the fruit. The side of the fruit. Wounds or cracks. The point of contact between two fruits. Infestation is common near the calyx because this area provides the larva with protection and a suitable hiding site.
