Appearance of the Entry Hole The entry hole is usually: Small. Circular or irregular. Dark at the margins. Surrounded by frass. Less conspicuous than the internal damage. The hole may be only a few millimeters in diameter while the tunnel extends through a large part of the fruit. Tunnels Inside the Fruit The larva feeds within: The flesh. The region close to the skin. Tissues surrounding the calyx. Internal locules in some cases. Tunnels appear as: Cavities. Brown tissue. Soft areas. Black frass. Internal decay. Exit Hole After completing feeding, the larva may leave the fruit, resulting in: An exit hole. External frass. An empty tunnel. Initial decay around the opening. More than one hole may occur in a single fruit because of movement by one larva or infestation by several larvae.

sectionTitle: Infestation of Green and Ripe Fruits
text: Fruits may be infested as soon as they form. Green Fruits Symptoms include: Minute holes. Brown spots. Subepidermal tunnels. Localized growth arrest. Deformation. Premature fruit drop in some cases. The fruit may continue growing while the damaged area expands or becomes invaded by rots. Colored and Ripe Fruits These are more prone to developing: Softness. Juice leakage. Rots. Abnormal odor. Tissue collapse. Loss of marketability and transportability. Infested fruits are difficult to store even when external damage appears limited.

Direct damage caused by larval feeding includes: Removal of leaf mesophyll. Death of growing points and buds. Boring into petioles and stems. Loss of some flowers. Fruit damage. Reduced marketable yield. Weakening of the plant. Increased leaf and fruit drop. Direct fruit damage represents the largest component of loss because an infested fruit often cannot be sold as premium fresh produce.

Indirect damage results from effects following larval boring. It includes: Entry of rot pathogens. Increased bacterial and fungal infection. Accelerated fruit decomposition. Increased loss during transport. Reduced shelf life. Increased sorting costs. Increased labor requirements. Increased number of control treatments. Greater risk of pesticide residues. Adverse effects on natural enemies due to repeated spraying. Possible rejection of the crop or shipment. The value of indirect damage may exceed the value of the tissue consumed directly by the larva.

Entry and exit holes and tunnels act as entry points for pathogens. Wounds may permit entry of: Soft-rot bacteria. Saprophytic fungi. Fruit-rot pathogens. Microorganisms present on the plant surface or in soil. Secondary infections lead to: Expansion of brown lesions. Fruit softening. Fluid leakage. Offensive odor. Tissue collapse. Spread of rot to contacting fruits. Not all rotted tissue should be attributed to direct larval feeding. Damage may begin with a small opening and then multiply because of microbial activity.

sectionTitle: Effect of Infestation on Plant Architecture
text: Under severe and repeated infestation, plant architecture changes because of: Death of growing points. Leaf loss. Emergence of lateral shoots. Weakening of stems. Irregular cluster distribution. Exposure of fruits to sunlight. Reduced canopy cover. Canopy collapse may lead to: Sunscald. Increased fruit temperature. Irregular coloration. Increased cracking. Reduced protection of fruits from weather conditions. Tuta can therefore cause additional losses even in fruits not directly entered by larvae.

Loss varies according to the crop-growth stage. Nursery Infestation This causes: Weak seedlings. Death of growing points. Transport of the pest into the field. Establishment of early infestation. Increased cost of discarding seedlings. Infestation After Transplanting The plant may compensate for part of the leaf damage, but growing-point infestation may delay growth and reduce early yield. Infestation During Flowering and Fruit Set This affects: Buds. Flowers. Newly set fruits. Leaves responsible for supplying fruit clusters. Infestation During Fruit Filling This is a critical stage because leaf loss reduces carbohydrate supply to fruits, while larvae begin entering fruits. Infestation Before Harvest This may not greatly reduce the number of fruits formed, but it causes: Fruit rejection. Increased rots. Higher sorting losses. Quality deterioration. Spread of larvae within containers.

Fresh tomatoes are sold according to fruit appearance and surface integrity, so sensitivity to fruit infestation is extremely high. A single hole may lead to: Exclusion of the fruit from premium grade. Price reduction. Downgrading. Complete rejection. Increased risk of decay during transport. Losses include: Reduced marketable yield. Increased manual sorting costs. Increased number of rejected packages. Reduced storage life. Increased buyer complaints. Shipment rejection in high-quality markets. The economic threshold for fruit infestation is therefore relatively low, especially for export and premium markets.

Acceptance of processing tomatoes does not depend on external appearance alone as it does in the fresh market, but Tuta infestation remains highly important. Fruit infestation causes: Reduced weight of sound fruits suitable for delivery. Increased rotted fruits. Increased proportion of rejected material. Increased impurities and decomposed tissues. Greater need for sorting and washing. Reduced efficiency of harvesting and transport. Possible rejection of a load when the proportion of infested fruits is high. Two effects must be distinguished: Leaf Damage Alone The plant may compensate for low or moderate leaf infestation, and the presence of some mines does not automatically mean a reduction in °Brix or yield. Fruit Infestation and Canopy Collapse These cause direct losses in processing yield through: Rejection of infested fruits. Entry of rots. Weight loss. Color deterioration. Fruit exposure to sunlight. Irregular ripening. There is no universal fixed numerical relationship between the proportion of leaf mines and °Brix because °Brix is affected by many factors, including cultivar, irrigation, maturity, crop load, and temperature. Assessment of Tuta in processing tomatoes therefore depends mainly on: Percentage of infested fruits. Weight of rejected fruits. Rot percentage. Processable yield. Canopy condition. Timing of infestation relative to harvest.

Losses vary greatly among countries, seasons, and production systems. Reviews and reports indicate that actual losses may range under many conditions between approximately: 11 and 43% While in severe outbreaks without effective management they may reach: 80–100% of marketable yield. A 100% loss does not mean that larvae consumed the entire crop biomass. It may mean that most fruits became: Perforated. Rotted. Unmarketable. Non-compliant with specifications. Uneconomic to harvest and sort. Loss is affected by: Larval density. Timing of pest entry. Season length. Cultivar. Presence of fruit infestation. Control level. Climatic conditions. Presence of natural enemies. Proximity of infested fields. Market or factory acceptance standards.

Infestation Density As the number of active larvae increases, so does the probability of: Coalescence of mines. Growing-point infestation. Movement of larvae into fruits. Accumulation of damage. Larval Instar Third and fourth instars consume more tissue than young instars. Duration of Infestation A low infestation persisting for several weeks may be more serious than a short temporary peak. Crop Stage Plant compensatory capacity varies among growth stages. Cultivar Cultivars differ in: Vegetative growth. Leaf resistance. Trichome density. Fruit weight. Compensatory capacity. Market value. Market Type Loss is greater in crops intended for: Export. Premium markets. Small packages. Sale by individual fruit. Some superficial defects may have less effect in processing, but holes and rots remain unacceptable. Presence of Secondary Diseases Rots multiply the value of damage resulting from mining.

The presence of a mine does not mean that a larva is still alive. Active Mine It is characterized by: Pale or silvery color. Presence of a live larva. Fresh frass. Soft margins. Continued expansion. Larval response when the mine is opened. Old Mine It is characterized by: Brown color. Desiccation. Absence of the larva. Presence of cast skin or old frass. No further expansion. Torn tissue. Control decisions should be based on larvae, eggs, and active infestation rather than on the combined number of old and new mines.

sectionTitle: Distinguishing Tuta from Liriomyza Leaf Miners
text: Characteristic Tuta absoluta Liriomyza Mine shape Usually broad, blotch-like, and irregular Usually winding or serpentine Larva Distinct head and legs Maggot-like and without visible legs Frass Dark aggregations within the mine Usually a fine, discontinuous line Growing-point and stem infestation Common Rare Fruit infestation Possible and important Not the usual symptom Adult insect Small moth Small fly Pupa On the plant or in soil Usually outside the mine or in soil, depending on species Mines may resemble one another when several infestations coalesce. Diagnosis must therefore depend on examination of the larva.

Characteristic Tuta absoluta Tomato Fruitworm, Helicoverpa armigera Larval size Small, generally less than 10 mm Large and may exceed 30 mm Leaf damage Internal mines Exposed feeding Fruit opening Usually small and inconspicuous Large and obvious Frass Inside the mine or around the opening Large masses outside the fruit Feeding site Inside leaves and fruits Exposed or inside fruits Leaf symptom Blotch mine Holes and edge feeding

sectionTitle: Distinguishing Tuta Damage from Leaf Diseases
text: Old mines may be confused with: Early blight. Late blight. Bacterial spots. Pesticide burns. Sunscald. Mechanical injury. A Tuta mine is usually characterized by: A thin epidermis above the cavity. Internal frass. A larva or cast skin. An entry or exit point. Irregular boundaries following feeding within the tissue. Disease lesions develop within the tissue without a larva or insect frass.

The percentage of infested plants is calculated as: Infested plants (%) = Number of plants carrying active infestation ÷ Total number of plants examined × 100 Example: If 100 plants are examined and active infestation is found on 18 plants: Infestation percentage = 18 ÷ 100 × 100 = 18% The definition of an infested plant must be specified, such as: Presence of eggs. Presence of an active mine. Presence of a live larva. Presence of an infested fruit. Different indicators should not be combined into one number without a clear definition.

The following should be recorded for each plant: Number of leaves examined. Number of leaves bearing active mines. Number of active mines. Number of live larvae. Position of infestation on the plant. The percentage of infested leaves is calculated as: Infested leaves (%) = Number of infested leaves ÷ Number of leaves examined × 100 This indicator alone does not describe the volume of damage. One leaf may carry a small mine while another carries several broad mines. The following should therefore be recorded with it: Number of mines per leaf. Damaged area. Number of live larvae.

This is calculated as: Infested growing points (%) = Number of plants with infested growing points ÷ Number of plants examined × 100 The type of infestation should be recorded: Active growing point containing a larva. Old dry growing point. Dead growing point. Lateral growth resulting from death of the growing point. Growing-point infestation is more important in young plants than in large plants.

The percentage of infested fruits is one of the most important economic indicators. It is calculated as: Infested fruits (%) = Number of fruits carrying Tuta infestation ÷ Total number of fruits examined × 100 Weight loss is also calculated as: Weight loss (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100 It is preferable to record separately: Fruits bearing holes only. Fruits containing larvae. Rotted fruits. Fallen fruits. Fruits that can be sorted. Fruits permanently rejected. The numerical percentage of infested fruits may differ from their percentage by weight.

Marketable yield is calculated as: Marketable yield = Total yield − Weight of rejected fruits Fruits rejected because of Tuta include: Fruits containing tunnels. Fruits with open holes. Rotted fruits. Severely deformed fruits. Fruits not meeting buyer specifications. The rejection rate differs according to market. A factory may accept a limited superficial defect that would be rejected by the fresh market, but it generally does not accept rotted fruits or fruits carrying live larvae.

sectionTitle: Economic Injury Level and Economic Threshold
text: Economic Injury Level Economic Injury Level This is the lowest pest density at which the value of crop loss equals the cost of control. Economic Threshold Economic Threshold This is the pest density at which intervention begins before the population reaches the economic injury level. The economic threshold is lower than the economic injury level because control requires time before its effect becomes apparent.

There is no single universal threshold applicable to all environments. A study conducted in tomato greenhouses in Ethiopia determined: Economic injury level: approximately 3 larvae per plant. Economic threshold: approximately 2.25 larvae per plant. Another field experiment estimated the economic threshold at approximately: 2.87 larvae per plant And the economic injury level at approximately: 3.82 larvae per plant. An Iranian study estimated economic injury levels between: 4.15 and 5.04 larvae per plant Depending on the tomato cultivar. These results demonstrate that the threshold varies according to: Cultivar. Crop value. Control cost. Efficacy of the control method. Production system. Plant growth stage. Target market. Percentage of fruit infestation. Climatic conditions. Therefore, a value of 2.25 or 3 larvae per plant should not be used as a fixed rule for all Egyptian tomato fields.

sectionTitle: Why Trap Counts Alone Are Insufficient to Determine Loss
text: Pheromone traps mainly attract males, whereas damage is caused by larvae originating from eggs laid by females. Trap catches may be high without severe damage because of: Immigration of males from a neighboring field. Low female numbers. High efficacy of natural enemies. Failure of part of the eggs to hatch. Trap catches may be low while damage is high because of: Presence of mated females. Reduced lure attractiveness. Unsuitable trap location. Large numbers of larvae already inside plants. Changes in wind direction. Traps must therefore be interpreted together with: Egg inspection. Live larvae. Active mines. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation.

A field decision depends on several indicators, most importantly: 1. Number of males captured and the weekly trend in catches. 2. Percentage of plants carrying eggs. 3. Percentage of plants bearing active mines. 4. Number of live larvae per plant. 5. Percentage of infested growing points. 6. Percentage of infested fruits. 7. Presence of advanced larval instars. 8. Activity of natural enemies. 9. Proximity to harvest. 10. Condition of neighboring fields. No single indicator provides a complete picture of risk.

sectionTitle: Intervention Threshold in Fresh-Market and Processing Tomatoes
text: Fresh-Market Tomatoes The threshold for fruit infestation is low because cosmetic damage directly affects price and acceptance. Intervention begins before larvae reach fruits, based on eggs and recent mines. Processing Tomatoes The crop may tolerate a limited level of leaf mining more than fresh-market tomatoes, but fruit infestation should remain as low as possible. The decision depends on: Factory specifications. Percentage of rejected fruits. Proximity to harvest. Probability of larvae moving into fruits. Condition of the canopy. Economic feasibility of treatment. Preharvest interval of the insecticide. Intervention should not be delayed until the percentage of infested fruits increases because damage occurring inside the fruit cannot be repaired.

Total loss consists of: Direct Yield Loss Reduced fruit number. Reduced weight of sound fruits. Rejected fruits. Rotted fruits. Fallen fruits. Control Costs Insecticides. Labor. Fuel and equipment. Traps. Biological-control agents. Inspection and monitoring. Postharvest Costs Additional sorting. Disposal of fruits. Cleaning of containers. Reduced packing efficiency. Shipment rejection. Indirect Losses Insecticide resistance. Pesticide residues. Mortality of natural enemies. Reduced confidence in the supplier. Loss of a supply contract or market.

If total yield is: 40 metric tons per feddan And the percentage of fruits rejected because of Tuta is: 8% Direct loss equals: 40 × 0.08 = 3.2 metric tons per feddan If the price per metric ton is: EGP 5,000 The value of direct loss is: 3.2 × 5,000 = EGP 16,000 per feddan The following must then be added: Control costs. Sorting costs. Rot losses. Any deductions applied by the buyer. This example does not include the effect of reduced yield resulting from leaf or growing-point loss.

sectionTitle: Preventing Infestation from Becoming a Loss
text: The difference between limited infestation and severe loss depends on the speed of intervention and program efficiency. Loss prevention depends on: Early detection of eggs. Targeting young larvae. Preventing L3 and L4 from reaching fruits. Removing heavily infested growing points and leaves when possible. Managing old fields and crop residues. Conserving natural enemies. Rotating insecticide groups. Optimizing coverage and timing. Monitoring fruits before harvest. Applying agricultural sanitation after the season ends.

Plant Part Main Symptom Potential Result Leaves Blotch mines and dark frass Reduced green leaf area Growing point Wilting and death of the bud Deformed growth and delayed production Flower bud Hole and desiccation Bud drop Flower Feeding and frass Reduced fruit set Leaf petiole Internal tunnel Leaf wilting Stem Boring and frass Weakness or death of the upper part Calyx Hidden larva and tunnels Initiation of fruit infestation Green fruit Holes and tunnels Deformation and rot Ripe fruit Softness, leakage, and rot Fresh-market or processing rejection Whole plant Loss of leaves and growing points Reduced growth and yield

Larvae of Tuta absoluta cause direct plant damage, whereas the adult moth performs the functions of reproduction and dispersal. Infestation usually begins with small leaf mines that expand as larvae grow. At high pest density, mines coalesce, tissues dry, and leaves lose a large part of their photosynthetic capacity. Larvae also attack growing points, buds, stems, flowers, and fruits. Growing-point infestation causes growth deformation, whereas fruit infestation represents the principal component of economic loss. Holes and tunnels permit entry of rot pathogens, allowing a small opening to develop into a large area of decomposed tissue. Losses vary according to infestation severity and timing, cultivar, market, and production system, and may range from low levels to 80–100% loss of marketable yield in severe unmanaged cases. The presence of an old mine does not necessarily require treatment. Decisions should be based on eggs, active mines, live larvae, and growing-point and fruit infestation. Some studies estimated the economic threshold at approximately 2.25–2.87 larvae per plant, while others estimated the economic injury level at approximately 3–5 larvae per plant depending on cultivar and environment. These values are not fixed universal thresholds because the threshold changes with crop price, control cost and efficacy, and market type. The percentage of infested fruits is the most important economic indicator, particularly in fresh-market and processing tomatoes intended for delivery. Successful management depends on preventing infestation from moving from leaves into growing points, stems, and fruits and targeting eggs and young larvae before infestation becomes an irreversible loss. References

Tuta absoluta is a pest that is difficult to manage using a single control method because of its biological and behavioral characteristics and its high capacity for reproduction, dispersal, and concealment within plant tissues. The pest possesses several characteristics that increase the difficulty of control, most importantly: High fecundity, which may reach approximately 260 eggs per female and may approach 300 eggs under favorable conditions. Short generation time under warm conditions. Production of numerous overlapping generations during the year. Simultaneous presence of eggs, larvae, pupae, and adult moths in the field. Larval feeding within mines in leaves, stems, and fruits. Difficulty of surface-applied insecticides reaching larvae after they enter plant tissues. Ability of advanced larvae to leave mines and move to other plant organs or plants. Pupation of a proportion of larvae in soil or plant residues. Persistence of the pest on volunteer tomato plants, black nightshade, and other solanaceous hosts. Movement of moths from old and neglected fields into newly planted crops. Rapid development of resistance when insecticides with the same mode of action are repeatedly used. These characteristics make complete eradication of Tuta absoluta within an open agricultural area an unrealistic objective in most situations. Integrated pest management therefore does not aim to eliminate every individual pest, but to keep pest populations and resulting damage below the level that causes unacceptable economic loss. Sustainable management is achieved by preventing the pest from reaching high densities rather than waiting for a severe infestation to develop and then attempting to reduce it through a succession of sprays. An integrated management program begins before planting, continues through all crop stages, and ends after harvest with the removal of pest carryover sources.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Why Is Chemical Control Alone Insufficient?
text: Insecticides may rapidly reduce some stages of Tuta absoluta, but they do not reach all individuals in the pest population equally. Eggs may be less exposed to some products, larvae inside mines or fruits may be partially protected, pupae in soil or plant residues are not reached by foliar sprays, and moths may continue immigrating from neighboring fields. Continuous dependence on insecticides alone leads to problems including: Mortality of natural enemies. Reinfestation after residual insecticidal activity declines. Outbreaks of secondary pests. Increased number of sprays. Higher control costs. Increased pesticide residues. Greater risk of crop rejection. Increased selection pressure for resistance. Declining efficacy of products over time. Contamination of soil, irrigation water, and the surrounding environment. Increased risk to workers and consumers. The presence of old mines on leaves may also lead to unnecessary spraying even though the larvae have already left or died. Chemical control is therefore one tool within an integrated pest-management program and not the entire program.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

text: Integrated pest management is a scientific decision-making system based on the study of the pest, crop, and agricultural environment. It combines preventive, monitoring, cultural, mechanical, behavioral, biological, and chemical methods to reduce pest populations to an economically and environmentally acceptable level. The word: Integrated Means that different methods are not used separately or randomly, but are coordinated so that they complement one another. The word: Pest Means that the decision focuses on the organism causing actual economic damage rather than merely on the presence of an insect in the field. The word: Management Means regulating pest populations and limiting damage rather than necessarily achieving complete eradication. Integrated management of Tuta absoluta can therefore be defined as: > A dynamic system based on prevention, early detection, and quantitative assessment of infestation, followed by integration of cultural, mechanical, behavioral, biological, and selective chemical methods to keep the pest below the economic injury level while conserving natural enemies and reducing resistance, residues, and environmental risks.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: What Integrated Pest Management Does Not Mean
text: Integrated pest management does not mean: Complete elimination of insecticide use. Merely replacing a chemical insecticide with a biological product. Installing a pheromone trap and then neglecting plant inspection. Mixing several different insecticides in one tank. Applying a fixed set of treatments in every field. Calendar-based spraying without inspecting infestation. Reducing all insects present in the field. Removing all weeds without understanding their role. Introducing natural enemies only after infestation reaches outbreak level. Considering every leaf mine an active infestation. Depending solely on trap catches for decision-making. Integrated pest management is not a product that can be purchased. It is a decision-making system that changes according to the field, season, crop stage, and level of risk.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: General Objective of Integrated Pest Management
text: The principal objective is to produce a sound and economically viable crop while keeping Tuta absoluta populations below the economic injury level and minimizing adverse effects on: Human health. Natural enemies. Biodiversity. Soil. Irrigation water. Neighboring crops. Fruit quality. Food safety. Sustainability of effective insecticides. Program success is not measured only by the number of insects killed, but by the final economic and environmental outcome.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Detailed Objectives of Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
text: Integrated management aims to achieve the following: 1. Prevent pest entry into the nursery or field as far as possible. 2. Delay the onset of infestation. 3. Detect the pest while population density is still low. 4. Prevent establishment of an early generation within the crop. 5. Reduce oviposition and hatching success. 6. Target young larvae before they penetrate deeply into plant tissues. 7. Prevent larval movement from leaves into growing points, stems, and fruits. 8. Reduce the percentage of infested fruits. 9. Prevent pest persistence in crop residues. 10. Break the green bridge between planting seasons. 11. Protect naturally occurring natural enemies. 12. Enhance the efficacy of biological control. 13. Reduce unnecessary chemical sprays. 14. Delay development of insecticide resistance. 15. Comply with preharvest intervals and maximum residue limits. 16. Reduce the cost of producing a sound metric ton of crop. 17. Increase the percentage of marketable or processable yield. 18. Prevent movement of infestation into neighboring fields. 19. Document infestation history and intervention results. 20. Develop a local program suited to the region, planting season, and production system.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Scientific Foundations of Integrated Pest Management
text: Integrated pest management is based on a set of interrelated scientific foundations. Biological Foundation The program depends on knowledge of: Pest life cycle. Duration of each stage. Four larval instars. Oviposition sites. Timing of hatching. Pupation sites. Moth activity. Alternative hosts. Effects of temperature and humidity. Number and overlap of generations. This knowledge determines: The stage that should be targeted. Appropriate timing of intervention. The most effective control method. The expected period before appearance of the next generation. Targeting a young larva before it enters a fruit is more effective than attempting to kill it after it has become established inside the fruit. Ecological Foundation Tuta absoluta does not live separately from the agricultural environment. It interacts with: Tomato plants. Alternative hosts. Weeds. Natural enemies. Temperature. Humidity. Wind. Irrigation and fertilization systems. Neighboring fields. Planting dates. Crop husbandry operations. A decision should therefore not be based on pest number alone, but on the condition of the entire agroecosystem. The presence of a particular number of eggs accompanied by high activity of egg parasitoids may require a different decision than the same egg density in the absence of natural enemies. Economic Foundation Not every occurrence of the pest justifies implementation of a control treatment. The decision compares: Expected value of crop loss. Cost of intervention. Efficacy of intervention. Crop value. Crop growth stage. Time remaining until harvest. Proportion of fruits at risk. Requirements of the market or processing plant. The program aims to prevent the pest from reaching the economic injury level. Monitoring and Information Foundation Integrated pest management cannot exist without regular field information. Information is obtained from: Pheromone traps. Plant inspection. Egg counts. Counts of active mines. Counts of live larvae. Inspection of growing points. Fruit inspection. Recording natural enemies. Temperature monitoring. Inspection of old and neighboring fields. Data should be: Regular. Quantitative. Comparable. Linked to date and location. Recorded in clear registers. Preventive Foundation Prevention is more effective and less costly than treating a severe outbreak. Preventive measures include: Pest-free transplants. Nursery sanitation. Removal of volunteer plants. Disposal of crop residues. Management of host weeds. Avoiding retention of old fields after harvesting has stopped. Breaking succession of solanaceous hosts. Cleaning tools and containers. Protecting greenhouses with suitable insect-proof screens. Monitoring neighboring infestation sources. Integration of Control Methods Integrated management does not depend merely on accumulating numerous methods, but on selecting compatible methods. A program may fail when: An insecticide kills the released parasitoid. Plants supporting natural enemies are removed without justification. Pheromone traps are used without managing old fields. Natural enemies are released only after infestation has become extremely high. A single insecticide is repeatedly used despite declining efficacy. True integration means that each method performs a specific function without cancelling the effect of other methods. Priority for Lower-Risk Methods Priority is given to methods that provide acceptable pest reduction with the least possible harm, including: Preventive measures. Agricultural sanitation. Traps. Removal of infestation foci. Conservation of natural enemies. Biological control. Biopesticides. Selective products. Chemical insecticides are used when necessary, not merely because the pest is present. Local Management Foundation No single fixed program is suitable for all regions. The program changes according to: Open fields or greenhouses. Winter or summer planting seasons. Northern Egypt or southern regions. Fresh-market or processing tomatoes. Season length. Level of salinity and stress. Immigration sources. Natural enemies present. Insecticides registered and locally effective. Recommendations should therefore be based on actual field conditions rather than direct copying of a program from another region. Continuous Evaluation A treatment does not end when it is applied. The field must be inspected afterward to determine: Reduction in eggs. Reduction in live larvae. Cessation of new-mine appearance. Continued infestation of growing points or fruits. Effect of the treatment on natural enemies. Need for additional intervention. Possibility of resistance or poor spray coverage. Evaluation is used to modify subsequent management decisions.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: General Principles of Integrated Pest Management
text: Integrated pest management is based on eight sequential general principles. Principle One: Prevention and Suppression of Pest Increase Management begins before damage appears. Preventive measures include: Crop rotation. Selection of planting date. Use of healthy transplants. Agricultural sanitation. Removal of confirmed alternative hosts. Residue management. Balanced fertilization and irrigation. Conservation of natural enemies. Prevention of pest movement among fields. The objective is to make the agricultural environment less favorable for pest establishment and multiplication. Principle Two: Diagnosis and Regular Monitoring The identity of the pest must be confirmed and distinguished from: Leaf-mining flies. Tomato fruitworm. Other leaf miners. Fungal and bacterial diseases. Physiological disorders. The pest is then monitored with suitable tools including: Pheromone traps. Direct inspection. Plant samples. Temperature records. Warning and forecasting systems. Decisions should not be based on a single observation or general impression. Principle Three: Threshold-Based Decision-Making Control methods should not be used merely because the pest is present. The decision depends on: Number of live larvae. Percentage of active mines. Trend in trap catches. Percentage of infested growing points. Percentage of infested fruits. Crop stage. Activity of natural enemies. Crop value. Treatment cost. Local thresholds should be used when available. A threshold from another country or production system should not be transferred without evaluation. Principle Four: Preference for Non-Chemical Methods Cultural, mechanical, behavioral, and biological methods are given priority when they provide sufficient control. These include: Agricultural sanitation. Traps. Mating disruption. Mass trapping. Removal of infestation foci. Egg parasitoids. Predators. Entomopathogenic microorganisms. Biopesticides. This does not mean rejecting chemical insecticides, but using them when lower-risk methods are insufficient. Principle Five: Selection of the Most Selective Method When insecticides are used, the selected product should: Target the pest stage present. Have demonstrated local efficacy. Be registered for the crop and pest. Cause less harm to natural enemies. Have a suitable preharvest interval. Pose minimal risk to pollinators and non-target organisms. Be compatible with other program components. Selection should be based not only on the trade name, but on the active ingredient and mode of action. Principle Six: Use of the Minimum Necessary Level of Intervention Reducing pesticide use does not mean applying randomly reduced rates below the registered rate because an inadequate dose may fail and increase resistance risk. The minimum necessary level means: Avoiding sprays when they are unnecessary. Reducing unnecessary treatment frequency. Treating localized foci when appropriate. Using the correct registered rate. Improving spray coverage. Selecting the most appropriate timing. Preventing repeated treatment caused by poor application. Use reduction must remain compatible with efficacy and must not increase resistance risk. Principle Seven: Insecticide Resistance Management Tuta absoluta is a high-risk pest for resistance development because of: Short generation time. Numerous generations. High fecundity. Frequent spraying. Repeated use of the same products. Continuous selection pressure on successive generations. Resistance management depends on: Rotation of modes of action. Avoiding reliance on trade names. Avoiding treatment of successive generations with the same group. Using effective products only when needed. Integrating biological and cultural control. Evaluating local efficacy. Complying with registered rate and timing. Avoiding unjustified mixtures. Changing the trade name does not mean changing the mode of action when the active ingredient or chemical group is the same. Principle Eight: Evaluation and Documentation of Results Program success must be evaluated using quantitative indicators such as: Number of new mines. Number of live larvae. Percentage of infested plants. Percentage of infested growing points. Percentage of infested fruits. Trap catches. Number of sprays. Presence of natural enemies. Marketable yield. Control cost. Cost of producing one sound metric ton. All treatments and results should be recorded to allow: Identification of successful methods. Detection of reduced efficacy. Program modification. Monitoring of insecticide resistance. Comparison among seasons and fields.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Specific Principles for Management of Tuta absoluta
text: In addition to the general principles, several principles are specific to the biology of Tuta absoluta. Prevention of Early Establishment The earlier the pest enters the crop, the more generations it can complete within the season. Targeting Eggs and Young Larvae Control is more effective before larvae enter deep mines and fruits. Prevention of Fruit Infestation Fruit infestation is the point at which plant infestation becomes a direct economic loss. Management of All Stages The program should include methods targeting: Moths. Eggs. Larvae. Pupae. Sources sustaining the next generation. Management of Immigration Control may succeed within the field and then infestation may recur because moths move from: An old field. Crop residues. A crop in which spraying has stopped. Volunteer tomato plants. Solanaceous weeds. Distinguishing Old Damage from Active Infestation Treatments should not be based on dry old mines, but on eggs, live larvae, and new mines. Linking Traps with Plant Inspection Traps alone are insufficient because they primarily monitor males and do not directly measure larval numbers or fruit infestation. Area-Wide Management Management is more successful when neighboring farms cooperate in: Removing crop residues. Managing old crops. Sharing trap data. Coordinating insecticide-resistance management. Preventing immigration sources.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

text: Integrated pest management follows this sequence: Level One: Prevention Prevent pest entry or reduce opportunities for establishment. Level Two: Monitoring Detect eggs, moths, and recent mines early. Level Three: Assessment Determine infestation density, dominant stage, and population trend. Level Four: Decision Determine whether intervention is required and select the appropriate intervention. Level Five: Non-Chemical Methods Apply cultural, behavioral, mechanical, and biological measures. Level Six: Selective Chemical Intervention Use when necessary, with correct selection of active ingredient, timing, and mode of action. Level Seven: Evaluation Measure the result after intervention. Level Eight: Modification Modify the program according to monitoring and evaluation results.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Components of the Integrated Pest-Management Program
text: Chapter Ten is divided into the following detailed sections, each of which is explained separately. Section One: Prevention and Exclusion Includes: Selection of planting site. Inspection of surrounding infestation sources. Host-free period. Crop rotation. Coordination among planting seasons. General sanitation. Section Two: Nursery and Transplant Management Includes: Production of pest-free transplants. Transplant inspection. Traps inside the nursery. Screens and barriers. Handling of infested transplants. Prevention of egg and larval movement into the field. Section Three: Monitoring, Early Detection, and Warning Includes: Pheromone traps. Number and distribution of traps. Trap height. Maintenance of traps and lures. Plant inspection. Division of the field into inspection points. Monitoring eggs, larvae, and fruits. Interpretation of results. Weekly monitoring records. Section Four: Economic Thresholds and Decision-Making Includes: Economic injury level. Economic threshold. Threshold differences according to market. Integration of trap data and plant inspection. Timing of intervention. Classification of risk levels. Section Five: Cultural Control Includes: Crop rotation. Planting dates. Residue removal. Tillage. Management of weeds and alternative hosts. Balanced fertilization. Irrigation management. Prevention of excessive vegetative growth. Management of old fields. Section Six: Mechanical and Physical Control Includes: Removal of infested leaves and growing points. Collection of infested fruits. Shredding of residues. Insect-proof screens. Double-door systems in greenhouses. Soil solarization. Isolation barriers. Management of containers and equipment. Section Seven: Behavioral and Pheromone-Based Control Includes: Pheromone traps. Mass trapping. Water and oil traps. Mating disruption. Female attractants. Limitations of each method. Effect of pest density on pheromone efficacy. Section Eight: Biological Control with Predators and Parasitoids Includes: Egg parasitoids. Larval parasitoids. Predators. Release programs. Conservation of natural enemies. Release timing. Indicators of biological-control success. Compatibility of natural enemies with insecticides. Section Nine: Microbial and Biological Control Includes: Bacillus thuringiensis. Entomopathogenic fungi. Entomopathogenic nematodes. Viruses and biological products. Suitable application conditions. Coverage and timing. Compatibility of biological products with the rest of the program. Section Ten: Rational Chemical Control Includes: When insecticides should be used. Selection of the active ingredient. Targeting the larval instar. Spray coverage. Spray-water volume. Timing of application. Preharvest interval. Selectivity. Avoidance of unnecessary sprays. Section Eleven: Insecticide Resistance Management Includes: Causes of resistance development. Mode-of-action groups. Window strategy. Rotation of groups. Cross-resistance. Evaluation of reduced efficacy. Mixing and repetition errors. Development of a seasonal resistance-management program. Section Twelve: Compatibility Among Control Methods Includes: Compatibility of insecticides with egg parasitoids. Compatibility of insecticides with predators. Effects of insecticides on beneficial fungi and bacteria. Safety intervals before and after natural-enemy release. Selection of selective products. Sequence of interventions within the program. Section Thirteen: Area-Wide Management Includes: Neighboring fields. Old fields. Nurseries. Crop residues. Coordination of planting seasons. Sharing trap results. Prevention of mass immigration. Cooperation among growers and companies. Section Fourteen: Integrated Program During the Season Includes: Preplanting. Nursery stage. Transplanting. Vegetative growth. Flowering and fruit set. Fruit filling. Preharvest. Harvest. Postharvest. Section Fifteen: Evaluation of Economic and Environmental Efficiency Includes: Percentage reduction in infestation. Percentage reduction in infested fruits. Number of sprays avoided. Program cost. Economic return. Marketable yield. Effect of the program on natural enemies. Effect on residues and resistance. Section Sixteen: Records and Practical Forms Includes: Trap record. Plant-inspection record. Natural-enemy record. Insecticide record. Mode-of-action record. Fruit-infestation record. Treatment-evaluation form. Weekly decision form.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Indicators of Success of an Integrated Pest-Management Program
text: A program is considered successful when it achieves a set of outcomes, not merely temporary larval mortality. Success indicators include: Delayed appearance of infestation. Reduction in eggs and active mines. Reduction in numbers of live larvae. Reduced growing-point infestation. Reduced percentage of infested fruits. Reduced movement of infestation from leaves into fruits. Conservation of natural enemies. Reduction in broad-spectrum sprays. Avoidance of repeated use of the same mode-of-action groups. Reduced control cost per metric ton. Increased sound yield. Compliance with residue limits. Absence of repeated insecticide failure. Prevention of the field becoming an infestation source for neighboring fields.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

text: Integrated management of Tuta absoluta is not a spray program, a single biological-control method, or a fixed set of treatments. It is an integrated decision-making system. It is based on: Understanding pest biology. Studying the agroecosystem. Prevention. Regular monitoring. Use of thresholds. Preference for non-chemical methods. Use of selective products when necessary. Resistance management. Evaluation of results. Continuous modification of the program. The program begins before the pest enters the field and continues until crop residues are removed after harvest. Preventing early establishment, targeting eggs and young larvae, conserving natural enemies, preventing fruit infestation, and managing immigration sources and resistance are the principal foundations of successful integrated management of Tuta absoluta.
partTitle: Introduction, Foundations, and General Principles of Integrated Pest Management

text: Prevention represents the first line of defense in the integrated management of Tuta absoluta. It is more effective and less costly than attempting to control the pest after overlapping generations have become established within the crop. Prevention does not mean completely preventing every moth from reaching the field, because this is difficult to achieve in open-field tomato production owing to the ability of moths to fly and migrate from surrounding fields. Rather, prevention means: Reducing the probability of pest entry. Reducing the number of individuals reaching the field. Delaying the onset of infestation. Preventing movement of eggs and larvae with transplants. Removing surrounding breeding sources. Preventing early establishment of the first generation. Making the agricultural environment less suitable for pest persistence. Delaying infestation is particularly important because every week that passes without establishment of a reproducing population in the field reduces the number of generations the pest can complete before harvest. If the pest enters the field from the nursery or immediately after transplanting, it may be able to produce several successive generations. If its entry is delayed until the later crop stages, the remaining period available for generation completion and damage is shorter.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

sectionTitle: Difference Between Pest Entry and Establishment
text: Two stages must be distinguished: Pest Entry This means arrival of any stage of Tuta absoluta in the field, such as: Arrival of a migrating adult moth. Movement of eggs with transplants. Movement of larvae inside transplant leaves. Movement of larvae inside infested fruits or residues. Movement of pupae with residues or contaminated containers. Pest Establishment This means that arriving individuals succeed in: Surviving. Mating. Laying eggs. Hatching. Completing larval development. Producing a new generation within the field. Some moths may enter a field without successfully establishing a persistent population, particularly when: Their numbers are low. Fertilized females are absent. Natural-enemy activity is high. Eggs and larvae are removed early. Environmental conditions are unsuitable. An effective preventive program is applied. The true objective of prevention is therefore not only to prevent entry, but to prevent establishment and early reproduction.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Tuta absoluta reaches a field or production area through several pathways. Infested Transplants Transplants represent one of the most serious pest-movement pathways because they may carry: Small eggs that are difficult to detect. Young larvae inside small mines. Larvae in growing points. Pupae inside folded leaves or containers. A transplant may appear healthy during a rapid visual inspection while carrying an early infestation. Planting infested transplants distributes the pest throughout the field from the first day rather than allowing it to enter gradually from field edges. Immigration of Moths from Neighboring Fields Adult moths can move from: Old tomato fields. Fields in which growers have stopped spraying. Fields where harvest has ended but plants have not been removed. Piles of crop residues. Infested greenhouses or nurseries. Infested potato or eggplant fields. Volunteer tomato plants. Host solanaceous weeds. Immigration increases when the old host dries or is suddenly removed, forcing moths to search for recently planted tomatoes. Infested Fruits Infested fruits may contain live larvae and may move with: Harvest crates. Sorting rejects. Market waste. Rejected fruits. Processing-plant waste. Fruits are not usually a major source of field establishment unless larvae or pupae reach plants or residues that permit life-cycle continuation, but they are an important pathway for movement of the pest between regions. Containers and Packing Materials Larvae or pupae may occur in: Cracks in crates. Folds of cardboard. Reused containers. Bags. Attached leaf and fruit residues. Vehicles used to transport infested produce. Risk increases when containers move directly from an infested field to a nursery or newly planted field without cleaning. Crop Residues Tomato residues may contain: Unhatched eggs. Larvae inside leaves. Larvae inside stems and fruits. Pupae inside folded leaves. Pupae in the surface soil layer. These stages may continue developing after harvest ends, after which moths emerge and move to new crops. Volunteer Plants and Host Weeds Volunteer tomato plants, black nightshade, and some Solanum species allow the pest to persist during periods when commercial tomato is absent. These plants function as: Hosts for eggs and larvae. Shelters for moths. A green bridge between planting seasons. Continuous sources of immigration into new fields.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Prevention does not begin only at the field boundary; it begins with assessment of the surrounding area. Before planting, the following should be identified: Locations of old tomato fields. Fields still being harvested. Fields where control has stopped. Nearby nurseries. Potato and eggplant fields. Tomato-residue accumulation sites. Areas where volunteer tomatoes occur. Solanaceous weeds along drains and roads. Prevailing wind direction. Direction of movement of workers, containers, and equipment. A simplified map of risk sources around the field should be prepared and classified as follows: Risk Level Source Very high An old, infested tomato field directly adjacent to the new field High A nursery or tomato field within the nearby production area Moderate Infested potato, eggplant, or solanaceous weeds Low Land free from host plants and properly managed This map helps determine: Field edges requiring intensive inspection. Initial trap locations. Direction from which sampling should begin. Areas where infestation is expected to appear first. Need for coordination with neighboring growers.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: When more than one planting site is available, preference should be given to a site that: Is distant from old infested tomato fields. Is not adjacent to an unmonitored nursery. Is free from crop-residue piles. Has few volunteer tomato plants. Has few solanaceous weeds. Does not receive containers or equipment from infested fields. Allows implementation of an appropriate crop rotation. Permits inspection of field boundaries and surrounding infestation sources. No single distance guarantees prevention of moth immigration because movement is affected by wind, topography, infestation density, and host availability. Distance should therefore not be used as the only preventive measure, but should be integrated with monitoring, agricultural sanitation, and area-wide management.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: A host-free period means continuous absence from the production area of plants on which the pest can complete its life cycle. It includes removal or prevention of: Commercial tomato crops. Volunteer tomato plants. Infested potato. Infested eggplant. Black nightshade. Confirmed solanaceous hosts. A host-free period helps to: Deprive larvae of food. Reduce oviposition opportunities. Prevent production of successive generations. Break the green bridge. Reduce moth numbers before the following crop. No fixed duration is suitable for every region. Effectiveness depends on: Temperature. Persistence of pest stages. Presence of pupae in soil. Presence of wild hosts. Presence of nearby greenhouses or nurseries. Overlap of planting seasons in the area. The host-free period provides no benefit if commercial tomato is removed while volunteer tomato plants or host weeds remain.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Crop rotation helps reduce pest persistence when non-host crops are planted after tomato. Rotation is more effective when: Tomato residues are completely removed. Potato or eggplant is not planted immediately afterward in the same area. Volunteer plants are removed. Solanaceous weeds are managed. A continuous interval without a suitable host is maintained. Non-solanaceous crops that may be included in rotation according to the local production system include: Cereals. Maize. Legumes that are not confirmed hosts. Brassica crops. Other crops that do not permit completion of the pest life cycle. Crop rotation within a single field is insufficient when infested tomato fields remain directly adjacent.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Overlapping planting dates create fields at different crop stages throughout the year, allowing continuous movement of the pest from old fields into new ones. Prevention improves when planting dates within the area are coordinated to: Reduce severe overlap among planting seasons. Create a break between crops. Prevent persistence of neglected crops. Standardize timing of residue removal. Reduce movement of moths into recently transplanted crops. This does not require all fields to be planted on the same day, but it requires reducing the continuous chain of host plants extending from the nursery to abandoned old fields.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Transplants should be: Produced in a monitored nursery. Free from eggs. Free from mines. Free from larvae. Free from infested growing points. Not located adjacent to old tomato fields. Accompanied by a clear inspection record. Preventive spraying alone should not be used to declare transplants pest-free because larvae may remain inside mines and survive treatment.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Inspection begins in the nursery before transplants are moved. It includes: 1. Inspecting plants along the nursery edges. 2. Inspecting plants near doors and openings. 3. Inspecting young leaves. 4. Examining both upper and lower leaf surfaces. 5. Searching for eggs. 6. Searching for small mines. 7. Opening suspected mines. 8. Inspecting growing points and buds. 9. Inspecting trays and the area beneath them. 10. Reviewing traps installed in the nursery. Inspection of only a few plants from above is insufficient. Samples should be taken from multiple nursery locations.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

sectionTitle: Inspection of Transplants on Arrival at the Field
text: Transplants should be inspected again before planting because infestation may occur during: Waiting periods. Transport. Temporary storage. Placement beside an infested crop. Batches showing any of the following should be isolated: Eggs. An active mine. A live larva. An infested growing point. Suspicious leaves. An infested batch should not be distributed throughout the field before the level of infestation is determined and an appropriate decision is made.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Transplants from different sources should not be mixed without identification and records. Each batch should have data including: Nursery name. Date of receipt. Cultivar. Number of trays. Inspection result. Any previous treatment. Area of the field in which it was planted. These records make it possible to identify the infestation source if early infestation appears in a specific part of the field.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

sectionTitle: Management of Infested Plants Before Transplanting
text: When a limited number of infested plants is found: They should be isolated immediately. They should not be left beside healthy transplants. They should be collected in a closed container. Infested leaves should not be allowed to fall in the field. They should be disposed of in a manner that prevents emergence of larvae or moths. Removing an infested leaf and leaving it on the ground is insufficient because the larva may continue developing and later pupate. When infestation affects a large proportion of the batch, rejection of the entire batch should be evaluated rather than introducing an early infestation source into the field.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Agricultural sanitation before field preparation includes: Removal of residues from the previous tomato crop. Collection of fruits left in the field. Removal of volunteer plants. Removal of confirmed infested solanaceous weeds. Cleaning field margins. Cleaning internal drainage channels. Removal of sorting piles. Preventing disposal of market or factory waste beside the field. Cleaning areas used for unloading transplants. Sanitation should not be judged only by general appearance, but by the absence of plant tissues containing living pest stages.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

sectionTitle: Management of Residues from the Previous Crop
text: Residues should be disposed of rapidly after the final harvest, and living plants should not be retained for uneconomic additional pickings. Measures include: Cutting plants. Shredding them. Collecting infested fruits. Preventing formation of exposed residue piles. Burying residues or composting them in a manner that ensures mortality of pest stages. Conducting appropriate tillage according to soil type and production system. Monitoring plants that regrow. Risk increases when spraying stops while the old crop remains alive because the field becomes a source of migrating moths.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Volunteer tomato plants should be removed from: The field. Field margins. Irrigation channels. Drainage channels. Compost piles. Fruit-collection areas. Farm roads. Areas surrounding processing plants or sorting stations. They should be removed before they permit completion of a full pest generation. Volunteer plants should be inspected before disposal to determine whether they carry: Eggs. Larvae. Pupae. Active mines.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Management should focus on confirmed or highly probable host weeds, especially: Black nightshade, Solanum nigrum. Wild Solanum species. Tree tobacco, Nicotiana glauca, where present. Infested wild solanaceous plants. Not all weeds should be removed indiscriminately because some non-host plants may support: Predators. Parasitoids. Pollinators. Nectar sources. Priority should be given to removal of weeds on which active infestation or the ability to support the pest life cycle has been demonstrated.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

sectionTitle: Timing of Removal of Infested Weeds
text: Removing a large mass of infested plants beside a new field may drive moths into the new crop. When a major infestation source is removed, the following should therefore be implemented: Collect infested plants and do not leave them at the site. Dispose of them rapidly. Increase monitoring of the neighboring field. Operate traps before removal. Inspect field edges facing the infestation source. Coordinate removal with surrounding fields. The objective is to eliminate the source, not merely to move moths from it into the new crop.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Tools and materials moving among fields should be cleaned, especially: Harvest crates. Transport vehicles. Produce trailers. Transplant trays and boxes. Sorting equipment. Clothing or covers that contact infested plants. The following should be removed: Leaves. Small fruits. Plant residues. Soil that may contain pupae. Larvae or cocoons. Containers arriving directly from a severely infested field should preferably be prevented from entering a nursery or newly planted field.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Workers may indirectly transport: Infested leaves. Small fruits. Plant fragments. Contaminated containers. Movement should be organized as far as possible from: Newer and less infested fields → toward older and more heavily infested fields And not in the opposite direction. Infested leaves or fruits should not be discarded along field edges during crop operations.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: In open fields, infestation frequently appears first along edges facing immigration sources. Preventive edge management includes: Removing infested hosts. Installing traps early. Increasing inspection intensity. Recording wind direction. Monitoring plants near roads and old fields. Identifying infestation foci immediately. Attention to edges does not mean neglecting the field interior because infested transplants may distribute the pest internally from the beginning.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Pheromone traps should be installed before transplanting or during field preparation to: Confirm the presence of moths in the area. Estimate immigration pressure before transplants enter the field. Identify the direction of infestation sources. Compare field edges. Establish a monitoring baseline. Installing a trap does not prevent pest entry unless it is part of a mass-trapping system designed for that purpose. A monitoring trap is a warning tool and not a substitute for prevention and agricultural sanitation.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: No commercial tomato cultivar can be considered completely immune to Tuta absoluta. Cultivars differ in: Attractiveness for oviposition. Trichome density. Defensive compounds. Plant compensatory capacity. Canopy density. Earliness. Crop duration. These differences should be considered within preventive management but do not replace other IPM components. Selecting a long-duration cultivar in a high-pressure area may increase the period during which the crop is exposed to successive pest generations.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Irrigation and fertilization do not directly kill the pest, but they affect plant suitability and its ability to tolerate damage. Management should include: Avoiding excessive nitrogen. Preventing excessive tender vegetative growth. Maintaining balance between vegetative and reproductive growth. Avoiding severe water stress. Maintaining a plant capable of compensating for limited damage. Avoiding excessive canopy density that obstructs spray coverage and inspection. The plant should not be deliberately weakened to reduce pest pressure because a stressed plant may have less capacity to compensate for damage.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: When transplants are produced in an open or poorly sealed nursery, greater importance should be given to: Locating the nursery away from old tomato fields. Installing traps. Frequent inspection. Removing solanaceous weeds. Preventing entry of contaminated harvest crates. Immediate disposal of infested transplants. Minimizing the period for which surplus transplants are retained. Preventing the presence of large infested tomato plants beside transplants. Surplus or delayed transplants require monitoring because they may become pest reservoirs.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Although this chapter focuses on open-field tomatoes, greenhouse prevention similarly depends on: Suitable insect-proof screens. Sealing openings. Double-door entry systems. Transplant inspection. Removal of infested plants. Prevention of residue persistence inside the structure. Cleaning the greenhouse between production cycles. Screens do not prevent pest entry when the transplants themselves are infested or when pupae and residues from the previous crop remain.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

sectionTitle: Plant Quarantine and Prevention of Movement Between Regions
text: Tuta absoluta is a transboundary pest and may move over long distances with plant material and trade. Official preventive measures include: Inspection of plants intended for planting. Inspection of host fruits. Monitoring packing materials. Prevention of movement of infested materials. Application of import and export requirements. Conducting surveys in pest-free areas. Documentation of transplant sources. The level of measures differs according to the pest status in the country or region, whether absent, of limited distribution, or established.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Prevention becomes more effective when implemented across the production area rather than in a single field. Cooperation includes: Coordinating the timing of removal of old fields. Preventing fields from being left after harvest stops. Sharing trap-catch data. Reporting infestation foci. Managing nurseries. Preventing movement of contaminated containers. Organizing residue movement and disposal. Coordinating the host-free period. Monitoring weeds and uncultivated areas. A well-managed field program may fail when the field is surrounded by continuous immigration sources.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Prevention can be divided into four levels. Level One: Prevention of Movement This includes transplants, fruits, containers, and equipment. Level Two: Removal of Breeding Sources This includes crop residues, volunteer tomato plants, and alternative hosts. Level Three: Reduction of Environmental Suitability This includes crop rotation, coordination of planting seasons, and irrigation and fertilization management. Level Four: Early Detection This includes traps and inspection before the pest succeeds in establishing a stable generation.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: The following table should be used before transplanting: Item Required Condition Previous crop Residues completely removed Volunteer tomatoes Absent Black nightshade and host weeds Infested plants removed Transplants Free from eggs and mines Transplant source Known and monitored Trays Clean Containers Not contaminated from an infested field Neighboring fields Assessed Traps Installed before or at transplanting Field edges Most exposed edges identified Inspection records Ready Work team Familiar with early infestation symptoms
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: The following signs indicate a preventive failure: Mines distributed throughout the field only a few days after transplanting. Similar infestation in plants from a single batch. Appearance of larvae before clear trap activity is recorded. Presence of infested volunteer plants around the field. Presence of a directly adjacent old field containing many moths. Persistence of tomato-residue piles. Use of contaminated containers. Failure to inspect transplants. Absence of traps before planting. Retention of surplus transplants without management. The infestation distribution pattern helps identify its source: Infestation distributed within the area planted with a particular batch suggests introduction with transplants. High infestation along one edge suggests immigration from an external source. Infestation near residue piles suggests emergence of moths from residues. Infestation around volunteer plants suggests persistence of the green bridge.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Prevention success is measured through: Delayed first moth detection. Delayed appearance of the first eggs. Low mine incidence during the first weeks. Absence of infestation foci associated with transplants. Reduced infestation along field edges. Absence of volunteer plants. Rapid disposal of old fields. Reduced number of early interventions. Lack of pest movement among fields. Maintenance of a low percentage of infested fruits later in the season. Successful prevention does not mean that the pest never appears, but that pest pressure is reduced and establishment is delayed.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Error One: Assuming a Green Transplant Is Healthy A leaf may carry eggs or a small mine that is not visible from a distance. Error Two: Installing Traps Only After Infestation Appears Monitoring should begin before or at transplanting. Error Three: Leaving the Old Field After Harvest Stops The field becomes a major source of migrating moths. Error Four: Removing Weeds and Leaving Them on the Ground Larvae may continue developing inside them and later pupate. Error Five: Removing Tomato but Leaving Black Nightshade The green bridge persists despite removal of the crop. Error Six: Rotating Only to Potato or Eggplant Both crops can support the pest life cycle. Error Seven: Bringing Contaminated Harvest Crates into the Nursery They may carry plant residues or living pest stages. Error Eight: Relying on Intensive Preventive Spraying Spraying does not compensate for poor sanitation or infested transplants and increases resistance risk. Error Nine: Applying Prevention in Only One Field Immigration may continue from surrounding fields. Error Ten: Removing All Weeds Without Distinction Management should focus on confirmed and infested hosts while conserving plants that support natural enemies when they do not act as pest sources.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: After the Previous Crop or Immediately After Its Harvest Remove the old crop. Shred crop residues. Collect fruits left in the field. Monitor pupae and moth-emergence sources. Remove volunteer plants. During the Period Between Crops Manage host weeds. Implement crop rotation. Continue monitoring traps in the production area. Clean containers and equipment. Monitor nurseries. Before Transplanting Inspect the field and neighboring fields. Install traps. Inspect transplants. Identify high-risk edges. Prepare monitoring records. On Transplanting Day Reinspect transplants. Do not mix batches. Isolate suspicious plants. Record the location of each batch. During the First Weeks Inspect field edges and transplant batches. Search for eggs and recent mines. Remove limited infestation foci. Monitor surrounding old fields. Prevent retention of surplus transplants.
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: Prevention is the foundation on which all other components of integrated management of Tuta absoluta are built. It aims to prevent or delay pest arrival, prevent movement with transplants, fruits, containers, and residues, and reduce breeding and immigration sources within the production area. The most important preventive measures include: Selecting a suitable site. Using healthy transplants. Inspecting transplants before planting. Removing crop residues. Eliminating volunteer tomato plants. Managing black nightshade and alternative hosts. Implementing crop rotation and a host-free period. Coordinating planting dates. Cleaning containers and equipment. Installing traps early. Monitoring field edges and neighboring fields. Cooperating at the area-wide level. Successful prevention does not mean completely preventing the pest from appearing, but delaying establishment, reducing the initial population, and limiting the number of generations that can be completed within the crop. Weak prevention allows the pest to enter early, making every subsequent management stage more difficult and costly. References
partTitle: Prevention, Exclusion, and Prevention of Pest Establishment in the Field

text: The nursery is one of the most sensitive stages in an integrated management program for Tuta absoluta, because infested transplants may carry eggs or larvae into the field from the very beginning of the season. Infestation introduced with transplants differs from infestation caused by moth immigration from outside the field. When the pest is transferred with transplants, infestation may appear distributed throughout the field during the first few days, and larvae may begin establishing a new generation before pheromone traps record a clear increase in moth numbers. Early infestation is particularly serious for the following reasons: The long period available for the pest to produce successive generations. The small size of transplants and their limited ability to compensate for growing-point damage. Difficulty of detecting eggs and recent mines. Possibility of distributing infested transplants over a large area. Movement of the pest from one nursery to several fields at the same time. Higher cost of discarding plants after transplanting than removing them in the nursery. Possibility of confusing Tuta infestation with transport injury, water stress, or nursery diseases. Plants intended for planting, including tomato transplants and solanaceous hosts, are recognized pathways for the movement of Phthorimaea absoluta among sites and regions. Transplant health must therefore be treated as a basic requirement for initiating the integrated management program, not as a supplementary measure. [1, 2, 3]
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Nursery management aims to achieve the following: 1. Prevent moths from entering the transplant-production area. 2. Prevent oviposition on transplants. 3. Detect infestation immediately after it appears. 4. Prevent larval movement among trays. 5. Isolate suspicious batches. 6. Dispose of infested plants safely. 7. Prevent pest movement with trays, containers, and transport vehicles. 8. Produce vigorous transplants capable of tolerating transplanting. 9. Maintain records allowing each batch to be traced. 10. Confirm that transplants are free from infestation before they leave the nursery. 11. Reinspect transplants upon arrival at the field. 12. Prevent surplus transplants from becoming a permanent pest reservoir. These objectives cannot be achieved through preventive spraying alone; they require appropriate nursery design, sanitation, inspection, and batch traceability.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: The Nursery as a Critical Point in the Pest Life Cycle
text: Transplants possess several characteristics that make them suitable for oviposition, including: Presence of young and tender leaves. Close plant spacing within trays. Continuous irrigation and growth. High plant density within a limited area. Presence of active terminal growth. Ease of larval movement between contacting leaves. Sufficient transplant-retention time for completion of part of the pest life cycle. A single fertilized female entering the nursery may lay a large number of eggs on a group of transplants, particularly when traps and regular inspections are absent. The effects of infestation are more severe in small transplants because a single mine or growing-point infestation may cause: Growth cessation. Loss of the terminal bud. Transplant deformation. Emergence of weak lateral shoots. Delayed transplanting. Poor uniformity. Transplant death in severe cases.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: The nursery should preferably be established at a site: Away from old and infested tomato fields. Away from tomato grading and packing areas. Away from piles of rejected fruits. Away from tomato-processing waste. Away from infested potato and eggplant fields. Free from volunteer tomato plants. Free from black nightshade and infested solanaceous hosts. Not directly downwind from an infested field. Where entry of workers and containers can be controlled. Whose surroundings can be monitored regularly. No single distance guarantees protection because moth movement depends on: Infestation density at the source. Wind direction. Presence of continuous hosts. Nature of the surrounding area. Duration of the nursery cycle. Presence of physical barriers. Assessment of the surrounding agricultural environment is therefore more important than relying on distance alone.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: A clean buffer zone should be established around the nursery and inspected regularly. Management of the buffer zone includes: Removal of volunteer tomato plants. Removal of infested black nightshade. Removal of wild solanaceous species carrying active infestation. Prevention of accumulation of tomato leaves and fruits. Avoidance of storage of contaminated harvest crates. Prevention of old or abandoned transplants remaining in the area. Inspection of weeds before removal to determine whether the pest is present. Installation of traps in locations suitable for detecting incoming moths. Removed plants should not be left in piles near the nursery because larvae may continue feeding or pupate and later produce adult moths.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Fully Enclosed Nursery This nursery is equipped with a structure, insect-proof screens, and controllable doors. It provides a high level of protection when: All openings are sealed.
