Screens contain no holes. Entry points are inspected continuously. Clean transplants and supplies are used. No infested batches are introduced. An enclosed nursery is not safe when infestation enters with plants, tools, or contaminated containers. Semi-Protected Nursery This nursery may be partially covered or contain inadequately sealed openings. It requires greater emphasis on: Traps. Frequent inspection. Removal of surrounding hosts. Reducing transplant-retention time. Isolation of suspicious plants. Open Nursery An open nursery is more exposed to moth immigration, particularly when located near tomato crops. Protection depends largely on: Site selection. Sanitation of the surroundings. Early monitoring. Frequent inspection. Rapid management of infestation foci. Prevention of transplants remaining after the scheduled date.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Insect-proof screens help reduce moth entry into protected nurseries. Their efficacy depends on: Mesh size being suitable for moth size. Quality of installation. Absence of gaps. Proper closure of doors. Lack of screen damage. Maintenance of adequate ventilation for transplants. Prevention of entry of infested plants from outside. Screens on walls are insufficient when: Doors are left open. Openings occur at ground level. Tears are present. Trays enter without inspection. Host weeds occur inside the structure. Screens should be inspected periodically, particularly at: Corners. Doors. Points where screens join the structure. Service openings. Locations through which hoses and cables pass.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Protected nurseries should preferably use a system that reduces direct insect entry, such as: Double-entry doors. Two doors that are not opened simultaneously. A vestibule between the outside and inside. Air or screen curtains where appropriate. Immediate door closure after workers pass. Entry of the following should be prohibited: Tomato harvest crates. Infested fruits. Leaves from external fields. Contaminated equipment. Clothing carrying plant residues. Nursery visits should be scheduled after visiting clean fields and before entering heavily infested fields, not after direct work in infested fields.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Internal sanitation includes: Daily removal of fallen leaves. Removal of dead transplants. Avoiding retention of rejected plants among trays. Cleaning walkways. Preventing weed accumulation. Cleaning beneath benches. Removing soil and plant residues. Cleaning reusable tools and trays. Disposing of surplus transplants when no longer needed. Preventing the presence of large or fruiting tomato plants inside the nursery. A small discarded leaf may contain a larva capable of completing development. Waste should therefore be collected in a closed container and not discarded near the nursery.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Tuta absoluta is not usually transmitted with tomato seed itself; transfer risk is associated with infested plants and plant parts. Nevertheless, trays and growing media require attention because of: Plant residues adhering to reused trays. Pupae occurring in cracks or beneath benches. Movement of trays from an infested nursery into a clean one. Mixing of old and new batches. Measures include: Cleaning trays before reuse. Removing root and leaf residues. Storing trays away from infested tomato crops. Keeping new trays away from residue piles. Using growing media free from host-plant residues.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: Separation of Batches Within the Nursery
text: Each transplant batch should be treated as an independent unit. The following should be recorded for each batch: Identification number. Cultivar. Sowing date. Seed source. Germination date. Tray location within the nursery. Inspection results. Treatments applied. Transport date. Destination field. Clear walkways should preferably be maintained between batches to allow: Inspection. Isolation. Identification of infestation sources. Prevention of plant mixing. Localized treatment when necessary. Mixing trays without identification makes it difficult to identify the infestation source after it appears in the field.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: Receiving Batches from Outside the Nursery
text: When transplants or plants are received from another source, they should not be placed directly among healthy transplants. The following procedure should be applied: 1. Place them in a separate receiving area. 2. Inspect leaves and growing points. 3. Review trap records or reports from the supplying nursery. 4. Inspect a representative number of trays. 5. Open suspicious mines. 6. Identify any larvae present. 7. Isolate the batch when suspicion exists. 8. Integrate it only after confirming that it is clean. A short isolation period helps reveal mines that were not visible at the time of receipt.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Pheromone traps are used for early detection of male Tuta absoluta moths. They help to: Detect moth entry. Determine the beginning of activity. Identify areas with greater exposure. Compare entrances and edges. Monitor increasing or decreasing trends. Traps should be placed: Near entrances. On sides facing infestation sources. Inside production units. Near the tray-receiving area. The number of traps should be determined according to: Nursery area. Number of separate units. Number of entrances. Surrounding risk level. Structure design. A single trap should not be relied upon in large or divided nurseries because it may not represent all areas. The trap remains a tool for monitoring males and cannot by itself confirm that transplants are free from eggs and larvae.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Trap maintenance should include: Inspection at fixed intervals. Counting suspected moths. Confirming Tuta identity. Removing excess insects when they obstruct counting. Replacing the sticky card when full or dirty. Replacing the lure according to product instructions. Recording the lure-installation date. Avoiding direct hand contact with lures when odor transfer is possible. Avoiding mixing lures for different species. Securing the trap in a clearly identified and recorded location. Catch should be recorded for each trap separately rather than only as a general total because spatial distribution may reveal the moth-entry direction.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Direct inspection of transplants is more important than relying on traps alone. Inspection should include: Cotyledons of small transplants. Newly formed true leaves. Lower leaf surfaces. Growing points. Contacting leaves. Transplants near doors. Edges facing the outside. Trays near rejected plants. Areas where traps have captured moths. Inspection frequency should increase when: Trap catches rise. An infested field is nearby. Screens are damaged. A new batch is received. Transplants remain longer than usual. Temperatures become favorable for pest development.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Inspection should not be limited to one row or one nursery area. A route covering the entire nursery should be followed, such as: A zigzag route. Sampling points distributed among corners and the center. Independent inspection of each batch. Increased sampling near doors and edges. At each point, transplants should be examined from: The beginning of trays. The middle. The end. Upper and lower shelves when trays are placed on racks. No single universal sample size applies to all nurseries. Sample size should be determined according to: Number of transplants. Number of batches. Infestation history. Risk level. Available resources. Inspection must be sufficient to detect small infestation foci before they are distributed.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Tuta absoluta eggs are small and difficult to see without training. They should be searched for mainly on: Lower leaf surfaces. Young leaves. Petioles. Tender stems. Growing points. Eggs initially appear: Cream-colored. Pale yellow. Darker as hatching approaches. When necessary, the following can be used: A hand lens. Side lighting. A dark background. Examination of selected leaves under suitable magnification. Absence of visible eggs should not be assumed to mean that the batch is pest-free because larvae may already have hatched and entered leaves.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Mines on transplants begin as: A transparent point. A small silvery area. An irregular spot. A short mine near the leaf base or vein. The following should be distinguished: An active mine containing a larva. Mechanical damage. A disease lesion. Leaf-miner fly infestation. Fertilizer or pesticide burn. A suspicious mine should be opened using a fine instrument while searching for: A small larva with a distinct head. Dark frass. An entry point. Larval movement. Cast skin.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Growing-point infestation in transplants may be more serious than a limited mine in a lower leaf. Signs include: Wilting of the growing point. Young leaves held together by silken threads. Dark frass. A hole in the tender stem. Cessation of elongation. Emergence of lateral shoots. Drying of the terminal bud. A transplant that has lost its growing point or is severely deformed should be discarded even if the larva is no longer present because transplant quality and uniformity have declined.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Liriomyza leaf-miner flies may occur in nurseries and produce mines that differ from those of Tuta. Characteristic Tuta absoluta Leaf-Miner Fly Mine shape Usually blotch-like and irregular Usually winding and serpentine Larva Distinct head and legs Maggot-like and without visible legs Frass Dark aggregations inside the mine Discontinuous line within the track Growing-point infestation Possible Unusual Stem movement Possible Less common Adult insect Small moth Small fly Diagnosis should not be based on mine shape alone, but also on the larva and adult insect.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Nursery status can be divided into practical alert levels. Green Level: No Evidence No confirmed moths in traps. No eggs. No active mines. No growing-point infestation. Preventive monitoring continues. Yellow Level: Suspicion or Limited Catch One moth or a limited number of moths recorded. Nearby infestation source. An unconfirmed mine. Screen damage. Inspection should be intensified and barriers reviewed. Orange Level: Limited Confirmed Infestation Confirmed eggs. A larva or active mine in a limited number of trays. A localized focus. The focus is isolated, its extent is determined, and tray movement is stopped. Red Level: Widespread Infestation Infestation in more than one batch. Larvae of different instars. Continued trap catches. Difficulty defining the boundaries of the focus. Suitability of the batches for transfer must be reviewed, and rejection may be safer than introducing them into the field. These levels are not formal economic thresholds, but an operational system for organizing nursery response.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: When the first confirmed egg, larva, or mine is detected: 1. Identify the batch number. 2. Stop movement of trays from that batch. 3. Mark infested plants. 4. Inspect neighboring trays. 5. Review catches from nearby traps. 6. Inspect screens and entry points. 7. Collect infested plants. 8. Determine whether the infestation is individual, localized, or widespread. 9. Record the event with date and location. 10. Reinspect after a short interval. Infested trays should not be moved to another open location without containment because this may distribute the pest within the nursery.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: When removing infested transplants: Pull out the complete plant when necessary. Prevent leaf drop. Place it directly into a sealed bag or container. Do not leave it in walkways. Do not discard it on an open compost pile. Do not use it for gap filling in fields. Dispose of it using a method that kills the pest stages present. Suitable disposal methods, subject to local regulations and available resources, include: Secure sealing followed by safe disposal. Fine shredding combined with suitable treatment. Deep burial where permitted. Heat exposure sufficient to kill all stages. Controlled thermophilic composting. Burning residues should not be treated as an automatic option unless it is legally permitted and environmentally safe.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: When Should an Individual Transplant Be Rejected?
text: A transplant should be discarded when it carries: A live larva. An active mine. Confirmed eggs. Growing-point infestation. Severe deformation. Loss of the terminal bud. Drying caused by stem boring. A transplant bearing an old empty mine may also be discarded if the damage has weakened it or if absence of other pest stages cannot be confirmed.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: When Should Surrounding Trays Be Isolated?
text: Surrounding trays should be isolated when: Their leaves contact those of infested trays. Several infested transplants occur at the same site. Moths occur near the area. Infestation boundaries cannot be defined. Trays were moved before the focus was discovered. Advanced larvae capable of movement are present. Removing only the visibly infested transplant without examining surrounding plants is insufficient because eggs and young larvae may be difficult to detect.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: Decision to Accept or Reject a Batch
text: The decision depends on: Number of infested plants. Distribution of infestation. Presence of multiple larval instars. Infestation occurring in more than one tray. Results of reinspection. History of trap catches. Proximity to the transfer date. Capacity for isolation and treatment. Value of the transplants. Risk of introducing the pest into the field. A Batch May Be Accepted After Verification When: Infestation was extremely limited. The focus was isolated. No new infestation appeared during reinspection. Trap catches were stable or absent. Remaining plants could be confirmed as clean. A Batch Should Tend Toward Rejection When: Infestation is distributed. Larvae of different instars are present. Growing points are infested. New mines continue to appear. Tray movement cannot be traced. Transplants from several batches are mixed. Freedom of remaining plants from infestation cannot be guaranteed. The decision should be more conservative when the transplants are destined for a low-infestation area or a large project.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: Role of Biological Control in the Nursery
text: Biological control may be integrated into nursery management, particularly in facilities that retain transplants for long periods. General options include: Conservation of naturally occurring predators. Use of egg parasitoids where appropriate. Use of microbial products targeting young larvae. Avoidance of insecticides highly harmful to natural enemies. Biological control should not be used to justify transfer of transplants carrying confirmed infestation into the field. The principal nursery objective is to produce plants that are as free from infestation as possible. Release programs require: Identification of the target stage. Early initiation of intervention. Suitable temperature and humidity. Insecticide compatibility. Assessment of parasitism or predation. Continuous monitoring. These aspects will be explained in detail in the biological-control sections.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: Use of Insecticides in the Nursery
text: The nursery program should not be based on repeated calendar spraying. When intervention is required, the following should be considered: Confirming pest identity. Determining whether eggs or larvae are present. Selecting an appropriate registered product. Targeting young larvae. Achieving good coverage of lower leaf surfaces. Avoiding excessive mixtures. Considering the effect on natural enemies. Rotating modes of action. Reinspecting after treatment. Not considering spraying automatic proof that transplants are pest-free. Larvae inside mines or growing points may survive. Post-treatment inspection therefore remains necessary.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: Risks of Intensive Spraying in the Nursery
text: Excessive spraying may cause: Early selection of resistant individuals. Mortality of natural enemies. Outbreaks of secondary pests. Concealment of symptoms without elimination of infestation. Increased transplant cost. Possible phytotoxicity. Greater worker exposure to insecticides. Transfer of apparently healthy transplants that still carry live larvae. Beginning the field season with a long history of exposure to chemical groups. Every product used in the nursery should be recorded so that the history of modes of action can be considered when developing the field program.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: The record for each batch should include: Item Required Details Batch number Clear identification code Cultivar Commercial or genetic name Sowing date Day and date Batch location Sector or bench number Trap results By date Inspection results Eggs, mines, larvae, and growing points Discarded plants Number and location Treatment Product or method Mode of action IRAC group number when an insecticide is used Treatment date Day and date Reinspection result After intervention Batch decision Accepted, isolated, or rejected Shipping destination Field or customer name Treatment history should be delivered to the field manager so that the same chemical group is not repeated unnecessarily.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Before transplanting, plants may be moved into a hardening area that is more open than the nursery. This stage is a critical point because: Screen protection may be reduced. Transplants are closer to fields. Their retention period may become longer. Batches prepared for shipment are placed close together. Entry of workers and transport vehicles increases. Hardening-stage measures include: Selecting an area away from infested fields. Continuing traps and inspection. Avoiding mixing of batches. Reducing waiting time. Removing any infested plant. Avoiding leaving transplants unprotected overnight where possible. Reinspection before loading.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Final inspection should be conducted immediately before shipment and should include: Reviewing the batch record. Inspecting a representative sample from all parts of the batch. Inspecting growing points. Inspecting lower leaf surfaces. Opening suspicious mines. Reviewing trap catches. Confirming that no infested leaves occur beneath trays. Confirming cleanliness of the transport vehicle. Attaching an identification label to the batch. Health certification should not depend on the most recent treatment alone, but on inspection and traceability results.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: During transport: Clean vehicles should be used. Transplants should not be transported with infested tomato fruits. Contaminated harvest crates should not be used. Leaf and plant residues should not be allowed to accumulate between trays. Stops near infested fields should be minimized. The load should be covered in a manner that reduces moth exposure without suffocating transplants. Identification of each batch should be maintained. Vehicles previously used to carry infested plants or fruits should be cleaned.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Transplants should be inspected when they arrive at the field even if they were inspected at the nursery. Infestation may occur during: Hardening. Loading. Transport. Waiting. Temporary storage. Receiving procedures include: 1. Do not distribute transplants immediately. 2. Inspect trays from different sides. 3. Review the batch number. 4. Search for eggs and mines. 5. Inspect growing points. 6. Isolate suspicious trays. 7. Record where each batch is planted. 8. Retain records of source and treatments.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

sectionTitle: Distribution of Batches Within the Field
text: Transplants from different sources should preferably not be mixed randomly. The location of each batch should be recorded because this helps interpret the pattern of infestation. If infestation appears: Distributed within one sector, transplants may be the source. Concentrated along one edge, external immigration may be the source. Associated with one cultivar or source, source-related or susceptibility differences may be involved. Near the tray-storage area, infestation may have occurred during waiting. Batch maps facilitate rapid decisions on intensive inspection.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Surplus transplants become a risk when left: Near the field. Beside a water source. In neglected trays. Without inspection or control. Until they grow into permanent hosts. The following should be implemented: Determine the number required for gap filling. Retain them for a defined period. Inspect them continuously. Dispose of them after the gap-filling period ends. Do not allow them to become large infested tomato plants. Surplus transplants may become a source of moths even when the field itself is under a good management program.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: When transplants are used to replace missing plants: Inspect each transplant before movement. Do not use transplants from a suspicious batch. Record the date and source of gap filling. Inspect replacement plants after planting. Do not move transplants from an infested field into another field. Late gap filling with unmonitored transplants introduces a new infestation source into the crop.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Indicators include: No confirmed infestation inside the nursery. Low or absent moth catches. No active mines before shipping. No growing-point infestation. Low number of discarded transplants. Complete batch records. Intact screens and controlled entrances. Absence of distributed early infestation in the field. Ability to trace any focus to its source. Reduced need for rescue sprays after transplanting. Absence of trap catches alone does not indicate program success unless it agrees with plant-inspection results.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: These include: Appearance of mines during the first days after transplanting in scattered sectors. Infestation of several plants from the same source. Detection of advanced larvae immediately after transplanting. Dead growing points before plants become established. Mixing batches and losing source identification. Absence of treatment records. Presence of infested surplus transplants near the field. Transporting transplants in contaminated vehicles. Continued appearance of infestation after removal of a limited number of plants. Reliance on spraying without reinspection.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Item Required Condition Location Relatively distant from infestation sources Surrounding area Free from infested hosts Screens Intact and properly sealed Entrances Controlled and not left open Trays Clean and clearly identified Batches Separated and numbered Traps Installed and recorded Plant inspection Regular Growing points Free from infestation Mines No active mines Rejected transplants Removed in closed containers Residues Not allowed to accumulate Treatments Recorded with mode of action Hardening area Monitored Transport Clean vehicles Pre-shipping inspection Completed and recorded Inspection on arrival Completed before distribution Surplus transplants Limited and monitored
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Date Batch Number Number of Trays Examined Number of Transplants Examined Eggs Active Mines Larvae Infested Growing Points Trap Catch Action The record should also include: Name of the inspector. Location of infestation. Photographs when necessary. Reinspection result. Batch decision.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Under Egyptian conditions, risk of Tuta movement from nurseries increases because of: Overlapping tomato planting seasons throughout the year. Presence of open nurseries near fields. Abundance of volunteer tomato plants. Reuse of trays and containers. Long-distance transport of transplants. Presence of old fields in which control has stopped. Retention of surplus transplants near farmland. High temperatures that accelerate egg hatching and larval development. Practical priorities are therefore: 1. Do not establish a nursery beside an old field. 2. Install traps before transplants appear. 3. Inspect growing points and leaves at least twice weekly, increasing frequency when risk rises. 4. Keep each transplant source isolated. 5. Do not transport batches carrying confirmed infestation. 6. Dispose of rejected transplants immediately. 7. Record insecticides used in the nursery. 8. Reinspect transplants in the field. 9. Remove surplus transplants after gap filling is completed. 10. Monitor the first three weeks after transplanting as a period for detecting introduced infestation. The number of inspections is not a fixed rule. A high-risk nursery may require more frequent inspection.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Error One: Assuming the Nursery Is Free from Tuta The moth can lay eggs on small transplants. Error Two: Relying on Traps Alone Traps monitor males and do not detect all eggs and larvae. Error Three: Treating Spraying as Proof of Pest-Free Transplants Larvae may remain inside mines or growing points. Error Four: Removing an Infested Transplant and Leaving It Beside the Nursery The larva may continue developing and later produce a moth. Error Five: Mixing Batches This prevents source tracing and makes isolation difficult. Error Six: Neglecting the Hardening Area Infestation may occur after the protected-production stage ends. Error Seven: Failing to Inspect Transplants on Arrival Infestation may enter during transport and waiting. Error Eight: Retaining Surplus Transplants for Too Long They become a pest reservoir. Error Nine: Transporting Transplants with Produce Crates Pest stages may move with residues and containers. Error Ten: Randomly Using Reduced Rates Treatment may fail and increase resistance selection pressure. Error Eleven: Repeating the Same Chemical Group in the Nursery and Field Selection pressure begins before the field season starts. Error Twelve: Ignoring Growing-Point Infestation An infested growing point may be more serious than a limited mine in an old leaf.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Before Transplant Production Begins Select the site. Remove surrounding hosts. Clean the structure and trays. Repair screens. Install traps. Establish the batch-numbering system. After Germination Begin visual inspection. Monitor traps. Remove weeds. Prevent entry of outside plants. During Transplant Growth Inspect leaves and growing points. Record results. Isolate any focus. Apply intervention when required. Evaluate intervention results. During Hardening Increase attention to immigration sources. Do not mix batches. Reduce waiting time. Inspect transplants before loading. Before Dispatch Conduct final inspection. Review records. Clean the transport vehicle. Issue a batch identification card. Upon Arrival at the Field Reinspect transplants. Isolate suspicious material. Record the location of each batch. Dispose of trays and residues properly. After Transplanting Inspect sectors according to transplant source. Search for early infestation. Dispose of surplus transplants after gap filling ends.
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: The nursery is a critical point in the prevention of Tuta absoluta because transfer of infested transplants establishes the pest in the field from the beginning of the season. Nursery management depends on an integrated set of procedures including: Selecting a site away from infestation sources. Using intact screens and barriers. Controlling entry and movement. Maintaining nursery sanitation. Separating transplant batches. Using pheromone traps. Direct inspection for eggs, mines, and growing-point infestation. Isolating infestation foci. Safe disposal of infested transplants. Recording all treatments. Final inspection before dispatch. Cleaning transport vehicles. Reinspection upon arrival at the field. Monitoring surplus transplants. Spraying transplants before transport is insufficient to declare them pest-free. The decision must depend on inspection, traceability, and absence of active infestation. Each batch should be treated as an independent unit so that its source, inspection results, treatments, and planting location can be identified. The best protection is achieved when nursery management begins before transplant emergence and continues through the first weeks after transplanting in the field. References
partTitle: Nursery and Transplant Management and Prevention of Pest Transfer to the Field

text: Regular monitoring is the foundation linking knowledge of the pest to control decisions. Genuine integrated pest management cannot be implemented on the basis of guesswork, observation of damage only after it has spread, or a fixed spray program applied every few days regardless of field conditions. Monitoring aims to detect Tuta absoluta before larvae reach growing points, stems, and fruits; determine population trends; identify the stages present in the field; and evaluate whether preventive, biological, and chemical measures are producing the required results. Early detection is particularly important because: Eggs are small and difficult to detect without systematic inspection. Newly hatched larvae enter plant tissues shortly after hatching. Larvae inside mines are less exposed to control measures. Fruit infestation cannot be reversed after it occurs. Generations overlap rapidly under favorable conditions. Increased moth catches may precede visible damage. Neighboring old fields may cause sudden immigration waves. Infestation may begin with transplants or along one field edge. Monitoring is not limited to pheromone traps. It depends on integrating several sources of information, including: 1. Pheromone traps. 2. Direct plant inspection. 3. Egg counts. 4. Counts of active mines. 5. Counts of live larvae. 6. Inspection of growing points and flowers. 7. Fruit inspection. 8. Monitoring of natural enemies. 9. Monitoring temperature and accumulated degree-days. 10. Monitoring neighboring fields and immigration sources.

A monitoring program aims to: Confirm pest presence or absence. Determine the date of first detection. Detect the beginning of moth flight. Identify the most exposed field edges and sectors. Determine whether infestation was introduced with transplants or came from an external source. Detect eggs before hatching. Determine the dominant larval instars. Distinguish old mines from recent mines. Identify the beginning of growing-point or fruit infestation. Assess the role of natural enemies. Select the timing of intervention. Evaluate treatment results. Detect reduced insecticide efficacy or possible resistance. Predict appearance of the next generation. Document infestation history throughout the season. Provide early warning to neighboring fields.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Detection, Monitoring, Assessment, and Warning
text: Detection Detection Detection aims to determine whether the pest is present or absent. It is used particularly: Before planting. In newly invaded areas. In nurseries. At the beginning of the season. After a host-free period. Initial detection may be confirmed by finding: A confirmed moth. An egg. A larva. An active mine. An infested fruit. Monitoring Monitoring Monitoring means collecting data periodically to determine pest-population trends. It answers questions such as: Are numbers increasing or decreasing? Where is infestation concentrated? Which stage is dominant? Is a new generation present? Was the previous intervention successful? Infestation Assessment Infestation Assessment This means measuring infestation on plants through: Percentage of infested plants. Number of eggs per plant. Number of active mines. Number of live larvae. Percentage of infested growing points. Percentage of infested fruits. Early Warning Early Warning Early warning is the use of trap results, plant inspection, temperature, and surrounding information to predict increasing risk before major economic loss occurs.

Pheromone traps are used at low numerical density for monitoring, whereas they are used at high density for mass trapping. The two systems must be distinguished. Monitoring Traps Their purpose is to: Detect pest presence. Monitor male flight. Determine population trends. Identify the timing of inspection and intervention. They are not expected to reduce the pest population substantially when used alone. Mass-Trapping Traps A large number of traps is used to capture a substantial proportion of males and reduce mating opportunities. Published mass-trapping densities may range between: 20 and 50 traps per hectare or more Depending on trap type, production system, and level of enclosure. These densities should not be used as monitoring densities.

Pheromone traps use a synthetic sex pheromone that mimics the odor released by females to attract males. Pheromone traps primarily capture male Tuta absoluta moths. Their advantages include: Relatively high specificity. Ability to detect the pest at low population densities. Continuous operation day and night without labor. Allowing comparison of activity among weeks. Detecting the beginning of flight before severe damage appears. Leaving no residues on the crop. However, they do not directly measure: Number of females. Number of eggs. Hatching percentage. Number of larvae within plants. Percentage of fruit infestation. Level of economic loss.

sectionTitle: Types of Traps Used for Monitoring
text: Delta Sticky Trap Delta Sticky Trap It consists of: A triangular housing. A sticky base. A pheromone lure. Its advantages include: Easy counting. Easy transport and installation. Suitability for detection and monitoring. Possibility of retaining moths for examination. Its disadvantages include: Saturation of the sticky card with insects. Dust accumulation. Reduced adhesive performance under heat and dust. Difficult counting at high pest densities. Water Trap It consists of a container holding: Water. A small quantity of a surface-tension-reducing material, such as unscented soap. A support holding the pheromone lure above the water. Its advantages include: High capture capacity. Low risk of rapid saturation. Suitability for mass trapping. Good performance in open fields. It requires: Monitoring of water level. Cleaning of the container. Replacement of evaporated water. Prevention of filling with dust and leaves. Secure placement to prevent overturning. Funnel or Bucket Traps Moths enter through an opening into a container from which escape is difficult. Their efficacy varies according to design, pheromone, and installation site. Light Traps Light attracts males, females, and other insects. Light traps may be used in research or specialized systems, but they: Are less specific. May capture natural enemies and beneficial insects. Require a power source. Are not a standard substitute for pheromone traps in routine field monitoring.

Monitoring traps should be installed: Approximately one to two weeks before transplanting when possible. Or when field preparation begins. And maintained throughout the season. They may remain for a period after harvest when immigration sources are being monitored. Early installation provides benefits including: Detecting moth presence before transplants enter the field. Comparing pressure before and after planting. Identifying fields contributing to immigration. Detecting the first flight peak. Preventing delayed initiation of plant inspection. Trap installation should not be delayed until mines appear.

Recommendations vary according to field area, shape, and surrounding conditions. References for open fields indicate approximately: 2–3 monitoring traps per hectare. Because one feddan is approximately 0.42 hectare, using: One trap per feddan Is equivalent to approximately: 2.38 traps per hectare And represents a practical monitoring rate within the commonly used range for open fields. This number is not rigid. Additional traps may be needed when: The field is large. Its shape is irregular. More than one direction presents risk. Edges are adjacent to old fields. Planting dates differ markedly within the site. Several cultivars or separate sectors are present. One trap cannot adequately represent the field. One trap per feddan for monitoring must not be confused with the high densities used for mass trapping.

sectionTitle: Distribution of Traps Within the Field
text: Traps should be distributed to represent both the field and risk sources. Distribution includes: One trap inside the field away from edges. One or more traps along high-risk edges. Traps facing old fields. A trap near the transplant-collection area during planting. Traps in different sectors when clear variation exists. All traps should not be placed on one edge because this does not represent the field interior. Traps should also not be placed: Directly beside a heavily dusted road. Beneath a sprinkler or water source. Inside a closed canopy that blocks air movement. Near another pheromone source in a way that causes interference. In a location inaccessible for counting and maintenance.

Trap height changes as plants grow. In practice: Before planting: approximately 20 cm above the soil surface. After transplanting: near the top of the canopy. As plants grow: traps are raised gradually. In many cases: height does not exceed approximately 50–60 cm in determinate field tomatoes. Some experiments have shown higher male catches when traps were placed at heights not exceeding 60 cm compared with higher positions. The lure and trap should remain within the airflow zone without being buried inside foliage or raised far above the crop.

Each trap should receive a permanent identifier, such as: T1. T2. T3. The following should be recorded for each trap: Coordinates or site description. Installation date. Edge direction. Cultivar or sector. Trap type. Lure type. Lure-replacement date. Sticky-card replacement date. Number of moths at each reading. Traps should not be moved repeatedly because this disrupts temporal comparisons. When relocation is necessary, record: Date of movement. Previous location. New location. Reason for movement.

The following should be observed: Store lures according to manufacturer instructions. Avoid exposure to high temperatures before use. Do not open the package before installation. Use gloves or clean forceps when possible. Do not handle different pheromone types with the same tool. Do not place the lure directly on an adhesive surface that covers it. Record the installation date. Replace the lure according to its specified service life. Many sources recommend replacing monitoring lures after approximately: 4–6 weeks However, actual longevity varies according to: Dispenser type. Pheromone load. Temperature. Wind. Storage quality. Manufacturer recommendation. High temperatures increase pheromone-release rate and may shorten the effective life of some lures.

sectionTitle: Replacement of Sticky Cards and Water
text: Sticky Cards They should be replaced when: Filled with moths. Covered with dust. Adhesiveness declines. Many leaves adhere to them. Counting becomes inaccurate. Weekly replacement may be required in very dusty areas or under high moth density. Water Traps Water should be checked regularly and replenished when: Evaporation occurs. Leakage occurs. The trap becomes filled with soil or insects. Water depth declines. A limited amount of unscented soap or a suitable oil may be added to reduce surface tension and prevent moth escape.

Traps are generally read: At least once weekly. Reading frequency should increase to twice weekly when: Numbers begin increasing. Old fields are nearby. Temperature rises. The crop enters flowering and fruiting. Active plant infestation is recorded. Weekly accumulation makes counting difficult. A rapid response to an immigration wave is being evaluated. Readings should preferably be taken on the same day each week so that time intervals remain comparable.

The actual number in each individual trap should be recorded. Terms such as the following are insufficient: Low. Moderate. High. When reading intervals differ, calculate: Mean daily catch = Number of moths captured ÷ Number of days since the previous reading Example: If a trap captures 35 moths in seven days: 35 ÷ 7 = 5 moths per trap per day. If the interval is five days, the result should not be compared directly with a seven-day reading until standardized on a daily basis.

Moth capture indicates active males in the area, but by itself does not prove economic loss. Historical advisory levels have included: Number of Males per Trap per Week Advisory Risk Level 1–3 Low 4–30 Moderate More than 30 High However, these values: Are not universal economic thresholds. Should not be used alone to trigger spraying. Are affected by trap and pheromone type. Are affected by lure performance. Are affected by trap location and height. Are affected by immigration and wind. May vary among seasons. These levels should be used as advisory signals for intensifying inspection, not as fixed commands for chemical intervention.

sectionTitle: Why Trap Catches Do Not Always Correspond to Damage
text: High trap catches may occur without high plant infestation because of: Immigration of males from a neighboring field. Low numbers of females. Successful egg parasitism. Predation of eggs and larvae. Low egg-hatching percentage. Failure of moths to establish within the field. Presence of a highly attractive trap. High damage may occur with low trap catches because of: Immigration of fertilized females. Weak lure performance. Poor trap location. Damage to the sticky card. Larvae resulting from an earlier infestation. Infestation introduced with transplants. A hatching wave occurring after male flight declines. Trap data must therefore always be linked with plant inspection.

sectionTitle: Trend Is More Important Than a Single Reading
text: A sequence of readings is more valuable than a number from one week. Important trends include: Stable low catch. Gradual increase. Sudden surge. Decline after treatment. Increase along one edge only. Simultaneous increase in all traps. Gradual Increase This may indicate establishment of a population within the area. Sudden Surge This may indicate: An immigration wave. Emergence of a generation from pupae. Removal of a neighboring field. Cessation of spraying in an old field. Decline This may result from: Successful management. Weather changes. Weak lure. Trap saturation. Trap relocation. Every decline should not be assumed to represent success without reviewing trap condition and plant infestation.

Direct plant inspection is the method that confirms whether captured moths have resulted in: Oviposition. Hatching. Active mines. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation. Inspection should be: Regular. Distributed throughout the field. Methodologically consistent. Comparable among weeks. Conducted separately for field edges and the interior.

sectionTitle: Division of the Field into Sectors
text: The field should be divided into homogeneous sectors according to: Cultivar. Transplanting date. Transplant source. Soil type. Irrigation direction. Proximity to old fields. Growth level. Treatment history. A large heterogeneous field should not be treated as a single unit. In large areas, the site may be divided into units of approximately: 2–5 feddans per sector Depending on the degree of uniformity. Each sector should have: An inspection route. Trap data. An infestation record. A management decision when necessary.

The following model may be used in small and medium-sized open fields: Select 5–10 points distributed within the sector. Inspect 5–10 plants at each point. Examine no fewer than approximately 50 plants per homogeneous sector. Add 10–20 plants along the highest-risk edge. Change the individual plants examined each week while maintaining the same overall distribution route. This model is not a fixed international threshold, but an operational protocol whose sample size should increase when: The area is large. Infestation is focal. More than one immigration source exists. Plant ages or cultivars differ. Harvest is approaching. Fruit infestation appears.

The inspection route should cover: All four edges. The field interior. Areas near entrances. Transplant-storage areas. Previous infestation foci. Prevailing wind direction. Sides facing old fields. Possible routes include: A W-shaped zigzag route. A double diagonal route. A fixed grid of sampling points. Separate routes for field edges and the interior. Inspection should not be limited to a single row, and apparently healthy plants should not be selected exclusively.

The following should be examined on each plant: Young Growth Growing point. The last three fully expanded leaves. Petioles. Buds. Young leaves are important sites for egg detection. Middle Section One or more leaves. Flower clusters. Leaves close to fruits. Lower Section Older leaves. Old and active mines. Potential pupation sites. Fruits Beginning at fruit set, inspect: The area beneath the calyx. Fruits touching leaves. Green fruits. Fruits close to the soil. Deformed or soft fruits.

The following should be recorded: Number of plants carrying eggs. Number of eggs per plant. Egg location. Egg color. Presence of blackened or parasitized eggs. Presence of empty hatched eggshells. Priority should be given to examining: The last three fully expanded leaves. Lower leaf surfaces. Petioles. Young growth. Egg counting requires: Good lighting. A hand lens. Training. Sufficient time. Viable eggs should not be combined with parasitized eggs or empty shells.

The following should be recorded for each mine: Is it recent or old? Does it contain a live larva? What is the approximate larval size? Is fresh frass present? Is the mine expanding? Is an exit hole present? Was the damage caused by Tuta or another insect? The combined total of old and recent mines should not be used as the sole indicator. It is preferable to record: Active mines per plant. Live larvae per plant. Percentage of plants bearing active mines.

Characteristic Active Mine Old Mine Color Pale or silvery Brown and dry Larva Usually present Absent Frass Fresh and dark Old and dry Tissue Relatively soft Brittle and torn Expansion Continuing Stopped Importance for decisions High Limited An empty old mine does not require direct targeting, but should be recorded to assess previous damage.

Estimating larval instar helps in selecting the intervention. Early Instars They are: Small. Pale-colored. Associated with limited mines. More exposed to biological and selective insecticides. Third and Fourth Instars They are: Larger. More destructive. Capable of movement. More likely to enter growing points, stems, and fruits. Simultaneous presence of multiple instars indicates: Continued oviposition. Overlapping generations. The inadequacy of relying on a single treatment. The need to monitor new hatching.

The following should be recorded: Percentage of plants with infested growing points. Presence of live larvae. Presence of frass. Death of the growing point. Emergence of lateral growth. Petiole or stem infestation. This indicator becomes particularly important: During early crop stages. In recently transplanted plants. When advanced larval instars are present. Before fruit infestation begins.

Fruit inspection should begin at fruit set and should not be delayed until coloration begins. The following should be recorded: Number of fruits inspected. Number of fruits bearing holes. Number of fruits containing larvae. Number of rotted fruits. Infestation location. Presence of infestation beneath the calyx. Presence of fallen fruits. Calculate: Infested fruits (%) = Number of infested fruits ÷ Total number of fruits examined × 100 For processing tomatoes, also calculate: Weight loss (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100

sectionTitle: Inspection of Field Edges and Hotspots
text: Field edges should be inspected independently because the field average may conceal a severe focus. Hotspots include: The edge facing an old field. Transplant-storage areas. Areas near residue piles. Areas near volunteer plants. Areas receiving poor spray coverage. Areas with dense vegetative growth. Sectors where infestation occurred during the previous week. The overall field average should not be used alone to make decisions about a focus that may later spread.

Natural enemies should be recorded alongside the pest. Monitoring includes: Parasitized eggs. Parasitoid larvae. Predators. Mirid bugs. Lacewings. Spiders. Other beneficial insects. Larvae killed by fungi or bacteria. This information affects decision-making because strong biological activity may limit pest population growth, whereas a broad-spectrum insecticide may remove this natural regulation.

Early-warning indicators include: 1. First moth captured in a trap. 2. Increased trap catches for two consecutive weeks. 3. Increased catches along a specific field edge. 4. Presence of an old field where control has stopped. 5. Sudden removal of a neighboring infested crop. 6. Detection of the first egg. 7. Detection of recently hatched eggshells. 8. Appearance of the first active mine. 9. Detection of the first larva in a growing point. 10. Beginning of fruit set while advanced larvae are present. 11. Increase in temperatures favorable for development. 12. Decline of natural enemies after insecticide use. Risk increases as more indicators occur simultaneously.

Green Level: Low Risk No catch or intermittent low catch. No confirmed eggs. No active mines. No growing-point or fruit infestation. Action: Continue monitoring. Maintain traps. Conduct weekly inspection. Monitor neighboring fields. Yellow Level: Early Warning Beginning of catch or limited increase. A nearby external source. Detection of a limited number of eggs. No fruit infestation. Action: Increase inspection intensity. Examine the last three leaves. Review field edges. Assess natural enemies. Prepare for intervention targeting eggs or hatching larvae. Orange Level: Active Infestation Continuing increase in catch. Presence of recent mines. Presence of live larvae. Beginning of growing-point infestation. Presence of multiple larval instars. Action: Identify infestation foci. Apply the appropriate intervention. Protect fruits. Reinspect after intervention. Assess neighboring fields. Red Level: High Risk Widespread infestation. Advanced larvae. Growing-point and stem infestation. Beginning of fruit infestation. Strong immigration wave. Reduced effect of the previous intervention. Action: Urgent integrated intervention. Prevent further fruit infestation. Review modes of action. Assess resistance and spray coverage. Manage the immigration source. Increase monitoring frequency. Critical Level Clearly elevated fruit infestation. Widespread L3 and L4 larvae. Fruit rot. Canopy collapse. Continued immigration. Action: Protect the remaining crop. Sort and remove infestation sources. Review the economic feasibility of treatments. Prevent the field from becoming a source for other fields.

sectionTitle: Use of Degree-Days in Warning Systems
text: Temperature data can be used to predict the rate of generation development. The first sustained increase in moth catches may be used as an approximate starting point: Biofix Degree-days are then accumulated. For the egg-to-adult cycle, an approximate model may use: Lower developmental threshold: approximately 8°C. Thermal requirement: approximately 416.7 degree-days. Daily degree-days are calculated in simplified form as: Daily degree-days = Mean daily temperature − 8 When the mean is above 8°C. This model should not be used alone to determine spray timing, but to assist in: Predicting accelerated hatching. Estimating emergence of a new adult generation. Intensifying inspection at the appropriate time. Interpreting trap peaks. Thermal models vary among studies and regions and should therefore be calibrated using local field data.

Percentage of Infested Plants Number of plants with active infestation ÷ Total number of plants examined × 100 Percentage of Infested Leaves Number of leaves with active mines ÷ Total number of leaves examined × 100 Mean Number of Larvae per Plant Total number of live larvae ÷ Number of plants examined Percentage of Infested Growing Points Number of plants with infested growing points ÷ Total number of plants examined × 100 Percentage of Infested Fruits Number of infested fruits ÷ Total number of fruits examined × 100 Trap-Catch Rate of Change Current-week catch ÷ Previous-week catch If the result is: Less than 1: downward trend. Approximately equal to 1: stable. Greater than 1: upward trend. This rate must be interpreted together with plant inspection and lure condition.

sectionTitle: Monitoring Timing According to Crop Stage
text: Before Transplanting Install traps. Monitor the production area. Inspect neighboring fields. Identify high-risk edges. From Transplanting to Three Weeks Search for infestation introduced with transplants. Inspect growing points. Compare sectors according to transplant source. Monitor the first eggs and mines. Vegetative Growth Monitor mines. Estimate larval instars. Record growing-point infestation. Assess natural enemies. Flowering and Fruit Set Increase inspection of growing points and clusters. Begin inspection of small fruits. Prevent larvae from moving into fruits. Fruit Filling Focus on the area beneath the calyx. Increase the number of fruits examined. Estimate numerical and weight loss. Consider the insecticide preharvest interval. Before and During Harvest Monitor fruit infestation. Sort infested fruits. Prevent accumulation of rejected fruits. Evaluate whether further intervention remains necessary. After Harvest Continue traps for an appropriate period. Estimate moth emergence. Guide crop-residue removal. Warn neighboring fields.

A treatment should not be judged immediately on the following day. Evaluation should be conducted according to the speed of action of the method used and should include: New eggs. New mines. Live larvae. Continued mine expansion. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation. Trap catches. Condition of natural enemies. The following should be distinguished: Old damage that remains visible. New infestation after intervention. Larvae killed inside mines. Larvae that survived or moved. Success should not be measured by disappearance of old mines because mines remain visible on leaves after larvae die.

A simple data-sharing system can be established among growers or between a company and contracted fields. The weekly report should include: Mean trap catch. Highest trap catch. Direction of change. Percentage of infested plants. Percentage of infested fruits. Presence of advanced larvae. Condition of old fields. Risk level. Recommended action. Priority warnings should be issued when: Trap catches surge. An old field is removed. Spraying stops in a neighboring source field. Fruit infestation appears. Efficacy of an insecticide group declines. An infestation wave appears in several neighboring fields.
Date Trap Number Location Number of Days in Reading Interval Number of Moths Moths/Day Lure Condition Trap Condition Notes
Date Sector Plants Examined Plants with Eggs Plants with Active Mines Live Larvae Infested Growing Points Fruits Examined Infested Fruits Natural Enemies

Trap Data Mean catch: Highest trap: Lowest trap: Trend compared with the previous week: Direction of risk: Plant Data Percentage of infested plants: Mean active mines: Mean live larvae: Percentage of infested growing points: Percentage of infested fruits: Biological Status Presence of parasitized eggs: Presence of predators: Presence of diseased larvae: Risk Level Green. Yellow. Orange. Red. Critical. Action Continue monitoring. Increase inspection. Treat infestation foci. Release a biological-control agent. Use a microbial product. Apply a selective chemical intervention. Manage an external source. Reevaluate.

Error One: Depending on One Trap in an Unrepresentative Location It may fail to detect trends along other edges or sectors. Error Two: Moving a Trap Without Recording the Change This invalidates comparison among readings. Error Three: Comparing Readings Taken Over Different Time Intervals Results should be converted to moths per day or reading duration should be standardized. Error Four: Failing to Replace the Lure The population may appear low while the pheromone is weak. Error Five: Treating Trap Counts as an Independent Spray Threshold Traps measure males and do not directly measure damage. Error Six: Counting Old Mines as Active Infestation This may lead to unnecessary spraying. Error Seven: Inspecting Only the Field Interior Infestation may begin along the edges. Error Eight: Inspecting Only Field Edges Infestation introduced with transplants may appear internally. Error Nine: Failing to Open Mines Tuta infestation may be confused with leaf-miner flies. Error Ten: Ignoring Growing Points and Fruits Infestation there may be more serious than visible leaf damage. Error Eleven: Ignoring Natural Enemies This may result in unnecessary or harmful chemical decisions. Error Twelve: Judging Treatment Success from Old Mines Old mines remain visible even after successful control. Error Thirteen: Combining Trap Counts Without Separating Locations A strong immigration source may disappear within the general average. Error Fourteen: Failing to Monitor Neighboring Fields A new crop may be surprised by a severe immigration wave.

sectionTitle: Indicators of a Successful Monitoring Program
text: A monitoring program is successful when it achieves: Early detection of first appearance. Identification of the infestation source. Documentation of trap trends. Detection of eggs before mines increase. Targeting larvae during early instars. Prevention or reduction of fruit infestation. Detection of foci before they spread. Evaluation of intervention efficacy. Reduction of unnecessary sprays. Protection of natural enemies. Provision of warnings to neighboring fields. Maintenance of comparable records.

Monitoring and early detection form the core information system of integrated management of Tuta absoluta. Pheromone traps are used to detect male moths and monitor flight activity, but by themselves do not measure the level of economic damage. One monitoring trap per feddan may be adopted in open-field tomatoes as a practical rate equivalent to approximately 2.38 traps per hectare, with additional traps placed along edges or near risk sources when necessary. Traps should be read at least weekly, with more frequent readings when risk increases, and each trap should be recorded separately. True assessment depends on plant inspection to determine: Eggs. Active mines. Live larvae. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation. Natural-enemy activity. Weekly catches of 1–3, 4–30, or more than 30 moths do not represent fixed economic thresholds. They are historical advisory levels that must be interpreted together with plant condition. The direction of change between weeks is more useful than a single reading, and old infestation must be distinguished from active infestation. Integrating traps, plant inspection, temperature, and information from neighboring fields enables early warning and intervention before larvae reach fruits. References

Monitoring does not aim merely to collect trap and larval counts. Its purpose is to convert these data into an appropriate management decision that prevents Tuta absoluta from reaching a level that causes economic loss. The presence of the pest in the field does not necessarily require insecticide use. The field may contain: A limited number of moths without successful oviposition. Eggs with a high proportion exposed to parasitism or predation. Old mines without larvae. Low leaf infestation that the plant can compensate for. A small focus that can be contained locally. Natural enemies capable of slowing pest increase. Conversely, an apparently low pest density may require rapid intervention when: The crop is at a sensitive stage. Larvae begin entering fruits. The target market is highly sensitive to defects. An immigration wave is arriving from a neighboring field. Temperature rises and the hatching period becomes shorter. Infestation occurs in growing points or clusters. Harvest is approaching and insecticide options are restricted by preharvest intervals. The decision must therefore be based on a group of indicators rather than on a single number.

Economic Injury Level Economic Injury Level — EIL This is the lowest pest density at which the monetary value of the expected loss equals the cost of intervention. At this point: Value of the crop protected by intervention = Cost of implementing the intervention If pest density continues to increase above the economic injury level, the expected loss becomes greater than the cost of control. Intervention should not be delayed until the pest reaches this point because control requires time before its effect becomes apparent. Economic Threshold Economic Threshold — ET This is the pest density at which intervention should begin to prevent the population from reaching the economic injury level. The economic threshold is usually lower than the economic injury level. The relationship can be expressed simply as: Economic Threshold < Economic Injury Level The economic threshold is also known as: Action Threshold. Control Threshold. Treatment Threshold. However, these terms may be used differently among studies, so the definition used in each study should be reviewed before comparing numerical values. Warning Level This is a level below the intervention threshold indicating the need to: Intensify inspection. Review trap data. Examine eggs. Assess natural enemies. Prepare for possible intervention. The warning level does not mean immediate chemical treatment. Decision Point This is the moment at which all available information is reviewed to determine the most appropriate action. The decision may be: Continue monitoring only. Increase inspection intensity. Remove a limited focus. Release a parasitoid or predator. Use a microbial product. Increase trap density. Manage an external immigration source. Use a selective insecticide. Apply no treatment. The intervention threshold is therefore not always equivalent to a “spray threshold.”

sectionTitle: General Equation for the Economic Injury Level
text: The economic injury level can be calculated using the general equation: EIL = C ÷ (V × I × D × K) Where: Symbol Meaning C Cost of control per unit area V Market value per unit of crop I Amount of injury or damage caused by one pest individual D Crop loss resulting from one unit of injury K Proportion of pest reduction achieved by the intervention The equation shows that the economic injury level is not a fixed number. When Crop Value Increases The pest density justifying intervention decreases because loss of a small quantity of a high-value crop is costly. When Control Cost Increases The economic injury level increases because intervention becomes profitable only when a larger loss is expected. When the Control Method Is Highly Effective The intervention threshold decreases relatively because the intervention can prevent a larger proportion of the loss. When Control Efficacy Is Low Intervention may not be economically justified even when infestation is present, or more than one method may need to be integrated.

sectionTitle: Why Is There No Universal Fixed Threshold for Tuta absoluta?
text: The economic threshold varies among countries, regions, and fields because of differences in: Cultivar. Plant age. Plant density. Vegetative-growth level. Timing of infestation. Market type. Crop price. Control cost. Insecticide efficacy. Natural enemies. Climate. Season length. Percentage of fruit infestation. Sampling method. Measurement unit. One study may express infestation as: Larvae per plant. Another may use: Larvae per 20 leaves. Percentage of infested leaves. Percentage of infested plants. Number of active mines. Males per trap per day. Percentage loss of leaf area. Percentage of infested fruits. These units cannot be converted directly into one another without a locally established experimental relationship.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Leaf-Based and Fruit-Based Thresholds
text: Leaf injury and fruit infestation must be considered separately. Leaf-Based Threshold This measures: Number of mines. Number of larvae. Percentage of infested leaves. Area of leaf injury. Tomato plants may compensate for part of the leaf injury, particularly during early vegetative growth. Fruit-Based Threshold This is more sensitive because an infested fruit may become unmarketable or unsuitable for processing. A small percentage of fruit infestation may be more costly than a higher percentage of leaf mining. The intervention threshold therefore declines as: Larvae approach fruits. Cluster formation begins. Fruit market value increases. Production is intended for export or premium markets. The probability of secondary rots increases.

Greenhouse Study in Ethiopia One greenhouse study estimated: Economic injury level: approximately 3 larvae per plant. Economic threshold: approximately 2.25 larvae per plant. The economic threshold was calculated to allow intervention before the population reached the economic injury level. This value should not be transferred directly to Egyptian open fields because it was developed under: Greenhouse conditions. Specific cultivars and prices. Particular control costs. Different climatic and production conditions. Open-Field Study in Iran A study estimating crop losses on different cultivars found that the economic injury level ranged approximately between: 4.15 and 5.04 larvae per plant The value differed among cultivars, confirming that: Plant response to damage differs. Production value differs. Compensatory capacity differs. The threshold is not a fixed property of the pest alone. Trap-Based Thresholds Some older systems used advisory male-catch values such as: 1–3 moths per trap per week: low risk. 4–30 moths per trap per week: moderate risk. More than 30 moths per trap per week: high risk. Some studies or extension programs reported much higher values, such as: Approximately 45 moths or more per trap per day in one Brazilian program. Approximately 100 males per trap per day in some older recommendations. These differences do not necessarily mean that one value is correct and another is wrong. They reflect differences in: Trap type. Lure strength. Trap density. Production system. Climatic conditions. Trap-capture efficiency. Local relationship between male catch and damage. Trap counts should therefore not be used alone as a fixed economic threshold.

sectionTitle: Limitations of Using Traps for Decision-Making
text: Pheromone traps primarily capture males, whereas actual damage depends on: Number of fertilized females. Number of eggs laid. Hatching percentage. Larval survival. Natural enemies. Ability of larvae to reach fruits. The following situations may occur: High Catch with Low Plant Infestation Because of: Immigration of males from outside the field. Low numbers of females. High levels of parasitism and predation. Poor plant suitability. Failure of part of the eggs to hatch. Low Catch with High Plant Infestation Because of: Presence of fertilized females. Weak lure performance. Incorrect trap placement. Infestation introduced with transplants. Larvae resulting from a previous wave. Trap saturation or loss of adhesiveness. Traps are therefore used to determine: Activity trends. Beginning of flight waves. Risk locations. Timing for intensified inspection. They should not be used alone to issue a spray decision.

sectionTitle: Indicators That Must Be Integrated into the Decision
text: Management decisions are based on six groups of indicators. First: Adult-Insect Indicators Number of males in traps. Weekly rate of increase. Distribution of catch among traps. Presence of an immigration wave. Proximity of an old or neglected field. Second: Egg Indicators Percentage of plants carrying eggs. Number of eggs per plant. Percentage of parasitized eggs. Presence of recently hatched eggshells. Location of eggs on the plant. Third: Larval Indicators Number of live larvae per plant. Dominant larval instars. Percentage of infested plants. Number of active mines. Presence of larvae in growing points or stems. Fourth: Fruit Indicators Percentage of infested fruits. Percentage of fruits containing larvae. Presence of rots. Numerical loss. Weight loss. Fifth: Crop Indicators Plant age. Flowering and fruit-set stage. Canopy density. Plant compensatory capacity. Proximity to harvest. Market type. Sixth: Natural-Regulation Indicators Percentage of parasitized eggs. Predator density. Presence of larvae killed by pathogens. Effect of previous insecticides on natural enemies.

Transplant and Early-Growth Stage Priority is given to protecting: The growing point. The main stem. Young leaves. Limited growing-point infestation may require faster intervention than a similar number of mines on leaves of a large plant. Risk increases when: Infestation is distributed throughout the field. Advanced larvae are present. The main growing point is infested. Temperature is high. Infestation was confirmed to have arrived with transplants. Vegetative-Growth Stage The plant has relatively greater capacity to compensate for limited leaf injury. The following should be considered: Rate of appearance of new mines. Number of live larvae. Continuation of growth. Natural enemies. Trap trend. The presence of old dry mines alone does not justify treatment. Flowering and Fruit-Set Stage Tolerance of infestation declines because: Leaves supply the clusters. Larvae may enter buds. Small fruits begin to appear. Advanced larvae move toward the calyx and fruits. Greater weight should be given in the decision to: Growing-point and cluster infestation. Presence of L3 and L4. Increased egg density. Proximity of larvae to fruits. Fruit-Filling Stage Priority shifts to preventing fruit infestation. A low number of advanced larvae near clusters may justify faster intervention than a higher number of young larvae in leaves distant from fruits. The following should be considered: Inspection beneath the calyx. Percentage of infested fruits. Presence of rots. Preharvest interval. Remaining intervention options. Near Harvest The economic value of intervention changes as the remaining period becomes shorter. Consider: Number of days until harvest. Preharvest interval. Speed of action of the method. Presence of a new hatching wave. Percentage of infested fruits. Value of the crop that can still be protected. Biological intervention or removal of foci and infested fruits may be more appropriate than an insecticide with a long preharvest interval.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Fresh-Market and Processing Tomatoes
text: Fresh-Market Tomatoes The threshold for fruit infestation is very low, especially when production is intended for: Export. Premium markets. Small packages. Markets rejecting holes and defects. A single hole may downgrade or reject a fruit. The decision should therefore be taken before widespread fruit infestation appears. Processing Tomatoes Processing tomatoes can tolerate a limited level of leaf injury more than fresh-market tomatoes, provided that: Effective canopy cover persists. The plant does not collapse. Fruit infestation remains low. Rots do not increase. Fruit infestation remains highly important because it causes: Increased rejection. Reduced processable yield. Higher sorting costs. Increased proportion of rotted fruits. Reduced harvesting and transport efficiency. In processing tomatoes, the intervention decision should focus on: Percentage of infested fruits. Weight of rejected fruits. Canopy condition. Time remaining until harvest. Factory specifications.

Trap Data Plant Inspection Fruits Natural Enemies Decision Level Low and stable No eggs or larvae Sound Present Continue monitoring Gradually increasing Limited eggs Sound Active Increase inspection and prepare High No confirmed infestation Sound Present Search for immigration source; do not spray automatically Low Active mines and larvae Sound Weak Intervene according to larval instar Moderate Eggs and young larvae Sound Good Give priority to biological and selective methods Moderate or high L3 and L4 larvae Beginning infestation near clusters Weak Rapid integrated intervention Any level Beginning of fruit infestation Limited infestation Any level Reduce pest pressure and prevent expansion Any level Widespread infestation High fruit infestation Low Rescue program and economic assessment Low after treatment No new mines Old infestation stable Recovering Do not repeat treatment Low after treatment New mines continue Infestation increasing Damaged Review coverage and resistance

Level One: Continue Monitoring Applied when: No confirmed plant infestation is present. Catch is low and stable. No dangerous immigration sources are present. Natural enemies are present. Action: Continue weekly inspection. Maintain traps. Monitor field edges. Level Two: Increase Vigilance Applied when: Trap catches increase. Limited eggs appear. Old fields are nearby. Temperature rises. Action: Increase inspection frequency. Inspect growing points and clusters. Assess parasitism. Prepare an intervention targeting hatching. Level Three: Preventive or Localized Intervention Applied when: A limited focus is present. Active mines occur in a small sector. No fruit infestation is present. Larvae are still in early instars. Action: Remove the focus where possible. Use a biological or selective method. Avoid treating the entire field unnecessarily. Reinspect. Level Four: Integrated Intervention Applied when: Numbers of live larvae increase. Multiple instars are present. Growing-point infestation appears. Larvae approach fruits. Natural enemies alone are insufficient. Action: Integrate appropriate methods. Manage the immigration source. Target young instars. Protect fruits. Monitor resistance. Level Five: Rescue Intervention Applied when: Fruit infestation increases. L3 and L4 are widespread. Canopy collapse occurs. Previous interventions fail. Immigration continues. Action: Protect the remaining crop. Remove severely infested fruits and foci. Review active ingredient, coverage, and resistance. Prevent the field from becoming an infestation source. Assess the economic feasibility of continuing.

sectionTitle: Effect of Natural Enemies on the Threshold
text: The same larval density should not be treated identically when natural-enemy activity differs. The presence of: A high percentage of parasitized eggs. Effective predators. Larvae killed by fungi or bacteria. A continuing decline in new mines. May permit chemical intervention to be delayed while monitoring is intensified. Conversely, absence of natural enemies or their exposure to a broad-spectrum insecticide increases the probability of rapid pest increase. A decision to delay intervention should not be based merely on observing one predator, but on: Repeated observations. Parasitism percentage. Pest-population trend. Level of fruit infestation.

sectionTitle: Effect of Temperature on Decision Timing
text: As temperature rises within the pest’s suitable range: Egg incubation becomes shorter. Larval development accelerates. The intervention window becomes shorter. The next generation appears sooner. Under moderately warm temperatures, eggs may hatch within only a few days. Delaying inspection for a full week after detecting a large number of eggs may therefore be insufficient. Under cool conditions, development is slower, but infestation does not disappear, and larvae may remain inside plants for longer periods. Inspection frequency should be adjusted according to: Temperature. Egg density. Flight wave. Crop stage.

The field may remain below the internal intervention threshold while a severely infested adjacent old field raises the expected risk. The decision should then also include: Wind direction. Proximity of removal of the old field. Cessation of control in that field. Edge trap catches. Presence of recent eggs in the new field. The most appropriate action may be: Managing the external source. Intensifying traps. Inspecting the exposed edge. Using early protective measures. The new field should not be treated in isolation while the immigration source remains unmanaged.

Localized treatment is used when: Infestation is limited to a clearly defined area. Its boundaries can be identified. Infestation is not widely distributed. No general immigration wave is present. The focus can be reinspected rapidly. It may include: Removal of infested leaves or growing points. Collection of infested fruits. Treatment of a limited sector. Increasing trap density near the source. Whole-field treatment should be considered when: Infestation is distributed. Larvae occur in multiple sectors. Trap catches rise at several locations.
