Plants are close together and the focus cannot be isolated. A general hatching wave is occurring. Uncontrolled reliance on localized treatment will not protect the rest of the field when infestation is already widespread.

sectionTitle: Timing of Intervention in Relation to Larval Instar
text: Eggs They may be targeted through: Egg parasitoids. Predators. Certain products with ovicidal activity. Timing intervention before hatching. First and Second Instars These are the best larval stages to target because they are: Smaller. Less destructive. Usually not yet inside fruits. More susceptible to several biological and selective products. Third and Fourth Instars Control becomes more difficult because of: Greater consumption. Movement among plant organs. Entry into growing points, stems, and fruits. Protection within plant tissues. Management should therefore not be based on waiting for large larvae to appear.

sectionTitle: Evaluation of the Decision After Intervention
text: The field should be reinspected after a period appropriate to the method used. Success is measured through: Reduction in new mines. Reduction in live larvae. Reduction in viable eggs. Cessation of growing-point infestation. Stabilization or reduction of fruit infestation. Conservation of natural enemies. Success is not measured through: Persistence of old mines. Color of damaged leaves. A decrease in trap catch for only one or two days. Observation of dead larvae without assessment of new infestation.

sectionTitle: Reasons for Intervention Failure Despite a Correct Decision
text: The decision to intervene may be correct, but implementation may fail because of: Selection of a product unsuitable for the stage present. Delayed application until larvae entered fruits. Poor coverage. Inappropriate spray-water volume. Repetition of a resistant mode-of-action group. Wash-off by rainfall or irrigation. A strong immigration wave. Poor product quality. Incorrect dose. Failure to calibrate the sprayer. High densities of pupae and eggs unaffected by the treatment. The following must therefore be distinguished: Decision error. Implementation error. Resistance. Reinfestation from outside.

sectionTitle: Practical Decision Framework for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
text: No single validated threshold can be applied to every tomato season in Egypt. A decision framework combining several indicators should therefore be used. Situation One: No Live Larvae Even when a limited number of moths occurs in traps: Continue monitoring. Inspect young leaves. Do not spray automatically. Situation Two: Limited Eggs Without Mines Assess parasitism percentage. Increase inspection. Estimate expected hatching time. Use appropriate preventive or biological methods. Situation Three: Limited Active Mines Define the focus. Remove heavily infested leaves when appropriate. Target L1 and L2. Reinspect. Situation Four: Distributed Live Larvae Determine mean larvae per plant. Review crop stage. Assess natural enemies. Begin integrated intervention. Situation Five: Larvae Near Fruits Increase intervention priority. Inspect the area beneath the calyx. Protect fruits before infestation expands. Situation Six: Beginning of Fruit Infestation Apply a suitable rapid intervention. Collect infested fruits. Review the immigration source. Increase monitoring. Situation Seven: Harvest Approaching Respect the preharvest interval. Calculate the value of crop that can still be protected. Use methods compatible with delivery requirements. Avoid spraying that provides no economic return or creates a residue problem.

Step One: Review Traps Are catches increasing or decreasing? Which trap has the highest catch? Is there a sudden surge? Step Two: Inspect Plants Are eggs present? Are active mines present? How many live larvae are present? Which instars dominate? Step Three: Inspect Sensitive Plant Organs Are growing points infested? Are clusters infested? Are holes present in fruits? Step Four: Assess Natural Regulation What percentage of eggs is parasitized? Are predators present? Has infestation declined naturally? Step Five: Assess the Crop What is the crop stage? What is the target market? How many days remain until harvest? What is the value of the crop at risk? Step Six: Select the Action No intervention. Increase inspection. Localized intervention. Biological control. Microbial product. Selective insecticide. Integrated intervention. Step Seven: Set the Reassessment Date The reassessment date should be set before leaving the field, and the decision should not be left without follow-up.

Indicator Result Mean trap catch Trap trend Increasing / Stable / Decreasing Highest-risk edge Percentage of plants with eggs Mean eggs per plant Percentage of active mines Mean live larvae Dominant instars L1 / L2 / L3 / L4 Percentage of infested growing points Percentage of infested fruits Percentage of parasitized eggs Crop stage Days until harvest Previous intervention Result Decision level Selected action Reinspection date

Error One: Using a Number from Another Country Without Adjustment Prices, cultivars, and production conditions differ. Error Two: Using Traps Alone Trap catches may not reflect larval density or fruit infestation. Error Three: Treating a Risk Level as a Spray Threshold Classification as low, moderate, or high risk does not automatically equal a chemical-treatment decision. Error Four: Ignoring Crop Stage The same infestation is more serious during fruit set and fruiting. Error Five: Counting Old Mines Decisions should be based on active mines and live larvae. Error Six: Ignoring Natural Enemies This may lead to unnecessary intervention. Error Seven: Waiting for Fruit Infestation Intervention should occur before larvae reach fruits. Error Eight: Using the Field Average and Ignoring Foci A focus may spread before its effect appears in the overall average. Error Nine: Treating Every Intervention as Spraying The most appropriate action may be cultural, biological, or localized. Error Ten: Failing to Reassess Decision success cannot be determined without follow-up inspection. Error Eleven: Reducing the Dose to Lower Cost An ineffective dose may increase both loss and resistance. Error Twelve: Ignoring the Preharvest Interval An entomologically correct intervention may cause delivery and residue problems.

sectionTitle: Indicators of a Successful Decision System
text: The system is successful when it results in: Fewer unnecessary sprays. Targeting of young larval instars. Reduced growing-point infestation. Prevention or reduction of fruit infestation. Conservation of natural enemies. Lower control cost. Compliance with residue limits. Reduced insecticide failure. Increased marketable yield. A record explaining the reason for each intervention.

The economic injury level is the pest density at which the value of crop loss equals the cost of control, whereas the economic threshold is the point at which action begins before the population reaches the injury level. There is no single universal threshold for Tuta absoluta because thresholds are affected by cultivar, environment, market, price, control cost, crop stage, and measurement unit. Published studies have recorded different thresholds, including 2.25 larvae per plant in one greenhouse study and economic injury levels ranging from approximately 4.15 to 5.04 larvae per plant on different cultivars in open fields. These values should not be transferred directly to all Egyptian conditions. Management decisions in open fields should integrate: Trap trends. Eggs. Active mines. Live larvae. Larval instars. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation. Natural enemies. Crop stage. Time remaining until harvest. The intervention threshold does not necessarily mean insecticide spraying. The decision may be to remove a focus, release a natural enemy, use a microbial product, or manage an immigration source. Tolerance of infestation declines at fruit set and fruiting, and the threshold for fruit infestation is much lower than the threshold for leaf injury. Every decision should be followed by reassessment based on new mines, live larvae, and fruit infestation rather than on the persistence of old visible damage. References

Cultural control is one of the principal pillars of integrated management of Tuta absoluta because it targets the environment in which the pest lives and the sources that sustain it, rather than relying solely on killing individuals present on plants at the time of treatment. Cultural control includes all practices related to field selection, crop rotation, planting dates, sanitation, management of alternative hosts, irrigation, fertilization, plant density, harvesting, and disposal of crop residues. These practices generally do not function as rapid eradication methods. Instead, they act to: Reduce the pest’s initial population. Delay establishment within the field. Reduce the number of generations the pest can complete. Remove eggs, larvae, and pupae. Prevent persistence of the green bridge. Reduce immigration from old fields. Improve the plant’s ability to tolerate injury. Facilitate monitoring and penetration of control measures. Conserve natural enemies. Reduce the need for chemical sprays. Cultural control affects more than one pest stage, and most of its components can be implemented before infestation reaches a high level. However, it usually does not provide complete control when used alone, especially in areas with overlapping planting seasons and continuous immigration sources. It must therefore be integrated with monitoring and biological, behavioral, and rational chemical control.

Cultural control aims to: 1. Interrupt host continuity between seasons. 2. Reduce sources of eggs, larvae, and pupae. 3. Prevent old fields from producing moths that migrate into new crops. 4. Remove volunteer tomato plants and important alternative hosts. 5. Reduce overlap among solanaceous crops. 6. Reduce field suitability for pest reproduction. 7. Produce a balanced plant capable of tolerating limited infestation. 8. Avoid excessive vegetative growth that hinders inspection and coverage. 9. Facilitate early detection of infestation foci. 10. Maintain canopy cover that protects fruits from sunlight. 11. Prevent accumulation of infested fruits and crop residues. 12. Reduce pest pressure at the production-area level.

sectionTitle: Position of Cultural Control Within the IPM Program
text: Cultural control begins before planting and continues until after harvest ends. It can be divided chronologically into: Before Planting Site selection. Assessment of the previous crop. Implementation of crop rotation. Disposal of residues from the previous crop. Removal of volunteer plants. Management of host weeds. Coordination of planting date. Establishment of a host-free period. During the Season Removal of infestation foci and infested plant parts when appropriate. Irrigation and fertilization management. Maintenance of suitable plant density. Prevention of accumulation of infested fruits. Field-edge management. Monitoring of alternative hosts and neighboring fields. After Harvest Termination of the crop at the appropriate time. Collection of fruits left in the field. Shredding or removal of plants. Appropriate soil cultivation. Removal of regrowth. Prevention of a new generation after production has stopped.

Crop rotation means avoiding continuous planting of tomato or suitable hosts of Tuta absoluta in the same field. Rotation is more effective when tomato is followed by a non-solanaceous crop on which the pest cannot complete its life cycle. Examples of crops that may be included in the rotation, according to region and production system, include: Wheat and barley. Maize. Brassica crops. Onion and garlic. Some legumes. Other crops locally confirmed not to be complete hosts of the pest. Changing only the cultivar or shifting from processing tomatoes to fresh-market tomatoes is insufficient because both belong to the same plant species and support the pest life cycle.

sectionTitle: Crops That Do Not Adequately Break the Cycle
text: Planting the following solanaceous crops immediately after tomato does not fully break the pest cycle: Potato. Eggplant. African eggplant. Pepino. Some cultivated or wild Solanum species. The pest can complete its life cycle on several of these plants, although they may be less suitable than tomato. Pepper is generally far less suitable and results are inconsistent among studies. Nevertheless, it should not be relied upon alone as a guaranteed barrier in a heavily infested area without field monitoring.

sectionTitle: Conditions Required for Successful Crop Rotation
text: Crop rotation fails to reduce the pest when: Tomato residues remain in the field. Volunteer tomato plants emerge. Black nightshade persists along field edges. Old tomato fields remain immediately adjacent. Infested greenhouses or nurseries persist in the area. Infested transplants are moved into the next crop. A suitable solanaceous crop is planted directly afterward. An effective rotation therefore consists of three interrelated elements: 1. Removal of the previous host. 2. Absence of confirmed alternative hosts. 3. A period during which the pest cannot reproduce successfully.

A host-free period is one of the strongest cultural measures at the area-wide level. It means the absence of plants capable of supporting the pest life cycle, including: Commercial tomato crops. Volunteer tomato plants. Infested potato. Infested eggplant. Black nightshade. Confirmed wild solanaceous hosts. A host-free period helps to: Prevent oviposition. Deprive larvae of food. Prevent production of successive generations. Reduce moth numbers before the next crop. Break the green bridge between planting seasons. No single fixed duration is suitable for every region because success is affected by temperature, pupal survival, wild hosts, and overlapping greenhouses and nurseries. The period loses its value when commercial tomato is absent but volunteer tomato plants or host weeds remain.

Crop succession should be considered at the production-area level rather than only at the individual-field level. A tomato crop may end in one field while neighboring fields contain: Transplants for a new planting season. Tomato in the vegetative stage. A potato crop. Long-duration eggplant. An old field still bearing a limited number of fruits. This overlap permits continuous movement of the pest from one host to another. The following should preferably be coordinated: Dates on which old crops end. Timing of crop-residue removal. Transplant-production dates. Transplanting date for the next crop. Intervals between solanaceous crops.

Planting date can affect crop exposure to Tuta absoluta through: Synchronization with flight peaks. Number of generations possible during the season. Presence of neighboring old fields. Temperature during sensitive crop stages. Length of time the crop remains in the field. There is no universal planting date that prevents infestation because pest abundance and climate differ among regions. The best date should be determined locally using: Trap records from several seasons. Historical timing of pest arrival in the area. Dates of removal of old crops. Expected temperatures. Supply or marketing schedules. Cultivar duration.

Early planting may provide an advantage in an area where pest numbers rise late, allowing plants to reach advanced stages before infestation peaks. However, early planting may be more hazardous when: It coincides with heavily infested old fields. Infested transplants are introduced. Moth activity begins early. A long growing season allows more generations to develop. Late planting may be exposed to heavy immigration from previous fields where control has stopped. The decision to plant early or late should therefore be based on temporal analysis of trap data and surrounding planting seasons, rather than a general rule.

The longer a cultivar remains in the field, the longer it is exposed to: Oviposition. Successive generations. Moth immigration. Accumulation of mines. Fruit infestation. Short-cycle cultivars may be exposed to fewer generations under some conditions, but cultivar selection does not depend on Tuta alone. It also depends on: Yield. Quality. Climatic adaptation. Processing or fresh-market use. Disease resistance. Factory or market schedules. No commercial cultivar is completely immune to the pest.

When alternatives are available, new tomato fields should preferably not be established beside: A heavily infested old tomato field. A field in which spraying has stopped. Unmanaged crop residues. An infested nursery. A sorting station discarding damaged fruits. Piles of tomato-processing waste. Large areas of volunteer tomato plants. A map of risk sources should be used to identify the most exposed field edges, distribute traps, and intensify inspection. Distance alone does not prevent immigration, but it may reduce pressure when integrated with source management.

Agricultural sanitation means removing tissues and sources containing living pest stages, not merely making the field appear visually clean. It includes: Removal of severely infested transplants. Collection of severely infested growing points. Removal of infested and fallen fruits. Prevention of accumulation of infested tomato leaves. Removal of volunteer plants. Management of host weeds. Cleaning sorting areas. Preventing rejected fruits from being discarded between rows. Sanitation must be implemented in a manner that does not transfer infestation elsewhere.

A limited number of heavily infested leaves may be removed when: Infestation is localized. Leaves can be collected without scattering them. Field size permits the operation. Removal will not eliminate a large leaf area. Leaves are collected in a closed container. Removing every leaf bearing a small mine is not recommended because it may cause: Loss of photosynthetic area. Fruit exposure to sunlight. Increased sunscald. Higher labor costs. Plant weakening. Spread of larvae during handling. Removal is more rational at the beginning of a focus and less practical after infestation spreads throughout the field.

sectionTitle: Removal of Infested Growing Points and Buds
text: An infested growing point may be removed when: It contains a larva or obvious frass. Infestation is limited. Alternative shoots can continue growth. The plant-training system allows removal. However, growing-point removal is not suitable for every determinate processing-tomato system because it may affect cluster number and plant architecture. The operation should not become indiscriminate broad pruning.

Fruits should be collected when they have: Entry or exit holes. Live larvae. Obvious tunnels. Secondary rots. Fallen because of infestation. Collection is important because an infested fruit may contain a larva capable of: Completing feeding. Exiting and pupating. Moving into another fruit. Rejected fruits should not be left in: Walkways. Field edges. Loading areas. Near irrigation sources. Open piles.

Cutting an infested plant part and dropping it on the soil is insufficient. Possible disposal methods include: Collection in sealed bags or containers. Fine shredding followed by a treatment that prevents survival of pest stages. Burial at a suitable depth where regulations and soil conditions permit. Thermophilic composting that reaches temperatures sufficient to kill the pest. Tight covering and exposure to high temperatures until decomposition occurs. Transport to a treatment site away from tomato fields. Open burning is not recommended as a general practice because of pollution and fire risk. It should be used only where officially permitted and under safety controls.

Volunteer tomatoes are among the most dangerous components of the green bridge because they are the principal host itself. They originate from: Fallen fruits. Seeds from the previous season. Sorting piles. Incompletely decomposed manure or compost. Processing residues. Fruit-washing areas. They should be removed from: Fields. Drains. Field edges. Service roads. Areas surrounding sorting stations. Waste-collection areas. They should preferably be removed before producing sufficient vegetative growth to support completion of the pest life cycle.

Management should focus on species demonstrated to support the complete pest life cycle, particularly: Solanum nigrum. Solanum sarrachoides where it occurs. Solanum coagulans and related species where present. Nicotiana glauca. Wild solanaceous species actually carrying infestation. All weeds should not be treated as equally suitable hosts. A distinction should be made among: A confirmed host. A plant supporting only limited experimental infestation. A plant on which only a moth was recorded. A non-host plant that benefits natural enemies.

Large, infested solanaceous host populations should be prevented from developing near the crop. However, sudden removal of a large infested plant mass may drive adult moths toward recently planted tomato. When removing a major source: Plants should be collected or shredded immediately. They should not be left wilting along the edge. Traps and inspections should be intensified in the new field. Wind direction should be monitored. Timing should be coordinated with neighboring growers. Plants should be removed before infestation reaches high density whenever possible.

Cultural control does not mean converting the production system into land completely devoid of plants other than tomato. Some non-host plants may provide: Nectar for parasitoids. Pollen for predators. Shelter for beneficial insects. Ecological diversity that improves natural control. However, they should be confirmed to: Not be a proven Tuta host. Not hinder ventilation and inspection. Not compete severely with the crop. Not harbor other serious pests or diseases. The decision should be based on plant identity and function, rather than the general label “weed.”

An old field retained after economic returns have declined represents a major risk, particularly when: Spraying stops. Green leaves and fruits remain. Irrigation continues. Infestation increases. A new planting is nearby. Such fields produce increasing numbers of: Migrating moths. Larvae inside fruits. Pupae in soil and residues. Infestation along neighboring field edges. A clear decision is required: Either continue managing the crop during the economically productive harvest period. Or terminate the crop and remove plants rapidly. Leaving a live field without economically worthwhile harvest or control is one of the most serious area-wide errors.

Growers may attempt to retain a field for a limited number of additional harvests, while the value of these pickings becomes lower than the damage caused by the field to the surrounding area. The decision should be evaluated according to: Quantity of remaining fruit. Infestation percentage. Crop price. Harvesting and control costs. Proximity of new plantings. Ability of the field to produce new moths. In contracted projects or clustered production areas, the impact of the field on neighboring growers should be included in the decision, not only the profit of the individual field.

sectionTitle: Removal of Crop Residues After Harvest
text: Crop residues may contain: Eggs. Larvae inside leaves. Larvae inside growing points and stems. Larvae inside fruits. Pupae between leaves or on the soil surface. Residues should therefore be removed or destroyed immediately after harvest ends. The process includes: 1. Stopping unnecessary continued growth. 2. Collecting fruits left in the field. 3. Cutting plants. 4. Shredding the canopy. 5. Treating or transporting residues. 6. Applying suitable soil cultivation. 7. Monitoring any regrowth. The field should not remain for weeks after the last economically worthwhile harvest.

sectionTitle: Field Evidence for the Importance of Residue Removal
text: A field study at two sites in Türkiye compared fields where tomato residues and weeds were removed and the soil cultivated with fields where residues remained without cleaning or cultivation. Adult moth density was higher in the uncleaned fields by approximately: 47.77% at one site. 73.97% at the other site. The results support combining crop-residue removal with postharvest soil cultivation. However, the study compared a combined package of: Cleaning. Weed removal. Tillage. The relative effect of each measure cannot therefore be separated from these results alone.

Fine shredding helps to: Mechanically destroy some larvae and pupae. Dry tissues rapidly. Accelerate decomposition. Facilitate incorporation into soil or transport. Prevent intact plants from continuing as hosts. Shredding alone may not kill all pest stages and should therefore be followed by: Collection. Burial. Tight covering. Thermophilic composting. Soil cultivation. Continued monitoring with traps. Shredding equipment should not be moved from a heavily infested field into a new field without cleaning.

Tuta absoluta may pupate: In or on the soil. Inside mines. Between leaves and residues. Within a silken cocoon. Soil cultivation after crop removal may contribute to: Burying some pupae. Exposing others to desiccation or predation. Mechanically destroying cocoons. Breaking down crop residues. Preventing regrowth of tomato stems. The effect of tillage varies according to: Cultivation depth. Soil moisture. Soil type. Presence of residues. Timing. Ability of moths to emerge from depth. Tillage should not be used alone while infested plants and fruits remain on the soil surface.

No single depth is appropriate for all soils and production systems. Depth should be selected according to: The following crop. Soil type. Irrigation system. Erosion risk. Need to maintain soil structure. Location of pupae. Equipment availability. Some guidance recommends deep burial of infested material, but this does not imply repeated deep tillage every season because it has costs and effects on soil structure. The selected system should terminate host residues while maintaining soil sustainability.

In conservation-agriculture systems, tillage may be reduced to protect soil structure. In this case, the absence of deep tillage should be compensated for by: Removing infested residues from the soil surface. Shredding and treating residues. Preventing volunteer tomato emergence. Managing pupae with biological methods where available. Continuing pheromone trapping after harvest. Preventing immediate planting of a new host. Dense infested residues should not be left on the soil surface without a management plan under the justification of conserving soil.

sectionTitle: Solarization and Heat Treatment of Residues
text: Heat can be used to kill pest stages in crop residues by: Placing residues in sealed bags or containers. Covering them with transparent plastic. Exposing them to sunlight for a sufficient period. Forming a small pile that permits temperature rise. Efficacy depends on: Air temperature. Pile thickness. Tightness of the cover. Duration of exposure. Moisture content of the residues. Covering an entire open field for solarization is not a standard economical Tuta-control measure under all conditions, but heat treatment may be suitable for residues or limited areas.

Crop residues can be converted into organic compost when decomposition is: Thermophilic. Controlled. Able to reach temperatures lethal to insect stages. Accompanied by pile turning. Managed to prevent moth emergence before temperature rises. An unmanaged cold residue pile may become: Shelter for larvae. A pupation site. A source of emerging moths. A future source of volunteer tomato plants.

Regular harvesting helps to: Reduce persistence of ripe fruits susceptible to decay. Detect infested fruits. Prevent accumulation of fallen fruits. Reduce the time available for larvae inside fruits. Improve sorting. During harvest: Infested fruits should be separated. They should not be discarded between rows. Crates should be cleaned. The area beneath the calyx should be inspected. Contaminated crates should not be moved into new fields.

In processing tomatoes intended for once-over harvesting, the field should not reach harvest with: A high density of rotted fruits. Large numbers of live larvae. Severe canopy collapse. A high percentage of fallen fruits. Harvest timing depends on: Percentage of ripe fruits. Factory specifications. Fruit condition. Expected infestation wave. Weather conditions. Preharvest interval after the final treatment. Advancing harvest by a limited period may be economically preferable to leaving the crop until fruit infestation doubles, provided maturity percentage and factory specifications permit.

The irrigation system does not directly kill Tuta absoluta, but it affects: Plant growth. Leaf quality. Canopy density. Plant defensive responses. Natural enemies. Moisture and temperature of soil surrounding pupae. Balanced irrigation aims to: Prevent repeated wilting. Maintain uniform growth. Avoid excessive vegetative growth. Support the plant’s ability to compensate for limited injury. Prevent fruit cracking and rots. Deliberately imposing drought should not be used as a control method because severe stress reduces yield and affects both plant tolerance and natural enemies.

Studies have shown that restricted water availability can affect Tuta absoluta performance through changes in plant quality and chemical composition. However, these effects are complex and may also affect: Tomato yield. Fruit size. Photosynthesis. Zoophytophagous predators. Plant compensatory capacity. Laboratory results should therefore not be translated into a recommendation to impose water stress on the crop. The field objective remains: Meeting the crop’s economic water requirement without excess or chronic stress.

Excess irrigation may lead to: Excessive vegetative growth. Heavy shading. Difficulty achieving coverage with biological or chemical sprays. Difficult plant inspection. Increased incidence of some diseases. Nutrient loss through leaching. There is no scientific rule that increased water always increases Tuta infestation, but unbalanced growth may reduce management efficiency.

An experimental study showed that increased salinity of irrigation water applied to tomato plants accelerated development of Tuta absoluta through plant-mediated effects. This does not mean salinity will increase pest populations to the same extent in every field, but it demonstrates that salinity is not a pest-reduction method. Salinity also causes: Plant weakening. Yield reduction. Disturbed nutrient uptake. Reduced ability of the plant to compensate for injury. Salinity should therefore be managed according to crop requirements, with recognition that it may alter the plant–pest relationship.

Fertilization indirectly affects Tuta absoluta through changes in: Leaf nutritional value. Nitrogen content. Chemical defenses. Vegetative growth. Quality of prey for predators and parasitoids. There is no single fertilizer program for controlling Tuta, and nutrients should not be reduced to levels that cause crop deficiency. The objective is: Balanced nutrition that achieves the required yield without excessive vegetative growth or severe nutritional deficiency.

Nitrogen is associated with: Formation of proteins and amino acids. Growth rate. Leaf area. Composition of defensive compounds. Experiments have shown that nitrogen level changes larval performance, food consumption, and plant response. However, the relationship is not a simple linear rule of: The higher the nitrogen, the greater the Tuta infestation in every case. It is influenced by cultivar, water availability, plant defenses, and natural enemies. Field management should therefore: Avoid exceeding crop requirements. Split nitrogen applications among growth stages. Rely on soil and plant analysis. Avoid large late applications. Balance nitrogen with potassium, calcium, and other nutrients. Assess canopy density.

Excessive nitrogen may lead to: Dense succulent growth. Delayed maturity. Internal canopy shading. More difficult inspection. Poor spray coverage. Increased susceptibility to some pests and diseases. It may also prolong the period during which the crop remains green, extending host availability. This does not mean that reducing nitrogen below the economic requirement will profitably reduce infestation; crop yield may decline before any entomological benefit is achieved.

Nitrogen deficiency may reduce food quality for larvae under some conditions, but it also causes: Plant weakness. Smaller leaves. Reduced yield. Reduced compensatory capacity. Negative effects on zoophytophagous predators. Nutritional deficiency should therefore not be used as a control measure.

Balanced nutrition contributes to: Tissue strength. Uniform growth. Fruit quality. Stress tolerance. Reduced cracking and rots. However, no potassium or calcium rate can be regarded as an insecticide against Tuta. Claims that any nutrient directly eliminates larvae should be avoided unless supported by experimental evidence. Nutrients should be managed according to: Soil analysis. Water analysis. Leaf analysis. Crop stage. Target yield.

Organic matter, compost, and vermicompost may affect: Plant growth. Microbial communities. Nutrient availability. Plant defenses. Plant tolerance of injury. Some studies have recorded changes in pest performance on plants grown in media containing particular levels of humates or vermicompost. Results vary according to: Type of amendment. Application rate. Compost maturity. Cultivar. Production system. Soil amendments should not be relied upon as substitutes for monitoring and direct control measures.

Plant density affects: Air movement. Ease of inspection. Spray penetration. Humidity within the canopy. Movement of larvae between touching plants. Ability of workers to remove infestation foci. Excessive density may produce an entangled canopy that is difficult to inspect and cover. Reducing density without calculation may lead to: Reduced yield. Greater fruit exposure to sunlight. Changes in the microclimate. Density should therefore be determined according to cultivar, training system, and mechanization, while avoiding unjustified crowding.

Spacing should permit: Worker movement. Trap reading. Inspection of leaves and fruits. Entry of spray equipment. Penetration of spray droplets into the canopy. Harvesting without breaking plants. There is no spacing specific only to Tuta, but any system that prevents access to the plant interior weakens management.

In indeterminate tomatoes, pruning may be used to improve ventilation and facilitate inspection. In determinate processing tomatoes grown in open fields, extensive pruning is not usually practiced because canopy cover is necessary for fruit protection and production. Only the following should be removed: Severely infested plant parts. Dead leaves obstructing inspection. Limited infestation foci when economically justified. Leaves close to fruit clusters should not be removed excessively in a way that exposes fruits to sunlight.

The desired canopy is: Vigorous. Balanced. Not excessive. Adequately ventilated. Protective of fruits. Accessible for inspection and spray coverage. This is achieved through: Suitable plant density. Balanced nitrogen. Regular irrigation. Avoidance of excessively delayed vegetative growth. Management of diseases that cause defoliation. Canopy collapse caused by Tuta or diseases may expose fruits and increase sunscald and other losses.

Tomato cultivars differ in: Female oviposition preference. Larval survival. Trichome density. Defensive compounds. Plant compensatory capacity. Crop duration. Some resistance sources in wild species are associated with glandular trichomes and chemical compounds, but commercial cultivars with complete high resistance are not broadly available for stand-alone reliance. Cultivar selection should be based on: Local performance. Yield and quality. Crop duration. Canopy vigor. Infestation results from comparative trials. A commercial cultivar should not be described as “Tuta-resistant” without documented comparative testing.

Intercropping means growing tomato with another plant in an organized pattern. It may act through: Disrupting host location. Increasing diversity. Attracting natural enemies. Altering volatile compounds. Providing nectar and pollen. A field study in Iran showed that intercropping tomato with sainfoin: Onobrychis viciifolia Reduced eggs, larvae, and mines and increased predator diversity compared with tomato monoculture. However, success depends on: Companion species. Pattern and spacing. Climate. Local natural enemies. Mechanization. Competition for water and nutrients. Economic feasibility. The system should not be transferred directly to Egyptian open-field processing tomatoes without local field trials.

Plants such as the following are being studied: Dill. Coriander. Basil. Garlic. Onion. Nectar-producing plants. Potential objectives include: Supporting natural enemies. Providing nectar. Increasing field diversity. Influencing pest behavior. A distinction must be made among: Plants Supporting Natural Enemies These provide food or shelter for parasitoids and predators. Repellent Plants These are intended to drive moths away from tomato. Trap Crops These attract the pest away from the principal crop. These functions are not demonstrated merely because a plant is aromatic. They require experiments on species, distribution, and density.

Companion planting may fail or cause undesirable effects when it: Competes with tomato for water and nutrients. Obstructs mechanical harvesting. Harbors other pests. Increases humidity. Is incompatible with pesticides. Becomes a weed in subsequent seasons. Attracts moths without eliminating them. A limited trial should therefore precede commercial expansion.

Plant strips or belts may be established to support: Egg parasitoids. Larval parasitoids. Predators. Pollinators. The selected plants should be: Not confirmed hosts of Tuta. Suitable for the region. Staggered in flowering. Noncompetitive with the crop. Manageable. Not carriers of major diseases. They should be planted where they do not obstruct: Spraying. Harvesting. Equipment movement. Monitoring of field edges. Design of plants supporting natural enemies will be discussed in the section on conservation of natural enemies.

A solanaceous border such as potato or eggplant should not be used to attract Tuta without a management program because it may become: A reproductive host. A source of eggs and larvae. A focus from which moths move into tomato. For a trap crop to succeed, it must: Be more attractive than the principal crop. Be planted at the correct time and location. Have pest populations removed or be destroyed before redistribution occurs. Have locally demonstrated feasibility. There is no globally standardized border crop approved for Tuta absoluta in open-field tomato.

sectionTitle: Management of Field Edges and Drains
text: Field edges and drains should be inspected for: Volunteer tomato plants. Black nightshade. Discarded fruits. Crop residues. Infestation foci. Abandoned harvest containers. Field edges should not become unmanaged areas while control is implemented only within crop rows. At the same time, non-host plants supporting natural enemies may be retained after evaluation.

Cultural control includes cleaning: Crates. Trailers. Harvesting machines. Shredders. Pruning tools. Transport vehicles. Reception areas. The following should be removed: Leaves. Small fruits. Larvae. Cocoons. Soil and residues that may contain pupae. Equipment arriving from an old field should be cleaned before entering a new field.

sectionTitle: Waste Management at Sorting Stations and Processing Plants
text: Sorting stations and tomato-processing plants may become centers for accumulation of: Infested fruits. Leaves. Calyces. Seeds. Wash water containing seeds. Contaminated containers. Management requires: Daily collection of waste. Transport to a treatment site. Prevention of disposal near fields. Thermal or industrial treatment of piles. Prevention of volunteer tomato emergence. Monitoring the site with traps when fields are nearby.

sectionTitle: Coordination of Irrigation, Fertilization, and Biological Control
text: Plant condition affects omnivorous predators that feed on both the plant and the pest. Severe water or nitrogen deficiency may negatively affect: Predator survival. Searching behavior. Reproductive capacity. Irrigation or fertilization should therefore not be reduced sharply in an attempt to weaken Tuta because this may also weaken the plant and natural enemies. The three-way system should be evaluated as: Plant — Pest — Natural Enemy Rather than focusing on the pest alone.

sectionTitle: Cultural Control at the Area-Wide Level
text: Cultural measures are more effective when neighboring fields implement them at approximately the same time. Collective management includes: Coordinated removal of old crops. Establishment of a host-free period. Control of volunteer tomatoes. Prevention of crop-residue piles. Organization of crate movement. Monitoring of sorting stations. Sharing trap data. Identification of immigration sources. Notification of neighboring fields before removing an infested crop. A single field cannot prevent continuous immigration from an unmanaged production area.

Before Selecting the Land Review infestation history. Identify old fields. Identify alternative hosts. Evaluate planting-season schedules. After Selecting the Land Remove crop residues. Remove volunteer plants. Manage solanaceous weeds. Implement crop rotation. Clean containers and equipment. Before Transplanting Confirm transplant health. Install traps. Prepare a field-edge map. Adjust plant density and spacing. Develop a balanced irrigation and fertilization program.

Inspect infestation foci. Remove severely infested plants or plant parts when economically justified. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Regulate irrigation. Maintain canopy ventilation. Manage hosts along field edges. Do not leave crop residues between rows. Monitor natural enemies.

sectionTitle: Program During Flowering and Fruit Set
text: Increase inspection of growing points and clusters. Preserve leaves close to clusters. Avoid excessive leaf removal. Regulate irrigation to prevent cracking. Collect infested small fruits. Review surrounding old fields.

Inspect beneath the calyx. Collect infested fruits. Prevent accumulation of fallen fruits. Avoid excessive late nitrogen. Maintain canopy cover. Determine the economically appropriate time to terminate the crop. Clean containers continuously.

Within the shortest possible period: 1. Collect remaining fruits. 2. Cut or uproot plants. 3. Shred the canopy. 4. Remove or treat residues. 5. Cultivate the soil according to the production system. 6. Remove solanaceous hosts. 7. Continue monitoring traps. 8. Remove regrowth. 9. Clean harvesting equipment. 10. Notify neighboring fields of the removal date.

Indicators include: Delayed first appearance of infestation. Lower initial-season population. Reduced edge infestation. Absence of volunteer tomatoes. Decline in moth numbers after crop removal. Fewer required sprays. Absence of neglected old fields. Lower percentage of infested fruits. Easier inspection and spray coverage. Conservation of natural enemies. Reduced transfer of the pest into the next planting season.

Component Good Moderate Poor Crop rotation Non-host crop and a break period Limited overlap Solanaceous crop after tomato Crop residues Removed immediately Delayed removal Left in the field Volunteer tomato plants Absent Limited Widespread Host weeds Monitored Present without clear infestation Infested and widespread Neighboring fields Managed Mixed conditions Old and neglected Irrigation Balanced Limited fluctuation Excess or stress Nitrogen Based on analysis Relatively high Excessive vegetative growth Plant density Allows inspection Limited crowding Very dense Infested fruits Collected Collected intermittently Left in the field Postharvest sanitation Immediate Delayed Not implemented

Error One: Considering Crop Rotation to Mean Only Changing the Tomato Cultivar The plant host must be changed, not merely the cultivar name. Error Two: Planting Potato After Tomato and Considering the Cycle Broken Potato is a confirmed host of the pest. Error Three: Removing Plants but Leaving Fruits Larvae may continue developing inside fruits. Error Four: Shredding Residues and Leaving Them Untreated Some pest stages may survive. Error Five: Leaving an Old Field After Spraying Stops It becomes an immigration source for the new crop. Error Six: Removing Black Nightshade and Leaving It Along the Field Edge Larvae may continue developing inside it. Error Seven: Excessive Nitrogen Fertilization It produces dense growth that is difficult to inspect and cover. Error Eight: Imposing Water Stress to Control Tuta It damages the crop and natural enemies and is not a reliable control measure. Error Nine: Relying on Salinity to Weaken the Pest Salinity may accelerate pest development and weaken the crop. Error Ten: Removing Too Many Leaves This exposes fruits to sunlight and reduces photosynthesis. Error Eleven: Planting an Aromatic Species and Assuming It Is a Proven Repellent Companion plants require local scientific testing. Error Twelve: Applying Sanitation in Only One Field Immigration may continue from neighboring fields. Error Thirteen: Discarding Sorting Rejects Near Fields They become sources of larvae and volunteer tomato plants. Error Fourteen: Randomly Burning Residues This causes pollution and hazards and should be used only in accordance with regulations.

sectionTitle: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
text: The principal priorities are: 1. Remove old fields immediately after harvesting ceases to be economically worthwhile. 2. Do not stop control while leaving a live field beside a new planting. 3. Remove volunteer tomato plants around fields and processing facilities. 4. Monitor black nightshade and solanaceous hosts. 5. Prevent continuous succession of tomato, potato, and eggplant without a break. 6. Collect infested fruits and do not discard them between rows. 7. Shred and treat tomato residues immediately after harvest. 8. Use traps to monitor moth emergence from old fields. 9. Regulate nitrogen and irrigation according to actual crop requirements. 10. Preserve canopy cover in processing tomatoes. 11. Clean harvest crates and equipment. 12. Manage waste from tomato-processing plants and sorting stations. 13. Coordinate measures among growers within the same production area. 14. Test supportive plants or intercropping on a limited scale before expansion. 15. Evaluate measures according to reductions in fruit infestation and spray number, not merely the visual cleanliness of the field.

Measure Target Stage or System Timing Expected Effect Crop rotation All stages through host interruption Between seasons Reduce pest persistence Host-free period Eggs, larvae, and moths Between planting seasons Break the green bridge Removal of volunteer tomatoes Eggs and larvae Throughout the year Remove an important reservoir Management of black nightshade Eggs and larvae Before and during the season Reduce an alternative host Collection of infested fruits Larvae During the season and harvest Prevent pupation and movement Removal of infestation foci Eggs and larvae At the beginning of infestation Contain spread Crop-residue removal Eggs, larvae, and pupae After harvest Reduce the next generation Residue shredding Larvae and pupae After harvest Destroy and dry tissues Soil cultivation Pupae and residues After crop removal Reduce survival Balanced irrigation Plant and tritrophic system Throughout the season Improve tolerance and management Balanced fertilization Plant, pest, and natural enemies Throughout the season Prevent excessive growth Suitable plant density Field environment At planting Improve inspection and coverage Intercropping Moths and natural enemies According to the system Potential effect requiring calibration Regular harvesting Larvae in fruits During maturity Reduce infested fruits Container cleaning Larvae and pupae Throughout the season Prevent transfer Collective management All stages Throughout the year Reduce regional immigration

Cultural control reduces the ability of Tuta absoluta to reach suitable hosts, complete its life cycle, and move among planting seasons. Its principal foundations include: Crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops. A host-free period. Organization of planting dates. Removal of volunteer tomato plants. Management of black nightshade and alternative hosts. Removal of infested plant parts and fruits when economically justified. Rapid termination of old fields. Removal and treatment of crop residues. Appropriate soil cultivation. Balanced irrigation and fertilization. Maintenance of suitable plant density and canopy cover. Cleaning containers and equipment. Area-wide management. Crop-residue removal is among the most important measures. A field study recorded moth populations approximately 47.77% and 73.97% higher in fields that were not cleaned or cultivated after harvest than in cleaned and cultivated fields. The effect of tillage cannot be separated from residue removal in that study, so the two measures should be viewed as a combined package. Irrigation and fertilization affect interactions among the plant, pest, and natural enemies, but water stress or nutrient deficiency should not be used as direct control methods. Intercropping and supportive plants may contribute to reducing pest pressure and increasing natural enemies, but they require local calibration before adoption in open-field processing-tomato systems. Cultural control cannot be completely successful in one field when surrounding old fields, crop residues, and alternative hosts remain continuous immigration sources. References

Mechanical and physical control comprise a group of interventions based on exclusion, isolation, organized removal of infested stages and their sources, or prevention of pest transfer among production units, without relying on the toxic action of insecticides. This concept does not include every incidental manual practice or any procedure that cannot be measured and repeated. For a method to be included within mechanical or physical control, it should have: A clearly defined mode of action. A known target stage. A standardized application method. Measurable efficacy. Practical suitability for the production system. Experimental evidence or reliable extension support. No crop damage exceeding its benefit. The methods with practical value against Tuta absoluta are concentrated in four principal areas: 1. Physical exclusion of moths using insect barriers. 2. Sanitary isolation of plants and suspected batches. 3. Selective removal of infested tissues and fruits. 4. Containment and safe disposal of plant residues and pest-transfer sources. When used alone, these methods cannot provide complete pest management in most open fields. However, they play an important role in reducing initial populations, containing early infestation foci, protecting nurseries and greenhouses, and preventing residues and containers from becoming sources of the next generation.

Mechanical control is a direct intervention that removes living pest stages or plant parts carrying them from the production system through organized operations such as: Rejecting infested transplants. Removing plants from early infestation foci. Removing severely infested plant parts. Collecting infested fruits. Shredding crop residues after crop termination. Cleaning equipment and containers of plant material. Transporting residues in containers that prevent spillage or pest escape. Its efficacy does not depend merely on separating the infested part from the plant, but on removing it from the pest life cycle and preventing eggs, larvae, or pupae within it from completing development.

Physical control is the use of a material barrier or isolation system that prevents moths from reaching plants or limits their movement among production areas. Its most established applications include: Insect-proof screens. Sealing greenhouse and nursery openings. Double-door systems and protected entry areas. Isolation of new or suspected transplant batches. Containment of residues and infested fruits during collection and transport. Prevention of pest transfer with containers and equipment. The principal effect of these methods is to reduce adult entry and oviposition opportunities, not to kill larvae already present inside mines.

sectionTitle: Position of Mechanical and Physical Control Within IPM
text: These methods perform different functions according to crop stage. Before Planting Protect the nursery from moth entry. Isolate transplant batches. Prevent entry of contaminated containers or equipment. Remove residues from the previous crop. Repair screens and doors. During Early Crop Stages Reject severely infested transplants or plants. Contain limited infestation foci. Remove plant parts containing confirmed infestation when economically justified. During Fruiting Collect infested fruits. Prevent accumulation of fallen and rotting fruits. Separate rejected fruits from sound produce. After Harvest Terminate remaining plants. Collect unharvested fruits. Shred the canopy. Contain residues and move them to a safe treatment system. Clean harvesting machinery, crates, and transport vehicles.

Concept of Exclusion Exclusion means preventing adult insects from entering a protected production area and is the clearest application of physical control. It primarily targets: Fertilized females searching for plants on which to lay eggs. Males entering from surrounding fields. Immigration waves resulting from removal of old fields. Exclusion is most suitable in: Nurseries. Transplant-production greenhouses. Commercial greenhouses. Protected tunnels. Temporary transplant-isolation units. In large open fields, complete area isolation is usually economically impractical. Exclusion therefore focuses mainly on nurseries and on routes through which transplants and equipment enter.

Screens act as barriers separating the internal production environment from insect populations outside. Their efficacy depends on several characteristics, particularly: Actual screen-opening dimensions. Thread diameter. Weaving pattern. Percentage of open area. Tightness of installation. Integrity of joints. Duration of door opening. Air movement within the structure. Describing a screen only by the number of openings or threads per inch is insufficient because two screens with the same count may differ in actual opening size owing to differences in thread thickness and manufacturing method. Screen specifications should document: Opening width. Opening length. Thread diameter. Percentage of open area. Airflow rate. Resistance to ultraviolet radiation and tearing.

sectionTitle: Conditions Required for Successful Insect Screening
text: A screen provides effective exclusion only when the following conditions are met: 1. All ventilation openings are covered. 2. No gaps occur between the screen and the structure. 3. Lower edges are sealed. 4. Openings around hoses and cables are closed. 5. Holes and tears are repaired immediately. 6. Door opening is controlled. 7. Transplants are inspected before entry. 8. Residual infestation from the previous crop is eliminated. 9. Residues are removed in closed containers. 10. Internal traps are used to detect any barrier breach. The mere presence of a screen does not prove that the structure is isolated. The pest may enter with transplants, trays, or residues, or through a door left open.

sectionTitle: Effect of Screens on the Internal Climate
text: Reducing screen-opening dimensions usually decreases air-exchange rate. Depending on structure design and climate, this may lead to: Higher temperature. Higher relative humidity. Reduced air movement. Increased risk of some diseases. Effects on fruit set. Changes in sugar accumulation and fruit quality. Greater ventilation or cooling requirements. Screens should therefore not be selected only for maximum exclusion, but through a balance between: Preventing moth entry + maintaining a crop-suitable climate The screen should be evaluated within the actual production system because the same screen may perform well in a well-ventilated structure and poorly in a low or poorly ventilated structure.

Partial Screening This includes fitting screens to: Side openings. Roof openings. Doors. Ventilation inlets. It can provide adequate protection when the remaining structure is properly sealed. Complete Screening This means enclosing all openings and faces with screens while using a controlled entry system. It may provide greater protection from external insects but may also have greater effects on: Ventilation. Temperature. Humidity. Fruit quality. Increasing the screened area should not be assumed to produce a proportional reduction in infestation because results are also affected by transplant entry, door management, internal infestation, and barrier condition.

sectionTitle: Double Doors and Protected Entry Areas
text: The entrance is a major weak point in protected structures. A double-entry system consists of: 1. An external door. 2. An intermediate chamber or space. 3. An internal door. The two doors should not be opened simultaneously. The protected entry area performs functions including: Reducing moth entry with air currents. Inspecting workers and tools. Temporarily receiving trays. Removing attached plant material. Holding a trap to detect incoming moths. Doors should be: Self-closing where possible. Free from gaps beneath them. Suitable for the size of carts and trays. Not obstructed by crates or equipment.

Moths may enter through small gaps around: Irrigation lines. Cables. Heating pipes. Ventilation fans. Drainage outlets. Points where the structure meets the soil. These points should be included in the barrier-inspection program rather than limiting inspection to visible screens and doors. Every defect or repair should be recorded with its location and date, and the nearest trap should then be checked to determine whether moth entry occurred.

Placing traps both inside and outside a structure helps assess exclusion efficacy. Possible results indicate the following: High Catch Outside and Low Catch Inside This indicates external pest pressure while the barrier remains relatively effective. High Catch Inside the Structure This may indicate: An opening. Poor door management. Introduction of infested transplants. Persistence of an internal generation. Presence of infested residues inside the structure. Internal catch should not be interpreted as screen failure alone before all entry pathways are reviewed.

Isolation is one of the most important physical interventions in the nursery. New batches or batches received from external sources should be placed in a separate area before integration with existing production. Isolation includes: Clear spatial separation. A dedicated trap. Inspection of leaves and growing points. Opening suspected mines. Recording the batch source. Preventing tray exchange among batches. Reinspection before transfer. Isolation aims to prevent a single infested batch from distributing the pest throughout the nursery or among several fields.

A transplant should be rejected when it contains: Confirmed eggs. An active mine. A live larva. Growing-point infestation. Stem boring. Damage reducing its capacity for normal growth. Removing one infested leaf and automatically accepting the transplant is insufficient because other undetected eggs or larvae may be present. When infestation occurs in more than one tray or several larval instars are found, the entire batch should be assessed, not only visibly infested plants.

sectionTitle: Removal of Infested Focus Plants in the Field
text: A whole plant may be removed when it represents a limited early-season focus and contains: Severe growing-point infestation. Several active mines. Larvae in the stem. Weakness making recovery uneconomic. Whole-plant removal is most useful when: The number of infested plants is limited. Plants are still small. Focus boundaries can be identified. Healthy replacement transplants are available. Removed plants are collected immediately. This method is unsuitable once infestation has spread through a large proportion of the field.

sectionTitle: Selective Removal of Infested Plant Parts
text: Selective removal is used to remove eggs or larvae from the production system before they complete development. It may include: An infested leaflet. A heavily infested leaf. An infested growing point. A bud or tender stem section. A severely damaged cluster. The operation is scientifically valid when it is based on: Confirmation of active infestation. Estimation of the number of parts removed. Measurement of leaf area lost. Collection of removed parts in a container. Reinspection of the focus. Comparison of removal cost with the value of crop protected. Random removal of every leaf carrying an old mine is neither scientific nor economical.

Selective removal should not become extensive defoliation. Suitability depends on: Crop stage. Cultivar. Leaf area affected. Proximity of leaves to fruit clusters. Percentage of fruits exposed. Plant compensatory capacity. Labor cost. Risks of leaf removal are greater in determinate processing tomatoes because the canopy contributes to: Protection of fruits from direct radiation. Regulation of fruit temperature. Carbohydrate production. Fruit filling. Uniform ripening. Removal should therefore be limited to severely infested parts within early infestation foci.

An infested leaf may carry eggs parasitized by egg parasitoids. Before removing the leaf, the following should be distinguished: Viable eggs. Hatched eggs. Parasitized eggs. Active mines. Old mines. Disposal of leaves carrying parasitized eggs may eliminate beneficial parasitoid individuals. When an egg-parasitoid release program is in place, mechanical removal should be coordinated with the release program so that a large proportion of parasitoids is not removed before emergence.

Collection of infested fruits is one of the most important mechanical interventions after fruiting begins. Fruits should be collected when they bear: An entry or exit hole. Frass around the calyx. An internal tunnel. A live larva. Rot associated with infestation. Fallen fruit resulting from boring. Collection achieves two objectives: 1. Reducing the proportion of rotted and rejected fruits within the field. 2. Preventing larvae inside fruits from completing development or exiting to pupate. Rejected fruits should not be left between rows or at loading points.

In processing tomatoes, selective collection should be implemented when: Infestation is still containable. Labor cost is lower than the expected loss. Sufficient time remains before harvest. Infested fruits represent a source of rots or larvae. The following should be recorded: Number of infested fruits. Their weight. Percentage loss in the sample. Location of infestation. Presence of live larvae. Weight loss is calculated as: Weight loss (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100 This indicator is used to evaluate whether manual collection remains economically justified or whether another intervention is required.

Containers used to collect infested leaves and fruits should be: Closed or closable. Structurally intact. Not used for sound fruits. Clearly marked by color or label. Easy to clean. Designed to prevent loss of contents. Open piles should not be used as temporary collection points because larvae may leave the tissues, while pupae or moths may continue development.

Tomato residues after harvest are a source of eggs, larvae, and pupae. Residues include: Leaves. Stems. Growing points. Unharvested fruits. Fallen fruits. Plants regrowing after cutting. Sorting waste discarded in the field. The crop should be terminated within a short period after economic harvesting stops because continued green growth allows the pest to reproduce and produce moths that move into the next planting season.

Scientific residue removal consists of an integrated sequence: 1. Collect remaining fruits. 2. Terminate living plants. 3. Cut or shred the canopy. 4. Collect or contain shredded material. 5. Move it to a safe treatment system or manage it on-site in a manner that prevents pest survival. 6. Clean equipment. 7. Continue monitoring the field with traps. 8. Remove any regrowth. Implementing only one step while leaving the rest of the residues unmanaged is insufficient.

Shredding: Tears mines. Reduces plant-material volume. Accelerates tissue drying. Facilitates transport or decomposition. Stops the intact plant from continuing as a host. However, shredding should not be regarded as a stand-alone guarantee that all stages are killed because: Larvae may remain inside fruits that were not collected. Pupae may occur in the soil. Large plant pieces may remain uncut. Individuals may have already left the plant material. Shredding should therefore be used as a preparatory stage preceding containment, treatment, or soil cultivation, rather than as the final step.

A recent field study showed that fields in which tomato residues and weeds were left after harvest retained higher moth densities than fields that were cleaned and subjected to soil cultivation. This result supports the need to terminate old fields and prevent residues and green hosts from persisting between seasons. However, the study should be interpreted as an evaluation of a package of measures including: Residue removal. Weed management. Soil cultivation. The independent effect of each measure cannot therefore be separated from the available results.

Sanitary disposal means using a pathway that prevents living pest stages in the residues from returning to the field. Acceptable pathways, depending on available resources and regulations, include: Transport to a closed treatment facility. Controlled thermophilic composting. Industrial treatment of residues. Containment within a closed system until tissues decompose. Burial or treatment according to local guidance and requirements. Anaerobic digestion in designated facilities. Discarding residues in an open pile, watercourse, or neighboring area is not sanitary disposal.

Tomato residues may be included in an organic-composting system when the system can: Generate uniform heat throughout the pile. Permit turning of materials. Prevent cold zones. Prevent insect escape before treatment is completed. Decompose plant tissues. Prevent subsequent germination of tomato seeds. Not every compost pile can be assumed to kill Tuta stages. A cold or uneven pile may allow larvae or pupae to survive or moths to emerge. The process should be treated as a treatment system, not merely a residue-collection site.

sectionTitle: Residues from Sorting Stations and Processing Plants
text: Sorting stations and tomato-processing plants become risk points when the following accumulate: Infested fruits. Calyces and leaves. Solid sorting residues. Contaminated crates. Wash water and residues. Seeds capable of producing volunteer plants. The management plan should include: Collection of residues in a designated area. Prevention of exposed accumulation near fields. Transport to a treatment system. Prevention of volunteer tomato emergence. Cleaning of vehicles and crates. Monitoring the site with traps when cultivated fields are nearby.

Containers can transfer the pest when they contain: Infested leaves. Small fruits. Hidden larvae. Pupae in corners or cracks. Soil and plant residues. Cleaning consists of: 1. Removing all plant material. 2. Cleaning corners and cracks. 3. Washing washable containers when necessary. 4. Drying them. 5. Collecting cleaning waste. 6. Storing containers away from residue piles. 7. Preventing their use for transplant transport before cleaning. Containers should not be moved directly from a heavily infested old field into a nursery or new crop.

Cleaning should include: Harvesting machines. Plant shredders. Trailers. Transplant vehicles. Fruit-transport vehicles. Conveyors and loading-area floors. Particular attention should be given to sites where the following accumulate: Leaves. Small fruits. Soil. Stems. Moist organic matter. Cleaning waste should be collected and treated as infested residue rather than discarded in the next field.

Residues should be transported in: Closed vehicles. Containers with intact sides. Covered transport units. Sealed bags for small quantities. Containment aims to prevent: Fruit spillage. Leaf dispersal. Larval escape. Moth dispersal. Establishment of volunteer tomato plants along roads and unloading areas.

Mechanical methods are more applicable in nurseries because of: Small area. High value of each transplant. Ease of batch traceability. Possibility of tray isolation. Basic measures include: Rejecting infested transplants. Isolating neighboring trays. Removing residues daily. Cleaning benches and walkways. Preventing leaf accumulation beneath trays. Using closed containers. Preventing surplus transplants from remaining after the gap-filling period. The nursery objective is to produce pest-free transplants, not merely to reduce pest abundance.

This includes: Removal of limited infestation foci. Removal of infested leaves and fruits in closed containers. Maintenance of screen integrity. Controlled door management. Cleaning floors and walkways. Terminating plants after harvest. Removing residues between crop cycles. Inspecting transplants before entry. These methods are generally more effective in greenhouses than in open fields because the structure has defined boundaries that can be isolated and monitored.

Economically useful measures in open fields focus on: Containing the first infestation foci. Rejecting severely infested young plants. Collecting infested fruits. Removing postharvest residues. Cleaning equipment and crates. Managing sorting waste. Preventing pest transfer with transplants. Large-scale removal of infested leaves or attempts to create barriers over extensive processing-tomato fields are generally of limited economic and practical value.

Barriers and organized removal have little direct toxic effect on natural enemies, but they may reduce their numbers indirectly when: Leaves carrying parasitized eggs are removed. Plant parts bearing predators are discarded. Screens prevent natural enemies from entering the structure. Temperature and humidity change inside the greenhouse. They should therefore be integrated with biological-control programs by: Inspecting eggs before leaf removal. Retaining plant parts containing successful parasitoids when crop condition permits. Releasing natural enemies inside isolated structures. Monitoring the internal climate.

Efficacy of mechanical and physical control should not be measured by the number of plant parts removed, but by its effect on pest populations and the crop. Evaluation indicators include: Reduction in newly appearing mines. Reduction in live larvae. Reduction in growing-point infestation. Reduction in the percentage of infested fruits. Reduction in trap catches inside protected structures. Delayed onset of infestation. Reduced transfer of the pest into the next crop. Avoidance of excessive leaf-area loss. Absence of serious deterioration in ventilation or fruit quality. Reduced need for rescue sprays.

Barriers should be evaluated using a combination of indicators including: Moth numbers outside and inside the structure. Percentage of openings remaining intact. Frequency of door opening. Eggs and larvae within the crop. Temperature and humidity. Air movement. Fruit-set percentage. Fruit quality. °Brix. Occurrence of diseases associated with high humidity. A barrier should not be considered successful when it reduces Tuta entry but causes substantial losses in fruit set or quality because of poor ventilation.

Focus removal is considered successful when: No new mines appear on neighboring plants. Live-larva numbers decline. The infested area does not expand. Growing-point or fruit infestation does not appear. The focus does not require repeated uneconomic removal. Major canopy loss does not occur. If infestation continues after removal, this may indicate: Undetected eggs. Larval movement. An immigration wave. Infestation extending beyond the identified focus. Need for an additional intervention.

sectionTitle: Levels of Use According to Production System
text: Method Nursery Greenhouse Open Field Insect-proof screens Essential Essential Limited Double-entry system Highly important Highly important Not applicable Batch isolation Essential Important At transplant receipt Rejection of infested plants Essential Useful for foci Useful at the beginning Removal of infested leaves Practical Practical under limited infestation Economically limited Collection of infested fruits Limited before fruiting Important Important Crop-residue removal Essential Essential Essential Container cleaning Essential Important Essential Equipment cleaning Important Important Essential Residue containment Essential Essential Essential

Error One: Classifying Any Manual Practice as Mechanical Control The method should be measurable and have a clear objective. Error Two: Removing an Infested Leaf and Leaving It in the Field The pest cycle is not interrupted if the larva can continue developing. Error Three: Removing All Leaves Carrying Mines Old mines do not necessarily represent active infestation, and excessive removal damages the crop. Error Four: Relying on Screens Without Inspecting Transplants The pest may enter the structure with plants. Error Five: Relying on Thread Count Instead of Actual Opening Size Screen specifications and air permeability should be evaluated. Error Six: Tightening Screening While Neglecting Ventilation Physiological or disease-related losses may occur. Error Seven: Leaving Doors Open This weakens the physical barrier regardless of screen quality. Error Eight: Treating Shredding as the Final Residue Treatment Shredding is a preparatory stage and should be followed by appropriate containment or treatment. Error Nine: Transporting Residues in Open Vehicles This causes fruits and leaves to fall and spreads the pest. Error Ten: Moving Harvest Crates into the Nursery Without Cleaning Crates may carry plant material and living pest stages. Error Eleven: Disposing of Residues in an Open Pile The pile may become a source of pupae, moths, and volunteer plants. Error Twelve: Applying Manual Removal After Infestation Has Spread Economic feasibility declines rapidly as field area and pest density increase.

sectionTitle: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
text: The practical priorities are: 1. Inspect transplants and isolate batches before transplanting. 2. Reject severely infested young plants. 3. Contain the first infestation foci before they spread. 4. Avoid removing a large leaf area from processing tomatoes. 5. Collect infested fruits and do not leave them between rows. 6. Allocate dedicated containers for infested material. 7. Terminate the field as soon as harvest is no longer economically viable. 8. Collect remaining fruits before shredding plants. 9. Use shredding as one stage within a disposal system, not as a stand-alone measure. 10. Transport residues in covered vehicles. 11. Clean harvesting machinery and crates before moving them into another field. 12. Manage sorting-station and processing-plant residues within a closed system. 13. Use screens and controlled doors in nurseries. 14. Compare trap catches inside and outside the nursery. 15. Evaluate the effect of screens on ventilation and transplant quality before adoption.

Method Mode of Action Target Stage Scope of Use Insect-proof screens Prevent entry and oviposition Adult moths Nurseries and greenhouses Double doors Reduce entry with air movement Adult moths Protected structures Isolation of transplant batches Prevent spread of infestation Eggs, larvae, and moths Nurseries Rejection of infested transplants Remove the infestation source Eggs and larvae Nurseries and newly planted fields Removal of infested plant foci Contain early infestation Eggs and larvae Greenhouses and fields Collection of infested fruits Prevent completion of development and pupation Larvae All production systems Crop-residue removal Remove sources of subsequent generations Eggs, larvae, and pupae All production systems Residue shredding Reduce volume and tear tissues Larvae and pupae, partially After harvest Containment and treatment Prevent emergence and dispersal Larvae, pupae, and moths Residue management Cleaning containers and equipment Prevent mechanical transfer Larvae and pupae All systems Covering residues during transport Prevent spillage and escape Larvae and pupae All systems

Scientifically based mechanical and physical control of Tuta absoluta depends on exclusion, isolation, organized removal, and sanitary containment of crop residues. Insect-proof screens and properly sealed entrances are among the most important exclusion tools in nurseries and greenhouses, but their efficacy depends on installation quality, door management, transplant inspection, and maintenance of adequate ventilation. Leaf removal should not be applied generally or randomly. It should be restricted to limited foci with active infestation while considering canopy cover, fruit protection, and natural enemies. Collection of infested fruits is an important measure for preventing larvae from completing development and for reducing rots and market or processing losses. Postharvest crop-residue removal is also a major intervention and should be implemented through a system that includes fruit collection, crop termination, shredding, containment or treatment, equipment cleaning, and continued field monitoring. Shredding alone does not guarantee elimination of all pest stages; it is one stage within a sanitary-disposal system. The relative importance of these methods varies with production system. Screens and isolation are essential in nurseries and greenhouses, whereas priorities in open fields are focus containment, collection of infested fruits, residue removal, and cleaning of equipment and containers. References

Behavioural control relies on exploiting the signals used by the insect to locate a mating partner, host plant, or oviposition site, with the aim of modifying its behaviour in a way that reduces its ability to reproduce and cause damage. Sex pheromones are the most important behavioural tools developed against Tuta absoluta. Adult females release volatile compounds that attract males and enable them to locate and reach females for mating. The principal components of this pheromone have been synthesized and used for: Early detection of pest presence. Monitoring male activity. Mass trapping. Mating disruption. Attract-and-kill. Evaluation of integrated management programs. A high number of moths captured does not necessarily mean that a trap has achieved actual control because trap density and purpose differ between monitoring and mass trapping. Likewise, a reduction in male trap catch alone does not prove a reduction in mating, egg deposition, or fruit infestation. The principal applications of behavioural control are concentrated in four areas: 1. Mass trapping of males using pheromone traps. 2. Mating disruption and prevention of males from locating females. 3. Attract-and-kill techniques. 4.
