Plant volatiles and food attractants, which remain at a more experimental stage than sex pheromones. These methods perform better when initiated early, pest density is low, and they are integrated with agricultural sanitation, biological control, and prevention of immigration by mated females from neighboring fields.

sectionTitle: Chemical Basis of the Sex Pheromone
text: The female sex pheromone of Tuta absoluta consists of a blend of long-chain acetate compounds. The principal component is: (3E,8Z,11Z)-3,8,11-tetradecatrien-1-yl acetate Abbreviated as: E3Z8Z11-14:Ac The secondary component is: (3E,8Z)-3,8-tetradecadien-1-yl acetate Abbreviated as: E3Z8-14:Ac Chemical studies demonstrated the presence of both compounds in female secretions, and several commercial formulations have used a blend close to: 90 parts of the principal component to 10 parts of the secondary component. However, lure efficacy does not depend on blend ratio alone. It is also affected by: Loaded amount. Chemical-isomer purity. Dispenser type. Release rate. Temperature. Lure age. Storage method. Trap design. Moth density in the field. Two lures carrying the same nominal amount may produce different catches because of differences in dispenser material and pheromone-release rate.

The female releases pheromone during the sexual-activity period. The male then responds through a behavioural sequence that includes: 1. Detection of pheromone molecules by the antennae. 2. Upwind flight. 3. Tracking the odour gradient. 4. Approaching the signal source. 5. Locating the female. 6. Initiating courtship and mating behaviour. The male does not fly in a permanently straight line but relies on repeated loss and reacquisition of the signal during flight. Mass trapping exploits this response to attract males into traps, whereas mating disruption interferes with the male’s ability to identify the true pheromone source.

Tuta absoluta is mainly nocturnal and crepuscular, although the timing of flight and pheromone response varies according to: Temperature. Day length. Light intensity. Moth age. Mating status. Greenhouse or field conditions. Some studies have shown increased male response to pheromone traps around dawn and during the early morning hours. This does not mean traps should operate only at these times. Pheromone traps remain active continuously, but understanding activity timing assists in studying pest behaviour and interpreting trap results.

Monitoring Traps These are used at low density to: Confirm pest presence. Monitor trends in male abundance. Determine the beginning of flight. Guide plant inspection. Evaluate immigration waves. Their purpose is not to capture an influential proportion of the pest population. Mass Trapping This uses a large number of traps to: Remove a high proportion of males. Reduce the probability of males locating females. Reduce mating frequency. Reduce the quantity of fertilized eggs in subsequent generations. Mating Disruption This does not depend mainly on capturing males. Instead, pheromone is released throughout the crop area at a concentration and distribution that prevent males from locating females. A monitoring-trap density should not be used and then described as a mass-trapping program.

Mass Trapping Mass trapping involves deploying a high density of sex-pheromone traps within and around the crop to capture the greatest possible proportion of males before they mate. A mass-trapping system consists of: An appropriate pheromone lure. A high-capacity trap. Sufficient deployment density. Regular maintenance. Early installation. Monitoring of plant infestation. Increasing trap number alone is insufficient. The rate of male removal must exceed the rate at which new males immigrate or emerge from the generation already present in the field.

sectionTitle: Mode of Action of Mass Trapping
text: Mass trapping acts through: Reducing the male-to-female ratio. Increasing the time required for females to find males. Delaying mating. Reducing the probability that some females will mate. Reducing the number of mating opportunities within the population. However, Tuta absoluta possesses characteristics that limit the efficacy of mass trapping when used alone, including: Males can mate more than once. Females are not attracted to the female sex pheromone. Males and females continue to immigrate from outside the field. Mating may occur before males reach traps. Initial pest density may be high. Immature stages are unaffected by traps. A very high proportion of males must therefore be removed before a clear effect on mating and egg deposition becomes evident.

Operational guides and reviews have reported approximate historical or advisory densities such as: Production System Historical or Advisory Trap Density Commercial greenhouses 20–25 traps/hectare Propagation nurseries Approximately 30 traps/hectare Open fields 40–50 traps/hectare A density of 40–50 traps per hectare is approximately equivalent to: 17–21 traps per feddan. These values are not fixed global recommendations because appropriate density varies according to: Trap type. Trap capacity. Lure strength. Field shape. Pest density. Degree of enclosure. Immigration sources. Cost of traps and maintenance. The stated density may be uneconomical or insufficient under some conditions and should therefore be experimentally evaluated in relation to plant and fruit infestation.

sectionTitle: Trap Types Suitable for Mass Trapping
text: Water Traps These are widely used because they: Hold large numbers of males. Do not saturate as rapidly as sticky surfaces. Are less affected by insect accumulation on adhesive material. Can be reused. They require: Maintenance of water level. Addition of a surface-tension-reducing material. Cleaning of containers. Prevention of overturning. Timely lure replacement. Sticky Traps These allow easy counting but may become less suitable for mass trapping at high densities because of: Surface saturation. Dust accumulation. Loss of adhesiveness. Need for frequent card replacement. Funnel or Container Traps These may be used when their design prevents moth escape and provides adequate capacity, but their performance should be tested against water or sticky traps under local conditions. Commercial trap appearance alone is insufficient. Performance should be compared through numbers of males captured and maintenance cost.

Mass trapping should begin: Before transplanting or at transplanting. Before male numbers increase. Before a large generation develops within the crop. At the first sign of activity in the area. Starting the program after infestation reaches high density provides limited efficacy because: Many females may already have mated. Eggs may already have been laid. Larvae may already be inside plants. Moths may continue emerging from pupae. Mass trapping should not be delayed until fruit infestation becomes visible.

Traps should be distributed evenly while considering: Edges facing immigration sources. Wind direction. Old fields. Entrances and worker routes. Transplant-storage areas. Field shape and area. Edges may require relatively higher density because they are more exposed to incoming moths. Traps should not be clustered in one part of the field while leaving the remaining area uncovered, because this creates weak zones within the program.

Mass trapping requires more intensive maintenance than monitoring and includes: Checking traps once or more each week. Replacing lost water. Cleaning containers. Removing insects and debris. Renewing the surface-tension-reducing material. Replacing saturated sticky cards. Replacing lures according to product longevity. Securing traps after wind or field operations. Recording lost or damaged traps. Loss of some traps or drying of water traps reduces actual network density even when the planned number remains unchanged on paper.

Efficacy should not be measured only by the total number of males captured. Capturing thousands of males may appear substantial but may represent only a small proportion of the actual population. Evaluation indicators include: Trend in male catch. Percentage of eggs on plants. Percentage of fertilized or hatched eggs. Number of new mines. Number of live larvae. Percentage of fruit infestation. Difference between edges and field interior. Comparison with a similar field without mass trapping, when possible. Reduction in leaf and fruit infestation is more important than the number of traps or total males captured.

Some studies showed that sex-pheromone mass trapping did not sufficiently reduce leaf or fruit infestation when used alone, even at relatively high trap densities. Reasons include: Attraction of males only. Multiple mating by males. Immigration of mated females. High initial density. Presence of eggs and larvae before program initiation. Inability of traps to capture all males. Mass trapping should therefore be used as a supporting component within IPM and not presented as a stand-alone substitute for biological control or other interventions under high infestation.

sectionTitle: When Is Mass Trapping More Appropriate?
text: The probability of success increases when: It begins before pest density rises. The area is relatively isolated. Immigration sources are limited. Old fields are managed. High-capacity traps are used. Traps are maintained regularly. The method is integrated with residue removal and natural enemies. Initial plant infestation is low. Trap coverage includes edges and entry sources. Efficacy declines in small fields surrounded by heavily infested crops because of continuous immigration.

Mating Disruption Mating disruption involves releasing synthetic pheromone throughout the crop area at a rate and distribution that interfere with communication between males and females, thereby reducing the probability that males locate and mate with females. It does not directly kill adult insects. Successful mating disruption is expected to cause: Reduced mating. Delayed mating. Reduced fertilized eggs. Fewer larvae in the next generation. Gradual reduction in damage. Its full effect does not appear immediately after dispensers are installed because eggs, larvae, and pupae already present are not directly affected by pheromone.

Mating disruption may occur through several overlapping mechanisms. Competitive Attraction Competitive Attraction Dispensers act as numerous false pheromone sources. Males move toward dispensers rather than females and expend time and energy tracking false sources. Efficacy increases when: Dispenser density is sufficient. Male density is low. Dispensers are evenly distributed. Release rate remains appropriate. Signal Camouflage Camouflage Pheromone concentration in the air increases to a level at which the odour plume from a female becomes difficult to distinguish. Sensory Adaptation or Fatigue Continuous exposure to high concentrations may temporarily reduce the responsiveness of olfactory receptors or male orientation behaviour. Not all dispensers act through the same mechanism. Performance depends on: Release rate. Number of sources. Spatial distribution. Air speed. Structure volume. Pest density.

Passive Dispensers These include: Polymer tubes. Strips. Ropes or threads. Rubber or plastic carriers. Slow-release gel points. They release pheromone continuously through diffusion and evaporation. Active Dispensers These include devices that release pheromone: In pulses. During periods of activity. At programmed rates. Through aerosol or spray devices. They use fewer release points but require: A power source or reliable device. Programming. Maintenance. Protection from environmental conditions. The number of passive dispensers cannot be compared directly with the number of aerosol devices because release rate per point and area of influence differ.

Dispensers should be installed: At transplanting or shortly before. At first male detection. Before infestation increases. Before large numbers of mated females are present. Delayed installation is a major cause of failure because disruption cannot reverse mating that has already occurred or kill larvae already inside the plant. When the program begins in a field with existing infestation, a corrective intervention targeting eggs and larvae may be required at the same time as dispenser installation.

sectionTitle: Effect of Pest Density on Mating-Disruption Success
text: Mating-disruption efficacy is affected by pest density. At low density: The distance between males and females is relatively greater. Random encounters are less likely. False sources become more effective. At high density: Males and females are closer together. Mating may occur without long-distance flight. Males are more likely to reach females despite disruption. A higher release rate or dispenser density may be required. Reducing the initial population is therefore a fundamental condition for successful mating disruption.

Early studies showed that mating disruption was more consistent in highly enclosed greenhouses than in poorly isolated structures. Protected structures reduce: Immigration of mated females. Immigration of new males. Rapid pheromone loss caused by wind. Irregularity of the pheromone cloud. In open fields, efficacy is more strongly affected by: Wind. Small treated areas. Edge effects. Immigration of mated females. Variation in topography. Presence of neighboring untreated fields. This does not mean mating disruption cannot be used in open fields. Some trials have recorded success, but results are more site- and system-dependent and should not be generalized without local testing.

Studies have used different systems that cannot be converted into one universal recommendation. Examples include: 500 dispensers/hectare, each carrying approximately 60 mg, equivalent to an initial pheromone dose of about 30 g/hectare in highly enclosed greenhouses. 1,000 dispensers/hectare with a total dose of approximately 60 g/hectare in other studies. 500–1,000 dispensers/hectare in comparative greenhouse programs. 675–900 tubular dispensers/hectare in a recent trial under high pest density. Limited numbers of active aerosol devices instead of hundreds of passive dispensers. Gel or point-source systems with different quantities and concentrations. Increasing dispenser number does not always increase efficacy proportionally because decisive factors include: Pheromone-release rate. Persistence throughout the season. Distribution. Field area. Pest density. Degree of enclosure. The registered dose of an approved product in the country should be used. Experimental doses or products from other countries should not be transferred directly into commercial use.

Dispensers should be distributed uniformly throughout the treated area while considering: Attachment to plants or supports according to product instructions. Raising them as plants grow when necessary. Increased edge protection. Avoidance of uncovered areas. Avoidance of excessive clustering at one point. Coverage of entrances and sides facing immigration sources. Irregular distribution creates low-concentration zones where mating may occur.

Air movement affects: Pheromone dispersal. Rate of loss. Direction of the odour cloud. Formation of zones with high or low concentration. In open fields, wind may carry pheromone outside the treated area, particularly when the plot is: Small. Long and narrow. Exposed on several sides. Adjacent to untreated fields. In greenhouses, poor ventilation may cause pheromone accumulation, whereas large openings may accelerate pheromone loss and permit entry of mated females.

Mating disruption generally becomes more effective as treated area increases and the ratio of edge to interior decreases. Small plots face problems including: Strong edge effects. Immigration of females. Pheromone loss. Difficulty in forming a uniform pheromone environment. Application across: A contiguous production block. A group of adjacent greenhouses. A coordinated production area. Is more logical than application in a small field surrounded by untreated infestation sources.

Dispenser density may be increased relatively along exposed edges, particularly those: Facing prevailing winds. Adjacent to old tomato fields. Near entrances. Bordering residue sources. However, edge reinforcement does not compensate for poor internal distribution or continued immigration of large numbers of mated females.

A reduction in male catch in pheromone traps is used as an indicator of disrupted communication and is sometimes called: Trap Shutdown However, trap shutdown or reduced catch alone does not prove successful control. Widespread pheromone may prevent males from reaching monitoring traps while some short-range mating continues within the canopy. Evaluation should therefore include: Eggs on plants. Hatching percentage. New mines. Live larvae. Fruit infestation. Distribution of infestation between edges and interior. Continued effectiveness of dispensers. Reduction in plant infestation and the following generation provides the true evidence of program success.

Dedicated pheromone traps are used to assess mating disruption, but their readings differ from normal monitoring. Low catch may indicate: Successful disruption. A weak lure. Low pest density. Trap malfunction. The following should therefore be reviewed together: Traps within the treated area. Traps outside it. Plant inspection. Lure age. Trap condition. Internal traps alone should not be used to estimate the true male population during mating disruption.

Effective life varies according to: Dispenser material. Pheromone amount. Release rate. Temperature. Solar radiation. Wind. Season length. Some dispensers may persist through a short crop cycle, whereas other systems require replacement or reapplication. The following should be recorded: Installation date. Batch number. Declared period of effectiveness. Replacement date. Thermal conditions during the season. An increase in infestation should not be the first indication that dispenser life has ended.

Trials in open fields have produced variable results. Early trials recorded: A major reduction in male access to traps. Without a comparable reduction in leaf or fruit infestation. This was often associated with: Small experimental areas. High pest density. Immigration of mated females. Pheromone loss caused by wind. Unsuitable dose or formulation. Later trials in particular areas and under specific systems recorded reduced infestation when appropriate mating-disruption systems were used. Mating disruption in open fields should therefore be classified as a promising, site- and system-dependent technique rather than a fixed general recommendation for every field.

The probability of success is higher in greenhouses when: Openings are fitted with screens. Double doors are used. Female immigration is prevented. Treatment begins early. Pest density is low. Residues are removed. Plant infestation is monitored. Studies have recorded substantial reductions in: Male catch. Leaf mines. Larval numbers. Fruit infestation. However, mating disruption cannot compensate for infested transplants or residues from the previous crop remaining inside the greenhouse.

sectionTitle: Combining Mating Disruption with Biological Control
text: Pheromones do not have direct insecticidal toxicity comparable to broad-spectrum insecticides and can therefore be integrated with: Egg parasitoids. Predators. Bacillus thuringiensis. Entomopathogenic fungi. Entomopathogenic nematodes. Mating disruption targets adult communication and mating, whereas natural enemies target eggs and larvae. The combined approach therefore covers several stages of the pest life cycle. Nevertheless, pheromones should not be assumed to be behaviourally neutral to all natural enemies. Experimental studies have reported responses by some Trichogramma populations to host pheromone or behavioural changes after exposure. Compatibility should therefore be evaluated within the local program.

Mating disruption may require complementary intervention when: The program begins after infestation is already present. Larval density is high. Mated females immigrate. Edge foci appear. The crop approaches fruiting. Complementary intervention should preferably target: Eggs and newly hatched larvae. Defined infestation foci. Stages not directly affected by mating disruption. Mating disruption should not be used to justify calendar spraying. Its purpose is to reduce reliance on insecticides while maintaining acceptable infestation levels.

Attract-and-Kill Attract-and-kill combines an attractant with a toxicant at a defined point. Males are attracted to the point and exposed to the active ingredient. Unlike a trap, the insect may not be physically retained in a container but may contact: A gel. A treated surface. A droplet or point containing insecticide. Potential benefits include: Use of a small quantity of insecticide. Reduced whole-canopy treatment. Targeting pheromone-responsive males. Lower exposure of non-target organisms when correctly designed. However, efficacy is affected by many of the same limitations as mass trapping: Attraction of males only. Immigration of mated females. High pest density. Degradation of the attractant or toxicant. Effects of heat and dust. Need for uniform distribution.

An attract-and-kill point should provide: An appropriate pheromone-release rate. Attraction that competes successfully with natural females. A rapidly acting toxicant. Stability under field conditions. Low risk of insecticide transfer to the crop. Sufficient spatial distribution. Compatibility with natural enemies. Legal registration for the intended use. Pheromone should not be mixed with an insecticide improvisationally because formulation, dose, and carrier determine both efficacy and safety.

sectionTitle: Position of Attract-and-Kill Within the Program
text: Attract-and-kill remains less widely used than traps and mating disruption, and its performance depends on the specific product and system. It should therefore be treated as: A specialized method. Requiring a designed and registered product. Subject to local evaluation. Not suitable for on-farm manufacture by mixing insecticide with a pheromone lure. Results from one gel product should not be generalized to all attract-and-kill systems.

Female attractants are important because capturing females may directly reduce oviposition. Volatile compounds from tomato and host plants are being studied, including odours released by: Leaves. Growing points. Flowers. Fruits. Infested plants. Females may respond to some compounds while searching for oviposition sites. However, development of a reliable commercial female lure requires: Identification of the effective blend. Field stability. Differentiation between attraction to a plant and attraction into a trap. Improvement in capture rate. Avoidance of attracting females into the crop without retaining them. Female attractants remain less ready for commercial application than male-targeted sex pheromones.

Light traps use attraction to a light source of a particular wavelength. They may capture both male and female Tuta absoluta, but their use faces scientific and environmental limitations including: Low specificity. Capture of non-target species. Potential capture of natural enemies. Effects of surrounding illumination and moonlight. Need for a power source. Variation in response among wavelengths. Light traps are therefore not standard replacements for pheromone traps and should not be incorporated into a large commercial program without evaluating their effects on non-target organisms.

sectionTitle: Compatibility of Light and Pheromone Traps
text: Combining light and pheromone may increase moth catches in some experiments, but increased catch does not necessarily prove improved control. Evaluation should include: Proportion of females captured. Proportion of males captured. Plant infestation. Beneficial insects captured. Energy consumption. Operating cost. The combination should not be recommended merely because a larger number of insects is captured.

sectionTitle: Effect of Weather on Pheromone-Based Methods
text: Temperature affects: Pheromone evaporation rate. Lure longevity. Flight activity. Male response. Wind affects: Shape of the pheromone plume. Direction of male movement. Pheromone loss from the field. Interference among traps. Other effects include: Dust on sticky surfaces. Rainfall on some point-source formulations. Solar radiation on material degradation. Humidity on moth behaviour. Lures and dispensers may therefore require more rapid replacement under hot conditions, according to product data and monitoring results.

Interference may occur when traps or dispensers are placed: Too close together. Near a strong mating-disruption source. Within a non-uniform pheromone cloud. Interference may cause: Reduced catch in some traps. Male attraction to an area between traps. Difficulty in interpreting results. Monitoring traps, mass-trapping devices, and mating-disruption dispensers should be clearly separated, with the location and function of each tool recorded.

Lures should be evaluated according to: Identity of compounds. Ratio among components. Isomeric purity. Loaded amount. Dispenser material. Release curve. Shelf life. Storage conditions. Lures should not be used when they are: From an unknown source. Stored under high temperature. Expired. Previously opened. Not intended for the target species. Weak lure performance may reduce catches and be misinterpreted as a genuine population decline.

Lures should be stored according to manufacturer instructions, usually: In their original packaging. In a cool location. Away from direct sunlight. Separate from insecticides and strong odours. Unopened until use. The following should be recorded: Batch number. Receipt date. Expiry date. Storage temperature. Installation date. Lures should not be left in a vehicle or hot storage area before use.

These include: A gradual decline in male numbers. Reduction in new eggs. Reduction in active mines. Reduction in live larvae. Reduction in fruit infestation. Absence of increasing infestation along field edges. Reduced need for chemical intervention. Continued trap operation without drying or saturation. Recording a large number of captured males alone is not sufficient as an independent indicator of success.

These include: Reduced male access to test traps. Reduced fertilized eggs or hatching. Reduction in mines in the following generation. Reduction in larval numbers. Reduction in fruit infestation. Uniform reduction between field edges and interior. Continued dispenser efficacy throughout the protection period. Reduced number of corrective interventions.

These include: Continued high male catch within the treated area. Continued increase in eggs and larvae. Concentration of infestation along field edges. Increased fruit infestation. Immigration of mated females. Delayed dispenser installation. Presence of uncovered zones. Expiry of dispenser effectiveness. High pest density before program initiation. Small treated area. Failure should not automatically be attributed to poor pheromone quality because it may result from program design, timing, immigration, or pest density.
Date Trap Number Location Trap Type Lure Condition Number of Males Water or Adhesive Condition Maintenance Notes The record should be linked with data on: Eggs. Active mines. Larvae. Infested fruits.

Item Required Data Product or dispenser type Pheromone material Pheromone quantity per dispenser Number of dispensers/hectare Number per feddan Installation date Edge locations Expected replacement date Test traps Infestation before application Infestation after application Complementary interventions Percentage of infested fruits

Field Situation Most Logical Strategy Detection of pest presence only Monitoring traps Low pest density and relatively isolated field Mass trapping within IPM Well-sealed greenhouse and low pest density Mating disruption Small open field near infested fields Do not rely on mating disruption alone Existing high infestation Reduce population first, then apply behavioural method Immigration wave from an old field Manage the source and field edges Existing biological-control program Use pheromones compatible with natural-enemy monitoring Near harvest Evaluate economic feasibility and speed of effect

Error One: Using One Monitoring Trap and Describing It as Mass Trapping Mass trapping requires a dense network and high-capacity traps. Error Two: Treating the Number of Captured Moths as Direct Evidence of Reduced Damage Eggs, larvae, and fruit infestation must be measured. Error Three: Starting Mass Trapping After Infestation Has Increased A large proportion of females may already have mated and laid eggs. Error Four: Using Mass Trapping Alone Immature stages and immigration sources remain untargeted. Error Five: Using a Fixed Trap Number Under All Conditions Requirements vary with area, isolation, and trap type. Error Six: Neglecting Dry Water Traps A dry trap does not retain males. Error Seven: Installing Mating Disruption After the Outbreak Begins It does not affect eggs and larvae already present. Error Eight: Applying Mating Disruption in a Small Area Surrounded by Infested Fields Immigration of mated females and edge effects increase. Error Nine: Treating Trap Shutdown as Sufficient Evidence of Success Mating may continue despite low trap catch. Error Ten: Copying a Dose from a Foreign Study Products, release rates, and registration conditions differ. Error Eleven: Irregular Dispenser Distribution This creates zones with low pheromone concentration. Error Twelve: Neglecting Field Edges Edges are the areas most exposed to immigration. Error Thirteen: Mixing Pheromone with Insecticide to Produce a Homemade Attract-and-Kill Product Only a designed and registered formulation should be used. Error Fourteen: Relying on Non-Specific Light Traps They may affect beneficial insects and do not provide selective control. Error Fifteen: Poor Lure Storage This may cause loss of active material before use.

sectionTitle: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
text: The priorities are: 1. Use pheromone traps primarily for monitoring and warning. 2. Do not consider one trap per feddan a mass-trapping program. 3. Begin any mass-trapping program before pest density increases. 4. Increase trap density along high-risk edges. 5. Maintain water traps continuously. 6. Link male catches with inspection of eggs, mines, and fruits. 7. Do not rely on mass trapping alone. 8. Do not generalize mating disruption to open fields without local testing. 9. Apply mating disruption over the largest contiguous area possible. 10. Reduce initial pest density before or at the start of disruption. 11. Manage old fields and sources of mated females. 12. Use the registered product dose rather than directly copying experimental doses. 13. Evaluate field edges and interior separately. 14. Continue plant inspection even when male trap catch stops. 15. Integrate pheromones with natural enemies and selective products.

Behavioural control of Tuta absoluta depends mainly on the female sex pheromone, which consists of a principal component: E3Z8Z11-14:Ac And a secondary component: E3Z8-14:Ac These compounds are used in monitoring, mass trapping, mating disruption, and attract-and-kill. Low-density monitoring traps should be distinguished from mass trapping, which requires a larger network of traps. Historical advisory mass-trapping densities of approximately 40–50 traps per hectare have been reported for open fields, equivalent to about 17–21 traps per feddan. However, this density is not a fixed rule and should be evaluated according to trap type, infestation level, and immigration pressure. Mass trapping does not always provide sufficient damage reduction when used alone because the pheromone attracts males only, males can mate several times, and mated females can immigrate from outside. Mating disruption depends on preventing males from locating females and performs best when: It begins early. Pest density is low. The treated area is large or the structure is well sealed. Immigration of mated females is prevented. Dispensers are distributed uniformly. Plant inspection continues. Several greenhouse studies under high containment have achieved successful results using different dispenser rates and densities, whereas results have been less consistent in open greenhouses and open fields. Trap shutdown alone is not sufficient evidence of successful mating disruption. A reduction in eggs, larvae, mines, and fruit infestation must also be demonstrated. Plant-based female attractants and attract-and-kill techniques remain promising, but are less mature and less widely used than traps and mating disruption and require registered products and local evaluation. References

Biological control is a fundamental component of sustainable management of Tuta absoluta. It relies on natural enemies capable of attacking eggs, larvae, pupae, or adults, thereby reducing pest survival, reproduction, and carryover into subsequent generations. Biological control does not merely mean releasing a beneficial insect into the field. It is a scientific system that requires: Correct identification of the natural enemy. Knowledge of the pest stage it attacks. Assessment of its searching ability and capacity to locate the host. Determination of suitable temperature and humidity conditions. Confirmation of compatibility with pesticides used. Selection of release timing and method. Measurement of establishment and reproduction within the crop. Evaluation of its effect on pest density and fruit infestation. Assessment of the economic feasibility of the program. Biological control is particularly important for Tuta absoluta because: Eggs are laid on plant surfaces accessible to egg parasitoids. Larvae remain inside mines that some parasitoids and predators can detect. Several generalist predators can consume eggs and newly hatched larvae. Multiple generations allow natural enemies to reproduce concurrently with the pest. The pest rapidly develops resistance to insecticides. There is a need to reduce pesticide residues in fresh-market and processing tomatoes. Natural enemies can be integrated with pheromones, microbial products, and selective insecticides. However, biological-control efficacy is not constant under all conditions. Results are affected by monitoring data, initial pest density, release timing, climate, cultivar, crop-management practices, immigration from neighboring fields, and pesticides used.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: A natural enemy is an organism that attacks, consumes, or uses a pest to complete its life cycle, thereby reducing pest abundance. Natural enemies of Tuta absoluta include: 1. Egg parasitoids. 2. Larval parasitoids. 3. Pupal parasitoids. 4. Predators. 5. Entomopathogenic organisms. This section is limited to insect parasitoids and predators. Entomopathogenic bacteria, fungi, and nematodes are discussed in the following section.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Difference Between a Parasitoid and a Predator
text: Parasitoid Parasitoid A parasitoid is an insect whose adult lays eggs inside or on the surface of an insect host. The immature stage then feeds on the host and usually kills it by the end of development. A parasitoid differs from a true parasite in that it: Usually requires one host to complete the development of one individual. Kills the host by the end of its development. Has a free-living adult stage. Searches for a specific pest stage. Predator Predator A predator is an organism that attacks and consumes several prey individuals during its lifetime. A predator is characterized by the fact that it: Kills more than one pest individual. May attack several pest stages. May feed on more than one pest species. Requires suitable food availability for establishment and reproduction. May partially feed on plants in some species.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Biological control is divided into four principal approaches. Natural Biological Control This is the reduction caused by naturally occurring natural enemies without deliberate releases. It includes: Local egg and larval parasitoids. Predatory bugs. Lacewings. Orius species. Spiders. Ground predators. Predatory wasps. Its importance varies among fields according to pesticide intensity, plant diversity, and climatic conditions. Conservation Biological Control Conservation Biological Control This aims to protect existing natural enemies and increase their capacity to function. It includes: Reducing broad-spectrum insecticides. Selecting more selective materials. Avoiding spraying when unnecessary. Maintaining supportive non-host plants. Providing nectar and pollen. Selective weed management. Avoiding unnecessary removal of parasitized eggs. Avoiding tillage or pruning that removes large numbers of natural enemies at sensitive times. This strategy is particularly suitable for open fields because insect releases may be affected by dispersal, immigration, heat, and wind. Inoculative Release Inoculative Release Relatively small numbers of a natural enemy are released early in the season to establish a population that reproduces within the crop and provides extended control. This approach is especially used with: Macrolophus pygmaeus. Nesidiocoris tenuis. Some larval parasitoids capable of establishment. Release should begin early because the predator or parasitoid requires time to reproduce and reach an effective density. Inundative Release Inundative Release Large and repeated numbers are released to achieve rapid suppression without necessarily relying on permanent establishment. This approach is commonly used with: Trichogramma species. Certain predators in specific situations. Some commercially produced parasitoids. Under this system, the natural enemy is treated as a control agent requiring a dose, timing, and repeat interval, but it remains a living organism affected by temperature, pesticides, and handling quality. Classical Biological Control Classical Biological Control This involves introducing a natural enemy from the pest’s native range to establish it permanently in the invaded region. It requires: Accurate taxonomic identification. Host-range assessment. Environmental risk evaluation. Quarantine. Regulatory approval. Post-release monitoring. A parasitoid or predator should not be moved between countries or a non-native species released without formal assessment because the natural enemy itself may affect non-target species or interfere with local natural enemies.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Criteria for Selecting a Natural Enemy
text: A species should not be adopted as a biological-control agent merely because it attacks Tuta absoluta in the laboratory. The following should be evaluated: Degree of host specificity. Pest stage attacked. Searching rate. Ability to reach eggs or larvae on or within the plant. Number of pest individuals killed. Reproductive rate. Thermal compatibility with the production season. Ability to establish on tomato. Compatibility with other natural enemies. Sensitivity to pesticides. Ease of mass production. Storage and transport quality. Potential effects on the crop. Potential attack on non-target species. Release cost. The strain, not only the species, should be selected because strains of the same species may differ in: Searching efficiency. Fecundity. Heat tolerance. Flight ability. Acceptance of Tuta eggs. Storage tolerance.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Natural-Enemy Group Target Stage of Tuta absoluta Principal Effect Trichogramma spp. Eggs Prevent larval hatching Necremnus tutae Larvae inside mines Parasitism and host feeding Bracon nigricans Larvae Host paralysis and ectoparasitism Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris Larvae Endoparasitism Pseudapanteles dignus Larvae Endoparasitism Macrolophus pygmaeus Eggs and newly hatched larvae Predation Nesidiocoris tenuis Eggs and newly hatched larvae Predation Dicyphus errans Eggs and larvae Predation Orius spp. Eggs and small larvae Predation Chrysoperla carnea Eggs and exposed larvae Predation No species targets all stages with equal efficacy. Integration is achieved when natural enemies targeting different stages of the pest life cycle are used together.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Eggs are a strategic control stage because killing the embryo before hatching prevents: Larval entry into the leaf. Formation of mines. Movement of larvae into growing points and fruits. Feeding damage. Production of the next generation. Egg parasitoids act before larvae become protected inside plant tissues. However, their success requires: Synchrony between parasitoid presence and oviposition. Good distribution within the field. Ability to search among plant trichomes. Absence of toxic pesticide exposure. Repeated releases during oviposition periods.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: The genus Trichogramma includes minute wasps that parasitize lepidopteran eggs. The female lays one or more eggs inside the pest egg, and the parasitoid larva feeds on its contents, preventing the Tuta larva from hatching. Parasitized eggs generally darken as parasitoid development progresses, and the adult parasitoid emerges through a small circular exit hole. Species studied against Tuta absoluta include: Trichogramma achaeae. Trichogramma pretiosum. Trichogramma evanescens. Trichogramma cacoeciae. Trichogramma euproctidis. Other local species and strains. Species and strains differ in efficacy. They should therefore be evaluated using Tuta absoluta eggs on tomato plants, not only on eggs of the factitious rearing host.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Trichogramma achaeae is among the best-known egg parasitoids studied against Tuta absoluta, particularly in Mediterranean greenhouse programs. Important characteristics include: Acceptance of Tuta eggs. Searching ability on tomato plants. Short life cycle. Capacity for production on factitious hosts. Suitability for repeated releases. Compatibility with predators, pheromones, and microbial products when properly managed. A Spanish greenhouse trial recorded a high reduction in damage using an experimental release rate of approximately: 75 adults per square meter every 3–4 days. This is approximately equivalent to: 750,000 individuals per hectare per release. Or approximately: 315,000 individuals per feddan per release. However, this rate formed part of a specific greenhouse experiment under particular temperature and plant-density conditions and does not represent a general commercial rate for open-field tomatoes. The practical rate should be determined according to: Egg density. Field area. Plant density. Number of release points. Parasitoid quality. Temperature. Immigration rate. Instructions for the registered product.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Two strains of T. achaeae may differ in: Number of eggs parasitized. Preferred host-egg age. Searching capacity. Heat tolerance. Female ratio. Emergence rate. Flight ability. Other Trichogramma species may outperform T. achaeae in specific tests. Species name alone should therefore not be regarded as a guarantee of efficacy. The following should be known: Strain source. Production date. Rearing host. Quality-control results. Suitability for local conditions.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Most egg parasitoids prefer relatively young eggs because their contents are more suitable for parasitoid development. Suitability generally declines as embryonic development advances and hatching approaches. Releases should therefore coincide with: Appearance of the first eggs. Increased moth catches. Expected oviposition periods. Hatching waves predicted from temperature. Late releases provide no benefit against eggs that have already hatched and larvae that have entered the plant.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Temperature affects: Rate of parasitoid emergence. Flight activity. Searching capacity. Adult longevity. Number of eggs parasitized. Synchrony with pest eggs. Experiments with T. achaeae showed more efficient movement among plants and host finding at moderate to warm temperatures such as 25–30°C than at lower temperatures such as 15°C. This does not mean that all strains share the same thermal range. The strain used should be tested under local conditions. Excessively high temperature may cause: Shorter adult lifespan. Desiccation. Reduced searching time. Reduced emergence quality from release cards.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Several release methods are used. Cards Containing Parasitized Eggs Cards contain eggs of a factitious host that have been parasitized and are close to parasitoid emergence. They should be: Distributed over a large number of points. Fixed within the canopy. Protected from direct sunlight. Placed outside irrigation and spray paths. Protected from ants and predation. Not concentrated in a few locations. Installed immediately after receipt or stored according to instructions. Release of Adults Adults may be released directly at distributed points, but this requires: Careful handling. Protection from heat. Release under calm weather. Minimizing time spent in the container. Ensuring distribution throughout the area. Cards are easier to transport and distribute, whereas adult release may provide faster action when handling quality is high.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Release should preferably be conducted: Early in the morning or near sunset. Away from the hottest and driest periods. Before application of incompatible products. At the beginning of egg presence. Repeatedly during oviposition waves. Timing should not depend only on the day of the week, but also on: Trap catches. Egg density. Temperature. Egg age. Previous treatment. Irrigation and spraying schedules.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Trichogramma usually requires repeated releases because: Oviposition continues over time. Adult lifespan is relatively short. New eggs appear daily. Some parasitoids disperse outside the treated area. Climatic conditions change. New moths immigrate from outside. Releases may be: Every 3–4 days in intensive experimental programs. Weekly in some operational programs. Adjusted according to egg density and weather. No fixed interval should be used in every field without egg inspection.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Apparent parasitism is calculated as: Parasitism (%) = Number of parasitized eggs ÷ Total number of eggs examined × 100 The following should be distinguished: Parasitized eggs. Healthy eggs. Hatched eggs. Eggs dead from other causes. Predated or missing eggs. Samples should preferably be held under controlled conditions until parasitoid emergence because dark coloration alone may not always be sufficient to confirm identity or successful emergence.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Limitations include: Short period available before egg hatching. Difficulty searching in dense canopy. Sensitivity to heat and desiccation. Sensitivity to pesticide residues. Dispersal outside the field. Reduced efficacy under extremely high egg density. Intraguild predation of parasitized eggs. Variation in quality among commercial batches. Egg parasitoids should not be used alone after larvae become widespread because they do not affect existing mines.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Larval parasitoids target the stage responsible for direct damage. Some species can: Locate larvae inside mines. Penetrate plant tissue with the ovipositor. Paralyze the larva. Lay eggs inside or on the larva. Kill larvae through parasitism or host feeding. Their effect may be greater than the measured parasitism percentage because some females kill larvae through host feeding without laying eggs.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Ectoparasitoids Ectoparasitoids The parasitoid develops externally on the larval host after paralyzing it or stopping its development. Examples include: Necremnus tutae. Bracon nigricans. Dineulophus phthorimaeae. Endoparasitoids Endoparasitoids The female lays eggs inside the larval host, and the parasitoid develops internally. Examples include: Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris. Pseudapanteles dignus. Idiobionts Idiobionts These paralyze the host or arrest its development at the time of parasitism. Koinobionts Koinobionts The host continues feeding and growing for a period after parasitism before the parasitoid eventually kills it. This difference affects the timing of damage reduction because an internally parasitized larva may continue feeding for some time before death.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Necremnus tutae is one of the most important ectoparasitoids of Tuta absoluta larvae in the Mediterranean region. In some older studies it was reported as: Necremnus near artynes. Necremnus nr. artynes. Subsequent taxonomic revisions showed that an important proportion of these records referred to N. tutae. The female searches for larvae inside mines, then: Penetrates the plant tissue with its ovipositor. Paralyzes the larva. Lays eggs near or on it. May feed on host fluids. It generally attacks larvae beyond the first instar, and the first larval instar is not its preferred target.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: A female N. tutae can kill some larvae by feeding on their fluids without producing offspring. This means that: Total mortality caused by the parasitoid may exceed the percentage of larvae from which adult parasitoids emerge. Evaluation should therefore record: Parasitized larvae. Paralyzed larvae. Larvae that died without parasitoid emergence. Larvae from which parasitoids emerged. Parasitoid emergence alone should not be used to estimate the full effect.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Natural parasitism varies among fields and seasons. Studies have recorded: Low or moderate averages in some fields. High levels reaching 75–80% under favorable local conditions. This depends on: Density of suitable larval hosts. Absence of toxic insecticides. Temperature. Availability of adult food. Canopy structure. Competition with other parasitoids and predators. The maximum percentage does not mean that this level is achieved consistently under commercial conditions.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Bracon nigricans is a gregarious ectoparasitoid of Tuta larvae and some other lepidopteran larvae. The female usually: Locates the larva inside the mine. Paralyzes it. Lays several eggs near or on its surface. Produces several parasitoid larvae on the same host. It has been recorded naturally in parts of the Mediterranean basin and Africa. Surveys in Kenyan fields recorded parasitism reaching approximately 21% at some locations, with clear variation among fields and environments. Commercial release requires: Stable mass production. Host-range assessment. Dose determination. Compatibility studies with predators. Evaluation of suitability for local temperatures.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris is a solitary larval endoparasitoid of Tuta absoluta originating from the Americas. It is characterized by the fact that it: Lays an egg inside the larva. Allows the host to continue developing temporarily. Later emerges to form a cocoon. Can track the host within the mine. It has become recorded or established in parts of the Mediterranean region and Africa after moving beyond its native range. It is considered promising, but its use requires attention to: Interaction with local parasitoids. Competition within the host. Climatic suitability. Formal regulation of movement and release. Possible changes in the local parasitoid community.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: The same larva may be attacked by more than one parasitoid. Competition may occur: Within the host among endoparasitoids. Between an endoparasitoid and an ectoparasitoid. When one parasitoid kills the host before another completes development. Through host feeding on an already parasitized larva. An ectoparasitoid that paralyzes the larva may outcompete an endoparasitoid that had previously laid an egg inside it because paralysis stops host growth and alters its resources. Combining several species does not therefore necessarily produce an additive effect. Outcomes may include: Complementarity. Neutrality. Competition. Displacement of one species. The combination should be tested before inclusion in a joint-release program.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Important species in different regions include: Pseudapanteles dignus. Dineulophus phthorimaeae. Neochrysocharis formosa. Stenomesius spp. Pnigalio spp. Agathis fuscipennis. Other species of Eulophidae, Braconidae, and Ichneumonidae. Not all records represent agents ready for biological-control use. Some species may be: Rare. Generalists. Poorly reproductive on Tuta. Unsuitable for mass production. Recorded from only a single incidental emergence. A distinction must be made between “a parasitoid recorded from the pest” and “a parasitoid suitable for a control program.”
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Predators kill several pest individuals during their lifetime. The principal predators used against Tuta attack: Eggs. Larvae during hatching. Newly hatched larvae in superficial mines. Larvae leaving mines. Generalist predators also contribute to the control of: Whiteflies. Thrips. Aphids. Mites. Other tomato pests. However, their generalist feeding can also lead them to consume other natural enemies or feed on the plant.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Predatory Bugs of the Family Miridae
text: Omnivorous mirid bugs are the most important group of predators studied against Tuta absoluta. Important species include: Nesidiocoris tenuis. Macrolophus pygmaeus. Dicyphus errans. Other Dicyphus species and local mirids. These insects are characterized by their ability to: Search on the plant surface. Consume eggs. Extract small larvae from mines. Establish relatively well on tomato. Feed on several pest species. Use plant tissues and water when prey is scarce.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Macrolophus pygmaeus is a generalist predator used in protected tomato programs and attacks: Tuta eggs. Newly hatched larvae. Whiteflies. Other small pests. Laboratory studies indicate that adults can consume large numbers of Tuta eggs. However, laboratory rates should not be transferred directly to the field because field predation is affected by searching difficulty, climate, and alternative prey. The predator tends to prefer: Eggs. Newly hatched larvae. Relatively exposed prey. Its ability to reach large larvae deep inside fruits or stems is limited.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Establishment is relatively slow. Release should therefore preferably occur: Early. In the nursery or immediately after transplanting. Before pest density rises. With supplementary food when prey is scarce. Greenhouse guidance programs have used rates of approximately: One individual per square meter at the beginning of the crop. Some trials have also used releases of approximately: 1–2 adults per square meter. Other programs have used higher repeated releases. These rates apply to the greenhouse products and conditions studied and are not fixed rates for open fields.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Tuta absoluta alone may not support rapid establishment of M. pygmaeus, particularly when predators are released preventively before pest appearance. Supplementary foods include: Eggs of Ephestia kuehniella. Treated Artemia cysts in some programs. Alternative prey or specialized commercial foods. Supplementary food helps: Increase survival. Accelerate oviposition. Increase nymph production. Retain predators within the crop. However, excessive or poorly distributed food may cause: Increased cost. Aggregation of predators at limited points. Temporary reduction in pest searching. Contamination of plant leaves. It should be used according to product instructions and monitoring results.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Nesidiocoris tenuis is one of the strongest predators used against Tuta absoluta in warm regions. It consumes: Eggs. Newly hatched larvae. Whiteflies. Other small pests. Its capacity to reproduce on tomato and tolerate warm conditions supports establishment in Mediterranean and other warm environments. It may reduce Tuta eggs more rapidly than some other mirids, but it exhibits more pronounced plant-feeding behavior.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: N. tenuis is a zoophytophagous predator. When its density is high or prey is scarce, it may cause: Necrotic rings around stems or petioles. Damage to growing points. Flower drop. Poor fruit set. Punctures or deformation of fruits. Reduced growth. Damage does not occur to the same degree under all conditions. It is influenced by: Predator density. Prey density. Cultivar. Temperature. Plant condition. Supplementary food. Crop stage. High N. tenuis abundance should not always be regarded as a positive result. Pest suppression must be balanced against potential plant injury.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Management of Risks Associated with Nesidiocoris tenuis
text: Management includes: Early release at a calculated rate. Monitoring predators per plant. Monitoring prey density. Providing supplementary food when required. Monitoring necrotic rings. Preventing predator numbers from increasing without supervision. Avoiding sudden removal of most prey with a broad-spectrum insecticide. Selecting a selective material if predator reduction becomes necessary. Avoiding repeated supplementary releases without data. A distinction should be made among: Presence of the predator. Establishment at a beneficial level. Increase to a damaging density.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Programs have been developed for releasing N. tenuis onto transplants before transfer to the greenhouse, providing time for predator reproduction before pest arrival. This system requires: Isolation of transplants. Supplementary food. Suitable temperatures. Avoidance of insecticides toxic to the predator. Uniform distribution. Inspection of transplants for excessive plant damage. The approach is more controllable in greenhouses than in open fields.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Dicyphus errans is a generalist omnivorous predator that can consume Tuta absoluta eggs and larvae. Potential advantages include: Ability to develop on multiple plant species. Consumption of pest eggs. Potential use in some European and Mediterranean environments. Lower expression of plant damage than some other species under particular conditions. Its commercial and operational use is less widespread than that of M. pygmaeus and N. tenuis. Release programs require further calibration according to climate and crop.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Orius species attack a range of small prey, and some have been recorded feeding on Tuta absoluta eggs. Their principal importance in tomato generally relates to control of: Thrips. Insect eggs. Small nymphs. Orius species are not currently primary substitutes for mirids in Tuta programs, but they may contribute to egg suppression within the natural predator community.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Larvae of the green lacewing Chrysoperla carnea feed on: Eggs. Exposed small larvae. Aphids. Whiteflies. Other soft-bodied prey. They may attack Tuta eggs and larvae during emergence, but their ability to reach larvae established deep inside mines is limited. Their value is greater within: Preventive releases. Programs targeting several pests. Conservation of biodiversity. They are not sufficient alone when larvae are widespread inside fruits.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Predation on Tuta has been recorded by: Spiders. Ants. Ground beetles. Social wasps. Predators from several other families. The presence of a predator in the field does not prove that it has a major economic effect. Demonstrating importance requires: Confirmation of prey in gut contents or by direct observation. Measurement of predation rate. Estimation of predator density. Association with pest reduction. Confirmation that the predator does not cause other damage.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Some mirids can insert their piercing mouthparts through the leaf epidermis and consume larvae in superficial mines. Efficacy declines when: Larvae become larger. Mines become deeper. Larvae move into stems. Larvae enter fruits. Frass and plant tissues accumulate within the mine. Targeting eggs and newly hatched larvae therefore remains fundamental to successful predator use.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Functional response refers to the change in the number of prey consumed as prey density changes. Predation generally increases as more eggs become available, then reaches a plateau because of: Handling time. Satiation. Grooming. Resting. Searching constraints. Laboratory predation rates should not be multiplied directly by predator numbers to predict field suppression because laboratory conditions remove dispersal, searching difficulty, and climatic constraints.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Integration of Egg Parasitoids and Predators
text: Combining egg parasitoids and predators can provide broader stage coverage because: Trichogramma parasitizes eggs. Predators consume non-parasitized eggs. Predators attack newly hatched larvae. Larval parasitoids target surviving individuals. However, predators may also consume: Parasitized eggs. Adult parasitoids. Tuta larvae containing internal parasitoids. The net effect depends on: The predator’s ability to distinguish parasitized eggs. Egg age. Availability of alternative prey. Release timing. Distribution of organisms on the plant.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Intraguild Predation Intraguild predation occurs when one natural enemy kills another natural enemy while both attack the same pest. Potential examples include: A mirid consuming a parasitized egg. N. tenuis preying on M. pygmaeus nymphs when prey is scarce. An ectoparasitoid killing a larva containing an endoparasitoid. Generalist predators consuming adult parasitoids. The occurrence of intraguild predation does not mean that combining two species always fails. The overall effect on the pest may remain positive, particularly when prey is abundant and species occupy different plant locations.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: The two species differ in parasitism strategy: Necremnus tutae is an ectoparasitoid that arrests larval development. Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris is an endoparasitoid that allows the host to continue developing temporarily. If N. tutae attacks a larva already parasitized internally, paralysis or host feeding may kill the endoparasitoid. The following should therefore be studied: Order of arrival of the two species. Preferred larval instar. Ability of each species to discriminate. Effect of competition on emergence. Final impact on pest suppression. The two species should not be released together automatically before compatibility is evaluated.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Adding more than one predator may: Increase egg consumption. Expand searching zones. Target different stages. However, it may also cause: Competition. Predation between predators. Lower total prey consumption than expected. Increased plant feeding when prey distribution is disrupted. The combination should be evaluated on whole plants, not only in laboratory arenas.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Broad-spectrum pesticides are among the most important constraints on biological control. Natural enemies may be exposed through: Direct spray. Contact with residues on leaves. Feeding on contaminated prey. Consumption of contaminated nectar or honeydew. Sublethal effects on fecundity, searching, and flight. Describing an insecticide as having low acute toxicity is insufficient because it may still cause: Reduced oviposition. Poor hatching. Behavioral changes. Reduced predation. Reduced parasitoid emergence. Prolonged residual effects.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Selectivity Is Not an Absolute Property
text: Pesticide selectivity varies according to: Active ingredient. Concentration. Commercial formulation. Natural-enemy stage. Exposure method. Residue age. Temperature. Plant species. A pesticide may have low impact on a parasitoid developing inside an egg but be highly toxic to emerging adults. Compatibility decisions should therefore rely on data specific to: The active ingredient. The natural-enemy species. Application method. Required interval before release. Compatibility with pesticide groups is discussed in detail in the dedicated section.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Adult parasitoids benefit from carbohydrate sources such as: Nectar. Plant secretions. Honeydew. Suitable flowering plants may help: Increase parasitoid longevity. Increase parasitoid fecundity. Improve searching capacity. Support predators. However, a supportive plant should be: Not a confirmed host of Tuta. A source of nectar accessible to the parasitoid. Not strongly competitive with tomato. Not a carrier of major pests or diseases. Suitable for the climate and field operations. Flowering alone is insufficient. Flowers may be too deep for minute parasitoids to access the nectar.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Supportive plants may be established: Along field margins. In strips between sectors. In areas that do not obstruct mechanization. Near points from which natural enemies enter. Considerations include: Staggered flowering periods. Prevention of weediness. Avoidance of obstruction to spraying and harvesting. Appropriate irrigation. Avoidance of insecticides that kill natural enemies on these plants. Monitoring to ensure they do not become reservoirs for other pests. Dill, coriander, basil, and other plants should be tested locally before fixed proportions are adopted in commercial programs.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: The following should be removed: Volunteer tomato plants. Black nightshade and other confirmed infested hosts. Plants that sustain the Tuta life cycle. At the same time, some non-host plants supporting natural enemies may be retained when they do not interfere with the crop. Ecological management therefore differs from indiscriminate total sanitation.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: The objective should be specified as one or more of the following: Prevent egg hatching. Reduce larval density. Establish a predator early. Augment an existing natural enemy. Treat an infestation focus. Protect the fruiting stage. The objective determines: Species used. Release rate. Release timing. Number of repeat releases. Evaluation indicators.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: The following should be recorded: Number of moths in traps. Egg density. Egg age. Number of active mines. Larval instars. Naturally occurring predators and parasitoids. Pesticides previously used. Temperature. Crop stage. Immigration sources. A release should not be implemented solely according to the calendar without knowing field conditions.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: The batch should be examined using indicators including: For Egg Parasitoids Emergence percentage. Female ratio. Deformities. Flight ability. Activity. Production date. Percentage of unparasitized eggs. Presence of contaminants or hyperparasitoids. For Predators Number of living individuals. Stage distribution. Activity. Injury or deformity. Count accuracy. Condition of the container. Transport duration. Mortality or cannibalism within the package. A package should not be accepted solely on the basis of the number stated on the label.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Natural enemies are living products affected by: Temperature. Humidity. Vibration. Light. Storage duration. Poor ventilation. The following should be observed: Follow the specified storage temperature. Minimize storage duration. Do not leave packages in hot vehicles. Transport them in the correct orientation. Do not freeze unless specifically instructed. Release as soon as possible after arrival. Record receipt and release times.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Natural enemies should be distributed throughout the area, with attention to: High-risk edges. Infestation foci. Field interior. Different sectors. Different cultivars or transplanting dates. Concentrating cards or predators at one point causes: Poor coverage. Increased competition. Delayed spread across the field. Greater loss through dispersal. The number of release points should be determined independently of the total dose.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Open-field releases face additional factors including: Wind. Solar radiation. Low humidity. Dispersal. Immigration from neighboring fields. Lack of boundaries preventing natural-enemy escape. Open-field programs therefore require: More release points. Appropriate release frequency. Selection of heat-tolerant strains. Release during moderate conditions. Protection of release cards. Conservation of supportive plants. Area-wide coordination of insecticides. Assessment of establishment after release. Conservation of local natural enemies may sometimes be more sustainable than complete dependence on repeated releases.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Greenhouses provide opportunities to: Retain natural enemies. Control entry and exit. Provide supplementary food. Track predator populations. Reduce immigration. However, greenhouses may face: High temperatures. Unsuitable humidity. Frequent leaf removal. Pesticides targeting other pests. Absence of prey at the beginning. Predator programs should begin early before Tuta density rises.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Relationship Between Pest Density and Release Rate
text: A higher pest density does not mean that simply doubling the natural-enemy release rate will always provide control. During a severe outbreak: Egg production may exceed parasitoid capacity. Larvae may enter tissues before being found. Predators need time to reproduce. Fruit infestation may increase before release effects appear. Biological control is most effective when used: Preventively. At the beginning of infestation. Within a program that prevents high pest density. A heavily infested field may require a selective corrective intervention while natural enemies are protected.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Absence of a Universal Release Rate
text: There is no single rate suitable for all: Trichogramma species. Predators. Greenhouses. Open fields. Temperatures. Infestation levels. The rate varies according to: Product unit. Actual number of emerging individuals. Female ratio. Plant density. Leaf area. Egg density. Dispersal capacity. Release frequency. Rates should be expressed in a clear unit such as: Adults/m². Individuals/plant. Individuals/hectare. Parasitized eggs/card. Cards/hectare. “Cards per feddan” is insufficient without knowing the number of individuals emerging from each card.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Scientific Example of an Egg-Parasitoid Program
text: The program consists of the following steps: 1. Install traps before transplanting. 2. Inspect eggs twice weekly when risk increases. 3. Begin releases when the first eggs appear, not after mines become widespread. 4. Determine the actual number of individuals in each card. 5. Distribute cards throughout the field. 6. Protect cards from sunlight and irrigation. 7. Repeat releases according to egg appearance. 8. Avoid incompatible pesticides. 9. Collect egg samples after release. 10. Calculate parasitism and parasitoid emergence. 11. Monitor mines and fruit infestation. 12. Modify release rate and frequency according to results.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Scientific Example of a Predator Program
text: 1. Select a species suited to the climate. 2. Release early before pest density rises. 3. Define the rate in individuals/m². 4. Distribute individuals among multiple points. 5. Provide supplementary food when required. 6. Avoid using a lethal pesticide after release. 7. Count adults and nymphs weekly. 8. Record eggs and larvae. 9. Monitor plant injury caused by omnivorous predators. 10. Stop supplementary releases if predator density becomes excessive. 11. Evaluate fruit infestation. 12. Compare program cost with the number of sprays avoided.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Egg parasitism (%) = Number of parasitized eggs ÷ Total number of eggs examined × 100 The following should be specified: Were the eggs naturally laid or deployed as sentinel eggs? Were hatched eggs included? Was parasitoid emergence confirmed? Were samples collected from both field edges and interior? Sentinel eggs placed on cards may overestimate or underestimate parasitism of natural eggs depending on their position and accessibility.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Larval parasitism (%) = Number of larvae from which a parasitoid emerged or on which parasitism was confirmed ÷ Total number of larvae collected × 100 Larvae should preferably be maintained in the laboratory until: Parasitoid emergence. Tuta pupation. Death of the sample. Mortality without parasitoid emergence should be recorded separately because it may result from: Host feeding by a parasitoid. Disease. Injury during collection. Insecticide exposure. Unknown causes.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Predation may be estimated using: Number of eggs lost from protected and exposed samples. Direct observation. Gut-content analysis. Predator counts linked to reductions in egg density. Sentinel eggs. Not every missing egg should be attributed to predation because eggs may fall, hatch, or be damaged by environmental conditions.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Indicators include: Adults per plant. Nymphs per plant. Presence of predator eggs. Distribution among sectors. Percentage of plants occupied. Persistence without further release. Prey density. The presence of nymphs is stronger evidence of establishment than the presence of released adults alone.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: These include: Reduced egg density. Reduced active mines. Reduced live larvae. Reduced growing-point infestation. Reduced percentage of infested fruits. Fewer insecticide sprays. Increased marketable yield. Reduced residues. Persistence of natural enemies. Absence of plant damage caused by predators. Benefit-to-cost ratio. A high parasitism percentage alone is insufficient if fruit infestation continues to increase.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Program costs include: Purchase of natural enemies. Refrigerated or specialized transport. Labor. Supplementary food. Inspection. Cards or release devices. Losses from any plant injury. Complementary interventions. Benefits include: Sprays avoided. Reduced fruit infestation. Increased acceptable yield. Lower residues. Delayed resistance. Protection of natural enemies acting against other pests. Calculate: Net return = Value of increase in acceptable yield + Value of sprays avoided − Cost of the biological-control program
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Error One: Releasing Natural Enemies After Infestation Reaches the Fruits Most biological-control agents need to act before outbreak development. Error Two: Selecting a Species by Name Without Evaluating the Strain Strains differ in efficacy and tolerance. Error Three: Counting Cards Without Knowing the Number of Emerging Individuals Cards may differ greatly in content. Error Four: Placing All Cards at One Location This prevents uniform distribution. Error Five: Exposing Trichogramma Cards to Direct Sunlight This affects parasitoid emergence and survival. Error Six: Using an Incompatible Pesticide After Release This may eliminate the released batch before it performs its function. Error Seven: Treating Black Eggs Alone as Complete Evidence of Success Parasitoid emergence should be confirmed and mines monitored. Error Eight: Releasing a Predator Without Food or Prey It may fail to establish or increase plant feeding. Error Nine: Failing to Monitor Plant Injury Caused by Nesidiocoris tenuis The predator may become damaging at high density. Error Ten: Randomly Combining Predators and Parasitoids Intraguild predation or competition may occur. Error Eleven: Moving a Non-Native Natural Enemy Without Evaluation This may create environmental or regulatory risks. Error Twelve: Evaluating Success by the Number Released Establishment and effects on the pest and fruit are the important outcomes. Error Thirteen: Relying on Laboratory Results Alone Efficacy may decline on whole plants and under field conditions. Error Fourteen: Ignoring Neighboring Fields Immigration may exceed the capacity of natural enemies. Error Fifteen: Removing Many Leaves After Predator Release Predator nymphs and eggs may be removed with the leaves.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
