text: Scientific priorities are: 1. Survey parasitoids and predators naturally present in tomato-production areas. 2. Identify species morphologically and molecularly when necessary. 3. Protect local natural enemies from broad-spectrum insecticides. 4. Do not transfer European greenhouse rates directly to Egyptian open fields. 5. Evaluate local or available Trichogramma strains using Tuta eggs. 6. Begin egg-parasitoid releases when eggs appear, not after larvae become widespread. 7. Distribute release points along field edges and in the interior. 8. Select release times with moderate temperatures. 9. Monitor emergence and parasitism percentages. 10. Evaluate predator establishment in open fields. 11. Monitor plant feeding by N. tenuis. 12. Use supportive non-host plants in structured experimental programs. 13. Do not apply incompatible insecticides after release. 14. Use percentage of infested fruits as the final performance indicator. 15. Coordinate programs across production blocks rather than individual fields only. There is no single scientifically validated Egyptian release rate suitable for all planting seasons, cultivars, and regions. Local rates should be developed through controlled field trials documenting: Number of individuals. Release points. Climate. Pest density. Parasitism percentage. Reduction in fruit infestation. Economic return.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

sectionTitle: Comparative Table of Principal Natural Enemies
text: Natural Enemy Group Target Stage Principal Advantage Principal Limitation Trichogramma achaeae Egg parasitoid Eggs Prevents hatching Requires repeated releases T. pretiosum Egg parasitoid Eggs Extensive experience in South America Strains differ T. evanescens Egg parasitoid Eggs Potential for local adaptation Efficacy against Tuta must be demonstrated Necremnus tutae Larval ectoparasitoid Mainly L2–L3 Parasitism and host feeding Affected by pesticides and competition Bracon nigricans Gregarious ectoparasitoid Larvae Produces several individuals per host Limited release programs D. gelechiidivoris Endoparasitoid Larvae Tracks the host within the mine Ecological and regulatory considerations M. pygmaeus Omnivorous predator Eggs and L1 Relatively low plant damage Slow establishment N. tenuis Omnivorous predator Eggs and L1 High predation capacity May damage plants D. errans Omnivorous predator Eggs and larvae Promising agent Limited commercial application Orius spp. Generalist predator Eggs and small stages Contributes to the predator community Not specialized on Tuta C. carnea Generalist predator Eggs and exposed larvae Attacks multiple pests Limited access to deep mines
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Before Planting Survey natural enemies. Review pesticide history. Select supplier and strain. Prepare supportive plants. Train the monitoring team. Nursery Release predators early when the system is adopted. Isolate batches. Provide supplementary food. Avoid toxic pesticides. After Transplanting Monitor predator establishment. Install traps. Inspect eggs. Begin Trichogramma releases when eggs are present. Vegetative Growth Calculate parasitism. Count predators and nymphs. Monitor active mines. Adjust frequency according to infestation. Flowering and Fruit Set Protect clusters and fruits. Monitor L2–L4 larvae. Support larval parasitoids. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides. Fruit Filling Monitor fruit infestation. Use compatible complementary interventions. Do not rely only on increasing release rates during a severe outbreak. After Harvest Record final performance. Conserve natural enemies in the surrounding environment. Remove pest sources. Evaluate costs and returns. Modify the program for the following season.
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Biological control of Tuta absoluta relies on egg and larval parasitoids and predators targeting different stages of the pest life cycle. Trichogramma species are the principal egg parasitoids. T. achaeae is among the most studied species in Mediterranean greenhouses, although its efficacy varies among strains, temperatures, release rates, and release timing. Egg parasitoids provide the greatest value when released in synchrony with oviposition, before larvae enter leaves and fruits. Important larval parasitoids include: Necremnus tutae. Bracon nigricans. Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris. Pseudapanteles dignus. Dineulophus phthorimaeae. The efficacy of larval parasitoids should not be measured only by adult parasitoid emergence because some species cause additional mortality through host paralysis and host feeding. Predatory mirids, especially Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis, are among the most important predators used in tomato and attack eggs and newly hatched larvae. N. tenuis has a high predation capacity but may cause plant injury when predator density is high or prey is scarce and therefore requires careful monitoring. Biological control performs best when: It begins early. Pest density is low. Natural enemies are well distributed. They are protected from non-selective pesticides. Suitable climatic and nutritional conditions are available. Immigration sources are managed. Results are measured using fruit infestation and acceptable yield. There is no single universal release rate for any parasitoid or predator. Rates from experiments or foreign greenhouses should not be converted directly into recommendations for Egyptian open fields without local calibration. References
partTitle: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators

text: Microbial control relies on the use of microorganisms or their biological products to infect Tuta absoluta, disrupt its physiological functions, or kill it, while reducing dependence on conventional chemical insecticides. The principal groups of microbial control agents studied against the pest include: 1. Entomopathogenic bacteria, particularly Bacillus thuringiensis. 2. Entomopathogenic fungi, such as Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae. 3. Entomopathogenic nematodes, such as species of Steinernema and Heterorhabditis. 4. Insect viruses, particularly granuloviruses belonging to the family Baculoviridae. 5. Symbiotic bacteria associated with nematodes, which remain an area of research not yet fully translated into direct commercial use. These agents differ fundamentally in how they enter the insect: The larva must ingest B. thuringiensis bacteria or their toxins. Fungi penetrate the external cuticle after spores germinate on it. Nematodes enter larvae through natural openings and then release symbiotic bacteria into the body cavity. The larva must ingest viral occlusion bodies before infection begins. The same rules therefore cannot be applied to all of these organisms, and they cannot be evaluated solely according to speed of kill.

sectionTitle: Importance of Microbial Control in the Management of Tuta absoluta
text: The importance of microbial agents increases because of: The pest’s ability to develop resistance to several chemical insecticides. Larval presence inside mines, reducing exposure to some contact insecticides. The need for products compatible with egg parasitoids and predators. The lower residue concern associated with correctly used registered microbial products. The possibility of targeting several stages of the pest life cycle. Their role within mode-of-action rotation programs. Suitability of some products for use near harvest according to the registered label. Their ability to reduce selection pressure caused by excessive chemical-insecticide use. However, describing a product as “biological” does not automatically mean that it is: Fast acting. Effective at all temperatures. Compatible with all pesticides. Unaffected by sunlight. Capable of reaching large larvae inside fruits. Equally effective among all strains and formulations.

sectionTitle: General Conditions for Success of Microbial Control Agents
text: Efficacy depends on the interaction of five components. First: The Pathogen This includes: Species. Strain. Concentration. Viability of spores or infective units. Product purity. Production date and storage conditions. Second: Pest Stage In general: Young larvae are more susceptible to Bacillus thuringiensis and viruses. Eggs and exposed larvae may be susceptible to some fungi. Larvae inside mines may be reached by some nematodes when a suitable water film is present. Pupae in soil are potential targets for fungi and nematodes. Third: Environmental Conditions These include: Temperature. Humidity. Duration of leaf-surface wetness. Intensity of ultraviolet radiation. Wind. Rainfall or overhead irrigation. Soil moisture. Fourth: Application Method This includes: Application timing. Spray-water volume. Coverage. Droplet size. Access to lower leaf surfaces. Access to growing points and clusters. Soil treatment when pupae are targeted. Repeat applications. Fifth: The Surrounding Integrated System This includes: Presence of predators and parasitoids. Pesticides used before and after application. Infestation level. Continued moth immigration. Residue removal. Harvest timing.

Bacillus thuringiensis, abbreviated Bt, is a Gram-positive, spore-forming bacterium that produces protein crystals with toxic activity against specific insect groups during sporulation. Products intended for lepidopteran larvae use strains or subspecies such as: B. thuringiensis subsp. kurstaki. B. thuringiensis subsp. aizawai. Other strains or mixtures containing different insecticidal proteins. Commercial products generally contain: Bacterial spores. Protein crystals. Carrier materials. Spreaders or adjuvants that differ among formulations. Bt products are not equivalent merely because they carry the same species name. They differ in strain, toxin composition, concentration, formulation, and field persistence.

Bt acts primarily as a stomach poison rather than as a conventional contact insecticide. Its mode of action proceeds as follows: 1. The larva ingests protein crystals and spores while feeding. 2. The crystals dissolve in the alkaline environment of the midgut. 3. Digestive enzymes activate the protoxins. 4. Active toxins bind to specific receptors on midgut cells. 5. Pores form or severe membrane damage occurs. 6. The larva stops feeding. 7. Midgut tissue collapses and systemic physiological disruption develops. 8. The larva dies after a period that varies according to dose, instar, and strain. This mechanism explains the importance of the larva ingesting a sufficient dose before it penetrates deeply into plant tissue.

Bt strains produce different groups of proteins. Cry Proteins These generally occur in crystals formed during sporulation and act after ingestion and activation in the midgut. Vip Proteins These are insecticidal proteins produced during vegetative bacterial growth and differ partly in receptors and binding mechanisms from some Cry proteins. Laboratory studies have shown activity of proteins such as Vip3Aa16 against Tuta absoluta larvae, with clear pathological changes in the midgut. However, results from purified or experimentally produced proteins cannot automatically be converted into field recommendations unless a stable registered formulation exists.

sectionTitle: Most Suitable Stages for Bt Application
text: Bt is most suitable for targeting: Newly hatched larvae. First-instar larvae. Second-instar larvae before deep penetration into tissues. Larvae in superficial mines still feeding close to treated surfaces. Practical efficacy generally declines with: Advancing larval instar. Larval entry into fruits or stems. Cessation of feeding. Poor coverage. Degradation of residues on leaves. Bt does not provide sufficient direct activity against: Adult moths. Pupae. Eggs before hatching, except where newly hatched larvae ingest surrounding residues.

Bt timing is determined according to: Beginning of oviposition. Egg age. Expected hatching date. Appearance of the first small mines. Accumulated degree-days. Increased trap catches together with confirmation of eggs on plants. The objective is to establish an effective residue layer on tissues where larvae will begin feeding immediately after hatching. Application should not be delayed until most of the population consists of third- and fourth-instar larvae.

Because Bt is non-systemic in conventional applications, efficacy depends on deposition at larval feeding sites. Coverage should include: Terminal growth. Most recently formed leaves. Lower leaf surfaces. Petioles. Areas near clusters. Leaves touching fruits. Uneven coverage leaves untreated plant parts through which larvae can enter without ingesting an effective dose.

No single spray-water volume is suitable for all fields. Volume is determined according to: Plant age. Canopy density. Nozzle type. Sprayer speed. Pressure. Leaf area. Cropping system. The selected volume should achieve: Uniform coverage. Deposition on both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Minimal excessive runoff. Minimal loss to the soil. The sprayer should be calibrated using water before the product is added.

Bt proteins and spores are degraded by: Ultraviolet radiation. Heat on the leaf surface. Rainfall. Overhead irrigation. Growth of new untreated leaves. The protection period is therefore generally shorter than that of many long-residual chemical insecticides. Application is often preferable during periods of lower radiation, while considering rainfall or overhead irrigation forecasts and following product instructions regarding reapplication. There is no fixed reapplication interval for all products. It may range from: Several days. Approximately one week. Other intervals according to the registered label and pest pressure.

Product efficacy may be affected by: Highly alkaline water. High salinity. Chlorine or disinfectants. Prolonged retention of the mixture in the tank. Mixing with incompatible products. The following should be observed: Use clean water. Test pH and electrical conductivity when a problem is suspected. Prepare the mixture shortly before application. Maintain agitation. Follow the mixing order stated on the label. Conduct a small compatibility test when clear data are unavailable. Acids, alkalis, or adjuvants should not be added randomly to a microbial product.

Immediate larval knockdown and mortality should not always be expected. The first biological effect may be cessation of feeding. Efficacy is measured through: Reduction in new mines. Cessation of expansion of existing mines. Reduction in live larvae. Reduction in growing-point infestation. Reduced movement of larvae into fruits. Reduced fruit infestation in the following generation. Old mines should not be used as the measure of treatment outcome because they remain visible after larval death.

Resistance to Bt proteins can develop when the same strain or toxins are used intensively and repeatedly. Resistance management includes: Avoiding exclusive reliance on Bt throughout the season. Rotating tools with different modes of action. Integrating with parasitoids and predators. Targeting young instars at the registered dose. Avoiding ineffective low doses. Monitoring gradual declines in efficacy. Not assuming that changing the trade name changes the toxin group. The product’s strain and toxin profile should be verified, not only its trade name.

The principal fungi studied against Tuta absoluta include: Beauveria bassiana. Beauveria pseudobassiana. Metarhizium anisopliae. Other species and strains of Metarhizium. Lecanicillium muscarium. Cordyceps fumosorosea, formerly known as Isaria fumosorosea. Other fungi evaluated in laboratory studies with variable results. Efficacy does not depend on species name alone. Two strains of the same species may differ greatly in: Germination speed. Capacity to penetrate the cuticle. Heat tolerance. Sporulation. Virulence against the target stage. Capacity for endophytic colonization.

Fungi do not need to be ingested to initiate infection. The process generally proceeds as follows: 1. A spore adheres to the insect body surface. 2. It absorbs moisture and begins germinating. 3. A germ tube and penetration structure form. 4. Enzymes are secreted to degrade cuticular components. 5. The fungus penetrates the body wall. 6. It spreads through the haemocoel. 7. It consumes nutrients and may produce toxic metabolites. 8. The insect dies. 9. Under suitable humidity, the fungus grows externally on the cadaver. 10. New spores form and may contribute to additional infections. Time to death varies according to strain, concentration, temperature, and insect stage.

Some strains can infect: Eggs. Newly hatched larvae. Larvae in superficial mines. Pupae in soil. Adult moths. Susceptibility differs among stages. Larvae inside plant tissues are partly protected from direct spore contact, whereas the following may be targeted: Larvae during hatching. Larvae moving between mines. Larvae close to the mine surface. Pupae in treated soil. Adult moths contacting spore-covered plant surfaces.

Spores require suitable moisture for germination and penetration. This does not mean plants must remain saturated with water. It means that the following are important: Suitable relative humidity in the boundary layer near the leaf surface. A sufficient period for spore germination. Prevention of immediate drying of residues after application. Efficacy generally declines under: Severe dryness. Dry winds. Temperatures unsuitable for the strain. Strong direct sunlight. Application near evening is therefore more suitable in many environments, provided it is not followed by rainfall or irrigation that washes the product off.

Ultraviolet radiation is one of the principal factors degrading fungal spores on plant surfaces. It may cause: Reduced germination. Spore damage. Shorter residual activity. Differences in efficacy between exposed and shaded surfaces. Some oil-based formulations and carriers may improve: Adhesion. Desiccation tolerance. Spore distribution. Relative persistence. However, formulation effects should be demonstrated for the specific product and not generalized to all oil formulations.

Foliar application aims to place viable spores on: Young leaves. Oviposition sites. Mine entrances. Growing points. Areas where adult moths occur. Requirements include: Use of the registered dose. Maintenance of spore viability. Avoidance of water or tank mixes toxic to the fungus. Good coverage. Suitable environmental timing. Avoiding prolonged retention of the mixture in the tank. The fungus should not be expected to penetrate fruit tissue and reach a deeply concealed larva merely because it was sprayed on the fruit surface.

Experimental studies have demonstrated susceptibility of Tuta absoluta pupae to products containing: Beauveria bassiana. Metarhizium anisopliae. When applied to soil. Treatments have resulted in: Reduced adult emergence. Differences in efficacy among formulations and strains. Reduced fertility of some surviving adults. However, field efficacy is affected by: Proportion of larvae actually pupating in soil. Pupation depth. Soil moisture. Temperature. Organic matter. Fungicides present. Distribution of the treatment solution. Soil targeting should therefore be used as a supplementary component rather than a substitute for egg and larval control.

B. bassiana is among the most extensively studied fungi against the pest. Its efficacy has been evaluated through: Foliar spraying. Direct treatment of eggs and larvae. Soil treatment. Endophytic colonization. Aqueous and oil-based formulations. Results vary among strains and products. One strain may be highly virulent while another performs poorly under the same conditions. The name B. bassiana alone is insufficient for predicting outcome.

Strains of M. anisopliae have shown the ability to infect: Larvae. Adult moths. Pupae in soil. Field trials have also shown that some strains can reduce damage and increase marketable yield. Variation among strains is substantial. Results from one strain should not be used to evaluate all Metarhizium products. The following should be documented: Strain number. Concentration. Formulation. Application method. Environmental conditions. Number of applications.

Strains of L. muscarium and C. fumosorosea have been tested against eggs, larvae, and adult moths. Good efficacy has been achieved in laboratory or greenhouse studies under: High spore concentrations. Suitable humidity. Direct contact with the target stage. However, commercial field data are less extensive than those available for Beauveria and Metarhizium. Products should therefore be used according to: The registered strain. Local trial results. Label instructions. Compatibility with the pesticide program.

Endophytic Colonization Some entomopathogenic fungal strains can colonize tomato tissues internally after application by methods such as: Foliar spraying. Root dipping. Seed treatment. Soil drench around roots. In some studies, endophytic colonization has resulted in: Reduced larval survival. Slower development. Reduced fecundity or population growth. Changes in plant response to infestation. However, endophytic colonization is not a fixed property of every strain. It may differ among: Roots. Stems. Leaves. Application methods. Plant age. Time after application. Detection of the fungus within the plant does not necessarily mean that it will provide economically effective field control.

In a two-season field trial in Uganda, M. anisopliae strain ICIPE 20 achieved: Reduced leaf and fruit damage. More than a 22% increase in marketable yield compared with the untreated control. A benefit-to-cost ratio greater than one and a high final economic return. This demonstrates the potential for field success, but the result applies to: A specific strain. A specific formulation. A specific agroecological environment. A specific application program. It should not be converted into a general recommendation for all Metarhizium strains.

Finding a dead insect is insufficient to confirm that the fungus caused mortality. Verification includes: Collecting dead individuals. Placing them in a suitable moist chamber. Monitoring external fungal growth. Isolating or identifying the pathogen when necessary. Comparing mortality with an untreated control. Calculating corrected mortality. Field evaluation also includes: Reduction in live larvae. Reduction in new mines. Reduction in adult emergence. Reduction in fruit infestation.

Entomopathogenic nematodes are microscopic worms belonging mainly to the families: Steinernematidae. Heterorhabditidae. Species studied against Tuta absoluta include: Steinernema feltiae. Steinernema carpocapsae. Other species and strains of Steinernema. Heterorhabditis bacteriophora. Local strains of Heterorhabditis. The stage used is known as the: Infective juvenile This is the stage capable of searching for and entering the host.

Entomopathogenic nematodes function in symbiosis with bacteria: Xenorhabdus in Steinernema species. Photorhabdus in Heterorhabditis species. Infection generally proceeds as follows: 1. Infective juveniles reach the insect body. 2. They enter through the mouth, spiracles, or anus, while some Heterorhabditis species may penetrate thin regions of the cuticle. 3. They reach the body cavity. 4. They release their symbiotic bacteria. 5. The bacteria multiply and cause septicaemia and physiological collapse. 6. The insect usually dies within a relatively short period. 7. The nematodes reproduce inside the cadaver. 8. New generations of infective juveniles emerge when resources are depleted. The lethal effect depends on both the nematode and its associated bacteria.

Laboratory studies have shown that some nematode strains can: Move through a water film on the leaf surface. Enter through mine openings. Reach larvae inside plant tissue. Kill larvae within leaf mines. Field success is more difficult because of: Rapid drying of the leaf surface. Solar radiation. Heat. Absence of a water film. Mine depth. Larval position. Laboratory results are therefore generally higher than those expected in open fields.

Foliar application of nematodes requires: Relatively high moisture on the plant surface. Avoidance of the most intense sunlight and drying periods. A spray-water volume sufficient to create a temporary water film. Nozzles and filters that do not damage nematodes. Continuous gentle agitation of the suspension. Immediate application after preparation. Removal of fine filters that obstruct passage, according to product instructions. Application is generally conducted when moisture loss is reduced, such as during the evening, while strictly following the product label.

The following may be targeted: Pupae in soil. Larvae leaving the plant to pupate. Stages occurring near the soil surface. Efficacy depends on: Soil moisture. Temperature. Soil type. Nematode movement. Pupal depth. Strain used. Survival of nematodes after irrigation. The soil should be moist but not waterlogged, and moisture should persist long enough to permit nematode searching.

Entomopathogenic nematodes are relatively sensitive to desiccation and ultraviolet radiation. Rapid drying causes: Loss of movement. Death of infective juveniles. Reduced ability to enter hosts. Foliar applications in open fields are therefore more challenging than applications in greenhouses or moist soil. Moisture-retaining adjuvants may be used, but they should be: Registered or recommended. Non-toxic to nematodes. Safe for the plant. Non-obstructive to nematode movement.

Tests have shown that local strains may differ from commercial or foreign strains in: Speed of larval kill. Ability to reach mines. Heat tolerance. Desiccation tolerance. Movement activity. Compatibility with local soils. In a laboratory study in Rwanda, local strains killed Tuta absoluta larvae inside mines, and some local strains outperformed foreign strains during the initial evaluation stages. The researchers nevertheless emphasized the need for field trials before results are generalized.

sectionTitle: Egyptian Trial of Combined Biological Control
text: An Egyptian study in commercial greenhouses tested the combination of: The egg parasitoid Trichogramma euproctidis. The nematode Heterorhabditis bacteriophora HP88. The two agents were used alternately during the season. The program produced a gradual reduction in Tuta absoluta abundance compared with the untreated greenhouse. The study demonstrates the possibility of combining an egg-targeting agent with a larval-targeting agent, but it was conducted: In only three greenhouses. On specific cultivars. During one winter production cycle. Under protected conditions. It therefore provides preliminary operational evidence rather than a fixed dosage program for all Egyptian conditions.

The product should be inspected before use through: Percentage of living infective juveniles. Their movement. Absence of abnormal odor or contamination. Expiry date. Storage temperature. Confirmation that the package was not exposed to freezing or excessive heat. A small sample may be examined under suitable magnification to confirm that most individuals are: Active. Normal in shape. Not settled in dead aggregates.

Nematodes should not be mixed with: Water disinfectants. Pesticides or fertilizers of unknown compatibility. Highly saline solutions. Harmful concentrations of acids or alkalis. Materials that clog or kill them. Product-specific compatibility charts should be consulted because some pesticides may be compatible at specified concentrations while others are highly harmful.

The principal viruses studied against Tuta absoluta belong to the family: Baculoviridae Particularly the genus: Betabaculovirus Isolates studied include: Phthorimaea operculella granulovirus — PhopGV. Granuloviruses isolated from Tuta absoluta, referred to in some studies as TuabGV. These viruses are relatively highly specific to particular groups of Lepidoptera.

The larva must ingest viral occlusion bodies. The infection process proceeds as follows: 1. The larva consumes occlusion bodies with leaf tissue. 2. The protein matrix dissolves in the alkaline gut. 3. Viral particles are released. 4. They infect midgut cells. 5. Infection spreads to other tissues. 6. The virus replicates within cells. 7. Larval growth and feeding gradually stop. 8. The larva dies after a period depending on dose, instar, and isolate. The virus generally acts more slowly than chemical neurotoxic insecticides, but feeding may stop before complete death.

Young larvae are generally more susceptible to granuloviruses because: Their body weight is low. They require a relatively smaller dose. They ingest residues when feeding begins. They have not yet entered deep mines or fruits. Advanced larvae: Require a larger dose. Are more protected inside tissue. May continue causing damage before death. Are more difficult to reach with spray deposits. Successful use therefore depends on application before or at the beginning of hatching.

Studies have demonstrated substantial variation among PhopGV isolates in their capacity to infect Tuta absoluta. Some African isolates were more effective than others against young larvae. This confirms that: Virus name alone does not determine efficacy. Genetic isolate is important. The product should contain an isolate tested against Tuta. A dose from a product intended for another pest should not automatically be transferred to Tuta.

Occlusion bodies are affected by: Ultraviolet radiation. Rainfall. Growth of new leaves. Uneven coverage. Leaf removal. Programs may therefore require repeated applications during hatching waves according to the product label and monitoring data. UV protectants or improved formulations may increase persistence, but they should be part of a tested and registered product.

Granuloviruses are highly specific and can theoretically be integrated with egg and larval parasitoids. A study examining interactions between PhopGV and the larval parasitoids: Necremnus tutae. Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris. Found that indirect effects were generally limited, although some interactions varied according to parasitoid species and timing of infection. This indicates potential compatibility, but application sequence matters because early larval death may affect a parasitoid developing within or on the host.

Principal limitations include: Relatively slow action. Requirement for ingestion. Reduced efficacy against large larvae. UV degradation. Need for repeated applications. Variation in virulence among isolates. Limited availability of registered products in some countries. High cost in some programs. Viruses are therefore integrated with: Egg parasitoids. Parasitoids of advanced larvae. Predators. Traps. Agricultural sanitation.

Steinernema species are associated with Xenorhabdus bacteria, whereas Heterorhabditis species are associated with Photorhabdus bacteria. These bacteria produce: Toxins. Enzymes. Immunosuppressive compounds. Metabolites that help kill the host and preserve the cadaver for nematode development. Bacterial cells or cell-free filtrates have been tested against Tuta absoluta in laboratory studies. However, the use of bacterial extracts or bacteria separately from nematodes remains a research area and is not a ready field alternative unless a registered and tested product exists. Bacterial extracts should not be prepared or used locally without evaluation of toxicity, stability, and effects on non-target organisms.

sectionTitle: Compatibility Between Bt and Natural Enemies
text: Bt is relatively more selective than many broad-spectrum insecticides because it mainly targets larvae that ingest the active proteins. However, compatibility should not be assumed automatically. The following may affect natural enemies: Formulation. Adjuvants. Direct exposure. Contaminated food. Dose. Compatibility should be reviewed for: Trichogramma. Mirid predators. Larval parasitoids. Bees and pollinators where present.

Fungi can be integrated with parasitoids, but fungal spores may infect: Adult parasitoids upon contact. The parasitized host. Immature parasitoids inside the host indirectly. Predators moving over treated surfaces. Compatibility depends on: Strain. Dose. Time interval. Parasitoid stage. Exposure method. A time interval between application and release may be necessary.

Fungicides are among the principal constraints on entomopathogenic fungi. They may: Kill spores. Prevent germination. Slow mycelial growth. Reduce infection. Reduce endophytic colonization. Entomopathogenic fungi should not be tank-mixed with a fungicide unless the product label or compatibility tests confirm safety. Existing fungicide residues on leaves should also be considered, not only direct tank mixing.

Possible outcomes include: Complementarity when different pest stages are targeted. Neutral interaction. Antagonism when one formulation contains materials harmful to the other organism. They should not be mixed automatically in the same tank. The following should be determined: Timing. Application site. Water quality. Adjuvants. Strain compatibility. Separate application may be safer than direct mixing.

Some copper and sulfur products may affect the viability of entomopathogenic fungi or nematodes depending on concentration, formulation, and timing. The following should therefore be observed: Review product data. Avoid undocumented tank mixes. Leave an appropriate interval where required. Test viability after exposure in research programs. Recommendations should not be based on the general names “copper” or “sulfur” because formulations and rates differ.

Selection is guided by pest status: Pest Situation Initially Most Suitable Agent Eggs close to hatching Bt or virus covering the hatching site, together with egg parasitoids L1 and L2 larvae Bt, virus, or a registered fungus Larvae inside relatively moist mines Tested foliar nematodes or a suitable fungus Exposed moths and eggs A registered entomopathogenic fungus Pupae in soil A fungus or nematode registered for soil application Severe infestation inside fruits Microbial agents alone are insufficient Near harvest A registered product with a suitable preharvest interval Existing parasitoid program An agent demonstrated to be compatible with the parasitoid The table does not imply that every agent is registered or available in every country.

The following should be confirmed: Active organism or material. Strain. Concentration. Registration number. Expiry date. Storage conditions. Package integrity. Registered crop and pest. Preharvest interval. Mixing and application instructions. For fungi and nematodes, viability should preferably be checked when resources permit.

Storage requirements differ greatly among products. The temperature specified on the package should be followed because improper storage can cause: Reduced spore viability. Nematode mortality. Loss of viral activity. Degradation of Bt proteins. Shorter field persistence. Products should not be stored in: Vehicles exposed to sunlight. Hot warehouses. Locations adjacent to fertilizers or volatile disinfectants. Open or poorly sealed containers.

General steps include: 1. Clean the tank of residues from previous pesticides. 2. Use suitable water. 3. Partially fill the tank with water. 4. Add the product according to label instructions. 5. Maintain appropriate agitation. 6. Complete the required water volume. 7. Add only registered adjuvants. 8. Use the mixture within the specified period. 9. Do not leave it until the following day. Residues of disinfectants or fungicides in the tank may kill the living agent before it reaches the plant.

Application near evening is suitable in many cases because it may: Reduce direct exposure to ultraviolet radiation. Extend the period of surface moisture. Improve survival of fungi and nematodes. Coincide with activity of some pest stages. However, the following should be considered: Absence of strong winds. No forecast of heavy rainfall. Avoidance of humidity levels that sharply increase disease risk. Product instructions. No single time rule applies to all agents because Bt, fungi, and nematodes have different requirements.

The number of applications should not be fixed in advance without monitoring. It is affected by: Product persistence on leaves. Oviposition waves. Growth of new leaves. Infestation level. Weather. Post-application efficacy. Entry of new moths. Reapplication is justified when data show: New eggs or hatching. Declining effective residues. Continued appearance of new mines. Expiry of the product’s protection period. It should not be repeated automatically when no active infestation is present.

Evaluation timing depends on the speed of action of the agent. Bt Focus on: Cessation of feeding. Reduction in live larvae. Reduction in new mines. Fungi Allow sufficient time for infection and death and confirm fungal growth where necessary. Nematodes Assess nematode survival and larval infection during the following days. Viruses Expect a slower effect and assess larvae and the following generation. These agents should not all be compared after only 24 hours.

These include: Reduced numbers of live larvae. Reduced appearance of new mines. Reduced growing-point infestation. Reduced fruit infestation. Reduced adult emergence from soil. Persistence of natural enemies. Fewer chemical sprays. Absence of phytotoxicity. Reduced marketable losses. Positive economic return.

Abbott’s formula may be used in experiments that include an untreated control: Corrected efficacy (%) = [(Survival in the control − Survival in the treatment) ÷ Survival in the control] × 100 In commercial fields, it is preferable to use indicators including: Change before and after treatment. A comparison field or sector. Percentage of infested fruits. Marketable yield. Program cost. Laboratory mortality alone is insufficient to predict field return.

The cost of microbial control includes: Product cost. Storage and transport. Refrigeration where required. Number of applications. Labor. Adjuvants. Inspection. Spray equipment. Reapplication after rainfall or degradation. Benefits include: Reduction in chemical sprays. Protection of natural enemies. Lower residues. Increased sound yield. Delayed resistance. Improved marketing opportunities. Calculate: Benefit-to-cost ratio = Value of benefits achieved ÷ Program cost A treatment is economically viable when the value of benefits clearly exceeds the cost.

Error One: Treating All Bt Products as Equivalent Strains, proteins, and formulations differ. Error Two: Applying Bt After Most Larvae Have Entered Fruits Bt must be ingested and is more effective against young instars. Error Three: Evaluating Bt After Only a Few Hours Feeding may stop before mortality is complete. Error Four: Using a Dose Below the Registered Rate This may cause treatment failure and increase resistance risk. Error Five: Applying Fungi During the Most Intense Sunlight and Dryness This reduces spore viability and persistence. Error Six: Mixing a Fungus with a Fungicide This may prevent germination or kill spores. Error Seven: Generalizing the Result of One Strain to the Entire Species Strains differ substantially in virulence. Error Eight: Assuming Endophytic Colonization Is Guaranteed It depends on strain, application method, plant, and time. Error Nine: Applying Nematodes to a Dry Surface Infective juveniles require a moisture film for movement and survival. Error Ten: Using Nozzles or Filters That Damage Nematodes Application-equipment specifications should be reviewed. Error Eleven: Exposing Nematodes or Spores to Heat During Transport This reduces viability before application. Error Twelve: Applying Viruses Only Against Large Larvae Young instars are more susceptible and easier to reach. Error Thirteen: Measuring Success Only by Numbers of Dead Insects The final indicator is reduced fruit infestation and rejected yield. Error Fourteen: Leaving a Biological Mixture in the Tank Until the Following Day The agent’s viability may decline. Error Fifteen: Using an Unregistered Product Research results alone do not demonstrate the safety and efficacy of a commercial product.

sectionTitle: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
text: Scientific priorities are: 1. Use only products registered for the crop and pest. 2. Identify the strain and concentration, not only the organism name. 3. Target eggs close to hatching and young larvae. 4. Link application to trap catches and field inspection. 5. Improve coverage of growing points and lower leaf surfaces. 6. Apply fungi and nematodes when desiccation and radiation are reduced. 7. Assess spray-water quality. 8. Do not mix fungi with fungicides without compatibility data. 9. Prevent exposure of living products to excessive heat during transport and storage. 10. Evaluate Egyptian or locally adapted fungal and nematode strains. 11. Study soil application of fungi or nematodes against pupae within integrated programs. 12. Build on the Egyptian trial combining T. euproctidis with H. bacteriophora through broader repeated trials. 13. Do not transfer greenhouse results directly to open fields. 14. Evaluate fruit infestation and marketable yield. 15. Document cost, return, and the number of chemical sprays avoided.

Agent Route of Entry Best Target Stage Principal Requirement for Success Principal Limitation B. thuringiensis kurstaki Ingestion L1–L2 Coverage and hatching-time application Relatively rapid degradation B. thuringiensis aizawai Ingestion L1–L2 Suitable dose and strain Product variability B. bassiana Cuticular penetration Eggs, larvae, adults, and pupae Humidity and viable spores Radiation and desiccation M. anisopliae Cuticular penetration Larvae, adults, and pupae Suitable strain and formulation Variable virulence L. muscarium Cuticular penetration Eggs and larvae Suitable humidity Limited field data C. fumosorosea Cuticular penetration Exposed stages Coverage and suitable climate Variable results S. feltiae Entry by infective juveniles Larvae inside mines Surface moisture Desiccation and radiation S. carpocapsae Entry by infective juveniles Larvae and pupae Direct access and moisture Limited foliar persistence H. bacteriophora Host entry or penetration Larvae and pupae Moist soil or surface Environmental sensitivity PhopGV Ingestion Young larvae Hatching-time application and coverage Slow action and UV degradation

Before Planting Review registered products. Test spray water. Clean application equipment. Prepare a compatibility program. Verify storage conditions. At First Moth and Egg Detection Determine expected hatching time. Prepare the registered Bt or virus product. Protect egg parasitoids. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides. At Appearance of L1 and L2 Apply Bt or the appropriate microbial agent. Achieve complete coverage. Reinspect. Monitor new mines. When Conditions Are Suitable for Fungi Select a registered strain. Apply when radiation and desiccation are reduced. Do not mix with an incompatible fungicide. Monitor infection and mortality. When Larvae Occur Inside Mines Evaluate registered or tested nematodes. Provide a suitable moisture film. Avoid heat and radiation. Evaluate the result under field conditions. When Soil Pupation Is High Evaluate soil treatment with a registered fungus or nematode. Maintain suitable moisture. Integrate treatment with residue removal. Monitor adult emergence. Near Harvest Review the preharvest interval. Use registered products only. Do not apply a slow-acting treatment that cannot protect a crop approaching imminent harvest. Focus on preventing infestation of the remaining fruits.

Microbial control of Tuta absoluta includes entomopathogenic bacteria, fungi, nematodes, and viruses. Bacillus thuringiensis is the principal bacterial agent used. It acts after larvae ingest toxic proteins and therefore provides the best results when young larvae are targeted before they penetrate deeply into leaves and fruits. Bt products are not equivalent. Subspecies, strains, proteins, and formulations differ, and efficacy is affected by coverage, radiation, rainfall, and larval age. Entomopathogenic fungi infect insects by penetrating the cuticle. Important species include: Beauveria bassiana. Metarhizium anisopliae. Lecanicillium muscarium. Cordyceps fumosorosea. Fungi require viable spores and suitable humidity and temperature and are affected by ultraviolet radiation and fungicides. Experimental evidence has demonstrated the possibility of targeting Tuta pupae in soil using B. bassiana and M. anisopliae. In addition, M. anisopliae strain ICIPE 20 produced positive economic results in a two-season field trial. Some entomopathogenic nematode strains can reach larvae inside mines when moisture is available and act through symbiotic bacteria belonging to the genera Xenorhabdus and Photorhabdus. Foliar nematode applications require protection from desiccation and radiation, and laboratory results should not be generalized without field verification. Granuloviruses are highly specific agents and perform best when ingested by young larvae, but they act more slowly and may require suitable coverage, timing, and repeated application in some programs. The best microbial-control results are achieved when: Programs begin early. The susceptible pest stage is targeted. A registered strain and product are used. Products are stored correctly. Environmental conditions are considered. Suitable coverage is achieved. Products are integrated with parasitoids, predators, pheromones, and agricultural sanitation. Performance is evaluated using reductions in fruit infestation and economic return. References

Insecticides remain one of the tools used to manage Tuta absoluta, but they are not the sole foundation of the program and do not provide sustainable management when used alone. The pest possesses several characteristics that make continuous reliance on insecticides vulnerable to failure, most importantly: A short life cycle under suitable temperatures. High reproductive capacity. Overlapping generations within the field. Larvae concealed inside mines in leaves, stems, and fruits. Continued moth immigration from neighboring fields. Exposure of several successive generations to insecticides within the same season. Documented resistance to several mode-of-action groups. Difficulty reaching advanced larvae inside fruits. Chemical control should therefore be used as a targeted intervention within an integrated pest-management program, after preventive, monitoring, cultural, biological, and behavioural measures have been implemented. Success of chemical control is not measured by the number of sprays or the rapid knockdown of some larvae, but by its ability to: Reduce new mines. Reduce live larvae. Prevent infestation of growing points and clusters. Prevent larvae from reaching fruits. Protect marketable or processable yield. Conserve natural enemies. Avoid exceeding maximum residue limits. Delay resistance development.

Chemical intervention becomes justified when monitoring data indicate that the pest population is approaching a level that available preventive and biological measures cannot contain in time. The decision depends on a group of indicators, including: Consecutive increases in trap catches. Presence of newly laid eggs or eggs close to hatching. Increase in active mines. Presence of live larvae in more than one sector. Appearance of infestation in growing points or buds. Presence of advanced larvae near clusters. Beginning of fruit infestation. Decline in natural-enemy activity. An immigration wave from an old field. The pest approaching the local intervention threshold. Chemical intervention is not justified solely by: Presence of old empty mines. Capture of a single moth. A fixed calendar schedule. Fear of possible infestation without evidence. A commercial recommendation unsupported by field inspection. Repeating the previous season’s program without evaluating current conditions.

sectionTitle: Scientific Questions Before Selecting an Insecticide
text: Before choosing any active ingredient, the following questions should be answered: 1. What is the dominant stage: eggs, young larvae, or advanced larvae? 2. Are larvae still in leaves or have they entered growing points and fruits? 3. Is infestation localized or distributed? 4. Which mode-of-action group was previously used? 5. Is there evidence of reduced susceptibility to that group? 6. Which natural enemies are present in the field? 7. How much time remains until harvest? 8. What is the registered preharvest interval? 9. What is the maximum number of seasonal applications? 10. Is the active ingredient currently registered on tomato and against the pest in the country? 11. Is it compatible with supply-market, processing-factory, or export requirements? 12. Is there a less harmful method capable of achieving the same result? An insecticide choice is not correct if it is effective against the pest but unregistered, incompatible with harvest timing, or highly disruptive to the biological-control program.

Newly hatched larvae and the first and second instars are the best stages for chemical control in most programs. These larvae are: Small. Usually not yet inside fruits. Present in limited superficial mines. Actively feeding. Relatively susceptible at lower doses. More sensitive to many insect-growth regulators and selective insecticides. Third- and fourth-instar larvae are more difficult to control because of: Larger body size. Expanded mines. Movement among plant organs. Entry into growing points, stems, and fruits. Reduced exposure to insecticide residues. Greater damage before the active ingredient takes effect. The program should therefore aim to prevent the larval population from reaching advanced instars, rather than waiting for large larvae to appear and then attempting to kill them.

sectionTitle: Spray Timing in Relation to Eggs and Hatching
text: The optimum timing depends on the mode of action. Active Ingredients Acting Through Feeding These are applied before hatching or at its onset so that residues are present on tissues when larvae begin feeding. Active Ingredients Affecting Growth and Molting These are more effective when young instars are exposed before approaching molt or entering deep tissues. Active Ingredients with Contact or Translaminar Activity These require coverage of egg sites and recent mines, and good coverage does not compensate for very late application. Hatching time is determined using: Egg age. Egg color. Empty eggshells. Temperature. Accumulated degree-days. Date of increased trap catches. Frequent inspection of young growth.

sectionTitle: Why Is Targeting Adult Moths Alone Insufficient?
text: Some insecticides can kill adult moths, but reliance on this effect alone is insufficient because: Some females may already have mated and laid eggs. New moths may enter after spraying. Eggs, larvae, and pupae are present simultaneously. Moths may remain concealed within the canopy. Their activity period may differ from application timing. Broad-spectrum adulticides may harm natural enemies. The decision should therefore focus mainly on preventing hatching and reducing young larvae, while adult control remains a supplementary element in specific situations.

An insecticide with proven efficacy may fail because of poor application rather than weakness of the active ingredient. Important factors include: Sprayer type. Nozzle calibration. Operating pressure. Tractor or operator speed. Spray-water volume. Droplet size. Canopy density. Nozzle direction. Boom height. Wind and temperature. Distribution of spray between field edges and the plant interior. Tuta absoluta management requires coverage of: The growing point. Most recently fully expanded leaves. Lower leaf surfaces. Petioles. Areas containing recent mines. Leaves close to clusters. The calyx–fruit junction. Internal canopy parts. Increasing spray pressure does not automatically improve control and may cause: Greater drift. Formation of very fine droplets lost outside the field. Spray rebound. Deposition only on external surfaces. Poor penetration into the canopy. Increased exposure of workers and neighboring areas.

The sprayer should be calibrated before insecticide use to determine: Nozzle flow rate. Uniformity among nozzles. Spray-water volume per unit area. Travel speed. Spray width. Operating pressure. Appropriate droplet size. Calibration includes: 1. Inspecting the tank and hoses. 2. Cleaning filters. 3. Confirming nozzle uniformity. 4. Replacing worn nozzles. 5. Measuring actual discharge. 6. Adjusting travel speed. 7. Testing coverage with water. 8. Using water-sensitive paper when available. 9. Reviewing deposition on lower leaf surfaces and growing points. 10. Recalibrating when plant size or nozzle type changes. A sprayer calibrated at the beginning of the season should not be assumed to remain suitable after canopy volume has doubled.

Spray-water volume should be determined according to plant size, production system, and equipment rather than a fixed number throughout the season. It should achieve: Adequate coverage. Canopy penetration. Minimal excessive runoff. Minimal loss of most spray to the soil. Uniform deposition on target sites. Excessively low volume leaves untreated areas, whereas excessive volume causes: Runoff. Soil contamination. Increased cost. Potentially elevated residues in some sites. Loss of active ingredient before fixation on leaves.

Some active ingredients or formulations may be affected by water characteristics, including: pH. Bicarbonates. Hardness. Salinity. Suspended materials. Chlorine and disinfectants. Water temperature. The product label should be reviewed before adjusting water. Acids or acidifying agents should not be added randomly because: Some active ingredients are more stable within a specific pH range. Excessive adjustment may cause degradation or phytotoxicity. Some mixtures react with fertilizers or copper. Tank pH alone does not represent overall solution quality. When several components are used, a small compatibility test should be conducted before preparing the full tank.

Adjuvants include: Spreaders. Wetting agents. Stickers. Oils. Antifoaming agents. pH modifiers. They may improve coverage or persistence for some products, but may also cause: Excessive penetration leading to phytotoxicity. Changes in residues. Incompatibility with natural enemies. Damage to spores in microbial products. Increased spray drift. Unexpected tank reactions. An adjuvant should therefore be used only when recommended on the product label or when compatibility has been demonstrated in a documented test.

Mode of action is the principal physiological site or pathway affected by an active ingredient within the insect. The Insecticide Resistance Action Committee, IRAC, classifies insecticides into numbered groups. Resistance-management programs are built around the mode-of-action group number, not: Trade name. Manufacturer. Package color. Minor differences in chemical name. Formulation differences alone. Two products may have different trade names but belong to the same group, and switching between them does not constitute true rotation.

sectionTitle: Mode-of-Action Groups Used or Studied Against Lepidoptera
text: The following table presents groups of scientific importance. Mention of an active ingredient does not imply that it is currently registered against Tuta absoluta or on tomato in Egypt. IRAC Group Target Site or Mode of Action Examples of Active Ingredients Principal Notes 1A Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors — carbamates Methomyl Relatively broad spectrum; resistance common in pests 1B Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors — organophosphates Multiple active ingredients Broad effects on natural enemies; registrations vary greatly 3A Sodium-channel modulators — pyrethroids Lambda-cyhalothrin, Deltamethrin High resistance risk and strong effects on beneficial organisms 4A Nicotinic acetylcholine-receptor competitive modulators Imidacloprid, Acetamiprid, and others Used mainly against sucking pests in tomato; not automatically primary Tuta options 5 Nicotinic acetylcholine-receptor allosteric modulators — spinosyns Spinosad, Spinetoram Same group despite different active ingredients 6 Glutamate-gated chloride-channel allosteric modulators Abamectin, Emamectin benzoate Same group; not independent rotation partners 11A Microbial disruptors of insect midgut membranes Bacillus thuringiensis Must be ingested and targets young instars 13 Uncouplers of oxidative phosphorylation Chlorfenapyr Respiratory activity; may strongly affect non-target organisms 15 Inhibitors of chitin biosynthesis Lufenuron, Chlorfluazuron, Novaluron, and others More suitable for young instars and slower acting 18 Ecdysone-receptor agonists Methoxyfenozide, Tebufenozide Disrupt molting and target larvae 22A Voltage-dependent sodium-channel blockers — oxadiazines Indoxacarb Requires careful resistance management 22B Voltage-dependent sodium-channel blockers — semicarbazones Metaflumizone Different subgroup but principal group number remains 22 28 Ryanodine-receptor modulators — diamides Chlorantraniliprole, Cyantraniliprole, Flubendiamide, Tetraniliprole All belong to group 28 and do not represent true rotation among themselves 30 GABA-gated chloride-channel allosteric modulators Broflanilide and others Newer active ingredients; use only when registered and locally effective 31 Baculoviruses Granuloviruses and NPVs Biological group with host-specific activity

This group includes: Spinosad. Spinetoram. These active ingredients act as allosteric modulators of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at a specific binding site. Registered products used against larvae generally act through: Ingestion. Contact to varying degrees. Feeding cessation. Neurotoxic activity. Important considerations include: Spinosad and Spinetoram belong to the same group. Rotating between them does not constitute a change in mode of action. Repeated group use against successive generations increases selection pressure. Some parasitoids and predators may be affected by direct exposure or residues.

This group includes: Emamectin benzoate. Abamectin. Other active ingredients within the same group. They act by modulating glutamate-gated chloride channels, causing disruption of nerve transmission and paralysis. Rotation between Emamectin benzoate and Abamectin should not be considered rotation between different groups because both carry IRAC group number 6. Efficacy is affected by: Larval age. Coverage. Light exposure. Susceptibility of the local population. Previous history of group use.

Bt is classified by IRAC as a microbial disruptor of insect midgut membranes. Its value within chemical and microbial programs includes: Targeting young larvae. A mode of action different from neurotoxic and muscle-active insecticides. Potential compatibility with natural enemies when demonstrated. Reduced pressure on chemical groups. Bt products are not identical and may differ in: Subspecies. Strain. Cry and Vip proteins. Concentration. Formulation. Differences among some Bt products may permit more precise resistance management, but this requires product-specific data rather than reliance on the general name.

Chlorfenapyr acts by uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation and disrupting energy production. Its mode of action differs from that of common neurotoxic insecticides, but it is not an unrestricted solution. The following should be considered: Current registration. Effects on natural enemies. Preharvest interval. Maximum number of applications. Previous history of use. Susceptibility of the local population. It should not be repeatedly used as a rescue solution following failure of other groups because repetition increases selection pressure against it.

Benzoylurea insecticides include active ingredients such as: Lufenuron. Chlorfluazuron. Novaluron. Diflubenzuron. Others. They interfere with synthesis of chitin required for molting. They are more suitable: Against early larval instars. Before larvae reach fruits. When sufficient time is available for the effect to appear. They may not provide rapid mortality because larvae may be affected at the following molt. Different active ingredients within group 15 do not represent rotation in mode of action.

This group includes active ingredients such as: Methoxyfenozide. Tebufenozide. They mimic the molting-hormone signal and induce an abnormal molting process that the larva cannot complete. They act more specifically against larvae than adults and should be applied: To the appropriate larval instars. Before larvae enter deep tissues. With recognition that their effect is slower than that of rapidly acting neurotoxic insecticides. Their efficacy should not be judged after only a few hours.

Indoxacarb is an oxadiazine and acts as a voltage-dependent sodium-channel blocker. Exposed larvae show: Feeding cessation. Reduced movement. Paralysis. Subsequent death. It should be managed within a true rotation because repeated use of the same group or its use in successive mixtures may increase resistance pressure. Metaflumizone in subgroup 22B should not be regarded as a complete change in the principal mode-of-action group number when designing a conservative resistance-management program. Guidance concerning subgroups should be reviewed.

This group includes: Chlorantraniliprole. Cyantraniliprole. Flubendiamide. Cyclaniliprole. Tetraniliprole. They act on ryanodine receptors, causing uncontrolled calcium release within muscle cells, followed by: Feeding cessation. Reduced movement. Muscle paralysis. Larval death. All of these active ingredients belong to IRAC group 28. Switching from Chlorantraniliprole to Cyantraniliprole or Flubendiamide therefore does not constitute true rotation in mode of action. Resistance to diamides has been documented in some Tuta absoluta populations, requiring: Limitation of the number of applications. Avoidance of use against successive generations. Avoidance of reliance on the group in every spray window. Monitoring of field performance. Compliance with the registered dose. Integration with non-chemical methods.

These include particularly: Organophosphates — 1B. Carbamates — 1A. Pyrethroids — 3A. They have historically been used against Tuta absoluta, but repeated reliance on them is associated with: High resistance in several populations. Mortality of predators and parasitoids. Outbreaks of secondary pests. Reduced natural control. Increased risk of human exposure. Registration and residue restrictions in some markets. This does not mean that every active ingredient in these groups is ineffective in every region, but use should depend on: Current registration. Local susceptibility data. Actual need. Effects on the biological-control program. Market requirements. They should not be used merely because of low price or apparent rapid action.

Most conventional neonicotinoids belong to group 4A. They are used in tomato mainly against sucking pests such as: Whiteflies. Aphids. Some leafhoppers. Their presence in a crop-protection program does not necessarily mean that they are effective or registered tools against Tuta absoluta larvae. Repeated use may also affect: Predators. Parasitoids. Pollinators. The wider pest-resistance program. Their use should therefore be linked to the actual target pest and should not be included in a Tuta rotation unless registration and locally documented efficacy exist.

Some oils, soaps, and botanical extracts may affect exposed stages through: Coating eggs or insects. Disrupting external membranes. Repellent or antifeedant effects. Their efficacy against larvae inside mines and fruits is generally limited. They may also cause: Phytotoxicity. Leaf burn. Effects on predators and parasitoids. Incompatibility with other pesticides. Increased penetration of some active ingredients. A material should not be considered safe or effective merely because it is natural. It should be used according to registration, dose, and local testing.

A commercial product may contain: One active ingredient. Two active ingredients. The same active ingredient at a different concentration. The same active ingredient under several trade names. The pesticide record should therefore include: Trade name. Active ingredient. Concentration. Formulation. IRAC group number. Application rate. Treatment date. Treated sector. Changing the trade name provides no resistance-management benefit when the active ingredient or group remains the same.

Mode-of-Action Window Approach The window system divides the season into periods corresponding approximately to one pest generation. Within each window: An active ingredient or active ingredients from one mode-of-action group may be used according to the label. The following window then uses a different effective group. The fundamental rule is: Do not expose two successive pest generations to the same group. The traditional IRAC model for Tuta absoluta has used windows of approximately 30 days, based on the short generation time under favorable conditions. However, this period is not fixed in all regions because generation time changes with: Temperature. Planting season. Production system. Greenhouse or open-field conditions. Daily thermal fluctuation. Windows should therefore be adjusted using monitoring and local thermal data.

sectionTitle: Avoiding Repetition of the Group Across Generations
text: If group 28 is used in one window, it should not be repeated in the following window even if the active ingredient changes from: Chlorantraniliprole. To Cyantraniliprole. Or Flubendiamide. Similarly: Spinosad and Spinetoram are both group 5. Abamectin and Emamectin benzoate are both group 6. Lufenuron and Chlorfluazuron are both group 15. Rotation should therefore be constructed using group numbers such as: 28 ← 5 ← 6 ← 15 Rather than trade names. This is only an illustrative sequence of different groups and is not a prescriptive program or evidence that the active ingredients are registered or suitable for a particular field.

sectionTitle: Number of Applications Within a Window
text: Some product labels may permit more than one application within the same window. The number of applications is determined by: The maximum stated on the label. Interval between applications. Infestation severity. Residual period. Generation duration. Local susceptibility. Availability of effective alternatives. The maximum permitted number should not be used automatically. It is a limit that must not be exceeded, not a compulsory target. Every unnecessary application increases: Selection pressure. Cost. Exposure. Residue risk. Harm to natural enemies.

Mixtures are not an automatic solution to resistance. For a mixture to provide a potential resistance-management benefit, it should: Contain two genuinely different groups. Include two active ingredients each independently effective against the target stage. Expose the target stage to both active ingredients simultaneously. Have relatively similar persistence of both components. Not contain an active ingredient to which high resistance already exists. Not involve known cross-resistance. Be registered and physically compatible. A mixture provides no resistance-management benefit when: Both active ingredients belong to the same IRAC group. One active ingredient is ineffective against Tuta. One active ingredient is used below an effective dose. The same mixture is repeated throughout the season. The mixture is used to compensate for poor timing or coverage. A mixture remains one component of a rotation program and is not a substitute for rotation.

Random mixing of three or four active ingredients can cause: Increased phytotoxicity. Physical incompatibility. Precipitation. Changes in pH. Reduced efficacy of some components. Increased residues. Mortality of natural enemies. Difficulty identifying the cause of success or failure. Higher treatment cost. Greater worker exposure. Increasing the number of active ingredients does not guarantee increased efficacy. Every tank component should have a clear and demonstrated purpose.

Insecticide resistance is a heritable change in the susceptibility of a pest population that results in repeated failure of a product to achieve the expected level of control when used according to the label. Resistance is not defined by: Survival of one larva. Failure of a single treatment. Poor coverage. Late application. Rain washing off the spray. Use of the wrong dose. Poorly stored product. Immigration of new moths after spraying. Application-related causes of failure should be excluded before resistance is declared.

Tuta absoluta may develop several resistance mechanisms. Target-Site Alteration A heritable change occurs in the protein or receptor targeted by the insecticide, reducing binding of the active ingredient. Metabolic Resistance The insect’s capacity to detoxify the active ingredient increases through enzymes such as: Cytochrome P450 monooxygenases. Esterases. Glutathione S-transferases. Reduced Penetration Cuticular properties change, reducing movement of the active ingredient into the body. Behavioural Change The insect may reduce contact with treated areas or alter its response. A population may carry more than one mechanism, resulting in: High resistance. Cross-resistance. Different responses among active ingredients within one group or among different groups.

Cross-resistance occurs when one mechanism confers resistance to more than one active ingredient. It is more likely among active ingredients that: Belong to the same group. Target the same site. Are detoxified by the same metabolic enzymes. Metabolic cross-resistance may also occur between different groups despite different IRAC numbers. Numerical rotation among groups is therefore insufficient when local tests demonstrate cross-resistance.

When a treatment fails, the following should be reviewed: 1. Was the pest correctly identified? 2. Is the active ingredient registered against the stage present? 3. Were larvae young or already inside fruits? 4. Was the registered dose used? 5. Was the sprayer calibrated? 6. Did the spray reach lower leaf surfaces and growing points? 7. Did rainfall or overhead irrigation occur? 8. Were the water or tank mixture incompatible? 9. Was the product expired? 10. Had the group already been repeated? 11. Is moth immigration occurring from a neighboring field? 12. Are the mines new, or is visible damage old? 13. Were larvae evaluated after sufficient time for the active ingredient to act? Increasing the dose or shortening the interval automatically when resistance is suspected is not recommended because it may increase selection and risk without restoring efficacy.

IRAC developed a standardized method for testing susceptibility of Tuta absoluta larvae known as Method No. 022. It is based on: Collecting individuals from the field. Rearing them to the F1 generation where possible. Using uniform second-instar larvae approximately 4–5 mm long. Dipping tomato leaves in specified insecticide concentrations. Exposing larvae to treated leaves. Recording mortality after the appropriate assessment period. Comparing results with a susceptible baseline or other populations. The test determines whether reduced efficacy is associated with a genuine change in susceptibility rather than poor field application.

When resistance is suspected: 1. Stop repeating the suspected group. 2. Collect larval samples. 3. Review treatment records. 4. Inspect calibration and coverage. 5. Use a different group demonstrated to be locally effective. 6. Intensify biological and cultural measures. 7. Manage immigration sources. 8. Notify the technical manager or competent authority. 9. Conduct a susceptibility test where possible. 10. Avoid issuing a definitive resistance conclusion before verification. Doses above the registered rate should not be used in an attempt to overcome resistance.

Chemical treatment may kill: Egg parasitoids. Larval parasitoids. Predatory mirids. Lacewings. Orius species. Spiders. Pollinating insects. Impacts can be reduced through: Selecting a more selective active ingredient. Applying treatment only when the threshold is exceeded. Treating infestation foci instead of the entire field where possible. Avoiding spraying during natural-enemy releases. Respecting the required interval before reintroducing a natural enemy. Avoiding broad-spectrum mixtures. Using the minimum necessary number of applications. Monitoring predator recovery after treatment. An active ingredient should not be described as generally safe to natural enemies because susceptibility differs among parasitoids and predators and between adults and immature stages.

Pre-Harvest Interval — PHI The preharvest interval is the minimum time required between the last insecticide application and crop harvest. It is determined according to: Active ingredient. Formulation. Application rate. Number of applications. Crop. Residue-decline data. National registration. The PHI should not be transferred from: One crop to another. One country to another. One product to another containing the same active ingredient in a different formulation. A research study to a commercial program. The PHI stated on the label of the registered product should be followed, while recognizing that the supply market may impose requirements more stringent than local regulations.

Maximum Residue Limit — MRL The maximum residue limit is the highest legally permitted concentration of a pesticide residue in food, usually expressed in milligrams per kilogram. An MRL is not a safe dose for adding pesticide to food. It is a regulatory limit derived from: Good agricultural practice. Residue trials. Toxicological assessments. Estimates of consumer exposure. Limits may differ among: Egypt. The European Union. Codex Alimentarius. The importing country. Processing-factory or company specifications. The target market should therefore be identified before the spray program is developed.

Compliance with the registered PHI reduces the probability of exceeding the MRL, but does not guarantee compliance when misuse occurs. Residues may increase because of: Excessive dose. Too many applications. Shortened intervals. Application of an unregistered treatment. Unrecommended mixtures. Excessive direct spraying of fruits. Errors in harvest dates. Poor coordination between spray and harvest teams. Drift from a neighboring field. Residue management therefore depends on complete compliance with good agricultural practice, not the PHI alone.

Restricted-Entry Interval — REI This is the period that must pass before workers may enter a treated field without the specified personal protective equipment. It differs from the preharvest interval. The following should be recorded: Time spraying ended. Date and time when entry is permitted. Required protective clothing. Operations prohibited during the interval. The REI is particularly important during: Harvesting. Pruning. Tying plants. Manual inspection. Release of natural enemies.

As harvest approaches, priority shifts toward: Reviewing the PHI. Reviewing target-market residue limits. Using registered active ingredients only. Avoiding unnecessary long-residual insecticides. Increasing reliance on suitable biological and mechanical methods. Avoiding unjustified preventive sprays. Coordinating pest-management and harvest teams. Documenting the final treatment in each sector. An active ingredient should not be used merely because another product has a long PHI. The selected material must be effective against the stage present and acceptable under regulatory requirements.

Rational use includes protection of workers from exposure. Measures include: Reading the product label. Wearing required personal protective equipment. Avoiding eating or smoking during preparation. Using dedicated measuring tools. Never mixing the spray solution by hand. Avoiding spraying against the wind. Never cleaning nozzles by mouth. Providing clean washing water. Changing contaminated clothing. Disposing of containers through the approved system. Training workers in first aid. Pesticide containers should never be reused for storing water, food, or animal feed.

Drift occurs when spray droplets move beyond the target area. It increases with: Wind. Very fine droplets. Excessive boom height. Inappropriate pressure. Heat and dry conditions. Spraying near field boundaries. Drift may cause: Contamination of neighboring crops. Harm to natural enemies. Contamination of waterways. Exposure of workers and residents. Unintended residues. Loss of part of the applied dose. Buffer zones and label restrictions concerning water bodies, residences, and neighboring crops should be followed.

sectionTitle: Management of Containers and Remaining Spray Mixture
text: The spray volume should be calculated to match the treated area and avoid excess mixture. Remaining spray should not be discharged into: Drains. Canals. Wells. Roads. Livestock areas. Locations near drinking-water sources. Containers should be rinsed according to approved guidance and punctured or collected through the authorized disposal system to prevent reuse.

The season may be divided into thermal or time-based windows. A theoretical example: Window Pest Situation Mode-of-Action Group Note First Early hatching Group A Do not use A in the following window Second New generation Group B Genuinely different from A Third Third generation Group C Consider natural enemies Fourth Fruiting stage Group D Consider the PHI Fifth Near harvest Biological method or suitable group According to registration and need The letters do not represent specific active ingredients. The actual program depends on: Groups locally effective. Registration. Monitoring. Resistance history. Harvest timing. Biological-control programs.

sectionTitle: Chemical Program According to Infestation Stage
text: No Active Infestation No chemical intervention. Continue monitoring. Maintain traps. Protect natural enemies. Limited Egg Presence Estimate hatching time. Assess egg parasitoids. Prepare an intervention targeting hatching if required. Do not use a broad-spectrum product without justification. Presence of L1 and L2 Select a registered active ingredient effective against young instars. Achieve precise coverage. Record the IRAC group. Reassess. Presence of L3 and L4 Review whether larvae have entered fruits. Do not expect complete efficacy from an active ingredient unable to reach them. Integrate treatment with collection of infested fruits and foci. Evaluate why detection was delayed. Beginning of Fruit Infestation Intervene rapidly according to the threshold and registration. Protect uninfested fruits. Collect infested fruits. Manage the immigration source. Shorten the reinspection interval. Near Harvest Review PHI and MRL requirements. Do not exceed the maximum number of applications. Select tools compatible with delivery requirements. Conduct residue testing where necessary.

Date Sector Active Ingredient Trade Name Concentration IRAC Group Registered Rate Water Volume Target Stage PHI Evaluation Result The record should also include: Number of previous applications of the group. Temperature and wind. Application time. Sprayer condition. Mixed pesticides. Supervisor’s name. Expected harvest date. Natural enemies present.

These include: Reduction in new mines. Reduction in live larvae. Cessation of growing-point infestation. Reduction in infested fruits. No need to repeat treatment because of poor execution.
