Persistence of natural enemies. Compliance with the allowed number of applications. No residue-limit violations. Reduced control cost per acceptable metric ton. No gradual decline in susceptibility. A large number of dead larvae alone is insufficient if new infestation continues to appear.

Error One: Following a Calendar Spray Program This causes unnecessary applications and high resistance pressure. Error Two: Changing the Trade Name While Keeping the Same IRAC Group This does not constitute rotation. Error Three: Using One Group Against Successive Generations This accelerates resistance selection. Error Four: Spraying Larvae After They Have Entered Fruits Protection is limited and damage has already occurred. Error Five: Using a Dose Below the Label Rate This may fail to kill moderately susceptible individuals and increase selection pressure. Error Six: Exceeding the Dose This does not overcome resistance and increases residues and risk. Error Seven: Mixing Several Active Ingredients Without a Clear Purpose This increases cost and problems without guaranteeing efficacy. Error Eight: Treating Every Mixture as a Resistance-Management Program Both active ingredients should be effective and belong to different groups. Error Nine: Repeating the Same Mixture This exposes the pest repeatedly to the same two groups. Error Ten: Neglecting Sprayer Calibration Application failure may appear to be resistance. Error Eleven: Using High Pressure Without Evaluating Deposition This may increase drift instead of coverage. Error Twelve: Neglecting Lower Leaf Surfaces and Growing Points This leaves oviposition and hatching sites unprotected. Error Thirteen: Judging Treatment by Old Mines New mines and live larvae should be counted. Error Fourteen: Repeating the Spray Immediately When Rapid Mortality Is Not Observed Speed of action differs among groups. Error Fifteen: Ignoring Natural Enemies Their mortality may cause pest resurgence. Error Sixteen: Ignoring the PHI This exposes the crop to rejection and residue problems. Error Seventeen: Treating the MRL as an Optional Guideline It is a legal or commercial limit that must be respected. Error Eighteen: Declaring Resistance Without Testing Application errors should be excluded and susceptibility evaluated.

sectionTitle: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
text: Priorities are: 1. Do not implement any treatment without monitoring. 2. Review current Egyptian registration before and during the season. 3. Record the IRAC group number for every active ingredient. 4. Do not repeat the same group against successive generations. 5. Target young larvae. 6. Recalibrate the sprayer as plant size changes. 7. Improve coverage of growing points and lower leaf surfaces. 8. Do not treat different trade names as rotation. 9. Protect predators and egg parasitoids. 10. Link every treatment to harvest timing. 11. Align the program with the supply or export market. 12. Do not base the program on organophosphates and pyrethroids. 13. Avoid excessive use of diamides, spinosyns, or avermectins. 14. Review performance after every treatment. 15. Collect samples for susceptibility testing when failure is repeated. 16. Manage old fields and immigration sources. 17. Do not use insecticides to compensate for poor sanitation or absence of traps. 18. Maintain a complete treatment record beginning in the nursery.

Chemical control against Tuta absoluta should be used as a targeted intervention within integrated pest management rather than as a fixed periodic program. The decision depends on monitoring, dominant pest stage, crop stage, natural-enemy activity, and harvest timing. Highest efficacy is achieved when eggs close to hatching and first- and second-instar larvae are targeted before they enter growing points, stems, and fruits. Resistance-management programs should be based on IRAC group numbers rather than trade names. Important examples include: Spinosad and Spinetoram: group 5. Abamectin and Emamectin benzoate: group 6. Bt products targeting Lepidoptera: group 11A. Chlorfenapyr: group 13. Lufenuron, Chlorfluazuron, and Novaluron: group 15. Methoxyfenozide and Tebufenozide: group 18. Indoxacarb: group 22A. Chlorantraniliprole, Cyantraniliprole, Flubendiamide, and Tetraniliprole: group 28. Rotation between two active ingredients within the same group does not constitute true rotation. Resistance management recommends avoiding exposure of successive generations to the same group and using a window strategy aligned with generation duration and local temperature. Mixtures are not substitutes for rotation. Each active ingredient in the mixture should be effective, registered, and belong to a different group, and the same mixture should not be repeated throughout the season. Resistance must also be distinguished from application failure. Failure may result from timing, coverage, dose, weather, immigration, or larvae already being inside fruits. Susceptibility can be scientifically verified using standardized tests, including IRAC Method No. 022 for Tuta absoluta larvae. Rational chemical control is completed by compliance with preharvest intervals, maximum residue limits, restricted-entry intervals, worker safety, and protection of the environment and natural enemies. References

Insecticide resistance is one of the most serious factors limiting the sustainability of Tuta absoluta management. Different pest populations have developed varying degrees of resistance to several old and modern insecticide groups as a result of repeated exposure to high selection pressure over successive generations. Resistance does not arise because an insect “becomes accustomed” to an insecticide during its lifetime. Rather, it develops through a heritable change in the genetic composition of the pest population. When an insecticide is applied, the most susceptible individuals die, while individuals carrying heritable traits that reduce their sensitivity are more likely to survive. Survivors mate and transmit resistance traits to their offspring, gradually increasing the proportion of resistant individuals within the population. The more frequently the same mode of action is used: The greater the selection pressure. The lower the proportion of susceptible individuals. The wider the spread of resistance alleles. The higher the dose needed to achieve the same level of mortality. The greater the probability of field failure. The objective of resistance management is to slow this process and preserve population susceptibility to effective insecticides for as long as possible.

Insecticide resistance is defined as: A heritable change in the susceptibility of a pest population that results in repeated failure of an insecticide to achieve the expected level of control when used at the registered rate and according to the registered application method. The definition includes four main elements: 1. The change is heritable. 2. It occurs at the population level, not merely in one individual. 3. It causes a measurable decline in susceptibility. 4. It is associated with repeated failure under correct-use conditions. Survival of a limited number of larvae after treatment is not conclusive evidence of resistance.

Reduced insecticide performance may result from causes unrelated to heritable changes in susceptibility. These include: Delayed application until larvae have entered fruits. Targeting an unsuitable larval instar. Poor sprayer calibration. Inadequate coverage of growing points and lower leaf surfaces. Use of a dose below the registered rate. Product degradation due to poor storage. High alkalinity or hardness of spray water. Incompatibility among tank-mix components. Rainfall or overhead irrigation after application. Continued moth immigration from a neighboring field. Incorrect pest identification. Interpreting old mines as new infestation. Evaluation before the active ingredient has completed its effect. These causes should be excluded before resistance is declared.

Every insect population contains a degree of natural genetic variation. Alleles conferring some resistance may exist before commercial use of an insecticide, but at a low frequency that causes no visible problem. When the active ingredient is repeatedly used: 1. Susceptible individuals are killed. 2. Less-susceptible individuals survive. 3. Their contribution to mating increases. 4. Resistance alleles are transmitted to offspring. 5. The frequency of resistance alleles rises. 6. The population gradually shifts from susceptible to resistant. The insecticide does not create the appropriate mutation in response to need. Instead, it selects pre-existing heritable traits or mutations arising randomly.

sectionTitle: Why Is Tuta absoluta at High Risk?
text: The pest has a high capacity to develop resistance because of the combined effect of several biological and management factors: Short generation time under favorable conditions. Multiple generations during the season. High fecundity. Overlapping life stages in the field. Large numbers of applications during one crop cycle. Exposure of successive generations to the same group. Larval concealment within tissues, which encourages higher doses and repeated applications. Year-round cultivation of tomato and solanaceous hosts. Movement of resistant individuals among regions. Use of the same products in nurseries and fields. Greater reliance on chemical control than on non-chemical methods. Repeated spraying while old mines remain visible. A high resistance risk does not mean that all populations possess the same level of resistance. Susceptibility may differ between neighboring areas according to use history and migration.

Selection pressure is the intensity of the effect imposed by an insecticide on the genetic composition of the pest population. It increases when: The same mode-of-action group is repeated. It is used against successive generations. A high number of applications is made. Doses permit survival of moderately susceptible individuals. Most of the population is exposed without sources of susceptible individuals. Residual activity is prolonged. The same group is used over large contiguous areas. The active ingredient is repeated in nurseries, greenhouses, and neighboring fields. Selection pressure is reduced by: Reducing the number of applications. Using an insecticide only when necessary. Rotating modes of action. Integrating biological and cultural control. Using traps and pheromones. Preventing population build-up from the beginning. Treating foci rather than the entire area when justified.

Resistance may be: Dominant. Partially dominant. Recessive. Incompletely recessive. Dominant Resistance Resistance is expressed even when an individual carries only one copy of the resistance allele and may therefore spread rapidly. Recessive Resistance An individual usually requires two copies of the allele to express full resistance. In some resistance systems, a high effective field dose may kill heterozygous individuals carrying only one allele, but this principle does not apply automatically to all insecticides and pests. Some cases of resistance in Tuta absoluta have shown inheritance that may be: Autosomal and not sex-linked. Incompletely recessive. Controlled by one major gene or a limited number of genes. Inheritance pattern affects the rate of spread and the ability to detect resistance.

Resistance traits may impose physiological costs that reduce insect performance in the absence of the insecticide. These may appear as: Reduced fecundity. Slower development. Lower survival. Delayed mating. Reduced host-searching ability. Shorter lifespan. Reduced competitive ability. Some spinosyn-resistant Tuta absoluta strains showed declining resistance after selection pressure was removed, indicating a fitness cost in those strains. However, resistance does not always decline after use stops because: Fitness costs may be limited. Compensatory mutations may evolve. The population may remain exposed to insecticides with cross-resistance. Resistant individuals may immigrate from other areas. The resistance allele may become common and stable. Temporary discontinuation of an active ingredient should therefore not be assumed to restore susceptibility automatically.

Movement of moths among fields affects resistance in two opposite ways. Introduction of Susceptible Individuals Mating with resistant individuals may reduce resistance-allele frequency, especially when the trait is recessive or associated with a fitness cost. Introduction of Resistant Individuals Migration may transfer resistance into fields and regions where the group has not been intensively used. Reduced efficacy may therefore appear in a field with limited spray history when it receives moths from: A heavily treated old field. A neighboring production area. Infested transplants or plant material. Greenhouses using intensive chemical programs. This supports the need for area-wide resistance management.

Insecticide resistance develops through four principal categories of mechanisms. Target-Site Alteration Target-Site Resistance A mutation occurs in the protein or receptor targeted by the insecticide, reducing the ability of the active ingredient to bind to it. A single mutation may cause a large increase in resistance when the target site is highly specific. Metabolic Resistance Metabolic Resistance The insect’s ability to detoxify the insecticide or convert it into a less toxic form increases before it reaches the target site. Important enzyme systems include: Cytochrome P450 monooxygenases. Glutathione S-transferases. Carboxylesterases. UDP-glycosyltransferases. Other metabolic enzymes. Reduced Penetration Cuticular properties or penetration rate change, allowing less insecticide to reach internal tissues. Behavioural Resistance Contact with the insecticide decreases through: Avoidance of treated surfaces. Changes in feeding time. Leaving the treated area. Increased concealment within plant tissues. Behavioural resistance has not been demonstrated in every case of Tuta-control failure and should be experimentally verified.

Multiple Resistance Multiple Resistance The population possesses more than one independent mechanism conferring resistance to different insecticide groups. A theoretical example: A mutation conferring pyrethroid resistance. Combined with increased P450 activity conferring resistance to another active ingredient. Cross-Resistance Cross-Resistance One mechanism confers resistance to more than one active ingredient. This may occur among: Active ingredients within the same group. Different active ingredients sharing the same target site. Different groups detoxified by the same metabolic enzyme. Rotation between different IRAC numbers does not guarantee success when one metabolic mechanism confers resistance to both active ingredients.

Laboratory Reduction in Susceptibility The lethal-dose value of a field population is higher than that of a susceptible reference strain. Field Resistance The reduction in susceptibility has reached a level that causes repeated product failure under correct use. A laboratory difference in LC50 may occur without field failure because the registered field dose remains higher than the amount required to kill the population. Conversely, field failure may occur before a complete laboratory-monitoring program is implemented. The following should therefore be integrated: Susceptibility bioassays. Field-performance data. Registered field dose. Application history. Analysis of resistance mechanisms.

sectionTitle: History of Resistance in Tuta absoluta
text: Reports of declining efficacy began in South America after years of intensive insecticide use. Resistance or reduced susceptibility was then documented to several groups, including: Organophosphates. Carbamates. Pyrethroids. Avermectins. Chitin-biosynthesis inhibitors. Spinosyns. Oxadiazines. Diamides. Other active ingredients according to region. After the pest spread to Europe, Africa, and Asia, independent resistance cases appeared or resistance alleles moved with pest populations. Global reports do not mean that all these groups fail in every country because susceptibility status differs among populations.

Pyrethroid resistance in Tuta absoluta is principally associated with changes in the voltage-gated sodium channel. Detected mutations include: L1014F. M918T. T929I. Some of these mutations are included within the concepts of: Knockdown resistance — kdr. Super-kdr. They reduce sodium-channel sensitivity to pyrethroids. Some populations have also shown increased activity of: Cytochrome P450. Glutathione S-transferases. This indicates that target-site and metabolic resistance may coexist in the same population. Because some sodium-channel mutations are widespread, repeated pyrethroid use may provide poor pest control while causing severe effects on natural enemies.

These groups target acetylcholinesterase. Resistance to organophosphates in Tuta absoluta has been associated with mechanisms including: Alterations in the acetylcholinesterase-1 gene. Increased metabolic detoxification. The combined action of more than one mechanism. One mutation associated with resistance in some populations is: A201S in the ace-1 gene. The presence of this mutation does not mean that all active ingredients in the two groups fail equally, but repeated use in a population carrying the mutation increases the probability of reduced efficacy. The problem is compounded by the broad effects of these insecticides on parasitoids and predators.

This group includes: Spinosad. Spinetoram. Some Tuta absoluta populations have developed very high resistance to spinosyns. One of the most important confirmed mechanisms is: The G275E mutation in the α6 subunit of the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor. This mutation alters the spinosyn-binding site on the receptor. Laboratory-selection studies have shown: Resistance can reach extremely high levels. It may be associated with a major gene in some strains. Fitness costs may occur after selection pressure is removed. Cross-resistance may occur between Spinosad and Spinetoram because both belong to the same group. Switching from Spinosad to Spinetoram therefore does not constitute true rotation.

This group includes: Abamectin. Emamectin benzoate. Reduced susceptibility or resistance to this group has been recorded in some Tuta absoluta populations. Studies indicate an important role for metabolic resistance, particularly: Increased P450-enzyme activity. Changes in detoxification systems. Possible contribution of other enzymes according to the population. No single universal target-site mechanism has been confirmed as explaining all cases of Emamectin benzoate resistance. Rotation between Abamectin and Emamectin benzoate is not true rotation because both belong to IRAC group 6.

Indoxacarb acts by blocking voltage-gated sodium channels after conversion to the active metabolite within the insect. Recent studies have reported mutations associated with reduced susceptibility, including: F1845Y. V1848I. Metabolic mechanisms may also contribute to different degrees according to the population. In some Iranian populations, laboratory resistance ratios ranged from low to moderate and were associated principally with target-site alterations. These mutations and resistance levels should not be generalized to all regions without testing.

Diamides target ryanodine receptors responsible for regulating calcium release within muscle cells. The group includes: Chlorantraniliprole. Cyantraniliprole. Flubendiamide. Tetraniliprole. Other active ingredients carrying group number 28. Mutations in the ryanodine receptor have been confirmed in resistant Tuta absoluta populations, including: G4903E. G4903V. I4746M. I4746T. Corresponding positions may be reported under slightly different numbers in other species because of differences in protein-sequence numbering. These mutations reduce receptor affinity for diamides. Functional studies have shown that the mutations may confer different levels of resistance to different active ingredients within the group, but this does not make rotation among them a reliable strategy because all target the same general site.

Diamide resistance does not always depend on ryanodine-receptor mutations. Spanish strains resistant to Chlorantraniliprole have been reported without the known receptor mutations, and resistance was associated with strong overexpression of: UGT34A23 An enzyme belonging to the UDP-glycosyltransferases. This result confirms that a population may develop resistance to the same group through different mechanisms: Target-site alteration. Increased detoxification. A combination of the two. Testing for one mutation is therefore insufficient to rule out group-28 resistance.

Benzoylureas were used extensively in some parts of South America, followed by clear declines in efficacy. Resistance may be associated with: Metabolic mechanisms. Polygenic changes. Changes in chitin-biosynthesis pathways or responses to the active ingredient. No single universal mutation has been demonstrated as explaining all cases of resistance in Tuta absoluta. Risk increases when: The group is used against unsuitable instars. It is repeated against successive generations. It is evaluated too early and spraying is repeated before the effect appears.

Reports of high Chlorfenapyr resistance remain less widespread than for some other groups, although differences in susceptibility have been detected among pest populations. Reduced susceptibility may involve: Metabolic mechanisms. Use history. Differences in the insect’s ability to activate or detoxify the active ingredient. Other multiple factors. A different mode of action does not make Chlorfenapyr immune to resistance; continuous repetition selects individuals able to tolerate it.

Susceptibility Baseline A susceptibility baseline is a measurement of the response of populations that have not been widely exposed to an insecticide, or are highly susceptible to it, before widespread use of the active ingredient. It is used to determine: Natural variation. LC50 and LC90 values. The dose–response curve. A diagnostic concentration. Subsequent changes in susceptibility. The baseline should be established using: Several geographical populations where possible. A standardized test method. The same larval instar. Constant temperature and humidity. The same active ingredient. A standardized assessment period. Results from two laboratories cannot be directly compared when test methods differ substantially.

Proactive Monitoring Proactive Monitoring This begins before clear field failure appears. Its objectives are to: Measure the susceptibility baseline. Track changes in LC50 or diagnostic-concentration response. Detect resistance at an early stage. Modify the rotation program before failure occurs. Reactive Monitoring Reactive Monitoring This begins after reduced efficacy or field failure is recorded. Its objectives are to: Confirm whether the failure is caused by resistance. Determine the resistance level. Identify affected active ingredients or groups. Develop a containment plan. Proactive monitoring is preferable for protecting new active ingredients, whereas reactive monitoring is necessary for investigating failure cases.

The sample should represent the field population. Larvae or infested leaves should therefore be collected from: Several points within the field. Both edges and interior. A large number of plants. More than one hotspot. More than one field when evaluating a whole area. The following should be avoided: Collecting all larvae from one plant. Sampling immediately after a recent spray. Mixing populations with different pesticide histories without recording them. Collecting dead or severely weakened individuals. Storing samples at high temperature. The following should be recorded with the sample: Location. Date. Cultivar. Crop age. Pesticide history. Active ingredients used. Date of the most recent treatment. Infestation level. Description of the field failure.

IRAC Method No. 022 is one of the standardized methods used to test susceptibility of Tuta absoluta larvae. Its basic principles include: 1. Using uniform larvae, preferably second instars. 2. Using healthy tomato leaves. 3. Preparing known concentrations of the active ingredient. 4. Dipping leaves in the treatment solution. 5. Allowing them to dry. 6. Placing larvae on treated leaves. 7. Holding them under constant conditions. 8. Assessing mortality after the period appropriate to the active ingredient. 9. Correcting mortality when control mortality occurs. 10. Analyzing the dose–response curve. The F1 generation of field samples should preferably be used where possible to provide uniform numbers and reduce the effects of direct previous exposure. Commercial premixes containing two active ingredients should not be used to estimate susceptibility to either component individually.

LC50 The concentration causing mortality of 50% of tested individuals. LC90 The concentration causing mortality of 90% of tested individuals. These values are used to: Compare populations. Establish a susceptibility baseline. Calculate a resistance ratio. Track changes in susceptibility over time. LC50 is not necessarily equivalent to the field dose. It is a laboratory value affected by: Exposure method. Larval instar. Assessment time. Test conditions.

The resistance ratio is commonly calculated as: Resistance ratio, RR = LC50 of the field population ÷ LC50 of the susceptible population Example: If the field-population LC50 is 10 mg/L and the susceptible-strain LC50 is 1 mg/L: RR = 10 ÷ 1 = 10-fold No universal boundaries always distinguish among: Susceptibility. Low resistance. Moderate resistance. High resistance. RR should be interpreted together with: Confidence intervals. Field dose. Mortality at the diagnostic concentration. Use history. Field performance.

A diagnostic concentration is a defined concentration expected to kill a very high proportion of a susceptible population. It is used to screen many populations more rapidly than conducting a full dose–response assay for every sample. An increased proportion of survivors at this concentration indicates: Presence of less-susceptible individuals. Possible increase in resistance-allele frequency. Need for a complete dose–response assay. Need to review use of the active ingredient in the area. The diagnostic concentration should be derived from a documented susceptibility baseline and not chosen arbitrarily from the field dose.

Synergist Bioassays Compounds such as the following are used to help investigate the role of detoxification-enzyme systems: Piperonyl butoxide — PBO. Triphenyl phosphate — TPP. Diethyl maleate — DEM. A substantial increase in insecticide toxicity after use of a synergist may indicate an enzymatic contribution to resistance. However, the test: Does not identify the responsible gene by itself. Is affected by synergist dose. Does not prove causality conclusively. Should be supported by enzyme measurements and molecular analysis. Synergists should not be used in the field merely because they were effective in a laboratory test unless registered for that purpose.

The activity of enzyme systems may be measured, including: P450 monooxygenases. Glutathione S-transferases. Esterases. These measurements are useful for comparing: A resistant population. A susceptible population. Different regions. Changes in activity after selection. Increased enzyme activity alone does not prove that it causes resistance. It may be: A general physiological response. Associated with larval age. Influenced by diet. A consequence of previous exposure. Demonstrating the mechanism requires integration of: Toxicity bioassays. Synergist tests. Enzyme measurements. Gene-expression analysis. Functional validation.

Molecular tests can detect known mutations, such as: Group Molecular Target Example Mutations Pyrethroids Sodium channel L1014F, M918T, T929I Organophosphates AChE1 A201S Spinosyns nAChR α6 G275E Indoxacarb Sodium channel F1845Y, V1848I Diamides Ryanodine receptor G4903E/V, I4746M/T Molecular markers allow: Detection of an allele before full field failure occurs. Measurement of allele frequency. Tracking of geographical spread. Comparison among fields and seasons. Absence of known mutations does not prove susceptibility because there may be: A new mutation. Metabolic resistance. Reduced penetration. Multiple untested mechanisms.

A field-performance record provides an early means of detecting gradual decline in efficacy. For every treatment, record: Active ingredient. IRAC group. Dose. Larval instar. Infestation before treatment. Coverage. Weather. Percentage of live larvae after treatment. New mines. Fruit infestation. Previous application history. The same evaluation method should be used each time to allow valid comparison.

When efficacy declines, investigation should proceed in the following order. Step One: Review Application Dose. Calibration. Coverage. Water quality. Weather. Storage. Tank mixing. Step Two: Review the Pest Larval instar. Location of larvae. Old and new mines. Entry into fruits. Step Three: Review Immigration Trap data. Old fields. Field edges. Cessation of control in neighboring fields. Step Four: Review Group History Number of applications. Repetition in the nursery. Use of the same active ingredient on neighboring crops. Mixtures containing the same group. Step Five: Collect Samples Conduct susceptibility bioassays. Screen for mutations. Measure enzyme activity where possible. The same group should not be reused during the investigation merely to reconfirm failure.

Resistance management recommends dividing the season into time- or heat-based windows corresponding to a generation or a defined portion of pest development. One group may be used within a window according to the product label, followed by a different group in the next window. The principal rule is: Do not expose two successive generations of Tuta absoluta to the same mode-of-action group. The window should be designed according to: Temperature. Generation duration. Date of first flight. Hatching waves. Crop stage. Trap data. A 30-day period should not be used as a fixed value in every planting season because the generation may be shorter or longer.

There are no perfectly distinct boundaries among Tuta absoluta generations in the field. The following may be present simultaneously: Eggs. Different larval instars. Pupae. Adult moths. The purpose of a window is therefore to reduce exposure of most individuals within one generation to multiple groups or exposure of successive generations to the same group. The program should: Define the start of the window using a clear biological event. Record the date of the first application. Avoid returning to the previous group merely because several days have passed. Use non-chemical methods between windows. Consider long residual activity.

Mixtures may provide benefit under specific conditions, but they do not guarantee prevention of resistance. Each component should meet the following requirements: A different mode of action. Independent efficacy against the same target stage. A full effective dose. Relatively similar protection periods. Simultaneous delivery to the pest. No widespread resistance to either component. Mixture value is low when: One component is ineffective. Resistance to one component is already present. Residual periods differ greatly. The same mixture is repeated across several windows. Both components belong to the same IRAC group. Mixtures should not be used to compensate for lack of rotation or poor application.

The best way to reduce resistance risk is to reduce pest-population exposure to insecticides. This is achieved through: Pest-free transplants. Crop-residue removal. Pheromone traps. Egg parasitoids. Predators. Bt. Entomopathogenic fungi and nematodes. Management of old fields. Collection of infested fruits. Use of thresholds. Localized treatment of foci. Every individual killed by a non-chemical method is an individual not subjected to insecticide selection pressure.

The registered dose demonstrated to be effective should be used. Unregistered Low Doses These may allow survival of: Moderately susceptible individuals. Heterozygous individuals. Individuals carrying partial resistance mechanisms. Doses Above the Registered Rate These may: Increase residues. Harm natural enemies. Increase environmental risks. Increase selection pressure. Fail to restore the efficacy of an active ingredient compromised by strong resistance. Increasing the dose is not a resistance-management strategy.

Late application increases resistance risk because: Large larvae require greater exposure. Some are already within protected tissues. A greater proportion survives. Spraying is repeated after a short interval. Applications from the same group accumulate. Targeted application against young instars provides greater efficacy with fewer treatments and reduces the proportion of survivors.

Uneven coverage can function as a spatially low dose. Some larvae receive a full concentration, while others receive lower concentrations in: Untreated lower leaf surfaces. The inner canopy. Growing points. Field edges. Areas affected by blocked nozzles. These zones increase the probability that moderately susceptible individuals will survive. Calibration and coverage are therefore components of resistance management, not merely requirements for immediate efficacy.

In some resistance-management systems, untreated areas allow susceptible individuals to survive and mate with resistant individuals. Deliberately applying a refuge concept in tomato against Tuta absoluta faces constraints including: High crop value. Rapid pest spread. Fruit infestation. Movement into neighboring fields. Absence of a standardized recommendation to leave infested sectors unmanaged. A “Tuta refuge” should therefore not be created randomly within the field. Susceptible individuals can be conserved more safely through: Reducing the number of applications. Periods without use of the group. Use of non-chemical methods. Avoiding unnecessary treatment of pest-free areas. Regional coordination.

The best rotation in one field may fail when surrounding fields repeatedly use the same group. Area-wide management includes: Sharing data on IRAC groups used. Avoiding dependence by all fields on the same group at the same time throughout the season. Coordinating mode-of-action windows. Reporting failure cases. Collecting samples from high-use areas. Managing old fields. Preventing circulation of unsupported recommendations. Training pesticide distributors and agricultural engineers. Coordination does not mean that all fields use the same insecticide. It means developing a plan that prevents continuous pressure on one group across the region.

Resistance pressure begins before transplants reach the field when insecticides are used in the nursery. Each transplant batch should therefore be accompanied by a record including: Active ingredients. IRAC groups. Number of applications. Date of the most recent treatment. Target pests. The nursery record should be integrated with the field program. If group 28 is used in the nursery and then used again immediately after transplanting, the pest population has been exposed during two successive periods despite the change in application location.

sectionTitle: Resistance Selection by Treatments Against Other Crop Pests
text: A particular group may be used against: Whiteflies. Thrips. Other caterpillars. Sucking pests. At the same time, Tuta absoluta is exposed to the residues. All applications to which Tuta is exposed should therefore be included in the resistance history, not only treatments recorded as specifically targeting Tuta. Resistance may be selected unintentionally by sprays directed against another pest.

An Egyptian study published in May 2026 showed differences in the susceptibility of Tuta absoluta populations collected from: Luxor. Assiut. Giza. The populations were tested against: Chlorantraniliprole. Chlorfenapyr. Indoxacarb. Emamectin benzoate. Spinetoram. The Luxor population recorded the highest LC50 values and relative resistance ratios, especially to Chlorfenapyr and Indoxacarb, whereas the Giza population was the most susceptible in the test. The Luxor population also showed higher activity of some detoxification enzymes, particularly P450 and GST, suggesting a possible contribution of metabolic resistance. Under the conditions of the study, Emamectin benzoate and Spinetoram remained highly effective against the tested populations. However, these results: Concern only three populations. Do not represent a national resistance map. Do not guarantee continued efficacy in following seasons. Do not justify repeated use of the two active ingredients. Require annual monitoring and inclusion of additional regions.

sectionTitle: Design of an Egyptian Monitoring Program
text: The proposed program should include: Regions The Nile Delta. Nubaria. Wadi El Natroun. Matrouh. Middle Egypt. Luxor and Aswan. Processing-tomato production areas. Greenhouse and nursery production areas. Timing Beginning of the planting season. Mid-season. After documented failure cases. End of the season when spray pressure is high. Groups Priority should be given to groups that are heavily used or economically important, such as: 5. 6. 13. 15. 22A. 28. Newer groups when registered. Tests IRAC Method 022. Diagnostic concentrations. Dose–response curves. Enzyme assays. Molecular markers where available. Linking results with field-use records.

Resistance risk for each field may be classified using the following indicators: Indicator Low Moderate High Number of applications of the group 0–1 2 More than 2 Use in the nursery No Unknown Yes Use in the previous generation No Uncertain Yes Previous decline in efficacy No Once Repeated Coverage Good Inconsistent Poor Use of biological methods High Moderate Absent Regional pressure Low Moderate High Pesticide record Complete Incomplete Absent Unregistered doses No Possible Repeated Random mixtures No Sometimes Repeated Risk level increases as more indicators from the high-risk column accumulate.

sectionTitle: Management Plan for a High-Risk Field
text: 1. Review all applications made during the season. 2. Identify the IRAC groups used. 3. Stop use of the repeated group. 4. Examine larval instars. 5. Review calibration and coverage. 6. Collect a sample for susceptibility testing. 7. Use an active ingredient from a different group demonstrated to be effective. 8. Increase biological and microbial control. 9. Manage hotspots and infested fruits. 10. Inspect neighboring fields. 11. Shorten the reassessment interval. 12. Record the outcome and notify technical management.

When resistance to a particular group is confirmed: Suspend use of the group in the affected area for an appropriate period. Do not use another active ingredient from the same group. Review mixtures containing the group. Notify neighboring fields and nurseries. Use different effective groups. Intensify non-chemical methods. Monitor resistance alleles or susceptibility periodically. Do not increase the dose or application frequency. Identify sources of spread of the resistant population. Review the following season’s plan. Suspending the group does not guarantee recovery of susceptibility. Testing should be conducted before reintroduction.

These include: Stable population susceptibility. No increase in LC50 across seasons. Fewer failure cases. Fewer applications. No repetition of one group against successive generations. Preservation of efficacy of key groups. Increased use of biological methods. Availability of complete pesticide records. Rapid investigation of failure cases. Data sharing among regions. Reduced reliance on poorly designed mixtures.

Error One: Assuming Resistance Always Results from Poor Product Quality The product may be sound and the population resistant, or the reverse may be true. Error Two: Declaring Resistance After One Treatment Application errors should first be excluded. Error Three: Increasing the Dose After Failure This does not overcome resistance and may increase risk. Error Four: Repeating the Active Ingredient Within a Short Period This increases selection pressure. Error Five: Changing Only the Trade Name This does not change the mode of action. Error Six: Switching Between Active Ingredients Within the Same Group This does not constitute true rotation. Error Seven: Forgetting Nursery Applications They form part of the exposure history. Error Eight: Using a Group Against Another Pest Without Accounting for Tuta Exposure This may select resistance unintentionally. Error Nine: Relying on the Same Mixture Throughout the Season This selects resistance to both components. Error Ten: Using Unregistered Low Concentrations This allows less-susceptible individuals to survive. Error Eleven: Ignoring Metabolic Resistance It may cause cross-resistance between different groups. Error Twelve: Relying on Detection of One Mutation Other mechanisms may be present. Error Thirteen: Comparing LC50 Values from Different Test Methods The comparison may be invalid. Error Fourteen: Collecting an Unrepresentative Sample The sample may reflect only one hotspot. Error Fifteen: Continuing to Use the Group After Failure Is Confirmed This accelerates the spread of resistance.

The priorities are: 1. Establish a national susceptibility baseline for the principal groups. 2. Apply IRAC Method 022 in specialized laboratories. 3. Collect samples from high-density and high-use areas. 4. Integrate nursery and field records. 5. Record the IRAC group on spray orders. 6. Prevent use of the same group against two successive generations. 7. Review all components of commercial mixtures. 8. Do not treat Spinosad and Spinetoram as different groups. 9. Do not treat Abamectin and Emamectin benzoate as different groups. 10. Do not rotate among diamides as though they were separate groups. 11. Investigate local declines in efficacy of Chlorfenapyr and Indoxacarb. 12. Expand the recent Egyptian study to additional regions and planting seasons. 13. Introduce molecular tests for known resistance mutations. 14. Monitor P450, GST, and other metabolic enzymes. 15. Strengthen biological control to reduce exposure frequency. 16. Educate growers and pesticide distributors about modes of action. 17. Issue regional warnings when resistance is confirmed. 18. Do not reintroduce a suspended group before susceptibility recovery is tested.

Date Sector Active Ingredient IRAC Group Window Number Target Generation Previous Applications of the Group Efficacy Natural-Enemy Status Decision for the Next Window The record should also include: Nursery applications. Active ingredients used against other pests. Mixtures. Failure cases. Susceptibility-test results. Transplant source. Neighboring fields. Harvest date.

Resistance is a heritable population-level change resulting from repeated selection of less-susceptible individuals. It is not a physiological habituation that develops within an individual during its lifetime. Resistance risk in Tuta absoluta is high because of the short life cycle, multiple generations, high fecundity, frequent applications, and overlapping planting seasons. The principal resistance mechanisms include: Target-site alteration. Increased detoxification-enzyme activity. Reduced penetration. Behavioural change. Mutations associated with resistance to several groups have been confirmed in Tuta absoluta, including: L1014F, M918T, and T929I for pyrethroids. A201S for organophosphates. G275E for spinosyns. F1845Y and V1848I for Indoxacarb. G4903E/V and I4746M/T for diamides. Metabolic resistance to diamides associated with overexpression of UGT34A23 has also been demonstrated, showing that absence of known ryanodine-receptor mutations does not rule out group-28 resistance. Resistance is managed through: Reducing the number of applications. Targeting susceptible instars. Applying the registered dose. Improving coverage. Rotating IRAC groups. Avoiding exposure of successive generations to the same group. Integrating non-chemical methods. Monitoring susceptibility. Scientifically investigating field failure. Coordinating management across regions. Changing only the trade name or switching between active ingredients within the same group does not constitute rotation. IRAC Method No. 022 is used to measure susceptibility of Tuta absoluta larvae, together with biochemical and molecular tests. An Egyptian study published in 2026 showed differences among Luxor, Assiut, and Giza populations, with relatively higher resistance in the Luxor population to some active ingredients and increased P450 and GST activity. This finding confirms the need for a continuous national program and does not represent a final judgment on all Tuta absoluta populations in Egypt. References

Successful integrated management of Tuta absoluta is not achieved by combining the greatest possible number of control methods in one program. It depends on selecting compatible methods that act in an integrated manner against different pest stages. A program may fail despite the use of individually effective tools when conflicts occur among them, such as: Applying a highly toxic insecticide after releasing egg parasitoids. Using a fungicide at the same time as an entomopathogenic fungus. Removing leaves carrying parasitized eggs. Mixing nematodes in a highly saline solution or one containing disinfectants. Using two predators that engage in intraguild predation. Applying a long-residual insecticide before releasing a sensitive natural enemy. Combining two agents targeting the same stage when one kills the other. Conversely, proper integration may: Target several stages of the pest life cycle. Increase total mortality. Reduce the number of chemical sprays. Reduce resistance-selection pressure. Improve fruit protection. Increase program stability throughout the season. Reduce the likelihood of pest resurgence after treatment. Compatibility testing is therefore part of program design, not a secondary step reviewed only after failure occurs.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Compatibility is the ability of two or more control methods to operate within the same system without causing an unacceptable negative effect on: Survival of the biological-control agent. Its fecundity. Its searching behaviour. Its predation or parasitism rate. Viability of a microbial organism. Insecticide efficacy. Plant safety. Yield. Non-target organisms. Compatibility does not necessarily mean complete absence of any negative effect. One method may cause a limited reduction in natural-enemy survival while the overall program remains economically and environmentally beneficial. The final outcome should be evaluated at the level of: Pest + natural enemy + plant + yield And not on the basis of one factor alone.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Toxicological Compatibility This concerns whether an insecticide or product causes an unacceptable level of mortality in: Parasitoids. Predators. Entomopathogenic fungi. Nematodes. Other beneficial organisms. Behavioural Compatibility This means not disrupting: Host searching. Recognition of eggs or larvae. Feeding. Mating. Dispersal within the crop. Responses to odours and pheromones. A natural enemy may survive exposure but become less able to detect the pest. Physiological Compatibility This concerns avoiding unacceptable effects on: Fecundity. Hatching percentage. Development of immature stages. Longevity. Female ratio. Successful parasitoid emergence. Spore growth or nematode viability. Physical and Chemical Compatibility This concerns whether products can be mixed without: Precipitation. Clumping. Separation. Excessive foaming. Severe pH change. Killing a living organism in the tank. Nozzle blockage. Reduced stability of the active material. Temporal Compatibility Two methods may be incompatible when used simultaneously but can be used within the same season if separated in time. Spatial Compatibility Conflict can be reduced through: Applying an insecticide to a defined hotspot. Protecting natural-enemy release areas. Treating soil instead of foliage. Leaving untreated areas for natural enemies. Separating production zones or batches.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Outcomes of Interaction Between Two Methods
text: Combining two methods may produce four principal outcomes. Additive Effect Additive Effect The combined effect is close to the expected sum of the effects of each method used alone. Example: A microbial agent kills part of the larval population. A parasitoid kills another part. Neither strongly affects the other. Synergistic Effect Synergistic Effect The combined effect is greater than expected from the sum of the separate effects. This may occur when: A microbial agent weakens host immunity. Parasitoid attack facilitates access by another agent. The two methods efficiently target successive stages. Antagonistic Effect Antagonistic Effect The combined effect is lower than expected because one method interferes with the other. Examples include: The insecticide kills the parasitoid. The fungus kills the host before parasitoid development is completed. The predator consumes parasitized eggs. A fungicide prevents germination of the entomopathogenic fungus. Neutral Effect The combination does not measurably alter the effect of either method compared with separate use. Synergy should not be assumed merely because both methods reduce pest density.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Conflict occurs through one or more of the following pathways: 1. Direct toxicity to the natural enemy. 2. Toxicity caused by residues. 3. Consumption of contaminated prey or host. 4. Reduced host quality after infection by another agent. 5. Killing the host before parasitoid development is completed. 6. Removal of a parasitized host. 7. Competition for the same host. 8. Intraguild predation. 9. Inhibition of fungal germination or nematode viability. 10. Alteration of searching or mating behaviour. 11. Reduced fecundity without direct mortality. 12. Differences in the timing of action of each method.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: A single laboratory result is insufficient for a final judgment on compatibility between a product and a natural enemy. Effects should be studied at several levels. Direct Laboratory Test The natural enemy is directly exposed to the insecticide or treated surface. This often represents the worst-case exposure scenario. Residual Test The natural enemy is exposed to a leaf or surface treated with the insecticide after a defined time interval. This helps estimate the duration of residual hazard. Contaminated Host or Prey Test The parasitoid or predator is exposed to: Treated eggs. Treated larvae. Prey that has ingested the insecticide. Contaminated nectar or honeydew. Semi-Field Test This is conducted on a whole plant, inside a cage, or in a small greenhouse. Field Test This measures: Natural-enemy survival. Establishment. Parasitism or predation rate. Actual impact on the pest. Final effect on yield. A laboratory test may show high toxicity while field risk is lower because exposure is reduced. The opposite may occur when residues persist for a long period.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: The International Organisation for Biological and Integrated Control, IOBC, developed standardized methods for assessing pesticide effects on beneficial organisms. The following categories are commonly used in laboratory tests: IOBC Class Corrected Effect on the Natural Enemy Interpretation 1 Less than 30% Harmless or slightly harmful 2 30–79% Slightly harmful 3 80–99% Moderately harmful 4 More than 99% Harmful “Effect” may include more than direct mortality, such as reduced reproduction or activity. The classification from a laboratory test should not be transferred directly to the field because: Field exposure may be lower. Residual effects may persist for different periods. Tested life stages may differ. Commercial formulation affects the outcome. Temperature and humidity alter toxicity. Application method changes exposure. Test details should be read, not only the class number.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Acute Toxicity This causes mortality of the natural enemy within a short period after exposure. Sublethal Effects These do not kill the individual directly but may cause: Reduced fecundity. Reduced oviposition. Lower female ratio. Poor flight capacity. Reduced searching rate. Reduced predation. Altered mating. Prolonged development. Reduced longevity. Effects on the following generation. An insecticide may appear compatible when mortality alone is measured while having severe population-level effects through reduced reproduction and searching.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Differences in Susceptibility Among Species and Life Stages
text: No active ingredient is equally safe or harmful to all natural enemies. An insecticide may be: Low in toxicity to a predator. Highly toxic to an egg parasitoid. Less harmful to a parasitoid developing inside a host egg. Highly harmful to a newly emerged adult. Relatively safe to nymphs. Harmful to adults. Susceptibility also differs among: Trichogramma achaeae. Trichogramma brassicae. Necremnus tutae. Macrolophus pygmaeus. Nesidiocoris tenuis. Different strains of the same species. Results from one species should not be transferred to another without evidence.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Routes of Parasitoid Exposure to Insecticides
text: Parasitoids may be exposed through: 1. Contact with direct spray. 2. Walking on leaf residues. 3. Contact with treated eggs or larvae. 4. Feeding on host fluids. 5. Feeding on contaminated nectar. 6. Developing inside a host exposed to the insecticide. 7. Emerging through a surface bearing residues. 8. Consuming contaminated supplementary food. The outcome varies according to parasitoid stage and its location relative to the host body.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Minute adult Trichogramma parasitoids are highly exposed to insecticide residues on leaves. Insecticides may affect: Female survival. Number of eggs parasitized. Ability to locate eggs. Emergence of the following generation. Female ratio. Longevity. The embryo and larva developing within the host egg receive partial protection from direct spray, but this protection is not complete because the insecticide may: Penetrate the eggshell. Affect the host and its resources. Kill the parasitoid at emergence. Remain as a residue around the exit hole.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Timing of Insecticide Application Relative to Trichogramma Cards
text: When cards containing parasitized eggs are used: They should not be installed before applying an incompatible insecticide. The duration of toxic residues should be known. Release should be delayed until risk declines to an acceptable level. Cards should be temporarily removed only when allowed by the product program and when this does not interfere with emergence timing. Cards should not be sprayed directly. They should not be placed on leaves still wet with insecticide. Spray time and expected parasitoid-emergence time should be recorded. No single time interval is suitable for all insecticides. The hazard from some active ingredients may persist for hours, while that from others may last for days or weeks.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Insecticides and Trichogramma: Variation in Results
text: Studies show that some neuroactive insecticides can cause high mortality or substantial sublethal effects on egg parasitoids. Studies on Trichogramma species used against Tuta absoluta have reported that: Spinosad may cause high acute toxicity to adults in some test systems. Mixtures containing Emamectin benzoate and Lufenuron may affect reproduction and some life-history traits. Diamide effects vary according to the active ingredient, species, and exposure stage. Bt is often more compatible when used correctly because it targets larvae that ingest it. These findings should not be converted into a permanent classification for every product because formulation, concentration, life stage, and test method change the outcome.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Compatibility of Insecticides with Larval Parasitoids
text: Larval parasitoids face two hazards: 1. Direct insecticide effects on the parasitoid. 2. Indirect effects through host mortality or reduced host quality. The effect differs according to the parasitoid’s biology. Endoparasitoid It may be partly protected inside the host body but is affected when: The host dies rapidly. Nutritional resources are altered. The insecticide penetrates the host body. Parasitoid emergence is delayed. Ectoparasitoid It is more directly exposed to residues on the host or mine surface. Host-Feeding Parasitoid It may ingest insecticide residues while feeding on larval fluids. Evaluation should therefore include: Adult parasitoid. Immature parasitoid stage. Parasitized host. Successful emergence. Fecundity of the resulting generation.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Recent experiments have shown that combining N. tutae with some biological agents may produce an additive reduction in Tuta absoluta. In one test: Beauveria bassiana. Bacillus thuringiensis. Were among the agents showing relatively high compatibility with N. tutae and the other parasitoid used in the study. Other materials affected: Survival of immature stages. Reproductive behaviour. Non-reproductive behaviour. Parasitoid attack capacity. Results should be interpreted within the conditions of the test and should not be generalized to all fungal strains, Bt products, or parasitoid populations.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Compatibility of Insecticides with Mirid Predators
text: Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis may be exposed through: Direct spray. Walking on treated leaves. Consumption of contaminated prey. Plant feeding from treated tissue. Drinking water droplets or plant exudates. Insecticides may affect: Adults. Nymphs. Eggs inserted into plant tissue. Fecundity. Predation. Predator-induced plant damage. One comparative study reported that Indoxacarb caused higher mortality than Azadirachtin and Spinosad in the two tested predators, with effects differing between M. pygmaeus nymphs and N. tenuis females. This result shows that: The two species do not have equal susceptibility. Nymphal susceptibility differs from adult susceptibility. A material selective to one natural enemy may be less selective to another.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Predator survival after treatment is not sufficient. Sublethal exposure may cause: Reduced number of eggs or larvae consumed. Reduced movement. Reduced searching activity. Delayed reproduction. Reduced oviposition. Altered level of plant feeding. Plant damage by N. tenuis may increase if prey density declines suddenly after insecticide use while the predator remains alive in the crop. The predator-to-prey ratio should therefore be evaluated after treatment, not predator survival alone.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Broad-spectrum insecticides, including many: Pyrethroids. Organophosphates. Carbamates. Non-selective neurotoxic mixtures. Are more likely to disrupt predator and parasitoid communities. Killing natural enemies may result in: Resurgence of Tuta absoluta. Increased whitefly abundance. Increased thrips or mite populations. Need for additional sprays. A shift toward complete chemical dependence. This does not mean that every active ingredient within a broad-spectrum group has identical toxicity, but the burden of proving compatibility is greater.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Bt targets lepidopteran larvae after ingestion of active proteins and therefore generally has less direct effect on: Egg parasitoids. Predators. Insects lacking the appropriate toxin receptors. It can be integrated with egg parasitoids because: The parasitoid prevents hatching of part of the egg population. Bt targets larvae emerging from non-parasitized eggs. It can also be integrated with predators consuming: Eggs. Newly hatched larvae. Larvae that did not ingest a lethal dose. However, the following should be considered: Product adjuvants. Direct spray on natural enemies. Changes in prey quality. Avoiding tank mixing with other living organisms without testing. Compatibility between Bt and natural enemies is one of the most logical forms of integration when demonstrated for the specific product and natural-enemy species.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Combining an entomopathogenic fungus with a parasitoid may produce one of the following outcomes. Complementarity The fungus infects larvae not reached by the parasitoid, while the parasitoid attacks other individuals. Additive Effect Each agent contributes part of the reduction without strongly interfering with the other. Conflict The fungus may infect: The adult parasitoid. The immature stage developing on the host. The host before parasitoid development is completed. The parasitoid cocoon. The interaction depends on: Fungal strain. Spore concentration. Timing of infection. Host age. Parasitoid species. Whether the parasitoid develops inside or outside the host. It is insufficient to describe Beauveria bassiana as generally compatible because strains and formulations differ.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Temporal Separation Between Fungi and Parasitoids
text: Conflict can be reduced by sequencing the applications. Possible systems include: Releasing the parasitoid first, then applying the fungus after a sensitive parasitoid stage has passed. Applying the fungus first, then delaying adult-parasitoid release until active spores on the surface have declined. Restricting the fungus to hotspots and using parasitoids in the rest of the field. Applying the fungus to soil while parasitoids are used on the foliage. The time interval should not be selected arbitrarily. It should be based on: Longevity of the fungal residue. Parasitoid susceptibility. Environmental conditions. Product-test results.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Predators may be exposed to fungal spores while: Walking on leaves. Feeding on infected prey. Receiving direct spray. Grooming body parts. Some fungal strains may have a low effect on one predator but be pathogenic to another. The following should be evaluated: Mortality. Predation rate. Fecundity. Transfer of spores among individuals. Persistence of the predator in the field. Fungi should not be applied directly over predator aggregation or release areas without compatibility data.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: This relationship is one of the most important conflict points in the program. A fungicide may affect: Spore germination. Germ-tube growth. Vegetative growth. Production of new spores. Persistence of the strain on the plant. Endophytic colonization. The risk is not limited to tank mixing. Fungicide residues on leaves may prevent germination of the entomopathogenic fungus even when no direct mixture is made. The effect varies according to: Active ingredient. Concentration. Formulation. Residue age. Fungal strain. Temperature. Plant-disease management and microbial insect-control programs should therefore be designed together rather than independently.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Copper and sulfur compounds may have antifungal effects to varying degrees. They cannot all be classified as: Compatible. Or incompatible. The effect varies with: Type of compound. Dose. Application timing. Fungal strain. Exposure route. Interval between treatments. A product may be acceptable when temporally separated but unsuitable for direct tank mixing. The labels “organic fungicide” or “mineral fungicide” should not be treated as evidence of safety to an entomopathogenic fungus.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Nematodes are affected by: Salinity. Extreme pH. Disinfectants. Desiccation. Some pesticides. Oils and solvents. Mechanical pressure. Temperature. Some chemicals may be compatible with one nematode species or strain while another active ingredient rapidly kills the infective juveniles. The following should be tested: Survival in the solution. Movement. Infectivity after exposure. Duration of exposure in the tank. Effect of spray water. Continued movement after mixing is insufficient evidence of compatibility because nematodes may lose their ability to infect the host.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Before mixing nematodes with any product, the following should be verified: 1. Compatibility of the active ingredient. 2. Compatibility of the commercial formulation. 3. pH. 4. Salinity. 5. Duration of the mixture in the tank. 6. Nozzle and filter type. 7. Temperature. 8. Effect of the mixture on infectivity. Separate application is preferable when clear data are unavailable. Nematodes should not automatically be mixed with fertilizers, acids, or pesticides merely to reduce the number of application operations.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Granuloviruses infect larvae after ingestion of occlusion bodies, whereas larval parasitoids may attack different instars. Studies on Tuta absoluta have shown that some combinations of virus with: Necremnus tutae. Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris. Were relatively highly compatible, with limited indirect effects on parasitoids under the study conditions. Potential complementarity results from: High viral efficacy against young larvae. Ability of some parasitoids to attack older instars. Different killing pathways. However, infection timing is important. If the virus kills the host before parasitoid development is completed, parasitoid emergence may decline.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Natural origin does not mean that a material is compatible with natural enemies. Oils, soaps, or botanical extracts may: Coat the body of a parasitoid or predator. Damage external membranes. Repel beneficial insects. Reduce oviposition. Inhibit fungal spores. Kill nematodes in the tank. Cause phytotoxicity. Responses may differ among: Raw material. Extract. Essential oil. Commercial product. Concentration. Some studies have recorded behavioural and reproductive effects of botanical materials despite low direct mortality.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Sex pheromones generally do not act as lethal toxins and are therefore among the tools most readily integrated with natural enemies. The following can be combined: Pheromone monitoring. Mass trapping. Mating disruption. Egg parasitoids. Larval parasitoids. Predators. Microbial agents. Pheromones target adults or sexual communication, while natural enemies target eggs and larvae. However, considerations include: Possible parasitoid responses to host odours. Effects of mating disruption on male distribution rather than females alone. Avoiding placement of release cards inside traps. Avoiding sticky surfaces near beneficial-insect release points. Potential capture of non-target organisms in non-selective traps. Species-specific sex pheromones remain more compatible than broadly attractive light traps.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Light traps differ from pheromone traps because they may attract: Parasitoids. Predators. Pollinators. Non-target nocturnal species. Intensive use may remove part of the beneficial-insect community. The following should be evaluated: List of captured species. Timing of light operation. Wavelength. Trap location. Ratio of Tuta to non-target organisms. A high total number of captured insects is not evidence that a light trap is compatible with IPM.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Interactions among natural enemies may include: Complementarity. Competition. Intraguild predation. Hyperparasitism. Multiparasitism. Killing of a parasitized host. Partitioning of stages or locations. Adding a second species should not be assumed to double efficacy. The following should be studied: Preferred stage of each species. Searching location on the plant. Activity timing. Ability to distinguish unparasitized from parasitized hosts. Density of alternative prey. Capacity of one species to prey on the other.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Egg parasitoids and predators may complement one another when: The parasitoid parasitizes part of the egg population. The predator consumes healthy eggs and hatching larvae. Alternative prey are available to the predator. The predator can distinguish parasitized eggs or consumes fewer of them. Conflict may occur when the predator consumes: Recently parasitized eggs. Eggs containing fully developed parasitoids. Minute adults after emergence. The predator’s capacity to discriminate varies with: Age of the parasitized egg. Changes in colour and odour. Predator species. Hunger level. Release timing should be coordinated to reduce losses.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: N. tutae may attack a larva already containing an internal parasitoid. Because it is an ectoparasitoid capable of paralyzing and host feeding, its attack may cause: Death of the endoparasitoid. Arrest of its development. Reduced successful emergence. Conflict becomes more likely when the two species target similar larval instars. Complementarity is more likely when: Preferred instars differ. Spatial distributions differ. The later-arriving species can discriminate parasitized hosts. Host density is sufficient.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Both species are omnivorous predators and share the following prey: Eggs. Newly hatched larvae. Whiteflies. Other prey. Interactions may include: Competition for food. Predation on small nymphs. Differences in establishment on the plant. Differences in plant damage. They should not be released together solely on the assumption that one will complement the other. In many programs, the species best suited to the climate and crop system is selected instead of establishing two high-density populations of omnivorous predators.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Mechanical removal is compatible with biological control when it targets: Severely infested fruits. Limited hotspots. Plants that cannot be saved. Postharvest residues. It may conflict with biological control when the following are removed: Leaves carrying parasitized eggs. Leaves bearing predators or nymphs. Larvae containing parasitoids. Plants supporting natural enemies. Workers should therefore be trained to distinguish among: Old mines. Active mines. Parasitized eggs. Beneficial life stages. Damage requiring removal.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Cultural practices support biological control when they include: Balanced fertilization. Regular irrigation. Protection of the canopy. Removal of confirmed pest hosts. Provision of non-host nectar plants. Dust reduction. Avoiding tillage or mowing at times that remove beneficial organisms. They may have negative effects when: Excess nitrogen alters the plant–pest balance. All flowering plants are removed. Tuta host weeds are retained under the pretext of supporting natural enemies. Extensive pruning removes predators. Severe water stress is imposed. No cultural practice is absolutely compatible. It should be evaluated according to its effect on the plant, pest, and natural enemy.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Temporal Separation Between an Insecticide and a Natural Enemy
text: When full compatibility is absent, temporal separation may be used. The interval should be based on: Residue half-life on leaves. IOBC test results. Natural-enemy supplier instructions. Weather conditions. Plant growth rate. Active ingredient and formulation. Life stage to be released. A general rule such as: Waiting three days. Or one week. Should not be used because an interval may be sufficient for one active ingredient and inadequate for another. The plan should specify: Date of the most recent spray. Active ingredient. Natural enemy. Earliest safe release date. Date for reassessing survival.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Natural enemies may be protected by: Treating the hotspot only. Avoiding treatment of areas with high parasitism. Using temporary barriers or covers over release points when instructions allow. Applying an active ingredient to the soil instead of foliage. Directing spray to specific plant parts. Using nozzles that reduce drift. Localized treatment is unsuitable when infestation is widespread or when leaving untreated sectors allows larvae to reach fruits.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Physiological Selectivity The active ingredient is more toxic to the pest than to the natural enemy because of differences in receptors, metabolism, or exposure. Ecological Selectivity This is achieved through application method, such as: Spraying when natural-enemy activity is low. Treating specific hotspots. Using baits. Treating soil. Selecting droplets that reduce drift. Temporal separation. An insecticide may lack physiological selectivity but its damage can be reduced through appropriate ecological selectivity.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Each project should establish a table linking all products and organisms used. The following codes may be used: Code Interpretation A Compatible according to documented data B Compatible under conditions or with a time interval C Incompatible; the combination should be avoided D Insufficient data F Requires a local field test Classification should not be based on impressions or marketing descriptions.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Two Methods Preliminary Expected Compatibility Principal Condition Pheromone + egg parasitoids High Use species-specific traps and do not place them near cards Pheromone + predators High Avoid non-selective light or sticky traps Bt + Trichogramma Relatively high Use a registered product and do not spray cards directly Bt + predators Relatively high Review adjuvants and direct exposure Baculovirus + larval parasitoids Relatively high Coordinate infection and parasitism timing Entomopathogenic fungus + larval parasitoid Conditional Test the strain and time interval Entomopathogenic fungus + fungicide Low or conditional Test spore viability and residues Nematodes + fertilizers or pesticides Unknown without testing Assess survival and infectivity Broad-spectrum insecticide + Trichogramma Generally low Avoid or use a documented time interval Broad-spectrum insecticide + mirids Generally low Use a selective alternative Fruit removal + natural enemies High Collect only severely infested fruits Leaf removal + Trichogramma Conditional Do not remove parasitized eggs Two omnivorous predators Conditional Evaluate competition and intraguild predation This table is a functional framework, not an approval document for a specific commercial product.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Steps for Evaluating Any New Combination
text: Before introducing two materials or agents together, the following sequence should be followed: 1. Define the objective of the combination. 2. Identify the target stage of each method. 3. Review their modes of action. 4. Review direct-toxicity and residual-effect data. 5. Assess sublethal effects. 6. Review physical compatibility in the spray tank. 7. Assess susceptibility of the strain or natural enemy used. 8. Conduct a limited test. 9. Measure both the pest and the natural enemy. 10. Expand application gradually. 11. Document the result. 12. Discontinue the combination if its net effect is lower than that of using the two methods separately.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: When preliminary mixing is permitted, a small-jar test should be conducted using: The same spray water. The same field-use proportions. The same order of addition. A similar waiting period. Observation for precipitation. Observation for clumping. Observation for separation and foaming. pH measurement where necessary. Successful completion of the jar test demonstrates only physical compatibility. It does not demonstrate: Survival of the fungus. Continued infectivity of the nematodes. Safety to the parasitoid. Absence of phytotoxicity. Retention of insecticidal activity.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: For living organisms, the following should be measured. Fungi Percentage of spore germination. Germ-tube growth. Colony-forming units. Pathogenicity after exposure. Nematodes Survival percentage. Movement. Ability to infect a test host. Survival after remaining in the tank for a defined period. Bacteria and Viruses Retention of insecticidal activity. Absence of degradation of active components. Infection efficacy after mixing. The external appearance of the solution is insufficient.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: The nursery program should include: 1. A complete pesticide record. 2. Identification of natural enemies planned for release. 3. Avoidance of long-residual products before release. 4. Use of lower-impact materials when necessary. 5. Isolation of treated batches. 6. Avoiding contact between biological-release cards or packages and pesticides. 7. Cleaning tanks of disinfectant residues. 8. Setting a time interval for each active ingredient. 9. Testing natural-enemy survival after release. 10. Providing the treatment record with the transplants. Compatibility management does not begin only after transplants reach the field because nursery residues may affect natural enemies released later.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: The basic steps include: Inventorying naturally occurring natural enemies. Recording all active ingredients. Reviewing the effect of each active ingredient on the dominant species. Using localized treatment where appropriate. Avoiding unnecessary spraying during peak parasitoid activity. Protecting supportive plant strips from spray drift.
