Separating application of entomopathogenic fungi from incompatible fungicides. Avoiding addition of fertilizers or acids to nematodes without testing. Reassessing natural enemies after treatment. Modifying the program when a severe population decline occurs.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Greenhouses allow greater control but require precise coordination because of intensive release programs. The program includes: A map of natural-enemy distribution. A record of fungicides and insecticides. Internal traps. Treating hotspots rather than the entire greenhouse where possible. Isolating heavily infested sectors. Providing supplementary food after materials that reduce prey. Monitoring plant damage caused by N. tenuis. Avoiding long-residual insecticides before a new release. Washing or replacing some surfaces or plant parts when hazardous residues persist. Assessing compatibility with tomato-disease programs.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: A Tuta program cannot be designed separately from management of: Blights. Powdery mildew. Grey mould. Bacterial diseases. Root diseases. Disease-management fungicides may conflict with: Beauveria. Metarhizium. Lecanicillium. Beneficial endophytic organisms. Some predators and parasitoids. A unified calendar should identify: Fungicidal active ingredient. Entomopathogenic fungus. Date of each application. Required interval. Application site. Disease or insect target. Conflict between programs should not be addressed by increasing doses or mixing all materials in one treatment.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Compatibility with Whitefly and Thrips Programs
text: Active ingredients used against whiteflies or thrips may severely affect natural enemies used against Tuta absoluta. A treatment directed at another pest may: Kill Macrolophus or Nesidiocoris. Reduce Trichogramma. Eliminate Orius. Disrupt the release program. The tomato-pest program should therefore be evaluated as one integrated system. Selecting a generalist predator attacking several pests may be more beneficial than using separate sequences of insecticides, provided its plant-feeding risks are managed.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Protecting natural enemies contributes to insecticide-resistance management by: Reducing the number of applications. Killing individuals that survive insecticide exposure. Targeting different pest stages. Reducing the size of the population subjected to selection. Extending the useful life of chemical groups. Conversely, use of an incompatible insecticide may cause: Loss of the natural enemy. Pest increase after residues decline. Need for repeated sprays. Greater resistance pressure. Insecticide selectivity is therefore not only an environmental issue but a central part of resistance management.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: A combination of methods may be biologically compatible but economically impractical. The following should be calculated: Cost of control agents. Release cost. Number of applications. Labor. Required time intervals. Sprays avoided. Reduction in fruit infestation. Value of acceptable yield. Cost of re-establishing a natural enemy after insecticide use. The least expensive chemical product may be more costly over the whole season if it kills natural enemies and causes the field to require several later interventions.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

sectionTitle: Indicators of a Successful Compatibility Program
text: A program is successful when it achieves: Survival of natural enemies after intervention. Continued parasitism or predation. Reduction in new mines. Reduction in fruit infestation. Fewer sprays. No pest resurgence. No outbreak of secondary pests. Successful performance of microbial agents. Fewer tank-mix failures. A record explaining every decision. Positive economic return.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Date Sector Product Used Active Ingredient or Organism Natural Enemy Present Exposure Type Compatibility Rating Time Interval Post-Application Result The record should also include: Commercial formulation. Dose. Temperature. Natural-enemy stage. Density before treatment. Density after treatment. Parasitism or predation rate. Fruit infestation. Any apparent sublethal effects.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Error One: Assuming Every Biopesticide Is Safe to Natural Enemies Fungi, oils, or extracts may affect parasitoids and predators. Error Two: Judging Compatibility from Mortality Alone Strong behavioural and reproductive effects may occur. Error Three: Transferring Results from One Species to Another Natural-enemy susceptibility differs among species. Error Four: Transferring Results from an Active Ingredient to All Commercial Formulations Adjuvants may alter toxicity. Error Five: Treating a Successful Jar Test as Evidence of Biological Compatibility It demonstrates physical compatibility only. Error Six: Mixing an Entomopathogenic Fungus with a Fungicide Spores may die before application. Error Seven: Mixing Nematodes with Fertilizers or Disinfectants Without Testing They may lose movement and infectivity. Error Eight: Releasing Trichogramma on Leaves Carrying Hazardous Residues Adults may die immediately after emergence. Error Nine: Removing Parasitized Leaves This causes loss of parasitoids. Error Ten: Using an Insecticide Against Another Pest Without Considering Its Effect on the Tuta Program It may disrupt biological control. Error Eleven: Combining Several Predators Without Study Competition or intraguild predation may occur. Error Twelve: Using One Fixed Time Interval for All Insecticides Residual persistence differs among active ingredients. Error Thirteen: Relying on the Term “Selective” in Marketing Claims The natural-enemy species, life stage, and test method should be known. Error Fourteen: Failing to Monitor Natural Enemies After Treatment Program effects cannot be determined without measurement. Error Fifteen: Treating Compatibility as a Permanent Property It may change with dose, temperature, formulation, and application method.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Scientific priorities are: 1. Establish a local database on the effects of registered insecticides on natural enemies. 2. Test T. achaeae, T. euproctidis, and local strains. 3. Evaluate N. tenuis and M. pygmaeus under Egyptian temperatures. 4. Do not transfer classifications from products used in other countries without matching formulation and dose. 5. Establish time-interval tables before natural-enemy release. 6. Include disease-control products in compatibility assessment. 7. Test copper and sulfur with the fungal strains used. 8. Evaluate nematode viability in local irrigation and spray water. 9. Analyze the effects of water salinity, bicarbonates, and disinfectants. 10. Protect naturally occurring natural enemies in open fields. 11. Use localized treatments where appropriate. 12. Prevent direct spray on parasitoid cards and release points. 13. Train workers to recognize parasitized eggs. 14. Include nursery applications in the field record. 15. Measure sublethal effects, not mortality alone. 16. Link compatibility to reduced fruit infestation and economic return. 17. Do not describe any product as “safe” without specifying the natural enemy and life stage. 18. Update compatibility tables when registrations and commercial formulations change.
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: Compatibility among control methods is not an absolute binary property. It is an outcome that changes according to: Active ingredient. Commercial formulation. Dose. Application method. Natural enemy. Its life stage. Timing. Environmental conditions. Exposure pathway. Acute toxicity, sublethal effects, and residues should all be assessed rather than direct mortality alone. IOBC methods provide a standardized framework for assessing effects on natural enemies, but laboratory results should not be transferred directly to field conditions without semi-field and field tests. Egg parasitoids, particularly Trichogramma, are sensitive to direct exposure and residues. Insecticide applications should therefore be coordinated with card placement and adult emergence. The susceptibility of Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis to the same active ingredient also differs, and a product cannot be considered safe to mirids as one undifferentiated group. Bt is among the agents most readily integrated with egg parasitoids and predators in many programs because it targets larvae after ingestion, while the specific product and adjuvants still require assessment. Recent studies have shown relatively good compatibility between B. bassiana, Bt, and some parasitoids of Tuta larvae, whereas other materials have shown clear toxicity or behavioural and reproductive effects. Granuloviruses show relatively high compatibility with some larval parasitoids because they target different larval ages, but the sequence of infection and parasitism remains important. Entomopathogenic fungi should be carefully coordinated with disease-control fungicides because fungicide residues may prevent spore germination even without direct tank mixing. Nematodes should not be mixed with an insecticide, fertilizer, or disinfectant unless their movement and infectivity remain verified. Every IPM program should include a compatibility matrix and a chronological record specifying: Products used. Natural enemies. Time intervals. Exposure route. Field outcomes. An integrated program succeeds not through the number of its components but through the ability of those components to function together without one undermining another. References
partTitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

text: A grower may implement a precise and integrated management program within a field, only for infestation to recur rapidly because moths migrate from an old tomato field, an infested nursery, or abandoned crop residues in the surrounding area. This situation shows that the movement boundaries of Tuta absoluta do not coincide with the boundaries of land ownership. The insect does not stop at: The end of a field. The boundary of a farm. A separating road. A change in owner. A change in company or contracting system. The end of a spray program in one field. Areas with overlapping tomato production cycles therefore require collective management targeting the pest population within a defined production landscape. Area-wide management does not mean standardizing all operations or spraying every field with the same insecticide. It means coordinating actions that fail when implemented separately by individual producers, such as: Removing old fields. Establishing an early-warning network. Sharing trap data. Preventing movement of infested transplants. Managing sorting and processing waste. Coordinating insecticide mode-of-action windows. Managing regional hotspots. Reducing the green bridge between production cycles.

sectionTitle: Definition of Area-Wide Integrated Pest Management
text: Area-wide integrated pest management, or: Area-Wide Integrated Pest Management — AW-IPM Is the application of an integrated program targeting the entire pest population within a defined geographical area, regardless of boundaries between fields or owners. This concept differs from conventional management focused on protecting an individual field. Conventional Field-Level Management It asks: What is the infestation level in this field? Does this field require intervention? What is the best treatment for this sector? Area-Wide Management It adds further questions: Where are the moths coming from? Which fields produce the largest numbers of emigrating individuals? Are production cycles overlapping? Are nurseries or sorting stations acting as sources? Is the region repeatedly using one insecticide group? Can the regional population be reduced before the next production cycle begins? Field-level decisions remain important but become part of a wider system.

sectionTitle: Why Does Tuta absoluta Require Regional Management?
text: The regional approach is important because of the following pest characteristics: Adult insects can move among fields. Oviposition continues as long as hosts are available. Multiple generations occur during the year. Several cultivated and wild hosts are present. The pest moves with transplants and plant material. Larvae survive inside fruits and residues. Moths emerge from fields after harvest has ended. Old and new production cycles overlap. Resistance spreads through movement of individuals. Field edges near infestation sources are highly affected. Field-level control alone becomes less sustainable when reinvasion from outside continues.

A management area should not be defined only by administrative boundaries, but by the unit within which the pest population moves and host sources are connected. The unit may be: A cluster of adjacent fields. A large agricultural project. An agricultural cooperative. An irrigation district. A tomato-production belt. A connected group of nurseries and fields. A factory supply zone. A group of agriculturally connected villages. The area may extend across more than one administrative district when fields, hosts, and transport operations are interconnected.

Level One: Within the Field This includes: Hotspots. Field edges. Cultivars. Transplanting sectors. Wind direction. Loading points. Level Two: The Farm This includes: Different fields. Nursery. Storage. Sorting station. Waste. Shared equipment. Level Three: The Agricultural Block This includes adjacent fields that exchange moths, equipment, and workers. Level Four: The Production Region This includes: Villages or cooperatives. Factories or sorting stations. Commercial nurseries. Markets and collection centers. Transport routes for crops and transplants. A program does not need to begin at the scale of an entire governorate. It may start within a manageable production block and expand gradually.

A source field is a field that produces more pest individuals than it receives and then exports moths to the surrounding area. A field may become a source when: It is old and heavily infested. The control program has stopped. It remains green after becoming uneconomic. It contains infested fruits and leaves. Overlapping generations are developing. It is removed gradually while living plants remain. A nearby young crop is attractive to moths. A source field need not be the highest-yielding or largest field. A small neglected area may produce large numbers of moths.

A recipient field receives pest individuals from surrounding sources. Young fields are particularly likely to receive moths when: They are close to an old field. They contain tender new growth. They lie downwind from the source. They lack traps or early monitoring. Their edges adjoin residues or alternative hosts. They are planted when the old crop is being removed. A recipient field may initially have low internal infestation, but external migration pressure accelerates pest establishment.

sectionTitle: Source and Sink Are Not Fixed Attributes
text: A field’s role may change during the season. A new field begins as a recipient, then becomes a source for newer crops after the pest reproduces within it. The sequence is: 1. Moths move from an old field. 2. Eggs are laid in the new field. 3. An internal generation develops. 4. Infestation increases. 5. Field harvest ends. 6. Control stops. 7. The field becomes a new source. Repeated cycles maintain the pest throughout the year.

The green bridge is the spatial and temporal continuity of plants capable of supporting the pest between seasons. It consists of: Overlapping tomato crops. Continuously productive greenhouses. Nurseries. Suitable potato or eggplant crops. Volunteer tomato plants. Wild solanaceous hosts. Old fields that have not been terminated. Fruits and residues that allow larvae to continue developing. The more continuous the green bridge, the less effective crop rotation in one individual field becomes.

Active Movement Moths move among plants and fields while searching for: Mating partners. Oviposition sites. New hosts. Shelter. Movement is affected by: Wind. Direction of host sources. Insect age. Mating status. Temperature. Agricultural-landscape structure. Passive or Human-Assisted Movement The pest may be transported with: Infested transplants. Trays. Fruits containing larvae. Leaves and crop residues. Harvest crates. Machinery. Vehicles. Packaging materials. Human-assisted movement is particularly important between distant areas where local flight alone cannot explain pest arrival.

There is no single universal isolation distance that can be adopted for Tuta absoluta under all environments. A fixed distance is difficult to define because of: Differences in wind speed. Differences in agricultural-landscape structure. Differences in host density. Moth behaviour. Difficulty separating active flight from human-assisted movement. Effects of traps on movement measurements. Scarcity of direct local field studies measuring dispersal distance. No specific distance can therefore be claimed to guarantee complete safety. The practical risk zone should be defined using: Trap networks. Direction of increase in catches. Field age. Host distribution. Appearance of eggs on edges. Movement of transplants and residues.

Spatial studies have shown that Tuta absoluta eggs and damage may have an aggregated rather than uniformly random distribution in open fields. This means that: Hotspots may appear before the field average increases. Spatial inspection is important. One sampling point is insufficient. Edges should be separated from the interior. Early localized intervention may be possible. Infestation maps are valuable. Aggregated distribution does not mean a hotspot remains fixed. It may expand or new hotspots may appear as females move.

Edges adjacent to a potential infestation source are among the first locations that should be inspected. High-risk edges include those: Facing an old tomato field. Near a nursery. Adjacent to a sorting station. Near piles of residues. Bordering dense wild hosts. Facing the prevailing wind from the source. The following should be compared: Edge catches. Interior catches. Eggs. Active mines. Growing-point infestation. An increase restricted to the edge may indicate external migration before infestation spreads internally.

The area should be defined through practical steps: 1. Inventory all tomato fields. 2. Record field ages. 3. Identify nurseries. 4. Identify greenhouses. 5. Record potato, eggplant, and important alternative hosts. 6. Identify sorting and collection stations. 7. Identify factories and waste locations. 8. Map transport routes for transplants and produce. 9. Identify old fields and their removal dates. 10. Analyze trap results. An operational zone should then be drawn to include sources and recipient fields requiring coordination.

The map should include: Code for each field. Farm or responsible person. Area. Transplanting date. Cultivar. Production purpose. Crop stage. Expected harvest date. Expected removal date. Trap locations. Infestation level. Most recent insecticide group used. Presence of a biological-control program. Waste sources. Alternative hosts. The map may be prepared using: Geographic information systems. Mobile applications. Satellite imagery. An updated paper map where resources are limited. The essential requirement is current data, not sophisticated software.

Each field should receive a permanent code, such as: Region – Farm – Field Number – Season Example: NUB-A07-TOM-2026 The code should be linked to all data on: Traps. Inspections. Pesticides. Natural enemies. Harvest. Residues. Failure cases. Informal names such as “Ahmed’s big field” should be avoided when they could cause confusion in records.

The trap network aims to: Detect the beginning of activity early. Compare sectors. Determine the direction of migration waves. Identify source fields. Issue warnings to young fields. Evaluate the effect of old-crop removal. The network should not be used to issue one uniform spray decision for all fields. The following should be standardized: Trap type. Lure type. Installation date. Trap height. Reading time. Method of recording counts. Lure-replacement date. Site code. When these elements differ, comparisons among fields become unreliable.

Regional Warning Traps These are placed at strategic locations to measure the general trend, such as: Entry to the region. Near nurseries. Near old fields. Near factories or sorting stations. Corridors between production cycles. Field Traps These are used inside each field to monitor local activity. Regional warning traps do not replace field traps because an internal field hotspot may not be evident in a distant regional trap.

Traps should be read on fixed days each week. The following should be recorded: Total catch. Number of exposure days. Trap condition. Lure condition. Surface saturation. Drying of water. Maintenance date. When exposure periods differ, calculate: Mean daily catch = Total males captured ÷ Number of days the trap was active A trap operating for seven days should not be compared with one operating for three days using raw counts alone.

Sex-pheromone traps are insufficient because male catches do not directly measure: Females. Eggs. Larvae. Fruit infestation. Indicator or reference fields should therefore be selected in each sector and inspected using a standardized method covering: Eggs. Active mines. Larval instars. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation. Natural enemies. Reference fields should preferably represent: Different production cycles. Different cultivars. Locations near and far from sources. Different management systems.

Measurement units should be standardized. Possible units include: Males/trap/day. Eggs/plant. Percentage of plants carrying eggs. Active mines/plant. Live larvae/plant. Percentage of infested growing points. Percentage of fruits infested by number. Percentage weight loss. Old and new mines should not be combined in a single decision-making value.

A multi-level descriptive system may be used. Green Level Activity is low or stable. No confirmed plant infestation is present. No major active sources are present. Action: Continue monitoring. Yellow Level Limited increase in trap catches. Appearance of eggs or limited hotspots. Presence of a nearby old field. Action: Increase inspection. Warn young fields. Review the risk source. Orange Level Repeated increases in trap catches. Active mines in several fields. A wave of old-crop removal. Weak natural-enemy activity. Action: Coordinate field interventions. Manage source fields. Review resistance-management programs. Red Level Regional spread. Increased fruit infestation. Multiple active sources. Repeated control failure. Action: Implement an emergency plan. Intensify monitoring. Terminate uneconomic sources. Investigate resistance. Prevent movement of infested transplants or residues. The colour categories are levels of organization and response, not uniform spray thresholds.

The bulletin should include: A map of risk levels. Catch trend compared with the previous week. Potential source fields. Dominant larval stages. Areas where hatching is beginning. Natural-enemy status. Dates for removal of old fields. Resistance warnings. Inspection recommendations. Restrictions related to harvest and residues. The bulletin should not recommend one commercial product for the entire region without inspecting each field.

Information loses value if it arrives after eggs have become larvae inside fruits. The system should define: Reading day. Data-entry deadline. Person responsible for review. Warning-release time. Communication channel. Person confirming receipt. Possible communication tools include: Professional communication groups. Text messages. Agricultural applications. Electronic dashboards. Direct calls to high-risk growers.

Data should be reviewed to detect: An implausible number. A dry trap. An expired lure. A missing reading. A changed trap location. Confusion between old and active mines. An incorrect measurement unit. Delayed entry of treatment records. Regional decisions should not be based on unverified data. Monitoring teams should preferably be trained using: Standardized photographs. Reference samples. Cross-calibration visits among inspectors. Periodic assessment of counting consistency.

A calendar should show for each week: Active nurseries. Transplanting dates. Vegetative growth. Flowering and fruit set. Beginning of ripening. Harvest. Crop removal. The most important risk periods occur when the following coincide: An old field is ending. Control has stopped in it. A nearby young field is present. Temperature is rising. Trap catches are increasing. The calendar can be used to predict migration waves before they occur.

Coordination does not mean planting the entire region on one day. It means reducing overlap that permits continuous pest movement. The following should be considered: Factory requirements. Transplant availability. Climatic risks. Distribution of harvest. Labor availability. Cultivar cycle length. It may be preferable to establish clear temporal blocks rather than planting a new crop every week beside a field approaching termination.

A host-free period is more effective when it covers most of the region rather than one individual field. It requires removal of: Commercial tomato. Volunteer tomato plants. Old fields. Surplus transplants. Confirmed solanaceous hosts. Sorting waste. The period is not achieved if infested greenhouses or nurseries remain active without isolation and management. Its duration should be determined according to: Temperature. Pupal survival. Production system. Alternative hosts. Trap data. No single duration is suitable for all regions.

sectionTitle: Protocol for Terminating an Old Field
text: Termination begins when harvesting is no longer economically viable. It includes: 1. Defining the final harvest. 2. Notifying neighboring fields. 3. Collecting remaining fruits. 4. Avoiding cessation of control while leaving plants alive for a long period. 5. Terminating plants within a short time window. 6. Shredding the canopy. 7. Containing or treating residues. 8. Managing the soil according to the production system. 9. Removing regrowth. 10. Continuing trap monitoring after removal. The effect of termination should be assessed through surrounding traps during the following period.

sectionTitle: Why Should Neighboring Fields Be Notified?
text: The following operations may cause adult moths to search for a new host: Final harvesting. Stopping irrigation. Drying. Cutting plants. Removing host weeds. When neighboring fields know the timing of these operations, they can: Increase inspection. Review traps. Inspect the facing edge. Prepare for egg-parasitoid release. Apply an early localized intervention if necessary. Notification does not mean automatic preventive spraying.

An abandoned field is one in which production or care has stopped while living plants or infested residues remain. The regional plan should include: Identification of the responsible person. Documentation of the site. Estimation of infestation level. A deadline for removal. Provision of shared equipment when necessary. Follow-up of implementation. Regulatory action in large projects or contractual systems. The region cannot be protected if highly infested fields remain without clear responsibility.

Nurseries are central points because one batch can distribute infestation to many fields. The regional system requires: Registration of nurseries. Use of screens. Internal and external traps. Inspection of every batch. Pesticide records. Isolation of new batches. Prohibition of sale of infested transplants. An inspection certificate or traceability record. Sanitary disposal of rejected transplants. Each field should be linked to its transplant source.

Each batch should carry a code identifying: Nursery name. Sowing date. Cultivar. Dispatch date. Pesticides used. Result of Tuta inspection. Fields receiving the batch. If early infestation appears in several fields supplied by the same batch, the nursery can be identified as a potential source and investigated rapidly.

Transplants should be moved in vehicles that are: Clean. Covered when necessary. Free of tomato residues. Not used immediately beforehand to transport infested residues. Not parked beside old fields for long periods. Trays should be inspected on arrival and should not enter the field directly when suspicious symptoms are present.

Sorting stations may receive infested fruits from multiple fields and accumulate: Larvae. Leaves. Calyces. Rejected fruits. Contaminated crates. The station requires: A closed or controlled waste area. Daily cleaning. Prevention of fruit disposal around the site. Monitoring traps. Cleaning of crates. Waste treatment. Prevention of volunteer tomato growth. Risk increases when the station is adjacent to a nursery or young crop.

Live larvae may reach the factory inside fruits. Waste arises from: Reception. Sorting. Washing. Conveyor systems. Rejected fruits. Solid materials. Untreated waste should not be returned to fields or left in open piles. The factory plan should include: Collection. Transport. Treatment. Controlled thermophilic composting. Anaerobic digestion or another suitable industrial use. Control of volunteer tomatoes around the site.

Infested plant material may be transferred with: Plastic crates. Cages. Pallets. Collection bags. Transport carts. These should be: Cleaned after every cycle. Prevented from moving directly from old fields to nurseries. Managed through a clean-container route. Accompanied by collection of cleaning waste. Traced by location in advanced systems.

This includes: Harvesting machines. Shredders. Tractors. Crop trailers. Pruning equipment. Movement should be ordered, where possible, from: 1. The least-infested fields. 2. Then moderately infested fields. 3. Then old or highly infested fields. Equipment should be cleaned before returning to a young field. Cleaning should include conveyors, corners, and areas where leaves and fruits accumulate.

sectionTitle: Regional Management of Weeds and Alternative Hosts
text: One grower may remove hosts from the field while high densities remain on: Drains. Roads. Neglected land. Factory surroundings. Canal banks. Cooperative boundaries. Joint campaigns are therefore needed to identify and remove confirmed hosts. Infested plants should not be removed randomly and left on the ground. They should be collected or treated. Non-host plants supporting natural enemies should be selectively conserved.

Biological control improves when neighboring fields avoid simultaneous use of highly toxic insecticides. Management includes: Identifying naturally occurring species. Protecting supportive vegetation. Coordinating selective active ingredients. Sharing release dates. Avoiding spray on flowering edges. Preventing drift from one field to another. Establishing sources for recolonization by natural enemies. A field using a broad-spectrum insecticide may disrupt a release program in a neighboring field through drift or movement of natural enemies between fields.

Release over a connected area may be more efficient than separate releases when: Organism quality is standardized. Timing is coordinated. Incompatible insecticides are reduced. Release points are distributed according to the infestation map. Regional parasitism is monitored. Large-scale release programs require: A reliable supplier. An appropriate transport chain. Training. Shared costs. Batch records. Impact evaluation. A non-native natural enemy should not be released without formal approval and environmental assessment.

Resistance develops within a pest population that moves among fields, so rotation within one field alone is insufficient. Management requires: Sharing the IRAC groups used. Preventing continuous regional repetition of the same group. Including nursery sprays. Monitoring failure cases. Collecting samples from hotspots. Issuing warnings when reduced susceptibility is confirmed. Preventing movement of transplants from unmanaged resistance hotspots. Coordinating mode-of-action windows. Coordination does not mean every field uses the same active ingredient. It means preventing continuous pressure on one group across the region.

Fields differ in: Transplanting date. Crop stage. Infestation level. Harvest date. Natural enemies. Previously used active ingredients. A single insecticide schedule therefore cannot be issued for all fields. Coordination is achieved by: Identifying groups that should not be repeated. Sharing previous exposure history. Proposing several alternatives. Considering each field’s status. Avoiding use of one group against successive generations across the region where possible.

In some situations, monitoring or intervention may be directed to high-risk edges instead of treating the entire field. Edge management includes: Increasing inspection points. Installing additional traps. Removing infested hosts. Collecting fruits and residues. Releasing parasitoids when eggs are present. Applying selective localized treatment when infestation is confirmed. Monitoring the direction of mine spread. Edge treatment should not be calendar-based when there is no evidence of migration or establishment.

sectionTitle: Response to a New Regional Hotspot
text: When a sudden increase appears in a specific area: 1. Verify identification and counts. 2. Identify affected fields. 3. Increase inspection density around the hotspot. 4. Identify the transplant source. 5. Inspect old fields. 6. Inspect sorting stations and waste sites. 7. Determine pest life stages. 8. Review previous insecticides. 9. Prevent movement of infested material. 10. Apply an integrated intervention in the hotspot. 11. Monitor surrounding fields. 12. Issue a targeted warning rather than an exaggerated general alert.

There is no single global number defining a regional outbreak. Operationally, it is identified through the convergence of several indicators, such as: Increased trap catches in multiple sectors. Infestation in fields of different ages. Increased eggs and active mines. Increased fruit infestation. Repeated control failure. Multiple unmanaged sources. Spread beyond the original hotspot. An outbreak should not be declared from one high trap count without plant inspection.

The emergency plan should include: A leadership team. A current map. Inspection teams. An identification and susceptibility laboratory. Rapid communication. Equipment for waste disposal. A list of registered tools. A natural-enemy protection program. Coordination with nurseries and factories. Daily or near-daily hotspot follow-up. The plan should focus on reducing the source population rather than increasing uncoordinated spraying.

Grower Inspect the field. Maintain traps. Record treatments. Report increases. Remove residues. Avoid selling infested transplants or materials. Agricultural Engineer Review data. Confirm diagnosis. Determine the decision. Follow compatibility and resistance management. Train workers. Nursery Produce pest-free transplants. Provide traceability records. Isolate batches. Dispose of rejected plants safely. Factory or Sorting Station Manage waste. Clean containers. Prevent hotspots around the facility. Share reception schedules. Cooperative or Organizing Company Coordinate the monitoring network. Issue warnings. Organize removal of old fields. Collect data. Support shared equipment. Regulatory Authorities Registration and oversight. Plant quarantine. Pesticide monitoring. Diagnostic support. Regulation of natural-enemy movement. Investigation of spread and resistance.

The program requires one clearly defined coordinating body. Its responsibilities include: Approving monitoring methods. Training teams. Reviewing data. Issuing risk levels. Coordinating source removal. Managing disputes. Monitoring compliance. Evaluating annual results. The program fails when several organizations issue contradictory instructions without a single technical reference.

Growers may hesitate to report infestation because of concerns about: Reputation. Crop rejection. Liability. Regulatory intervention. The system should therefore: Use data for pest management. Protect commercial information. Present general maps without unnecessarily identifying growers. Encourage early reporting. Distinguish technical reporting from deliberate non-compliance. Prevent unfair use of data against cooperating growers. Concealing a hotspot harms the entire region.

A grower may bear the cost of removing an old field while most of the benefit accrues to neighboring growers. This creates the problem of: Individual cost versus collective benefit Possible solutions include: Subsidizing shredding operations. Providing shared equipment. Incentives for early crop termination. Contractual requirements. Reducing trap costs. Collective purchase of natural enemies. Linking delivery acceptance to compliance with records. A shared fund for hotspot management. Collective action cannot rely on awareness alone when economic incentives are misaligned.

Lack of cooperation by a limited number of fields may lead to: More sprays in the region. Accelerated resistance. Increased fruit infestation. Rejected shipments. Higher residues. Collapse of biological control. Movement of the pest into new production cycles. Higher production costs for everyone. These costs should be quantified using regional records.

sectionTitle: Performance Indicators for the Regional Program
text: Biological indicators include: Regional mean trap catch. Number of new hotspots. Duration of source-field persistence. Mean egg and active-mine density. Percentage of infested fruits. Natural-enemy activity. Operational indicators include: Percentage of registered fields. Percentage of traps read on time. Warning-delivery time. Percentage of old fields removed on schedule. Number of inspected nurseries. Quantity of waste treated. Economic indicators include: Number of sprays. Control cost. Acceptable yield. Rejection rate. Cost per ton. Residue-limit violations. Resistance indicators include: IRAC groups used. Frequency of each group. Number of failure cases. Susceptibility-test results.

Performance should be compared with a baseline established before regional management begins. The comparison should include: Date of first annual appearance. Highest trap catch. Duration of flight waves. Number of applications. Fruit infestation. Yield. Control cost. Number of source fields. Participation rate. Improvement should not be attributed to the program alone without considering differences in: Climate. Area planted. Planting dates. Prices. Cultivars. Pest pressure among seasons.

Indicator Northern Sector Central Sector Southern Sector Active fields Mean traps/day Change from previous week Percentage of fields with eggs Percentage of fields with active mines Highest fruit infestation Existing old fields Fields scheduled for removal Natural enemies Warning level Regional action
Item Data Field code Location Crop age Date of final harvest Infestation level Trap catch Remaining fruits Most recent treatment Adjacent young fields Removal date Responsible person Follow-up result

Warning level: Orange Repeated increases in male Tuta absoluta catches were recorded this week in the eastern sector, coinciding with the beginning of removal of several old tomato fields and the appearance of fresh eggs on the edges of two newly transplanted fields. Required actions: Inspect edges facing old fields. Record eggs and active mines. Review traps and lures. Notify the team before removing any old field. Do not conduct a general spray based on traps alone. Make decisions for each field according to inspection and dominant life stage. Review the IRAC group used in the previous window.

sectionTitle: Operational Model for an Egyptian Processing-Tomato Region
text: The region may be divided into sectors, each containing: A technical supervisor. A trap network. Reference fields. A production-cycle register. A list of old fields. A data-collection point. A communication channel. Shared waste-management equipment. Weekly Cycle Day One Read traps. Inspect reference fields. Day Two Review data. Identify hotspots. Visit abnormal fields. Day Three Issue the risk map. Notify nurseries and fields. Remaining Days Implement actions. Follow source fields. Evaluate response. The cycle should not be tied rigidly to one weekday when rapid pest development under high temperatures requires more frequent follow-up.

A supply contract may require: Registration of transplanting date. Disclosure of transplant source. Installation of traps. Pesticide records. Compliance with resistance-management groups. Reporting infestation. Field removal after delivery. Prohibition of dumping sorting waste. Compliance with preharvest intervals. Permission for technical inspection. In return, the factory or company may provide: Monitoring services. Weekly warnings. Training. Support for traps. Shared shredding equipment. Residue analyses. Compliance incentives.

GIS assists with: Mapping field distribution. Determining distances among production cycles. Analyzing spread direction. Linking traps with crop age. Identifying risk zones. Planning reference-field locations. Directing inspection teams. Evaluating intervention effects. A map does not replace field data, and its accuracy depends on: Correct coordinates. Current crop-status information. Standardized readings. Diagnostic quality.

Aerial or satellite images can assist with: Locating tomato fields. Monitoring field removal. Detecting continued green cover. Estimating growth stage. Directing visits. They cannot confirm Tuta absoluta infestation because reduced vegetation may result from: Disease. Salinity. Water shortage. Harvest. Plant senescence. Other pests. Suspicion must be confirmed through field inspection.

Image-analysis applications may classify leaves or estimate damage severity, but limitations include: Similarity of mines to other damage. Variation in lighting. Leaf overlap. Inability to consistently distinguish active from old mines. Cultivar differences. Need for local training datasets. These tools should support inspectors rather than replace full field diagnosis.

Temperature data help estimate: Egg development. Hatching date. Generation completion. Timing for intensified inspection. Timing of biological releases. It is preferable to use: Distributed weather stations. Local temperature data. Temperatures close to the crop. A model validated in the region. Regional spray decisions should not be based on degree-days alone but integrated with traps and field inspection.

Each field may be linked to the most recent IRAC group used, producing a map showing: Intensity of group 5 use. Intensity of group 6 use. Diamide use. Areas of active-ingredient failure. Sites for susceptibility sampling. The map helps prevent continued pressure from one group within the same sector. Commercially sensitive details should not be disclosed without appropriate data protection.

At the end of the season, the review should ask: What were the principal infestation sources? When did the first wave begin? Did warnings arrive on time? Which fields became sources? Why was their removal delayed? Did the trap network perform adequately? Which chemical groups were most used? Were failure cases recorded? How did natural enemies perform? What was the percentage of infested fruits? What was the cost of the program? The results should be converted into clear modifications for the following season.

Error One: Treating Every Field as an Independent Unit The pest moves among fields and facilities. Error Two: Standardizing the Spray Decision Across the Entire Region Infestation, life stage, and crop stage differ among fields. Error Three: Relying on Traps Without Plant Inspection Male catch alone does not measure damage. Error Four: Comparing Counts from Different Trap and Lure Types This weakens the validity of comparisons. Error Five: Reading Traps at Irregular Intervals Without Adjusting Exposure Duration This produces misleading numbers. Error Six: Neglecting Nurseries They may distribute infestation over a wide area. Error Seven: Leaving Old Fields After Control Stops They become migration sources. Error Eight: Removing Fields Without Notifying Neighbours This may cause them to miss the timing of early inspection. Error Nine: Managing Waste Within Fields but Neglecting Sorting Stations or Factories The source may shift outside the field. Error Ten: Establishing a Host-Free Period on One Farm Only It fails if hosts continue in the surrounding region. Error Eleven: Standardizing the Insecticide Instead of Coordinating Modes of Action This may increase regional resistance. Error Twelve: Declaring an Emergency from One High Trap Count Spread should be confirmed through plant inspection. Error Thirteen: Collecting Data Without an Organization to Analyze Them Unused data do not provide an early warning. Error Fourteen: Delayed Warning Issuance The message may arrive after larvae have entered the plant. Error Fifteen: Penalizing Early Reporting This encourages growers to conceal hotspots. Error Sixteen: Ignoring Economic Incentives A grower may not implement an action benefiting neighbours if bearing the full cost alone.

The priorities are: 1. Define production blocks for processing and fresh-market tomatoes. 2. Register fields, nurseries, and crop schedules. 3. Establish a standardized trap network. 4. Select reference fields for plant inspection. 5. Issue a weekly risk bulletin. 6. Identify old fields before control stops. 7. Notify neighbours before field removal. 8. Support shredding and field termination. 9. Regulate waste from sorting stations and factories. 10. Trace transplant batches. 11. Clean shared containers and machinery. 12. Inventory solanaceous hosts along edges and drains. 13. Protect natural enemies at the production-block level. 14. Coordinate IRAC groups. 15. Establish a system for reporting insecticide failure. 16. Collect resistance samples from high-risk areas. 17. Link technical compliance to supply contracts. 18. Provide incentives to growers who remove source fields. 19. Introduce digital mapping gradually. 20. Evaluate the program by fruit infestation and cost, not trap counts alone.

Tuta absoluta moves among fields, nurseries, greenhouses, sorting stations, and factories. The boundaries of the pest population therefore do not coincide with the boundaries of land ownership. Area-wide management aims to target the pest population within an interconnected geographical area while field-level intervention decisions continue to be based on actual field conditions. The principal components of regional management include: Identifying source and recipient fields. Breaking the green bridge. Coordinating production-cycle timing. Removing old fields. Establishing a standardized trap network. Plant inspection of reference fields. Issuing rapid warnings. Managing nurseries and transplants. Containing sorting and processing waste. Cleaning containers and equipment. Protecting natural enemies. Coordinating insecticide-resistance management. Spatial studies have shown that Tuta absoluta eggs and damage have an aggregated pattern in open fields, supporting the value of mapping, hotspot inspection, and edge monitoring. There is no single isolation distance that guarantees prevention of pest movement. Risk zones should instead be defined through traps, inspection, host distribution, field age, and movement routes of transplants and residues. Old fields in which harvesting or control has stopped while plants remain alive are among the most important sources of reinvasion of young crops. Nurseries, sorting stations, and factories are also central points capable of spreading the pest when traceability, sanitation, and containment are absent. Collective action does not mean standardizing insecticides or implementing regional spraying. It means coordinating shared actions while preserving field-level decisions based on monitoring. The program requires clear technical leadership, standardized data, economic incentives, rapid communication, and indicators measuring infestation, yield, cost, and resistance. References

Tuta absoluta is not managed through a single treatment or a fixed spray schedule. It is managed through a sequential programme that begins before transplants reach the field and continues until the final crop residues are disposed of. Management objectives change during the season. At the beginning, the objectives are to: Prevent pest entry. Reduce the initial population. Detect eggs and early hotspots. During vegetative growth and fruiting, the objectives shift to: Prevent population build-up across generations. Protect growing points and clusters. Prevent larvae from moving into fruits. Conserve natural enemies. At the end of the season, the objectives are to: Reduce fruit infestation. Comply with preharvest intervals. Prevent the field from becoming a source for the next crop. Remove residues and remaining pest stages. The field programme is not a rigid calendar tied to fixed days after transplanting because the rate of pest development varies with: Temperature. Production cycle. Location. Cultivar. Crop-cycle length. Migration sources. Natural-enemy abundance. Insecticide history. The programme is therefore based on plant stage, pest stage, and risk level, rather than chronological age alone.

sectionTitle: Governing Principles of the Seasonal Programme
text: The programme is based on the following principles: 1. Prevention costs less than outbreak control. 2. An infested transplant may establish infestation before monitoring begins. 3. Traps detect male activity but do not replace plant inspection. 4. Eggs and young larvae are the most important intervention stages. 5. Old mines should not be used alone for decision-making. 6. Biological control must begin early. 7. Chemical insecticides are a targeted backup tool, not the foundation of the programme. 8. Field edges and migration sources should be inspected separately. 9. Resistance management begins with the first treatment. 10. The fruiting stage requires a higher protection level than early vegetative growth. 11. Approaching harvest changes the choice of intervention tools. 12. Crop removal is part of pest management, not a separate agricultural operation. 13. Every action should be evaluated by its outcome, not merely by its implementation. 14. All decisions should be documented in the field record.

A conventional calendar programme assumes that an action or spray is performed at fixed intervals. A stage-based programme instead depends on: Trap catch. Presence of eggs. Hatching percentage. Number of active mines. Larval instars. Crop stage. Percentage of fruit infestation. Natural-enemy activity. Temperature. History of previous interventions. One week may pass without any need for direct intervention, while another week may require: Intensified inspection. Parasitoid release. Use of a microbial agent. Localized treatment. Selective chemical intervention. Choosing not to spray when there is no need is a technical decision, not a deficiency in the programme.

The programme consists of five interconnected layers. Layer One: Prevent Entry and Establishment This includes: Healthy transplants. Agricultural sanitation. Waste disposal. Management of old fields. Removal of alternative hosts. Nursery screening. Layer Two: Early Detection This includes: Traps. Plant inspection. Edge inspection. Recording eggs and new mines. Layer Three: Biological and Behavioural Suppression This includes: Egg parasitoids. Predators. Bt. Registered fungi, nematodes, and viruses. Pheromones. Layer Four: Selective Corrective Intervention This includes: Hotspot treatment. Collection of infested fruits. Use of a selective insecticide when the intervention level is exceeded. Layer Five: Containment and Prevention of Spread This includes: Outbreak management. Removal of uneconomic fields. Waste disposal. Coordination with neighbouring fields. Resistance investigation. The programme should not move directly to layer four before the preceding layers have been assessed.

The field should be classified as low, moderate, or high risk. Low Risk No old tomato fields nearby. No crop residues. Transplants from a reliable source. A host-free interval is present. Previous infestation history is limited. Moderate Risk Tomato crops are present in the region. Limited alternative hosts are present. Previous infestation was moderate. The transplant source is reliable, but regional pest activity is present. High Risk An old infested field is adjacent to the site. Production cycles overlap. Transplants come from an unverified source. A sorting station or crop residues are nearby. Severe infestation occurred in the previous season. Insecticides have been used intensively. Previous failures were recorded. Volunteer tomatoes or widespread solanaceous hosts are present. The classification determines: Monitoring intensity. Number of inspection points. Trap-installation timing. Need for a preventive biological programme. Level of coordination with surrounding fields.

sectionTitle: Six to Eight Weeks Before Transplanting
text: Activities include: 1. Reviewing field history. 2. Identifying the previous crop. 3. Reviewing infestation during the previous season. 4. Mapping surrounding solanaceous crops. 5. Identifying old fields. 6. Identifying transplant sources. 7. Inventorying sorting and waste sites. 8. Reviewing insecticides used during the previous season. 9. Selecting the monitoring system. 10. Assigning the monitoring team. A risk list should be prepared before transplants are ordered, not after infestation appears.

The following should be reviewed: Was the previous crop tomato, potato, or eggplant? Were crop residues removed? Did volunteer tomato plants emerge? Are pupae likely to remain in the soil? Was infestation severe? Were insecticides used intensively? Was failure recorded for any group? Where residues or volunteer plants are present, they should be removed before the new crop is established.

Preference should be given to a site that: Is not adjacent to an old, heavily infested tomato field. Is not close to an open waste pile. Does not depend on an uninspected nursery. Allows access to all areas for inspection and spraying. Allows management of field edges. Has access to climatic data or a nearby weather station. Distance alone does not guarantee prevention of migration, but it forms part of risk assessment.

The rotation should be reviewed to confirm that a suitable host is not being maintained continuously. A non-solanaceous crop between tomato crops is preferable, together with: Removal of volunteer tomatoes. Management of wild hosts. Removal of potato and eggplant residues where infestation is present. Prevention of green regrowth after crop termination. Crop rotation does not provide full protection when hosts remain in neighbouring fields.

No commercial cultivar is completely immune, but cultivar choice influences the programme through: Duration in the field. Canopy density. Ease of inspection. Ripening time. Plant capacity to compensate for damage. Fruit protection from sunlight. Harvest timing. Long-cycle cultivars require: A longer monitoring period. More precise management of overlapping generations. Extended protection of natural enemies. Early planning for the preharvest period.

Before the season, the costs of the following should be defined: Traps and lures. Inspection labour. Natural enemies. Microbial products. Backup insecticides. Waste collection. Shredding. Residue analysis. Susceptibility testing when needed. Advance planning prevents emergency decisions based only on the cheapest available insecticide.

The project should maintain a register showing: Active ingredient. Trade name. IRAC number. Registered target pest. Rate. Preharvest interval. Maximum number of applications. Compatibility with natural enemies. Market or factory acceptance. A material should not be introduced into the programme merely because it is widely used in the region.

The plan includes: Available mode-of-action groups. Groups used in the nursery. Groups used during the previous season. Groups suspected of reduced efficacy. Biological alternatives. Approximate division of the season into windows. Final insecticide names should not be selected before the dominant pest stage and crop stage are known.

The following should be prepared: A sufficient number of traps. Correctly stored pheromone lures. Replacement containers or boards. Hand lenses. Sample bags. Identification labels. An application or record books. A field map. Markers for identifying hotspots. Reading procedures should be calibrated among team members before the season begins.

The Tuta programme begins in the nursery, not after transplanting. It includes: Appropriate screening. Properly sealed doors. Internal and external traps. Removal of solanaceous weeds. Tray inspection. Batch isolation. Removal of infested transplants. Pesticide records. Sanitary disposal of residues. Prevention of unnecessary old tomato transplants remaining in the nursery. The nursery objective is: Zero infested transplants at delivery Not merely reduction of infestation.

The batch should be inspected before loading and again upon arrival. Inspection should include: Growing points. Lower leaf surfaces. Small mines. Eggs. Larval frass. Stem infestation. Fallen leaves within trays. Batches should be separated according to: Sowing date. Cultivar. Nursery location. Pesticide history. A batch should not be accepted when infestation is distributed or multiple larval instars indicate establishment of a population within the nursery.

Each batch should be accompanied by a record including: Nursery name. Batch code. Cultivar. Sowing date. Delivery date. Inspection result. Pesticides used. IRAC groups. Date of most recent treatment. Storage and transport conditions. The record should be integrated into the field file.

When a suspect plant or tray is detected: 1. Isolate the batch. 2. Inspect adjacent trays. 3. Collect samples for confirmation. 4. Review traps. 5. Determine the age of infestation. 6. Reject infested plants. 7. Reinspect before transport is permitted. The batch should not be treated quickly with an insecticide and moved immediately without confirming the absence of eggs and larvae.

Before transplanting, remove: Volunteer tomatoes. Residues from the previous crop. Old fruits. Infested solanaceous hosts. Waste piles. Rejected transplants. The area surrounding the field should be inspected, not only the planting lines.

Monitoring traps should be installed before or at transplanting to establish a baseline for male activity. The following should be observed: Standardize trap type. Record coordinates. Place one or more traps near the main risk direction. Do not confuse monitoring traps with mass-trapping devices. Replace lures according to product instructions. Read traps on a fixed schedule. Trap numbers alone should not be used to make an intervention decision before plant inspection is conducted.

The field should be divided according to: Transplanting date. Cultivar. Transplant source. Wind direction. Proximity to old fields. Differences in soil or growth. Service roads. Each sector should receive an independent code. This prevents a high-infestation hotspot from being hidden within a low overall field average.

Edges should be identified when they: Face an old field. Are near a road or loading area. Adjoin the nursery. Are close to residues or alternative hosts. Face prevailing winds from an infestation source. Edges and the field interior should be inspected as separate units.

Workers should be trained to reject: Transplants bearing mines. Deformed growing points. Leaves carrying eggs. Plants weakened by stem infestation. Trays containing fallen infested leaves. Rejected plants should be placed in a container and not left at the field edge.

sectionTitle: Recording the Source of Each Sector
text: When different batches are planted, the map should show: Location of each batch. Nursery code. Arrival date. Transplanting time. Any infestation notes. This helps identify the source of early infestation when it appears in a particular sector.

During the first days, inspect: Transplant survival. Any mines present before transplanting. Newly laid eggs. Weak plants. Fallen leaves. Traps. Infestation appearing after several days should not automatically be assumed to have originated in the field; it may have been carried with the transplants.

When a limited number of plants is infested: Define the hotspot boundary. Inspect adjacent plants. Remove severely infested plants when justified. Collect infested parts. Review the transplant source. Increase inspection frequency. Select a biological or selective intervention according to the pest stage. A general treatment should not be applied automatically if infestation remains localized.

The objectives are to: Prevent establishment of the first generation. Detect eggs. Protect growing points. Establish natural enemies. Prevent movement of infestation among plants. This stage is more suitable for preventive biological control than waiting until larval numbers increase.

The field should be inspected at least once weekly under low risk and twice or more when: Trap catches increase. An old field is nearby. Temperature rises. Eggs appear. A hotspot is detected. A neighbouring crop is removed. The inspection interval should not remain fixed when egg development is rapid.

sectionTitle: Inspection of Growing Points and Young Leaves
text: Inspect: The upper parts of the plant. The two or three newest leaves. Lower leaf surfaces. Buds. Petioles. Record: Eggs. New mines. Larvae. Parasitized eggs. Predators. Differentiate new from old mines using: Tissue colour. Mine moisture. Presence of frass. Presence of a live larva. Continued expansion of the mine.

Egg-parasitoid release should begin when fresh eggs are present or when a locally validated preventive programme supports early release. Consider: Batch quality. Emergence percentage. Actual number of individuals. Distribution. Temperature. Previous insecticides. Repeat release according to new egg presence. Predators should be introduced early enough to reproduce before the pest population increases.

A registered agent should be used when: Eggs are close to hatching. L1 and L2 larvae begin to appear. Surface feeding continues. Conditions are suitable for application. The following should then be reassessed: New mines. Live larvae. Expansion of the hotspot. Treatment should not be repeated merely because old mine symptoms remain visible.

As the canopy expands: Oviposition area increases. Spray coverage becomes more difficult. Mines become hidden within the plant. Sprayer recalibration becomes more important. Hotspots may develop beyond the field edges. The following should therefore be adjusted: Spray-water volume. Inspection route. Number of sampling points. Trap height. Distribution of natural enemies.

The objective is balanced growth rather than excessive vegetative development. Avoid: Excess nitrogen. Fertilizer pulses unrelated to analysis. Repeated water stress. Excess irrigation. Excessive canopy growth: Makes inspection more difficult. Reduces spray penetration. Prolongs the presence of tender tissue. May delay ripening. Water stress or nutrient deficiency should not be used as a control method.

As plant size changes, the sprayer should be recalibrated to verify: Penetration into the canopy. Coverage of lower leaf surfaces. Coverage of growing points. Nozzle uniformity. Absence of excessive runoff. Water-sensitive paper or another suitable verification method should be used where possible.

Monitoring may continue without intervention when: Egg numbers are limited. Parasitism or predation is high. New mines are not increasing. Growing points are not infested. Infestation is not approaching fruits. No migration wave is present. The reason for non-intervention should be documented so that it is not later interpreted as neglect.

Biological intervention should be strengthened when: Eggs continue to appear. Pest density remains low to moderate. Sufficient time remains before fruiting. Natural enemies can establish. No toxic pesticide residues are present. The agent can be distributed effectively. Biological releases alone should not be expected to stop a severe outbreak within a few days.

A selective insecticide may be considered when: Active mines increase consecutively. Live larvae are widely distributed. Parasitism and predation decline. Infestation is moving toward growing points and clusters. The local intervention threshold is expected to be exceeded. Other methods cannot act quickly enough. The insecticide should be selected according to: Larval instar. Previous IRAC group. Natural enemies present. Registration status. Crop stage. Harvest timing.

Damage becomes more serious because larvae may move from leaves to: Flowers. Calyces. Fruit pedicels. Young fruits. Loss of growing points or leaves close to clusters also affects: Fruit set. Fruit nutrition. Canopy cover. Final yield. Tolerance for increasing larval numbers is therefore lower than during early vegetative growth.

Inspect: Beneath sepals. The calyx–fruit junction. Leaves touching clusters. Fruit pedicels. Dry flowers. Young fruits. Record: Eggs. Entry holes. Frass. Larvae. Fallen fruits. Inspection should not be limited to outer leaves.

At this stage, predator and parasitoid populations should begin to establish if the programme has been managed correctly. The following should be done: Count adults and nymphs. Calculate parasitism. Avoid long-residual products. Use localized treatment where possible. Separate insecticide applications from biological releases. Monitor plant injury caused by omnivorous mirids. A broad treatment should not be applied merely because a limited number of mines is present when biological suppression is evident.

This is achieved by: Targeting L1 and L2. Removing the first infested fruits. Managing hotspots. Maintaining effective residues during hatching when needed. Reducing migration sources. Not leaving an old neighbouring field unmanaged. Once a larva enters a fruit, the efficacy of many direct interventions declines.

sectionTitle: Shift in Focus from Leaves to Fruits
text: At this stage, percentage leaf mining alone is insufficient for estimating economic damage. The following indicators should become central to decision-making: Percentage of infested fruits. Number of fruits containing live larvae. Weight loss. Spread of infestation among clusters. Percentage of rotted fruits. Proximity of harvest. Leaf damage may remain moderate while fruit loss increases.

Infested fruits should be collected, particularly when: Sufficient time remains before harvest. Live larvae are present. Infestation is concentrated in hotspots. Larvae may move to other fruits. Secondary rots are beginning. Weight loss should be calculated to assess the economic value of the operation.

The sample should be divided into: Sound fruits. Fruits bearing holes. Fruits containing larvae. Rotted fruits. Suspect fruits. Record: Fruit infestation by number (%) = Number of infested fruits ÷ Total fruits examined × 100 Weight loss (%) = Weight of fruits rejected because of Tuta ÷ Total sample weight × 100 Both indicators should be used because fruit sizes vary.

The tolerance level is lower when: Fresh-market tomatoes have high value. The market requires a high visual standard. Rot risk is increasing. Infestation beneath the calyx is difficult to detect. Processing tomatoes should be evaluated according to: Factory specifications. Defect tolerance. Effects of infestation on rot and quality. Rejection percentage. Cost of harvesting and transporting unacceptable fruits. Processing use does not mean accepting fruits containing larvae or rot without limits.

Sufficient canopy cover should be maintained to protect fruits from: Sunscald. Excessive heat. Colour disorders. Quality loss. Large numbers of leaves should not be removed merely to eliminate mines if this exposes the fruits. Hotspots should be managed without causing canopy collapse.

A list of all applications should be prepared, including: Active ingredient. Application date. Preharvest interval. Number of applications. Field or sector. Expected harvest date. Target market. No treatment should be applied without reviewing this list.

sectionTitle: Change in Intervention Tools Near Harvest
text: As harvest approaches: Chemical options become more limited. Collection of infested fruits becomes more important. Registered Bt and other suitable short-PHI agents become more important. Timely economic harvest becomes more important. Residues become a decisive factor. A product should not be selected merely because it has a short preharvest interval if it is ineffective against the stage present.

Earlier harvest may be considered when: Ripeness is acceptable. Fruit infestation is expected to increase rapidly. A major migration wave is present. Fruits meet factory or market requirements. The cost of waiting exceeds the cost of earlier harvest. The decision should be made jointly by: The agricultural engineer. Harvest management. The factory or buyer. Quality-control personnel. Early harvest should not substitute for sound management during the season.

Analysis may be required when: Exporting. Supplying a factory with a defined list. Multiple products have been used. Treatment overlap is suspected. The consignment is high risk. Drift from a neighbouring field is suspected. The sample should represent the sector rather than consist of a few selectively chosen fruits.

Workers should be trained to separate: Infested fruits. Rotted fruits. Fruits bearing holes and frass. Fruits containing live larvae. A clear route should be assigned for rejected produce. Rejected fruits should not be discarded between rows or near loading points.

Repeated harvesting allows: Detection of infested fruits. Gradual collection. Reduced persistence of infested fruits in the field. Monitoring of damage progression. However, repeated worker entry may: Transfer leaves and larvae. Open entry points in greenhouses. Increase movement of crates. Containers should therefore be cleaned and worker movement organized.

Before mechanical harvest, evaluate: Percentage of infested fruits. Fallen fruits. Rots. Canopy condition. Sorting capacity of the harvester. Factory specifications. The harvesting machine should be cleaned after heavily infested fields, especially before moving to a young field or another region.

Monitoring should not stop when picking begins because: Some fruits remain in the field. Moths may continue laying eggs. The field may become a source. The harvest period may be prolonged. Inspection should continue until a clear crop-termination decision is made.

The point should be identified at which the value of remaining fruits becomes lower than: Crop-maintenance cost. Control cost. Harvest cost. Risk posed by the field to neighbouring crops. A field should not be kept alive for a marginal yield if it is producing large numbers of moths.

This includes: 1. Defining the final harvest date. 2. Notifying neighbouring fields. 3. Collecting remaining fruits. 4. Preventing the plant from continuing as a host. 5. Shredding or removing the canopy. 6. Treating residues. 7. Cleaning equipment. 8. Managing the soil according to the production system. 9. Removing regrowth. 10. Continuing trap monitoring for a period after removal. Termination is not complete when plants are cut and left on the soil surface.

sectionTitle: Assessment of Moth Emergence After Removal
text: Traps around the field should be used to assess: Moth-emergence waves. Effects of shredding and sanitation. Pest movement into neighbouring fields. Persistence of remaining hosts. Catch may temporarily increase when plants are disturbed, so young neighbouring fields should be warned before crop removal.

During the following weeks: Remove volunteer tomatoes. Inspect solanaceous weeds. Review the efficacy of residue treatment. Clean containers. Record the final outcome. Review resistance status. Update the regional map. This period is the beginning of the following season’s programme.

A five-level ladder is used. Level One: Basic Monitoring Conditions: Low trap catch. No eggs or active mines. Action: Continue monitoring. No direct intervention. Level Two: Intensified Monitoring Conditions: Trap catches increase. A nearby risk source is present. Limited eggs are detected. Action: Increase inspection. Inspect field edges. Review natural enemies. Level Three: Biological or Localized Intervention Conditions: Repeated egg presence. Limited fresh mines. Infestation remains containable. Action: Release egg parasitoids. Apply Bt or another registered agent. Remove hotspots or infested fruits. Support predators. Level Four: Selective Corrective Intervention Conditions: Active mines are increasing. Larvae are widespread. Clusters or fruits are at risk. Biological suppression is insufficient. Action: Apply a registered insecticide from an appropriate group. Protect natural enemies. Manage the source. Reassess rapidly. Level Five: Outbreak Management Conditions: Fruit infestation is increasing. Several larval instars are present. A migration wave is occurring. Repeated failure has occurred. Action: Investigate the cause of failure. Change mode of action. Collect infested fruits. Manage source fields. Conduct resistance testing. Evaluate harvest or crop termination.

The round consists of six stages. Stage One: Review the Record Most recent trap reading. Most recent intervention. Resistance status. Harvest date. Stage Two: Inspect Field Edges Edge facing the source. Eggs. Mines. Moths. Alternative hosts. Stage Three: Inspect the Field Interior Distributed sectors. Random plants. Growing points and clusters. Stage Four: Inspect Fruits From fruit set onward. Beneath the calyx. Fallen fruits. Stage Five: Natural Enemies Parasitized eggs. Predators. Parasitoids. Plant-damage symptoms caused by predators. Stage Six: Decision No intervention. Intensify monitoring. Biological intervention. Localized intervention. Chemical intervention. Manage an external source.

The sample should represent: Field edges. The interior. Sectors with different growth. Cultivars. Transplanting dates. Areas near sources. All plants should not be sampled from one route. A zigzag route or predetermined distributed points are preferable. The counting unit should be standardized, such as: A defined number of plants. A defined number of leaves per plant. A defined number of clusters. A fixed-size fruit sample.
Indicator Edge Interior Hotspot Plants inspected Eggs Parasitized eggs New mines Live larvae Infested growing points Fruits inspected Infested fruits Predators Migration notes

Depending on project size, participants may include: Monitoring officer. Crop-protection engineer. Irrigation and fertilization manager. Harvest manager. Quality and residue officer. Nursery or factory representative where needed. The meeting should review: Weekly change. Pest stage. Fruit status. Biological programme. IRAC record. Harvest. External risks. One documented decision should be issued for each sector.

The programme should be based on: Early initiation. Quality of natural enemies. Appropriate distribution. Protection from insecticides. Monitoring of establishment. Supplementary food for predators where required. Evaluation of parasitism and predation. Releasing parasitoids or predators is not a separate operation from the rest of management. It affects insecticide selection and timing of leaf removal.

Use: Bt or a virus near hatching. Fungi when humidity and temperature are suitable. Nematodes according to pest location and moisture. Soil treatments when there is evidence that soil pupation is important. Consider: Strain. Viability. Compatibility. Radiation. Spray water. Assessment timing. All biological products should not be managed in the same way.

An insecticide should be used when: A demonstrated need exists. The pest stage is susceptible. The group differs from the previous window. The product is compatible with harvest timing. Its effect on natural enemies is acceptable. The sprayer is calibrated. The reason for selection should be recorded, not only the product name.

When trap catches rise suddenly near an old field: 1. Confirm trap and lure condition. 2. Inspect the facing edge. 3. Search for newly laid eggs. 4. Notify the manager of the old field. 5. Intensify follow-up for several days. 6. Use egg parasitoids or hatching-stage interventions when oviposition is confirmed. 7. Do not conduct a general spray based on trap catch alone. The objective is to prevent immigrants from establishing a generation within the field.

High temperature affects: Pest-development rate. Egg duration. Hatching rate. Longevity of insecticides and pheromones. Survival of natural enemies. Viability of fungi and nematodes. Plant stress. It requires: Shortening the inspection interval. Reviewing expected hatch time. Protecting release cards from sunlight. Applying microbial agents at a suitable time. Avoiding sprays on stressed plants under phytotoxic conditions. Reviewing lure and dispenser longevity. Low trap catch during extreme heat should not automatically be interpreted as population decline because flight activity may change.

Wind and dust cause: Spray drift. Reduced pesticide adhesion. Contamination of sticky surfaces. Changes in pheromone dispersal. Drying of microbial agents. Difficulty releasing natural enemies. The programme should: Postpone spraying when label limits are exceeded. Clean traps. Secure cards. Reassess coverage. Monitor the direction of migration.

These may: Wash off Bt, viruses, and some insecticides. Reduce spores on leaves. Increase humidity favourable to some fungi. Disrupt water or sticky traps. Alter pest behaviour. Treatment should not be repeated automatically after rainfall. Review: Time between application and rainfall. Rainfall amount. Product rainfastness. Inspection results. Label instructions.

Processing tomatoes are often characterized by: Relatively high plant density. Determinate growth. One harvest or a limited number of harvests. Importance of uniform ripening. Economic sensitivity to the percentage of rejected fruits. A critical period before mechanical harvest. The programme therefore focuses on: Protecting canopy cover. Preventing fruit infestation. Avoiding extensive leaf removal. Managing insecticide use before harvest. Coordinating harvest timing with infestation level. Cleaning the harvester. Terminating the field immediately after delivery.

Fresh-market tomatoes are characterized by: Repeated harvests. Higher visual-quality requirements. Rejection of fruits bearing holes or rots. A longer crop duration in some production systems.
