Repeated entry of workers and crates. The programme therefore requires: More frequent fruit inspection. Continuous removal of infested fruits. Strict review of preharvest intervals between pickings. Cleaning of containers. Organized movement of workers between infested and uninfested fields. Conservation of natural enemies throughout the harvest period.

Short Production Cycle This generally includes fewer pest generations but may be exposed to a severe migration wave from a preceding crop. Long Production Cycle This requires: A more complex resistance-management programme. Extended protection of natural enemies. Timely replacement of lures and dispensers. Monitoring of multiple pest waves. Prevention of cumulative larval build-up. The same chemical programme should not be used for both crop cycles without modification.

Egyptian production cycles differ in: Temperature. Season length. Overlap among tomato, potato, and eggplant crops. Pest pressure. Factory schedules. Soil types and winds. Each region should therefore develop a local calendar based on: Historical trap data. Transplanting dates. Temperature records. Field-removal dates. Resistance records. Insecticide lists accepted by the target market. One fixed programme should not be used for the Delta, Nubaria, Upper Egypt, and the North Coast.

sectionTitle: Operational Programme for a Small Area
text: In a small field, the agricultural engineer can implement: A hand-drawn map. Standardized traps. One comprehensive inspection round. A weekly record. Localized collection. Direct coordination with neighbours. However, small fields may be severely affected by migration when surrounded by infested crops, so ease of inspection does not necessarily mean ease of control.

sectionTitle: Operational Programme for a Large Area
text: The area should be divided into sectors, each with: An inspection officer. Traps. Sampling points. A transplanting date. A pesticide record. A biological programme. A harvest date. A centralized warning system should be used, while interventions should not be standardized across all sectors when infestation levels differ.

These include: Males/trap/day. Percentage of plants carrying eggs. Eggs/plant. Parasitism percentage. New mines/plant. Live larvae/plant. Percentage of infested growing points. Percentage of infested fruits. Predator density. Number of interventions. Outcome of the most recent intervention. Trends over time should be displayed, not only the current value.

These include: Date of first detection. Date of first egg. Date of first larva. Highest density. Number of hotspots. Number of biological releases. Number of chemical sprays. IRAC groups used. Percentage of fruit infestation. Marketable yield. Rejection percentage. Residue results. Control cost. Duration for which the field remained after the final harvest.

Direct costs include: Traps. Lures. Labour. Biological agents. Insecticides. Spraying. Collection of infested fruits. Residue management. Analyses. Indirect costs include: Yield loss. Fruit rejection. Harvest delay. Damage to natural enemies. Resistance. Effects on the following crop. Two insecticides should not be compared by package price alone, but by the cost of protecting one acceptable tonne of produce.

When infestation increases, six areas should be reviewed. Prevention Were infested transplants introduced? Did residues remain? Monitoring Was egg detection delayed? Were traps functioning correctly? Timing Was the correct life stage targeted? Application Was coverage adequate? Was the sprayer calibrated? Compatibility Were natural enemies killed? Resistance and Migration Did the insecticide fail? Was there a migration wave from a neighbouring field? Failure should not be managed by adding more sprays before its cause is identified.

Week/Crop Stage Principal Risk Monitoring Preventive or Biological Intervention Condition for Corrective Intervention Outcome Assessment Before transplanting Transplants and sources Nursery and trap inspection Sanitation and isolation Reject infested batch Reinspection After transplanting Early hotspots Growing points and leaves Remove infested plants Spread of eggs or mines Within days Vegetative growth Generation establishment Eggs and mines Parasitoids and Bt Increase in larvae Count new mines Flowering Growing points and clusters Eggs and larvae Protect natural enemies Infestation approaching clusters Intensive inspection Fruit set Young fruits Inspect beneath the calyx Remove hotspots Fruit infestation or spread of L2–L3 Fruit percentage Fruit filling Fruit loss Numerical and weight sample Collect infested fruit Increasing loss Weight loss Ripening Residues and harvest Fruits and spray record Registered short-residual tools Confirmed economic risk Delivery quality After harvest Source field Traps and residues Shredding and treatment Continued moths or regrowth Post-removal monitoring

Error One: Starting the Programme After Mines Appear The programme should begin before planting. Error Two: Using a Fixed Spray Schedule This ignores pest stage, risk, and natural enemies. Error Three: Relying on Traps Alone They do not measure eggs, larvae, or fruits. Error Four: Inspecting Only the Edges Internal hotspots may be present. Error Five: Using the Field Average and Hiding Hotspots Sectors should be assessed separately. Error Six: Delaying Biological Control Natural enemies require time to establish. Error Seven: Evaluating Treatment Using Old Mines New mines and live larvae should be measured. Error Eight: Failing to Adjust the Sprayer as Plants Grow Coverage declines. Error Nine: Removing Too Many Leaves This exposes fruits and harms the crop. Error Ten: Ignoring the Nursery Record An IRAC group may be repeated or infestation may enter with transplants. Error Eleven: Failing to Change the Decision Near Harvest Priorities and preharvest intervals change. Error Twelve: Stopping Monitoring During Harvest The field may become a source. Error Thirteen: Leaving the Crop After the Final Picking The pest continues reproducing. Error Fourteen: Treating Every Failure with an Additional Spray The cause should first be diagnosed. Error Fifteen: Evaluating the Programme Only by the Number of Sprays The criteria are sound fruit, cost, and sustainability.

1. Install traps before transplanting. 2. Reject infested transplants. 3. Separate edges from the interior in monitoring. 4. Inspect eggs and new mines at least weekly. 5. Increase inspection during heat or migration. 6. Begin egg-parasitoid and microbial interventions early. 7. Protect natural enemies. 8. Target L1 and L2. 9. Use IRAC groups within windows. 10. Link every treatment to harvest timing and target market. 11. Measure fruit infestation by number and weight. 12. Avoid extensive removal of canopy in processing tomatoes. 13. Notify young fields before removing an old crop. 14. Clean equipment and crates. 15. Terminate the field as soon as it becomes uneconomic. 16. Continue trap monitoring after crop removal. 17. Analyze causes of failure. 18. Evaluate cost per acceptable tonne. 19. Develop a separate programme for each region and production cycle. 20. Review the season before the next crop begins.

A season-long integrated programme for Tuta absoluta is not a spray schedule. It is a sequence of decisions beginning before planting and continuing after harvest. The programme depends on: Field-risk classification. Production of pest-free transplants. Removal of pest sources. Early trap installation. Division of the field into sectors. Inspection of eggs and active mines. Early initiation of biological control. Targeting young larvae. Protection of fruits during set and filling. Insecticide management according to mode of action. Residue review before harvest. Collection of infested fruits. Crop termination and residue treatment. Monitoring intensity and intervention change with plant stage, temperature, and migration sources. Intervention decisions are not made from traps alone but by integrating: Trap catch. Eggs. Hatching. New mines. Larvae. Natural enemies. Fruit infestation. The principal turning point in the programme is preventing the pest from progressing from eggs and young larvae to advanced instars and fruits. Processing tomatoes require protection of canopy cover, measurement of fruit weight loss, and coordination of harvest timing and field termination. Fresh-market tomatoes require more frequent inspection of fruits and containers and strict review of preharvest intervals during repeated pickings. The programme is successful when it achieves: Reduced fruit infestation. Fewer sprays. Conservation of natural enemies. Compliance with residue limits. Delayed resistance. Prevention of pest movement into the following crop. A positive economic return. References

An integrated management programme for Tuta absoluta is not complete merely because traps have been installed, natural enemies released, or insecticides applied. The actual outcome achieved by these actions must be measured. A programme may appear entomologically successful because larval numbers decline, yet be economically inefficient because application costs are too high. Another programme may produce a high total yield while the value of marketable produce declines because small holes occur in a large proportion of fruits. The programme should therefore be evaluated through six interrelated dimensions: 1. Biological efficacy against the pest. 2. Protection of plants and fruits. 3. Conservation of natural enemies. 4. Crop quality and safety. 5. Operational efficiency. 6. Economic viability. Success should not be judged from a single indicator such as: Number of moths in traps. Parasitism percentage. Number of sprays. Total yield. Insecticide cost. Indicators should be integrated to provide a balanced assessment.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Evaluation aims to answer the following questions: Did pest density decline? Did active mines decline? Did fruit infestation decline? Did marketable yield improve? Did the programme conserve natural enemies? Did the number of chemical applications decline? Did the crop comply with residue limits? Was programme cost lower than the value of losses prevented? Which components were successful? Which components increased cost without a clear return? What caused any failure? Can the programme be repeated in the following season?
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Operational Evaluation This measures the extent to which the plan was implemented as designed, such as: Regularity of trap readings. Number of inspection rounds completed. Compliance with release schedules. Calibration of spray equipment. Removal of old fields. Completeness of pesticide records. Biological Evaluation This measures programme effects on: Eggs. Larvae. Mines. Moths. Successive generations. Agronomic Evaluation This measures: Integrity of growing points. Canopy cover. Fruit set. Number of fruits. Fruit weight. Total yield. Quality Evaluation This measures: Infested fruits. Rots. Rejected fruits. Processing or market quality. Residues. Economic Evaluation This compares: Programme cost. Value of the protected crop. Avoided losses. Net return. Benefit–cost ratio. Environmental and Sustainability Evaluation This measures: Number of chemical applications. Extent of broad-spectrum group use. Survival of natural enemies. Insecticide-resistance pressure. Non-target effects.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Change cannot be assessed without knowing the previous condition. The baseline includes: Infestation level before programme initiation. Infestation history in previous seasons. Average number of conventional sprays. Previous percentage of infested fruits. Average marketable yield. Previous control costs. Chemical groups used. Status of natural enemies. Cases of residue exceedance or shipment rejection. The baseline is used for comparison with the results of the new programme.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The programme may be compared with: An untreated sector in a research trial. The grower’s conventional programme. A traditional chemical programme. An alternative IPM programme. Data from the previous season. The mean of several similar fields. The comparison should be similar in: Cultivar. Transplanting date. Plant density. Soil. Irrigation and fertilization. Crop stage. Climatic conditions. Pest pressure. Yield differences should not be attributed to the Tuta programme when sectors differ substantially in irrigation, fertility, or cultivar.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

sectionTitle: Difference Between a Research Trial and Commercial Evaluation
text: Research Trial This requires: Defined treatments. An appropriate control. Replication. Randomization. Statistical analysis. A fixed protocol. Commercial Evaluation This focuses on: Infestation trend. Comparison with historical data. Differences among sectors. Actual cost. Accepted yield. Causes of success or failure. A non-replicated commercial evaluation cannot prove causality to the same degree as a controlled experiment, but it remains important for practical decision-making.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This is used to evaluate: Beginning of male activity. Increasing or decreasing trends. Migration waves. Differences between edges and interior. Effects of removing an old field. Success of mating disruption through reduced access to traps. It should not be used alone to measure final efficacy because: The trap targets males. Catch is affected by lure strength. Catch may decline under mating disruption without mating stopping completely. Trap catch may increase while plant infestation remains limited. Fertilized females may still be present despite low male catch. When exposure periods differ, results should be expressed as: Males/trap/day
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This is measured using: Eggs per plant. Eggs per leaf or group of leaves. Percentage of plants carrying eggs. Number of eggs on field edges compared with the interior. It is useful for: Assessing oviposition pressure. Timing egg-parasitoid releases. Estimating the hatching wave. Evaluating success of mating prevention. The following should be distinguished: Viable eggs. Hatched eggs. Parasitized eggs. Dead eggs. Empty eggshells.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This is calculated as: Egg parasitism (%) = Number of parasitized eggs ÷ Total evaluable eggs × 100 Old eggshells or eggs of indeterminate status should not be included in the denominator without explanation. Successful parasitism should preferably be confirmed through: Change in egg colour. Parasitoid emergence. Presence of a characteristic exit hole. Laboratory holding of samples where necessary. A high parasitism percentage does not necessarily mean the programme succeeded when total egg density is extremely high, because a large absolute number of viable eggs may remain. Example: 80% parasitism of 10 eggs/plant leaves two viable eggs. 50% parasitism of one egg/plant leaves only 0.5 egg. The absolute number of surviving eggs should therefore be evaluated together with the percentage.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Active mines are more informative than the total number of visible mines. An active mine is usually characterized by one or more of the following: A live larva. Fresh frass. Moist or recently damaged tissue. Increase in mine area between two visits. A fresh, non-dry edge. Old mines may remain visible after: Larval death. Larval exit. Parasitism. Successful treatment. The indicator may be expressed as: Active mines/plant Or: Percentage of plants with active mines
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Larvae should be classified as: L1. L2. L3. L4. Larval-age distribution helps evaluate: Timing of infestation entry. Success of hatching-stage targeting. Cause of treatment failure. Persistence of the generation. Risk of fruit entry. A high proportion of L3 and L4 after a programme intended to target hatching indicates: Delayed intervention. Poor coverage. Inadequate inspection. Possible resistance. Immigration or undetected hatching waves.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The following should be recorded: Percentage of infested growing points. Number of bored stems. Number of plants that lost the growing point. Presence of compensatory lateral growth. This is particularly important in: Transplants. Young plants. Determinate cultivars. Stages before full fruit set. Infestation of a small number of growing points may be more damaging than a larger number of small mines in old lower leaves.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This is calculated as: Fruit infestation by number (%) = Number of Tuta-infested fruits ÷ Total fruits examined × 100 Infested fruits include: Fruits with a confirmed entry or exit hole. Fruits containing a larva. Fruits with an internal tunnel. Fruits rotted because of the infestation wound when the rot can be linked to Tuta. The following should preferably be recorded separately: Active infestation. Old infestation. Infestation with rot. Fruits containing live larvae.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This is calculated as: Weight loss caused by Tuta (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100 The result may differ from numerical fruit infestation when infested fruits are: Smaller than average. Larger than average. Partially damaged. Completely rejected despite retaining most of their weight. Both indicators should be used together.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Total Yield The total weight of harvested fruits, including fruits that may later be rejected. Marketable Yield The weight of fruits meeting market or factory specifications. Total yield may remain high despite major economic loss when some fruits carry small holes that make them unacceptable. Therefore: Marketable or processable accepted yield Is the most important economic yield indicator, rather than total yield alone.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Calculate: Acceptance rate (%) = Weight of accepted fruits ÷ Total harvested fruit weight × 100 And: Rejection rate (%) = 100 − Acceptance rate Reasons for rejection should be identified separately: Tuta absoluta. Rots. Sun injury. Colour defects. Unripe fruits. Mechanical damage. Other defects. This prevents all rejected fruits from being attributed to the pest.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Depending on factory requirements, evaluation may include: Percentage of infested fruits. Rotted fruits. Foreign material. Colour grade. °Brix. Texture. Percentage of green or unripe fruits. Permitted defect level. Acceptance percentage at reception. Tuta absoluta should not be assumed to reduce °Brix directly in all cases. Its principal economic effects may instead occur through: Fruit rejection. Secondary rots. Reduced fruit integrity. Increased sorting and waste.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Market value can be strongly reduced even by limited superficial infestation because of: Reduced appearance. Possibility of a larva inside the fruit. Rots. Loss of confidence in the consignment. Difficulty detecting all infested fruits during grading. Tolerance in the fresh market may therefore be lower than in processing tomatoes, depending on buyer specifications.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Record: Number of active ingredients detected. Concentration of each residue. Comparison with legal and commercial limits. Compliance with preharvest intervals. Number of materials not accepted by the target market. Exceedance cases. Shipment rejections. A programme is not successful if it suppresses Tuta but causes: MRL exceedance. Use of an unregistered material. Rejection by the factory or market. Repeated use of a large number of active ingredients.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This should be measured using fixed units such as: Predators/plant. Nymphs/plant. Percentage of plants carrying a predator. Predators per defined number of leaves. The following should be recorded separately: Adults. Nymphs. Different species. Presence of nymphs indicates reproduction and establishment, whereas adults alone may represent recently released individuals.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: These are assessed through: Parasitism percentage. Number of cocoons. Number of emerged parasitoids. Host mortality resulting from host feeding. Species diversity. Every dead larva should not be classified as parasitized without confirmation.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: A simple index may be calculated as: Natural-enemy retention (%) = Natural-enemy density after intervention ÷ Density before intervention × 100 This should be interpreted cautiously because density is also affected by: Movement. Changes in prey abundance. Temperature. Crop stage. Immigration. It is more informative when compared with a reference sector or time series.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Record: Total number of sprays. Number of sprays targeting Tuta. Number of sprays targeting other pests that also exposed Tuta to the active ingredient. Number of IRAC groups. Number of repetitions of each group. Number of mixtures. Compare the programme with: Previous practice. A conventional field. The seasonal plan. Reduced spray number should not be considered successful when fruit infestation or economic losses increase unacceptably.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: A project may develop an internal index assigning points to each application according to: Breadth of activity. Residual duration. Effects on natural enemies. Repetition of the IRAC group. Proximity to harvest. Actual need for treatment. This index should not be treated as a legal standard, but as a tool for comparing programmes and seasons.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: These include: Repetition of the same group. Reduced mortality after application. Increased numbers of surviving larvae. Increased LC50. Resistance ratio, RR. Frequency of known mutations. Increased detoxification-enzyme activity. Number of confirmed failure cases. Preserving pest susceptibility can be considered a long-term economic benefit, even when its full value is not realized within one season.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Calculate: Inspection compliance (%) = Number of rounds completed on schedule ÷ Number of planned rounds × 100 Also record: Delayed rounds. Uninspected sectors. Incomplete samples. Differences among inspectors. Programme failure may result from poor implementation rather than weakness of the scientific components.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Indicators include: Percentage of functioning traps. Percentage of traps with sound lures. Percentage of readings completed on schedule. Dry or saturated traps. Actual exposure period. Number of lost traps. Calculate: Trap-network operational efficiency (%) = Number of traps suitable for reading ÷ Total number of planned traps × 100
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The time may be measured between: First egg detection. Application of a hatching-targeted intervention. Or between: Exceedance of the intervention threshold. Implementation of treatment. The longer the delay, the greater the likelihood of: Larval entry into plant tissues. Need for a stronger product. Fruit infestation. Higher cost.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This is evaluated through: Sprayer calibration. Nozzle uniformity. Spray-water volume. Coverage of lower leaf surfaces. Coverage of growing points. Operating speed. Weather conditions. Percentage of the area not adequately covered. A result should not be attributed to the active ingredient unless delivery to the target sites has been demonstrated.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Measure: Time between final harvest and crop termination. Percentage of remaining fruits collected. Percentage of the area shredded. Residue-treatment method. Presence of regrowth. Trap catch after crop removal. Delayed removal indicates increased risk of the field becoming a source.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Economic analysis determines whether the monetary value of losses prevented by the programme exceeds the cost of implementation. A programme may be more entomologically effective yet: Too expensive. Labour intensive. Unsuitable for the target market. Difficult to apply on large areas. Unprofitable during a low-price season. Conversely, a programme with higher direct costs may be more profitable when it: Increases accepted yield. Reduces rejection. Reduces later sprays. Protects a high-value market. Prevents residue exceedance.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: These include: Traps. Lures. Parasitoid cards. Predators. Supplementary food. Microbial products. Insecticides. Adjuvants. Labour. Fuel and spray equipment. Personal protective equipment. Collection of infested fruits. Residue shredding. Residue analyses. Susceptibility tests. Labour cost should be included even when permanent farm workers perform the work, because labour is an economic resource used by the programme.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: These include: Management and supervision time. Training. Delays to other agricultural operations. Damage to natural enemies. Insecticide resistance. Risk of crop rejection. Equipment contamination. Cost of re-establishing predator populations. Losses imposed on neighbouring fields by unmanaged residues. Some of these costs are difficult to quantify precisely but should not be ignored when programmes are compared.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: These include: Increased marketable yield. Reduced fruit infestation. Reduced rots. Reduced rejection. Higher value of better grades. Fewer chemical applications. Reduced sorting cost. Reduced corrective labour. Protection of late harvests.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: These include: Delayed insecticide resistance. Increased natural-enemy abundance. Reduced pesticide residues. Continued acceptance by the factory or market. Improved worker safety. Prevention of the field becoming an infestation source. Reduced pest pressure in the following season. These benefits are not fully expressed in a one-season calculation but are important when programme sustainability is assessed.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Partial Budget Analysis This is used to compare a new programme with the standard practice. It consists of four elements. Additional Revenue Such as the value of additional accepted yield. Reduced Costs Such as the value of sprays that were not applied. Additional Costs Such as the cost of parasitoids, traps, and inspection. Lost Revenue Such as yield lost because of a slower intervention or land occupied by supporting plants. Calculate: Net economic change = Additional revenue + Reduced costs − Additional costs − Lost revenue A positive result indicates that the new programme is economically superior according to the components included.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Calculate: Gross revenue = Marketable yield × Unit price Where price grades exist: Gross revenue = Sum of weight in each grade × Its price Total yield should not be multiplied by the premium-grade price when part of the crop is rejected or sold at a lower grade.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Calculate: Gross margin = Gross revenue − Total variable costs Total variable costs may include: Crop-protection costs. Harvesting. Packaging. Variable labour. Other inputs included in the comparison. When only the Tuta programme is being evaluated, a partial budget may be used instead of recalculating all production costs.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Benefit–Cost Ratio This can be calculated using more than one definition, so the equation used should always be stated. Overall Ratio BCR = Total benefits ÷ Total costs Incremental Ratio for an IPM Programme Incremental BCR = Value of avoided losses or additional revenue ÷ Additional programme cost Interpretation: Less than 1: calculated benefits are lower than costs. Equal to 1: approximate break-even. Greater than 1: benefits exceed costs. Results from two studies should not be compared unless both use the same definition.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Return on Investment Calculate: ROI (%) = Net return generated by the programme ÷ Programme cost × 100 Or in incremental comparison: Incremental ROI (%) = Incremental net benefit ÷ Incremental cost × 100 The calculation should state whether programme cost includes: Tuta management only. Or all crop-production costs.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

sectionTitle: Cost of Protecting One Accepted Tonne
text: Calculate: Cost of protecting one accepted tonne = Total cost of the Tuta programme ÷ Tonnes of accepted yield This is useful for comparing: Fields. Production cycles. Chemical and biological programmes. Cultivars. Areas. A programme with a lower cost per feddan may have a higher cost per tonne when accepted yield is low.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

sectionTitle: Cost of Preventing One Kilogram of Loss
text: Calculate: Cost of preventing one kilogram of loss = Additional programme cost ÷ Additional accepted yield The result should be compared with the selling price per kilogram. When the cost of protecting one kilogram exceeds its market value, the programme needs adjustment unless it provides other important benefits such as: Protection of an export market. Resistance management. Residue reduction. Protection of the following season.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The break-even point is the additional yield required to recover programme cost. Calculate: Additional yield required for break-even = Additional programme cost ÷ Selling price per unit of yield General example: If the additional programme cost is 3,000 monetary units and the price per tonne is 3,000 monetary units: Required break-even yield = One additional marketable tonne Net price should be used after subtracting additional harvesting and packaging costs associated with the extra yield.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: When comparing two programmes: Marginal rate of return (%) = Increase in net benefit ÷ Increase in cost × 100 This determines whether the additional cost of the more intensive programme is justified by the increased return. A programme does not become economically preferable merely because it produces the highest yield if the additional income is lower than the additional cost.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The general equation is: EIL = C ÷ (V × I × D × K) Where: EIL = Economic injury level. C = Cost of the control action per unit area. V = Value per unit of crop. I = Injury caused by one pest individual. D = Yield loss resulting from one unit of injury. K = Proportionate reduction expected from the control measure. In studies directly relating pest density to crop loss using one regression slope, the equation can be simplified to: EIL = C ÷ (V × b × K) Where b is the loss caused by each unit increase in pest density. The units used should remain consistent.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The economic threshold, or intervention threshold, is the pest level at which action should begin before the population reaches the economic injury level. The threshold is lower than the EIL because it accounts for: Time required to detect the problem. Decision-making time. Time required to implement intervention. Pest population growth rate. Speed of action of the control measure. Proximity of the crop to a sensitive stage. There is no fixed percentage of the EIL that the threshold must equal in every situation.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The threshold changes with: Tomato price. Control cost. Control efficacy. Cultivar. Crop stage. Fresh-market or processing use. Factory acceptance percentage. Climate. Pest-development rate. Resistance. Natural-enemy abundance. An increase in crop price generally lowers the amount of damage that can be tolerated, while an increase in control cost may raise the EIL. Old or foreign thresholds should therefore not be used automatically.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Studies have produced different values, including: Approximately 3 larvae/plant as the economic injury level and 2.25 larvae/plant as the intervention threshold in an Ethiopian greenhouse trial. Approximately 3.82 larvae/plant as the economic injury level and 2.87 larvae/plant as the intervention threshold in an Ethiopian open-field trial. Approximately 5.7–6.3 larvae/plant in a two-season open-field trial in western Iran. The differences do not necessarily represent contradiction because they reflect differences in: Cultivar. Control costs. Crop prices. Environment. Method of artificial infestation. Loss indicator. Treatment efficacy. None of these values should be transferred directly to Egyptian tomatoes without local calibration.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Pest density may be related to: Total yield. Accepted yield. Number of infested fruits. Weight of infested fruits. Number of mines. Loss of growing points. Rejection percentage. For Tuta absoluta: Number or percentage of infested fruits May be more economically informative than total yield loss because a fruit may retain its weight, yet a small hole may make it unmarketable.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Early Vegetative Growth The plant can compensate for limited leaf injury when growing points and fruits remain intact. Flowering and Fruit Set Tolerance declines because of the risk to growing points and clusters. Fruit Growth Tolerance for larvae close to clusters and fruits becomes lower. Near Harvest The calculation changes because of: Limited remaining time. Preharvest intervals. Value of fruits already present. Limited capacity of an intervention to protect a crop close to picking. One threshold should therefore not be used throughout the season.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

sectionTitle: Thresholds in Processing and Fresh-Market Tomatoes
text: Fresh Market Limited infestation may result in complete rejection of a fruit, so tolerance is generally low. Processing The following should be considered: Factory specifications. Defect allowance. Rots. Larvae. Effects of damaged fruit on raw-material quality. Sorting cost. The lower price of processing tomatoes does not mean that high infestation can be tolerated, because a high rejection percentage may make harvesting and transport uneconomic.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This is used when a treatment and an untreated control are present and infestation is measured after treatment. Corrected efficacy according to Abbott (%) = [(Survival in the control − Survival in the treatment) ÷ Survival in the control] × 100 It is suitable for experiments in which initial pest density does not differ substantially among treatments.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: This is more appropriate when pest numbers before treatment differ among treatments and the control. Corrected reduction (%) = [1 − (Pest number after treatment ÷ Pest number before treatment) × (Pest number before treatment in the control ÷ Pest number after treatment in the control)] × 100 It may be used with: Larval numbers. Active mines. Appropriate density indicators. The equation should not be applied mechanically when sampling units or counting methods differ among visits.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Relative reduction is calculated as: Reduction in fruit infestation (%) = [(Fruit infestation in the comparison − Fruit infestation in the programme) ÷ Fruit infestation in the comparison] × 100 The comparison should be specified as: An untreated control. The grower's programme. A previous season. A similar field.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Control tools differ in how rapidly effects appear: Neuroactive insecticides may rapidly reduce movement and feeding. Growth regulators require time until molting. Bt stops feeding before complete mortality. Fungi and viruses act relatively slowly. Parasitoid effects appear within the generation. Mating-disruption effects appear in mating and the following generation. Assessment dates should therefore be selected according to mode of action, and all tools should not be compared after only 24 hours.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: A treatment should not be evaluated at only one time point. Measure: Speed of onset. Maximum efficacy. Duration of suppression. Time until new mines reappear. Need for renewed intervention. One material may produce rapid high mortality but have a short effect, while another may provide slower but more sustained suppression.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: In scientific trials, the experimental unit should be clearly defined, such as: A plot. Defined rows. A separate greenhouse. An isolated sector. Sufficient replication should be used to distinguish treatment effects from natural variability. A large number of plants within one plot should not be treated as independent replicates when they all receive the same treatment and experience the same conditions; this constitutes pseudoreplication.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Treatments should be randomized or assigned through an appropriate design to reduce the effects of: Wind direction. Proximity to edges. Soil differences. Irrigation source. Adjacent fields. Shading. Where a clear field gradient exists, a randomized complete block design may be used.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The sample should be: Representative. Equal in size among treatments. Distributed across edges and interior according to the design. Measured using the same method. Collected at approximately the same time. The following should be defined in advance: Number of plants. Number of leaves. Number of fruits. Position on the plant. Sampling frequency.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Pretreatment measurements allow: Confirmation of treatment comparability. Calculation of corrected reduction. Determination of response onset. Interpretation of failure. Post-treatment measurements alone are insufficient when initial pest densities are unknown.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The appropriate test depends on data structure. Data may require: Linear or mixed models. Binomial models for percentage fruit infestation. Poisson or negative-binomial distributions for counts. Repeated measures over time. Survival analysis. Separate economic analysis. The mean alone should not be used without estimating variability and confidence intervals.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Dimension Indicator Current Week Previous Week Trend Acceptance Level Monitoring Males/trap/day Eggs Eggs/plant Parasitism Percentage of parasitized eggs Larvae Live larvae/plant Damage Active mines/plant Fruits Percentage of infested fruits Beneficials Predators/plant Pesticides Seasonal applications Residues PHI status Operations Inspection completion
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Indicator Target Result Deviation Interpretation Percentage of infested fruits Total yield Accepted yield Rejection rate Number of sprays Number of IRAC groups Parasitism percentage Predator density Tuta-programme cost Cost per accepted tonne Net benefit Residue result Time to field termination
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: A project may create a composite index, but it should not conceal component details. Example weighting: 30% fruit protection. 20% economic viability. 15% pesticide reduction. 15% natural enemies. 10% residues. 10% quality of implementation. Weights should be selected according to project objectives. In an export project, residue compliance may receive greater weight, whereas in processing tomatoes greater importance may be assigned to: Acceptance percentage. Cost per tonne. Speed of field termination. The composite index should not be used without presenting each component separately.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: When a target is not achieved, the cause should be classified. Biological Causes High pest pressure. Migration. Temperatures favourable to reproduction. Weak natural-enemy activity. Operational Causes Delayed inspection. Trap malfunction. Poor distribution. Inadequate spraying. Chemical Causes Unsuitable group. Resistance. Incorrect rate or mixture. Repetition of the group. Agronomic Causes Excessive vegetative growth. Failure to remove residues. Plant stress. Delayed harvest. Economic Causes High cost of natural enemies. Low crop price. Increased labour cost. Unnecessary interventions. The cause should be identified before the following season is modified.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Interpretation should account for differences in: Temperature. Transplanting date. Crop duration. Cultivar. Regional pest pressure. Tomato price. Input costs. Market specifications. A lower number of sprays in a cool season does not necessarily indicate greater programme success than in a hot, high-pressure season.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Some IPM benefits do not appear within one season. The following should preferably be tracked: Resistance trends. Stability of natural enemies. Change in date of first pest appearance. Number of sprays. Control cost. Fruit infestation. Residue exceedance cases. Infestation level in neighbouring fields. Establishment costs may be high in the first year and then decline as: Workers are trained. The same equipment is reused. The monitoring programme stabilizes. Conservation biological control improves.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Assume the following: Marketable yield under the conventional programme = Y1 tonnes/feddan. Marketable yield under IPM = Y2 tonnes/feddan. Price per tonne = P. Cost of the conventional programme = C1. Cost of IPM = C2. Then: Increase in accepted yield = Y2 − Y1 Value of the increase = (Y2 − Y1) × P Additional cost = C2 − C1 Incremental net benefit = [(Y2 − Y1) × P] − (C2 − C1) Incremental ROI (%) = Incremental net benefit ÷ Incremental cost × 100 The value of reduced costs, such as avoided sprays, should also be added to benefits.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

sectionTitle: Example of Evaluating the Addition of a Biological Component
text: When evaluating addition of egg parasitoids to the programme, calculate: Additional Costs Purchase of the parasitoid. Transport. Distribution. Inspection. Benefits Fewer sprays. Reduced fruit infestation. Increased accepted yield. Conservation of natural enemies. Delayed resistance. A decline in Tuta should not be attributed to parasitoids alone when the following also changed: Insecticides. Weather. Migration source. Transplanting date.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

sectionTitle: Interpreting Lack of Profitability Despite Biological Efficacy
text: An IPM programme may reduce infestation substantially without producing the highest profit when: Component costs are high. Pest pressure is low and intensive intervention was unnecessary. Crop price declines. Fixed interventions are used without thresholds. Yield in the comparison field remains high because of seasonal conditions. Higher fruit quality does not receive a price premium. This indicates a need to improve: Intervention timing. Rates. Number of releases. Sector targeting. Threshold use. Collective purchasing of inputs. It does not mean that IPM is scientifically invalid.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

sectionTitle: Evaluating Risk Rather Than the Mean Alone
text: A conventional programme may produce a high mean profit while carrying greater risk of: Resistance. Residue exceedance. Sudden insecticide failure. Shipment rejection. Mortality of natural enemies. An IPM programme may produce a slightly lower average profit but be more stable among seasons and less risky. The evaluation should therefore consider: Return variability. Worst possible outcome. Probability of crop rejection. Dependence on one active ingredient. Programme sustainability.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Error One: Evaluating Success by the Number of Moths Captured Traps are monitoring indicators, not final crop-performance measures. Error Two: Counting All Mines as Active Old mines remain after successful control. Error Three: Using Total Yield Alone A large proportion may be unmarketable. Error Four: Measuring Fruit Infestation by Number Without Weight Weight loss may differ. Error Five: Measuring Weight Without Counting Rejected Fruits An infested fruit may retain its weight despite being rejected. Error Six: Ignoring Labour Cost Inspection, distribution, and collection have economic costs. Error Seven: Treating Every Reduction in Sprays as a Benefit It should not be accompanied by an unacceptable increase in damage. Error Eight: Comparing Fundamentally Different Fields Differences may result from irrigation, cultivar, or soil. Error Nine: Using BCR Without Defining the Equation Calculation methods differ among studies. Error Ten: Transferring an Economic Threshold from Another Country Prices, costs, cultivars, and climates differ. Error Eleven: Using One Threshold Throughout the Season Crop sensitivity changes with growth stage. Error Twelve: Evaluating Slow-Acting Tools Too Early Fungi, viruses, and parasitoids require time. Error Thirteen: Ignoring Resistance and Residues Both are real costs and risks. Error Fourteen: Assuming the Most Entomologically Effective Programme Is Automatically the Most Profitable Its cost may exceed the value of additional benefits. Error Fifteen: Changing Several Factors Simultaneously Without Documentation This makes it difficult to identify the component responsible for the outcome.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: The priorities are: 1. Use accepted yield rather than total yield alone. 2. Measure fruit infestation by number and weight. 3. Separate Tuta losses from other causes of rejection. 4. Record the cost of every programme component. 5. Include labour and inspection costs. 6. Calculate the cost of protecting one accepted tonne. 7. Establish a baseline for each region and production cycle. 8. Do not transfer foreign thresholds directly. 9. Develop separate Egyptian thresholds for processing and fresh-market tomatoes. 10. Include factory and market prices in EIL calculations. 11. Incorporate natural-enemy efficacy into decision-making. 12. Evaluate programmes over two or more seasons. 13. Link residue compliance to economic viability. 14. Calculate the value of avoided sprays. 15. Treat resistance risk as a future cost. 16. Compare programmes in similar fields. 17. Use partial budgeting when modifying one component. 18. Review causes of deviation after the season. 19. Convert field records into an analyzable database. 20. Update calculations as crop and input prices change.
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: An integrated management programme for Tuta absoluta should be evaluated through a multidimensional framework that includes: The pest. Damage. Fruits. Accepted yield. Natural enemies. Insecticides. Residues. Costs and returns. A decline in pest numbers alone is insufficient to demonstrate economic viability, just as high total yield is insufficient when part of the crop is unmarketable. The most important final indicators include: Percentage of infested fruits. Weight loss. Marketable or processable yield. Rejection rate. Cost per protected tonne. Net benefit. Benefit–cost ratio. Return on investment. Active mines and live larvae should be used instead of total old mines when evaluating treatment efficacy. The economic injury level changes with: Crop price. Intervention cost. Control efficacy. Cultivar. Crop stage. Market. Climatic conditions. Published values ranging from approximately 3 to more than 6 larvae per plant represent the specific conditions under which they were calculated and should not be treated as general thresholds for Egyptian fields. The number or percentage of infested and rejected fruits may be more informative than total yield weight because a small hole may cause complete fruit rejection without substantially reducing its weight. Evaluation should also determine whether the programme: Conserved natural enemies. Reduced chemical applications. Managed IRAC groups correctly. Complied with preharvest intervals. Prevented the field from becoming a source for the next crop. A programme is successful when it achieves the best possible balance among crop protection, profitability, and sustainability, not when it produces the lowest possible pest population at any cost. References
partTitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

text: Sustainable management of Tuta absoluta depends on the capacity of the agricultural system to support and conserve natural enemies, not merely on releasing them or detecting them within the field. Highly uniform crop fields may provide the pest with an abundant host plant, but they do not necessarily provide parasitoids and predators with their requirements for: Nectar. Pollen. Alternative prey. Shelter. Suitable humidity. Reproductive sites. Plants that help them survive between pest outbreaks. This is the basis of the biological belt concept: a planned vegetation structure established along field margins or within the field to improve the functional environment of natural enemies and increase their ability to reach and establish within the tomato crop. A biological belt does not function as a direct insecticide against Tuta absoluta, nor does it alone guarantee reduced infestation. Rather, it is one component of: Conservation Biological Control Its principle is to protect naturally occurring natural enemies and provide the resources that allow them to survive, reproduce, and perform their function within the agroecosystem. The biological belt must be scientifically designed because unsuitable plant selection may: Support certain pests. Host plant pathogens. Compete with the tomato crop. Obstruct spraying and harvesting. Attract natural enemies to the belt without encouraging movement into the crop. Increase production costs without reducing fruit infestation. The belt should therefore not be evaluated by the abundance of flowers or insects observed on it, but by its capacity to improve biological-control indicators within the tomato crop.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: A biological belt is a planned vegetation area consisting of one plant species or a mixture of species, positioned around the field or in strips within it to support beneficial organisms and improve ecological functions associated with biological control. It may consist of: Flowering plants around the field perimeter. Vegetation strips between tomato sectors. Small vegetation islands. Perennial plants along field edges. A mixture of annual plants with staggered flowering. Plants providing nectar, pollen, or alternative prey. A biological belt differs from leaving random weeds because the belt: Is deliberately selected. Has a defined location and area. Is planted at a specified time. Receives irrigation and maintenance. Is monitored for pests and diseases. Is evaluated using defined performance indicators. Random plants may support some natural enemies, but they may also serve as hosts for the pest or diseases or obstruct field operations.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Distinction Among Terms Associated with Biological Belts
text: Biological Belt Biological Belt A continuous or semi-continuous vegetation area around a field or one of its sectors, intended to support beneficial biodiversity. Flower Strip Flower Strip A strip planted primarily with flowering plants to provide nectar and pollen. Insectary Plants Insectary Plants Plants selected for their capacity to provide food, shelter, or suitable conditions for parasitoids and predators. Banker Plants Banker Plants Plants carrying an alternative prey or host that does not damage the crop, used to maintain and multiply a natural enemy within the production system. Trap Crop Trap Crop A plant more attractive to the pest than the main crop, planted to draw the pest away and then managed before it becomes a source of spread. Companion Plant Companion Plant A plant grown with the crop for multiple purposes, such as: Influencing pest behaviour. Supporting beneficial organisms. Increasing plant diversity. Producing an additional crop. Vegetative Barrier Vegetative Barrier A vegetation strip used to reduce wind, spray drift, or movement of some insects, without necessarily having the primary purpose of providing food resources to beneficial organisms. These terms should not be treated as synonyms because each system has a different mechanism, risk profile, and management requirement.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: A biological belt functions by modifying the agricultural environment in favour of natural enemies. Its principal mechanisms include: 1. Providing carbohydrates to adult parasitoids. 2. Providing pollen to some predators. 3. Providing alternative prey when Tuta absoluta density is low. 4. Providing shelter from heat, desiccation, and wind. 5. Increasing opportunities for survival and reproduction. 6. Providing vegetative connectivity that facilitates movement between habitats and the crop. 7. Accelerating recolonization of the field after disturbance. 8. Increasing parasitoid and predator diversity. 9. Extending the period during which natural enemies remain present. 10. Improving system resilience when pest density increases. Not all plants operate through the same mechanisms. A plant may be suitable for feeding a minute parasitoid but unsuitable for a predator requiring pollen or alternative prey.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Many adult parasitoids require carbohydrate sources for flight, searching, and survival. These may be obtained from: Floral nectar. Extrafloral nectar. Honeydew. Plant exudates. Fruits or plant sap. Availability of sugar resources influences: Longevity. Time available for searching. Distance the parasitoid can travel. Number of hosts it can locate. Oviposition capacity. Survival during periods of low pest density. The presence of nectar does not necessarily mean it can be used. The natural enemy must be able to reach it according to mouthpart length and flower structure.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Some omnivorous predators benefit from: Pollen. Small insects occurring on the plant. Non-damaging mites. Alternative insect eggs. Plant exudates. This can help predators persist when Tuta absoluta density is low. However, supporting alternative prey requires monitoring because some may be: Economic pests. Virus vectors. Sources of whitefly or thrips outbreaks. The objective is not to multiply an alternative pest, but to maintain a limited population of harmless resources or to use suitable food plants.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The belt may provide: Partial shade. Slightly higher relative humidity. Protection from wind. Sites for hiding and reproduction. Stability between agricultural operations. These functions are especially important in open fields exposed to: Heat. Desiccation. Dust. Wind. Disturbance from spraying and mechanized operations. An excessively dense belt may, however, increase humidity or obstruct ventilation and movement. A balance is therefore required between providing shelter and avoiding excessive density.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The belt may be designed to support groups including: Egg Parasitoids Such as: Trichogramma spp. They may benefit from sugar resources, but their small body size makes nectar accessibility a critical factor. Larval Parasitoids Such as: Necremnus tutae. Bracon nigricans. Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris. Other local parasitoids. Some of these species require carbohydrates to maintain activity, while obtaining protein through host feeding. Mirid Predators Such as: Nesidiocoris tenuis. Macrolophus pygmaeus. Dicyphus errans. They may benefit from pollen, alternative prey, and plants that support persistence. Other Predators Such as: Orius spp. Chrysoperla carnea. Predatory beetles. Spiders. Hoverflies. Some predatory wasps. No single plant supports all of these groups equally.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Biological Belts Do Not Kill Tuta absoluta Directly
text: A distinction must be made between: Increasing natural enemies within the belt. Increasing control within the tomato crop. A belt may attract many beneficial insects without causing sufficient movement into the crop. Parasitoid abundance may increase at the field edge without a clear decline in: Eggs. Mines. Larvae. Fruit infestation. The belt should therefore not be declared successful merely because insect diversity increases. Its effect must be demonstrated within the tomato crop. Field studies have shown that flower strips increased parasitoid abundance and some beneficial insects, while pest incidence within the crop did not decline significantly in all cases. This confirms that the belt is: A supporting tool. Not a substitute for monitoring. Not a substitute for management of source fields. Not a substitute for early intervention.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Plant selection should be based on an integrated set of criteria. Biological Criteria Provision of food for the target natural enemy. Ease of nectar access. No substantial increase in survival or fecundity of Tuta absoluta. Unsuitability as a host for the pest. No hosting of dangerous pests. Support for more than one beneficial group where possible. Agronomic Criteria Climatic suitability. Tolerance of soil and water conditions. Ease of germination. Seed availability. Capacity to flower during the tomato season. Ease of cutting and regrowth. Low risk of becoming a difficult weed. Operational Criteria No obstruction of spraying. No obstruction of harvesting. No obstruction of machinery. No blocking of visibility. Ability to provide a separate irrigation line. Ease of removal when necessary. Economic Criteria Seed cost. Planting cost. Irrigation and maintenance cost. Potential for commercial harvest. Area occupied. Value of ecological services provided.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Many insect parasitoids have short mouthparts. Shallow flowers or exposed inflorescences are therefore more usable than deep flowers. Some plants in the family Apiaceae have open inflorescences bearing many small flowers, including: Dill. Coriander. Fennel. Wild carrot. Similar species. Plants with small clustered inflorescences may also provide nectar suitable for several parasitoids. Total nectar quantity alone is insufficient. A plant may produce abundant nectar in a location inaccessible to minute insects.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Selectivity in Favour of the Natural Enemy
text: The plant should benefit the natural enemy more than the pest. Some adult moths may also benefit from sugars and nectar, which may: Extend lifespan. Increase mating opportunities. Increase egg production. The plant should therefore be tested for effects on: Parasitoid survival. Parasitoid fecundity. Survival of female Tuta. Oviposition. Pest movement within the field. Tests on several flowering plants showed that some flowers benefited larval parasitoids of Tuta more than they benefited adult pests, but results differed among plant species. Selectivity cannot be assumed without testing.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The belt fails when all plants flower briefly and finish before the main risk period. The design should aim for: Early initiation of flowering. Continued resources during vegetative growth and fruiting. Inclusion of fast and longer-lived species. Staggered sowing. Cutting parts of the belt without removing it all. The flowering plan should be based on: Tomato transplanting date. Temperature. Development time of support plants. Peak periods of Tuta activity. Parasitoid and predator generations. Continuous flowering is not necessarily required after the crop ends if the belt would become a pest source or obstruct field termination.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Plants capable of supporting Tuta absoluta or major tomato pests should be avoided unless a clear management plan exists. Particularly avoid solanaceous plants that may act as hosts, such as: Tomato. Black nightshade species. Eggplant under some conditions. Potato. Wild or volunteer solanaceous plants. The plant should also be assessed for potential hosting of: Whiteflies. Thrips. Aphids. Mites. Viruses. Bacterial or fungal diseases. Nematodes. A solanaceous trap crop should not be used without a strict protocol for eliminating the pest within it.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Anethum graveolens Dill is a candidate biological-belt plant because of: Open umbel inflorescences. Numerous small flowers. Potential access of parasitoids and minute insects to nectar. Relative suitability for winter and spring cultivation in Egypt. Seed availability. Potential for harvest or sale. Its success is influenced by: Planting date. Temperature. Speed of flowering. Irrigation. Nitrogen fertilization. Duration of persistence. Under unsuitable timing or conditions, it may remain vegetative for a prolonged period without flowering. It should be monitored for: Aphids. Apiaceae-associated insects. Diseases. Ant aggregations associated with honeydew.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Coriandrum sativum Coriander is a strong candidate because of: Exposed inflorescences. Availability of nectar and pollen. Ease of cultivation. Potential as an additional marketable crop. Inclusion in floral mixtures that attracted parasitoids and beneficial insects in tomato fields. Some studies also associated coriander intercropping with reduced infestation or increased predators in particular systems. These findings do not demonstrate that coriander suppresses Tuta absoluta under all conditions. The following should be evaluated: Flowering time. Capacity to persist under heat. Hosting of aphids or other insects. Competition with tomato. Movement of beneficial organisms into the crop.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Foeniculum vulgare Fennel has open umbel inflorescences and can provide food resources over a relatively extended period. It may have: Long flowering duration. Relative tolerance of certain conditions. High biomass production. Potential use on permanent field edges. Its large size may: Obstruct movement. Compete with tomato for water. Shade field edges. Obstruct spraying. Require greater space. It is therefore more suitable for some external belts than for narrow internal strips.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Ocimum basilicum Basil is a potentially multifunctional plant because it may provide: Flowers for beneficial insects. Plant odours influencing the behaviour of some insects. An additional marketable crop. Extended flowering under appropriate cutting and management. Behavioural experiments have shown that plants of the genus Ocimum or their volatiles may reduce attraction or oviposition by female Tuta absoluta under experimental conditions. However, laboratory results, cage or greenhouse tests, and actual effects in a large open field should be clearly distinguished. Volatiles may dissipate in the field and are affected by wind, heat, and plant density. Basil should also be monitored for possible hosting of: Whiteflies. Thrips. Spider mites. Foliar diseases. Basil should not be treated as a guaranteed Tuta repellent, but as an experimental multifunctional component.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Fagopyrum esculentum Studies have shown that buckwheat can benefit some parasitoids of Tuta absoluta. Its flowers have increased: Survival of female Necremnus tutae. Survival of some Bracon parasitoids. Egg load of some parasitoids. This is associated with nectar accessibility and inflorescence characteristics. Application in Egypt requires evaluation of: Heat tolerance. Planting time. Seed availability. Flowering duration. Irrigation requirements. Competitive capacity. Economic feasibility. Locally adapted plants should not be replaced with buckwheat before comparative field testing.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Lobularia maritima Sweet alyssum flowers have shown benefits for some larval parasitoids of Tuta, especially: Increased survival of N. tutae. Support of egg load in some parasitoids. Its flowers are small and nectar is relatively accessible. It may be useful in Mediterranean environments, but the following require evaluation: Performance under Egyptian temperatures. Seed availability. Flowering across different production cycles. Hosting of other pests. Ability to persist in open fields.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Tagetes spp. Marigolds may perform several functions, including: Providing flowers. Supporting some beneficial insects. Producing root or volatile compounds with ecological effects. Serving as a border plant. Studies have shown that Tagetes patula improved egg load in some Tuta parasitoids, but it was not the best plant for all parasitoid species. Marigold species and cultivars differ in: Flower size. Nectar accessibility. Flowering duration. Plant height. Effects on pests. All Tagetes species should not be assumed to be equivalent.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Calendula officinalis Calendula is sometimes used to support or conserve certain predators around tomato systems. However, feeding trials with some larval parasitoids of Tuta did not show that it was the best plant for increasing survival or egg load. This illustrates that a plant may benefit one natural-enemy group while providing little benefit to another. The target group should therefore be defined before the plant is selected.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Sinapis alba and Eruca vesicaria Flowering crucifers can provide: Nectar. Pollen. Rapid ground cover. Relatively early flowering. Some studies showed that white mustard supported egg load in certain Tuta parasitoids. Rocket also appeared in flowering mixtures tested along processing-tomato field margins. Crucifers may, however, host: Aphids. Leaf-feeding caterpillars. Flea beetles. Other pests. They should not become a source of a new crop-protection problem.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Candidate plants may include: Phacelia tanacetifolia. Achillea millefolium. Ammi majus. Local species with exposed flowers. Suitable aromatic or forage species. Any species should undergo local evaluation for: Germination. Flowering. Pest hosting. Beneficial groups supported. Water use. Ease of management. Compatibility with agricultural operations. Species should not be added merely to increase numerical diversity. One unsuitable plant may be sufficient to undermine belt function.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Why Is a Plant Mixture Preferred?
text: Different parasitoid species may benefit from different flowers. In tests on larval parasitoids of Tuta: N. tutae benefited from certain species. A Bracon species benefited from others. Other parasitoids responded to different plants. A mixture may therefore provide: A longer flowering period. Diversity in flower structure. Food resources for different groups. Greater resilience if one species fails. Support for predators and pollinators together. Increasing the number of species is not an objective in itself. Each species should be: Suitable. Not a hazardous host. Manageable. Assigned a clear function.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Proposed Egyptian Model: 50% Dill – 30% Coriander – 20% Basil
text: The following model can be tested in open-field tomatoes: 50% dill. 30% coriander. 20% basil. This model is: A proposed experimental field design, not a universal scientifically fixed ratio. The functional reasoning behind the proportions is as follows. Dill — 50% Open umbel inflorescences. Numerous small flowers. Targeting parasitoids and minute beneficial insects. Easy seed availability. Potential for staggered planting. Coriander — 30% Adds floral diversity. May attract several parasitoid and predator groups. Potential market value. Flowering time may differ from dill when sowing dates differ. Basil — 20% Relatively longer persistence. Extended flowering under proper management. Potential behavioural effects on some pests. Provides a different vegetation structure and shelter. The proportions should be modified when monitoring shows that one species: Does not flower at the required time. Hosts a dangerous pest. Uses excessive water. Does not attract the target natural enemies. Obstructs field operations.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Defining Proportions by Area or Length
text: The 50:30:20 ratio should not be calculated by seed weight because species differ in: Seed size. Seed weight. Germination percentage. Planting density. Plant spacing. More accurately, proportions should be calculated according to: Area allocated to each species. Length of strip allocated to each species. Number of planned vegetation units. Example: If the belt area is 100 square metres: 50 m² dill. 30 m² coriander. 20 m² basil. The seed quantity for each species is then calculated according to its recommended sowing rate and germination percentage.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Seed quantity is calculated as: Required seed quantity = Area allocated to the species × Recommended seed rate per unit area Where correction for purity and germination is required: Required commercial seed = Required pure live seed ÷ (Decimal purity × Decimal germination) Example: If purity is 95% and germination is 80%: Pure-live-seed factor = 0.95 × 0.80 = 0.76 The quantity should therefore be adjusted according to this factor. A final seed quantity should not be specified before knowing: Cultivar. Seed size. Sowing method. Germination percentage. Required plant density.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Four principal designs may be used. Complete External Belt This extends around all field sides. Advantages: Continuous vegetation connection. Ease of identification and maintenance. Protection of edges. Reception of natural enemies from the surrounding environment. Limitations: Requires area and water. May be exposed to drift. May receive pests from outside. May obstruct movement around the field. Belt on Selected Sides This is placed along: The side adjacent to natural habitats. The side least conflicting with machinery. Areas that can be protected from spray. It is less costly but provides less connectivity. Internal Strips These are established between field sectors. Advantages: Reduce the distance between resources and the crop. Improve movement of beneficial organisms into the field interior. Suit large areas. Limitations: Remove land from the crop. May obstruct mechanization. Increase irrigation and spraying complexity. Vegetation Islands These are small patches distributed within the field or at road intersections. Advantages: Flexible distribution. No need for a long strip. Can be positioned near risk zones. However, they may provide less connectivity than continuous strips.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Position of the Belt Relative to the Infestation Source
text: A belt should not automatically be placed beside a heavily infested field with the intention of stopping moths, because it is not a guaranteed barrier. Placement near an infestation source may: Expose the belt to large numbers of moths. Allow direct pest movement into tomato. Increase difficulty of protecting the belt from insecticides. Turn it into a mixed zone of pests and beneficial organisms. The supporting-belt concept should be separated from the concept of a barrier against migration. Location should be selected according to: Source of natural enemies. Machinery routes. Direction of spray drift. Irrigation feasibility. Neighbouring fields. Plant species used.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: There is no universal belt width suitable for all fields. Width should be determined according to: Field area. Field shape. Plant species. Planting method. Equipment. Water resources. Belt objective. Relatively narrow strips may be tested in initial field trials and expanded after benefit is demonstrated. More important than width alone are: Quality of flowering. Continuity. Protection from pesticides. Movement of beneficial organisms into tomato.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Calculate: Belt area = Belt length × Mean width And calculate its percentage of the field: Belt-area percentage (%) = Belt area ÷ Field area × 100 Where several strips are present: Total area = Sum of strip lengths × Width of each strip It should be stated whether the belt area is: Outside the tomato-planted area. Or deducted from within it. This affects the economic calculation.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The area of one Egyptian feddan is: 4,200 m² Assume a rectangular field measuring: 60 × 70 metres Its perimeter is: 260 metres If a belt with a width of: 0.5 metre Is established, belt area is: 260 × 0.5 = 130 m² Representing approximately: 3.1% of the feddan area Under the 50:30:20 model, approximate areas are: Dill: 65 m². Coriander: 39 m². Basil: 26 m². This is only a calculation example. Actual field dimensions may differ, and the belt may be planted outside the tomato area.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The area of ten feddans is: 42,000 m² Assume an approximately rectangular production block measuring: 200 × 210 metres Its perimeter is: 820 metres If an external belt 0.75 metre wide is established: 820 × 0.75 = 615 m² If two internal strips are added, each 200 metres long and 0.75 metre wide: 2 × 200 × 0.75 = 300 m² The total belt area becomes: 915 m² Equivalent to approximately: 2.18% of the ten-feddan area Under the 50:30:20 model: Dill: approximately 457.5 m². Coriander: approximately 274.5 m². Basil: approximately 183 m². The design should be adjusted according to: Harvest routes. Row direction. Irrigation network. Field subdivision. Service areas.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: A biological belt should not be introduced over a large area for the first time without testing. The preferred sequence is: First Year or Initial Trial A small sector. One belt or strip. Comparison with a sector without a belt. Detailed monitoring. Second Stage Expansion after benefit is demonstrated. Adjustment of proportions. Removal of unsuitable species. Addition of promising species. Third Stage Integration of the belt into permanent farm design. Linkage with the pesticide programme. Full economic analysis.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Part of the belt should reach flowering near the beginning of natural-enemy and pest activity. The belt may therefore be planted: Before tomato transplanting. During land preparation. In successive batches after transplanting. At different times for each species. A fixed number of days should not be used for all species and production cycles because flowering speed is affected by: Temperature. Day length. Cultivar. Irrigation. Fertilization. Plant density. Timing should preferably be based on local germination and flowering trials.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: To avoid simultaneous termination of flowering, the dill and coriander areas may be divided into two or three sowing batches. Operational example: The first batch before or near tomato transplanting. The second after tomato growth begins. The third before flowering of the first batch ends. The interval should be adjusted according to plant-development rate in the production cycle. Basil may persist longer when: Partial cutting is used. Old inflorescences are removed. New growth is maintained. Plants are not stressed. All plants should not be cut on the same day.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The belt should preferably have: A separate irrigation line. Or controllable emitters. Or an independent irrigation sector. This aims to: Prevent competition with tomato. Avoid excessive humidity at the crop edge. Adjust water supply for each plant species. Allow irrigation of the belt to be stopped without affecting tomato. Excessive irrigation should not be used to increase flowering because it may: Increase vegetative growth. Delay flowering. Encourage diseases. Increase weeds. Raise costs.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies
