text: The belt does not require an intensive fertilization programme comparable to tomato. Excess nitrogen causes: Excessive vegetative growth. Delayed flowering. Increased susceptibility to aphids and sucking insects. Higher water use. A programme sufficient to maintain the following is appropriate: Moderate growth. Continuous flowering. No severe nutrient deficiency. Limited competition with the crop.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Cutting may be used to: Renew growth. Extend flowering. Prevent excessive seed production. Prevent plant spread. Remove infested parts. Complete simultaneous cutting causes sudden loss of resources. It is preferable to: Cut one belt section. Leave another section flowering. Alternate sectors. Remove severely infested plants. Residues should be collected when they carry pests or diseases.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The belt should be inspected weekly or according to risk level to record: Flowering percentage. Beneficial species. Aphids. Whiteflies. Thrips. Mites. Caterpillars. Diseases. Ants. Weeds. The belt should not be allowed to become a pest source in the name of biodiversity conservation.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The belt loses its value when repeatedly exposed to broad-spectrum pesticides. The programme should: Avoid direct spraying. Reduce drift. Use directed nozzles. Apply under low-wind conditions. Consider floral activity. Avoid spraying flowering plants with materials hazardous to beneficial insects. Use localized treatment when required. Record every material reaching the belt. When belt plants become infested, the selected action should: Avoid eliminating natural enemies. Avoid contaminating flowers. Prevent pest movement into tomato. Removing the infested section may be more suitable than spraying it with a broad-spectrum product.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Flowering plants may attract: Honey bees. Wild bees. Hoverflies. Other pollinators. The programme should therefore: Avoid spraying during pollinator activity. Follow product-label instructions. Prevent pesticide drift onto flowers. Avoid non-selective traps near the belt. Prevent contamination of drinking water or surrounding soil. Belt management becomes part of: Integrated Pest and Pollinator Management Not only Tuta management.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Flowering plants may provide sugar sources for egg parasitoids, but success depends on: Flower size and depth. Nectar accessibility. Distance between flowers and Tuta eggs. Belt exposure to pesticides. Parasitoid movement within the field. The belt does not compensate for poor distribution of Trichogramma cards or delayed release. The following should be compared: Parasitoid emergence from cards. Adult survival. Egg-parasitism percentage near and far from the belt. Number of unparasitized eggs.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: The Belt and Necremnus tutae and Bracon nigricans
text: Larval parasitoids require carbohydrate resources to support survival and activity, while some species require host feeding to obtain protein needed for egg production. Tests have shown that: Buckwheat. Sweet alyssum. Certain other plants. May increase survival or egg load in larval parasitoids of Tuta, with responses differing among species. This supports use of a plant mixture rather than dependence on one species. Parasitoids must nevertheless move from the belt to: Mines. Larvae. Tomato sectors. Their persistence on flowers alone is insufficient.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Mirids may benefit from: Pollen. Alternative prey. Shelter. Plants that allow oviposition. Certain plants may help maintain Macrolophus or Nesidiocoris between crop cycles or when tomato prey is scarce. However: N. tenuis may cause plant injury. High predator abundance is not an absolute objective. Alternative prey may be pests. Some plants may attract predators more strongly than tomato. Movement of adults and nymphs into the crop should be measured.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: The Belt and Orius and Chrysoperla
text: Orius may benefit from: Pollen. Thrips and other small prey. Flowering plants. Adult lacewings benefit from: Nectar. Pollen. Honeydew. Supporting these predators may contribute to suppression of: Tuta eggs. Exposed newly hatched larvae. Aphids. Thrips. Whiteflies. Their efficacy against larvae deep within mines remains limited.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Natural enemies need to move among: The belt. Tomato plants. Natural field margins. Neighbouring fields. A belt isolated from other habitats may be less useful than an interconnected vegetation network. Spatial studies indicate that: The pest. Parasitoids. Predators. May respond to the agricultural landscape at different spatial scales. A small belt alone therefore cannot solve the problems of a highly homogeneous regional landscape or heavily infested source fields. The belt should be integrated with area-wide management.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: The Belt Is Not a Trap Crop for Tuta
text: Dill, coriander, or basil should not be assumed to attract Tuta absoluta away from tomato as trap crops. Their principal purpose is to: Support natural enemies. Potentially modify some behavioural signals. A trap crop must be more attractive to the pest, which may allow the pest to reproduce within it unless it is destroyed at the correct time. The belt should therefore not be described as “pulling Tuta away” without evidence.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Some solanaceous plants may be proposed to attract Tuta, but their use carries high risk because they may: Receive eggs. Allow larval development. Produce adult moths. Become permanent sources. Increase pressure on tomato. Such a system should only be used in a structured experiment with: Intensive monitoring. A clearly defined destruction time. Prevention of generation completion. Scientific comparison.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: A banker plant differs from a flower strip because it carries an alternative host or prey that permits natural-enemy multiplication. It may be more suitable in greenhouses than in open fields. The alternative prey should: Not attack tomato. Not transmit diseases. Not spread outside the system. Support the target natural enemy. There is currently no standardized banker-plant system for Tuta absoluta in Egyptian open fields.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Basil may represent a potential component of the “push” element if it is shown to reduce oviposition or female attraction. A complete push–pull strategy requires: A repellent or deterrent plant within or near the crop. An attractive plant or trap at another location. Management of the attractive component. Demonstration of pest movement. Demonstration of reduced infestation and fruit damage. A repellent effect in the laboratory alone is insufficient to claim an effective field push–pull system.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: These include: 1. Hosting new pests. 2. Hosting virus vectors. 3. Increasing diseases. 4. Competing with tomato for water and nutrients. 5. Obstructing mechanization. 6. Increasing labour cost. 7. Exposure to pesticide drift. 8. Attracting beneficial organisms away from tomato. 9. Failure of flowering to coincide with pest activity. 10. Plants becoming weeds. 11. Seed production and unwanted spread. 12. Increased ants and honeydew. 13. Increased fire risk from dry vegetation. 14. Difficulty terminating the field. 15. Creating a false sense of security and reducing monitoring. These risks should be included in biological and economic evaluation.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The following should be compared: A sector containing the belt. A similar reference sector without a belt. Or several comparable fields. The following should be similar: Cultivar. Transplanting date. Irrigation and fertilization. Migration sources. Pesticide programme. Area. Soil. Samples should be collected at different distances from the belt, such as near, intermediate, and interior zones, to determine how far the effect extends. These distances do not represent a fixed biological range, but an experimental design that should be adjusted according to field area.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Plant Indicators Germination percentage. Ground-cover percentage. Date of flowering onset. Flowering duration. Number of flowering species. Water requirement. Diseases and pests. Natural-Enemy Indicators Number of parasitoids. Number of predators. Number of species. Nymphs and eggs. Percentage moving into tomato. Parasitism percentage. Predation rate. Tuta absoluta Indicators Eggs/plant. Active mines. Live larvae. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation. Trap catch. Operational Indicators Planting cost. Irrigation cost. Labour. Area removed from tomato. Ease of spraying and harvesting. Number of maintenance operations. Economic Indicators Number of sprays avoided. Accepted yield. Reduction in loss. Value of support plants if marketed. Benefit–cost ratio.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: The belt is successful when it produces one or more of the following outcomes without unacceptable cost or damage: Increased parasitism within tomato. Increased predators within the crop. Reduced eggs or larvae. Delayed increase in Tuta. Reduced fruit infestation. Reduced number of sprays. Increased accepted yield. Improved stability of the biological programme. Positive economic return. The following alone are insufficient: More insects on flowers. Greater biodiversity. Improved visual appearance of the field. When no functional or economic effect is demonstrated.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Belt costs include: Seeds. Land preparation. Planting. Irrigation. Fertilization. Cutting. Inspection. Area removed from tomato. Management of pests occurring within the belt. Removal after the season. Benefits include: Reduced insecticide use. Increased natural enemies. Reduced fruit infestation. Production of dill, coriander, or basil. Increased biodiversity. Support for pollinators. Delayed resistance. Calculate: Net benefit of the belt = Value of additional crop + Value of sprays avoided + Value of marketed belt plants − Total belt cost − Value of tomato yield lost because of occupied area
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Proposed Experimental Programme for One Feddan
text: Before Planting Define the belt location. Calculate its area. Analyze risks. Select the seed source. Establish a separate irrigation line. Record surrounding plants. Planting Apply the 50:30:20 model by area. Divide dill and coriander into staggered batches. Do not mix seeds randomly when sowing depth and density differ. Place labels identifying each sector. Monitoring Record flowering onset. Count beneficial organisms. Inspect pests weekly. Compare field edge and interior. Protect the belt from pesticide spray. Evaluation Compare parasitism percentage. Compare active mines. Compare fruit infestation. Calculate costs and returns.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Proposed Experimental Programme for Ten Feddans
text: The design may include: An external belt. Two internal strips. Vegetation points near road intersections. A reference sector without an internal strip. Implementation may be divided as follows. Sector One The complete 50:30:20 model. Sector Two Dill and coriander only. Sector Three An external belt without internal strips. Reference Sector Conventional management without a planted belt, while maintaining the other IPM components. This design allows evaluation of: Basil effect. Effect of internal strips. Effect of distance from the belt. Actual cost. Pesticide programmes should not differ among sectors unless necessary, so that results remain interpretable.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Condition Decision Good flowering and increased beneficial organisms within tomato Continue management Good flowering but beneficial organisms remain confined to the belt Modify the design or add internal connectivity Increased aphids without sufficient natural enemies Remove or apply selective localized treatment Appearance of whiteflies or virus vectors Immediate assessment and source reduction Flowering ends too early Establish a new batch or use staggered cutting Clear competition with tomato Reduce belt width or separate irrigation Obstruction of spraying or harvesting Modify location or design Repeated pesticide exposure of the belt Establish a protection zone or change the application route No reduction in Tuta despite increased beneficial organisms Continue IPM and do not rely on the belt alone High cost without measurable benefit Redesign or discontinue the uneconomic component
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Error One: Treating the Belt as a Direct Insecticide Its role is to support the biological system. Error Two: Planting Any Flowering Species Host status, associated pests, and nectar accessibility must be evaluated. Error Three: Treating the 50:30:20 Ratio as a Fixed Scientific Fact It is an experimental model requiring validation. Error Four: Calculating Proportions by Seed Weight They should be calculated by area or strip length. Error Five: Establishing the Belt After Infestation Has Increased The belt requires early establishment and flowering. Error Six: Allowing All Plants to Finish Flowering at the Same Time Staggered planting should be used. Error Seven: Spraying the Belt with Broad-Spectrum Pesticides This destroys its principal function. Error Eight: Leaving the Belt Unmonitored It may become a pest source. Error Nine: Using Solanaceous Plants That Host Tuta They may increase the pest instead of suppressing it. Error Ten: Measuring Insects on Flowers Only Movement into tomato should also be measured. Error Eleven: Ignoring Lost Crop Area This should be included in economic evaluation. Error Twelve: Overfertilizing the Belt This delays flowering and increases sucking pests. Error Thirteen: Cutting the Entire Belt at Once This abruptly removes resources. Error Fourteen: Describing Basil as a Guaranteed Repellent Field evidence requires local validation. Error Fifteen: Discontinuing Other IPM Methods The belt is a supporting component, not a stand-alone programme.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

sectionTitle: Research and Practical Priorities in Egypt
text: 1. Test dill, coriander, and basil individually and in mixtures. 2. Measure the accessibility of their flowers to Tuta parasitoids. 3. Determine flowering schedules across different production cycles. 4. Study hosting of whiteflies, thrips, and aphids. 5. Measure movement of natural enemies from the belt into tomato. 6. Evaluate Trichogramma near and far from the belt. 7. Evaluate Necremnus tutae and local parasitoids. 8. Study mirid predators. 9. Compare external belts with internal strips. 10. Determine the minimum area providing economic benefit. 11. Evaluate water use. 12. Measure effects of pesticide drift. 13. Test buckwheat, sweet alyssum, and local plants. 14. Study belts in processing tomatoes. 15. Study belts in fresh-market tomatoes. 16. Calculate costs for one and ten feddans. 17. Evaluate the potential to market belt plants. 18. Measure effects over more than one season. 19. Study the surrounding agricultural landscape. 20. Develop locally data-based recommendations rather than assumed ratios.
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: A biological belt is a planned vegetation structure intended to support and conserve natural enemies within the agroecosystem. It operates through: Provision of nectar. Provision of pollen. Provision of alternative prey. Provision of shelter. Improvement of ecological connectivity. Increased survival of parasitoids and predators. A biological belt is not a direct method for killing Tuta absoluta and does not alone guarantee reduced infestation or fruit protection. The natural enemy should benefit from the plant more than the pest does, nectar should be accessible, and flowering should coincide with periods of beneficial-organism activity. Studies have shown that buckwheat and sweet alyssum were among the most beneficial plants for some larval parasitoids of Tuta, particularly Necremnus tutae and Bracon nigricans, although parasitoid responses differed among species. Field studies also showed that flowering mixtures may increase parasitoid and beneficial-insect abundance along processing-tomato field margins, but increased beneficial abundance does not always result in a direct decline in pest incidence. A mixture of: 50% dill. 30% coriander. 20% basil. Is proposed for testing as an experimental Egyptian model, with proportions calculated by area or strip length rather than seed weight. Belt design should include: Staggered planting. Continuous flowering. A separate irrigation line. Protection from pesticides. Pest monitoring. Evaluation of movement into tomato. Cost-and-return analysis. The belt should not be considered successful merely because flowers or beneficial insects are present. Success requires demonstrated improvement in: Parasitism. Predation. Tuta abundance. Fruit infestation. Number of sprays. Accepted yield. Economic viability. The biological belt is not field decoration. It is a functional component requiring scientific design, management, and measurement. References
partTitle: Biological Belts and Plants Supporting Natural Enemies

text: Decision-making is the central point in integrated management of Tuta absoluta, because the efficacy of any control measure depends largely on selecting: The appropriate method. The appropriate insect stage. The appropriate timing. The appropriate location. The appropriate intervention level. A farm may have: Pheromone traps. A monitoring programme. Parasitoids and predators. Biological and chemical pesticides. Good application equipment. Yet the programme may still fail when decisions are made: Too late. On the basis of one indicator. Without distinguishing larval instars. Without reviewing natural enemies. Without considering crop stage and harvest. Without analysing the infestation source. Without reviewing the history of insecticide groups. The decision matrix converts field data into a logical sequence that determines: 1. Is there a genuine problem? 2. What is its risk level? 3. Which stage should be targeted? 4. Is natural control sufficient? 5. Is the infestation internal or caused by immigration? 6. What is the least disruptive action capable of preventing loss? 7. When should the outcome be assessed? 8. What is the next action if the expected reduction is not achieved? The matrix is not a spray schedule or fixed prescription. It is a decision-making system that changes according to field conditions.

sectionTitle: Difference Between the Matrix and a Conventional Schedule
text: Conventional Spray Schedule This often depends on: Number of days after transplanting. Repeated spraying at fixed intervals. Changing trade names. Applying treatment regardless of pest status. Decision-Making Matrix This depends on: Trap trend. Fresh eggs. Active mines. Larval instars. Infestation location. Percentage of infested fruits. Natural enemies. Crop stage. External pressure. Pesticide record. Harvest date. This means that two fields transplanted on the same day may require different decisions when they differ in: Infestation source. Egg density. Parasitism percentage. Fruit stage. Pesticide history. Harvest date.

The matrix aims to: Prevent unnecessary sprays. Detect the need for intervention early. Direct control towards eggs and young larvae. Protect natural enemies. Reduce repetition of IRAC groups. Prevent infestation from reaching fruits. Distinguish local hotspots from external immigration waves. Reduce errors arising from trap interpretation. Link control decisions to harvest timing. Standardize the reasoning process among technical-team members. Document the reason for every decision. Facilitate review of success or failure.

The matrix is based on the following principles: 1. No spray decision should be made from traps alone. 2. Decisions should not be based on old inactive mines. 3. Presence of eggs does not always require a chemical insecticide. 4. Larval instar is more important than count alone when selecting an intervention. 5. Fruit infestation is economically more serious than a similar number of leaf mines. 6. Parasitism percentage should not be interpreted separately from total egg density. 7. Natural enemies are part of the intervention decision, not an additional note. 8. Crop stage determines the acceptable level of damage. 9. Approaching harvest limits intervention options. 10. High infestation along field edges may indicate external pressure. 11. Treatment failure does not automatically mean resistance. 12. Rotation is based on IRAC group number, not trade name. 13. Every intervention must be followed by a defined evaluation. 14. Non-intervention is a valid technical decision when there is no economic or biological justification. 15. Numerical thresholds require local calibration.

The matrix is not built from one reading, but from six principal data groups. Group One: Adult Activity Measured through: Pheromone traps. Direct observation. Catch direction. Difference between edges and interior. Association of an increase with a neighbouring field. Group Two: Immature Stages Including: Fresh eggs. Dark eggs or eggs close to hatching. Parasitized eggs. L1 and L2 larvae. L3 and L4 larvae. Pupae where they can be monitored. Group Three: Plant Damage Including: Active mines. Growing-point infestation. Stem tunnelling. Flower and cluster infestation. Fruit infestation. Group Four: Natural Enemies Including: Egg-parasitism percentage. Larval parasitoids. Adult predators. Nymphs. Signs of entomopathogenic diseases. Survival of natural enemies after treatments. Group Five: Crop Status Including: Plant age. Physiological stage. Canopy density. Beginning of fruit set. Fruit growth. Beginning of ripening. Harvest date. Group Six: External and Operational Risk Factors Including: Old fields. Removal of a neighbouring crop. Overlapping production cycles. Temperature. Wind. Pesticide history. Results of previous treatments. Registration and preharvest interval.

The entire field should not be treated as one decision unit when clear differences exist. The decision unit should be defined according to: Sector. Cultivar. Transplanting date. Transplant source. Growth stage. Edge direction. Pesticide programme. Harvest date. The decision may be: No intervention in the field interior. Localized treatment on one edge. Or: Intervention in an early sector. Deferral of the decision in a later sector. This prevents general treatment because of a limited hotspot.

Before interpreting results, answer the following questions: Is the trap functioning correctly? Is the lure within its effective life? Is the exposure period known? Was a sufficient number of plants inspected? Did the sample include both edges and interior? Do mines classified as active contain evidence of activity? Were Tuta eggs distinguished from eggs of other insects? Was larval instar identified with reasonable accuracy? Is the fruit sample representative? Were natural enemies recorded? Do the data belong to the same sector? Was a recent treatment applied that may alter the result? When data quality is poor, the correct decision is often: Repeat the inspection and improve measurement quality Rather than proceed directly to spraying.

sectionTitle: Trend Is More Important Than a Single Reading
text: The following should be analysed: Current reading. Previous reading. Rate of change. Duration of the trend. A high reading may be less serious if it is clearly declining, whereas a moderate reading may be more important if it rises week after week and coincides with fresh egg appearance. Trends may be classified as: Stable and low. Fluctuating without a clear direction. High on one occasion. Continuously increasing. Declining after intervention. High after removal of a neighbouring field. A number should not be interpreted without its trend and context.

Field status is divided into five levels. Level Description Principal Objective Green No active infestation or only limited stable evidence Conservation and monitoring Yellow Early-warning indicators without confirmed economic damage Intensify monitoring and prepare Orange Active infestation still containable before reaching fruits Early biological or localized intervention Red Spread threatening growing points, clusters, or fruits Immediate corrective intervention Critical Advanced infestation, repeated failure, or field becoming a source Emergency containment and comprehensive diagnosis These levels are not substitutes for an economic threshold, but a framework for standardizing response.

A field is classified as green when most of the following indicators occur together: Trap catches are low or stable. No consecutive increase in catch is present. Eggs are absent or extremely scattered. Active mines are not increasing. Live larvae are not widely distributed. No fruit infestation is present. Natural enemies are present. No nearby high-risk source field exists. No recent immigration wave has occurred. Green does not mean the pest is completely absent from the region. It means there is no justification for corrective intervention.

These include: 1. Continue trap monitoring. 2. Conduct inspection on schedule. 3. Protect natural enemies. 4. Remove isolated suspect plants or fruits where present. 5. Maintain the biological belt. 6. Do not apply unjustified preventive sprays. 7. Review neighbouring fields. 8. Update the field record. 9. Prepare intervention alternatives without using them. The principal decision is: Active monitoring without chemical intervention.

The yellow level represents early warning and may apply when one or more of the following indicators are present: Trap catch increases above baseline. Catch rises along one field edge. Scattered fresh eggs appear. The first active mine appears. An old field is nearby. A neighbouring crop begins to be removed. Temperature rises and development accelerates. Natural enemies decline slightly. Suspect transplants are present. A limited infestation remains localized. Yellow does not mean that spraying is required. It means establishment of a new generation must be prevented.

These include: 1. Shorten the interval between inspections. 2. Increase the number of plants inspected. 3. Inspect the field edge and interior separately. 4. Locate fresh eggs. 5. Estimate hatching time. 6. Measure parasitism percentage. 7. Inspect predator presence. 8. Review the immigration source. 9. Prepare egg parasitoids or the appropriate microbial agent. 10. Review the IRAC record and preharvest intervals. 11. Map hotspots. The usual decision is: Intensify monitoring and prepare for early intervention without automatic whole-field treatment.

The level changes when: Egg laying continues in two consecutive inspections. The number of plants carrying eggs increases. Eggshells and recent hatching appear. L1 or L2 larvae appear. Active mines increase. The hotspot expands. Parasitism falls below the level capable of containing the eggs. Infestation appears near growing points or clusters. The decision should not wait until large larvae appear.

Orange represents an active infestation that remains containable before major loss occurs. Its indicators include: Repeated presence of fresh eggs. Recent hatching. Dominance of L1 and L2. Increasing active mines. Limited spread at several points. No fruit infestation or only very early cases. Natural enemies are present but insufficient alone. Time remains to intervene before larvae enter deeper tissues. This level is the most important opportunity for preventing an outbreak.

Actions should be arranged from least disruptive to more intensive: 1. Release or augment egg parasitoids when egg stage and residues are suitable. 2. Use Bt or a registered microbial agent targeting young larvae. 3. Increase monitoring intensity. 4. Remove limited hotspots or early infested fruits. 5. Protect existing natural enemies. 6. Treat affected edges or sectors where separation is possible. 7. Use a registered selective insecticide if infestation trend exceeds the capacity of biological methods. 8. Review spray coverage and equipment. 9. Define the reassessment date before most larvae reach advanced instars. An orange-level response should not become a broad chemical mixture merely to achieve rapid kill.

sectionTitle: Decision When Egg Density and Parasitism Are Both High
text: Parasitism percentage should not be interpreted separately from total egg number. Conceptual example: A very large egg population with high parasitism may still leave a substantial number of viable eggs. A limited egg population with moderate parasitism may produce fewer larvae. The decision should consider: Total eggs. Unparasitized eggs. Eggs close to hatching. Rate of new egg appearance. Crop stage. Fruit infestation. Capacity of parasitoids to persist. The decision may be: Protect parasitoids. Combine this with a selective agent against hatching larvae. A pesticide highly toxic to egg parasitoids should not be used merely because total egg density is high.

sectionTitle: Decision When Egg Density Is High and Parasitism Is Low
text: This condition indicates the possibility of a large larval wave. It requires: Confirming that eggs are alive and fresh. Increasing egg-parasitoid release where justified. Timing Bt or another larval agent before or near hatching. Shortening the reinspection interval. Reviewing causes of low parasitism. Checking for pesticide residues toxic to parasitoids. Identifying the oviposition source. The priority is to prevent hatching or kill larvae immediately after feeding begins.

sectionTitle: Decision When L1 and L2 Dominate
text: This is the best opportunity for larval control. Potential measures include: A registered and suitable Bt product. Registered insect viruses. A selective insecticide effective against early instars. Supporting predators. Removing limited hotspots. Combining compatible methods. Good coverage is required at: Growing points. Young leaves. Lower leaf surfaces. Leaves near clusters. Intervention should not be delayed until completion of a full weekly evaluation when temperature permits rapid development.

A field is classified as red when there is a direct threat to the crop, such as: Larvae distributed across several sectors. Increasing L2 and L3. Growing-point infestation. Flower and cluster infestation. Confirmed fruit infestation. Increasing mines despite a previous intervention. Weak natural-enemy activity. Continued immigration pressure. Infestation approaching an economically damaging level. The objective is: To stop the increase and protect sound fruits, not merely reduce moth numbers.

These include: 1. Identify the most heavily infested sectors. 2. Collect infested fruits. 3. Use a registered corrective measure effective against the dominant stage. 4. Select an IRAC group different from the previous window. 5. Protect natural enemies as far as possible. 6. Coordinate management of the external source field. 7. Review calibration and coverage. 8. Shorten the result-assessment interval. 9. Collect samples where resistance is suspected. 10. Review harvest timing. 11. Prevent rejected fruits from being left between rows. The red level does not justify exceeding the label rate or randomly mixing several products.

sectionTitle: Decision When L3 and L4 Dominate
text: When advanced instars dominate, recognize that: Some larvae are inside large mines. Some may be moving into fruits. Bt may be less effective than against young larvae. Part of the damage may already have occurred. The response should include: Protecting uninfested tissues and fruits. Collecting infested fruits. Using a registered material proven active against the stage present. Confirming coverage. Avoiding repetition of the group that failed. Analysing why larvae reached these instars without detection. Redesigning the monitoring programme. Mortality of some large larvae after treatment should not be interpreted as complete recovery of the damaged crop.

sectionTitle: Decision at the Beginning of Fruit Infestation
text: The first confirmed fruit infestation marks a major increase in risk. The following should be done: 1. Determine the percentage of infested fruits. 2. Confirm whether live larvae are present. 3. Inspect adjacent clusters. 4. Identify larval instars present on leaves. 5. Collect infested fruits and do not leave them in the field. 6. Raise the monitoring level. 7. Protect sound fruits. 8. Review harvest timing. 9. Confirm the preharvest interval. 10. Assess the infestation source. The programme should be adjusted before fruit infestation becomes widespread.

This represents a condition beyond routine intervention and may include: High densities of several insect stages. Increasing fruit infestation. Failure of one or more treatments. Suspected resistance. Collapse of natural enemies. A heavily infested old field producing large numbers of emigrating moths. Inability to comply with the preharvest interval of some options. Declining economic viability of continuing the crop. Transformation of the field into a regional source. This level requires an administrative and economic decision, not merely selection of an insecticide.

These include: 1. Form a rapid-assessment team. 2. Verify the diagnosis. 3. Determine fruit infestation and expected loss. 4. Review all previous treatments. 5. Collect samples for susceptibility testing. 6. Stop use of the group suspected of failure. 7. Use a registered alternative with a different mode of action when economically justified. 8. Collect or destroy infested fruits. 9. Evaluate advancing harvest. 10. Evaluate termination of uneconomic sectors. 11. Prevent movement of residues. 12. Notify neighbouring fields. 13. Continue trap monitoring after removal. 14. Document the causes leading to the critical condition. Terminating a severely infested sector may be more economical than continuing repeated sprays that cannot protect an economic crop.

Traps Eggs Larvae and Mines Fruits Natural Enemies Decision Level Principal Action Low and stable Absent Absent Sound Present Green Continue monitoring and protect beneficial organisms High once Absent Absent Sound Present Yellow Intensify inspection and review the source Increasing Limited eggs No clear hatching Sound High Yellow Protect parasitoids and reinspect Increasing Repeated eggs Limited L1 Sound Moderate Orange Egg parasitoids and a compatible early-stage agent Moderate or high Present Increasing L1 and L2 Sound Low High orange Early selective intervention and rapid evaluation Any level Limited Localized hotspot Sound Present Localized orange Treat the hotspot rather than the entire field Increasing Present Widespread L2 and L3 First infestation Weak Red Corrective intervention and fruit protection High Continuous Multiple instars Increasing infestation Collapsed Critical Containment, resistance investigation, and economic evaluation Low Present Active larvae Infested Insufficient Red Do not cancel action because trap catch is low High Absent Absent Sound Present Yellow Do not spray from trap data alone; identify the immigration source

sectionTitle: High Trap Catch Without Plant Infestation
text: This may result from: Passage of migrating moths. A nearby source field. A highly effective lure. Male aggregation around the trap. Oviposition not yet beginning. An inadequate plant sample. The correct decision is to: Confirm trap condition. Increase plant inspection. Inspect field edges. Identify the source. Prepare for an egg-laying wave. The correct decision is not automatically to spray.

sectionTitle: Low Trap Catch with Eggs and Larvae Present
text: This may occur because of: A weak lure. Poor trap location. Trap loss. Entry of already mated females. Differences in male activity. Failure of the trap to represent the hotspot. An internal generation established earlier. In this case, plant data take priority. Required intervention should not be cancelled because trap catch is low.

sectionTitle: High Mine Density Without Live Larvae
text: The mines may be: Old. Empty after larval exit. Containing a parasitized larva. Produced by a wave preceding treatment. Dry and inactive. The decision should be to: Avoid automatically counting them as active infestation. Check for fresh frass. Distinguish new mines. Mark and follow a group of mines. Search for larvae in fruits or growing points. Spraying should not be repeated merely because visible damage remains.

sectionTitle: Limited Leaf Mines with High Fruit Infestation
text: This condition may indicate: Larval movement into fruits. Inadequate leaf sampling. Infestation concentrated near clusters. Loss of infested leaves. Old infestation within fruits. It requires: Expanded fruit inspection. Examination beneath the calyx. Collection of infested fruits. Protection of sound fruits. Review of the timing of detection of the previous generation. Low leaf-mine counts should not reduce the perceived seriousness of the condition.

Eggs should be classified as: Fresh and viable. Close to hatching. Parasitized. Dead or non-viable. Hatched. Calculate: Expected number of hatching eggs = Live unparasitized eggs Recognizing that field assessment contains some error. When parasitism is high: Avoid pesticides harmful to parasitoids. Monitor successful parasitoid emergence. Measure continued appearance of new eggs. Do not rely on percentage alone.

The following should be recorded separately: Adults. Nymphs. Species. Evidence of reproduction. Presence of alternative prey. Predator value is greater when: Several nymphal stages are present. Predators are distributed across the field. Egg consumption is evident. Severe plant injury is absent. No recent toxic insecticide exposure has occurred. Necessary intervention should not be delayed when pest density rises rapidly and predators are scarce or localized.

Larvae and mines should be examined for: Host paralysis. External parasitoid larvae. Cocoons. Emergence signs. Cessation of host feeding. The effect of larval parasitoids may not immediately appear as a reduction in mine numbers. When their activity is high: Select compatible insecticides. Avoid unnecessary direct spraying. Allow time for their effect where fruits are not threatened. When infestation is approaching fruits, a slow future effect should not be relied upon alone.

Before Flowering Priority is given to: Protecting growing points. Preventing establishment of the first generation. Supporting natural enemies. Flowering and Fruit Set Greater importance is given to: Clusters. Flowers. Leaves adjacent to fruits. Fruit Growth Decision sensitivity increases because larvae may enter fruits. Ripening Greater importance is given to: Preharvest interval. Residues. Economic justification of intervention. Possibility of advancing harvest. During Harvest Priority is given to: Sorting. Collection of infested fruits. Preventing the field from becoming a source.

sectionTitle: Decision Matrix According to Crop Stage
text: Crop Stage Most Important Indicator Tolerance Level Priority Nursery Presence of any egg or mine Extremely low Prevent transfer of infestation After transplanting Growing points and eggs Low Prevent generation establishment Vegetative growth Active mines and instars Conditionally moderate Support natural enemies and target hatching Flowering Growing points, flowers, and clusters Low Protect fruit set Small fruits Eggs and L1 near clusters Extremely low Prevent fruit entry Fruit filling Percentage of infested fruits Extremely low Protect acceptable yield Ripening Fruits and PHI Determined economically and regulatorily Protect harvest without violations Postharvest Remaining stages and residues No tolerance for a source Break the pest cycle

The operational threshold in the nursery is extremely low because infested transplants can distribute the pest over large areas. When No Infestation Is Present Continue screening and traps. Inspect batches. Maintain sanitation. When One Suspect Plant Is Present Isolate it. Inspect adjacent trays. Confirm identification. When Infestation Is Distributed Stop batch dispatch. Isolate the batch. Remove infested plants. Assess the extent of establishment. Reject the batch if freedom from live stages cannot be guaranteed. Spraying the batch and moving it immediately is insufficient.

sectionTitle: Decision-Making During the First Weeks After Transplanting
text: Priority is given to identifying the infestation source. Infestation Restricted to One Transplant Batch This suggests association with the nursery or batch. Infestation on the Edge Facing an Old Field This suggests immigration pressure. Randomly Distributed Infestation Within the Field This requires review of transplant history and moth arrival. Intervention should be early and localized where possible, while preventing spread into new growing points.

sectionTitle: Decision-Making During Flowering and Fruit Set
text: Risk should be increased when the following occur: Eggs on leaves near flowers. Mines in growing points. Larvae near clusters. Holes in flowers or calyces. Declining canopy cover. The decision should be less tolerant of delay because damage can move rapidly from leaf to fruit.

The following indicators should be included: Percentage of infested fruits. Location of infested fruits. Presence of live larvae. Rot percentage. Weight rejected. Time remaining until harvest. Market or factory specifications. The decision should be based on the value of fruits already present, not only expectations of vegetative recovery.

Before any intervention, review: 1. Preharvest interval. 2. Maximum number of applications. 3. Materials used previously. 4. Target market. 5. Ripening percentage. 6. Possibility of advancing harvest. 7. Percentage of infested fruits. 8. Time required for the measure to act. A slow-acting method should not be used when harvest will begin before benefit appears, unless the objective is to prevent the field becoming a postharvest source.

External risk should be rated high when: A heavily infested old field is present. Spraying has stopped in that field. Final harvesting has begun. Irrigation has stopped. Plants are being shredded. Living residues remain. Trap catches increase on the facing edge. This does not automatically require preventive spraying, but it does require: Intensified inspection. Egg inspection. Edge protection. Coordination with the source field. Readiness for rapid intervention.

Evidence of External Immigration Sudden increase in edge traps. Eggs concentrated near the edge. Increase associated with removal of a neighbouring field. Lower infestation in the field interior. Evidence of Internal Reproduction Presence of multiple instars. Mines distributed across the field. Presence of pupae or a succeeding generation. Continued oviposition without a clear external source. Increased internal trap catch. Both processes may occur together, requiring management of the external source and the internal generation.

Natural-Enemy Status Pest Status Decision High and stable Pest low Protect and continue monitoring High Eggs high but few remain unparasitized Defer chemical treatment and reassess High Larvae and fruit infestation increasing Use a compatible selective intervention Low Egg density beginning to increase Early biological augmentation Low Increasing L1 and L2 Early-stage agent or selective intervention Collapsed after pesticide Pest increasing Stop the disruptive material and rebuild the programme Unknown Data insufficient Measure before deciding when time permits The presence of one natural enemy on several plants does not mean natural regulation is sufficient.

Monitoring Only Selected when no active infestation exists or the trend is stable and low. Intensified Prevention Includes: Increased inspection. Removal of sources. Edge protection. Support of the biological belt. Biological Intervention Selected when: A suitable pest stage is present. Pest density is low or moderate. Sufficient time exists for the effect to appear. Harmful residues are absent. Microbial Intervention Selected when: Eggs are near hatching or young larvae are present. Application conditions are suitable. A viable registered product is available. Localized Intervention Selected when infestation is spatially limited. Selective Chemical Intervention Selected when damage is likely to exceed the capacity of natural regulation or threatens fruits. Containment or Sector Termination Selected when economic viability declines or the sector becomes a source of risk.

Selection should follow these criteria: 1. Capacity to target the dominant stage. 2. Required speed of action. 3. Protection of fruits. 4. Compatibility with natural enemies. 5. Legal registration. 6. Preharvest interval. 7. Previous IRAC group. 8. Resistance risk. 9. Achievable coverage quality. 10. Cost. 11. Environmental effect. 12. Ability to evaluate the outcome. The least expensive product should not be selected if it cannot reach the stage present or causes loss of natural control.

sectionTitle: Reviewing the IRAC Record Before a Decision
text: Before any insecticide is used, record: Active ingredient. Group number. Date of most recent use. Number of applications. Current window. Group used against the previous generation. Any previous failure. Switching between active ingredients within the same group is not true rotation. Declining efficacy should not be addressed by repeating the same group under a different trade name.

As harvest approaches, exclude any option that does not permit compliance with: The registered preharvest interval. The permitted number of applications. Market conditions. Factory requirements. Residue limits. A short preharvest interval does not mean the material is biologically optimal; it may not target the stage present or may harm natural enemies.

The reassessment date should be set before treatment. It depends on: Mode of action. Temperature. Age of eggs and larvae. Speed of infestation development. Proximity to fruits. Harvest stage. Reassessment should be more rapid when: Temperature is high. Eggs are close to hatching. Fruit infestation has appeared. Immigration pressure continues. Treatment failure is suspected. One interval should not be used for all control methods.

An intervention is successful when one or more of the following occur: Live unparasitized egg density declines. New hatching declines. Active mines stop increasing. Live larvae decline. Infestation does not spread to new sectors. Fruit infestation stops increasing. Natural enemies persist. No rapid unplanned repeat treatment is required. Good coverage is achieved. Marketable yield is protected. Disappearance of moths from traps alone is not evidence of success.

These include: Continued appearance of L1 after the expected action time. Increasing L2 and L3. New mines appearing in several sectors. Increasing fruit infestation. Live larvae remaining in well-covered areas. Collapse of natural enemies. Need to repeat the same group. Strong variation among field areas. Continued high immigration from outside. The following should be distinguished: Product failure. Application failure. Timing failure. Area-wide programme failure.

When a treatment fails, follow this sequence. Question One: Was the Measured Infestation New? If mines were old, there is insufficient evidence of failure. Question Two: Was the Target Stage Appropriate? A product intended for early instars may fail against L4 inside fruits. Question Three: Did the Material Reach the Target? Review coverage and calibration. Question Four: Did Weather Affect the Treatment? Review wind, rainfall, and temperature. Question Five: Was the Registered Dose Used? Review calculations and preparation. Question Six: Is There a New Immigration Wave? Review field edges, traps, and neighbouring fields. Question Seven: Was the Group Repeated? Review the IRAC window. Question Eight: Is Resistance Present? Collect samples and conduct susceptibility testing. The investigation should not jump directly to the final question.

A project may create a scoring system to help rank fields, provided it is not treated as a validated economic threshold. Example: Indicator 0 1 2 3 --------- - - - - Trap trend Low Fluctuating Increasing Sharp increase Eggs Absent Scattered Repeated Dense Active mines Absent Localized Widespread Rapidly increasing Larval instar Absent L1 L2–L3 L3–L4 Fruits Sound Suspect Limited infestation Increasing infestation Natural enemies High Moderate Low Collapsed External source Absent Possible Nearby Highly active Proximity to harvest Distant Intermediate Near Ongoing Scores should not be summed mechanically to trigger spraying. They may be used to identify: Fields requiring an urgent visit. Sectors requiring additional data. Priority level for response. The system should be calibrated using Egyptian field results before adoption.

sectionTitle: Why Is There No General Numerical Threshold for Egypt?
text: Because intervention levels change with: Crop price. Control cost. Cultivar. Fresh-market or processing use. Plant stage. Percentage of acceptable fruits. Natural-enemy efficacy. Temperature. Pest-development speed. Resistance. Coverage level. Immigration pressure. Published studies have produced different economic-threshold values under different countries, environments, and production systems. A figure expressed as: Larvae/plant. Males/trap/week. Mine percentage. Should therefore not be used as a general Egyptian threshold without local trials.

Development of an Egyptian threshold requires: 1. Selecting a defined region and production cycle. 2. Selecting a known cultivar and production system. 3. Establishing different pest densities. 4. Measuring larvae and mines. 5. Measuring fruit infestation. 6. Measuring marketable yield. 7. Relating density to loss. 8. Calculating intervention cost. 9. Estimating control efficacy. 10. Using the actual crop price. 11. Repeating the experiment over two or more seasons. 12. Updating the threshold as prices and resistance change. Separate thresholds should be developed for: Processing tomatoes. Fresh-market tomatoes. Short-cycle crops. Long-cycle crops. Winter production cycles. Hot production cycles.

Date Sector Traps Eggs Parasitism Active Mines Larval Instars Fruit Infestation Natural Enemies Risk Level Decision Assessment Date The record should also include: Crop stage. Potential source field. Previous active ingredient and IRAC group. Harvest date. Person responsible for implementation. Decision outcome.

Before issuing a spray order, complete the following information. Evidence of Need Which indicator increased? Is damage active? What is the larval instar? Are fruits threatened? Evaluation of Alternatives Is localized treatment sufficient? Can a biological agent be used? What is the status of natural enemies? Product Review Is it registered? What is its IRAC group? Was it used against the previous generation? What is the preharvest interval? What is its effect on natural enemies? Evaluation Plan When will the field be reinspected? What is the success indicator? What action will follow failure? A spray order should not be issued without a reviewable justification.

Record that: Trap catches do not show a persistent increasing trend. No substantial live egg population is present. Mines are old. Live larvae are absent or limited. Fruits are sound. Natural enemies are effective. No high-risk external source is present. Reinspection will occur on the specified date. Documenting non-intervention demonstrates that it is based on monitoring rather than absence of follow-up.

Scenario Most Likely Interpretation Decision Increased trap catch only Immigration or adult activity Intensify inspection Fresh eggs without larvae Beginning of establishment Parasitoids and hatching-timed action High percentage of parasitized eggs Potential biological regulation Protect parasitoids and reassess L1 and L2 in hotspots Early infestation Biological or localized intervention Widespread L1 and L2 Hatching wave Early selective intervention L3 and L4 in growing points Delayed detection Corrective intervention and cause analysis First infested fruit Economic damage begins Collect infested fruit and protect sound fruit Increasing fruit infestation Containment failure Red or critical level Mines remain after treatment but no larvae Old damage Do not repeat automatically Live larvae after a well-applied treatment Possible resistance Comprehensive investigation and change of MoA Predator collapse Pesticide effect or resource shortage Protect and re-establish Removal of an adjacent old field Potential immigration wave Edge warning and intensified monitoring Near harvest with limited infestation Economic and regulatory decision Collect infested fruit and review PHI Uneconomic, heavily infested field Regional source Organized termination

Particular priority should be given to: Canopy cover. Fruit infestation by number and weight. Factory rejection percentage. Harvest timing. Preharvest interval. Feasibility of mechanical harvesting. Equipment cleaning. Field termination after delivery. The decision should not be based on leaf mines alone when fruits remain sound and the canopy can still perform its function. Conversely, limited fruit infestation should not be underestimated when it indicates a wave likely to expand before harvest.

Decision sensitivity is higher because of: Low acceptance of fruits with holes. Repeated harvests. Overlapping preharvest intervals. High value of external appearance. Possibility of a larva inside a fruit that appears partly sound. The programme should: Inspect fruits during every round. Separate rejected fruits. Review the most recent treatment before every picking. Avoid any product incompatible with the harvest programme. Give greater weight to fruit-infestation indicators.

sectionTitle: Adjusting the Matrix According to Temperature
text: When temperature is favourable for rapid pest development: Egg duration shortens. Larval development accelerates. The intervention window narrows. Yellow-level fields require earlier reassessment. Orange may become red within a short period. Under cooler conditions: Development slows. Egg and larval stages may persist longer. Infestation does not necessarily stop. One generation may remain in the crop for a longer period. Reassessment timing should therefore be linked to thermal units and local temperature, not calendar time alone.

Error One: Treating Trap Catch as a Spray Order Traps are warning tools, not an independent general threshold. Error Two: Ignoring Plant Data When Trap Catch Is Low Mated females or an internal generation may be present. Error Three: Counting Old Mines This leads to unnecessary repeat treatments. Error Four: Failing to Identify Larval Instar This makes intervention selection imprecise. Error Five: Relying on Parasitism Percentage Alone Expected surviving eggs should also be calculated. Error Six: Ignoring Fruit Infestation It may be more serious than leaf damage. Error Seven: Using One Threshold for All Crop Stages Plant tolerance changes with development. Error Eight: Treating Predator Presence as a Permanent Reason Not to Intervene Predator density may be insufficient. Error Nine: Using the Matrix as a Rigid Schedule It is a framework requiring data and interpretation. Error Ten: Summing Indicator Scores and Automatically Triggering a Spray The decision requires biological and economic review. Error Eleven: Ignoring Neighbouring Fields Pressure may be external and continuous. Error Twelve: Failing to Define a Reassessment Date This prevents determination of success or failure. Error Thirteen: Repeating the Same Group After Failure This increases resistance pressure. Error Fourteen: Using a Product near Harvest Without Reviewing the PHI This may cause crop rejection. Error Fifteen: Failing to Document the Reason for the Decision This makes seasonal analysis and improvement difficult.

1. Develop a separate matrix for each region and production cycle. 2. Standardize inspection of eggs, mines, and fruits. 3. Separate field edges from the interior. 4. Record trap trend rather than raw catch alone. 5. Do not establish a universal spray threshold from traps. 6. Develop local thresholds for processing tomatoes. 7. Develop separate thresholds for fresh-market tomatoes. 8. Introduce fruit infestation into the decision at an early stage. 9. Estimate live unparasitized eggs. 10. Identify larval instars. 11. Record predator and nymph density. 12. Include immigration source in the risk level. 13. Link the decision to the nursery record. 14. Review IRAC groups before every intervention. 15. Link the decision to harvest timing and residue requirements. 16. Use localized treatment where possible. 17. Set a defined assessment date for every action. 18. Train teams to distinguish old from new mines. 19. Analyse non-intervention decisions as carefully as intervention decisions. 20. Convert the matrix into a standardized paper and digital form.

The decision-making matrix is a system for integrating data on: Traps. Eggs. Larvae. Active mines. Fruits. Natural enemies. Crop stage. Neighbouring fields. Previous pesticides. Harvest timing. A control decision should not be made from a single indicator. The matrix uses five levels: Green for monitoring. Yellow for warning and preparation. Orange for early intervention. Red for corrective intervention. Critical for containment, investigation, and assessment of crop continuation. The orange level is the most important opportunity for success because it often coincides with eggs and young larvae before they reach fruits. High trap catch does not require spraying when eggs or plant infestation are absent. Likewise, low trap catch does not mean the field is safe when larvae or infested fruits are present. The following should be distinguished: Live eggs. Parasitized eggs. Old mines. Active mines. Early instars. Advanced instars. Crop stage determines risk. Larvae near clusters and fruits are more serious than a similar number on leaves of a young plant capable of compensation. Natural enemies should be incorporated into the decision without assuming they are always capable of containing the infestation. No universal fixed numerical threshold can be used for all tomato fields in Egypt because the decision changes with: Region. Production cycle. Cultivar. Market. Price. Intervention cost. Crop stage. Resistance. Temperature. Immigration. A correct decision is complete only when it specifies in advance: Reason for intervention. Selected method. Target stage. Success indicator. Reassessment date. Alternative action if failure occurs. References

Tomato production in Egypt is characterized by a wide range of production regions, variable temperatures, overlapping planting dates, and the prolonged presence of solanaceous hosts during substantial parts of the year. It is therefore not possible to develop one national programme for managing Tuta absoluta and apply it identically across all governorates and production cycles. A programme suitable for a long-cycle processing-tomato field in newly reclaimed land may not be suitable for a short-cycle fresh-market tomato field in the Nile Delta. Likewise, a programme that performs well during a relatively cool period may fail during a period when pest development accelerates. The Egyptian programme is influenced by: Geographical region. Prevailing temperatures. Transplanting date. Crop duration. Production purpose. Cultivar. Presence of old tomato fields. Overlap of tomato with potato and eggplant. Transplant source. Irrigation system. Canopy density. Harvest timing. Factory or market requirements. Local insecticide-resistance status. Presence of naturally occurring or released natural enemies. The technically correct question is therefore not: What is the Tuta absoluta programme for Egypt? But: What is the appropriate programme for this field, in this region, in this production cycle, under the current risk level?
partTitle: Applied Programmes for Managing Tuta absoluta Under Egyptian Conditions

text: The development rate of Tuta absoluta changes with temperature, and therefore the following also vary: Egg-incubation period. Rate of progression through larval instars. Generation duration. Number of generations that may overlap with the crop cycle. Time available for intervention. Speed of fruit infestation. Egyptian regions also differ in: Severity of winter cold. Timing of temperature increase. Dryness. Wind. Proximity to the coast. Length of the tomato season. Overlap among production cycles. Egyptian studies have shown that pest and natural-enemy population dynamics are not constant among locations or periods. Thermal models have also shown that expected generation number and adult-emergence timing differ among Giza, Qena, and Marsa Matrouh. The following should therefore be used: Local measurements. Weather stations. Traps. Infestation history. Records of old fields. Rather than relying on one national calendar.
partTitle: Applied Programmes for Managing Tuta absoluta Under Egyptian Conditions

sectionTitle: Basic Units for Building the Egyptian Programme
text: The programme is built from five overlapping units. Unit One: Production Type Processing tomatoes. Fresh-market tomatoes. Transplant production. Long- or short-cycle production. Unit Two: Climatic Production Cycle Cool or winter cycle. Transitional spring cycle. Hot or summer cycle. Autumn or late cycle. Unit Three: Region The Delta and Lower Nile Valley. Newly reclaimed land in Nubaria and Wadi El Natroun. The North Coast and Matrouh. Middle Egypt. Upper Egypt. Sinai and similar desert regions. Unit Four: External Pressure Level Low. Moderate. High. Active immigration wave. Unit Five: Crop Stage Nursery. Post-transplanting. Vegetative growth. Flowering and fruit set. Fruit filling. Ripening. Harvest. Postharvest. The final decision results from the intersection of these units, not from one factor alone.
partTitle: Applied Programmes for Managing Tuta absoluta Under Egyptian Conditions

text: An applied programme is a sequence of: Preventive actions. Monitoring procedures. Decision points. Intervention alternatives. Reassessment dates. Postharvest measures. An applied programme does not consist of a list of insecticides used sequentially without need. Its content changes weekly according to: Trap trend. Live eggs. Larval instars. Parasitism percentage. Predator status. Fruit infestation. Expected weather. Harvest timing.
partTitle: Applied Programmes for Managing Tuta absoluta Under Egyptian Conditions

sectionTitle: Data Required Before Selecting a Programme
text: Before the season begins, collect the following data: 1. Location and coordinates. 2. Field area. 3. Cultivar. 4. Production type. 5. Transplanting date. 6. Expected crop duration. 7. Transplant source. 8. Previous crop. 9. Nearest old tomato field. 10. Harvest timing of neighbouring fields. 11. Presence of potato or eggplant. 12. Site history of Tuta infestation. 13. Active ingredients previously used. 14. Control-failure cases. 15. Natural enemies recorded in the region. 16. Expected harvest date. 17. Target market. 18. List of accepted insecticides. 19. Irrigation network. 20. Farm capacity for weekly monitoring. The programme is not complete until this information is known.
partTitle: Applied Programmes for Managing Tuta absoluta Under Egyptian Conditions

sectionTitle: Characteristics of Processing Tomatoes Affecting the Programme
text: Processing tomatoes are often characterized by: Determinate growth. Moderate or high plant density. Relatively concentrated yield. One harvest or a limited number of pickings. Importance of canopy cover for fruit protection. Importance of uniform ripening. Harvest linkage to factory schedules. Limited flexibility in delaying delivery. Transport of large quantities over a short period. The Tuta programme in processing tomatoes should achieve four objectives: 1. Maintain a functional canopy until harvest approaches. 2. Prevent fruit infestation. 3. Comply with factory lists and residue requirements. 4. Terminate the field immediately after delivery ends.

text: The following should be implemented: Review old fields. Eliminate volunteer tomatoes. Confirm transplant health. Install traps before transplanting. Divide the field into sectors. text: During the first weeks, priorities are: Detect infestation carried with transplants. Inspect growing points. Record eggs. Prevent establishment of the first generation. sectionTitle: Fruit Set and Filling in Processing Tomatoes

text: These include: False reassurance from low trap catches. Leaving mines unexamined because they develop slowly. Prolonged larval persistence inside leaves. sectionTitle: Priorities in the Winter Production Cycle

sectionTitle: Microbial Agents Under Relatively Cool Conditions

sectionTitle: Common Errors in Applying Regional Programmes
text: Error One: Using a Delta Programme in Upper Egypt Temperature, development rate, and operational conditions differ. Error Two: Using a Winter Programme in Summer Hatching time and reassessment intervals differ. Error Three: Assuming Processing Tomatoes Need Less Fruit Protection Fruit infestation affects acceptance, quality, and rots. Error Four: Using the Same Programme for Short and Long Cycles Generation number and resistance pressure differ. Error Five: Setting Rigid Monthly Dates Weather and transplanting dates change among years. Error Six: Ignoring the Transplant Source The field may begin with internal infestation. Error Seven: Neglecting Old Fields They may undermine the best programme in a new field. Error Eight: Using Foreign Release Rates Without Testing Temperature, wind, strain, and production system differ. Error Nine: Applying Fungi Under Unsuitable Conditions This reduces viability and efficacy. Error Ten: Assuming Winter Is Risk-Free A generation may persist for a longer period. Error Eleven: Assuming Extreme Heat Eliminates the Pest Mines and fruits protect many larvae. Error Twelve: Failing to Adjust Spray Equipment as Plants Grow Coverage declines. Error Thirteen: Ignoring the Harvest Programme The selected material may conflict with the preharvest interval. Error Fourteen: Failing to Update the Programme Weekly Risk level can change rapidly. Error Fifteen: Evaluating Success by Number of Sprays Alone The final criteria are accepted yield and sustainability.

text: No single programme is suitable for all tomato regions and production cycles in Egypt because the development rate of Tuta absoluta, number of generations, immigration sources, and crop stage differ among regions and seasons. Egyptian programmes should be built by integrating: Production type. Climatic production cycle. Geographical region. External pressure level. Crop stage. Processing tomatoes require protection of canopy cover and fruits, review of factory requirements, and immediate field termination after delivery ends. Fresh-market tomatoes require frequent fruit inspection, precise management of preharvest intervals between pickings, and organized handling of crates and rejected fruits. During winter, pest development may slow but does not stop, and long crop duration may permit several generations. Spring is characterized by the risk of faster development and moth movement from old winter fields. Summer requires closer inspection and faster response to eggs and young larvae, together with protection of biological agents from heat, dryness, and radiation. Autumn may begin under high pressure resulting from accumulated summer generations and intensive insecticide-use history. In the Delta, priorities include coordination among holdings and management of drainage canals, nurseries, and old fields. In Nubaria and Wadi El Natroun, priorities include dividing large areas into sectors, using maps, and organizing harvesting and residue management. On the North Coast and in Matrouh, data should be interpreted according to local climate and wind. In Upper Egypt, rapid pest development requires shorter intervals between inspection and decision, together with protection of biological agents from heat and dryness. Short-cycle crops require prevention from the transplant stage, whereas long-cycle crops require intensive resistance-management and biological-control systems. An Egyptian programme should not contain a fixed spray schedule, but a weekly system that reviews: Weather. Traps. Eggs. Larval instars. Fruit infestation. Natural enemies. Neighbouring fields. Pesticide record. Harvest timing. Programmes become sustainably applicable when they move from general recommendations to documented local protocols whose results and costs are reviewed at the end of every season. References
partTitle: Applied Programmes for Managing Tuta absoluta Under Egyptian Conditions

text: Integrated management programmes for Tuta absoluta do not always proceed as planned. Trap catches may rise suddenly, a wave of moths may enter from an old field, advanced larval instars may appear despite completed treatments, or fruit infestation may increase close to harvest. Not every sudden increase means the programme has collapsed, and survival of larvae after treatment does not necessarily mean that the pest has become resistant to the insecticide. Possible causes include: Trap-reading error. External immigration wave. Continued hatching of eggs laid before treatment. Poor coverage. Application against a relatively insensitive larval instar. Unsuitable weather. Reduced product quality. Failure to use the correct rate. Incompatibility with another component. Genuine resistance. The danger of an unscientific response lies in the engineer immediately resorting to: Increasing the dose. Mixing several materials. Repeating the same mode-of-action group. Shortening the interval between sprays. Cancelling biological control. Treating the whole field when infestation is confined to a hotspot. Such responses may increase resistance, kill natural enemies, and raise residues without addressing the true cause. Emergency management therefore depends on three rules: 1. Diagnose before escalating. 2. Contain the risk source before repeating treatment. 3. Evaluate economic viability before continuing costly interventions.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: An emergency is an acute or unexpected change in pest status or programme performance that requires a response faster than the routine decision cycle. It may include: A sharp increase in trap catch. Appearance of dense egg populations over a short period. A widespread hatching wave. First occurrence of fruit infestation. Increased larval abundance after treatment. Detection of infestation in transplants. Collapse of predators and parasitoids. Entry of an immigration wave from a neighbouring field. Inability to comply with harvest timing or the preharvest interval. Suspected insecticide resistance. Not every high reading should be classified as an emergency. It should also pose a risk to: Pest establishment. Clusters. Fruits. Programme efficacy. Marketable yield. Neighbouring fields.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

sectionTitle: Difference Between an Emergency and Natural Fluctuation
text: Tuta absoluta numbers may naturally change between inspection rounds because of: Temperature change. Changes in flight activity. Trap relocation. Lure ageing. Differences among inspectors. Uneven egg distribution. Female movement within the field. An increase becomes an emergency only after confirming: The measurement is correct. The trend is genuine. Active infestation or imminent risk is present. The time available for intervention is limited. The term “outbreak” should not be used on the basis of: One trap. One mine. One heavily damaged leaf. A small, unrepresentative sample.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: The response aims to: 1. Confirm that the problem exists. 2. Determine its location and scale. 3. Identify the dominant stage. 4. Identify the infestation source. 5. Protect sound fruits. 6. Prevent spread into new sectors. 7. Protect natural enemies. 8. Avoid repeating a failed material. 9. Collect evidence before changing the programme. 10. Reduce economic loss. 11. Prevent the field from becoming a regional source. 12. Derive lessons that prevent recurrence.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: An emergency plan should be prepared before a problem occurs and should include: A team with clearly defined responsibilities. A sector map of the field. Trap records. A list of registered materials. An IRAC-group record. Sampling equipment. Containers for preserving larvae. Access to a laboratory capable of conducting susceptibility tests. Equipment for collecting and destroying infested fruits. A communication channel with neighbouring fields. Biological and chemical intervention alternatives. A table of preharvest intervals. A crop-termination plan. An outbreak cannot be managed efficiently if the team begins searching for information only after infestation rises.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Depending on project size, the team may include: Monitoring engineer. Crop-protection engineer. Spray and calibration officer. Biological-control officer. Quality and residue officer. Harvest officer. Sector manager. Factory representative for processing tomatoes. Responsibilities should be defined: Who confirms the diagnosis? Who collects samples? Who stops use of the suspected insecticide? Who notifies neighbouring fields? Who reviews harvest timing? Who authorizes sector termination? Conflicting decisions should be prevented during emergencies.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Follow this sequence. Step One: Verification Reread the trap. Reinspect the crop. Confirm that the damage is caused by Tuta. Confirm that the mines are active. Step Two: Delimitation Define the hotspot. Identify affected edges. Separate unaffected sectors. Record coordinates. Step Three: Stage Identification Eggs. L1. L2. L3. L4. Pupae. Adult moths. Step Four: Source Identification Transplants. Old field. Internal reproduction. Residues. Sorting station. Immigration wave. Step Five: Programme Review Most recent treatment. IRAC group. Dose. Coverage. Weather. Natural enemies. Step Six: Containment Collect infested material. Treat the hotspot. Protect field edges. Stop movement of residues. Notify neighbours. Step Seven: Reassessment At a time appropriate to the development rate of the stage. Using the same indicator. With documented results.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: A common error is applying an emergency spray before collecting information, which prevents later diagnosis of the cause. Where time permits, before treatment: Photograph mines and fruits. Record infestation distribution. Collect live larvae. Record the product used previously. Preserve weather data. Inspect the sprayer. Retain the product container or batch number. Record mixing and application times. Identify the water source. This evidence helps distinguish among: Resistance. Poor application. Product deterioration. Subsequent hatching.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: A trap may record a large increase compared with the previous week while no clear plant symptoms are present. This does not automatically mean larvae are widespread in the field. The trap measures male activity and may reflect: Immigration. High sexual activity. A nearby old field. Weather change. Replacement with a stronger lure capsule. A different exposure period.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Confirm the lure-replacement date. 2. Confirm that exposure duration did not differ. 3. Inspect other traps. 4. Compare field edges with the interior. 5. Inspect young leaves for eggs. 6. Search for nearby old fields or residues. 7. Review wind direction. 8. Reinspect after a shorter interval.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: When Eggs and Mines Are Absent Do not apply a general spray. Intensify inspection. Identify the direction from which moths arrived. Read traps daily or at shorter intervals when risk is high. When Fresh Eggs Appear Begin estimating hatching time. Review egg-parasitoid activity. Prepare an intervention targeting hatching and young larvae. Protect the most exposed edge. When Several Larval Instars Are Present This indicates that the problem is not only recent immigration; an internal cycle is already established, and the decision level should be escalated.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Spraying on the basis of one trap reading. Mixing insecticides “as a precaution”. Assuming all moths originated inside the field. Ignoring eggs because mines are absent. Leaving the source field unmanaged.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Increased catches in traps facing the old field. More eggs on the field edge. Lower infestation in the field interior. An increase coinciding with cessation of irrigation, harvesting, or shredding. Presence of heavily infested old plants. Moth movement when plants are disturbed.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Determine the timing of operations in the old field. 2. Notify the manager of the source field. 3. Increase traps and inspection along the edge. 4. Record fresh eggs. 5. Increase egg-parasitoid releases where appropriate. 6. Time Bt or another early-stage agent to hatching. 7. Remove host plants between the two fields. 8. Prevent old-field residues from remaining.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

sectionTitle: Why Treating the New Field Alone Is Insufficient
text: A continuing source may cause: Repeated moth waves. New oviposition after each treatment. Higher control costs. Misinterpretation of immigration as insecticide failure. Repeated spraying without reducing pressure. The source should be managed or terminated while the recipient field is protected.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: When eggs are confined to the edge, the response may include: Intensified releases or monitoring along the edge. Sectoral or hotspot treatment. Continued monitoring of the interior. The whole area should not automatically be treated unless spread is confirmed.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure
