text: This often indicates: Immigration of females. Beginning of a new generation. A nearby source. Edge growth suitable for oviposition. This stage presents a strong opportunity because damage has not begun or remains limited.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Record: Number of eggs. Age and colour. Parasitism percentage. Plants carrying eggs. Distance of spread into the field. Presence of eggs on clusters or fruits. Presence of hatched eggshells. Estimate the number of live unparasitized eggs expected to hatch.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Intensify egg-parasitoid releases where the system permits. Protect naturally occurring parasitoids. Plan an intervention targeting hatching larvae. Reinspect before all eggs hatch. Identify the external source. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: The situation shifts from warning to corrective intervention when: Synchronous hatching appears. L1 numbers increase. Eggs spread into the field interior. Parasitism declines. Infestation approaches clusters. Development accelerates because of temperature.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: L1 and L2 represent the best opportunity to target larvae before they: Expand mines. Move into growing points. Enter stems. Reach fruits. Become less susceptible to some microbial agents.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Estimate the percentage of infested plants. 2. Determine whether infestation is localized or widespread. 3. Review eggs close to hatching. 4. Assess achievable coverage. 5. Review natural enemies. 6. Identify the most recently used IRAC group. 7. Select a compatible early-stage intervention.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Depending on registration and conditions, use: Bt. A registered insect virus. A suitable biological agent. A selective insecticide active against young instars. Hotspot treatment. Removal of a limited number of heavily infested leaves or plants without exposing fruits. The availability of several options should not delay action until larvae become advanced instars.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Measure: New live larvae. Mines initiated after treatment. Continued hatching. Expansion of the hotspot. Natural-enemy status. Total old damage should not be used to judge efficacy.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: This usually indicates: Delayed monitoring. Failure to target hatching. Poor coverage. An undetected hatching wave. Selection of an unsuitable intervention. Possible resistance. Part of the damage may already be irreversible.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Determine the distribution of advanced instars. 2. Inspect fruits immediately. 3. Use a registered product effective against the stage present when economically justified. 4. Collect heavily infested growing points or fruits in limited hotspots. 5. Protect new growth. 6. Review the reason for delayed detection. 7. Do not rely on Bt alone as a rescue treatment against advanced instars. 8. Review resistance and coverage.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: The objective is not to restore damaged tissues, but to: Kill surviving larvae. Protect uninfested fruits. Prevent formation of a new generation. Stop hotspot expansion. This should be made clear when treatment performance is evaluated.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Increasing the dose above the label rate. Random, closely spaced sprays. Repeated reliance on one material. Ignoring fruits while focusing only on leaves. Delaying harvest without reviewing risk.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

sectionTitle: Why Is This a Turning Point?
text: Fruit infestation means the pest has moved from vegetative damage that the plant can partly compensate for to direct loss of marketable or processable yield. A small hole may lead to: Fruit rejection. Entry of rot-causing organisms. Reduced raw-material quality. Presence of a larva in the consignment.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Confirm that the damage is caused by Tuta. 2. Measure the percentage of infested fruits. 3. Check for live larvae. 4. Identify larval instars present on leaves. 5. Collect infested fruits. 6. Inspect neighbouring fruits. 7. Increase protection of clusters. 8. Determine time remaining until harvest. 9. Review the preharvest interval.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: This depends on: Infestation percentage. Direction of change. Larval instar. Ripening stage. Market. Natural enemies. Previously used materials. The response may include: Localized collection. An early-stage agent against continuing hatching. A selective intervention. Advancing harvest. Terminating an uneconomic sector.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: A decline in leaf mines alone is insufficient. The following should stop increasing or decline: Newly infested fruits. Live larvae in fruits. Spread among clusters. Rejection percentage.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Several factors conflict at this stage: Need to protect fruits. Short time available. Preharvest interval. Residues. Ripening percentage. Factory or market timing. Slow action of some control methods. The best decision is not necessarily to apply another treatment.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Collect infested fruits. Advance harvest when maturity and specifications allow. Use a registered agent compatible with the remaining interval. Intensify sorting. Treat unripe hotspots. Immediately terminate completed sectors.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: When: Harvest begins before the effect will appear. The preharvest interval is longer than the time remaining. Most fruits are already infested. Treatment cost exceeds the value of fruits that can be protected. The sector is scheduled for termination.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Calculate: Protectable yield. Value of that yield. Intervention cost. Probability of success. Cost of delay. Residue risk. The decision should not be driven merely by reluctance to leave the field unsprayed.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

sectionTitle: Do Not Jump to a Resistance Diagnosis
text: Larvae may persist because of: Early assessment. Slow mode of action. Application against advanced instars. New hatching after treatment. Poor coverage. Failure of spray droplets to reach the inner canopy. Rate error. Product deterioration. Unsuitable weather. Genuine resistance.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Determine: When was treatment applied? How long should the material take to act? Did feeding stop before death? Were surviving larvae present at the time of treatment? Did they emerge from eggs that hatched later? A slow-acting insecticide should not be judged by the standard of a rapid knockdown product.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: If a product intended for young instars was applied to a population dominated by L3 and L4, poor performance may reflect incorrect product selection rather than resistance. Larval-instar distribution should be recorded before treatment.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Review: Actual treated area. Tank volume. Product concentration. Active-ingredient rate. Mixing order. Spray-water pH. Time the mixture remained in the tank. Nozzle blockage. Tractor speed. Do not assume the operator applied the correct rate without reviewing the record.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Inspect: Growing points. Lower leaf surfaces. Inner canopy. Leaves near clusters. Areas distant from the spray path. Apparent “insecticide failure” may occur in areas not reached by the spray, while efficacy remains good in well-covered areas.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Include: Wind. Temperature. Rain. Dew. Drying rate. Radiation. Rain after application or wind during spraying may reduce the amount reaching the plant.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Review: Container source. Registration. Expiry date. Batch number. Storage conditions. Exposure to heat. Possibility of counterfeiting or adulteration. Suspension uniformity. Retain a sample from the container when quality is suspected.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: If the Cause Is Poor Application Correct calibration. Retrain the operator. Improve coverage. Do not describe the case as resistance. If the Cause Is Larval Instar Select a method appropriate to the stage. Improve monitoring in the next generation. If the Cause Is New Hatching Recalibrate intervention timing. Continue egg monitoring. If Resistance Remains Suspected Stop repeating the group. Collect samples. Use a different registered group. Begin susceptibility testing.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Resistance becomes more likely when several lines of evidence coincide: Repeated use of the same group. Survival of larvae after a well-executed treatment. Use of the registered rate. Appropriate larval stage. Good coverage. No rain or storage error. Repeated failure in more than one field. Success of other groups with different modes of action. Failure of one spray is insufficient to prove resistance.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Collect live larvae from: Well-covered locations. Several points within the field. More than one instar when necessary. Fields in which failure was recorded. Record: Material used. Rate. Treatment date. Number of previous applications. Region. Cultivar. Transplanting date. Larvae from unsprayed edges should not be used to prove resistance.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: A standardized method should preferably be used, such as the IRAC method for Tuta absoluta larvae, with: Known concentrations of the active ingredient. An untreated control. Uniform-age larvae. Controlled test conditions. Comparison with a susceptible strain or baseline. Distinguish among: A diagnostic test. A complete dose–response curve. An uncontrolled field test.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Suspend the suspected group in the field and nearby area. Do not increase the dose. Do not mix it with another material to conceal failure. Use a method with a different mode of action. Strengthen biological and mechanical control. Notify the technical team. Monitor neighbouring fields.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: The programme should: 1. Exclude the group from the current window. 2. Review potential cross-resistance. 3. Update the regional record. 4. Coordinate neighbouring fields. 5. Reduce the number of applications. 6. Introduce non-chemical periods. 7. Avoid rapid reintroduction of the group. 8. Monitor susceptibility in the future. 9. Train suppliers and growers. 10. Document the case formally.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Increasing the dose. Mixing two failed materials. Switching to another trade name within the same group. Applying several materials at short intervals. Using unregistered mixtures. Concealing the failure case.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Disappearance of predator nymphs and adults. Sudden decline in parasitism percentage. Absence of parasitoids after good-quality releases. Increase in secondary pests. Increase in Tuta following a broad-spectrum treatment. Presence of dead beneficial organisms on leaves.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: An incompatible insecticide. Drift from a neighbouring field. Lack of prey or food. Severe heat or dryness. Poor quality of the natural-enemy batch. Poor transport and storage. Unsuitable release timing. Removal of the biological belt. Tank mixtures or fungicides affecting microbial agents.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Stop the causal factor where possible. 2. Review materials used. 3. Protect areas still carrying beneficial organisms. 4. Re-release after hazardous residues have declined. 5. Provide food or supporting plants when required. 6. Use selective materials. 7. Treat hotspots rather than the whole area. 8. Measure success of re-establishment.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Collapse of natural enemies should not immediately be replaced with an intensive spray programme because this may make the collapse permanent and prevent recovery. A temporary bridge should combine: A selective intervention. Collection of infested material. Re-release. Edge protection. Close monitoring.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Infestation carried with transplants is more likely when: Mines appear only a few days after transplanting. Infestation is concentrated in one batch. Advanced instars are present that do not match the time spent in the field. Infestation recurs in fields supplied by the same batch.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Stop transplanting the remainder of the batch. 2. Map the location of every planted tray. 3. Inspect neighbouring plants. 4. Remove heavily infested transplants. 5. Collect contaminated leaves and trays. 6. Notify the nursery. 7. Inspect associated batches. 8. Intensify monitoring. 9. Implement an early intervention appropriate to the stage.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Review: Screening. Traps. Location of mother plants. Weeds. Pesticide records. Tray movement. Disposal of rejected plants. Other dispatched batches. An infested batch should not be distributed to additional fields.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Spraying the transplants rapidly and continuing distribution. Leaving rejected plants near the nursery. Failing to notify other fields. Concealing the infestation source. Treating nursery applications as separate from the field IRAC plan.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

sectionTitle: Risk Posed by the Neglected Field
text: It may function as a continuous moth factory when: Live leaves and fruits remain available. Control has stopped. Life stages overlap. Plants remain after the final harvest. Fruits are left on the ground.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Document the location. Contact the responsible person. Estimate infestation level. Set an urgent removal date. Notify recipient fields. Increase edge monitoring. Prevent movement of residues. Provide shredding equipment where necessary.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: In large projects or contract farming systems, there should be: Contractual requirements. Clear responsibility. Support for field removal. A reporting system. A regulatory authority. Repeatedly spraying the sound field will not address the immigration source.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Measure: Elimination of green cover. Absence of remaining fruits. Prevention of regrowth. Decline in surrounding trap catches. Absence of new eggs in the recipient field.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Continued high catches in internal traps. Appearance of fertile eggs. Increasing mine density. Inadequate distribution of dispensers or traps. High immigration pressure. Incomplete coverage of the treated area. Expired lures.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Review: Dispenser or trap rate. Distribution. Installation date. Storage. Height. Treated-area size. Field shape. Untreated surrounding fields. Entry of already mated females.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Replace damaged or expired dispensers or lures. Correct gaps in distribution. Manage immigration sources. Integrate the method with egg parasitoids and larval agents. Continue plant inspection. Do not declare success from reduced trap catch alone.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

sectionTitle: When Is the Technique Less Suitable?
text: It may be less effective when: The treated area is small and surrounded by high infestation. Field shape is irregular. Neighbouring fields do not participate. Application begins after the population has increased. Large numbers of mated females immigrate. An appropriate pheromone density cannot be maintained.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Use against advanced instars. Radiation exposure. Wash-off by rain. Poor storage. Low product viability. Poor coverage. Unsuitable temperature or humidity. Unsuitable water. Mixing with an incompatible material. Assessment too early or too late.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Review: Larval age. Application time. Solar radiation. Humidity. Production and expiry dates. Storage conditions. Water source. Mixed materials. Coverage quality. Feeding cessation before death.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Redirect use toward hatching and young instars. Apply at an environmentally suitable time. Use a sound batch. Avoid unverified mixtures. Integrate with egg parasitoids or another compatible method. Do not use it alone in an advanced outbreak.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

sectionTitle: When Should It Not Be Used Alone?
text: When: L3 and L4 dominate. Fruits are already infested. Time before harvest is short. Immigration is continuing. Suitable application conditions are unavailable.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: These may wash off: Bt. Viruses. Some surface residues. Fungal spores. Review: Time between application and rainfall. Amount of water. Product rainfastness. Infestation level. Treatment should not be repeated automatically without evaluation.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: This may cause: Faster hatching. Rapid droplet drying. Reduced survival of some natural enemies. Reduced performance of fungi and nematodes. Plant stress. It requires: Shorter inspection intervals. Changing application time. Protecting biological-control agents. Avoiding increased concentration to compensate for heat.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: These may cause: Spray drift. Poor coverage. Trap contamination. Disturbance of pheromone dispersion. Desiccation of biological organisms. The programme should: Stop application under unsuitable conditions. Clean traps. Inspect drift. Reassess poorly covered sectors.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: This may slow: Pest development. Expression of some product effects. Natural-enemy movement. Slow mortality should not be interpreted as failure before the appropriate assessment period has elapsed.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Widespread fruit infestation. Multiple pest stages. Collapse of canopy cover. Failure of successive interventions. Proximity to the end of the crop cycle. High cost of continuation. Potential crop rejection. Transformation of the field into a source.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Continue control to protect clearly economic yield. 2. Advance harvest. 3. Terminate a defined sector. 4. Terminate the entire field. 5. Harvest marketable fruits before removal. 6. Prevent residue movement. 7. Protect neighbouring fields.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Calculate: Weight of protectable fruits. Their value. Intervention costs. Harvest cost. Probability of rejection. Effect of delay. Risk posed to new crops. The programme should not continue merely because costs have already been incurred. The decision should be based on expected future benefit.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: This is justified when: Loss has already occurred. No effective options exist within the preharvest interval. Continuation costs exceed crop value. The sector is a strong pest source. The remainder of the field can be protected by isolating and terminating the sector.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Notify neighbours. Harvest acceptable fruits where possible. Collect infested fruits. Kill plants within a short period. Shred residues. Prevent uncovered transport. Clean equipment. Continue trap monitoring. Remove regrowth.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Leaving the infested sector to dry naturally. Applying several final sprays without economic justification. Moving infested fruits among farms. Shredding plants while leaving fruits behind. Moving equipment into a young field without cleaning.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Situation First Step Principal Action Action to Avoid Increased trap catch only Inspect plants and identify the source Intensify monitoring Automatic spraying Dense eggs along the edge Estimate live eggs and parasitism Parasitoids and hatching-targeted action Broad-spectrum insecticide harmful to parasitoids Widespread L1 and L2 Determine distribution Compatible early intervention Delay L3 and L4 Inspect fruits Corrective intervention and diagnosis of delay Reliance on Bt alone First infested fruits Measure infestation Collect infested fruits and protect sound fruits Waiting for damage to increase Infestation near harvest Review PHI and ripeness Harvest or appropriate intervention Incompatible product Larvae after spraying Review timing and coverage Diagnose the cause Immediate declaration of resistance Suspected resistance Collect samples Suspend the group and switch Increasing the dose Collapse of beneficials Review pesticides Protect and re-establish Broad spray programme Infested transplants Stop the batch Isolate and inspect the nursery Continuing distribution Neglected neighbouring field Identify the responsible person Terminate the source Protecting only the recipient field Failure of mating disruption Review distribution and immigration Correct the system and integrate methods Relying on it alone Poor Bt performance Review stage and conditions Recalibrate timing Rescue use against large larvae Uneconomic outbreak Analyse yield and cost Harvest or terminate Continuing unprofitable spraying
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Item Data Date and time Field and sector Person reporting Case description Trap status Eggs Larval instars Active mines Fruit infestation Natural enemies Most recent treatment IRAC group Sprayer condition Weather Potential source Samples collected Decision Assessment date Result Lessons learned
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: After control is restored or the field is terminated, conduct a review answering: When did the problem actually begin? When was it detected? Was reporting delayed? Were traps functioning? Was the sample adequate? Which stage was missed? Was coverage adequate? Was an IRAC group repeated? Was the source internal or external? Were natural enemies protected? Was the decision economically justified? What modification is required for the following season? Results should be converted into a written procedure rather than left as verbal observations.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: Error One: Spraying Before Diagnosis This may destroy evidence of the true cause. Error Two: Declaring Resistance After One Treatment The cause may be technical or related to timing. Error Three: Increasing the Dose This raises risk without restoring susceptibility. Error Four: Mixing Several Insecticides This may conceal failure and increase resistance and residues. Error Five: Repeating the Same Group This intensifies resistant selection. Error Six: Ignoring the Source Field This leads to continued reinvasion. Error Seven: Delaying Response Until Larvae Reach Fruits Opportunities for protection become limited. Error Eight: Relying on Old Mines This leads to unnecessary spraying. Error Nine: Failing to Document Weather Treatment interpretation becomes difficult. Error Ten: Failing to Collect Live Samples This prevents susceptibility testing. Error Eleven: Killing Natural Enemies During an Emergency This creates a later resurgence. Error Twelve: Delaying Field-Termination Decisions This turns the field into a regional source. Error Thirteen: Concealing Failure This allows it to spread to other fields. Error Fourteen: Repeating Treatment Without an Assessment Date Its efficacy cannot be determined. Error Fifteen: Treating Every Emergency as an Insecticide Problem The problem may originate in the nursery, residues, or harvest management.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: 1. Establish a standardized protocol for investigating insecticide failure. 2. Train engineers to determine larval-instar distribution. 3. Provide laboratories for susceptibility testing. 4. Use a standardized method for Tuta absoluta. 5. Link failure cases to the IRAC record. 6. Notify neighbouring farms of immigration waves. 7. Prevent dose increases and unregistered mixtures. 8. Trace batch numbers of suspected products. 9. Test spray-water quality. 10. Calibrate sprayers regularly. 11. Establish a register of source fields. 12. Require nursery traceability. 13. Establish a plan for removing neglected fields. 14. Include quality and residue officers in emergency decisions. 15. Develop an economic decision process for advancing harvest or terminating a sector. 16. Protect natural enemies during corrective intervention. 17. Provide equipment for collecting residues. 18. Conduct a written review after every case. 19. Share confirmed resistance cases among regions. 20. Convert emergency experience into updates to the decision matrix.
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: A sudden increase in Tuta absoluta does not mean that the solution is to increase the number of sprays, and survival of larvae after treatment does not prove that resistance has developed. Emergency management begins with: Verifying data accuracy. Delimiting the hotspot. Identifying the insect stage. Determining the infestation source. Reviewing the previous application. Protecting sound fruits. Containing the source. Immigration waves from old fields are among the most important causes of sudden increases, and they cannot be managed sustainably by treating the recipient field alone. Eggs appearing along field edges provide an opportunity to intervene before damage occurs, while L1 and L2 represent the best window for larval control. Dominance of L3 and L4 indicates delayed monitoring, poor timing, or poor coverage and requires protection of the remaining crop together with review of the causes of failure. Appearance of the first infested fruit is an escalation point at which infestation percentage should be measured, infested fruits collected, and harvest timing and the preharvest interval reviewed. When larvae persist after insecticide application, review: Assessment timing. Larval instar. Dose. Coverage. Weather. Product quality. New hatching. Resistance should not be declared until these factors have been excluded and an appropriate susceptibility test has been conducted. When resistance is suspected, stop repeating the mode-of-action group, do not increase the dose, and use different alternatives within an integrated programme. Collapse of natural enemies may also directly cause pest resurgence. They should therefore be protected and re-established rather than completely replaced by an intensive chemical programme. During an advanced outbreak, continuing control should not become an objective in itself. Advancing harvest or terminating an uneconomic sector may be the most efficient decision. Emergency management succeeds when every failure case becomes information that improves: Monitoring. Timing. Application. Resistance management. Coordination among fields. Economic decision-making. References
partTitle: Emergency Scenarios and Management of IPM Failure

text: An outbreak of Tuta absoluta does not begin when infested fruits appear. It begins earlier, during a period in which several warning signals may pass unnoticed or be interpreted incorrectly. The first indication of increasing risk may be: A gradual increase in male trap catches. Appearance of eggs along field edges. A decline in parasitism percentage. An increase in the proportion of eggs close to hatching. An increase in first- and second-instar larvae. Beginning of removal of a nearby old tomato field. Rising temperature that accelerates development. A decline in predators after use of a broad-spectrum insecticide. Repetition of an insecticide group to which susceptibility has declined. Movement of infestation from leaves to growing points and clusters. The function of early warning is to detect these changes before they develop into: Extensive mines. Advanced larval instars. Fruit infestation. An internal reproduction wave. Economic loss. Intensive dependence on insecticides. Early warning does not mean certain prediction of the future. It means estimating the probability of increasing infestation on the basis of a set of biological, climatic, spatial, and operational indicators. The earlier, more accurate, more action-linked, and more verifiable the warning is, the greater its value in preventing an outbreak.

Early warning is an organized system for collecting and analysing a set of data to detect changes that precede an economically important increase in the pest, followed by issuance of an alert specifying: Location of the risk. Risk level. Most likely cause. Time available. Required action. Reassessment date. Sending a trap count alone does not constitute an early-warning system because that number does not explain: Whether females are present. Whether mating has occurred. Whether eggs have been laid. Whether the eggs are viable. Whether larvae are present. Whether fruits are threatened. Whether the increase originated inside or outside the field.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Monitoring, Early Warning, and Forecasting
text: Monitoring Monitoring Monitoring is the periodic measurement of pest status, such as: Number of moths in traps. Number of eggs. Number of active mines. Percentage of infested fruits. It answers the question: What is happening now? Early Warning Early Warning Early warning is the interpretation of current changes that may lead to a near-term increase. It answers the question: Are there signs that risk is escalating? Forecasting Forecasting Forecasting uses temperature, population, stage, and immigration-source data to estimate: Hatching time. Appearance of a new generation. The likely period of increasing moth activity. Probability that infestation will move into fruits. It answers the question: What may happen next, and when? Action Alert Action Alert An action alert links the warning to a specific measure, such as: Increasing inspection. Inspecting the eastern edge. Estimating parasitism percentage. Preparing an intervention targeting hatching. Notifying recently planted fields that an old field is being removed.

A system may fail because of: Dependence on one trap. Use of different lure and trap types. Delay in reading data. Failure to inspect plants. Use of a non-local threshold. Ignoring egg and larval age. Failure to link results with temperature. Neglect of old fields. Failure to measure natural enemies. Issuing a warning without specifying an action. Delivering the message after larvae have entered fruits. Absence of a person responsible for response. The value of the system does not lie in collecting the largest possible quantity of data, but in detecting indicators that change the decision before loss occurs.

Leading Indicators Leading Indicators These appear before economic damage, such as: Consecutive increases in trap catches. Beginning of oviposition. Eggs close to hatching. Rising temperature. Removal of an old field. Declining parasitism. Declining predator abundance. Repetition of a material with weak performance. They allow preventive action. Lagging Indicators Lagging Indicators These appear after the problem has advanced, such as: Numerous large mines. Dominance of L3 and L4. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation. Rots. Reduced accepted yield. They are useful for assessing damage severity but do not always provide sufficient time to prevent it. Early warning should therefore rely on leading indicators, while lagging indicators are used to verify its accuracy.

The system consists of seven principal layers: 1. Pheromone traps. 2. Egg inspection. 3. Mine and larval inspection. 4. Fruit and cluster monitoring. 5. Natural-enemy monitoring. 6. Climatic and thermal data. 7. Field maps and external sources. No single layer is sufficient to issue a reliable warning.

sectionTitle: Role of Traps in Early Warning
text: Traps are used to detect: Beginning of male activity. Population trend. Flight waves. Differences between field edges and the interior. Activity resulting from old fields. Persistence of moths after crop removal. Traps are among the earliest indicators, but they do not directly measure: Female abundance. Mating. Oviposition. Larval density. Fruit infestation. They should therefore be used as a gateway for increasing inspection, not as an independent spray order.

For comparisons to be valid, standardize: Trap design. Type of adhesive or water. Pheromone type. Supplier or formulation where possible. Lure-installation date. Trap height. Location. Exposure duration. Reading date. Cleaning and replacement method. Changing lure type may increase catches without a genuine increase in the pest population.

sectionTitle: Converting Catch to a Comparable Unit
text: When trap operating periods differ, calculate: Mean daily catch = Total males captured ÷ Actual number of operating days Example: A trap captured 35 males over 7 days. Mean daily catch = 5 males/trap/day. Exclude days during which the trap was: Dry. Missing. Covered with dust. Saturated. Non-functional.

The current reading should not be interpreted alone; it should be compared with a time series. Trends may be classified as: Stable Low Trend No clear increase across readings. Single Increase An increase in one round that may result from temporary immigration, a lure change, or weather. Consecutive Increase An increase over two or more rounds, whose importance rises when accompanied by fresh eggs. Peak Followed by Decline This may represent a flight wave, and eggs and hatching arising from it should be investigated. Continued Increase Despite Intervention This may indicate: A continuing external source. Failure of mating disruption. An internal generation. Poor management of old fields.

A descriptive indicator may be calculated: Edge-to-interior ratio = Mean catch in edge traps ÷ Mean catch in interior traps When the ratio is clearly high and a nearby external source is present, external immigration becomes more likely. When interior traps increase and several life stages are distributed throughout the field, internal reproduction becomes more likely. The ratio is not a fixed threshold. It is a comparative tool for the same field over time.

Catch becomes more important when combined with one or more of the following: Beginning of removal of a neighbouring field. Appearance of fresh eggs. Rising temperature. Decline in parasitoid activity. Concentration of catches along one edge. Presence of an attractive young crop. A previous history of immigration waves during the same period. A high trap catch without plant evidence should trigger an inspection alert, not a spray alert.

Eggs are the closest direct indicator that a new generation is becoming established in the field. Their detection allows: Identification of oviposition sites. Prediction of hatching. Evaluation of egg parasitoids. Timing of Bt or another early-stage agent. Containment of the edge before larvae spread. Early warning should focus on: Fresh eggs. Eggs close to hatching. Live unparasitized eggs.

Calculate: Plants carrying eggs (%) = Number of plants on which eggs were found ÷ Total plants inspected × 100 This indicator measures the distribution of oviposition, not only egg density. There may be: High density on a few plants. Or low density distributed over a large area. The response differs between these situations.

This may be expressed as: Eggs/plant. Eggs/leaf. Eggs per fixed number of leaflets. Eggs/sampling unit. The unit should remain constant among inspection rounds. Data collected from: One leaf in one round. And three leaves in another. Should not be compared directly.

sectionTitle: Egg Age as a Temporal Indicator
text: Eggs should be classified as: Newly laid. Intermediate age. Close to hatching. Hatched. Parasitized. Dead. This classification is important because: Fresh eggs provide more time for release or preparation. Eggs close to hatching indicate that the response window is narrowing. Hatched eggshells indicate larvae have already begun to emerge. Recording only “eggs present” without identifying their status is insufficient.

An approximate indicator may be calculated: Eggs at risk of hatching = Total live eggs − Parasitized or dead eggs This number represents a more direct risk than the raw total number of eggs. However, consider: Difficulty of visually detecting early parasitism. Possibility of non-viable eggs. Variation in timing of colour change. When uncertain, retain samples for rearing or magnified examination.

Field studies have shown that Tuta absoluta eggs may have an aggregated distribution and that high densities may occur along crop edges. An increase in eggs along one edge warns of: A nearby immigration source. Beginning of localized colonization. Potential spread along rows. Inspection should be increased: Along the edge. In the following rows. Along the direction of planting. In the interior to confirm the extent of spread.

Appearance of the first new mines indicates that: Hatching has occurred. The opportunity to target eggs has partly passed. Larvae have begun feeding. The programme requires rapid intervention or assessment. New mines should not be confused with old mines.

sectionTitle: Percentage of Plants with Active Mines
text: Calculate: Plants with active mines (%) = Number of plants carrying at least one active mine ÷ Total plants inspected × 100 This indicator measures the spread of infestation. Also record: Number of active mines per plant. Their location on the plant. Larval instar present.

An increasing proportion of early instars warns of: A recent hatching wave. An intervention opportunity that is still available. A likely rapid increase in mines over the following days. The response should occur before they develop into: L3 and L4. Growing-point infestation. Fruit entry. The warning value is greater under rising temperatures.

This is not an early-warning indicator in the strict sense. It shows that part of the preventive opportunity has already been lost. It indicates: Delayed egg detection. Weak inspection. Failure of an early intervention. Presence of an old hotspot. Possible proximity to fruit infestation. The field should be escalated from monitoring to corrective response.

A group of plants or leaves can be marked and reinspected. Calculate: Rate of new-mine appearance = Number of mines appearing since the previous round ÷ Number of plants or days This is more informative than the cumulative total number of mines. It helps determine: Is infestation still increasing? Did it stop after intervention? Is hatching continuing?

sectionTitle: Growing-Point Infestation as an Escalation Signal
text: Appearance of mines or larvae in growing points indicates: Activity on young tissues. Risk of losing terminal growth. Proximity of larvae to flowers and clusters. Greater difficulty in reaching them. The indicator becomes more serious in: Transplants. Young plants. Determinate cultivars. The beginning of flowering.

Eggs or mines on leaves touching clusters are a stronger warning signal than those on distant lower leaves. Risk signs include: Eggs near flowers. Larvae in leaves adjacent to fruits. Frass near the calyx. Initial holes in flowers or sepals. Larval movement between a leaf and a cluster. Inspection frequency should be increased without waiting for large holes to appear in fruits.

The first confirmed infested fruit marks a transition from warning to actual economic damage. Immediately: Measure infestation percentage. Determine whether live larvae are present. Inspect neighbouring clusters. Review larval instars on leaves. Collect the infested fruit. Raise the warning level. One fruit is not evidence of a general outbreak, but it should not be ignored.

sectionTitle: Increase in Infested Fruits Between Rounds
text: Calculate: Rate of increase in fruit infestation = Current infestation percentage − Previous infestation percentage When the rate increases over two rounds, this indicates that: Larvae are still reaching fruits. The previous intervention did not stop infestation. The hatching wave is continuing. Leaf inspection may not fully represent the risk. The response level should be escalated even if trap catch declines.

sectionTitle: Natural Enemies as Part of Early Warning
text: An early-warning system should not measure the pest alone because outbreak probability is influenced by the capacity of natural enemies to slow population growth. Monitor: Egg parasitism. Larval parasitoids. Predators. Nymphs. Natural fungal or microbial infections. Effects of insecticides on these organisms. An increase in eggs may be less serious when accompanied by strong, stable biological regulation, whereas a limited increase may be more serious when beneficial organisms have collapsed.

A decline in parasitism becomes a warning when: It was high and then fell suddenly. It coincided with insecticide use. Live unparasitized eggs increased. A hatching wave appeared. Parasitoids failed to emerge from release cards. Investigate: Residual toxicity. Quality of the parasitoid batch. Temperature. Distribution of release points. Release timing relative to egg age.

Parasitism percentage alone is insufficient. Example: If there are: 20 eggs/sampling unit. And parasitism is 70%. Approximately 6 eggs remain unparasitized. If there are: Only 2 eggs. And parasitism is 40%. Approximately 1.2 eggs remain unparasitized. The first situation may therefore be more serious despite the higher parasitism percentage.

A decline in predators is a warning when it coincides with: Broad-spectrum spraying. Removal of the biological belt. Decline in alternative prey. Increased temperature or dryness. Rising egg and larval abundance. Distinguish between: A temporary decline due to movement. A genuine collapse indicated by absence of nymphs and reproduction.

An internal descriptive indicator may compare: Egg and larval density. Predator density. There is no universal fixed ratio for Tuta absoluta that applies to all species and conditions. However, rapid pest increase while predator density remains stable or declines indicates weakening natural regulation.

sectionTitle: Temperature as a Principal Driver of Development
text: Temperature affects: Egg-incubation period. Larval-development rate. Pupal duration. Adult emergence. Number of generations. Reinspection timing. Temperature alone should not be used to predict damage because population dynamics are also influenced by: Humidity. Host plants. Immigration. Natural enemies. Insecticides. Soil and canopy conditions.

Degree-Days Degree-days measure accumulated heat above the lower developmental threshold. In the simplest form: Daily degree-days = Mean daily temperature − Lower developmental threshold Provided that the mean temperature lies within the developmental range. More accurate calculations may use: Simple averaging. Single triangle. Sine-wave methods. Upper and lower thermal cut-offs. One consistent, locally validated model should be used.

Studies have produced different values for developmental thresholds and thermal constants according to: Strain. Insect origin. Temperatures tested. Analytical method. Life stage calculated. Diet. Laboratory conditions. Therefore, one should not combine: A threshold from one study. And a thermal constant from another. The model should be used as an integrated unit.

Forecasting may begin from: A clear flight peak. Or detection of a fresh egg cohort. Thermal accumulation is then used to estimate: Beginning of hatching. Peak L1 period. Timing of the next generation. Sex-pheromone traps do not precisely identify oviposition date. Hatching forecasts are therefore more accurate when temperature is linked to eggs observed on the plant.

sectionTitle: Rising Temperature as a Signal to Shorten the Interval
text: When temperature rises over several days: Shorten the interval between inspections. Do not wait for the traditional weekly visit. Review eggs close to hatching. Accelerate distribution of egg parasitoids. Prepare an L1-targeted intervention. A change in developmental speed is itself a warning, even before pest density changes.

When temperature declines: Development slows. Life stages persist longer. Flight activity may decline. Treatment effects may be delayed. Eggs and larvae may nevertheless continue, and a later increase in temperature may produce a synchronized development wave. Monitoring should therefore not stop during cool periods.

Temperatures at: The leaf surface. Inside a mine. Inside a fruit. In the soil. Within the canopy. May differ from weather-station temperature. Models should therefore be interpreted cautiously, especially in: Dense crops. Desert areas. Extremely hot days. Frequently irrigated fields. A weather station as close to the field as possible should be used.

Humidity affects: Survival of entomopathogenic fungi. Activity of some natural enemies. Egg survival. Plant condition. Wind affects: Moth movement. Immigration direction. Pheromone dispersion. Pesticide drift. Desiccation of biological agents. Rainfall or overhead irrigation may affect: Residues. Traps. Fungi. Hatching rate. These factors should be integrated with temperature rather than used separately.

sectionTitle: An Old Field as an Early-Warning Indicator
text: An old field becomes a risk source when: It approaches the end of harvest. Control stops. It remains green. It contains infested fruits. Plants begin to dry. Shredding or removal begins. The expected date of each operation should be recorded because immigration may begin before complete removal.

The warning map should include: Transplanting date of each field. Crop stage. Infestation level. Harvest date. Removal date. Field position relative to prevailing wind. Approximate distance. Presence of residues. A change in a neighbouring field from: Active production → Final harvest → Irrigation cessation → Removal Represents a sequence of risk signals for recently planted fields.

The warning level should be raised near: A nursery with confirmed infestation. A sorting station where rejected fruits accumulate. A factory that fails to manage waste. A tomato-crate collection area. Transport or harvest waste. Monitoring traps should be installed and surrounding plants inspected at these sites.

Increasing infestation in: Potato. Eggplant. Wild solanaceous plants. Volunteer tomatoes. May warn of risk to nearby tomato crops. However, it should be confirmed that the host supports completion of the pest life cycle rather than merely carrying transient individuals.

An administrative alert should be issued when: Traps stop functioning. Readings are delayed. The inspector is absent. Sample sizes decline. Old and new mines are confused. Fruits are not inspected. Data arrive after the decision deadline. Absence of data is itself a risk factor because infestation may develop undetected.

The installation date of every lure should be recorded. Before its effective period ends: Prepare a replacement. Replace it without a monitoring gap. Avoid changing all lure types mid-series without documentation. Do not interpret a decline caused by a weak lure as a decline in the pest.

Repetition of an IRAC group is a warning of risk of: Reduced susceptibility. Resistance. Failure of the following intervention. Increased survival of larvae. The warning dashboard should display: Group used. Date of use. Number of applications. Performance level.

sectionTitle: Declining Performance of the Previous Treatment
text: This becomes an escalation indicator when: New mines continue to appear. Live larvae remain in well-covered areas. Fruit infestation increases. The pattern recurs in more than one sector. First verify: Assessment timing. Larval instar. Coverage. Weather. New hatching.

Every day that a completed field remains with living plants may increase the opportunity for: Completion of life stages. Adult emergence. Movement into new fields. The following should be included among regional warning indicators: Number of days from final harvest to field termination

Indicators: Low or stable trap catch. Absence of a substantial live egg population. Absence of new mines. No fruit infestation. Stable natural enemies. No active external source. Action: Continue routine monitoring. Maintain traps. Protect natural enemies. Do not implement an unjustified intervention.

Possible indicators: A single increase or the beginning of an upward trap trend. Appearance of limited eggs. Presence of a nearby old field. Rising temperature. A limited decline in parasitism. Failure in part of the monitoring network. Action: Increase inspection. Inspect field edges. Correct the trap network. Estimate egg age. Prepare for early intervention.

Indicators: Consecutive increases in trap catch. Repeated or widespread eggs. Recent hatching. Increasing L1 and L2. Declining natural enemies. Beginning of source-field removal. Appearance of new mines in more than one sector. Action: Apply a biological, microbial, or localized intervention. Protect field edges. Shorten the reassessment interval. Review the IRAC record. Notify the technical team.

Indicators: Widespread L2 and L3. Infestation of growing points and clusters. First confirmed fruit infestation. Continued increase after intervention. Continuous immigration wave. Clear weakness of natural enemies. Action: Apply a selective corrective intervention. Collect infested fruits. Protect sound fruits. Investigate the source. Conduct rapid outcome assessment.

Indicators: High densities of several life stages. Increasing fruit infestation. Failure of successive treatments. Suspected resistance. Collapse of beneficial organisms. Proximity to harvest with limited options. Declining economic viability. Transformation of the field into a source. Action: Activate the emergency plan. Collect samples. Suspend the suspected group. Evaluate advancing harvest or terminating the sector. Notify neighbouring fields. Contain residues.

Traps Eggs Larval Instars Fruits Temperature and Source Warning Level Stable Absent Absent Sound No source Green Single increase Absent Absent Sound Nearby old field Yellow Increasing Limited Absent Sound Rising temperature High yellow Increasing Repeated Limited L1 Sound Potential source Orange High Eggs and hatching Widespread L1 and L2 Sound Nearby field being removed High orange Any level Present L2 and L3 First infestation Suitable temperature Red Fluctuating Continuous Multiple instars Increasing infestation Active source Critical Low Absent L3 and L4 Infested fruits Internal generation Red or critical

An internal index may be developed to rank field priority. Example: Indicator Zero One Point Two Points Three Points Traps Low Single increase Consecutive increase Sharp increase Eggs Absent Limited Repeated Dense and close to hatching Larvae Absent Localized L1 Widespread L1–L2 L3–L4 or multiple instars Fruits Sound Suspect Limited infestation Increasing infestation Beneficials High Moderate Low Collapsed External source Absent Possible Nearby Active and being removed Temperature Slow development Stable Rising Rapid acceleration Previous treatment Successful Unclear Weak Repeated failure The total score may be used to: Rank field visits. Identify urgent sectors. Compare weekly trends. It should not be used alone to issue a spray decision and should not be adopted before local calibration.

sectionTitle: Change-Based Warning Rather Than Absolute Numbers
text: In some fields, baseline trap catch is relatively high while plant damage remains low. Therefore: Weekly rate of change May be more useful than the absolute count. Calculate: Relative change (%) = [(Current reading − Previous reading) ÷ Previous reading] × 100 When the previous reading is zero or very low, misleading mathematical interpretation should be avoided. Use the actual number and field context.

The rate of increase may be compared between periods. Example: Mines increased from 1 to 2. Then from 2 to 5. This indicates acceleration of the increase. Acceleration becomes important when combined with: Rising temperature. Declining parasitism. Spread of L1 and L2. The crop approaching the fruiting stage.

This aims to predict what may occur over the following days, such as: Hatching of an egg cohort. Progression of L1 to L2. Appearance of new mines. Larval arrival at clusters. It uses: Egg age. Temperature. Date of detection. Existing instars. Results of the previous inspection round. It is more accurate than forecasts extending over several weeks.

This aims to estimate: Timing of a new adult generation. Period of increasing trap catch. Timing of risk of movement among fields. End of an old-field cycle. It requires: A thermal model. A trap time series. A crop-cycle map. Estimates of stage survival. Uncertainty increases as the forecast period lengthens.

The model begins from a clear biological event, such as: A flight peak. Beginning of oviposition. A hatching wave. Degree-days are then accumulated to estimate: Completion of a stage. Appearance of the following generation. The forecast should be validated in the field using: Eggs. Mines. Traps. When the forecast diverges from field observations, review: Developmental threshold. Weather-station data. Starting date. Source of immigrating moths.

sectionTitle: Thermal Models Do Not Calculate Immigration
text: A thermal model may forecast development of locally present individuals, but it cannot by itself predict: Entry of moths from a distant field. Transport of infested transplants. Removal of a neighbouring field. Movement of fruits or residues. Models should therefore be integrated with regional mapping and trap data.

The bulletin should include: 1. Issue date. 2. Region. 3. Risk level. 4. Trap trend. 5. Dominant pest stage. 6. Crop stage. 7. Expected temperature. 8. Source fields. 9. Natural-enemy status. 10. Required action. 11. Next inspection date. 12. Confidence level of the forecast. It should not contain an automatic recommendation for one insecticide across all fields.

Situation: A limited increase in male catch along the northern edge coinciding with the beginning of removal of a nearby old tomato field, with no confirmed active mines to date. Action: Inspect eggs along the northern edge. Increase sample size. Recheck traps after a shorter interval. Do not spray on the basis of catch alone. Notify the team when eggs or hatching are detected.

Situation: Consecutive increases in trap catches, appearance of fresh eggs and initial L1 hatching along the eastern and northern edges, together with a decline in parasitism compared with the previous round. Action: Intensify inspection of edges and the following inner rows. Protect existing parasitoids. Apply a compatible early intervention targeting hatching when decision criteria are met. Reassess before larvae progress to advanced instars.

Situation: L2 and L3 are distributed in more than one sector, the first infested fruits have appeared, and immigration from an old field is continuing. Action: Collect infested fruits. Apply a selective corrective intervention according to the IRAC record and preharvest interval. Coordinate termination of the source field. Assess the result within a short period. Collect samples if larvae persist in well-covered areas.

Warnings should be classified as: High Confidence When the following agree: Traps. Eggs. Mines. Temperature. External source. Moderate Confidence When two or three indicators are present with some uncertainty. Low Confidence When based on: One trap. A small plant sample. Distant weather data. Unconfirmed diagnosis. The bulletin should state the confidence level so that the forecast is not treated as certain fact.

The system should define: Trap-reading time. Data-entry time. Analysis time. Message-dispatch time. Person responsible for receiving it. Confirmation that inspection was completed. A weekly bulletin may lose value during hot periods if collection and analysis take several days.

Possible tools include: Paper records. Electronic spreadsheets. Mobile applications. GIS systems. Digital dashboards. Documented photographs. Climatic sensors. The tool should be: Simple. Standardized. Updatable. Not burdensome to the inspector. Capable of detecting errors. A simple system used consistently is better than a complex system whose data are not updated.

After issuing a warning, review: Did hatching occur at the expected time? Did mines increase? Did infestation move into the field interior? Did parasitism actually decline? Did trap catches reach a new peak? Did fruit infestation appear? Record the result as: Correct forecast – Forecast too early – Forecast too late – False alarm Then adjust the model.

A false alarm is a high-level warning without occurrence of the expected risk. It may result from: An unrepresentative trap. A change in lure strength. A moth wave that did not result in oviposition. Incorrect interpretation of temperature. Ignoring natural enemies. Exaggerating a localized condition. A false alarm does not mean monitoring is useless, but it requires improvement of interpretation rules.

This occurs when the warning is issued after: Spread of L3 and L4. Growing-point infestation. Larval entry into fruits. Causes include: Long inspection intervals. Delayed data entry. Failure to inspect eggs. Dependence on traps alone. Failure to adjust intervals as temperature rises. Neglect of field edges. A late warning is more serious than a false alarm because it removes the opportunity for prevention.

Use indicators including: Percentage of outbreaks preceded by a warning. Mean number of days between warning and appearance of damage. Percentage of false alarms. Number of cases detected only after fruit infestation. Message-delivery time. Percentage of fields responding. Reduction in preventive sprays. Reduction in fruit infestation. System cost. Value of avoided losses.

sectionTitle: Sensitivity and Specificity of the Warning System
text: Sensitivity The system's ability to detect cases that will develop into genuine risk. Specificity Its ability to avoid issuing a high-level warning when no genuine risk exists. A highly sensitive system may generate many false alarms, whereas an overly conservative system may miss outbreaks. A balance should be achieved according to: Crop value. Pest-development speed. Cost of response. Residue risk.

sectionTitle: Adapting Early Warning for Processing Tomatoes
text: Greater weight should be given to: Protection of canopy cover. Proximity of infestation to clusters. Percentage of infested fruits. Factory schedule. Timing of mechanical harvest. Presence of large old processing-tomato fields. The warning should include: Days remaining until harvest. Preharvest-interval status. Ripening percentage. Economic justification for intervention.

sectionTitle: Adapting Early Warning for Fresh-Market Tomatoes
text: Greater weight should be given to: First fruit infestation. Harvest frequency. Preharvest interval before the next picking. Quality specifications. Rejected produce. Movement of crates and workers. Appearance of a limited number of infested fruits may raise the warning level more rapidly than in processing tomatoes, depending on market specifications.

The Egyptian early-warning system should be divided according to: The Delta. Nubaria and Wadi El Natroun. The North Coast. Middle Egypt. Upper Egypt. Winter, spring, summer, and autumn production cycles. One time window for forecasting hatching should not be used across all regions. Each region should be linked to: A weather station. A trap network. Indicator fields. A crop-cycle register. A list of source fields. An analytical team.

sectionTitle: Simplified Model for an Egyptian Field
text: The following data should be collected weekly: Indicator Result Males/edge trap/day Males/interior trap/day Percentage change from previous round Live eggs/plant Parasitism percentage Percentage of plants with active mines Dominant larval instars Percentage of infested growing points Percentage of infested fruits Predators/plant Mean temperature Nearby old field Yes/No Old-field removal date Warning level Action Reinspection date

Error One: Treating the Trap as a Complete Early-Warning System It is only one layer. Error Two: Establishing a Universal Spray Threshold from Trap Catch The relationship between catch and damage differs among situations. Error Three: Comparing Different Lures The difference may reflect attraction strength rather than pest density. Error Four: Ignoring Eggs This removes the most important intervention window. Error Five: Recording Egg Presence Without Egg Age This does not show how much response time remains. Error Six: Relying on Total Mine Counts New and active mines should be measured. Error Seven: Ignoring Natural Enemies This may overestimate or underestimate risk. Error Eight: Using an Unvalidated Non-Local Thermal Model This may incorrectly predict stage timing. Error Nine: Combining a Threshold from One Study with a Thermal Constant from Another This produces an internally inconsistent model. Error Ten: Failing to Include Old Fields Forecasts for an isolated field become unreliable. Error Eleven: Issuing a Warning Without an Action This does not change the outcome. Error Twelve: Delaying the Message It may arrive after hatching or fruit infestation. Error Thirteen: Using a Point Total as an Automatic Spray Order Technical interpretation remains necessary. Error Fourteen: Failing to Assess Forecast Accuracy This prevents system improvement. Error Fifteen: Relying on the regional average and concealing hotspots Risk locations should be identified.

sectionTitle: Research and Applied Priorities in Egypt
text: 1. Standardize trap and lure types within monitoring networks. 2. Develop catch baselines for each region and production cycle. 3. Link catch data with eggs, larvae, and fruit infestation. 4. Develop a locally validated thermal model. 5. Use weather stations close to fields. 6. Develop maps of old and newly planted fields. 7. Identify early indicators specific to processing tomatoes. 8. Develop separate indicators for fresh-market tomatoes. 9. Study egg distribution along Egyptian field edges. 10. Measure the effect of wind direction. 11. Include parasitism percentage in the warning dashboard. 12. Include predator and nymph density. 13. Establish a database of IRAC groups. 14. Link warnings with removal of old fields. 15. Develop a simple Arabic-language application for engineers. 16. Measure the sensitivity and specificity of warning levels. 17. Analyse false and late warnings. 18. Train monitoring teams to identify egg and larval age. 19. Issue rapid regional bulletins. 20. Update the model every season.

Early warning for Tuta absoluta does not depend on a single trap count. It depends on integrating a set of leading indicators, including: Trap trend. Fresh eggs. Live unparasitized eggs. Hatching. Early larval instars. Temperature. Source fields. Natural-enemy status. Performance of previous treatments. Traps are used to detect male activity and flight waves, but they do not directly measure oviposition or fruit infestation. Eggs are the most important direct indicator that a new generation is becoming established. They should be classified by: Age. Viability. Parasitism. Proximity to hatching. New mines and presence of L1 and L2 also indicate a hatching wave requiring a response before larvae advance. Appearance of L3 and L4 and fruit infestation are lagging indicators showing that part of the preventive opportunity has been lost. Temperature affects the time available for response. Inspection intervals should therefore be shortened when development accelerates rather than remaining fixed at weekly intervals. Degree-days can help estimate hatching and generation timing, but they do not independently forecast immigration or movement of transplants and residues. Old fields, sorting stations, nurseries, and alternative hosts should be included in the warning map. The operational system consists of five levels: Green for monitoring. Yellow for preparedness. Orange for early intervention. Red for corrective intervention. Critical for containment and assessment of crop continuation. A warning is useful only when it specifies: Risk location. Indicators that triggered it. Required action. Reinspection date. Confidence level of the forecast. The system is effective when it provides sufficient time to target eggs and young larvae, reduces fruit infestation and preventive sprays, and prevents old fields from reinvading newly planted crops. References

Case studies provide scientific and practical value that general lists of control methods cannot offer because they show how the pest, crop, environment, and management measures interacted within an actual production system. However, a case study may be misleading if its results are transferred without considering: Location of the study. Greenhouse or open-field conditions. Cultivar. Temperature. Initial infestation level. Area size. Immigration rate. Labour and input costs. Sampling method. Natural enemies present. Legal registration of materials. Harvest timing. Target market. A programme may achieve high efficacy in a well-sealed greenhouse and then fail in an open field surrounded by immigration sources. Likewise, a biological intervention may clearly suppress the pest but be less profitable than another programme because of product cost or number of applications. Analysis of a case study should therefore not be limited to the question: Did the treatment succeed? It should answer the following questions: 1. What problem did it target? 2. Under what conditions was it implemented? 3. Which indicators of success were used? 4. Did the pest, damage, or fruit infestation decline? 5. Did marketable yield increase? 6. Was the result economically viable? 7. Was the method compatible with natural enemies? 8. Can it be transferred to Egypt? 9. What modifications are required before application? 10. What were the study's limitations, and what did it not prove?
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Uncritical transfer of studies may lead to errors such as: Using a greenhouse release rate in open fields. Transferring the number of pheromone dispensers from an enclosed area to an open area. Treating laboratory results as field efficacy. Considering insect mortality as proof of increased yield. Ignoring treatment cost. Ignoring insecticide effects on natural enemies. Transferring a rate that is not locally registered. Applying a mixture that has not been tested for compatibility or residues. Using results from one cultivar for all cultivars. Treating one season as definitive evidence. Every study should be regarded as one piece of evidence, not a ready-made prescription.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Evidence used in a case study may be ranked as follows. Level One: Laboratory Observation This tests: Toxicity. Parasitism. Predation. Survival. Fecundity. It is useful for identifying potential, but it does not prove field performance. Level Two: Semi-Field Experiment This is conducted in: Cages. Tunnels. Plant units. Small greenhouses. It adds realism but limits immigration and environmental variation. Level Three: Commercial-Greenhouse Trial This reflects a relatively realistic production system, but a greenhouse differs from an open field in: Isolation. Wind. Humidity. Persistence of natural enemies. Moth movement. Level Four: Replicated Open-Field Trial This provides stronger evidence of field performance, provided it includes: A control. Replicates. Randomisation. Pretreatment measurements. Statistical analysis. Level Five: Multi-Site, Multi-Season Commercial Application This provides the strongest evidence of practical applicability, especially when measuring: Yield. Quality. Cost. Sustainability. Regional variation. Level Six: Unreplicated Commercial Observation This is useful for identifying problems and generating hypotheses, but it does not prove that the intervention caused the result.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Criteria for Transferring a Case Study to Egypt
text: Before transferring any programme, review the degree of similarity in: Temperature. Humidity. Production system. Crop-cycle duration. Plant density. Field size. Immigration pressure. Natural enemies. Product registration. Input costs. Crop price. Harvest system. Market specifications. Transferability may be classified as: High When study conditions are similar to the target field and the method is registered and available. Moderate When the mechanism is comparable, but modification is required in: Rate. Timing. Release rate. Number of applications. Low When study conditions differ fundamentally, such as transferring a sealed-greenhouse programme to an open field under high immigration pressure. Unacceptable When: The material is not registered. The study depends on an organism not authorised for release. Safety data are unavailable. The scientific design is weak. The method conflicts with market requirements.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Following the invasion of southern Europe by Tuta absoluta, egg parasitoids were investigated for use before larvae entered leaves and fruits. Tests showed that the egg parasitoid: Trichogramma achaeae Could attack pest eggs, after which it was tested in tomato greenhouses in Spain.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The experimental programme used frequent releases that, in one application, reached: Approximately 30 adult parasitoids per plant. Equivalent to about 75 parasitoids per square metre. Releases repeated every three to four days during the trial period. Under the conditions of the study, damage reduction exceeded 90% compared with the reference treatment.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Potential success was associated with: Targeting eggs before hatching. Repeated releases. High parasitoid density. A relatively protected environment. Reduced parasitoid loss due to wind. Close plant spacing. Ability to distribute release points. Relative control over insecticides used.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The result does not mean that a rate of 75 parasitoids/m² should be applied in Egyptian open fields because: The study was conducted in a greenhouse. Parasitoid loss may be greater in open fields. Temperature and wind differ. Cost may be high. Egg distribution is more variable in open fields. Immigration pressure may continue from neighbouring fields. Strain and batch quality differ among producers. Repeating releases every three or four days may also be uneconomic over large areas.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The main lesson is not to copy the release rate, but to: Begin release when eggs appear. Use repeated releases rather than one release. Link the rate to egg density. Measure emergence percentage from cards. Protect the parasitoid from insecticides. Distribute release points effectively. Assess parasitism within the crop. Egyptian fields require trials to determine: The minimum effective rate. The appropriate interval. Effects of temperature and wind. Cost per feddan. Reduction in fruit infestation.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: One study evaluated three agents: Trichogramma brassicae against eggs. Bacillus thuringiensis against early larvae. Spinosad as an insecticide derived from a microbial source. The agents were used alone and in paired treatments in a tomato greenhouse.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Combined treatments achieved higher efficacy than some individual treatments. The combination of Spinosad and Bt produced the highest mortality in the study, approaching 88%. The results showed that targeting more than one life stage or using two agents with different mechanisms may increase efficacy.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: High mortality does not mean that mixing Spinosad and Bt is always the standard option because: Mortality is not the only indicator. Fruit infestation and yield should be measured. The materials may affect egg parasitoids. Physical and biological compatibility should be verified. Local registration should permit the mixture. Repeated Spinosad use increases resistance pressure. Greenhouse results do not equal open-field results. Simultaneous combination may not be better than temporal separation. Using an egg parasitoid followed by a material that harms it may negate part of the benefit of release.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Integration should be based on separation of functions: The parasitoid targets eggs. Bt targets L1 and L2. The selective insecticide is retained for escalation when needed. The timing sequence should preferably achieve: Protection of the parasitoid. Targeting of hatching. Reduction in chemical applications. Prevention of repeated use of the same mode of action.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Bacillus thuringiensis has been tested in several Tuta absoluta management systems, particularly against newly hatched larvae. Its efficacy depends on larvae ingesting crystalline and protein toxins during feeding.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Bt efficacy improves when: It is applied close to hatching. L1 and L2 are targeted. Deposits reach feeding sites. The formulation is stored correctly. It is not rapidly degraded by radiation or washed off. Applications are repeated according to new hatching rather than a rigid calendar. It is integrated with compatible predators or parasitoids.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Results may differ because of: Strain used. Spore and crystal concentration. Larval instar. Plant size. Coverage quality. Ultraviolet radiation. Temperature. Assessment timing. Infestation level. Bt is less suitable as a rescue treatment when advanced instars dominate inside mines or fruits.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The value of Bt lies in: Early intervention. Relative protection of natural enemies. Reduced dependence on neurotoxic insecticides. Integration with egg parasitoids and predators. Its value does not lie in using it after L3 and L4 appear as an immediate chemical substitute.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Mating disruption was tested in tomato greenhouses on the island of Sardinia using pheromone dispensers at high density. One programme used: About 1,000 dispensers per hectare. Each dispenser containing a dose of sex-pheromone components. A total of approximately 60 grams of active material per hectare.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The programme resulted in: Severe reduction or near cessation of trap catch inside some greenhouses. Reduced numbers of damaged plants or fruits compared with reference greenhouses. Reduced pest pressure when application began early and the treated area was suitable.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Reduced trap catch alone does not prove mating cessation because disruption also prevents males from reaching monitoring traps. The following should be measured: Eggs. Egg fertility. Mines. Fruit infestation. The technique is also affected by: Unit size. Greenhouse sealing. Ventilation openings. Initial pest density. Entry of mated females. Neighbouring fields. Dispenser distribution. Uniformity of pheromone release.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Direct transferability to open fields is limited because: Wind disperses the pheromone cloud. Areas may be small or irregular. Neighbouring fields may not participate. Mated females may immigrate. Open edges reduce uniformity. However, the technique may be studied in: Large contiguous areas. Contract-production blocks. Well-sealed greenhouses. Regional programmes involving several fields.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: A field study evaluated two strains of the entomopathogenic fungus: Metarhizium anisopliae ICIPE 20. M. anisopliae ICIPE 69. They were compared with a commercial chemical insecticide and an untreated control over two seasons.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: In the first season, fruit infestation was approximately: 26.48% in the untreated control. 13.92% with ICIPE 20. 18.84% with ICIPE 69. 10.87% with the reference chemical insecticide. Yield loss caused by the pest was approximately: 43.41% in the control. 10.48% with ICIPE 20. 18.41% with ICIPE 69. 6.73% with the chemical insecticide. Differences changed between seasons, demonstrating the influence of season, weather, and pest pressure.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: All treatments increased marketable yield compared with the control. In the first season, the benefit–cost ratio was approximately: 8.92 for the reference chemical insecticide. 4.31 for ICIPE 20. 3.43 for ICIPE 69. In the second season, the values declined to approximately: 6.30 for the reference insecticide. 2.84 for ICIPE 20. 2.14 for ICIPE 69.
