Under the conditions of the study, the fungal programme cost more than the reference insecticide.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The study shows that a biological agent can: Reduce fruit infestation. Reduce yield loss. Increase marketable yield. Produce a positive economic return. However, it also shows that: Greater environmental sustainability does not automatically mean greater short-term profit. Cost of the biological product is decisive. Strains differ in efficacy. Weather conditions affect results. Fungal action is slower than that of some insecticides. Results vary between seasons.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: An entomopathogenic fungus should be evaluated according to: Strain, not species name alone. Viability and concentration. Temperature and humidity. Application time. Protection from radiation. Fruit infestation. Marketable yield. Cost. Demonstration of larval mortality in the laboratory is insufficient before recommending field use.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Field studies evaluated entomopathogenic nematodes against Tuta absoluta larvae inside mines, using formulations that help nematodes persist on the foliage.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: One field study showed that suitable nematode formulations reduced larvae inside mines by approximately: 37%. To 68%. Other studies in Morocco recorded larval reductions approaching 60–80% under some conditions. The percentage of fruits bearing holes also declined, although a significant increase in total yield weight was not always detected.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: This result is important because a programme may: Fail to increase total yield weight. Yet increase the percentage of saleable fruits. A larva may not substantially reduce fruit weight, but the hole or associated rot may make the fruit unacceptable. The following should therefore be measured: Crop quality. Percentage of sound fruits. Alongside weight.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Foliar use of nematodes faces challenges including: Radiation. Desiccation. Low humidity. Leaf-surface temperature. Difficulty reaching larvae. Rapid drying of the spray solution. Efficacy depends strongly on: Species and strain. Carrier material. Application time. Humidity. Number of applications. Laboratory or greenhouse results are therefore often higher than open-field results.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Potential usefulness is greater when: Applications are made in the evening. Temporary humidity is available. A protective formulation is used. Hotspots are targeted. A suitable production cycle is selected. Use may be more difficult under: Upper Egypt summer conditions. Dry winds. Highly exposed open fields.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Field surveys in Egypt recorded several natural enemies associated with Tuta absoluta, including: Trichogramma achaeae. Nesidiocoris tenuis. Macrolophus pygmaeus. Chrysoperla carnea. Their abundance varied with pest abundance and seasonal stage.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: These observations indicate that the Egyptian environment does not begin from zero; it already contains resources that can be supported through: Reduced use of broad-spectrum insecticides. Biological belts. Habitat conservation. Selective materials. Avoidance of unjustified spraying. Local species may also form the basis of mass-rearing or release programmes after evaluation.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Presence of a natural enemy does not prove that it reduced the pest to an economic level. An observational study demonstrates: Association. Synchrony. But it does not always demonstrate: Magnitude of predation. Reduction in fruit infestation. Yield saved. Density required for control. Predator abundance may rise only after pest abundance increases, meaning its response occurs after damage has begun.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The next step should move beyond recording presence to measuring: Parasitism percentage. Number of eggs consumed. Nymph density. Response to insecticides. Effect on fruit infestation. Potential for commercial mass production. These natural resources should not be destroyed by a non-selective chemical programme.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Several tomato cultivars were compared in Assiut for: Eggs. Mines. Larvae. Infestation by other pests. Some cultivars recorded fewer eggs or mines than others.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: One cultivar recorded relatively low values for some infestation indicators but showed high larval survival inside leaflets in another test. Not all differences among cultivars were statistically significant. This shows that resistance or tolerance cannot be reduced to one indicator.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: A cultivar may be: Less attractive for oviposition. Yet suitable for survival of larvae that enter it. Or it may: Tolerate damage without a large yield reduction. Without reducing the pest population. Distinguish among: Antixenosis Reduced oviposition preference. Antibiosis Reduced larval survival or development. Tolerance The plant's capacity to produce yield despite infestation.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Cultivar selection should not replace IPM. A less susceptible cultivar may be a supporting component, provided the following are evaluated: Yield. Quality. Crop-cycle length. Canopy cover. Processing suitability. Effect on subsequent pest generations. A cultivar should not be described as resistant on the basis of low egg numbers in one season.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Commercial formulations of: Metarhizium anisopliae. Beauveria bassiana. Were tested against larvae collected from tomato fields and exposed under controlled laboratory conditions. The formulations showed pathogenic activity against the larvae.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: These tests demonstrate: The strains can infect the pest. Larvae are not completely protected inside mines. The formulation warrants progression to more realistic testing. They do not demonstrate: Field performance. Persistence under sunlight. Reduction in fruit infestation. Economic viability. Compatibility with insecticides.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Testing should proceed through successive stages: 1. Laboratory test. 2. Plant or cage test. 3. Greenhouse trial. 4. Field trial. 5. Commercial application. 6. Economic analysis. One should not move directly from mortality in a Petri dish to a per-feddan recommendation.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Following the invasion of Tuta absoluta into several African countries, many growers responded by: Increasing spray frequency. Mixing multiple products. Repeating the same groups. Shortening spray intervals. Using unreliable products. Neglecting monitoring and biological control.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Sole reliance on insecticides led to: Higher production costs. Variable or weak efficacy. Greater resistance risk. Mortality of natural enemies. Increased worker exposure. Residue risks. Persistent infestation despite intensive spraying. This does not mean all insecticides are ineffective. It means their use outside a monitoring and resistance-management programme rapidly reduces their value.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: An insecticide may produce rapid suppression in the first season and be interpreted as a complete success. With repeated use of the same group, however: Less-susceptible individuals survive. The resistant population reproduces. Growers require more interventions. Natural enemies are killed. Pest management becomes increasingly dependent on insecticides. Short-term success is thereby converted into long-term failure.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Programme evaluation should not be based only on: Speed of mortality. It should include: Sustainability of susceptibility. Number of applications. IRAC groups. Natural enemies. Residues. Cost per accepted tonne. Declining efficacy should be investigated rather than addressed by increasing the dose.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: This case represents an applied field observation from Egyptian processing-tomato fields, not a published or replicated research experiment. The following were observed: Nearby early fields had completed production. Their control programmes had stopped or declined. Plants and residues remained for a period. Pest pressure moved into later plantings. Traps were absent or installed late at the beginning of follow-up. Infestation increased in the late field. The canopy was weak, exposing part of the fruit.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Infestation development can be interpreted through the following sequence: 1. Pest multiplication in early fields. 2. Cessation of control while plants remained. 3. Increased adult emergence. 4. Movement into the more recent field. 5. Oviposition on new growth. 6. Delayed early detection. 7. Larvae reaching advanced instars. 8. Damage to leaves and canopy cover. 9. Increased fruit exposure to sunlight and the pest.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Potential errors include: Failure to install traps from the beginning of the production cycle. Absence of a map of old fields. Stopping management of the source field before its removal. Failure to notify later plantings. Delayed shredding of residues. Reliance only on interventions within the recipient field. Failure to separate edge inspection from interior inspection.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Risk could have been reduced through: Installing traps before transplanting. Recording termination dates of neighbouring fields. Continuing management of the old field until termination. Immediate shredding when the crop became uneconomic. Increasing inspection along the facing edge. Targeting eggs and hatching. Protecting canopy cover. Coordinating decisions among growers.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Scientific Value and Limitations of the Case
text: The case illustrates the logic of immigration and the problem of source fields, but it does not quantitatively prove: Number of moths transferred. Distance travelled. Proportion of damage attributable solely to immigration. Efficacy of any particular intervention. It should therefore be used as: A training case. A hypothesis requiring verification. It should not replace a replicated experiment.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Case Production System Principal Method Main Result Principal Limitation Trichogramma in Spain Greenhouse Release of egg parasitoids High reduction in damage High release rate and protected environment Trichogramma + Bt + Spinosad Greenhouse Integration of several agents High mortality in combined treatments Compatibility, resistance, and registration Bt in the Mediterranean Greenhouse/Semi-field Bacterial agent Good efficacy against early instars Weak performance against advanced instars Mating disruption Greenhouses Pheromones Reduced trap catch and damage Immigration, area size, and enclosure Metarhizium in Uganda Open field Entomopathogenic fungus Reduced damage and increased marketable yield Slow action and high cost Entomopathogenic nematodes Open field EPN Reduced larvae and improved fruit quality Desiccation, radiation, and humidity Natural enemies in Egypt Field survey Conservation biological control Confirmation of beneficial species Does not prove magnitude of economic suppression Cultivars in Assiut Field/Laboratory Genetic variation Variation in some infestation indicators Variable results and insufficiency of cultivar alone Fungi in Rwanda Laboratory Commercial EPF Confirmation of pathogenic capacity Does not prove field performance Excessive insecticide use in Africa Commercial systems Chemical control Rapid suppression followed by efficacy and resistance problems Absence of IPM Immigration among Egyptian fields Commercial observation Uncoordinated management Infestation of the late crop Unreplicated case
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The following elements recur in successful programmes: Early intervention. Targeting eggs and L1 and L2. Repeated monitoring. Protection of natural enemies. Matching the method to the environment. Effective distribution. Use of more than one compatible component. Evaluation of fruits, not leaves alone. Management of source fields. Documentation of cost and return. Sustainable success does not depend on the strength of one isolated method.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: These include: Starting the programme after larvae have reached advanced instars. Transferring laboratory results directly into practice. Using a slow-acting method in an advanced emergency. Relying on traps alone. Failing to manage immigration. Repeating insecticide groups. Killing natural enemies. Ignoring fruit infestation. Failing to measure economic viability. Leaving the field after harvest ends.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Difference Between Biological Efficacy and Agronomic Success
text: A treatment may produce: High larval mortality. Without producing: Increased yield. It may also produce: Moderate pest suppression. But result in: A large increase in saleable fruit. The following should therefore be distinguished. Entomological Efficacy Reduction of eggs or larvae. Protective Efficacy Prevention of leaf or fruit infestation. Agronomic Efficacy Increase in yield or quality. Economic Efficacy Increase in return relative to cost. Environmental Efficacy Protection of beneficial organisms and reduction of risks.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Why Do Results Differ Among Seasons?
text: The same intervention may produce different results because of: Pest pressure. Temperature. Humidity. Immigration timing. Plant density. Product quality. Population resistance. Natural enemies. Crop price. Number of applications. One-season trials are therefore insufficient for permanent programme adoption.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Difference Between Greenhouses and Open Fields
text: Element Greenhouse Open Field Immigration Can be reduced with screens Open and recurrent Wind Limited Important Humidity Higher and more stable Variable Parasitoid persistence Often higher Greater loss and dispersal Mating disruption More uniform More difficult Fungi and nematodes Relatively better conditions Exposed to radiation and desiccation Coverage More controllable Affected by wind and area Source fields Less influential when sealed A major factor Greenhouse programmes should not be transferred to open fields using area-based conversion alone.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Transferring Fresh-Market Tomato Results to Processing Tomatoes
text: Consider differences in: Crop-cycle length. Plant density. Canopy cover. Harvest frequency. Fruit value. Rejection percentage. Harvest system. Factory list. Collection of infested fruit may be relatively easy in a repeatedly harvested crop but difficult in dense processing tomatoes intended for mechanical harvest.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Effect of Price on Case Interpretation
text: A programme may be economically viable when: Tomato price is high. And uneconomic when: Price is low. The following also matter: Cost of the biological agent. Labour cost. Spray cost. Value of rejected produce. A benefit–cost ratio should therefore not be transferred from one country to another without recalculation.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Success of a material or mixture in a study does not mean that its use is permitted. Verify: Registration on tomato. Registration against the pest or approved use. Rate. Preharvest interval. Number of applications. Target market. Maximum residue limits. An unregistered product should not be included in an applied programme merely because a study evaluated it.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Framework for Writing an Egyptian Case Study
text: Each future case should include: Site Data Governorate. Region. Soil type. Area. Coordinates. Crop Data Cultivar. Transplanting date. Plant density. Production purpose. Harvest date. Pest Data Traps. Eggs. Larvae. Mines. Fruits. Natural Enemies Species. Density. Parasitism. Previous treatments. Intervention Method. Rate or release density. Timing. Application method. Weather conditions. Results Pest reduction. Fruit infestation. Accepted yield. Residues. Cost. Analysis Reason for success or failure. Limitations. Repeatability.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Item Data Case title Location Production system Cultivar Transplanting date Initial infestation level Potential source Natural enemies Intervention Number of applications IRAC group Pest outcome Fruit infestation Accepted yield Cost Net benefit Limitations Lesson learned
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Error One: Selecting Only the Highest Mortality Percentage The treatment may not be the most profitable or the least harmful to beneficial organisms. Error Two: Ignoring the Control The degree of improvement cannot be determined without comparison. Error Three: Treating Correlation as Causation A natural enemy may increase because the pest increased, not because it prevented the increase. Error Four: Transferring Greenhouse Results to Open Fields Environmental pressure and immigration differ. Error Five: Transferring a Rate Without Local Registration The use may be illegal or unsafe. Error Six: Ignoring the Second Season Results may differ substantially among seasons. Error Seven: Relying on Total Weight A large proportion of fruit may be unmarketable. Error Eight: Treating All Strains as Equivalent Strains of fungi, Bt, and parasitoids differ. Error Nine: Using Commercial Observation as Experimental Evidence It can generate a hypothesis but cannot prove it. Error Ten: Ignoring Implementation Cost A programme may be effective but not scalable.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: The priorities are: 1. Conduct multi-site open-field trials. 2. Evaluate processing tomatoes separately from fresh-market tomatoes. 3. Test Trichogramma release rates locally. 4. Evaluate Bt with predators and parasitoids. 5. Test fungi under heat and dry conditions. 6. Test nematodes in protective formulations. 7. Measure fruit infestation by number and weight. 8. Calculate marketable yield. 9. Record local natural enemies. 10. Measure effects of biological belts. 11. Study immigration from old fields. 12. Evaluate mating disruption in large production blocks. 13. Develop a resistance-testing network. 14. Conduct economic analysis. 15. Repeat studies for at least two seasons. 16. Publish negative as well as positive results. 17. Document commercial failures. 18. Separate published data from field observations. 19. Build an Egyptian case-study database. 20. Convert lessons into decision protocols.
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: Case studies show that Tuta absoluta can be managed using: Egg parasitoids. Predators. Bt. Entomopathogenic fungi. Nematodes. Pheromones. Selective insecticides. However, the success of each method depends on the system within which it operates. Trichogramma achaeae achieved a high reduction in damage in Spanish greenhouses when released at high and repeated rates, but those results cannot be transferred directly to open fields. Integrated programmes showed that targeting eggs and larvae with different methods can increase efficacy, but compatibility, resistance, and registration should be considered. Mating disruption performed better in protected and contiguous environments, whereas its use in small open fields remains limited by immigration and wind. Field trials with Metarhizium anisopliae showed that fungi can reduce fruit infestation and yield loss and produce a positive economic return, but they were slower and more expensive than the reference chemical insecticide under the conditions of the study. Entomopathogenic nematodes also reduced larvae and improved fruit quality without always producing a confirmed increase in total weight, highlighting the importance of measuring marketable yield. Egyptian surveys confirmed the presence of important parasitoids and predators, but their presence alone does not prove economic control; their effects on eggs, larvae, and fruits should be measured. Cultivar studies also show that reduction of one infestation indicator is insufficient to describe a cultivar as resistant and that cultivar choice does not replace integrated management. Cases of excessive insecticide use demonstrate that rapid suppression may become long-term failure when resistance management and natural enemies are neglected. The applied Egyptian case of moth immigration from old fields confirms that managing the recipient field alone is insufficient while surrounding infestation sources remain active. The principal message is that a case study should not be used to copy a programme, but to understand: Why did it succeed? Where did it succeed? What were its limitations? What modifications are required before transfer? References
partTitle: Case Studies and Critical Analysis of Tuta absoluta Management Programmes

text: A scientific programme for managing Tuta absoluta cannot be implemented without a clear recording system because sound decisions require knowledge of what actually happened in the field, not what the team remembers several days or weeks later. Records make it possible to determine: When did infestation begin? Where did it first appear? What was the trap direction? Which insect stage was dominant? What percentage of eggs was parasitised? Which materials were used? What was the mode-of-action group? Was the sprayer calibrated? Did new infestation appear after treatment? Did an immigration wave occur from a neighbouring field? What was the programme's effect on fruit and yield? Was the programme economically viable? What caused success or failure? Records are not an administrative activity separate from pest management. They are part of the system for decision-making, resistance management, residue compliance, and performance analysis.

The recording system aims to: 1. Standardise data-collection methods. 2. Reduce dependence on memory and subjective impressions. 3. Compare field edges with the field interior. 4. Compare weeks and seasons. 5. Link pest status with weather and crop stage. 6. Document the reasons for intervention and non-intervention. 7. Manage IRAC groups. 8. Monitor preharvest intervals. 9. Detect poor coverage or calibration. 10. Investigate insecticide-failure cases. 11. Estimate natural-enemy efficacy. 12. Calculate fruit infestation and accepted yield. 13. Calculate programme cost. 14. Develop local thresholds in the future. 15. Build an analysable Egyptian database.

A record should be: Simple. Clear. Based on defined units. Linked to a known field and sector. Dated. Signed or attributable to a specific inspector. Reviewable. Free from ambiguous terms. Relevant to the decision required. Easy to convert into a database. The following should not be entered alone: “Infestation is high.” “Spraying was good.” “Natural enemies are abundant.” “Trap catch is high.” Instead, use values or defined categories, such as: 6 eggs/20 plants. 3 active mines/20 plants. 18 males/trap/7 days. 2% fruit infestation by number. 65% coverage on water-sensitive cards.

The recording system is divided into four levels. Daily Records Such as: Spraying. Natural-enemy releases. Weather conditions. Harvesting. Emergency cases. Weekly Records Such as: Trap readings. Egg and larval inspection. Natural-enemy assessment. Decision meetings. Stage-Specific Records Such as: Transplant inspection. Beginning of flowering. Beginning of fruit set. Beginning of harvest. Field termination. End-of-Season Records Such as: Total yield. Accepted yield. Rejection percentage. Programme cost. Number of applications. Resistance assessment. Lessons learned.

Each field should receive a fixed code. Example: NUB-26-TOM-03 Where: NUB = Region. 26 = Season year. TOM = Crop. 03 = Field number. Each sector should receive a sub-code: NUB-26-TOM-03-A. NUB-26-TOM-03-B. NUB-26-TOM-03-North Edge. The same code should be used in: Traps. Leaf samples. Spray records. Residue analyses. Harvest records. Field photographs.

The field identification card is the primary reference for all records and should be completed before planting or when monitoring begins. Item Data Field code Farm name Governorate and region Coordinates Area in feddans Field shape and dimensions Production type Processing/Fresh market/Experimental Cultivar or hybrid Seed company Seed-lot number Transplant source Nursery sowing date Transplanting date Plant density Row spacing Plant spacing Irrigation system Soil type Previous crop Previous-crop harvest date Date of previous-crop residue disposal Expected harvest date Market or factory Technical officer

The card should include a map showing: North direction. Crop rows. Entrances. Irrigation network. Traps. Sampling points. Biological strips. Adjacent fields. Waste sources. Sorting stations. Roads and machinery routes. The field should preferably be divided into sectors for: Monitoring. Hotspot treatment. Harvesting. Infestation analysis.

This form records surrounding sources of migration and reinvasion. Field Direction Adjacent Crop Age/Stage Distance Infestation Level Harvest Date Removal Date Risk Level North South East West The following should also be recorded: Presence of volunteer tomato plants. Presence of potato or eggplant. Presence of wild solanaceous plants. Presence of a sorting station. Presence of a pile of rejected fruit. Presence of a completed field that has not been shredded.

sectionTitle: Assessment of Risk from an Adjacent Source
text: Risk may be classified as: Low A non-host crop. Or a healthy, newly planted tomato field. Moderate Tomato or potato in production with limited infestation. High An old tomato field. Active infestation. Harvest approaching completion. Critical Spraying and irrigation have stopped. Infestation density is high. Shredding or plant removal has begun. A newly planted tomato field lies in the opposite direction.

To prevent transfer of live pest stages into the field. Item Data Nursery name Batch code Cultivar Number of trays Inspection date Transplant age Number of trays inspected Number of transplants inspected Suspected eggs Active mines Live larvae Pupae Moths in traps Screen condition Presence of host weeds Most recent treatment IRAC group Decision Accept/Isolate/Reject

Accept the Batch When: Live stages are absent. Transplants are healthy. Records are complete. No clear infestation source is present. Isolate the Batch When: A suspect finding requires confirmation. Limited infestation is confined to separate trays. Treatment records are incomplete. Reject the Batch When: Mines or larvae are widespread. Multiple instars are present. Freedom from infestation cannot be guaranteed. The batch is distributed across more than one area within an infested nursery. Spraying the batch alone should not be accepted as proof that it is safe for transport.

Item Data Trap code Field and sector code Trap type Lure type Company Batch number Installation date Expiry date Height Coordinates Position Edge/Interior Edge direction Expected replacement date Responsible person

Distribution should consider: Separating edge traps from interior traps. Coding each trap individually. Not changing location without recording it. Avoiding placement where plants obstruct the trap. Adjusting height as the crop grows. Documenting additional traps used during emergencies. Counts from traps with different designs or lures should not be combined into one time series without explanation.

Date Trap Code Previous Reading Date Operating Days Number of Males Males/Trap/Day Lure Validity Trap Condition Notes Trap condition should be recorded as: Functional. Dry. Full. Covered with dust. Missing. Broken. Position changed.

Daily catch = Number of males ÷ Number of valid operating days Days during which the trap was non-functional should not be counted. Also record: Change from the previous reading. Difference between edge and interior. Direction of increase or decrease.

Trap Code Lure-Change Date Reason for Change New Lure Type Trap Cleaned/Replaced Responsible Person This record prevents an increase caused by installation of a new lure from being interpreted as a genuine increase in pest abundance.

Item Data Date and time of round Field and sector code Crop age Physiological stage Number of plants inspected Method of plant selection Edge/Interior Inspector's name Most recent treatment and date Weather conditions

Plant No. Live Eggs Parasitised Eggs Active Mines L1 L2 L3 L4 Growing-Point Infestation Predators Notes --------: --------: ----------------: -----------: -: -: -: -: ------------------------- --------: ----- 1 2 … Detailed data should not be replaced by one mean alone because distribution may be aggregated.

Calculate: Live eggs/plant. Parasitised eggs/plant. Percentage of plants carrying eggs. Parasitism percentage. Active mines/plant. Percentage of infested plants. L1 and L2/plant. L3 and L4/plant. Percentage of infested growing points. Predators/plant.
To distinguish live eggs expected to hatch from parasitised, hatched, or dead eggs. Sample No. Fresh Eggs Intermediate-Age Eggs Close to Hatching Parasitised Hatched Dead/Non-viable Undetermined

Parasitism (%) = Number of parasitised eggs ÷ Total evaluable eggs × 100 The denominator should be clearly defined. Eggs of undetermined status should not be included in the denominator without explanation.

An operational estimate may be used: Eggs expected to hatch = Live unparasitised eggs Bearing in mind that: Early parasitism may not yet be visible. Some eggs may die for other reasons. The number is an estimate, not an absolute prediction.

Sample No. New Mine Active Mine Old Mine Live Larva Dead Larva Parasitoid/Cocoon Basis of Classification Evidence of activity should be recorded, such as: Fresh frass. Moist tissue. Live larva. Increase in mine area. Recent feeding.

Percentage of a larval instar = Number of larvae in the specified instar ÷ Total live larvae × 100 Instar distribution assists in: Selecting the control method. Estimating hatching time. Assessing delayed intervention. Analysing failure of Bt or insecticides targeting early instars.

Plant No. Infested Growing Point Larva in Growing Point Bored Stem Infested Flowers Threatened Cluster Eggs Near Cluster Action Greater weight should be given to growing points and clusters in: Young plants. Beginning of flowering. Beginning of fruit set. Determinate cultivars.

The following should be specified: Number of plants. Number of clusters. Number of fruits. Fruit position. Ripeness stage. Edge or interior. Only visibly infested fruits should not be selected; a representative sample should be inspected.
Sample No. Total Fruits Tuta-Infested Fruits Fruits with Live Larva Fruits with Secondary Rot Old Infestation Unconfirmed

sectionTitle: Calculation of Fruit Infestation by Number
text: Fruit infestation by number (%) = Number of infested fruits ÷ Total fruits inspected × 100 Confirmed infestation should be separated from holes of undetermined origin.
Item Weight in Kilograms Total sample weight Weight of sound fruits Weight of Tuta-infested fruits Weight of fruits with associated rot Weight of fruits rejected for other reasons

Weight loss caused by Tuta (%) = Weight of fruits rejected because of Tuta ÷ Total sample weight × 100 All rotten fruits should not be attributed to Tuta without evidence of an associated wound or infestation.
Date Sector Trichogramma/Egg Parasitism Larval Parasitoids Adult Nesidiocoris Nesidiocoris Nymphs Macrolophus Orius Chrysoperla Spiders/Other Predators

Nymphs should be recorded separately because their presence indicates: Predator reproduction. Establishment within the field. That observations are not limited to migrating or recently released adults. Also record: Predator eggs. Predatory behaviour. Plant injury caused by mirids where present.
Larvae Inspected Parasitised Larvae Parasitoid Cocoons Parasitoids Emerged Larvae Dead from Unknown Causes Confirmed Parasitism (%) Not every dead larva should be classified as parasitised.

Item Data Parasitoid species Company/Laboratory Batch number Production date Receipt date Transport conditions Number of units received Nominal number of individuals Expected emergence percentage Release date Field and sector
Release Point Number of Cards/Units Distribution Time Temperature Sun Exposure Secure Attachment Operator

A reference sample of cards should be retained to estimate: Parasitoid emergence percentage. Presence of unparasitised host eggs. Contamination. Delayed emergence. Female percentage where possible. Emergence (%) = Number of parasitoid emergence holes ÷ Number of evaluable parasitised hosts × 100

After release, record: Egg parasitism in the field. Distance from the release point. Presence of pesticide residues. Timing of new egg appearance. Need for a subsequent release. Distribution of cards alone is not proof of programme success.

Item Data Predator species Stage used Adult/Nymph/Egg Supplier Batch number Number received Mortality on receipt Release date and time Number/feddan or plant Release points Supplementary food Most recent pesticide used Waiting period

Assessment should be conducted at successive intervals and include: Adults. Nymphs. Distribution. Reproduction. Predation. Plant injury. Pesticide effects. Predator success should not be assessed after only one day.
Plant Species Area Planting Date Planting Batch Germination (%) Start of Flowering End of Flowering Dill Coriander Basil Other species
Date Flowering (%) Parasitoids Predators Pollinators Aphids Whiteflies Thrips Diseases Action Movement of beneficial organisms into tomato should be recorded, not only their presence within the belt.
Item Data Product Organism/Strain Concentration Batch number Expiry date Storage conditions Target stage Infestation level Application date Application time Water volume Materials mixed
Temperature Humidity Wind Speed Direct Radiation Rain Forecast Leaf-Surface Moisture These data are especially important for: Fungi. Nematodes. Viruses. Bt.

Assessment Date Live Larvae Before Live Larvae After New Mines Signs of Infection Fruit Infestation Result Product success should not be measured by mortality alone, but also by feeding cessation, new infestation, and fruit protection.

Also record: Target larval instar. Infestation level before spraying. Reason for selecting the material. Previous group. Materials mixed. Natural-enemy status. Name of decision source. Name of applicator. Reassessment date.
Generation/Window Period Group Used Active Ingredients Number of Applications Efficacy Result Resistance Notes Switching between two active ingredients in the same group is not genuine rotation.

sectionTitle: Record of All Treatments Affecting Tuta
text: Applications targeting the following should also be included: Whiteflies. Thrips. Leaf-feeding caterpillars. Aphids. Other pests. When the material may affect Tuta absoluta or its natural enemies. Exposure to the group occurs even when Tuta is not the pest named in the spray order.

Item Data Order issue date Field and sector Decision level Green/Yellow/Orange/Red/Critical Evidence of need Target stage Selected method Alternatives rejected and reason Material or organism Rate/Release density Water volume Timing Beneficial-organism precautions PHI Reassessment date Approval source

Item Data Decision date Field and sector Traps Live eggs Active mines Larval instars Fruit infestation Natural enemies Reason for non-intervention Reinspection date Responsible person This card demonstrates that not spraying was a technical decision based on data.

Item Data Sprayer type Sprayer number Nozzle type Number of nozzles Operating pressure Travel speed Working width Output of each nozzle Calculated volume/feddan Calibration date Person performing calibration
Nozzle No. Output in One Minute Deviation from Mean Decision 1 2 … Nozzles showing unacceptable deviation from the others should be replaced.

Where possible, water-sensitive cards should be placed at: Top of the plant. Middle of the canopy. Bottom of the plant. Upper surface. Lower surface. Near clusters. Position Droplet Count Coverage (%) Uncovered Areas Required Adjustment
Start Time End Time Wind Speed Temperature Humidity Actual Water Volume Area Remaining Solution Notes

Name Position Attendance Signature/Approval

Indicator Current Week Previous Week Trend Interpretation Males/trap/day Live eggs/plant Parasitism percentage Active mines/plant L1 and L2 L3 and L4 Fruit infestation Predators Risk from adjacent fields
Item Decision Risk level Priority sectors Required action Person responsible for implementation Deadline Success indicator Assessment date Contingency plan in case of failure
Active Ingredient Date of Most Recent Application PHI in Days First Permitted Harvest Date Actual Harvest Date Compliance

Item Data Sample code Field and sector Sampling date and time Sampling method Number/Weight of fruits Ripeness stage Most recent treatments Sample collector Storage conditions Laboratory-delivery date Laboratory-report number
Active Ingredient Result Applicable MRL Reference Market Status Action Compliant/Non-compliant The relevant market or standard should be confirmed because limits may differ among markets.
Date Sector Total Weight Accepted Weight Rejected Because of Tuta Rejected Because of Rot Other Reasons Acceptance (%)
Acceptance (%) = Accepted yield weight ÷ Total harvested weight × 100 Tuta should be separated from: Colour defects. Green fruits. Blossom-end rot. Sunscald. Mechanical damage. Diseases.
Fruits Inspected Sound Tuta Hole Live Larva Associated Rot Undetermined Damage Notes
Item Data Field and sector Treatment date Active ingredient Group Rate Water volume Target instar Population before treatment Date failure was detected Population after treatment

Question Yes No Unknown Notes -------- --- -- ------- ----- Was the diagnosis correct? Were the mines new? Was the instar appropriate? Was the registered rate used? Was the sprayer calibrated? Was coverage good? Did rain or wind occur? Was the product sound? Did hatching occur after spraying? Is continuous immigration occurring? Was the group repeated? Is resistance possible?

The final cause should be classified as: Diagnostic failure. Timing failure. Failure to select the correct stage. Calibration failure. Coverage failure. Weather-related failure. Product-quality failure. New hatching. External immigration. Suspected resistance. Confirmed resistance. Undetermined cause. The phrase “the insecticide does not work” should not be used without assigning a classification.

Item Data Sample code Location Field and sector Collection date Larval instar Number of larvae Plant sampled Most recent material used Number of times the group was used Sample collector Transport conditions Laboratory-receipt time Recipient's name

Item Data Test method Active ingredient Test concentrations Number of replicates Larval age Reference strain Control mortality LC50 or diagnostic concentration Resistance ratio Result The test should be conducted using a standardised protocol and should control the concentration of active ingredient, not merely the commercial product.

Item Data Report date Report time Field and sector Case type First person to detect it Size of affected area Stages present Fruit-infestation percentage Potential source field Most recent treatment Immediate action Samples collected Person responsible for follow-up Reassessment date Result

The following codes may be used: T1: Sudden increase in trap catch. T2: Egg wave. T3: Spread of L1 and L2. T4: Advanced instars. T5: Fruit infestation. T6: Treatment failure. T7: Suspected resistance. T8: Collapse of natural enemies. T9: External source field. T10: Uneconomic outbreak. Coding allows the number of each emergency type to be analysed at the end of the season.

Date Sector Type of Material Removed Quantity Reason for Removal Transport Method Disposal Method Responsible Person Fruits/Leaves/Plants Infested material should not be left beside the field or along workers' routes.

Item Data Date of final harvest Date removal began Number of days between them Percentage of fruits remaining Final infestation level Plant-shredding method Method of handling fruits Root and regrowth status Equipment cleaning Continued trap operation Date catch stopped or declined

No living green plants remain. No infested fruits remain on the soil surface. No residue piles remain. Equipment has been cleaned. Regrowth has been removed. Adjacent fields have been notified. Monitoring continues for an appropriate period. Date of complete termination has been recorded.

To verify consistency of measurements among inspectors. Item Inspector 1 Inspector 2 Difference Action Eggs detected Active mines Larvae Infested fruits Predators A joint periodic inspection should be conducted to prevent differences in definitions and counting methods.

They should be trained to: Identify eggs. Distinguish parasitised eggs. Distinguish active mines. Determine larval instars. Inspect fruits. Identify natural enemies. Use counting units. Enter zero instead of leaving a cell blank. A blank cell may mean: The inspection was not conducted. Or the pest was absent. The two meanings should be distinguished.

Item Quantity Unit Price Cost Traps Lures Inspection and monitoring Parasitoids Predators Bt Fungi/Nematodes Insecticides Spray applications Collection of infested fruits Biological belt Residue analyses Resistance analyses Waste disposal
Item Value Increase in accepted yield Value of sprays avoided Reduction in rejection Value of biological-belt crop Reduction in grading cost Other measurable benefits
Net benefit = Total benefits − Total costs Benefit–cost ratio = Total benefits ÷ Total costs Cost of protecting one accepted tonne = Cost of the Tuta programme ÷ Tonnes of accepted yield
Indicator Target Result Deviation Interpretation Mean trap catch Live eggs Parasitism percentage Active mines Fruit infestation Total yield Accepted yield Rejection rate
Indicator Target Result Assessment Inspection rounds completed Functional traps Mean response time Number of emergencies Field-termination time Record completeness

Indicator Result Total applications Applications targeting Tuta Number of IRAC groups Highest repetition of one group Suspected failure cases Confirmed resistance cases PHI violations Residue non-compliance cases Natural-enemy status

The following questions should be asked: Which indicator first detected infestation? Was detection early? Were traps representative? Were eggs and hatching targeted? Which sectors were most infested? Was the source internal or external? Which treatment was most successful? Which treatment was least effective? Was an IRAC group repeated? Was fruit infestation reduced? Was the programme economically viable? What modification is essential for the following season?

Component Concise Result Field and cultivar Transplanting and harvest dates Highest trap catch First date eggs were detected First date mines were detected First date fruit infestation was detected Highest fruit-infestation percentage Principal natural enemy Number of releases Number of sprays IRAC groups Total yield Accepted yield Programme cost Principal reason for success Principal point of failure Recommendation for the next season

The database should preferably contain separate, linked tables: 1. Fields table. 2. Sectors table. 3. Traps table. 4. Trap-readings table. 5. Plant-inspection table. 6. Fruits table. 7. Natural-enemies table. 8. Releases table. 9. Treatments table. 10. Weather table. 11. Harvest table. 12. Residues table. 13. Emergencies table. 14. Costs table. They should be linked through: Field code. Sector code. Date. Operation number.

Every record should contain: A unique ID. Creation date. Operation date. Field code. Sector code. Inspector or operator name. Unit. Value. Verification status. Notes. Field names alone should not be used because the same names may recur across seasons.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Zero and a Blank Cell
text: Zero Means that inspection was conducted and the indicator was not detected. Blank Cell Means that data are unavailable or the inspection was not conducted. The system should not automatically convert blank cells to zero because this creates an artificial decline in mean infestation.

Fixed lists should be used to reduce data-entry errors. Crop Stage Transplant. Vegetative growth. Flowering. Fruit set. Fruit filling. Ripening. Harvest. Terminated. Decision Level Green. Yellow. Orange. Red. Critical. Mine Status New. Active. Old. Undetermined. Larval Instar L1. L2. L3. L4. Undetermined.

The system may generate an alert when: A trap reading is entered without operating days. An application date is later than the harvest date. The IRAC group is missing. The rate falls outside the entered range. PHI information is incomplete. Fruit infestation is entered without the number of fruits inspected. Parasitism exceeds 100%. Number of parasitised eggs exceeds the total. A treatment has no reassessment date. A terminated field lacks a removal record.

The system should: Save regular backups. Specify who enters data. Specify who edits data. Retain an audit trail of changes. Prevent deletion of original records without documentation. Save photographs and reports using field codes. These procedures are important when data are used for: Commercial decisions. Resistance studies. Residue compliance. Research. Contractual disputes.

The dashboard may display: Field map. Risk-level colour. Trap trend. Eggs and mines. Fruit infestation. IRAC groups used. Harvest date. Source fields. Overdue actions. The dashboard should not issue an automatic spray order without technical review.

Records should be reviewed weekly to confirm: Completion of fields. Correct units. Consistency of dates. Absence of duplicate records. Logical consistency of results. Presence of a signature or responsible person's name. Linkage of decisions to data.

The supervisor should select a sample of records and repeat: Trap reading. Inspection of a number of plants. Review of the insecticide container. Inspection of the sprayer. Review of the product store. Actual conditions should be compared with the recorded information.

These include: Percentage of complete records. Percentage of rounds recorded on time. Percentage of traps with valid data. Number of errors detected. Data-entry time. Number of unjustified decisions. Percentage of treatments that were reassessed. Percentage of samples with a complete chain of custody.

Records should be retained for a period sufficient to allow: Comparison among seasons. Residue review. Resistance analysis. Supplier evaluation. Research support. Commercial traceability. The following should be retained: Paper copies. Digital files. Label photographs. Laboratory reports. Field maps. Harvest records. Retention should comply with project procedures and regulatory and commercial requirements.

Error One: Recording Only the Mean This conceals hotspots and variation among plants. Error Two: Failing to Code Fields and Sectors This causes data to be mixed. Error Three: Leaving Cells Blank When the Pest Is Absent It becomes impossible to know whether inspection was conducted. Error Four: Recording the Product Name Without the Active Ingredient Mode-of-action analysis becomes difficult. Error Five: Failing to Record the IRAC Group Resistance management fails. Error Six: Failing to Record Other Target Pests Tuta may be exposed indirectly to the same group. Error Seven: Failing to Record Weather Application failure becomes difficult to interpret. Error Eight: Failing to Record Trap Condition Data from a non-functional trap may be used. Error Nine: Failing to Separate Edges from the Interior Evidence of immigration is lost. Error Ten: Counting Old Mines as Current Activity The population is artificially inflated. Error Eleven: Failing to Separate Reasons for Fruit Rejection Other losses are attributed to Tuta. Error Twelve: Recording a Release Without Evaluating Emergence This does not prove biological-agent quality. Error Thirteen: Implementing a Treatment Without a Documented Order The reason for treatment becomes unclear. Error Fourteen: Failing to Document a Non-Spray Decision A technically correct decision may appear to be neglect. Error Fifteen: Modifying Original Data Without an Audit Trail The system loses credibility.

sectionTitle: Minimum Forms for a Small Grower
text: When all forms cannot be implemented, the following minimum set should be used: 1. Field identification card. 2. Trap record. 3. Plant-inspection record. 4. Fruit-infestation record. 5. Insecticide and IRAC-group record. 6. Harvest and rejection record. 7. Field-termination record. They should preferably be combined in one simple notebook.

sectionTitle: Minimum Requirements for a Large Commercial Project
text: These should include: All core records. A digital map. A central database. Residue record. Calibration record. Chain of custody for resistance samples. Natural-enemy record. Cost-and-return record. Weekly dashboard.

1. Adopt one standard code for each field. 2. Standardise the unit used for trap readings. 3. Separate field edges from the interior. 4. Record live and parasitised eggs. 5. Distinguish active mines. 6. Record larval instars. 7. Measure fruit infestation by number and weight. 8. Record natural enemies. 9. Document all IRAC groups. 10. Link insecticides to harvest timing. 11. Record sprayer calibration. 12. Establish a standard treatment-failure form. 13. Maintain resistance samples under a chain of custody. 14. Record neighbouring old fields. 15. Measure days between final harvest and field termination. 16. Create a database for factories and projects. 17. Train staff in data entry. 18. Audit data in the field. 19. Analyse results at the end of the season. 20. Use the data to develop local thresholds.

Field records are not additional paperwork. They are the structure linking: Monitoring. Decision-making. Biological control. Insecticides. Resistance. Residues. Harvest. Economic evaluation. The system should begin with a clear field identification card and a map of sectors, traps, and neighbouring fields. Transplants should be inspected before movement, and the decision to accept, isolate, or reject each batch should be documented. Traps require installation, maintenance, and reading records that specify: Lure type. Operating days. Trap condition. Daily catch. Plant inspection should record: Eggs. Parasitism. Active mines. Larval instars. Growing points. Fruits. Natural enemies. Every biological release should be recorded from receipt through assessment of emergence and field establishment. Every chemical treatment should also be recorded according to: Active ingredient. Group. Rate. Target stage. Preharvest interval. Reassessment date. Changing the trade name is not rotation when the mode-of-action group remains the same. Treatment failure should be linked to an investigation form reviewing: Diagnosis. Larval instar. Rate. Coverage. Weather. Product quality. Immigration. Resistance. Resistance samples require a chain of custody that preserves knowledge of their origin, date, and transport conditions. The system is completed by recording: Harvest. Rejection. Residues. Costs. Field termination. Lessons learned. A sound database converts individual experience into institutional knowledge that can be used to develop more efficient programmes, local economic thresholds, and an Egyptian early-warning system. References

Tuta absoluta management programmes do not always fail because insecticides are unavailable or ineffective. Many failures begin with a small error in the nursery, a short delay in inspection, or an inaccurate trap reading, after which errors accumulate until larvae reach the fruits and correction becomes more difficult and costly. Tuta absoluta is a pest that strongly penalises sequential mistakes because: Eggs are small and difficult to detect. Larvae enter plant tissues rapidly. Insect stages overlap within the field. Adults can move among crop cycles. The pest can develop resistance to insecticides. Limited fruit infestation may cause major marketing losses. Old fields may reinvade newly planted fields. One error may act as the primary cause, while others increase its severity. For example: 1. Infested transplants enter the field. 2. Traps are absent. 3. Inspection is delayed. 4. Larvae are detected only after reaching L3 and L4. 5. An insecticide unsuitable for the instar is used. 6. The same group is repeated when performance is weak. 7. Natural enemies collapse. 8. Infestation reaches the fruits. Failure should therefore be analysed as a causal chain, not as an isolated incident.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: A critical error is an action or omission that can, alone or in combination with other errors, lead to one or more of the following outcomes: Establishment of the pest within the field. Loss of the window for targeting eggs and young larvae. Spread of infestation to fruits. Collapse of biological control. Increased insecticide-resistance pressure. Violation of preharvest intervals or residue limits. Increased control cost without a return. Transformation of the field into a regional infestation source. Loss of a substantial proportion of marketable yield. Not every error necessarily causes complete failure on every occasion. Some errors may pass without obvious damage during a low-pressure season and cause severe loss in another season.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Catastrophic Errors These can cause programme collapse or wide-scale spread of the pest, such as: Distributing infested transplants to several fields. Leaving a heavily infested field without termination. Repeating an insecticide group that has shown reduced performance. Ignoring increasing fruit infestation. Using an unregistered material close to harvest. High-Risk Errors These greatly reduce the probability of success, such as: Relying on traps alone. Delaying intervention against hatching. Poor sprayer calibration. Killing natural enemies. Failing to manage field edges. Moderate-Risk Errors These may not cause failure alone, but they weaken the programme, such as: Failing to standardise recording units. Failing to link inspection frequency to temperature. Failing to measure labour costs. Poor documentation of the biological belt. The risk associated with a moderate error increases when it is repeated or coincides with high pest pressure.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Classification of Errors by Stage of Occurrence
text: Errors may be divided into: 1. Preseason planning errors. 2. Nursery and transplant errors. 3. Prevention and field-preparation errors. 4. Monitoring and sampling errors. 5. Data-interpretation errors. 6. Cultural and mechanical-control errors. 7. Behavioural-control errors. 8. Biological-control errors. 9. Microbial-product errors. 10. Insecticide and resistance-management errors. 11. Spray-application errors. 12. Fruit and harvest-management errors. 13. Field-termination errors. 14. Area-wide-management errors. 15. Economic-evaluation and documentation errors.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error One: Beginning to Think About Tuta Only After Infestation Appears
text: One of the most dangerous practices is to regard the Tuta programme as a response that begins when mines appear. This causes loss of opportunities to: Inspect the nursery. Install traps before transplanting. Prepare an insecticide list. Design IRAC windows. Identify source fields. Prepare natural enemies. Organise the biological belt. Train workers. Larvae may already be inside the plant before the team begins preparing the programme. Corrective Action The programme should begin before transplanting through: Assessment of site history. Inspection of surrounding fields. Review of the nursery. Establishment of the monitoring network. Definition of responsibilities. Preparation of intervention alternatives.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Two: Preparing a Fixed Seasonal Spray Schedule
text: A fixed schedule depends on the number of days after transplanting, regardless of: Pest presence. Insect stage. Natural enemies. Temperature. Harvest date. Immigration pressure. This may lead to: Unnecessary sprays. Mortality of beneficial organisms. Increased resistance. Depletion of effective groups before fruiting. Increased residues. Corrective Action Replace the spray schedule with: A monitoring programme. A decision matrix. Graduated intervention alternatives. Resistance-management windows. Reassessment after every intervention.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Three: Failing to Define Responsibilities
text: Good data may be collected without a decision being issued because: The trap reader does not communicate with the crop-protection engineer. The spray operator does not know the target instar. The harvest officer does not know the preharvest interval. The nursery fails to report a suspected infestation. An old field is removed without notifying neighbouring fields. Corrective Action Define in writing: The monitoring officer. The data-analysis officer. The person authorised to decide on intervention. The calibration officer. The residue officer. The field-termination officer.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Four: Failing to Establish a Baseline
text: Without data from previous seasons, the team does not know: The usual trap-catch level. When increases normally begin. Which field edges are most at risk. Which materials have shown reduced efficacy. Average fruit-infestation percentage. Cost of the conventional programme. Corrective Action Establish a baseline including: Traps. Mines. Fruits. Natural enemies. Applications. Yield. Costs. Residues.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Moving infested transplants is one of the fastest routes for establishing the pest within a field. A transplant may carry: Eggs. A small larva within a limited mine. An advanced larva. A pupa in plant debris or surrounding substrate. Distributing an infested batch creates multiple hotspots whose source later becomes difficult to trace. Corrective Action Inspect a representative sample from each batch. Inspect trays, not only individual transplants. Isolate suspect batches. Reject the batch when infestation is widespread. Trace the source of every batch within the field.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Six: Spraying Infested Transplants and Moving Them Immediately
text: Spraying may kill some stages, but it does not guarantee: Entry of the material into all mines. Egg mortality. Pupal mortality. Absence of hatching after movement. It may also begin an IRAC sequence in the nursery without being recorded within the field programme. Corrective Action After any corrective treatment: Isolate the batch. Reinspect it. Confirm that new mines have stopped appearing. Record the active ingredient and IRAC group. Do not move the batch until evaluation is complete.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seven: Leaving Nursery Rejects Near Greenhouses or Trays
text: Rejected plants may allow the pest to complete its life cycle and return to healthy transplants. Corrective Action Collect rejects immediately. Place them in sealed containers. Dispose of them in a manner that prevents insect escape. Clean the tray area. Remove solanaceous weeds.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eight: Failing to Include Nursery Insecticides in the Resistance Plan
text: Transplants may reach the field after the pest has been exposed to several insecticide groups, after which the field uses the same group again. Corrective Action Each batch should be accompanied by a record showing: Active ingredients. IRAC groups. Treatment dates. Number of applications. Inspection results.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Nine: Planting Beside an Infestation Source Without a Plan
text: A newly planted field may be adjacent to: Old tomato. Infested potato. Eggplant. Sorting waste. Volunteer plants. The season may therefore begin under continuous immigration pressure. Corrective Action Map infestation sources. Increase monitoring of field edges. Coordinate with managers of old fields. Determine the removal date. Install traps before transplanting.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Ten: Leaving Volunteer Tomatoes and Solanaceous Hosts
text: Volunteer plants may act as bridges between crop cycles and allow the pest to persist when no commercial crop is present. Corrective Action Remove: Volunteer tomato plants. Potato residues. High-risk wild solanaceous plants. Infested plants around stores and crop containers.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Excessive nitrogen fertilisation causes: Dense, tender growth. Difficult inspection. Poor spray penetration. Delayed ripening. Extended crop duration. Potentially increased plant suitability for some pests. This does not mean that plant starvation should be used to manage Tuta. Corrective Action Use balanced nutrition that achieves: Functional growth. Adequate fruit cover. Ease of inspection and spray coverage. No unnecessary extension of the crop cycle.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twelve: Excessive Pruning or Leaf Removal
text: Some growers may believe that removing large numbers of infested leaves reduces the pest, but excessive removal may: Expose fruits to sunlight. Reduce photosynthesis. Increase secondary disorders. Transfer larvae during handling. Leave infested residues inside the field. Corrective Action Removal should be used only: For limited hotspots. With collection of removed parts in containers. Without weakening canopy cover. With safe disposal of residues.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirteen: Failing to Install Traps Early
text: Installing traps only after mines appear deprives the team of data on: Beginning of flight. First immigration wave. Entry direction. Relationship between adults and eggs. Corrective Action Install traps: Before or at transplanting. In fixed locations. With separate coding for edges and interior. With a clear maintenance record.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fourteen: Relying on Traps Alone
text: Traps measure males and do not independently prove: Presence of mated females. Oviposition. Larval presence. Fruit infestation. Catch may be high without plant infestation, or low despite an established internal generation. Corrective Action Always link traps with: Egg inspection. Active mines. Larval instars. Fruits. Adjacent fields.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifteen: Converting a Trap Number into a General Spray Threshold
text: The relationship between catch and damage differs according to: Trap type. Lure. Field. Region. Immigration. Natural enemies. Crop stage. Corrective Action Use catch to determine: Risk direction. Inspection priority. Threatened edges. Do not use it alone as a spray order.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Traps may differ in: Pheromone type. Lure age. Operating duration. Height. Adhesive type. Maintenance condition. Corrective Action Standardise: Trap type. Lure. Replacement date. Height. Reading period. Unit of males/trap/day.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventeen: Placing Traps in Unrepresentative Locations
text: All traps may be placed: Near one edge only. Near a light source. Within overly tall crop growth. Away from immigration sources. Corrective Action Distribute traps among: Different edges. Field interior. Contrasting sectors. Locations facing old fields.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighteen: Using a Fixed Inspection Interval Throughout the Season
text: Weekly inspection may be appropriate during one period but become too long when temperature rises and egg duration shortens. Corrective Action Adjust the interval according to: Temperature. Eggs close to hatching. Fruit stage. Immigration pressure. Upward trend.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Nineteen: Taking an Unrepresentative Sample
text: Examples include: Inspecting only plants near the road. Avoiding dense areas. Selecting visually infested plants. Failing to inspect field edges. Changing sample size every week. Corrective Action Use a fixed route or stratified sample including: Edges. Interior. Sectors. Cultivars. Different transplanting dates.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty: Counting Old Mines as Active Infestation
text: Mines remain after: Larval death. Larval exit. Parasitism. Successful treatment. Counting them may lead to unnecessary repeat intervention. Corrective Action Distinguish an active mine through: A live larva. Fresh frass. Fresh tissue damage. Increased mine area between two visits.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty-One: Failing to Identify Larval Instar
text: L1 and L4 differ in: Susceptibility. Feeding location. Probability of fruit entry. Suitability for Bt. Speed of damage. Corrective Action Record instar distribution before every decision, then select the method appropriate to the dominant stage.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty-Two: Interpreting Parasitism Percentage Without Total Egg Number
text: A parasitism rate of 70% may appear high, but when egg density is very large, many eggs may remain capable of hatching. Corrective Action Calculate: Total eggs. Parasitised eggs. Live unparasitised eggs. Eggs close to hatching.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty-Three: Ignoring Differences Between Edges and Interior
text: Infestation may represent: Immigration from outside. Or an internal generation. Combining data obscures the source of the problem. Corrective Action Record separately: Each edge. The interior. Sectors facing risk sources.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: The team may focus on declining leaf mines while fruit infestation continues. Corrective Action From fruit set onward, include: Fruit infestation by number. Weight loss. Live larvae. Associated rots.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty-Five: Interpreting Correlation as Causation
text: Predator abundance may increase as Tuta increases because prey becomes more available, not because predators caused the increase or prevented damage. Corrective Action Use: Time trends. Sector comparisons. Measurement of predation or parasitism. Fruit assessment.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty-Six: Leaving Infested Fruits and Plant Parts in the Field
text: Larvae may continue developing in removed fruits or leaves. Corrective Action Collect residues. Place them in sealed containers or areas. Dispose of them in a manner preventing insect emergence. Do not leave them between rows.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty-Seven: Removing Extensive Plant Parts Without Assessment
text: Agronomic damage may exceed the benefit of removing the pest. Corrective Action Use removal when infestation is: Limited. Clearly delimited. Collectable. Unlikely to expose fruits or severely reduce leaf area.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty-Eight: Using Tillage Alone as a Termination Method
text: The following may remain: Fruits. Plant parts. Plants that were not buried effectively. Adult moths that left before tillage. Corrective Action Use an integrated termination programme including: Fruit collection. Plant shredding. Tillage or residue treatment. Monitoring of regrowth. Temporary continuation of trap monitoring.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Twenty-Nine: Ignoring Equipment and Containers
text: Equipment may transport: Infested fruits. Leaves. Plant residues. Pupae. Corrective Action Clean: Harvesting machinery. Containers. Transport vehicles. Workers' tools. Before moving to younger fields.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty: Treating Water Traps Alone as a Control Programme
text: Water traps may reduce part of the male population, but they do not guarantee: Prevention of mating. Capture of females. Cessation of oviposition. Prevention of immigration. Corrective Action Use them within a programme combining: Monitoring. Sanitation. Natural enemies. Targeting of eggs and larvae.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-One: Beginning Mass Trapping After Population Density Has Become High
text: At high density, a limited number of males may be sufficient to mate with females. Corrective Action Begin mass trapping early, while maintaining: Appropriate trap density. Trap maintenance. Management of the infestation source.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-Two: Declaring Mating Disruption Successful Because Trap Catch Declines
text: Trap catch may decline because males are confused, while some mating may continue. Corrective Action Evaluate: Eggs. Egg fertility. Mines. Fruits. Not traps alone.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-Three: Applying Mating Disruption in a Small, Highly Open Area
text: Mated females may immigrate from outside, while wind disperses the pheromone plume. Corrective Action Evaluate the technique in: Large blocks. Contiguous areas. Well-sealed greenhouses. Coordinated area-wide programmes.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-Four: Releasing Natural Enemies After Infestation Has Advanced
text: Egg parasitoids provide no benefit when eggs are absent, and a small number of predators cannot repair a crop whose fruits are already infested. Corrective Action Begin releases: Preventively or early. According to presence of the target stage. Before the red risk level is reached.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-Five: Failing to Assess the Quality of a Biological Shipment
text: A shipment may arrive with: High mortality. Premature emergence. Delayed emergence. A low proportion of females. Heat exposure. Corrective Action Record: Batch number. Transport conditions. Mortality. Emergence percentage. A retained reference sample.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-Six: Poor Distribution of Release Points
text: Concentrating cards or predators in a few locations leaves large areas unprotected. Corrective Action Distribute uniformly, with additional points near: High-risk edges. Hotspots. Areas containing eggs.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-Seven: Exposing Natural Enemies to Sunlight and Heat
text: Cards or containers may be damaged before organisms emerge. Corrective Action Transport under appropriate conditions. Distribute during moderate periods. Attach cards within the canopy. Prevent contact with water or direct sunlight according to product instructions.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-Eight: Using an Incompatible Insecticide After Release
text: The insecticide may kill natural enemies before they perform their function. Corrective Action Review: Direct toxicity. Residual effect. Required interval. Natural-enemy stage. More selective alternatives.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Thirty-Nine: Treating Predator Presence as Sufficient Evidence of Control
text: The presence of several adults does not prove that a stable population exists or that infestation can be contained. Corrective Action Measure: Adults. Nymphs. Reproduction. Distribution. Pest-to-predator ratio. Fruit infestation.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty: Ignoring Plant Injury Caused by Mirids
text: Some predators may injure plants when their density is high or prey is scarce. Corrective Action Monitor: Predator density. Feeding rings. Flower drop. Growing-point condition. Availability of prey and food.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-One: Planting Any Flowering Species
text: Not every flowering plant is beneficial. A plant may be: A host for the pest. A source of whiteflies. Attractive to thrips. Competitive with tomato. A source of nectar inaccessible to parasitoids. Corrective Action Select plants according to: Selectivity. Flower structure. Flowering period. Failure to host dangerous pests. Climatic suitability.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-Two: Applying the 50:30:20 Mixture as a Fixed Prescription
text: The proposed dill–coriander–basil model requires local testing and may not suit all crop cycles. Corrective Action Apply it experimentally. Calculate proportions by area. Measure flowering. Monitor pests and beneficial organisms. Modify the mixture according to results.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-Three: Spraying the Biological Belt
text: Broad spraying kills the insects the belt was established to support. Corrective Action Protect the belt from drift. Use hotspot treatment. Remove an infested section when necessary. Select selective materials.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-Four: Measuring Insects Only Within the Belt
text: Beneficial organisms may remain concentrated on flowers without moving into tomato. Corrective Action Measure: Beneficial organisms within the belt. Beneficial organisms within tomato at different distances. Parasitism percentage. Fruit infestation. Number of sprays.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-Five: Using Bt Against Advanced Instars
text: Bt is generally more effective when young larvae ingest it before entering deeply into plant tissues. Corrective Action Time application with: Hatching. L1. Beginning of L2. Coverage of new growth.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-Six: Assuming a Slow-Acting Biological Agent Has Failed
text: Larval feeding may stop before mortality occurs, and fungi and nematodes may require time to express their effect. Corrective Action Set assessment timing according to: Mode of action. Temperature. Humidity. Target instar.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-Seven: Applying Fungi Under Intense Sunlight and Severe Dryness
text: This reduces spore survival and germination. Corrective Action Apply at an appropriate time. Select a suitable strain. Review humidity. Avoid direct radiation. Use registered protective formulations.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-Eight: Allowing Nematodes to Dry on the Leaf Surface
