text: Nematodes are highly sensitive to desiccation and radiation. Corrective Action Apply in the evening or under suitable conditions. Provide temporary moisture. Use an appropriate carrier. Target hotspots.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Forty-Nine: Mixing Microbial Organisms Without Compatibility Testing
text: The following may reduce organism viability: Acidity. Fungicides. Disinfectants. Salts. Oxidising agents. Corrective Action Refer to: The product label. Compatibility tests. A small-scale jar test. Temporal separation when uncertain.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty: Selecting an Insecticide by Trade Name
text: Different trade names may contain the same active ingredient or the same mode-of-action group. Corrective Action Base the decision on: Active ingredient. IRAC group number. Target stage. Previous record.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty-One: Repeating an IRAC Group
text: This is one of the most serious causes of resistance, especially when successive generations are exposed to the same group. Corrective Action Use mode-of-action windows. Avoid repeating the group in the following generation. Introduce non-chemical methods. Record all applications.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty-Two: Treating a Change of Active Ingredient Within the Same Group as Rotation
text: The two active ingredients may share the same target site, so selection pressure continues. Corrective Action Rotate among genuinely different groups while reviewing the potential for cross-resistance.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty-Three: Increasing the Rate When Efficacy Declines
text: A higher rate does not restore susceptibility and may cause: Increased residues. Phytotoxicity. Mortality of beneficial organisms. Increased selection pressure. Violation of registration requirements. Corrective Action Investigate the cause of failure. Suspend the group when resistance is suspected. Use only the label rate. Conduct susceptibility testing.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Mixtures may cause: Incompatibility. Precipitation. Phytotoxicity. Mortality of natural enemies. Multiple residues. Concealment of resistance to one component. Corrective Action Use a mixture only when it is: Registered or supported by evidence. Compatible. Necessary. Not unnecessarily duplicating modes of action.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty-Five: Preventive Chemical Spraying Without Demonstrated Risk
text: This consumes effective groups and kills beneficial organisms before they are needed. Corrective Action Reserve chemical insecticides for situations in which the following are demonstrated: Active infestation. An increasing trend. Threat to fruits. Insufficiency of other methods.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty-Six: Using a Material Against an Unsuitable Instar
text: An active ingredient may be effective, but applying it after larvae have entered fruits produces a weak result. Corrective Action Determine larval instar before issuing the spray order.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty-Seven: Failing to Include Sprays Against Other Pests
text: Tuta may be exposed to an IRAC group used against a leaf-feeding caterpillar or another pest, causing repeated pressure that is absent from the Tuta record. Corrective Action Record all insecticide applications used in the field within the comprehensive resistance-management plan.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty-Eight: Neglecting the Preharvest Interval
text: An insecticide may perform well biologically, yet the crop may be rejected because of: Premature harvest. Residue exceedance. Use of a material not accepted by the factory. Corrective Action Before every decision, review: PHI. Harvest date. Market. Number of applications. Permitted-material list.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Fifty-Nine: Failing to Calibrate the Sprayer
text: This may produce: An insufficient rate. An excessive rate. Variation among rows. Product wastage. Apparent material failure. Corrective Action Measure: Nozzle output. Travel speed. Pressure. Working width. Actual water volume.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty: Keeping Water Volume Constant as Plant Size Changes
text: The water volume used immediately after transplanting is insufficient to cover a dense fruiting crop. Corrective Action Recalibrate as: Plant height increases. Rows close. The number of leaf layers increases. Clusters appear.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty-One: Focusing on the Upper Leaf Surface
text: Many eggs, larvae, and predators occur on lower leaf surfaces and within the canopy. Corrective Action Adjust: Nozzle direction. Suitable pressure. Travel speed. Droplet size. Water-sensitive-card testing.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty-Two: Spraying Under Unsuitable Wind or Temperature
text: This causes: Drift. Rapid drying. Poor coverage. Worker exposure. Contamination of the biological belt. Corrective Action Select a suitable application window and record weather conditions.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: The following may affect the stability or efficacy of some products: Alkalinity. Hardness. Salts. Suspended materials. Corrective Action Measure water quality and use permitted conditioners when required.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty-Four: Preparing the Spray Solution and Leaving It for a Long Period
text: The efficacy of some active ingredients or biological organisms may decline while remaining in the tank. Corrective Action Prepare immediately before use. Maintain agitation when required. Do not store diluted spray solution. Follow product instructions.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty-Five: Evaluating the Treatment Too Early
text: There may not have been sufficient time for the effects of the following to appear: Insect growth regulators. Bt. Fungi. Nematodes. Parasitoids. Corrective Action Set the assessment date in advance according to mode of action.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty-Six: Evaluating the Treatment Too Late
text: The treatment outcome may become confounded with: New hatching. New immigration. Development of a subsequent generation. Corrective Action Use more than one assessment date where necessary: Early assessment. Intermediate assessment. Assessment of protection duration.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty-Seven: Using Total Mine Number as the Success Indicator
text: Mines remain after larvae die. Corrective Action Measure: Live larvae. New mines. Cessation of mine expansion. New fruit infestation.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty-Eight: Declaring Resistance After One Treatment Failure
text: The cause may be: Poor application. Larval instar. Weather. Product quality. New hatching. Immigration. Corrective Action Conduct a full investigation and a standardised susceptibility test.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Sixty-Nine: Leaving Infested Fruits During Harvest
text: Larvae continue developing within fruits and may reach collection areas or the factory. Corrective Action Separate infested fruits. Collect them in separate containers. Dispose of them. Do not throw them between rows.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy: Stopping Monitoring When Harvest Begins
text: A fresh-market field may remain in production for weeks, while a processing field may remain a source until final mechanical harvest. Corrective Action Continue: Trap monitoring. Fruit inspection. Recording rejected produce. Monitoring adjacent fields.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy-One: Delaying Harvest Without Analysis
text: Delay may cause: Increased fruit infestation. Canopy collapse. Increased rots. Transformation of the field into a source. Corrective Action Link the delay decision to: Ripeness. Fruit quality. Pest pressure. Factory schedule. Weather. Expected return.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: All damaged fruits may be attributed to Tuta despite the presence of: Blossom-end rot. Sunscald. Mechanical damage. Diseases. Corrective Action Classify rejection by cause and separate confirmed Tuta infestation.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy-Three: Stopping Pest Management Before Field Termination
text: Some growers stop managing the pest after the final economic harvest while plants remain alive for a long period. Corrective Action Continue containment until: Fruits are collected. Plants are shredded. Regrowth is terminated. Emergence of a new generation is prevented.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy-Four: Allowing the Field to Dry Naturally
text: Gradual drying does not kill all stages, and moths may leave for other fields. Corrective Action Use rapid, organised termination rather than waiting for natural plant death.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy-Five: Shredding Plants and Leaving Fruits Behind
text: Larvae may continue within fallen fruits even after the canopy is destroyed. Corrective Action Collect fruits before or during shredding and dispose of them safely.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy-Six: Failing to Clean Harvesting and Shredding Machinery
text: Equipment may transport infested residues into the next field. Corrective Action Clean equipment before movement, recording: Cleaning date. Previous field. Next field.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy-Seven: Failing to Monitor Regrowth
text: Plants may regrow after irrigation or moisture and become pest hosts. Corrective Action Revisit the field after termination and remove any new growth.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy-Eight: Managing Each Field as an Isolated Island
text: Moths can move among fields, causing excellent management in one field to fail because of neighbouring fields. Corrective Action Coordinate: Traps. Field-removal dates. Warning exchange. Resistance plans. Sanitation at sorting stations.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Seventy-Nine: Failing to Share Information on Failure and Resistance
text: Concealing reduced performance of an insecticide group causes continued use in the region and increased resistance. Corrective Action Establish a reliable technical system for sharing: Failure cases. Susceptibility results. Repeated groups. Suspect products.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighty: Fundamentally Different Programmes Among Neighbours
text: One field may use a biological programme, while the adjacent field applies broad-spectrum insecticides or leaves residues unmanaged. Corrective Action Develop a shared minimum standard covering: Field termination. Resistance management. Immigration notification. Protection of biological areas.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighty-One: Ignoring Sorting Stations and Factories
text: Infested fruits and residues may accumulate near collection areas. Corrective Action Remove waste daily. Prevent host plants around the site. Install monitoring traps. Manage wash water and waste.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighty-Two: Evaluating the Programme by Insect Numbers Alone
text: Pest density may decline without increasing accepted yield, or yield may increase despite persistence of some mines. Corrective Action Measure: Fruit infestation. Accepted yield. Rejection rate. Cost. Residues. Natural enemies.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: The biological belt, fruit collection, or release programme may appear inexpensive when labour is not included. Corrective Action Include all costs, even permanent farm labour.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighty-Four: Treating the Lowest Number of Sprays as Success
text: Reducing sprays is not an independent objective when it causes: High fruit infestation. Yield reduction. Transformation of the field into a source. Corrective Action Measure success through balance among: Protection. Cost. Sustainability. Residues.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighty-Five: Treating the Highest Kill Percentage as the Best Programme
text: A material may produce rapid mortality but also: Kill beneficial organisms. Increase resistance. Increase residues. Cost more. Corrective Action Use multi-criteria evaluation covering short- and long-term outcomes.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighty-Six: Failing to Analyse Causes of Failure
text: At the end of the season, the conclusion may simply be: “Tuta pressure was high this year.” Without determining: When did it enter? Why was detection delayed? Which group failed? Was the source external? Was coverage poor? Corrective Action Conduct an end-of-season review linking every result to a causal chain.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighty-Seven: Failing to Document a Non-Intervention Decision
text: Not spraying may be correct, but lack of documentation makes it appear to be neglect. Corrective Action Record: Trap data. Eggs. Mines. Fruits. Beneficial organisms. Date of the next inspection.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

sectionTitle: Error Eighty-Eight: Failing to Convert Experience into a Written Procedure
text: An experienced engineer may know when to intervene, but that knowledge is lost when the team changes. Corrective Action Convert experience into: Decision matrices. Records. Protocols. Training. A database.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: The most operationally serious errors may be ranked as follows: 1. Accepting or distributing infested transplants. 2. Leaving old fields after they cease to be economically viable. 3. Beginning control only after larvae reach fruits. 4. Relying on traps alone. 5. Repeating IRAC groups. 6. Killing natural enemies with broad-spectrum insecticides. 7. Poor sprayer calibration and coverage. 8. Ignoring eggs and larval instars. 9. Failing to manage immigration waves among fields. 10. Ignoring preharvest intervals and residues close to harvest. This ranking does not mean that other errors are unimportant. Any error may become critical under specific field conditions.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: This may be represented as follows: Weak prevention ← absence of early monitoring ← delayed detection ← advanced larval instars ← unsuitable chemical intervention ← weak result ← repetition or mixing of insecticides ← mortality of beneficial organisms ← resistance and resurgence ← fruit infestation ← increased rejection and cost The chain should be broken at the earliest possible point rather than at its end.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Healthy transplants ← traps and early inspection ← egg detection ← protection and augmentation of parasitoids ← targeting L1 and L2 ← hotspot treatment ← genuine rotation of IRAC groups ← fruit protection ← organised harvest ← rapid field termination Each step reduces the burden on the following step.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: When failure occurs, use the following sequence. What Was the Final Problem? Example: Increased fruit infestation. Why Did It Occur? Because advanced larvae reached the clusters. Why Did Larvae Reach Advanced Instars? Because the hatching wave was not detected. Why Was It Not Detected? Because inspection remained weekly despite rising temperature, and field edges were not inspected. Why Were Edges Not Inspected? Because sectors were not defined and responsibility was unclear. The root cause is therefore administrative and related to monitoring, not simply weak insecticide performance.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Error Expected Result Detection Indicator Corrective Action Infested transplants Early hotspots Instars inconsistent with field age Isolate and trace the batch No traps Loss of flight-onset data Mines detected first Install before transplanting Reliance on traps Incorrect sprays Mismatch between catch and eggs Link traps with plant inspection Old mines counted Repeat spraying Absence of live larvae Distinguish active mines Failure to identify instars Incorrect method selection Persistence of L3 and L4 Inspect instar distribution IRAC repetition Resistance Consecutive efficacy decline Change group and test susceptibility Poor coverage Surviving hotspots Differences within the canopy Calibrate and test with cards Mortality of beneficial organisms Pest resurgence Sudden predator decline Use selective materials and re-establish Delayed removal of infested fruits Continued generation Larvae inside rejected fruit Collect and destroy Old field left standing Immigration Increased edge-trap catch Terminate the source Ignoring PHI Crop rejection Harvest before permitted date Review the record Premature evaluation of a biological product Unnecessary repeat treatment Delayed mortality Use an appropriate assessment date
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: The system is built on four layers. Layer One: Prevention Protocols. Checklists. Training. Field coding. Layer Two: Detection Record auditing. Trap review. Joint inspection. Water-sensitive cards. Layer Three: Correction Modify the decision. Recalibrate. Change the IRAC window. Manage the source. Layer Four: Prevention of Recurrence Root-cause analysis. Protocol update. Team training. Lesson sharing.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Before issuing a control order, confirm: Correct diagnosis. Presence of active infestation. Identification of the stage. Identification of infestation location. Knowledge of fruit status. Review of natural enemies. Review of the most recent IRAC group. Review of registration. Review of PHI. Confirmation that the sprayer is calibrated. Definition of the assessment date. When these data are absent and no severe emergency exists, they should be completed before intervention.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Review: Application of the correct rate. Actual water volume. Weather conditions. Coverage. New mines. Live larvae. New hatching. Status of beneficial organisms. Fruit infestation. Need for a subsequent action.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Errors and failure cases should not be concealed out of fear of blame because concealment causes: Repetition. Spread of resistance. Delayed protection of other fields. Loss of evidence. The system should focus on: Identifying the cause. Correcting the procedure. Documenting the lesson. Defining professional responsibility without discouraging early reporting.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: 1. Establish a mandatory transplant-inspection protocol. 2. Install traps before transplanting. 3. Standardise the definition of an active mine. 4. Train engineers to identify larval instars. 5. Include fruit infestation in every inspection after fruit set. 6. Require projects to maintain an IRAC record. 7. Record nursery sprays and treatments against other pests. 8. Calibrate sprayers regularly. 9. Prevent rate increases and random mixtures. 10. Protect local natural enemies. 11. Develop a plan for removing old fields. 12. Clean harvesting and shredding equipment. 13. Organise waste from sorting stations and factories. 14. Review PHI before every harvest. 15. Investigate every failure before retreatment. 16. Provide standardised susceptibility testing. 17. Measure accepted yield, not total yield alone. 18. Document intervention and non-intervention decisions. 19. Conduct root-cause analysis at the end of the season. 20. Convert recurring errors into administrative performance indicators.
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: Failure of a Tuta absoluta management programme rarely results from one cause. It usually arises from a chain of errors beginning before visible damage appears. The most serious failure points include: Moving infested transplants. Delaying trap installation. Relying on traps without inspecting plants. Ignoring eggs and early instars. Confusing old and active mines. Ignoring fruit infestation. Repeating IRAC groups. Poor coverage and calibration. Killing natural enemies. Leaving old fields and residues. A timing error cannot be corrected by increasing the insecticide rate. Once a larva enters a fruit, it becomes more difficult to target, and treatment cannot reverse damage already caused. Changing the trade name is not rotation when the mode of action remains the same. Resistance should not be declared after one failed treatment before reviewing: Larval instar. Coverage. Weather. Rate. Product quality. New hatching. Immigration. Biological control requires early initiation, good transport and distribution, and protection from insecticides. Releasing natural enemies without measuring emergence and field establishment is insufficient. Likewise, a biological belt cannot perform its function when its plants are established without assessment, sprayed directly, or allowed to become sources of other pests. Harvest and postharvest stages are as important as the beginning of the season because infested fruits, old fields, and residues can maintain the pest cycle and transfer it to the following crop. The most efficient programme is the one that prevents the failure chain at its beginning through: Planning. Early monitoring. Targeting appropriate stages. Data-based decision-making. Conservation of natural enemies. Resistance management. Organised field termination. References
partTitle: Critical Errors Leading to Failure of Integrated Pest Management Programmes

text: This protocol represents the operational synthesis of integrated management of Tuta absoluta in open-field tomatoes. It aims to convert the scientific principles presented in this chapter into an ordered sequence of practical steps beginning before planting, continuing throughout all crop-growth stages, and ending only after field removal and follow-up of remaining infestation sources. This protocol should not be used as: A spray schedule. A fixed calendar programme. A list of commercial products. A single numerical threshold for all fields. A substitute for field inspection. A substitute for the legal registration of products. A substitute for the experience of the agricultural engineer. Rather, it should be used as: A framework for organising operations. A checklist for the technical team. A decision-making system. A reference for defining responsibilities. A basis for developing a field-specific programme. A tool for evaluating seasonal success or failure. The protocol is based on one fundamental fact: The earlier intervention begins within the pest life cycle, the lower the need for costly corrective interventions close to harvest.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The protocol aims to achieve the following outcomes: 1. Prevent movement of the pest with transplants. 2. Detect moth entry early. 3. Detect eggs before hatching. 4. Target L1 and L2 before they enter deep tissues. 5. Maintain functional canopy cover. 6. Prevent larvae from reaching fruits. 7. Protect parasitoids and predators. 8. Reduce unnecessary insecticide use. 9. Manage resistance from the first application. 10. Comply with preharvest intervals and residue requirements. 11. Prevent old fields from reinvading newly planted crops. 12. Preserve marketable or processable yield. 13. Reduce control cost per accepted tonne. 14. Document every decision and action. 15. Improve the programme from one season to the next.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The programme should not be implemented if the following principles are absent: No transplanting without transplant inspection. No traps without reading and maintenance records. No spray decision based on traps alone. No counting of old mines as current activity. No intervention without identifying the target stage. No use of an unregistered material. No exceeding the label rate. No random repetition of an IRAC group. No release of natural enemies without reviewing pesticide compatibility. No harvest before completion of the preharvest interval. No leaving infested fruits and residues inside the field. No end to the season without eliminating the infestation source. No evaluation of success from insect mortality alone. No adoption of a foreign threshold without local calibration. No use of a trade name in place of the active ingredient and mode of action.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The protocol is applied at the level of: Field. Sector. Cultivar. Transplanting date. Field edge. Physiological stage. A large project should not be treated as one decision unit when it contains differences in: Transplanting dates. Cultivars. Infestation levels. Immigration sources. Harvest dates. Previous pesticide programmes. The northern sector may require intervention while the field interior remains at monitoring level only.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Depending on project size, the team consists of: Monitoring Officer Responsible for: Traps. Plant inspection. Recording eggs and mines. Identifying larval instars. Issuing alerts. Biological-Control Officer Responsible for: Receiving natural enemies. Assessing quality. Release. Monitoring emergence and establishment. Protecting organisms from pesticides. Pesticide-Application Officer Responsible for: Calibration. Rates. Water quality. Weather conditions. Implementing the treatment order. Quality and Residue Officer Responsible for: Permitted-material lists. Preharvest intervals. Number of applications. Residue samples. Factory or market requirements. Harvest and Residue Officer Responsible for: Collecting infested fruits. Managing rejected produce. Cleaning equipment. Field termination. Decision Manager Responsible for integrating data, issuing the final decision, and documenting it. On small farms, one person may perform more than one function, but responsibilities should not be lost.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The protocol uses five levels: Green Level Monitoring continues. No significant active infestation is present. No direct intervention is required. Yellow Level Risk indicators are present. Inspection is intensified. Intervention tools are prepared. Orange Level Eggs, hatching, or L1 and L2 are present. Compatible early intervention begins. Red Level Advanced instars or a direct threat to clusters and fruits are present. Urgent corrective intervention is implemented. Critical Level Fruit infestation is increasing, failure is repeated, or crop viability is declining. Comprehensive containment, early harvest, or sector termination is implemented.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The planning stage should begin sufficiently before planting to allow: Study of site history. Communication with the nursery. Preparation of traps. Identification of neighbouring fields. Ordering natural enemies. Preparation of the insecticide record. Design of the biological belt. The programme should not begin only on the day transplants arrive.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Collect the following data: Were tomato, potato, or eggplant previously grown? Was severe Tuta infestation recorded? Which active ingredients were used? Were failure cases observed? How long did residues remain after harvest? Where did infestation first appear? Which edge was most at risk? What was the previous season's fruit-infestation percentage? What was the accepted yield? What was the status of natural enemies? Classify the site as: Low historical pressure. Moderate risk. High risk. Suspected resistance or failure site.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Survey of Surrounding Fields and Hosts
text: Prepare a map with a radius appropriate to the region, including: Tomato fields. Potato. Eggplant. Nurseries. Sorting stations. Factories or fruit-collection points. Waste piles. Wild and volunteer solanaceous plants. For each field, record: Crop stage. Infestation level. Harvest date. Expected removal date. Direction relative to the new field.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The planting date should not be determined by Tuta alone, but it should be evaluated in relation to: Overlap with old fields. Expected temperature. Pest-development rate. Crop duration. Factory schedule. Project monitoring capacity. Transplanting immediately beside large, heavily infested old fields may increase immigration pressure from the beginning of the season.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Before the season, prepare a table containing: All registered and available active ingredients. IRAC groups. Target stages. Effects on natural enemies. Preharvest interval. Maximum number of applications. History of use in the nursery. Results from the previous season. Then divide options into: Early-stage tools. Fruiting-stage tools. Corrective tools. Materials to avoid because of reduced susceptibility or harmful effects on beneficial organisms. Including a material in the plan does not mean it must be used.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The plan should specify: The organism intended for use. The pest stage it attacks. Supplier. Experimental or approved release rate. Time to begin release. Transport conditions. Biological waiting periods after pesticide use. Method for evaluating success. Alternative plan if the shipment is unavailable. Natural enemies should not be ordered only after infestation reaches fruits.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: When the belt is part of the programme, define: Location. Area. Plant species used. Staggered planting dates. Irrigation line. Protection from spraying. Method for monitoring pests within it. Indicators for evaluating movement of beneficial organisms into tomato. The model consisting of: 50% dill. 30% coriander. 20% basil. Should be treated as an experimental model requiring local evaluation, not as a mandatory composition.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Define in advance: Trap locations. Trap codes. Edge traps. Interior traps. Inspection routes. Number of plants in each sample. Inspection units. Reading schedule. Responsible person. Data-entry method. The network should cover: Potential immigration sources. Differences among sectors. Different cultivars or transplanting dates.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Planting should not begin until the following are confirmed: A field identification card exists. A field map exists. Transplant source is known. Traps are prepared. Monitoring responsibility is defined. An IRAC record exists. Factory or market list is known. Residue-disposal tools are prepared. An emergency plan exists. Expected harvest date is known.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The nursery should implement: Intact screening when a protected structure is used. Secure doors. Pheromone traps. Periodic inspection. Removal of host weeds. Separation among batches. Cleaning of rejected plants. An insecticide record. Traceability for every batch.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Inspection of Mother Plants and Trays
text: Inspect: Lower leaf surfaces. Young leaves. Small mines. Growing points. Edge trays. Plants near doors. Weeds. Traps. Early mines may be small and difficult to detect.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Decision to Accept a Transplant Batch
text: Accept the batch when: No confirmed live stages are present. No active mines are present. No uninspected suspect hotspots remain. The insecticide record is complete. Transplant source is known. Trays are sound. Isolate the batch when suspicion exists. Reject the batch when infestation is: Distributed. Present in multiple instars. Untraceable. Impossible to guarantee as removed.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: When Infestation Is Detected in the Nursery
text: Implement: 1. Stop movement of the batch. 2. Isolate trays. 3. Determine the extent of spread. 4. Remove infested plants. 5. Inspect adjacent batches. 6. Review traps. 7. Identify the stage. 8. Implement a documented intervention where required. 9. Reinspect after an appropriate interval. 10. Record the group used within the field programme. Transplants should not be moved immediately after spraying without reassessment.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Approximately One to Two Weeks Before Transplanting
text: Implement the following: Install traps or confirm that they are ready. Take an initial moth reading. Remove volunteer plants. Inspect neighbouring fields. Confirm removal of previous-crop residues. Establish or maintain the biological belt. Fix sector codes. Prepare the inspection record. Review sprayer readiness. Confirm the accepted-material list.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Before transplants enter, record: Trap catches. Presence of infested hosts around the field. Natural-enemy status. Residue condition. Removal date of neighbouring fields. This reading later helps distinguish between: Infestation arriving with transplants. Immigration occurring after transplanting.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Review: Nozzles. Output. Pressure. Speed. Water volume. Tank cleanliness. Condition of measuring tools. Personal protective equipment. Calibration should not be postponed until the first emergency infestation.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: When transplants arrive, review: Batch code. Cultivar. Nursery source. Tray condition. Insecticide records. Any mines or symptoms. Transport duration and conditions. Keep batches separate within the field so traceability remains possible.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Record the location of each batch on the map. Trays from different sources should not be mixed randomly within the field because this prevents identification of the infestation source if early symptoms appear.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The following are prohibited: Burying infested plants within rows. Leaving trays and rejects in the field. Preventive chemical spraying without justification. Using an unregistered material. Ignoring previous nursery treatments. Discarding batch records after planting.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The principal objective is: To detect infestation carried with transplants or the first immigration wave before establishment of a stable generation. Priority is given to growing points and young leaves.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Inspect: Delayed or weak plants. Different batches. Edges facing old fields. Young leaves. Small mines. Eggs. Traps. Increase inspection frequency when: A source field is present. Temperature rises. Trap catches increase. Infestation is reported in the supplying nursery.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Infestation Associated with One Batch Sector This suggests a transplant-related problem. Infestation Beginning from One Edge This suggests immigration. Distributed Infestation with Advanced Instars This may indicate old infestation carried with transplants. High Trap Catch Without Eggs This requires closer inspection, not automatic spraying.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: When a small hotspot is present: Mark affected plants. Carefully remove infested parts when this does not damage the plant. Collect residues. Inspect the surrounding area. Treat the hotspot where necessary. Reinspect promptly. The whole field should not be treated when infestation has not spread.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Release begins when the following conditions are met: Eggs are present or a documented risk of oviposition exists. No harmful residues are present. Parasitoid-batch quality is acceptable. Weather is suitable. Release points are distributed. A reassessment plan exists. One release rate should not be used under all conditions without calibration.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The objective is to: Prevent accumulation of mines. Protect growing points. Establish natural enemies. Maintain ease of plant inspection. Prevent development of a widespread internal generation.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Record: Trap catches. Live eggs. Parasitism. New mines. L1 and L2. L3 and L4. Infested growing points. Predators and nymphs. Biological-belt status.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Decision When Eggs Are Present Without Hatching
text: Follow this sequence: 1. Estimate egg age. 2. Estimate parasitism. 3. Determine distribution. 4. Protect egg parasitoids. 5. Set the reinspection date. 6. Prepare a hatching-stage intervention where necessary. Automatic chemical intervention is not required.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Decision When L1 and L2 Appear
text: This is the principal intervention window. Rank alternatives as follows: Natural regulation when sufficient. Parasitoids and predators. Bt or a registered microbial agent. Hotspot treatment. A selective insecticide when infestation is increasing. Define success through: Cessation of new-mine appearance. Reduction in live larvae. No expansion of the hotspot.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Decision When L3 and L4 Appear
text: The following should be done: Inspect future fruits and clusters early. Use a registered method suitable for the instar where required. Review why hatching was detected late. Improve coverage. Do not rely on Bt alone as a rescue treatment. Do not increase the rate randomly.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: As plants increase in size: Eggs become more difficult to see. Spray penetration into the canopy declines. Water-volume requirements change. Shaded and humid zones develop. Access to lower leaf surfaces becomes more difficult. Recalibration is therefore required.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Increase representation of: Inner canopy. Middle leaves. Plant-contact zones. Interior rows. Leaves near developing clusters. Inspection should not be limited to growing points visible from the road.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Management should maintain: Balanced growth. No excessive nitrogen. No drought stress. No excessively dense canopy. Adequate cover to protect future fruits. Agronomic stress should not be used as a Tuta-control method.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: At flowering and fruit set, infestation near: Growing points. Flowers. Clusters. Becomes more important than lower mines located far away. Tolerance of continuing active larvae declines.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Inspect: Eggs near clusters. Leaves touching flowers. Sepals. Growing points. Petiole mines. Presence of frass. Predators on young growth.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Raise the intervention level when: Eggs recur near clusters. Recent hatching appears. L1 and L2 increase. Parasitism declines. Mines spread among sectors. Flower damage begins. Priority remains targeting early instars with minimum harm to beneficial organisms.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: What Must Be Prevented at This Stage
text: Waiting for fruit infestation. Using a material highly toxic to pollinators and beneficial organisms without necessity. Removing extensive canopy cover. Repeating a previously used IRAC group. Spraying without covering clusters and adjacent leaves.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The direct objective is: To prevent larvae from moving into fruits and preserve accepted yield. Fruit inspection becomes a mandatory component of each round.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Inspect: The calyx. Pedicel attachment area. Small holes. Frass. Fruits touching infested leaves. Fruits along field edges. Fruits near infested growing points. Record: Number of fruits inspected. Infested fruits. Presence of live larvae. Associated rots.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: When the first confirmed infestation is detected: 1. Collect the fruit. 2. Inspect neighbouring clusters. 3. Increase sample size. 4. Identify larval instars on the plant. 5. Review the previous intervention record. 6. Raise the decision level. 7. Review harvest timing and PHI. Do not wait for infestation to spread.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Collection should be implemented when it is: Practical. Economically justified. Capable of reducing the larval source. Place fruits in: Separate containers. Bags or vessels preventing larval escape. The approved disposal route. Do not throw fruits between rows.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Avoid: Extensive leaf removal. Plant stress. Premature reduction of irrigation. Excessive use of materials causing scorch or leaf drop. Fruit exposure increases: Sunscald. Heat stress. Difficulty in maintaining quality. Fruit exposure to the pest and environmental factors.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Priorities change to: Protecting existing fruits. Reducing rejected produce. Complying with the preharvest interval. Determining whether intervention is justified. Maintaining harvest timing. Preventing the field from becoming a source.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Before any treatment, review: Date of the most recent application. Active ingredient. IRAC group. Number of applications. Preharvest interval. Factory list. Target market. Expected harvest date. No treatment order should be issued before documenting these data.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Feasibility of Intervention Close to Ripening
text: Ask the following questions: Are there enough fruits that can still be protected? Will the method act before harvest? Is the PHI suitable? Is infestation localised or general? Is advancing harvest more economical? Does treatment create a residue risk? Are there generations likely to move into neighbouring fields?
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: This may include: Collecting infested fruit. Intensifying grading. Hotspot treatment. Targeting a continuing hatching wave. Advancing harvest where ripeness permits. General spraying is not always necessary.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Review: Percentage of infested fruits. Weight loss. Remaining crop. Intervention cost. PHI. Probability of success. The decision may shift to: Corrective intervention. Early harvest. Sector termination. Field containment.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Complete a checklist covering: Completion of preharvest intervals. Residue-analysis results where required. Fruit-infestation percentage. Ripeness percentage. Factory or market date. Status of neighbouring fields. Rejected-produce plan. Equipment-cleaning plan. Postharvest removal plan.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: A final treatment should not be applied merely because harvest is approaching. It should have: A clear objective. A defined target stage. Sufficient time to act. Legal registration. A suitable PHI. An expected return. When these conditions are absent, collection and grading or advancing harvest may be more appropriate.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: When required, collect the sample according to: A representative method. Multiple sectors. Suitable ripeness stages. A chain-of-custody record. Correct storage and transport. Linkage with the most recent treatments. Apparently sound fruits should not be collected from only one location with the aim of obtaining a low result.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Monitoring should not stop when harvest begins, especially in: Fresh-market tomatoes with multiple pickings. Processing fields with an extended harvest period. Fields adjacent to recently planted crops. Continue: Fruit inspection. Traps. Edge monitoring. Recording rejected produce.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Where possible, work should begin in: The least-infested sectors. Then the more heavily infested sectors. Train workers to: Identify holes. Separate infested fruit. Avoid discarding rejected fruit in the field. Report hotspots. Clean tools.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Separate: Sound fruit. Tuta-infested fruit. Rotted fruit. Mechanically damaged fruit. Fruit with other defects. Do not attribute all rejected produce to Tuta.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: For processing tomatoes, consider: Timing of concentrated harvest. Ripeness percentage. Fruit infestation by number and weight. Cleaning the harvester. Preventing transfer of infested fruit to another field. Recording factory rejection.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Consider: Repeated pickings. Reviewing PHI before every picking. Separating infested containers. Avoiding storage of rejected fruit near the field. Monitoring infestation development between pickings.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Decision on the End of Economic Viability
text: The field should not be retained merely because: Some small fruits remain. Or in the hope of a weak final picking. The end of economic viability should be determined according to: Value of the remaining crop. Harvest cost. Infestation percentage. Plant condition. Risk posed to neighbouring fields. Cost of additional control.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Interval Between Final Harvest and Removal
text: This interval should be as short as operationally possible. Record: Date of final harvest. Date removal began. Number of days. Reason for any delay. Every unjustified delay allows the pest cycle to continue.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Before or during shredding: Collect infested fruits. Collect fallen fruits. Prevent movement with equipment. Dispose of them in a manner preventing insect emergence. Shredding the canopy while leaving fruits on the soil surface is insufficient.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: This includes: Shredding plants. Killing regrowth. Managing residues according to the farm system. Preventing survival of green plant parts. Monitoring regrowth. Allowing plants to dry naturally is not an adequate measure.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Clean: Harvesting machinery. Shredders. Tractors. Containers. Transport vehicles. Hand tools. Before moving to a younger field.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Keep some traps temporarily to: Assess emergence of moths from residues. Measure decline of the source. Warn neighbouring fields. Post-removal catches should not be interpreted without considering shredding date and surrounding fields.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Reinspect the field for: Plants that did not die. New growth. Volunteer tomatoes. Remaining fruits. Alternative hosts. Remove them immediately.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Hold a meeting to review: 1. Traps. 2. Eggs. 3. Parasitism. 4. New mines. 5. Larval instars. 6. Fruits. 7. Natural enemies. 8. Temperature. 9. Neighbouring fields. 10. IRAC record. 11. Harvest date. 12. Result of the previous intervention. Then issue a decision for each sector.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Minimum Data Required Before a Decision
text: No intervention decision should be issued without knowing: Infestation location. Direction of spread. Dominant stage. Fruit status. Natural enemies. Most recent insecticide group. PHI. Date of the next assessment. During a severe emergency, a rapid containment measure may be taken while the investigation is completed immediately.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The report should include: Risk level for each sector. Principal indicators. Actions implemented. Delayed actions. Materials used. Status of beneficial organisms. Date of the next round. New source fields. Operational problems.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: When Should Monitoring Become Daily or Near-Daily?
text: When: Trap catch increases suddenly. Removal of a neighbouring field begins. An egg wave occurs. Eggs close to hatching are present. Temperature rises. The first fruit infestation appears. Treatment failure is suspected. Harvest is near while active infestation persists. Daily monitoring does not mean inspecting the entire field. It means following selected rapid indicators.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: This includes: Edge traps. Marked eggs. L1 hotspots. Infested fruits. The source field. Weather status. Result of the most recent treatment.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Confirm: The target stage is present. The correct organism is supplied. The shipment is sound. Residues are not harmful. Weather is suitable. Workers are trained. Release points are marked.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Record: Time. Temperature. Number of units. Distribution. Any damage. Sector. Operator's name. Do not leave cards or containers under direct sunlight.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Evaluate: Parasitoid emergence. Predator survival. Parasitism percentage. Nymph distribution. Change in eggs and larvae. Effect of any later pesticide. Do not judge the release from empty containers alone.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Presence of suitable instars. A registered product. Correct storage. Suitable water. Appropriate environmental timing. Good coverage. No incompatible mixture.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Priority is given to: Before expected hatching. During hatching. When L1 and L2 dominate. Rescue value declines when advanced instars dominate inside fruits.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Measure: Feeding cessation. Larval mortality. New mines. Fruit infestation. Continued hatching. Natural-enemy status. Immediate mortality should not be the only criterion.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: Conditions for Issuing a Spray Order
text: The following should be present: Active infestation. Risk to the crop. A known target stage. Insufficiency of less disruptive methods. A registered material. An appropriate IRAC group. A suitable PHI. A calibrated sprayer. A reassessment date.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Selection should be based on: Active ingredient. Mode-of-action group. Larval instar. Use history. Natural enemies. Preharvest interval. Local resistance. Weather conditions. It should not be based on: Trade name. An undocumented recommendation. Grower mixtures. Desire for a “strong knockdown” alone.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Apply the window principle so that: Successive generations are not exposed to the same group. Necessary applications of a group are confined to a defined window according to approved recommendations. The following window changes to a genuinely different group. Periods relying on non-chemical methods are included. Using two active ingredients from the same group is not rotation.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Review: Weather conditions. Water quality. Rate. Water volume. Pressure. Machine speed. Nozzles. Personal protective equipment. Protection of the biological belt. Absence of harvest workers from the sector.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Record: Start and end times. Actual area treated. Quantity used. Weather. Any interruption. Any uncovered area. Re-entry time. Assessment date.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Immediately prohibit: Automatic repetition of the material. Increasing the rate. Adding further materials to a mixture. Declaring resistance without investigation. Begin investigating: Timing. Instar. Coverage. Weather. Rate. Product quality. New hatching. Immigration. Resistance.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: An edge is classified as high risk when: It is close to an old field. Its trap catches are high. Egg density increases. Infestation has historically recurred there. It faces a clear immigration direction. Increase the following along this edge: Inspection. Release points. Fruit monitoring. Hotspot intervention where required.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: When an internal hotspot appears: Mark it on the map. Inspect the surrounding area. Investigate its cause. Prevent workers from moving from the hotspot to sound areas before cleaning tools. Treat it separately where possible. An internal hotspot may indicate: An infested transplant batch. Poor coverage. An irrigation problem or dense growth. Localised resistance. A residue source.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

sectionTitle: When Does Hotspot Treatment Become General Treatment?
text: When: Hotspots recur in several sectors. Eggs become widespread. Multiple instars appear. Clear boundaries cannot be defined. Fruit infestation begins in more than one sector. The immigration source continues. The decision remains linked to risk level and crop stage.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: A field is suspected as a source when: Trap catches facing it increase. Infestation within it is high. Control has stopped. Final harvest begins. Irrigation stops. Shredding or plant removal begins. Eggs appear along the facing edge.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Notify the source-field manager. Set a removal date. Prevent fruits from being left behind. Increase monitoring in the recipient field. Coordinate equipment use. Clean shredders and machinery. Share trap data. Protecting the recipient field alone is insufficient while the source remains active.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Action: Verify the trap and lure. Inspect eggs. Inspect field edges. Review old fields. Do not implement automatic spraying.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Action: Determine egg age. Estimate parasitism. Monitor hatching. Augment egg parasitoids. Prepare an L1 intervention. Shorten the reinspection interval.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Action: Implement an early compatible intervention. Cover new growth. Review continuing hatching. Protect natural enemies. Conduct rapid assessment.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Action: Inspect fruits. Use a registered method suitable for the instar where required. Review the cause of delay. Collect infested material. Do not rely on Bt alone as a rescue treatment.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Action: Measure the percentage. Collect infested fruit. Inspect neighbouring clusters. Review PHI. Protect sound fruits. Evaluate advancing harvest.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Action: Stop repetition. Collect evidence and samples. Review calibration. Review instar and weather. Use an alternative mode of action when necessary. Request susceptibility testing.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Action: Calculate protectable yield. Calculate the cost of continuation. Evaluate advancing harvest. Terminate the sector when no longer viable. Prevent it from becoming a source.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Trap trend. Live eggs/plant. Parasitism percentage. New mines. Instar distribution. Predators and nymphs. Growing-point infestation. Fruit infestation.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Percentage of inspection rounds completed. Percentage of functioning traps. Response time to an alert. Percentage of treatments reassessed. Accuracy of IRAC records. Time to field removal after harvest. Quality of sprayer calibration.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Programme cost/feddan. Programme cost/accepted tonne. Percentage of rejected fruit. Number of sprays avoided. Value of accepted yield. Net benefit. Benefit–cost ratio.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Number of broad-spectrum sprays. Survival of natural enemies. Number of IRAC groups used. Cases of PHI violation. Cases of residue non-compliance. Quantity of infested residues collected. Fields removed on schedule.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Review site history. Map source fields. Contract with a reliable nursery. Prepare traps. Develop an IRAC plan. Prepare natural enemies. Design the biological belt. Calibrate the sprayer.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Inspect leaves and growing points. Monitor traps. Isolate batches. Record insecticides. Reject widespread infestation. Do not move a batch that has not been reinspected.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Install traps. Remove hosts and residues. Record the baseline reading. Code fields and sectors. Review neighbouring fields.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Inspect batches. Focus on field edges. Search for eggs and small mines. Identify the source of any infestation. Treat hotspots early.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Monitor eggs and parasitism. Target L1 and L2. Protect growing points. Conserve beneficial organisms. Recalibrate the sprayer as plants grow.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Inspect leaves near clusters. Lower tolerance of active larvae. Prevent infestation from reaching flowers. Use selective and early interventions.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Inspect fruits during every round. Collect infested fruit. Protect canopy cover. Include PHI in the decision. Do not wait for infestation to increase.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Review the factory list. Evaluate the feasibility of every intervention. Do not use a slow-acting material immediately before harvest. Evaluate advancing harvest. Collect residue samples where required.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Continue monitoring. Separate rejected produce. Do not leave infested fruits. Clean containers and equipment. Record acceptance and rejection.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Do not leave the field alive. Collect fruits. Shred plants. Clean equipment. Continue trap monitoring. Remove new growth. Notify neighbouring fields.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The protocol is considered successful when it achieves most of the following: Infestation is detected before fruit infestation. Intervention is concentrated at the egg, L1, and L2 stages. Advanced instars do not accumulate. Unjustified treatments decline. Natural enemies persist. IRAC groups are not repeated in violation of the plan. Fruit infestation declines. Preharvest intervals are respected. Accepted yield increases. The field is terminated rapidly. An acceptable economic return is achieved.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: These include: First detection of the pest inside fruits. Spread of L3 and L4 before the first intervention. Repeated dependence on corrective treatments. Absence of egg data. Repetition of the same IRAC group. Collapse of natural enemies. Repeated failure without investigation. High fruit rejection. PHI violation. Delayed field removal. Movement of the pest into neighbouring fields.
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Review: What was the first indicator that warned of the problem? Was a response made? When did eggs appear? When was the first intervention implemented? What was the dominant instar at intervention? Which edges were most exposed? Did natural enemies succeed? Which materials showed declining efficacy? What was the fruit-infestation percentage? What was the programme cost? Was the field removed on schedule? What modification is required for the following season?
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: The operational protocol for managing Tuta absoluta begins before tomato planting, not after mines appear. The first steps towards success are: Understanding site history. Identifying source fields. Using healthy transplants. Installing traps early. Preparing biological-control and resistance-management plans. In the nursery, movement of eggs and larvae into the field should be prevented. Spraying infested transplants and moving them without reassessment is insufficient. After transplanting, priority is given to detecting infestation carried with transplants or immigrating from field edges and managing it before a stable generation develops. Eggs and first- and second-instar larvae represent the most important intervention window, whereas spread of L3 and L4 indicates delayed monitoring, poor timing, or inadequate application. At flowering and fruit set, tolerance of infestation near growing points and clusters should decline. Once fruits appear, fruit inspection becomes a mandatory component of every round. No treatment decision close to harvest should be made without reviewing: Preharvest interval. Target market. Harvest date. Yield that can still be protected. Economic viability. The programme continues during harvest through: Grading. Collection of infested fruits. Cleaning equipment. Recording rejected produce. The programme ends only after: Collecting remaining fruits. Destroying the canopy. Cleaning equipment. Continuing trap monitoring. Removing regrowth. Chemical intervention depends on: Presence of active infestation. Identification of the target instar. Use of a registered material. Selection of an appropriate IRAC group. Calibration. Reassessment. Weak performance should not be managed by increasing the rate or repeating the group. It requires a technical investigation and susceptibility testing where necessary. Protocol success results from integration of several small, continuous actions, not from one spray or an insecticide described as the strongest. References
partTitle: Final Operational Protocol for Integrated Pest Management

text: Tuta absoluta cannot be managed sustainably through a single decision made within a tomato field because the pest life cycle extends across a broad production system that includes: Seed companies. Nurseries. Growers. Agricultural engineers. Farm managers. Pesticide suppliers. Producers of natural enemies. Tomato-processing factories. Collection and grading stations. Export companies. Laboratories. Researchers. Agricultural extension services. Regulatory authorities. Contract-farming programmes. One field may implement an advanced programme and still fail because of: Infested transplants arriving from the nursery. A neighbouring old field that was not terminated. Use of an unsuitable pesticide in a nearby field. Movement of infested fruits and residues. Poor quality of a commercial natural enemy. Absence of a unified warning system. Failure to share resistance cases. Sustainable management therefore depends on: Distribution of responsibilities, data sharing, and holding each stakeholder accountable for the part of the pest cycle under its control.

The system should be based on the following principles: 1. Every stakeholder is responsible for preventing the production or movement of a new pest source. 2. Responsibility for failure should not be placed entirely on the grower or the insecticide. 3. Movement of transplants, pesticides, produce, and residues should be documented. 4. Warnings should be shared among neighbouring fields. 5. Commercial interests should be separated from scientific product evaluation. 6. No biological or chemical product should be adopted without quality control. 7. The factory should be linked to the field programme before harvest, not only after rejecting the consignment. 8. Research organisations should provide solutions that are applicable and economically measurable. 9. Regulatory authorities should support area-wide management, not merely paper registration. 10. Every stakeholder is responsible for protecting the future efficacy of insecticides and natural enemies.

The agricultural engineer is the centre for integrating information, not merely the executor of a spray programme. Responsibilities include: Diagnosis. Monitoring. Data interpretation. Selection of the decision level. Compatibility review. Resistance management. Implementation follow-up. Outcome evaluation. Decision documentation. Engineer performance should not be measured by the number of treatment orders issued, but by the ability to protect the crop with the least possible disruption to the production system.

The engineer should: 1. Ensure that traps are installed before or at the beginning of transplanting. 2. Separate edge traps from interior traps. 3. Review lure and trap validity. 4. Inspect eggs, mines, larvae, and fruits. 5. Distinguish active mines from old mines. 6. Identify larval instars. 7. Record natural enemies. 8. Link data to temperature. 9. Review neighbouring fields. 10. Avoid making a decision from a single trap reading.

Each decision should specify: Evidence that intervention is required. Infestation location. Target stage. Crop stage. Fruit status. Selected method. Reason alternatives were rejected. Reassessment date. Success indicator. A general instruction such as: “Spray for Tuta” Should not be issued without identifying the stage, sector, and objective.

The engineer should: Record the active ingredient. Record the IRAC group. Include nursery treatments. Include applications against other pests. Avoid repeating a group under a different trade name. Investigate declining efficacy. Prevent rate increases. Collect samples when resistance is suspected. Introduce periods relying on non-chemical measures.

The engineer should: Record predator and parasitoid species. Review the effect of every pesticide on them. Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides unless necessary. Protect the biological belt. Assess release quality. Select timings that reduce exposure of beneficial organisms. Monitor recovery of natural enemies after intervention. Beneficial organisms should not be treated as a marginal note in the report.

The engineer should review: Preharvest interval. Factory or market list. Ripeness percentage. Fruit infestation. Protectable yield. Treatment feasibility. Harvest timing. Residue analyses. The engineer should refuse to issue a treatment order when there is no: Biological justification. Sufficient time for the method to act. Compatibility with harvest.

The engineer may be evaluated according to: Detection of infestation before it reaches fruits. Percentage of completed inspection rounds. Response speed. Accuracy of larval-instar identification. Compliance with IRAC windows. Reduction of unjustified treatments. Persistence of natural enemies. Accepted yield. Completeness of records. Speed of terminating completed fields.

The grower is a partner in decision-making, not a passive recipient of recommendations. Responsibilities include: Purchasing healthy transplants. Allowing regular monitoring. Avoiding treatments outside the programme. Complying with the rate and preharvest interval. Managing residues. Informing neighbours when severe infestation occurs. Terminating the field when it is no longer economically viable.

The grower should: Deal with a reliable nursery. Request a treatment record for the batch. Inspect transplants on receipt. Reject trays carrying mines or widespread infestation. Avoid mixing transplant sources without documentation. Retain the nursery name and batch number. Infested transplants should not be accepted merely because they are cheaper.

The grower should not demand spraying merely because of: Seeing one moth. A high reading in one trap. Persistence of old mines. Observing a neighbouring field being sprayed. Likewise, necessary intervention should not be delayed when there is confirmed: High egg density. A hatching wave. Cluster or fruit infestation.

The grower should: Avoid purchasing an insecticide by trade name alone. Avoid increasing the rate. Avoid mixing several products without review. Avoid repeating a material that has shown reduced efficacy. Avoid unknown or unregistered materials. Avoid spraying the biological belt without reason.

The grower should: Collect infested fruits. Avoid throwing them between rows. Avoid moving them into another field. Clean containers. Remove the crop when economic viability ends. Prevent volunteer tomato growth.

The grower should notify neighbouring fields when: Removal of a heavily infested field begins. A major outbreak occurs. Resistance is suspected. Infested transplants from a shared source are discovered. Concealing infestation may increase pressure across the entire area.

The farm manager converts the technical programme into an implementable system through: Resource allocation. Definition of responsibilities. Approval of records. Quality monitoring. Prevention of conflicting decisions. Coordination among fields.

The manager should identify: Trap officer. Inspection officer. Biological-control officer. Spray officer. Residue officer. Harvest officer. Final decision-maker. A substitute should be available for each role in case of absence.

A serious management error is to reduce inspection costs and then spend several times more on corrective treatments. A budget should be allocated for: Traps and lures. Technical labour. Magnifying lenses and tools. Weather stations. Digital maps. Resistance samples. Residue analyses.

The spray officer should not receive different instructions from: The crop-protection engineer. Production manager. Pesticide supplier. Grower. Factory. The treatment order should come from one authorised source after data review.

In large projects: Divide the area into sectors. Issue an independent decision for each sector. Use hotspot maps. Avoid spraying the entire project because of localised infestation. Compare cultivar and transplanting-date performance. Manage equipment movement.

The manager should hold periodic meetings reviewing: Data. Treatments. IRAC groups. Emergencies. Source fields. Fruit infestation. Harvest. Costs. The review should produce defined decisions, each with a responsible person and implementation date.

sectionTitle: Treating the Nursery as the First Line of Defence
text: A nursery distributing infested transplants does not merely transfer a few larvae; it may establish tens or hundreds of hotspots across multiple fields. Freedom of transplants from the pest should therefore be considered a basic quality criterion.

The nursery should: Maintain screens and doors. Install pheromone traps. Remove weeds and host plants. Separate batches. Clean trays. Manage rejected plants. Prevent entry of infested tomato fruits or plants. Train workers.
Inspect: Trays near doors. Edges. Young leaves. Lower leaf surfaces. Small mines. Weak plants. Traps. Inspection frequency should increase when regional activity rises.

Every batch should carry: A code. Cultivar. Sowing date. Seed source. Inspection date. Insecticide record. Delivery date. Names of receiving fields. This enables notification of all clients when infestation linked to the batch is detected.

When infestation is detected: 1. Stop distribution. 2. Isolate the batch. 3. Determine the extent of spread. 4. Remove infested plants. 5. Inspect adjacent batches. 6. Implement a documented intervention where necessary. 7. Reinspect. 8. Inform customers who received transplants from the same batch. The case should not be concealed to protect commercial reputation.

The certificate should preferably include: Batch number. Date of the most recent inspection. Trap status. Active ingredients used. IRAC groups. Date of the most recent treatment. Result of pre-shipment inspection. The certificate is not an absolute guarantee, but it improves traceability and accountability.

The company should provide data on: Correct species. Strain or origin. Number of individuals. Emergence percentage. Female proportion where relevant. Production date. Storage conditions. Shelf life. Transport method. Proposed release rate.

One rate should not be marketed as suitable for all: Greenhouses and open fields. Cool and hot production cycles. Egg densities. Field sizes. Immigration levels. Rates should be presented as: A starting point. Linked to pest stage and density. Adjustable according to monitoring.

The company is responsible for: Maintaining an appropriate temperature. Minimising transport time. Preventing exposure to sunlight. Documenting warehouse-dispatch time. Inspecting packages at delivery. Explaining how delays should be managed.

The company's role should not end with selling the organism. It should include: Worker training. Review of pesticide residues. Evaluation of release points. Monitoring emergence. Monitoring parasitism or establishment. Investigating weak performance.

The company should explain: Natural enemies are not knockdown insecticides. Their efficacy is affected by pesticides, weather, and immigration. They require an early start. Release alone does not guarantee fruit protection during an advanced outbreak. Greenhouse efficacy percentages should not be used to market the product for open fields without explaining the difference.

Local production programmes should preferably comply with: Taxonomic identification. Contamination prevention. Quality testing. Strain preservation. Standardisation of packaging units. Batch records. Field-efficacy testing.
The company's responsibility is not limited to demonstrating toxicity; it also includes protecting product efficacy and reducing misuse.

The company should clearly state: Active ingredient. IRAC group. Target stage. Maximum number of applications. Preharvest interval. Effect on natural enemies. Water-quality requirements. Coverage requirements. Resistance risks. Incompatible mixtures.

Statements such as the following should be prohibited or avoided: Completely eliminates Tuta. Resistance does not occur. Can be used against all instars. Suitable for weekly spraying.
